# CANoe Configuration Guide (Vector CANoe 15.0)

> 本文档基于 Vector CANoe 15.0 主帮助文档整理，涵盖 CANoe 仿真配置、测量设置、总线配置、测试模块、诊断、COM 接口等。
> 适用对象：CANoe 配置工程师、自动化测试工程师、仿真工程师。
> 生成日期：2026/06/01
> 注: v15 在保留 12.0 全部配置能力的基础上，新增 RT Setup、SIL (Software-in-the-Loop)、Standalone Manager、ADAS 仿真等。

---

## 目录

- CANoe 仿真配置概述
- 总线与网络配置 (Networks)
- 通道分配 (Channel Assignment)
- 数据库加载与符号访问
- 测试模块与 Test Service Library
- 诊断 (Diagnostics) 配置
- SIL (Software-in-the-Loop) 配置 - v15 新增
- RT Setup - v15 新增
- Standalone Manager - v15 新增
- COM Interface API

---

### CANoeCANalyzerConcept


# CANoe | Concept

CANoe » Concept

| Introduction |
| --- |
| CANoe is a universal development, test and analysis environment, which is made available to all project participants over the entire development process. The system producer is supported in functional distribution, functional checking and integration of the overall system. The supplier obtains an ideal test environment by simulation of the remaining bus and environment. |
| Architecture |
| Real-time Area Time-critical sequences are processed in the real-time area. The incoming bus data are read in via a bus interface, provided with a time stamp there, and then processed promptly. The sending of the data to the bus also takes place in the real-time area. All blocks (e.g., CAPL programs, test modules, test units, generators) that you insert in the Simulation Setup or Test Setup are processed in the real-time area. For this reason, you should only insert blocks there that use little computing time and thus do not influence the reaction of the system. To prevent the real-time area from being influenced by time-consuming analysis, the incoming data are forwarded from the real-time area to the analysis area via a buffer. Analysis Area Non-time-critical sequences are processed in the analysis area. The data from the buffer are forwarded to the individual analysis windows via the Measurement Setup. The data are thereby processed from left to right and from top to bottom. In the Measurement Setup you can insert various f

[...truncated for brevity...]

### CANoeCANalyzerProductFamily


# CANoe/CANalyzer Product Family

CANoe/CANalyzer Product Family

| CANoe CANoe is a comprehensive software tool for the development, testing and analysis of entire ECU networks and individual ECUs. It supports you throughout the entire development process – from planning to startup of entire distributed systems or individual ECUs. CANoe's versatile functions and configuration options are used by network designers, development and test engineers at OEMs and suppliers. Product Variants CANoe pex The project execution variant provides a graphical user interface exclusively. Simulation, test cases and results are easily controlled without the need to specifically evaluate the underlying messages. CANoe run The runtime variant is suitable for users who want to quickly and easily test their ECU in interaction with a specified remaining bus simulation. Configurations cannot be changed, analysis functions are fully available and network nodes can be easily connected and disconnected. CANoe pro The professional variant is intended for users who want to take advantage of the full range of CANoe functions. Simulation models can be created with CAPL and .NET. Test cases are easy to model with the Test Feature Set. CANoe4SW CANoe4SW is the comprehensive tool for development, test, and analysis of software in cyber physical systems – individual software components as well as subsystems and entire distributed systems. It supports software developers and testers in all markets (medical, ra

### [A429/basicsA429] A429basics


# Basics

A429 » Basics

## ARINC word format

In ARINC 429, data are transmitted in an ARINC word with the following format:

The ARINC word contains fields with a defined meaning:

- P: Parity bit Definition: Parity bit Bit 31 contains the parity bit for the ARINC word. The specification sets the parity to “odd”.

- SSM: Sign/Status Matrix

- data: Area with application data

- SDI: Source/Destination Identifier

- label: ARINC label Definition: ARINC label Each ARINC word is identified uniquely on the bus by an ARINC label. The label can assume values of 1..255 and is specified as an octal value in the specification (see /1/). The transmission on the bus takes place as bit-inverted data. If an octal representation is carried out within CANoe, a small “o” is appended to the octal value. Example The label 125o (hex: 0x55, dec: 85) is transmitted: LSB MSB 1 0 1 0 1 0 1 0

## Transmission on the bus

There is one sender in each case on an ARINC 429 bus. All other network nodes connected to the bus are receivers. ARINC words are transmitted at an agreed communication speed, and the electrical signal on the bus cables is RZ-coded.

Transmission can take place at low-speed (12..14.5 kbit/s) or high-speed (100 kbit/s). The permissible tolerances of the transmission rate are shown in the following table. The average transmission speed can deviate by up to 1% over the duration of the ARINC word. For an individual bit, a deviation of 2.5% is allowed.

|  | High-speed | Low-speed |
|

A429 » Basics » Create a Configuration and Analyze the Bus Traffic

Eight ARINC channels and the Trace Window are already preconfigured here. Assign one or more relevant databases. A database may be generated with the provided tools. Start the measurement with <F9> and observe the Trace Window. |

### [A429/basicsA429] A429dbSupport


# Database Support

A429 » Basics » Database Support

ARINC 429 words can be written in a database (DBC format). The application data are then available as signals within CANoe. In addition, all network information can be represented symbolically.

The label is used as a unique key within the database. A new database can be created with the Aerospace Message Editor. Alternatively CANdb++ can be used (only for 8Bit long labels). The template A429_template.dbc is to be used for this.

## Network Attributes

The database contains a network object that is described using the following attributes. These attributes are created automatically.

| Name | Data Type | Values | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| BusType | String | Default: “ARINC429” | This attribute ensures that the database is interpreted as a network description for ARINC 429. This attribute must not be changed by the user! |
| Baudrate | Integer | Value range: 10500..110000 Default: 100000 | Bit rate for the ARINC 429 bus This value is not used for initialization of a channel. Rather it serves only as a comment. |
| A429Parity | Enumeration | Value range: unknown, odd, even Default: odd | This value is not used for initialization of a channel. Rather it serves only as a comment. |
| ProtocolType | String | Default: "Aerospace" | This attribute activates "extended multiplexing". This enables a protocol interpretation using DBC signals. This attribute must not be changed by the user! |

## Node Attributes

An a

### [A429/basicsA429] A429ErrorDetection


# Bus and Line Errors

A429 » Basics » Bus and Line Errors

With the VN0601, errors can only be detected if a transmission of ARINC words also takes place. The error detection for a Rx channel is dependent to a large extent on the setting of the channel parameters.

|  | Cross Reference You will find more information about the error handling via selectors in CAPL here. |

## Rx errors

| Error Description | Cause | Details |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Gap Violation | The minimum gap for two consecutive ARINC words (gap) has been fallen below. The value for the minimum gap can be changed in the hardware configuration. | The Gap Violation message is output in the Trace Window. The error was caused with the ARINC word that chronologically followed the error message. The system variables GapErrors and WordErrors are increased. |
| Parity Error | The received data do not match the parity bit. The evaluation of the parity can be changed in the Hardware Configuration. | The Parity Error message is output in the Trace Window. The data for the ARINC word are displayed in the Trace Window, but may also be incorrect. The system variables ParityErrors and WordErrors are increased. |
| Bit length exceeded/Bit length too short | The duration of an individual bit of the ARINC word is outside the defined tolerance band. The tolerance band can be changed in the hardware configuration. | The Bit length exceeded or Bit length too short message is output in the Trace Window. The system variables Bit

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [A429/basicsA429] A429GenerateDatabase


# Generate Database

A429 » Basics » Generate Database

A large share of the ARINC labels can be described statically. This means that the interpretation of single bits in the ARINC word is already defined. Label descriptions are generally provided by the aircraft manufacturer or a system supplier. The ICD format (Airbus proprietary) is used as the format here. Additionally the ARINC specification defines numerous ARINC words for different purposes.

Vector provides several tools in the scope of the option .A429 to help the user in creating DBC files.

## Aerospace Message Editor

You may import ICD-, ARINC 825 or XAME files by means of the Aerospace Message Editor. AFDX and ARINC 825/CAN messages, as well as ARINC 429 labels are internally stored in a message library and may be exported to DBC files if required. A number of predefined ARINC labels are already provided in the shipped package. You can create new labels in the library or modify existing ones.You may open the Aerospace Message Editor directly from CANoe under the ribbon tab tools|network tools|AME.

## Vector CANdb++

In principle, you can create and modify ARINC429 DBC files with CANdb++. But for more convenience, we recommend the Aerospace Message Editor.

|  | Cross Reference You can find further information about the discussed tools in the appropriate help. |

Option .A429

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [A429/capl] a429ChannelReconfig


# Reconfigure a Channel

A429 » CAPL Support » Reconfigure a Channel

For channel configuration purposes a specific variable type a429settings is provided. Channel configuration settings may be accessed via the functions a429GetConfiguration, a429SetConfiguration and a429ResetEx.

Note that the configuration settings may overwrite settings in the hardware configuration dialog.

## Selectors

| Keyword | Description | Type |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Bitrate | channel bit rate (Bit/s) Tx: This bit rate has to be used for transmitting ARINC words. Value range: 10500..16000; 90000..110000 Rx: This is the expected bit rate for any traffic on this channel. Value range: 0 (AUTO); 10000..120000 If 0 is provided the bit rate is detected automatically. The detection area is limited by RxMinBitrate and RxMaxBitrate. | dword |
| RxMinBitrate | Lowest allowed bit rate (Bit/s) Tx: no meaning Rx: If the detected bit rate is lower an error is generated; value range: 10000..120000 | dword |
| RxMaxBitrate | Highest allowed bit rate (Bit/s) Tx: no meaning Rx: If the detected bit rate is higher an error is generated; value range: 10000..120000 | dword |
| MinGap | Minimum allowed distance between two consecutive ARINC words (1/8 fractions of a bit time) Tx: The defined gap time precedes every ARINC word Rx: If the time between two consecutive ARINC words falls below that value, an error is generated. Value range: 1..1024 | dword |
| Parity | Parity support Tx: parity bit in ARINC word may be modi

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [A429/capl] a429DefineARINCword


# Declaration of ARINC Words

A429 » CAPL Support » Declaration of ARINC Words

ARINC words may be controlled by means of a CAPL program. An ARINC word object is created with the keyword a429word. ARINC words defined in a database may also be used for initialization.

For more convenience in the programming environment a CAPL include file is provided. The file gives the programmer a number of symbolic constants and some helper routines as a supplement for the CAPL API. Use this statement in the include section of your CAPL code:

#include "A429/A429utils.cin".

|  | Create ARINC word objects a429word 0x82 myA429msg1; // (1) a429word Label_123 myA429msg2; // (2) a429word * myA429msg3; // (3) |

The members of the ARINC word object are accessed via selectors.

|  | Transmit an ARINC word on request a429word * myA429msg4 = {msgChannel = 1, id = 0x82}; // create ARINC wordmyA429msg4.dword(0) = 0x1234 << 10; // shift behind SDIoutput(myA429msg4); // transmit it |

## ARINC word specific event procedures

If there is a valid ARINC word event received, an event procedure is called. Note that ARINC word events may be triggered from a connected hardware channel or even internally from a simulated node or an Interactive Generator.

The event procedures

- on a429Word and

- on a429Worderror

are available.

|  | Note Most of the API functions need an ARINC word as a parameter. Note that in this ARINC word a valid channel needs to be assigned with the selector msgChannel. |

Option .A4

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [A429/capl] a429Scheduling


# Scheduling an ARINC Word

A429 » CAPL Support » Scheduling an ARINC Word

An ARINC word may be transmitted either on request or cyclically (supported by the hardware scheduler. The transmission behavior is controlled via the selectors TxCtrl and TxCycle. The transmission of an ARINC word is initiated with the function output or a429Transmit. If a real hardware is used the ARINC word is transferred to the hardware with this call.

|  | Schedule the cyclic transmission of an ARINC word variables { a429Word LBL_103 myWord = {msgChannel = 1};}on start { myWord.SSM_103 = 3; output (myWord); // start scheduling}on key '+' { myWord.SelAirspeed.phys += 22.5; output(myWord); // update}on key '-' { myWord.SelAirspeed.phys -= 22.5; output(myWord); // update} |

If an ARINC word is initialized based on a message in a database, the cycle time is taken from the database attribute A429Cycle. For every ARINC word you may also modify the minimum distance to the previous sent ARINC word via the selector gap.

|  | Transmit ARINC words on request variables { a429Word LBL_103 myWord = {msgChannel = 2}; msTimer myTimer; // major schedule}on start { a429SetScheduleTx (myWord, 0); // erase cycle time myWord.SSM_103 = 3; setTimer (myTimer, 10); // fire up timer}on timer myTimer { int i; setTimer (myTimer, 10); // rearm timer // send 3 ARINC words with 40, 80 and 120 us gap for (i = 0; i < 3; i++) { a429SetGap (myWord, ((i * 40000) + 40000)); myWord.SelAirspeed.phys += 22.5; output(myWord); // upd

[...truncated for brevity...]

A429 » CAPL Support » ARINC Word Selectors

## ARINC word selectors

| Keyword | Description | Type | Access Limitation |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| time | When an event handler (on a429Word/on a429Worderror) is triggered, the time stamp of the event is stored in the selector. unit: 10 µs | dword | read-only |
| time_ns | When an event handler (on a429Word/on a429Worderror) is triggered, the time stamp of the event is stored in the selector. unit: nanoseconds | int64 | read-only |
| msgChannel | Transmission channel (output) or the channel which triggered an event handler (on a429Word/on a429Worderror) Value range: 1..32 | word | — |
| ID | Label value Value range: 0...255 (for labels with 8Bit length) and 0...1023 (for labels with 10Bit length) Example on a429Word * { if (this.ID == 55) { write("label o%o received; triggering logging...", this.ID); trigger(); }} |  | Example on a429Word * { if (this.ID == 55) { write("label o%o received; triggering logging...", this.ID); trigger(); }} | dword | — |
|  | Example on a429Word * { if (this.ID == 55) { write("label o%o received; triggering logging...", this.ID); trigger(); }} |
| name | name of the ARINC word taken from the database (DBC) | char[] | read-only |
| DIR | This direction is related to the corresponding event. A dedicated ARINC-429 channel may be either an Rx or Tx channel, but not both. Value range: Rx (default) Tx Example on a429Word * { if (this.DIR == Rx) { write("label o%o received", thi

### [A429/windows] a429BusStatisticsWindow


# Bus Statistics Window

A429 Windows » Bus Statistics Window

The Bus Statistics Window displays ARINC-429-specific data and values.

|  | Note All bus statistics values are available as system variables: SysVars::_Statistics::A429_x::<variable> x: ARINC-429 channel number (1, 2, 3 …) <variable>: Value from the Variable column |

The following information is available:

| Statistics Value | Variable | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Direction | Dir | Communication direction Rx for receive channels Tx for transmit channels Tx (Sim.) in Simulated bus mode |
| Max. Bit rate [bit/s] | MaxBitRate | Maximum communication speed of the connected channel Tx: n/a (not applicable) is always displayed. Rx: Maximum value of Bit rate [bit/s] |
| Bit rate [bit/s] | BitRate | Communication speed of the connected channel Tx: The setting of the driver is applied as initial value. Rx: Value for the last received message |
| Min. Bit rate [bit/s] | MinBitRate | Minimum communication speed of the connected channel Tx: n/a (not applicable) is always displayed. Rx: Minimum value of Bit rate [bit/s] |
| Bus load [%] | BusLoad | Current bus load |
| Peak load [%] | PeakLoad | Maximum value of Bus load [%] |
| Words [fr] | TotalWords | Number of ARINC 429 words that were processed error-free |
| Word rate [fr/s] | WordRate | Number of ARINC 429 words per second that were processed error-free This error detection is also dependent on the set (channel) parameters. |
| Word errors [fr] | WordErrors

### [A429/windows/filter] filterA429Columns


# Columns

A429 Filter » Columns

You can show and hide the following columns in the A429 Filter.

| Column | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Active | activates or deactivates a filter |
| Name | For ARINC-429 words that are present in the network description/database, the name of the ARINC-429 word from the database is displayed. The name is not editable. |
| ID | message identifier The placeholder * allows all ARINC-429 words for, e.g., one channel, to pass. |
| Label | ID value in octal |
| Channel | number of the channel The possible selections depend on the number of configured channels. Multiple selected channels can be changed at the same time using the Channel context menu. The value Any filters the ID for all channels. |
| Label Error | Filters according to the status of the received label The possible selections are: Only: label errors only Included: correct and incorrect labels Excluded (default value): correct labels only |
| Direction | direction filter viewed from CANoe (adapter) The possible selections are: Rx: received labels Tx: transmitted labels Rx/Tx: all directions (default value) |
| Database | name of the database |

Option .A429

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [A429/windows/filter] filterA429Toolbar


# Toolbar

A429 Filter » Toolbar

From the toolbar, you can configure general settings for the A429 Filter.

| Setting | Description |
| --- | --- |
| [Activate/Deactivate] | This button is used to activate or deactivate the A429 Filter. In the active state, the filter conditions are evaluated, and only those ARINC-429 words satisfying the filter conditions are passed. When the filter is deactivated, all ARINC-429 words are passed. |
| Type | From the selection list, you can select the operating mode of the A429 Filter. In the Pass Filter operating mode, only those ARINC-429 words are passed that satisfy the configured filter conditions. In the Stop Filter operating mode, only those ARINC-429 words are passed that do not satisfy the configured filter conditions. |
| View | Show Overview: Here you select whether or not the configuration overview is to be displayed. Grouping: Channel: The A429 Filters in the list are grouped and sorted by channel. Columns…: Opens the configuration dialog for configuring the displayed columns. Columns that cannot be deselected are inactive. |
| Edit | Here you can add new filter conditions as well as deactivate or delete existing filter conditions. Note Filter conditions which you add during a running measurement are not configured and would route all ARINC-429 words. Therefore these filter conditions are deactivated at first. |  | Note Filter conditions which you add during a running measurement are not configured and would route all ARINC-429

### [A429/windows/hwConfig] hwConfigA429PageA429


# Network Hardware Configuration - ARINC429

Network Hardware » ARINC429

AFDX » Network Hardware Configuration - ARINC429

Ribbon: Hardware Tab|Network Hardware|ARINC429

Configuration options are available for every defined channel. Not connected channels are advertised with . A is used if the channel is not yet enabled to be used by CANoe.

With the context menu the assigned communication direction is changed between Rx and Tx. A measurement may be started only if the number of available hardware channels is sufficient. In the mode Simulated bus there is no such restriction. The assignment of connected hardware to a specific application channel may be modified with the Application Channel Mapping dialog.

## VN0601 (For a connection to ARINC 429 the ARINC interface VN0601 is necessary.)

The VN0601 is clocked internally at 64 MHz. All internal time measurements are based on this clock, and the bit rate is also derived from it. The minimum time resolution is 15.625 ns. The bit rate is an integer ratio of the clock rate. This produces a “grid” that is taken into account in the dialog for all time-related specifications.

## Tx direction

If Presetting is activated, it is possible to change between the High-Speed and Low-Speed parameter sets.

|  | Default Values for High-Speed | Default Values for Low-Speed |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Bit rate | 100000 bit/s | 12500 bit/s |
| Parity | Odd | Odd |
| Word gap | 4.5 bit times | 4.5 bit times |

If Presetting is deactivated, the pa

### [A429/windows/ig] A429IGConfig


# Configuration

A429 Windows » A429 Interactive Generator » Configuration

The A429 IG configuration dialog is divided in the following areas:

## Toolbar

The toolbar contains the functions:

- add ARINC 429 words to Send List

- removing ARINC 429 words from Send List

- undo/redo user steps

- column configuration of Send List view and signals view

## Send List View

Displays the currently available ARINC 429 words and their properties. The properties can also be configured within this view.

## ARINC-429-Words Property View

Displays the properties of the selected ARINC 429 words from the Send List view. The properties are editable and can be configured within this view.

## Signals View

The Signals view displays the signals of the selected ARINC 429 word from the Send List view. If the selected word is no ARINC 4 29 word from an assigned database, the Signals view is empty and deactivated. (See also Signal Generator).

## Raw Data View

To configure the data of the ARINC 429 word by bytes the Raw Data view can be used. The Raw Data view is available for both database and raw ARINC 429 words.

Option .A429

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [A429/windows/ig] A429IGConfigPropertyView


# A429 Word Properties

A429 Windows » A429 Interactive Generator » A429 Word Properties

This view displays all properties of the currently selected ARINC 429 word in the view Send List. Reduced, the view tab is located on the right of the view Send List. Click […] on the right of the column Send Configuration or by clicking the view tab.

Additionally to the data in the columns of the Send List view, the view A429 Word Properties shows the set gap of the selected word in microseconds as well as, if defined, the min./max. cycle time of the ARINC 429 word defined in the database.

### [A429/windows/ig] A429IGConfigRawDataView


# Raw Data

A429 Windows » A429 Interactive Generator » Raw Data

The view raw data allows editing single bytes of the ARINC 429 words selected in the view Send List. It is possible to switch between the view Raw Data and the view Signals if the ARINC 429 word is defined in the database.

The label value of the ARINC word (first byte of the word) can only be modified in the view Send List. The remaining 3 bytes of the ARINC 429 word contain the optional bit fields SDI and SSM, signal data bits and the parity bit.

In the view raw data, two modes for data input are supported.

## Bitwise Parameterization of the bit fields SSM, SDI and signal data bits via corresponding buttons:

| Button | Value type/Option | Value | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Byte 3 | Byte | 0..FF | Contains the parity bit and/or the SSM (optionally) |
| Byte 1 | Byte | 0..FF | Contains the SDI (optionally) |
| Parity | Bit | 0..1 | The parity bit can only be entered manually or calculated on demand if the parity calculation of the ARINC 429 word is set on disabled. In all other cases, the parity bit is displayed as not editable and is calculated automatically. |
| SSM Type | SSM not used | - | SSM is not used , 2 bits are added to the bit field payload |
|  | SSM (BCD) | 0..3 | 0 = Plus, North, East, Right, To, Above 1 = No Computed Data 2 = Functional Test 3 = Minus, South, West, Left, From, Below |
|  | SSM (BNR) | 0..3 | 0 = Failure Warning 1 = No Computed Data 2 = Functional Test 3 = Norm

[...truncated for brevity...]

|  |  | — | If the trigger condition is periodic and currently active, this button appears. Clicking it stops the periodic transmission of the ARINC 429 word. |
| Trigger | Manual / On Key | Yes | The ARINC 429 word is send on bus when the button is pressed. Additionally a trigger key can be defined for the frame. If the key is pressed the ARINC 429 word is send on bus. Manual and OnKey trigger conditions can be used together. |
|  | Periodic | No | The ARINC 429 word is send on bus, with the 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [A429/windows/ig] A429IGold


# Interactive Generator (A429)

IG » Configuration » A429

With the Interactive Generator (IG), valid ARINC-429 words can be transmitted cyclically and event-based. For an existing database the payload can be processed byte-oriented as well as signal based. For already transmitted or received ARINC-429 words, it is possible to import the properties of these messages from the Trace Window to the IG.

The properties of ARINC-429 words can be displayed and changed via the A429 page of the Interactive Generator and the associated data columns.

Several specifics exist for ARINC-429 words that are explained hereafter.

## Copying and Pasting ARINC-429 messages from the Trace Window

Previously transmitted or received ARINC-429 messages can be copied using the Copy to clipboard context menu of the Trace Window and imported into the Interactive Generator using the Paste | Paste A429 Word list entry.

The following assumptions and special treatments are applied automatically when messages are pasted:

- For received (Rx) ARINC-429 messages, the first Tx channel is used.

- If the same database is assigned to multiple channels, the message with the first applicable transmit channel is pasted.

- If more than one ARINC-429 word is present on the clipboard, duplicates are not imported. An ARINC-429 word is recognized as a duplicate if the following properties match: network name, message name, DBC name, label, and payload.

## Signals

If the signal sequences in the signal list of the 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [ADAS/Basics] ADASConcept


# Concept

ADAS » Concept

| Overview |
| --- |
| ADAS for supports the basic concepts of CANoeCANoe and extends them. For the stimulation of ADAS algorithms, different ADAS objects are simulated between simulation environments, sensors or development environments based on the communication concept. ADAS specific windows are available for analysis. The use of Test Windows enables the complete test of the ADAS algorithm. |

The information contained, such as the distance to the object, the dimensions, and the speed can be displayed in detail in a Scene Window. |
| Simulation and Stimulation |
| With the ADAS Feature Set, ADAS scenarios can be simulated and ADAS algorithms to be tested can be stimulated. First simple scenarios can be created with the CANoe Scenario Editor. With these scenarios, an ADAS algorithm can be subjected to basic tests. Various interfaces enable the connection of complete simulation environments. The complex, dynamic Closed-Loop scenarios created with this can fully test an ADAS algorithm. |
| Test |
| If an ADAS algorithm created in a development environment (e.g. Microsoft Visual Studio) is to be tested, data must be transferred from CANoe to the development environment. This is made possible by the Vector Plugin SAB. If the ADAS objects are exchanged with SAB between

### [ADAS/Basics] ADASDataModel


# Data Model

ADAS » Data Model

The ADAS data model consists of Detected Objects and sensors that have detected these objects. The sensors and objects are based on the communication concept for CANoe. Put simply, this concept uses network-independent communication objects that are available globally in CANoe and its measurement and analysis windows. These communication objects can also be influenced by programming.

In the ADAS Feature Set, the underlying data structures of the communication objects correspond to the ASAM OSI3 standard. This defines an object-based description of the environment in order to enable compatibility between automated driving functions and the large number of available driving simulation frameworks.

|  | Cross Reference Detected Objects and Sensors CANoe Communication Objects for ADAS Programming ASAM OSI3 |

ADAS

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [ADAS/Basics] ADASDetectedObjectsSensors


# Detected Objects and Sensors

ADAS » Data Model » Detected Objects and Sensors

## Detected Objects

The Detected Objects represent the objects detected by a sensor. The associated data is encapsulated in the OSI standard in the SensorData interface. The OSI standard defines the following classes for this purpose, which have also been adopted in the ADAS data model:

| Class | Short Description |
| --- | --- |
| DetectedStationaryObject | A stationary object |
| DetectedMovingObject | A moving object |
| DetectedTrafficSign | A traffic sign |
| DetectedTrafficLight | A traffic light |
| DetectedRoadMarking | A road marking |
| DetectedLaneBoundary | A lane boundary |
| DetectedLane | One lane |
| DetectedOccupant | A vehicle passenger |

## Environment Objects

In addition to Detected Objects, the ADAS data model also contains Environment Objects. These are encapsulated in the OSI standard in the GroundTruth interface and thus represent objects that are described in terms of the global coordinate system. Analogous to the Detected Objects, there are also corresponding classes in OSI/vCDL for the Environment Objects.

## Sensors

The information about a sensor is encapsulated in the SensorViewConfiguration interface. This is another data structure that can be processed by CANoe as a top-level message. In addition to information such as sensor ID, this also contains other information such as the mounting position of the sensor on the vehicle or its range.

ADAS

| Version 15©

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [ADAS/Basics] ADASStandardSAMOSI3


# ASAM OSI3 Standard

ADAS » Data Model » ASAM OSI3 Standard

## Open Simulation Interface (OSI)

The ADAS data model is based on the ASAM OSI standard. This was originally developed in an open Github project by several OEMs and Tier 1 suppliers to describe generic interfaces for sensor models and driving simulations. In order to ensure the neutral further development of the standard, the rights to OSI were transferred to ASAM.

## Structure

In general, a distinction can be made between the two top-level messages GroundTruth and SensorData, each of which contains information about the simulated objects. However, CANoe also supports SensorViewConfiguration as another top-level message:

- GroundTruth: Describes the simulated objects with respect to the global coordinate system.

- SensorData: Describes the simulated objects with respect to the coordinate system of a specific sensor.

- SensorViewConfiguration: Contains information about a specific sensor such as sensor ID, mounting position or range.

## Google Protocol Buffers

The interfaces described by the OSI standard are provided in Profobuf message format on this website. CANoe also works internally with the Protobuf library provided by Google to process the received Protobuf byte streams.

## Versioning

Support for the SensorData interface was not introduced until version 3.0.0 (OSI3), so CANoe only supports data structures from this version onward. CANoe currently uses version 3.2.0 of the interfaces.

## ADAS data

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [ADAS/Basics] ADASUseCases


# ADAS Use Cases

ADAS » ADAS Use Cases

ADAS for CANoe can support the following use cases, among others:

- Stimulation of ADAS algorithms with simple scenarios

- Stimulation of ADAS algorithms with complex scenarios

- Test of ADAS algorithms

- Analysis of sensor data via network communication

- Stimulation of ADAS algorithms via network communication

- Analysis of log files

- Analysis of scenarios

## Stimulation of ADAS algorithms with simple scenarios

For a first test of an ADAS algorithm it is often sufficient to stimulate it with a simple scenario. The Scenario Editor offers the possibility to create exactly such simple scenarios. An ego vehicle and other vehicles can be placed on a self-created route. Freely configurable sensors can be added to the ego vehicle. After importing the created scenario with the Scenario Manager, all vehicles that detect these sensors are made available as Detected Objects in CANoe. They can thus be analyzed in the Scene Window, for example, or passed on to the ADAS algorithm under test using CANoe SiL Adapter Builder. It should be noted that scenarios created in this way do not offer any track accuracy, no models are stored for the driving physics, and no closed-loop tests are possible. On the other hand, they can be created very quickly and easily.

## Stimulation of ADAS algorithms with complex scenarios

For a complete software-based test of an ADAS algorithm, complex scenarios are required that, among other things, provide lane

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [ADAS/Basics/Programming] ADASProgADASdoAPI


# ADAS DO API

ADAS » Programming » ADAS DO API

The programmatic access to the ADAS data model is done via the DO interfaces ISensor and IScenarioManager. These interfaces are available in CAPL and C# when the ADAS Binding is activated under Ribbon|Simulation|ADAS Binding.

The interfaces and their usage are described under Sensor API and Scenario Manager API. For the use of sensors (like a radar sensor) create an object of this interface in the Model Editor and assign the SensorId as attribute.

No object needs to be created to use ScenarioManager to control scenarios, it is created automatically after the ADAS Binding is activated under the name ScenarioManager.

ADAS

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [ADAS/Basics/Programming] ADASProgADASSensorAPI


# ADAS Sensor API

ADAS » Programming » ADAS Sensor API

The API access to sensors is done via the DO interface ISensor. From an ISensor interface a DO object can be created in the model editor and assigned with the SensorId (attribute).

The methods are described below. In the examples it is assumed in each case that a DO object named mySensor of the type ISensor was created.

## General API for Sensors

## GetDetectedObjects

This function returns the detected objects of a sensor as a string array.

|  | Example ADAS.DetectedObject_List detectedObjectNames = mySensor.GetDetectedObjects.Call(); |

## GetDetectedObjectsCondtional

This function returns the detected objects of a sensor as a string array. Only those objects are considered that meet the condition passed in the parameters. Possible conditions are a distance and a relative speed to the sensor above or below a certain value.

|  | Example /// <param name="criterion">Type of the condition</param>/// <param name="value">Limit value of the condition</param/// <param name="triggerCondition">Indicates whether the value must be above or below the limit value</paramADAS.DetectedObject_List detectedObjectNames = mySensor.GetDetectedObjectsConditional.Call_Phys(_ADAS.ECriterion.Distance, 30, _ADAS.ETriggerCondition.Lesser); |

## GetOrCreateDetectedObject

This function returns the name of a detected object of a sensor as a string. If no object with the given trackingId exists, it will be created.

|  | Example /// <param 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [ADAS/Interfaces] ADASCAPIProtobuf


# C-API / Protobuf

ADAS » C-API / Protobuf

## Brief description

The programming examples demonstrate the use of the C-API. In the ADAS project, the cclSetGpbOSI3Data2 function is used to import protobuf bytestreams into CANoe. This allows third-party simulation environments to be connected to CANoe by transferring their own protobuf bytestreams with the respective environment data to CANoe. It should be noted that the transmitted Protobuf structures must comply with the OSI standard so that they can be correctly interpreted and processed by CANoe.

## Procedure

In the ADAS project of CANoeCCL.sln, an ADAS.dll is first compiled, which is later integrated into the sample configuration. The integration of the DLL is done in the Options dialog|Programming|C Libraries. This way the behavior programmed in the DLL is started at measurement start.

In the example implementation first a file GPBData.data is read in, which contains an exemplary protobuf bytestream. This encapsulates data in the SensorData as a top-level message. The type of the top-level message must also be passed as a parameter to cclSetGpbOSI3Data2 so that it can be interpreted correctly by CANoe. The following dataTypes apply to the three supported top-level messages:

- SensorData: 0

- GroundTruth: 1

- SensorViewConfiguration: 2

The selected bytestream is then sent periodically to CANoe. Here the data of the byte stream is extracted and written to the corresponding communication objects, which in turn are 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [ADAS/Interfaces] ADASMatlabInterfaceSFunction


# Matlab Interface / S-Function

ADAS » Matlab Interface / S-Function

## Matlab Interface

For the connection of the ADAS feature set to DYNA4 and other simulation environments using MATLAB, the S-Function protobufOSI is used. This is part of the MATLAB Integration Package. It can be used to read serialized OSI3 sensor data with Google Protocol Buffer into CANoe.

To use the function, the MATLAB Integration Package must first be installed. Instructions on how to do this can be found here.

A description of the S-Function and its parameters can be found here.

ADAS

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [ADAS/Interfaces] ADASVehicleNetworks


# Vehicle Network

ADAS » Vehicle Network

Vehicles communicate internally via network messages (e.g. via CAN, Ethernet, ...). The ADAS Feature Set works on abstract data types in ASAM OSI format.

To be able to analyze network data with the ADAS Feature Set, the network data must be mapped to the abstract data types of the ADAS Feature Set using the ADAS Sensor API.

The ADAS data model is object-based, which means that each ADAS configuration contains at least one sensor that can manage detected objects. The sensors have to be created before the measurement start.

At runtime, network messages can be reacted to via corresponding CAPL functions. Detected objects, the so-called DetectedObjects, can be created via the ADAS Sensor API.

In the following example, messages come from a radar. The object is detected via an ID that is present in each message. The information is spread over two messages.

- In the first message, the DetectedObject is created and then set to incomplete, the sensor to updatend status.

- In the second message, the object is identified by its TrackingId and fetched. Finally, the object is set to complete and an update is triggered on the sensor.

|  | Example In the first message, the sensor is set to Begin Update status, then all previously detected objects are removed from the sensor. In the second message, the object is identified via its TrackingId and created. After all information is set, the detected object is set to complete and an update is tr

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [ADAS/Procedures/ADASProcAnalysis] ADASAnalyzeLogfiles


# Analyze Logging Files

ADAS » Procedures » Analyze Logging Files

You can create logging files for ADAS communication objects and replay them later in offline mode to analyze the ADAS objects.

## Create Logging Files

To create a logging file, activate the Logging Block in the Measurement Setup. If there is no Logging Block yet, you can insert a new Logging Block via the ribbon. Afterwards you can determine e.g. the name and the target directory of the logging file via [Logging file configuration...]. Select here BLF as file format. After the configuration you can start the measurement. In the background, the ADAS objects are recorded via the Logging Block and saved in the specified logging file.

## Replay Logging Files

To do this, switch to offline mode. You can then specify the logging files to be replayed in the Measurement Setup under [Offline file list]. Add the previously recorded logging file here. You can then start the measurement and observe (e.g. in the Trace Window) that the logged ADAS objects are now being replayed.

ADAS

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [ADAS/Procedures/ADASProcAnalysis] ADASAnalyzeScenarios


# Analyze Scenarios

ADAS » Procedures » Analyze Scenarios

The scenarios must first be imported. This can be done either via DYNA4 or via the Scenario Editor. These can then be analyzed in the individual analysis windows. The options in the individual windows are listed below.

## Trace Window

All Detected Objects, Sensors and Ground Truth Objects are Distributed Objects and are therefore displayed in the Trace Window. The individual objects can be expanded so that all fields of the objects can be displayed.

In the Trace window there is an ADAS column set to display additional relevant information in the columns. For sensors, the Sensor Id and the number of Detected Objects detected by the sensor are displayed. For all ADAS objects, the object classification and object type are displayed.

Figure 1: Trace window with Detected Object expanded

## Scene Window

In the Scene Window, the ADAS objects are visualized relative to the reference point of the sensors. Here you can select the settings for the colors of the individual objects and the display mode.

Figure 2: Scene window with a sensor detecting three objects.

## Map window

If objects are simulated using the Scenario Manager or their geographical position (longitude and latitude) is known from other sources, the objects can be drawn into the Map Window using the MapWindow API. This is available in .NET and CAPL.

- Map Window API (CAPL)

- Map Window API (.NET)

## Graphics Window and Data Window

Individual values 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [ADAS/Procedures/ADASProcConfig] ADASCreateConfig


# Create a Configuration

ADAS » Procedures » Create a Configuration

## Create configuration and analyze messages

To create an ADAS configuration and then analyze it, perform the following steps.

|  | Step by Step Procedure Start CANoe. Create a new configuration using the template under File|Sample Configurations|ADAS. Customize and create sensors.Within the vCDL, a sensor is already preconfigured with SensorId 1. You can edit this in the System and Configuration Setup using the Model Editor. See also the section Working with the Model Editor further down this page. Create a scenario, either via the Scenario Editor, DYNA4 or via third-party simulation environments. Optional: Add a new application model in the system and communication setup. Here you can react to changes to the sensor via callbacks and evaluate its object list programmatically. Start the measurement with <F9> and observe the trace window. |

|  | Note In the ADAS Feature Set the underlying data structures of the communication objects correspond to the ASAM OSI3 standard. Object lists are transferred according to the SensorData scheme, therefore all ADAS configurations require a sensor with set and matching SensorId. |

## Working with the Model Editor

|  | Step by Step Procedure Navigate to Distributed Objects|Objects. To change the SensorId of the existing sensor, enter the desired SensorId instead of 1 in the SebsorId field in the object properties. To change the name of the sensor, enter the desired n

- Demonstration of all features with a complex simulation environment (ADAS System DYNA4).

- Demonstration of all features with a simple simulation environment (ADAS System Scenario Editor).

- Template configuration for new configurations (Template Configuration).

- Analysis of frames with sensor data (Sensor Analysis Configuration).

### [ADAS/Procedures/ADASProcSimStim] ADASMapCommunicationOnDOs


# Mapping of Network Communication on DOs

ADAS » Procedures » Mapping of Network Communication on DOs

Mapping data to the ADAS data model that is received via a bus system in CANoe - such as signals and messages from a CAN database - is possible programmatically via CAPL or C# scripts.

To do this, create a C# measurement script, for example, and add it to the system and communication setup as an application model. In the C# script, event handlers can be implemented for signals from databases, for example, and their values can be written to DO objects of the ADAS data model:

|  | Example [OnCANFrame] public void OnCANFrame(NetworkDB.Frames.MyCANFrame canMsg) { Radar1.sensor_info.sensor_view_configuration.range.PhysValue = canMsg.range; // … } |

A sample configuration for mapping CAN messages to ADAS objects can be found here.

ADAS

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [ADAS/Procedures/ADASProcSimStim] ADASSimScenariosDYNA4


# Simulate Scenarios with DYNA4

ADAS » Procedures » Simulate Scenarios with DYNA4

## Create a DYNA4 scenario

To create a DYNA4 scenario, you must have a DYNA4 Studio license. In DYNA4 Studio you can create any scenarios you like and then later integrate them into CANoe. On this web page you will find a Quickstart Tutorial for DYNA4.

## Integrating a DYNA4 scenario in CANoe

|  | DYNA4 Studio offers the possibility to provide the scenario in a compiled nodelayer DLL. The nodelayer DLL can be included as an application model in the system and communication setup. In this case, only a DYNA4 run license is required for execution. A detailed procedure can be found in the video Tutorial with CANoe as Master on this webseite. |

## Closed - Loop: Influencing a DYNA4 scenario from CANoe

|  | Loading the Nodelayer DLL automatically creates some DYNA4-specific system-defined system variables in CANoe, including the following: DYNA4ExternalFunctionInput::Active DYNA4ExternalFunctionInput::AccPedalPos DYNA4ExternalFunctionInput::BrakePedalPos |

If DYNA4ExternalFunctionInput::Active is set to 1, the first person vehicle of the scenario can then be accelerated (DYNA4ExternalFunctionInput::AccPedalPos) or braked (DYNA4ExternalFunctionInput::BrakePedalPos) using these two other variables.

This can be done in any CAPL /C# network node, test node or application model.

|  | C# Example Code: // Callback on update of the ADAS algorithm[OnUpdate(ADASAlgorithm.MemberIDs.isWarningActive)]pu

[...truncated for brevity...]

ADAS » Procedures » Simulate ADAS Scenarios with the Scenario Editor

Also explained is how to react in C# to messages sent via the Scenario Editor.

The procedure is described using the following scenario:

|  | Example Two vehicles drive behind each other. The first vehicle (Vehicle_2) makes an emergency stop after a certain time, to which the following vehicle (DuT) should react. The DuT detects the vehicles ahead by a sensor, which is attached to the front part of the vehicle. The detected vehicles are managed and sent as DetectedObjects in a list at the sensor. |

### [ADAS/Procedures/ADASProcTest] ADASTFSAPI


# Use Test Feature Set API

ADAS » Procedures » Use Test Feature Set API

The Test Feature Set API helps you to test your ADAS function. For this purpose, possibilities are provided to wait for events to occur. The exact procedure differs for CAPL and C# test code. The ADAS System sample configuration demonstrates how to use the API.

## CAPL Procedure

In CAPL, there are dedicated wait functions for the distance and speed of Detected Objects. An overview of all ADAS TFS CAPL functions can be found here.

Basically, the system first waits until a defined event occurs, e.g. a Detected Object approaches the reference point to less than 30m. If required for your test case, the function TestGetWaitADASDetectedObject can be used to access the name of the Detected Object and the function lookupDistObj can be used to return a reference to the Detected Object.

The reference can now be used to access individual fields of the Detected Object. If a Detected Object was successfully waited for, the return of your ADAS function can be checked via CANoe SAB and the test case result can be set.

|  | Example enum VWaitThreshold{ kUnderThreshold = 0, kOverThreshold = 1};distObjRef ::ADAS::IDetectedMovingObject detMovingObj;char buffer[100];if(TestWaitForADASDistance(kUnderThreshold, 30, 1000)) //Wait for 1s for Detected Object below 30m distance{ if(TestGetWaitADASDetectedObject(buffer) == 0) //Get the name of the Detected Object that triggered the Wait Condition { detMovingObj = (distObjRe

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [ADAS/SimEnvironments] ADASPathfinderSimEnv


# Pathfinder for Simulation Environments

ADAS » Pathfinder for Simulation Environments

## Scenario Editor

The Scenario Editor is a simulation environment that can be used to create simple scenarios very quickly. These scenarios are often sufficient for an initial test of an ADAS algorithm. An ego vehicle and other vehicles can be placed on a user-defined route. Freely configurable sensors can be added to the ego vehicle. After importing the created scenario with the Scenario Manager, all vehicles that detect these sensors are made available as Detected Objects in CANoe. They can thus be analyzed in the Scene Window, for example, or passed on to the ADAS algorithm under test using the CANoe SiL Adapter Builder. Note that scenarios created in this way do not offer any track accuracy, no models are stored for the driving physics, and no closed-loop tests are possible. On the other hand they are very quickly and easily created.

## DYNA4

For a complete software-based test of an ADAS algorithm, complex scenarios are required that, among other things, offer lane accuracy, have models for the driving physics stored and are closed-loop capable. Such scenarios can be created using the DYNA4 simulation environment. DYNA4 already supplies the sensor data and object data in ASAM OSI format. A direct connection to CANoe can be made via the Matlab interface. CANoe interprets this data as Detected Objects within the framework of its data model. These can be analyzed in the Scene Window

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [ADAS/SimEnvironments] ADASSimEnv3rdParty


# Third Party Simulation Environments

ADAS » Third Party Simulation Environments

Third-party simulation environments can also be connected to CANoe. This is made possible via documented interfaces such as an S-Function in the Matlab interface or a C-API interface. The prerequisite is that the simulation environments supply data in ASAM OSI format. If this is the case, CANoe interprets this data as Detected Objects within the framework of its data model. They can then be analyzed in the Scene Window, for example, or passed on to the ADAS algorithm under test using CANoe SiL Adapter Builder.

In a sample configuration, the mechanisms and procedures are demonstrated using a practical example.

## Schematic Setup

ADAS

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [ADAS/SimEnvironments] ADASSimEnvDYNA4


# DYNA4

ADAS » DYNA4

DYNA4 is an open simulation environment for virtual driving tests with passenger cars and utility vehicles. The physical models include vehicle dynamics, powertrain, combustion and electric engines as well as sensors and traffic. Virtual test drives with DYNA4 enable efficient development of ECU functions. Closed-loop-simulations with DYNA4 run on the computer in multiple real time, e.g. for use in early development phases (MIL, SIL), and can be run on HIL systems as soon as ECUs are available in hardware. The 3D environment simulation in DYNA4 with road infrastructure and traffic provides a virtual test environment for assisted and automated driving, where environment sensing plays a central role.

The models for sensors and traffic provide data based on the ASAM OSI standard. CANoe interprets this data as Detected Objects within the framework of its data model. These can be analyzed in the Scene Window, for example, or passed on to the ADAS algorithm under test using CANoe SiL Adapter Builder. The exact procedure can be found here. In a sample configuration, the mechanisms and procedures are demonstrated using a practical example.

## Schematic Setup

ADAS

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |

### [ADAS/Windows/ScenarioEditor] ADASScenarioEditorFunctionality


# Functionality

ADAS » Scenario Editor » Functionality

Ribbon|Ribbon Tab Tools|Group Network Tools|Scenario Editor

## Use in CANoe

Scenarios created with the Scenario Editor can be loaded into CANoe via the Scenario Manager. You can open the Scenario Manager via Ribbon|Ribbon Tab Environment|Scenario Manager.

In the window of the Scenario Manager, the stations of the scenario are listed. Stations of the same name as a node of a loaded database are identified as the node. These stations are displayed with a yellow icon to the left and the preceding database name. Stations that have the same name as a GeoObject of a loaded ADAS database are displayed with a blue icon on the left side.

You can open the Scenario Editor via the button [Scenario Editor].

If a scenario was loaded via the Scenario Manager in the respective CANoe configuration, it is used when opening the scenario editor.

The following requirements must be met to be able to use the values of the attributes of the stations specified in the scenario file as well as the scenario callback functions in CAPL.

### [ADAS/Windows/Scene] ADASSceneWindowDisplay


# Display

Sample Configurations » ADAS » Scene Window » Display

## Simple Mode and Complex Mode

In the toolbar, two buttons can be used to switch between Simple Mode and Complex Mode.

In Simple Mode, objects are only drawn in two dimensions according to their length and width. The Complex Mode contains a three-dimensional representation of the detected objects.

Figure 1:Simple Mode (left) and Complex Mode (right) in the same scene

## Display Settings

The options Show Inactive Objects and Show Ground Truth Data can be set via the Display Settings in the toolbar of the scene window.

If the Show Inactive Objects option is enabled, the Detected Object view will also show objects that were detected in the past and are no longer detected.

If the option Show Ground Truth Data is activated, ground truth data will be displayed in addition to the detected objects. Ground truth data is modeled as environment objects within the ADAS feature. An overview of the individual object types in the ADAS data model can be found on the page Detected Sensors and Objects.

## Color Settings

The Color Settings for Objects toolbar of the Scene Window opens a configuration dialog.

In this, a color mode for Detected Objects can be selected. Either Sensor Based or Type Based can be selected. With the Sensor Based option, the Detected Objects have the same color as the sensors that detect them. With the Type Based option, a color can be selected for each object type. The setting of the colors 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [ADAS/Windows/Scene] ADASSceneWindowFunctionality


# Functionality

ADAS » Scene Window » Functionality

## General

After measurement start, ADAS objects (Sensors, Detected Objects and Environment Objects) are automatically drawn into the Scene Window according to their position and size.

For sensors, the following fields are used by the Scene Window:

| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| sensor_view_configuration.sensor_Id | SensorID must be unequal 0. Otherwise the sensor will not appear in the Scene Window. |
| sensor_view_configuration.field_of_view_horizontal | Opening angle of the sensor. |
| sensor_view_configuration.range | Range of the sensor. |
| sensor_data.mounting_position.position | Installation position of the sensor relative to the reference point. |
| sensor_data.mounting_position.orientaion.yaw | Rotation angle of the sensor. |

For Detected and Environment Objects the following fields are used by the Scene Window:

| Field | Description |
| --- | --- |
| baseInfo.dimension | Size of the object. For the Simple Mode, width and length are sufficient. For the Complex Mode, the height must also be set. |
| baseInfo.position | Position of the object. |
| baseInfo.orientation | Orientation of the object. |

## Highlighting objects

If you highlight entries in the Sensor, Detected Object or Ground Truth view, then the associated objects are highlighted in the Scene View.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |

### [ADAS/Windows/Trace] ADASTraceData


# ADAS Data in the Trace Window

ADAS » ADAS Data in the Trace Window

The data of the ADAS objects (sensors and detected objects) are displayed in CANoe in the Trace Window. For ADAS, a column layout called ADAS Layout is available that contains special columns for ADAS objects.

Here is an example of the display of a radar sensor (Radar1) that detects 3 moving objects (Detected Moving Objects):

The Detected Objects are dyamic DO objects, i.e. they are created and appear in the Trace window as soon as they are detected by the sensor. The Sensor object has a list (detected_objects) with the names of the objects that were detected at the current time. These names are references to the dynamic Detected Objects, which are displayed per line.

Furthermore, the number of detected objects is displayed in the column Detected Objects, as well as the Sensor Id, the type of the object (Object Type) and the classification (Object Classification).

To filter certain contents, the analysis filters of the Trace window can be used as usual by dragging and dropping the lines to be filtered into the Stop Filter area, for example:

In addition, the display can be configured using predefined filters.

The Value property can be used to define whether values of DOs are displayed. The interfaces of DOs can also contain methods, the calls are then also visible in the trace window, but can be switched invisible via the Method property of the predefined filters.

When dynamic DOs are created, a Dyn

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [AFDX] AFDX


# Option AFDX

Standards – Bus Systems, Protocols, Databases » Option AFDX (ARINC-664)

|  | AFDX (ARINC-664) is an Ethernet-based protocol (full duplex, 100 MBit/s). To assure deterministic behavior, there are specific extensions such as limited frame sizes, sequence numbers and requirements related to time behavior. For redundancy purposes, two Ethernet channels (Line-A and Line-B) are also normally bundled into one AFDX channel to increase fault tolerance. Application Area: Networking in avionics industry. |

| Quick Access New Features CAPL Reference Sample Configurations Feature Matrix AFDX Working Areas Analysis: Concept / Function overview Stimulation: Concept / Function overview General Configuration: AFDX AFDX Windows Protocol overview CANoe.AFDX Quick Start Guide | Quick Access | New Features CAPL Reference Sample Configurations Feature Matrix | AFDX Working Areas | Analysis: Concept / Function overview Stimulation: Concept / Function overview | General | Configuration: AFDX AFDX Windows Protocol overview CANoe.AFDX Quick Start Guide | Basics Definitions Functional Data Set/ FDS (AFDX) Operating Modes for AFDX Protocol Checks (AFDX) Scheduling Concepts (AFDX) Light AFDX Database Support (AFDX) Network Hardware Configuration CAPL Support A664 Messages versus A664 Frames (AFDX) Declaration of AFDX Messages Declaration of AFDX Frames AFDX Selectors | Basics | Definitions Functional Data Set/ FDS (AFDX) Operating Modes for AFDX Protocol Checks (AFDX) Scheduling Concept

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [AFDX] AFDXconcept


# AFDX for CANoe | Concept

AFDX » AFDX for CANoe » Concept

| Overview |
| --- |
| CANoe.AFDX supports the basic concepts of CANoe and extends them with functions and mechanisms for analyzing and stimulating in the field of Avionics Full Duplex Switched Ethernet networks (ARINC 664 Part 7-1). |
| Analysis |
| Option AFDX supports and extends the basic analysis of CANoe. Existing analysis windows are extended to interpret and analyze AFDX frames. Protocol information such as header and payload can be displayed in detail. |
| Stimulation |
| Option AFDX supports the basic stimulation of CANoe and extends the stimulation of AFDX networks by sending AFDX frames. |

### [AFDX/afdxBasics] afdxDefinitions


# Definitions: Messages, Packets and Frames

AFDX » Definitions: Messages, Packets and Frames

The communication between networking devices may be easily described with the OSI Reference Model.

On application level information between the devices is exchanged by means of messages. E.g. you specify that a networking device "A" sends a message containing a temperature value to a networking device "B". Or you specify in a more general way that "A" sends data to "B". Depending on the OSI layer you are referring to the exchanged units of information are called differently.

|  | Definition: Message In this document, a message is the unit of transmission at TCP or UDP level. A message consists of a transport protocol header followed by application data. To be transmitted end-to-end through the AFDX, a message must be encapsulated inside an IP datagram. |

In every layer of the OSI model the communication between networking devices is done via protocols. Logically a PDU (Protocol Data Unit) is exchanged between the communication partners. Since the connection between the networking devices is established by the physical layer a PDU has to be passed through every layer below the originating one. In every layer the PDU is called by different names. For Ethernet the naming according to the following figure is established. In the link layer the PDU is named "frame" and in the network layer it is named "packet”.

Option .AFDX

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [AFDX/afdxBasics] afdxFunctionalDataSet


# Functional Data Set (FDS)

AFDX » Functional Data Set (FDS)

## General

A Functional Data Set in the option AFDX conceptionally corresponds to a PDU in CANoe.

## Composition

A Functional Data Set (FDS) is organized as a block of up to four data sets (DS) with an associated Functional Status Set (FSS). This way, each data set (DS) is transmitted with the associated Functional Status (FS).

## Functional Status

A Functional Status is part of a 32 bit value and occupies 8 bit.For the Functional Status (FS) the following values are valid:

| Enumeration | Meaning | Value (hex) | Value (dec) |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| ND (no data) | There are no valid data in the data set. This is also the case for a failure or warning condition where the data content is meaningless. | 0x00 | 0 |
| NO (normal operation) | valid data: normal operating conditions | 0x03 | 3 |
| FT (functional test) | equipment runs under test conditions | 0x0C | 12 |
| NCD (no computed data) | invalid data: the equipment is under normal operating conditions but cannot compute reliable data | 0x30 | 48 |

Option .AFDX

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [AFDX/afdxBasics] afdxLightAFDX


# Light AFDX

AFDX » Light AFDX

The behavior of the AFDX software stack can be switched from the regular so called Full AFDX mode (as defined by ARINC-664) to a simplified variant, called Light AFDX individually for each channel.

In this mode basically the same rule set is applied as for regular AFDX with following exceptions:

- There is no redundancy support, which means only one line can be assigned to an AFDX channel.

- The redundancy check is therefore explicitly disabled, the integrity check always enabled.

- No SubVL’s supported.

- The VL-scheduler does not only support the usual VL-BAG rules, but in addition limits the maximum frame rate per ES by introducing a minimal distance of 500 µsec between its frames (can be modified in can.ini via the parameter AFDX:LightAFDXMinDistance) (see figure below)..

- Violations of this minimal distance for received frames of the same ES are detected and signaled.

- If the strictness setting is higher than 0 (Low), then the payload size is not only checked against the expected size defined in the database, but also against a fix maximum VL frame size of 256 bytes (can be modified in can.ini via the CAN.ini switch AFDX: LightAFDXMaxFrameSize).

Option .AFDX

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [AFDX/afdxBasics] afdxLogging


# Log Status and Bus Statistic Events

AFDX » Log Status and Bus Statistic Events

## Status Events

Now, status events are also saved in BLF and ASC logging files. They contain detailed information on all state changes of the Ethernet adapters being used. This makes it possible to detect any link problems or changes in bit rate, etc., such as when replaying in Offline mode.

|  | Cross Reference For details see file CAN_and_General_BLF_format.pdf in theCANoe installation directory. |

## Bus Statistic Events

Until now, bus statistics events were written to logging files, but only in BLF format. So, no statistical values, or just rudimentary values, were available when replaying ASC files in Offline mode.

Since version 8.2 of CANoe the bus statistics events are now also saved in ASC format.

|  | Note This continues to apply to the replay of PCAP files as well. There have been changes to the bus statistics information in logging files; now the values are saved as lines, and some new statistical values were added, while others were eliminated. Old logging files can still be played back, but with loss of information; the old event attributes are assigned to new event attributes as follows: New Statistic Values Old Statistic Values always lineA – mStatRxPacketCount mRxPacketCount mStatRxBytesHW mRxByteCount mStatTxPacketCount mTxPacketCount mStatTxBytesHW mTxByteCount mStatInvalidPacketCount mCollisionCount mStatRxErrorCountHW mErrorCount + mStatRedundantErrorPacketCount + mS

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [AFDX/afdxBasics] afdxOperatingModes


# Operating Modes for AFDX

AFDX » Operating Modes for AFDX

Since Version 9.0 SP2, CANoe supports two different operating modes with a different conceptual approach for the AFDX option. Besides the conventional frame mode, you can also use the FDS mode. You will find information on how to configure and use the operating modes on the page Use the Right Operating Mode.

## The Frame Mode (Bus-Oriented View)

The basic view of CANoe initially provides a bus-oriented view for the AFDX option with the frame mode. This means that all messages and signals on the bus are received and evaluated. This approach is especially well-suited for checking and evaluating time response and protocol behavior. For test purposes, however, the usefulness of this approach is limited because large signal quantities and a high data throughput are typically produced, which can significantly impair performance in frame mode.

## The FDS Mode (Application-Oriented View)

To handle the large signal quantity and high data throughput for test purposes, CANoe enables an application-oriented view with the FDS mode. For testing of application behavior, for example, during integration tests, a few signals are usually stimulated and the reaction or signal value changes of many signals are monitored. Because the messages and signals are sent periodically - even if no signal has changed - in frame mode this requires significant computing effort or the throughput is limited by the CPU computing power. The FDS mod

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [AFDX/afdxBasics] afdxProtocolChecks


# AFDX Protocol Checks

AFDX » Protocol Checks

## Integrity Check

Each AFDX frame contains a sequence number, which is counted from 1 to 255 by the VL and then starts with 1 again. After a restart or reset the sequence number starts with 0.

Based on IP length and Ethernet length the integrity check analyzes if a sequence number is available and if its value is 1 or 2 higher than the sequence number received before, in consideration of the skip from 255 to 1. The value 0 is allowed always. Packets with wrong sequence number are marked as erroneous.

## Redundancy Check

If redundant channels are used, only the first valid packet should be evaluated for redundant packets and the slower packet should be discarded (referred to AFDX). Therefore both packets have to be valid, have to pass the integrity check and the second packet must be received in a specified time limit (SkewMax) which is defined in the database.

If a second packet is received but violates the criteria, it is not discarded but detected as redundancy error for the statistics as well as a missing redundant packet.

## Value Range Checks

The AFDX specification ARINC-664p7 defines several requirements referring to value ranges of protocol relevant parameters, e.g. IP addresses, UDP ports etc. With a strict check a violation of this requirements within a packet should be handled as error and consequently the payload (signals) should not be decoded.

Within test environments the requirements are not fulfilled int

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [AFDX/afdxBasics] afdxScheduling


# Scheduling Concepts

AFDX » Scheduling Concepts

For an AFDX compliant stimulation of an end system, besides the message contents, timing aspects in particular are also relevant. It must be possible to send the messages periodically or sporadically, depending on their type, and they may also need to be fragmented. In addition, it must be assured that the frames fulfill BAG requirements.

|  | Definition: BAG Bandwidth Allocation Gap This is the time in milliseconds that must elapse at a minimum between the transmission of two AFDX messages on the same VL. |

To this end, there are two different schedulers in CANoe which work on different levels: the Message Scheduler and the VL Scheduler.

## Message-Scheduler

The Message Scheduler accepts message information (payload and control information) from all CANoe components (IG, Replay, CAPL, Signal Server) intended for this purpose.

### Periodic transmission

If a message is defined in the database, the AfdxMsgTxRate > 0 attribute is set, and the control information periodic sending, the Message Scheduler makes sure that this message is sent to the VL Scheduler at the given period without requiring further user actions. Message updates by the clients (2, 4, 6) are then only used to modify the payload without affecting the send rhythm, unless the control information requires that sending be stopped.

### Sporadic transmission

For non-periodic messages or for <Single Shot> control (e.g. in replaying logging files), the message

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [AFDX/afdxDBsupport] afdxDBsupportIntro


# Database Support

AFDX » Database Support

Option AFDX can use a database to describe AFDX messages. The primary key of this database is the message ID. This message ID is built by the VL ID and the destination UDP port:

message ID = (VL ID * 65536) + UDP port number

the complete address information of a message and the assignment to a Virtual Link are stored in message attributes. Additionally the payload range can be described with signals (FDS) for each message. The relation between the single data and the appropriate state is defined via signal attributes.

An ICMP connection is clearly assigned to a node (application) in a DBC file. The ICMP addressing details are defined with node attributes.

Option .AFDX

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |

| AfdxMsgTxRate | Integer | 0..<value> Message Transfer Rate of the message This value is usually greater or equal to the BAG of the corresponding VL. | 0 (aperiodic) |
| AfdxPortMasterName | String | name of the AFDX port master This name may be used for display purpose only. | DBC_default |
