### [AFDX/afdxIL] afdxILInclude


# Include the AFDX Interaction Layer

AFDX Interaction Layer » Include the AFDX Interaction Layer

If you want to access the AFDX network from a CAPL program, you must include the AFDX Interaction Layer (AFDX IL) AFDX_IL.dll. Afterward, you can send and receive AFDX packets. You have to include the AFDX IL too if you want to send from panels.

## Setting up the AFDX Interaction Layer

The AFDX Interaction Layer extends the functionality of a simulated node by additional CAPL functions. For a node to utilize the AFDX extensions, you must assign the AFDX IL to the node.

|  | Step by Step Procedure Open the configuration dialog for the node from the context menu. Switch over to the Components page. Open the file selection dialog with [Add...]. Search for the AFDX_IL.dll file in the Exec32 directory of your CANoe installation. Exit the dialog with [OK]. In the Simulation Setup, the node is shown with the additional Afdx designator, and you can use CAPL functions for AFDX. |

As an alternative, you can link the AFDX IL with the help of a database.

|  | Step by Step Procedure Open the CANdb++ database editor and select the View | Attribute Definitions command. Open the context menu and select the New… command. Add the NodeLayerModules attribute. Set the object type to Node and the value type to String. Close the dialog and open the overview view by choosing the View | Overview command. Select the node and open the context menu. There you choose the Edit node… command and switch 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [AFDX/afdxIL] afdxILOverview


# AFDX Interaction Layer

AFDX » AFDX Interaction Layer

The AFDX Interaction Layer (AFDX IL) simplifies the process of simulating an AFDX node with CAPL. In addition, you can assign the AFDX IL to a node as a component in the Simulation Setup. This allows the CAPL program to access the Packet API and the Signal API.

|  | Cross Reference For an overview of CAPL functions provided by the AFDX IL, see the CAPL reference. More information on how to include the AFDX Interaction Layer. |

## Packet API

The Packet API can be used to receive and send AFDX packets. It is possible to access the fields of the contained protocols. Moreover, packets can be generated and provided with protocol information, and fields such as the checksum and length can be computed.

For testing purposes, it is also possible to generate packets with protocol errors such as incorrect checksums.

## Signal API

The Signal API can be used to easily extract signals from received packets and to set or overwrite signal values for sending.

Option .AFDX

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [AFDX/capl] afdxA664MessagesVSA664Frames


# A664Messages Versus A664Frames

AFDX » CAPL Support » A664Messages Versus A664Frames

There is a conceptual difference between a664Messages and a664Frames:

A664Message

A664Messages are intended to be always valid regarding the protocol aspects, so they have valid selectors either by proper default values or by initialization using the Init-function.

Therefore it is by intention not allowed to modify any selectors afterwards in CAPL; the primary goal is to fill the payload with signal values and send the message.

A664Frames

The A664Frames in contrast are intended to build up Ethernet frames of different types, being valid or not. This allows the construction of IP-fragments as well as error injection of regular frames.

As a consequence all selectors can be manipulated (read / write access).

Because it is not easy to create a frame starting completely from scratch the recommended method is to create a suitable a664Message as origin and copy it to a a664Frame object using the copy-operator.

Option .AFDX

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [AFDX/capl] afdxDefineAFDXframe


# Declaration of AFDX Frames

AFDX » CAPL Support » A664Messages Versus A664Frames » Declaration of AFDX Frames

An AFDX frame is created with the keyword a664Frame. The difference between a664Message and a664Frame is explained here.

In contrast to a a664Message the a664Frame allows to set all header-specific selectors and therefore in principle to define any Ethernet message. However as this needs expert knowledge the recommended usage is to initialize a a664Message and copy it to a a664Frame using the Copy-operator or within the a664Frame-callback using the this-token.

Create an AFDX frame objects

|  | Example // variables sectionvariables {a664Frame DEMOMSG20_INT myAfdxFragment2; // (1)a664Frame * myAfdxFragment3; // (2)a664Frame * myAfdxFragment4 = {msgChannel = 1}; // (3)a664Frame * myAfdxFragment5[2] = { {msgChannel = 1}, {msgChannel = 1}}; // (4)a664Frame * myAfdxFragment6 = {IpLength = 800}; // (5)} |

The members of the frame are accessed via selectors. Note that the byte ordering for Ethernet is big endian. Nevertheless all selectors which are not arrays are presented in the host byte order.

Option .AFDX | See Also

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [AFDX/capl] afdxDefineAFDXmessage


# Declaration of AFDX Messages

AFDX » CAPL Support » A664Messages Versus A664Frames » Declaration of AFDX Messages

AFDX is accessible via two different interfaces:

- a separate DLL (AFDX Interaction Layer) or

- natively via AFDX message objects.

AFDX messages may be controlled by means of a CAPL program. An AFDX message is created with the keyword a664Message. You need a message for the following scenarios:

- create AFDX traffic and control the payload

- store received AFDX messages for later evaluation/comparison

The complete declaration includes the message identifier or message name when working with symbolic databases. The message identifier is entered as a number (integer) in decimal or hexadecimal notation to identify messages. The entry of “*” signifies that at first this variable does not have any message identifier. If the message is created with a message name from a database (example (1)), the corresponding setup information (e.g. VL, IP addresses and UDP ports) from the database attributes is used for initialization.

A message object created based on a number or “*” has no valid address information. If you would like to transmit a message you need the following steps:

|  | Step by Step Procedure Create the message object (see example statements 2 – 6) A664InitMessage() : initialize addressing information A664VLConfig() : configure the virtual link for the message A664MsgConfig() : configure the Tx behavior (IP fragmentation, output line, sub VL ID) A664

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [AFDX/procedures] afdxCreateConfigAnalysis


# Create a Configuration and Analyze the Network Traffic

AFDX » Procedures » Create a Configuration and Analyze the Network Traffic

To analyze an AFDX network, perform the following steps.

|  | Step by Step Procedure Start CANoe. Create a new configuration via File ribbon tab|New|Create Configuration. Use the AFDX template. An AFDX channel and the Trace Window are already pre-configured here. Open the Network Hardware Configuration dialog by choosing the Hardware ribbon tab|Network Hardware. Switch to the AFDX1|Network Hardware page. Make sure that the underlying Ethernet adapter is available and is accessible in the network.If other Ethernet adapters are available but cannot be selected, open the Options dialog. Select Measurement|General and increment the number of allowed AFDX channels to the desired value in area Channel Usage. Allocate the Ethernet adapter to be used to the desired AFDX channel, either for Line-A or Line-B (according to the physical connection), or for both if redundancy behavior is to be studied. Note The channel assignment and interface parameterization for VN5610 adapters is exclusively done in the Vector Hardware Config dialog. So the assignment is already done. Confirm with [OK]. Set the communication parameters for your configuration; in particular, one or more relevant databases should be entered (Generation from ICDs). Confirm with [OK]. Start the measurement with <F9> and observe the Trace Window. |  | Note The channel assignment and interfa

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [AFDX/procedures] afdxCreateStatisticSysVar


# Create VL Statistics System Variable

AFDX » Procedures » Create VL Statistics System Variable

In the AFDX VL Statistics Window you can use statistics system variables for evaluation. These VL statistics system variables are available for each statistics value, each received end system and each received VirtualLink.

## Follow the next steps to create a VL statistics system variable for AFDX

|  | Add an AFDX VL Statistics Window to the Measurement Setup if not available. Start the measurement. The AFDX VL Statistics Window shows the received VirtualLinks. Right-click the statistics value, the end system or the VirtualLink for which you want to create the statistics system variable (for example Throughput Rate[B/s] to create the statistics system variable ThroughputRate). The context menu opens. Select command Add System Variable. |

When the statistics system variable is created you can add it to the Graphics or Data Window with drag and drop from the Symbol Explorer.

The complete list of statistics system variables is located under the namespace _Statistics in the Symbol Explorer.

Statistics system variables can be deleted in the AFDX VL Statistics System Variables Manager.

|  | Notes AFDX statistics system variables can be added during measurement or after measurement stop only. Then they can be saved together with the configuration and are available on next measurement start. Statistics system variable are updated independently on the Statistics Windows. For exampl

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [AFDX/procedures] afdxGenerateDbc


# Generate a Database

AFDX » Procedures » Generate a Database

A large share of the AFDX messages in a network can be described statically. This is especially true of signal based messages, in which the data to be sent is organized in Functional Data Sets (FDS). These descriptions are generally provided by the aircraft manufacturer or a system supplier. The ICD format is used as the format here.

## Vector ICD Manager

With the Vector ICD Manager you can generate DBC files from ICD files, that are required for CANoe.

Furthermore it provides the combination of several ICD files to one DBC file, at which signal information as well as message attributes can be combined (combination of Rx and Tx attributes as well signals).

You can save configurations and comfortably update DBC files if the basis ICD files have been changed too.

## Vector ICD Converter

The command line tool ICD2DBC.exe is used by the Vector ICD Manager to convert ICD files to DBC files. The detailed syntax to use the converter is listed with the -h option.

## Vector CANdb++

In principle, you can create and modify AFDX DBC files with CANdb++. Because of the large amount of signals that is typical for AFDX, this is inadvisable.

## Aerospace Message Editor

You may import ICD files by means of the Aerospace Message Editor. The imported messages are internally stored in a message library and may be exported to DBC files if required. You can create new messages or modify existing ones.You may open the Aerospa

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [AFDX/procedures] afdxGenerateTimestampMetainfo


# Generating Timestamp Metainformation

AFDX » Procedures » Generating Timestamp Metainformation

All AFDX error events are not only stored as raw data in the different log file variants, but now also generate an additional Metainformation especially in BLF format, which can be read in extremely quickly even with very large log files.

This MetaInfo contains the time stamps and descriptions of all stored ErrorEvents.

Using the time defaults in the Replay block, you can quickly display the corresponding time period in the trace window, even if the log file is extremely large;

This allows faults signaled by alarms to be quickly examined during long test runs.

Option .AFDX

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [AFDX/procedures] afdxMonitoringAfdxSwitch


# Monitoring at the AFDX Switch Monitoring Port

AFDX » Procedures » Monitoring at the AFDX Switch Monitoring Port

If the measurement system is to be operated at an AFDX switch's monitoring port, the following aspects must be considered:

- No send functionality is available, because the monitoring port can only output packets.

- The data rate at the monitoring port may be very high, depending on the configuration of the switch, especially when all AFDX channels are being operated and are output by monitoring. In such cases, it is urgently advisable to use driver-based packet filters to filter out all non-relevant packets as early as possible.

- If the entire data traffic should still be analyzed with as few losses as possible, it is advisable to proceed as follows:

- Deactivate all analysis windows such as Trace Windows, data Windows, etc.

- Activate logging by the BLF format and record the data traffic.

- Then analyze the data without load effects in offline mode or with the Replay Block.

- Many ICDs or many DBCs or large DBCs may be necessary for complete signal decoding, since the packet multicast can result in messages accumulating from many end systems via the switches.

Option .AFDX

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [AFDX/procedures] afdxMonitoringEndsystem


# Monitoring at the End System (ES)

AFDX » Procedures » Monitoring at the End System (ES)

If an end system is to be monitored, it makes sense to place the measurement system between the switch and the end system, where physical access may be obtained via the TAP (Test Access Port), VN5610 in bypass mode or test switch.

This approach offers the following advantages:

- It does not significantly affect the timing or behavior of the end system or remaining system.

- Only those messages that are relevant to the end system are transmitted; this reduces the data rate, and the number of required ICDs is small.

- If necessary, and with the right configuration, packets may also be sent out (VN5610 in bypass mode or for switch).

Option .AFDX

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [AFDX/procedures] afdxSendReceiveICMPmessages


# Send and Receive ICMP Messages

AFDX » Procedures » Send and Receive ICMP Messages

An ICMP may only be transmitted with an a664Frame. The following steps are necessary:

|  | Step by Step Procedure Create an a664Frame object A664InitICMP(): Initialize the ICMP message A664VLConfig(): If the initialization is not done based on the node name (database), the VL needs to be configured separately. A664TriggerFrame(): Transmit the ICMP message |

A received ICMP message is handled within the event procedure "ICMP". Usually the following steps are necessary to answer an ICMP request.

|  | Step by Step Procedure Event procedure is called Check for expected IP address and corresponding VL ID For this kind of checking, the function a664isICMP() is provided. Send reply message Create an a664Frame object Copy the received frame A664InitICMP(): swap the IP addresses (source, destination) and set message contents to ECHO REPLY (with parameter SWAP) A664VLConfig(): If the initialization is not done based on the node name (database), the VL needs to be configured separately A664TriggerFrame(): Transmit the ICMP message |

Option .AFDX

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [AFDX/procedures] afdxStimulation


# Stimulation

AFDX » Procedures » Stimulation

Stimulation using messages requires control over the time a message is sent (scheduling) and the associated message contents (payload).

## Triggering mechanisms

- [A] Replay block: When a recording that was made using the Logging block is replayed, the message is triggered at the time of the recorded time stamp.

- [B] AFDX Interactive Generator: The message is triggered using the Sending column of the message list.

- [C] CAPL: The message is triggered using the AfdxOutputPacket and AfdxOutputPacketRaw functions.

- [D] CAPL: The message is triggered using the AfdxSchedule function.

- [E] CAPL: The message is triggered using the A664TriggerMessage or A664TriggerFrame function.

- [F] The message is triggered via a change of the payload using mechanisms [3], [4], and [6].

## Mechanisms for specifying the payload

- [1] Replay block: The entire message, and thus also the payload, is replayed as it was recorded.

- [2] AFDX Interactive Generator: Modification of the values in the signal list

- [3] Panels: Modification of signals using panels

- [4] Mapping: Modification of signals using mapping to system variables

- [5] CAPL: Access using AFDX CAPL functions

- [6] CAPL: direct access using signals (Version 8.2 SP2 or higher)

## Possible combinations of payload change and message trigger

|  | Schedule Transmission |
| --- | --- |
| [A] | [B] | [C] | [D] | [E] |
| Modify Payload | [1] | X |  |  |  |  |
| [2] |  | X |  |  |

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [AFDX/procedures] afdxUseOperatingModes


# Use The Right Operating Mode

AFDX » Procedures » Using the Right Operating Mode

CANoe provides various operating modes for the AFDX option that you can select and configure as required. You will find information on the different conceptual approaches and the mode of functioning of the operating modes on the page Operating Modes for AFDX.

|  | Note The display in the Trace Window is different for each mode. |

## Frame Mode

CANoe with the AFDX option is in frame mode by default. If necessary, ensure that the FDS mode in the AFDX FDS Mode area (Options dialog) is deactivated. The bus-oriented frame mode is especially well-suited for the following:

- Receiving and evaluating all messages on the bus

- Receiving and evaluating all signals on the bus

- Checking and evaluating time response

- Checking and evaluating protocol behavior

## FDS Mode

You activate the FDS mode in the AFDX FDS Mode area (Options dialog).The application-oriented FDS mode is especially well-suited for the following:

- Processing large signal quantities and high data throughput

- Stimulating a few signals and monitoring many signals

- Monitoring signal value changes individually (fading), especially with periodic sending of messages and signals

- Performing tests of application behavior, e.g., integration tests

- For other test purposes

- Significantly increasing the performance in lab environments or high computing time

## Changeover Between Frame Mode and FDS Mode

If you want to change 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [AFDX/protocols] afdxProtocolAfdx


# AFDX Protocol

AFDX » Protocol Overview » AFDX Protocol

On the frame level, the AFDX protocol does not contain its own header, rather its only visible element is the sequence number at the end of the AFDX frames. However, the MAC addresses are specifically interpreted, and certain limitations apply to valid AFDX packets and their Ethernet headers.

An AFDX frame has the following layout of content (without Preamble or Interframe GAP).

| 6 bytes | 6 bytes | 2 bytes | 46..1500 bytes | 4 bytes |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 20 bytes | 8 bytes | 1..1471 bytes | 0..16 bytes | 1 byte |
| MAC Destination Address | MAC Source Address | Type (0x800) | IP Header | UDP Header | AFDX Payload | Padding | Seq Num | Frame Check Seq |

## MAC destination address

The MAC destination address must conform to the following format:

| Ethernet MAC destination address mapping, 48 bits |
| --- |
| Constant field, 32 bits | Virtual link identifier, 16 bits |
| Xxxx xx11 xxxx xxxx xxxx xxxx xxxx xxxx | VLId |

## MAC source address

Unlike in normal networks, the MAC source addresses are neither unique nor constant, rather they are put together based on various network parameters.

| Ethernet MAC Controller ID (MAC source address), 48 bit |
| --- |
| Constant field, 24 bits | User defined ID, 16 bits | Interface_ID | 5 bits |
| Network_ID | Equipment_ID |
| Const, 4 bits | Domain_ID | Side_ID | Location_ID |
| 0x020000 | "0000" | 4 bits | 3 bits | 5 bits | 3 bits | "00000" |

- The Domain_

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [AFDX/protocols] afdxProtocolAppLayer


# Application Layer

AFDX » Protocol Overview » Application Layer

Except for ICMP packets, which are used for analysis purposes, all AFDX messages are typically UDP-based. However, in principle TCP is not excluded.

From the header information, it is possible to clearly determine the message contents based on the the quintuplets that follow (MessageId in the DBC!):

Source UDP port + Source IP address + VL-ID (destination MAC address) + Destination IP address + Destination UDP port

To achieve redundancy and performance, the following parameter subset is currently used for organization within the DBC:

MessageId = VL-ID * 0x10000 + Destination UDP port

## Messages in the Application Layer

|  | Definition: Application Data Application Data is the unit of data passed across the interface between an application and the Communication Ports or SAP ports implemented in the End-System. Application data consist of only the data sent or received by this application. Application data are transmitted by encapsulation inside an UDP datagram or a TCP segment. The term "Application Message" may also be used sometimes instead of "Application Data". |

As a rule, the messages can be subdivided into two groups:

- protocol-based messages such as TFTP and SNMP: The contents may be decoded based on the protocol specification (e.g. by a special signal protocol DLL)

- signal-based messages: Signals are organized in so-called Functional Data Sets (FDS) in AFDX. Then they are decoded by CANoe,

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [AFDX/protocols] afdxProtocolEthernet


# Ethernet

AFDX » Protocol Overview » Ethernet

| Quick Access |
| --- |
| Protocol Information |
| Protocol Dependencies |
| Protocol and Packet Format |
| Header Format |

## Protocol Information

Before each Ethernet Frame the preamble with 7 bytes (AAh) and the Start Frame Delimiter (D5h) are sent.

The data of an Ethernet Frame (MAC-SDU) can contain further protocols and the payload. The data must have a minimum size of 46 bytes and a maximum size of 1500 bytes. If less data are available a padding is necessary, more data have to be fragmented.

Following the payload the checksum (CRC) is sent.

|  | Note Preamble, Start Frame Delimiter and checksum are not part of an Ethernet packet in CANoe and are not evaluated. Received packets with an incorrect checksum are discarded and not displayed in the Trace Window. |

## Protocol Dependencies [▲ top]

## Protocol and Packet Format [▲ top]

| Ethernet |
| --- |
| Protocol Designator | eth |
| Token | Token Designator | Offset [byte.bit] | Length [byte] |
| Header | header | 0 | 14 |
| Payload | data | 14 | 0..1500 |

## Header Format [▲ top]

| Token | TokenDesignator | Offset[byte.bit] | Length[bit] | Packet API |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| AfdxInitProtocol/AfdxInitPacket | AfdxCompletePacket |
| Target | destination | 0 | 48 | 0 | - |
| The destination MAC address specifies the target system. It can also be a multicast or broadcast address. |
| Source | source | 6 | 48 | 0 or local MAC address of the node | - |
| Th

### [AFDX/protocols] afdxProtocolICMP


# ICMP

AFDX » Protocol Overview » ICMP

The ICMP protocol (Internet Control Message Protocol) is generally used for control tasks. With AFDX, it is exclusively used for the ping application (echo request/ reply).

This checks if an equipment or an application is available in an AFDX network. The protocol consists of a request with the corresponding response and uses different virtual links each time.

Option .AFDX

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |

## Protocol Information

Besides IPv6 the IPv4 protocol (Internet Protocol Version 4) is the lowermost protocol of the internet protocol family respectively the TCP/IP model that is independent of the transmitting medium. With Ethernet IPv4 uses the Ethertype 0800.

## Protocol Dependencies [▲ top]

## Protocol and Packet Format [▲ top]

| IPv4 |
| --- |
| Protocol Designator | ipv4 |
| Token | Token Designator | Offset [byte.bit] | Length [byte] |
| Header | header | 0 | 20.60 |
| Payload | data | 20..60 | 0..1480 |

|  | Note IPv4 can only be contained in a single Ethernet packet in CANoe. So the maximum length is restricted to the maximum Ethernet payload length (MTU) of 1500 byte. |

## Header Format [▲ top]

| Token | TokenDesignator | Offset[byte.bit] | Length[bit] | Packet API |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| AfdxInitProtocol/AfdxInitPacket | AfdxCompletePacket | AfdxAddToken/AfdxRemoveToken |
| Version | version | 0 | 4 | 4 | - | - |
| 4-bit internet protocol version number = 4 |
| Header Length | ihl | 0.4 | 4 | 5 | X | - |
| Internet header length This is the length of the IP header as a multiple of 4 bytes. It is necessary due to possible options. |
| DSCP | dscp | 1 | 6 | 0 | - | - |
| The Differentiated Services Code Point value (DSCP) sets the priority of the packet. The first th

### [AFDX/protocols] afdxProtocolUDP


# UDP

AFDX » Protocol Overview » UDP

| Quick Access |
| --- |
| Protocol Information |
| Protocol Dependencies |
| Protocol and Packet Format |
| Header Format |
| Packet API (obsolete) |
| Native API |

## Protocol Information

UDP (User Data Protocol) is a network protocol with minimal protocol header. It is a connectionless protocol and doesn't use data segmentation, that means the packet size in CANoe is restricted to the maximum Ethernet size. With a checksum transmission errors can be identified. With UDP a service on the target system can be addressed with port numbers.

## Protocol Dependencies [▲ top]

## Protocol and Packet Format [▲ top]

| UDP |
| --- |
| Protocol Designator | udp |
| Token | Token Designator | Offset [byte.bit] | Length [byte] |
| Header | header | 0 | 8 |
| Payload | data | 8 | 0..1472 |

|  | Note UDP can only be contained in a single Ethernet packet in CANoe. So the maximum length is restricted to the maximum Ethernet payload length (MTU) of 1500 byte. |

## Header Format [▲ top]

| Token | TokenDesignator | Offset[byte.bit] | Length[bit] | Packet API |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| AfdxInitProtocol/AfdxInitPacket | AfdxCompletePacket |
| Source Port | source | 0 | 16 | 0 | partly specified by the next protocol, e.g. DHCPv4 |
| port number of the source (optional), 0: unknown port |
| Destination Port | destination | 2 | 16 | 0 | partly specified by the next protocol, e.g. DHCPv4 |
| port number of the destination |
| Length | length |

### [AFDX/vn5610] vn5610afdxFeatures


# Sending at a Precise Time with the VN5610

AFDX » Scheduling Concepts » Sending at a Precise Time with the VN5610

Along with offering normal transmitting functionality, in which frames are sent immediately (default), the VN5610 Ethernet module also offers a special Tx mode for sending at a precise time.

For one, this enables conformance to AFDX-specific requirements for jitter and redundancy time behavior. The time is set by providing the frames to be sent by CANoe with a desired time stamp, i.e. the time at which they should be placed on the Ethernet bus.

To circumvent stochastic delays by Windows task switching and USB transfers, the times of these time stamps are typically set a few additional milliseconds into the future. The VN5610 module buffers these frames as long as necessary, and then sends them out based on the desired time stamp with a jitter of typically just a few micro seconds. If the time intervals of the desired time stamps are less than the bus transfer rate permits, the frames are sent out by a "best effort" method.

The AFDX Scheduler automatically generates suitable time stamps for both periodic frames and single frames.

|  | Note For time-triggered transmission, at least the software time synchronization must be configured. When multiple VN5610 interfaces or other synchronization-capable hardware (e.g. CAN or third party vendor) is used, hardware time synchronization should be used as well! When the time-triggered Tx mode is used, "multi applicati

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [AFDX/windows] afdxBusStatisticWnd


# Bus Statistics Window (AFDX)

AFDX Windows » Bus Statistics Window (AFDX)

The Bus Statistics Window shows AFDX-specific or Ethernet-specific data and values. For each AFDX channel, the values are shown in two columns. Each column contains the values for one line (Line A, Line B).

The underlying data are determined by both the hardware (Bus view) and by CANoe (CANoe view). The results may differ if any hardware filtering is active.

For AFDX, the following information is shown in the Bus Statistics Window:

| Statistics Value | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Link speed [Mbit/s] | communication speed of the connected links from hardware polling |
| Link status | current link state -status from hardware polling Up (link is active) Down (link is inactive) |
| Link down total | number of link interruptions (change of link status from active to inactive) that occurred during the measurement |
| Rx frames raw [total] | number of Ethernet frames received by the hardware adapter, including corrupt frames Note Corrupt frames may be recorded differently depending on the type of adapter used. |  | Note Corrupt frames may be recorded differently depending on the type of adapter used. |
|  | Note Corrupt frames may be recorded differently depending on the type of adapter used. |
| Rx frames [total] | number of Ethernet frames actually received in CANoe This value may be smaller than the value Rx frames raw due to hardware filters or queue overruns. |
| Rx frames [fr/s] | rate of frames

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [AFDX/windows] afdxLoggingBlock


# Logging

AFDX » Logging

You can log received Ethernet frames in logging files in ASCII, BLF (Binary Logging format) or MDF4 format. You can then play back these files in Offline mode or using a Replay Block.

|  | Note ASCII is not recommended at high data rates due to its poorer performance. BLF format is much better suited for this.You can also reduce the memory requirements by using the trigger block with trigger conditions for symbolic messages, signals, system variables or for AFDX raw frames. On the other hand, ASCII format is advisable if specific subareas of the Trace window need to be exported as a template with the intention of making specific modifications to the file and replaying the revised file later. |

## Libpcap and LibpcapNG File Format

The libpcap file format is also supported for replay. This format does not contain any statistical, channel and direction information.

The following special conditions should be observed here:

- Replay Block (libpcap format): In the channel mapping, only the AFDX 1 channel exists, and it can be replayed on any other desired channels. If the selected replay channel has two Ethernet port allocations (Line A and Line B), then a frame is allocated to a port based on the MAC source address. If the Interface_ID has the value 2, the frame is sent on Line B. If there is only one port for the replay channel, all Ethernet frames are sent over this port.

- PCAPNG files logged with Wireshark do not contain information about the 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [AFDX/windows/afdxFilter] afdxFilterFrameAttr


# Frame Attributes

AFDX Filter » Frame Attributes

| Message Filter | Source Filter | VL Filter | Frame Attributes |  |

Packets can also be filtered according to their frame attributes. The Frame Status and Error Type tables provide three states in the Activated column for each bit switch: Ignored (bit is ignored), Activated (packet is filtered if bit is set), and Deactivated (packet is filtered if bit is not set).

## Frame Status

| Flag | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Interface is B | The frame was received on the assigned B interface. |
| Frame is redundant | The frame is the later frame in the case of redundant frames (received simultaneously on line A/B). |
| Frame is fragmented | The frame is an IP fragment and not yet a complete or valid message. |
| Packet is reassembled | The message was composed from multiple fragments. |
| Packet is SAP | The message is an SAP message (only identifiable with database support). |

## Error Type

| Flag | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Wrong interface | The interface byte of the MAC address does not match the physical line on which the frame was received. |
| Invalid frame | The frame is not a valid AFDX frame. |
| Integrity error | The frame does not meet the requirements of a consecutive sequential number according to ARINC-664p7 Ch. 3.2.6.2.1. This check must be activated in the hardware dialog. |
| Redundancy error | The frame violates the redundancy requirements according to ARINC-664p7 Ch. 3.2.6.2.2. This check must be activ

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [AFDX/windows/afdxFilter] afdxFilterMessage


# Message Filter

AFDX Filter » Message Filter

| Message Filter | Source Filter | VL Filter | Frame Attributes |  |

You insert a message filter:

- Using the context menu (right mouse button)

- Using the menu bar (Edit menu)

- Add message from database...: Opens the symbol selection dialog via which you can insert messages from the database.

- Add message filter...: Opens the parameter dialog for configuration of a message filter using the message identifier and the AFDX application channel.

- Insert from Trace Window...: Takes the channel and identifier information from the AFDX packets copied in the Trace Window and adds it to the list.

The parameters for message filters are divided into two views. The left side displays all message filters (main view). The right side displays the properties of the currently selected filter (properties view).

## Columns of the main view

| Column | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Active | activates or deactivates a filter |
| Name | For messages that are present in the network description/database, the name of the message from the database is displayed. The name is not editable. |
| ID | message identifier |
| End System | name of the sender end system, if known; otherwise, the representation of the sender IP address |
| VL Name | Virtual Link name or number, on which the message is sent |
| Channel | number of the application channel The possible selections depend on the number of configured channels. |
| Direction | direction filte

### [AFDX/windows/afdxFilter] afdxFilterToolbar


# Toolbar

AFDX Filter » Toolbar

From the toolbar, you can configure general settings for the AFDX Filter.

| Setting | Description |
| --- | --- |
| [Activate/Deactivate] | This button is used to activate or deactivate the AFDX Filter. In the active state, the filter conditions are evaluated, and only those packets satisfying the filter conditions are passed. When the filter is deactivated, all packets are passed. |
| Type | From the selection list, you can select the operating mode of the AFDX Filter. In the Pass Filter operating mode, only those packets are passed that satisfy the configured filter conditions. In the Stop Filter operating mode, only those packets are passed that do not satisfy the configured filter conditions. |
| View | Here you can select if the configuration overview should be displayed. |
| Edit | Here you can add new filter conditions as well as deactivate or delete existing filter conditions. Note Filter conditions which you add during a running measurement are not configured and would route all packets. Therefore these filter conditions are deactivated at first. |  | Note Filter conditions which you add during a running measurement are not configured and would route all packets. Therefore these filter conditions are deactivated at first. |

### [AFDX/windows/afdxHardwareConfig] afdxHardware


# AFDX Network Hardware Configuration

AFDX » AFDX Network Hardware Configuration

|  | Caution! Use Option AFDX with special care if the computer is connected to an Ethernet network. Under some circumstances, packets might be sent in the network that could lead to problems. To avoid this case, follow the instructions on page Exclusive use of an Ethernet interface. |

You can use option AFDX with the following network hardware:

- any network card (NIC) which provides the operating system as an interface

- VN5610 (Vector interface with USB interface for Ethernet and CAN)

- AFDX hardware or special network adapter from third party vendors

First select the number of AFDX channels if not done yet:

|  | Step by Step Procedure Open the Options dialog from the File ribbon tab. Switch to page Measurement|General. In the AFDX list, enter the number of AFDX channels, under consideration of redundancy requirements. Close the dialog with [OK]. |

Now enter the configuration menu to adjust the settings for every single channel.

When you open the AFDX<channel number> page, the protocol pages are shown. Here, you can make additional settings, such as setting up a filter or entry of a signal protocol DLL.

## Network Hardware Configuration

The following pages are available in the AFDX Network Hardware Configuration dialog:

| AFDX | Filters | Signal Protocol |  |

Option .AFDX | See Also

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [AFDX/windows/afdxHardwareConfig] afdxHardwarePageAfdx


# Network Hardware Configuration - AFDX

Network Hardware » AFDX

Ribbon: Hardware Tab|Network Hardware|AFDX

| AFDX | Filters | Signal Protocol |  |

On this page you can assign the relevant hardware interfaces to all AFDX channels or exclude those interfaces that are not to be used. Please consider the information on page Exclusive use of an Ethernet interface.

To configure your hardware, follow the next steps:

|  | Step by Step Procedure Open the dialog from the Hardware ribbon tab|Network Hardware. Switch to the page of the AFDX channel AFDX<channel number> to be configured. For Line A and/or Line B, select the physically connected AFDX or Ethernet adapter under consideration of redundancy requirements, i.e. adapters connected to Line-A should be entered under Line-A and vice versa. Otherwise an error message is displayed. For redundancy, both lines (A and B) must be entered. Configure the AFDX channel, depending on the physically connected network hardware considering the limitations. Repeat steps 3 and 4 for each AFDX channel. Select Unused for lines that you don't need. Close the dialog with [OK]. |

## Configuration of default network interfaces

|  | Note If no Ethernet adapter are available in the list (only <unknown> entry) although Windows network adapter are available, you have to install the Vector IP driver first. The device settings for network adapters, e.g. bit rate, must be configured in the device manager as usual under Windows. |

### [AFDX/windows/afdxHardwareConfig] afdxHardwarePageAfdxFilter


# Network Hardware Configuration - Filters

Network Hardware » Filters

Ribbon: Hardware Tab|Network Hardware|AFDX|Filters

| AFDX | Filters | Signal Protocol |  |

On this page, you can define low-level filters on the driver level so that you only see the relevant packets and improve performance. Various input options are available for this.

## Source and VL based filters

This special dialog lets you conveniently define the filters based on the selection of senders and receivers. For a usage of the dialog the senders and receivers should be defined in the database but not necessarily.

You can use the [Select…] button to open the Hardware Filter dialog, in which you can define new filters and add them to the list of active filters. The input methods are the same as for Source and VL filters of the AFDX Filter.

The filters generated from this are integrated in the MAC and IP-based filters. Only active filters are considered. This means that the two filter types may be combined.

## MAC or IP based filters

You can define filters for the IEEE 802.3 specified fields Destination MAC address, Source MAC address, Ethertype, and Source IP address.

Pressing the [Add…] button opens the Add Filter dialog, in which you can define a new filter and add them to the list of active filters. A new filter requires entry of the following values:

- Field: Selection of the filter type from the selection box

- Code: Acceptance code with which the relevant bits of the packet (specified by t

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [AFDX/windows/afdxHardwareConfig] afdxHardwarePageAfdxSignalprotocol


# Network Hardware Configuration - Signal Protocol

Network Hardware » Signal Protocol

Ribbon: Hardware Tab|Network Hardware|AFDX|Signal Protocol

| AFDX | Filters | Signal Protocol |  |

On this page you can specify the use of a signal protocol (in the form of a DLL) by activating the Enable signal protocol option.

You can input the name of the DLL directly in the DLL filename field (the file is searched in the configuration directory and in the installation directory (Exec32 directory) of CANoe) or you can open the file selection dialog with the [...] button to look for the file yourself.

|  | Note In the distributed mode of CANoe the DLL must be stored in the Exec32 directory of CANoe, because the configuration directory cannot be accessed on the RT computer. |

### [AFDX/windows/afdxIG] afdxIGDefaultIPaddress


# Information About Using the Default Source IP Address

AFDX Interactive Generator » Using the Default Source IP Address

## Global setting of the default source IP address

You can set the default source IP address as global so that it is used for all new AFDX configurations and AFDX Interactive Generators. You can do this in the Options dialog of CANoe.

## Setting of the default source IP address in the AFDX IG

You can set the default source IP address independently for each AFDX IG. The address is relevant only for the managed messages.

Thus if you want to send messages with the IP addresses of different end systems, you can do that with several AFDX IGs and the appropriate messages.

|  | Note Consider that setting the default source IP address in the AFDX IG may overwrite the global setting. |

## Which default source IP address is used in which case?

The following figure displays which default source IP address is used in which case and what dependencies must be considered.

Option .AFDX | See Also

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [AFDX/windows/afdxIG] afdxIGSendMessages


# Send AFDX Messages

AFDX Interactive Generator » Send AFDX Messages

The AFDX Interactive Generator can send messages that are described in the database. All relevant database attributes must be available. Inconsistent messages cannot be added to the Message List. In this case, a related error message is shown in the Write Window.

|  | Note If messages or message parts (FS, signals, Source IP address) contain any errors or parameter inconsistencies, these elements are marked with a red exclamation mark. These messages are not sent until the errors are resolved. If several message parts contain errors, they are all displayed in the tooltip in text form. To show the tooltip hover the mouse pointer over the error marker until the tooltip with the error description appears. |

To send messages with the AFDX IG, follow the next steps.

|  | Step by Step Procedure Insert the desired message in the Message List. If the message was imported from the Trace Window, the message can be sent immediately with the same signal values (Step 4). Steps 2 and 3 are only necessary if you wish to change the signal values. Set the desired raw values of the signals in the Signal List. If the signal state should be set to Normal Operation (NO), it is mandatory to enter a signal value. An exception are string signals, empty signal values are valid for these signals. For signals with the No Data (ND), Functional Test (FT) or No Computed Data (NCD) signal state, no signal value needs to be entered. 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [AFDX/windows/afdxIG/afdxIGFSView] afdxIGFSView


# FS View

AFDX Interactive Generator » FS View

In the FS View, the signal states (Functional Status) are shown for all messages that are selected in the Message List. An entry is created in the FS View for each message. Expand the message in the FS View to show all signal states of the message.

The different signal states are highlighted in color in the FS View. In addition, the signals assigned to the status are shown in color in the Signal List.

The following colors are used to display signal states.

| Signal State | Description | Color |
| --- | --- | --- |
| ND | No Data | pink |
| NO | Normal Operation | white |
| FT | Functional Test | yellow |
| NCD | No Computed Data | blue |

Erroneous signal states are marked with an error icon. Messages with such signal states cannot be sent.

## Change signal states

Signal states can be modified in the FS View as follows:

- Select the desired value from the list in the Status column.

- Select one or more states or one or more messages. Set the value from the context menu by the commands Status | ND, Status | NO, Status | FT or Status | NCD. You can modify multiple signal states simultaneously using this method.

## Filter signal states

Under the FS View you will find the FS Filter. You can use this filter to reduce the number of signal states shown in the list. To do this, enter the desired character string in the input box. The list of displayed signal states is then automatically adapted. If only messages are being dis

[...truncated for brevity...]

AFDX Interactive Generator » FS View » Columns in the FS View

The following columns are available to you.

| Column | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Name | name of the message or signal state (Functional Status) from the database The unique message number from the Message List is shown as a prefix to the left of the name. |
| Status | Select the signal state (Functional Status) from the list: ND (No Data) NO (Normal Operation) FT (Functional Test) NCD (No Computed Data) |

Option .AFDX | See Also

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |

| --- | --- |

| Status | NO | sets all selected signal states (Functional Status) to the value Normal Operation |
| Status | FT | sets all selected signal states (Functional Status) to the value Functional Test |
| Status | NCD | sets all selected signal states (Functional Status) to the value No Computed Data |
| Reset | resets the selected signal states to default value |

### [AFDX/windows/afdxIG/afdxIGMessageList] afdxIGMessageList


# Message List

AFDX Interactive Generator » Message List

In the upper area of the dialog, you will find the message list. Insert all of the messages that you wish to send with the AFDX IG in this list.

The messages are parametrized via the signal list and the FS view in the lower area of the dialog. There, you can set all signal states (Functional Status) and signal values of the messages that are selected in the Message List.

Erroneous messages are marked with an error icon. Such messages cannot be sent.

To select multiple messages, hold down the <Ctrl> key and select the desired messages with the left mouse button.

## Insert messages

Messages that you add to the Message List are always inserted at the end of the list.

You have the following ways to add a message to the list:

- Choose the Add | Message… command. In the Selection of Messages and Bus Events dialog, select the desired messages.

- Choose the Add | Message via Signal Selection… command. In the Selection of Signals dialog, select the desired signals. All messages to which the selected signals are assigned are entered into the Message List.

- Select a message in the Symbol Explorer and move it to the Message List by drag and drop.

- Select a message in the Trace Window and move it to the Message List by drag and drop. With this method you not only copy the message, but also the signal values of the selected message. The message can be sent immediately with the same signal layout.

- Copy a message in t

[...truncated for brevity...]

Nonetheless, the channel number can st

### [AFDX/windows/afdxIG/afdxIGSignalList] afdxIGSignalList


# Signal List

AFDX Interactive Generator » Signal List

In the Signal List the signals of all signal states (Functional Status) and messages selected in the FS View are shown. You can set specific signal values or signal states in this list. When the signal values are transmitted depends on the send type of the message and the specific settings in the Message List.

Erroneous signals are marked with an error icon. Messages with such signals cannot be sent.

## Select multiple lines

Multiple lines are selected with one of the following procedures:

- Hold down the <Ctrl> key and select the desired lines with the left mouse button.

- Select the first line of the desired range with the left mouse button. Hold down the <Shift> key and select the last line of the range.

- Select all lines with <Ctrl>+<A>.

## Change signal states

Signal states can be modified in the Signal List as follows:

- Select the desired value for the signal state in the list in the Status column.

- Select one or more signals and set the signal state from the context menu by the commands Status | ND, Status | NO, Status | FT or Status | NCD. You can modify multiple signal states simultaneously using this method.

## Change signal values

Signal values can be modified in the Signal List as follows:

- Enter the desired signal value in the Value column.

- Select the desired value from the list (lists are offered for signals with the data types Bool and Enum).

- In the context menu of the Signal List,

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [AFDX/windows/afdxStatisticWnd] afdxStatisticsWndDatarateMonitoring


# Data Rate Monitoring

AFDX VL Statistics Window » Data Rate Monitoring

In a typical AFDX environment, most messages are strictly cyclical and the number of frames averaged over a sufficiently long period of time is therefore fairly constant.

A spontaneous deviation of this data rate can therefore be used as an indication to detect changes in communication behavior in the overall system.

For this purpose, the standard deviation of the total data rate of the VL statistics is monitored.

If the change in the standard deviation exceeds a specified percentage relative to the mean value, an AFDX error event is generated as a warning with the corresponding description.

The threshold value can be modified in CAN.ini:

- [AFDX]

- StdDevCalcLimit = 10

The value is expected in[%]; typically 10%.

The value 0 switches off the monitoring.

Option .AFDX

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |

### [AFDX/windows/afdxStatisticWnd] afdxStatisticWndSysVarManager


# AFDX VL Statistics System Variables Manager

AFDX VL Statistics Window » AFDX VL Statistics System Variables Manager

All AFDX VL statistics system variables are shown in a tree view in this dialog. The layout is based on the scheme: End system | Virtual Link | Statistic value.

Individual nodes, as well as hierarchies of nodes and their elements, can be created and deleted. For deletion use command Delete in the toolbar or the appropriate command in the context menu of the selected element.

With [Cancel] you will undo all changes. This means deleted elements are restored and newly created elements are deleted.

|  | Note System variables from old configurations that are no longer supported are highlighted in red font and should be deleted. It is not possible to remove AFDX VL statistics system variables via the Symbol Explorer. |

Option .AFDX | See Also

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [AFDX/windows/afdxStatisticWnd] afdxStatisticWndViews


# Views

AFDX VL Statistics Window » Views

The following views are available, all of them show the same statistic data.

| View | Description | Display Hierarchy |
| --- | --- | --- |
| SV (Statistic quantity) | This view focuses on the statistic quantities. The displayed value is a hierarchical summation of each virtual link and end system. This view is suitable to create statistic system variables too. | Statistic quantity | End system | Virtual Link |
| ES (End system) | The hierarchical summation is done for each Virtual Link. The topmost hierarchy level is built by the end systems. This simplifies the VL related analysis of the statistic quantities. Note The name of the end system is taken from the DBC. If no DBC information is available, or if the symbolic display is deactivated, the name of the end system is taken from the User-ID (per ARINC-664 specification). Here, the prefix ES_ is extended by the two middle bytes of the Source-IP, and the bytes are delimited by a dot (.) to make the character of the address clearly discernible. If a system variable is added, the dot between the bytes is replaced by an underscore (_) in CAPL. |  | Note The name of the end system is taken from the DBC. If no DBC information is available, or if the symbolic display is deactivated, the name of the end system is taken from the User-ID (per ARINC-664 specification). Here, the prefix ES_ is extended by the two middle bytes of the Source-IP, and the bytes are delimited by a dot (.) to ma

There are various predefined layouts for the AFDX Trace Window.

| Data | Payload Data | payload data of the AFDX packet | – |
| Source | Source MAC | MAC address of the sender | • |

### [AFDX/windows/afdxTraceWnd] traceWndAfdxConfig


# Configuration

Trace Window (AFDX) » Configuration

## Columns

You can use the [Accept standard defined columns] button to activate one of the AFDX standard layouts with additional columns. You can activate other columns with [Add column(s)] or you can define your own User-defined layout.

## View

### Event Highlighting

AFDX specific frames can be highlighted depending on different categories to highlight important events and messages to find them in the Trace Window more quickly.

You can open the dialog to set the colors via the context menu of the Trace Window with the entry Configuration|View|Standard Highlighting|[Change…]. Alternatively you can open this dialog via the CANoe Options dialog (Windows/Blocks|Trace Window|Event Highlighting).

### Symbolic Interpretation

Symbolic interpretation gives you a simple evaluation of the packet information. You get a text-based display of the source and destination addresses and of the packet data. You can also use this display type in the detailed view.

To switch between symbolic and numeric interpretation, use the [sym/num] buttons on the Home ribbon tab of CANoe.

|  | Cross Reference You can find more configuration options here. |

Option .AFDX

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [AFDX/windows/afdxTraceWnd] traceWndAfdxErrorEvents


# Error Events

Trace Window (AFDX) » Error Events

All protocol-related errors are directly linked to the respective message in CANoe / CANalyzer and displayed in the trace window and counted in the statistics.

However, error events that are not directly linked to an AFDX message cannot be processed in this way. Instead, a special AFDX ErrorEvent is generated in these cases.

This includes

- The event timestamp

- A description of the error

- A variable attribute list e.g. for the error class (Info, Warning, Error)

Typical errors include hardware errors, queuing errors and fragment errors (missing fragments). These events are also logged and displayed in the trace window. Using the callback reference <OnAfdxError>, you can also react to these events in CAPL.

Option .AFDX

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [AFDX/windows/afdxTraceWnd] traceWndAfdxHighlighting


# Event Highlighting

Trace Window (AFDX) » Event Highlighting

## Event Groups

Within the area AFDX colors can be set for the following event groups:

| Group | Priority | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |

# Trace Window: Predefined Filter - AFDX Events

Trace Window » Filter » Predefined Filter » AFDX Events

Trace Window (AFDX) » AFDX Events

AFDX Events

For the AFDX bus system there are predefined filter which can be used to reduce and adapt the output in the Trace Window. The set filter options are saved in the configuration.

Packets

Define here if AFDX packets are filtered. An activated check box indicates that packets are let through and displayed in the Trace Window.

Status Events

Define here if status events are filtered. An activated check box indicates that status events are let through and displayed in the Trace Window.

Error Events

Define here if error events are filtered. An activated check box indicates that status events are let through and displayed in the Trace Window.

Fragments

Define here if message fragments are filtered. An activated check box indicates that status events are let through and displayed in the Trace Window.

# Bus Statistics System Variables for AFDX

AFDX Windows » Symbol Explorer » Bus Statistics System Variables for AFDX

In the Symbol Explorer, the following system-defined bus statistics system variables are automatically available for AFDX under the namespace _Statistics::AFDX[n][A/B] (n is the channel number):

| Bus Statistics Value | System Variable | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Link speed [Mbit/s] | LinkSpeed | communication speed of the connected links from hardware polling |
| Link status | LinkStatus | current link state - status from hardware polling Up (link is active) Down (link is inactive) |
| Link down total | LinkDownEvents | number of link interruptions (change of link status from active to inactive) that occurred during the measurement |
| Rx frames raw [total] | RxFramesRaw | number of Ethernet frames received by the hardware adapter, including corrupt frames Note Corrupt frames may be recorded differently depending on the type of adapter used. |  | Note Corrupt frames may be recorded differently depending on the type of adapter used. |
|  | Note Corrupt frames may be recorded differently depending on the type of adapter used. |
| Rx frames [total] | RxFrames | number of Ethernet frames actually received in CANoe This value may be smaller than the value Rx frames raw due to hardware filters or queue overruns. |
| Rx frames [fr/s] | RxFrameRate | rate of frames received in CANoe per second |
| Tx frames [total] | TxFrames | total number of Ethernet f

### [AFDX/windows/symbolExplorer] symbolExplorerMessageStatistics


# Message Statistics for AFDX

AFDX Windows » Symbol Explorer » Message Statistics for AFDX

Predefined signals for message-related statistical values are generated for a message defined in the database.

| Signal | Description |
| --- | --- |
| current gap [ms] | measured time spacing between two messages for the same ID, Line (A/B) and direction (Tx/Rx) For fragmented messages, this is the interval between the last frames of the two messages. |
| bag [ms] | measured time spacing to the last message in the same VL with the same Line and direction |
| total [fr] | total number of transmissions of the message (Tx and Rx). Only complete messages are counted, fragments are not considered in calculating this value. |
| max. gap [ms] | maximum cycle time of the message |
| min. gap [ms] | minimum cycle time of the message |
| average gap[ms] | mean value of the cycle time of the message |

You can add the predefined statistic signals to the Graphics or Data Window by drag and drop from the Symbol Explorer. For example you can add the predefined signal BAG to the Graphics Window to observe its response over time.

To do this, open the message whose statistical values you wish to observe. When you do this, you will find the predefined signals under the message signals.

Option .AFDX

### [AMDXCP] AMDASAP2Updater


# ASAP2 Updater

.AMD/XCP » ASAP2 Updater

The option .AMD/XCP comes with an ASAP2 Updater tool. This tool enables you to update measurement object addresses in an existing A2L file based on the corresponding Linker Map file. An address update is needed e.g. to insert the new addresses for the measurement objects into the A2L file after performing a software update on the ECU.

Please note that only the ASAP2 Updater tool comes with the option .AMD/XCP.Vector offers other tools for creating and merging A2L files, but these are not included with the option.

- The ASAP2 Updater can be launched via command line, so you can embed it automatically into build and update processes: <CANoe Installation>\ASAP2Updater\ASAP2Updater.EXE

- A GUI that lets you execute address updates interactively is also available: <CANoe Installation>\ASAP2Updater\ASAP2UpdaterGUI.EXE

- User manual: <CANoe Installation>\ASAP2Updater\Manual.PDF

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [AMDXCP] AMDGenericMeasurement


# Generic Measurement

.AMD/XCP » Generic Measurement

Generic measuring is a method for reading out variables from a control unit via XCP without having to perform an address update beforehand. When a connection is made with the control unit, the control unit transfers the necessary address information to CANoe which is the XCP master.

Advantage

- Address information from the control unit is always up to date and correct.

- Automatic version control in order to ensure consistency between BSW modules and generic A2L.

- Generic measuring can be combined with normal measuring (address information available in A2L).

## Background

Generic measuring was developed in order to measure important variables of the AUTOSAR Basic Software (BSW) that represent statuses or configuration settings. These variables are always the same in different control units. Generic measuring allows you to measure the status of the BSW in different control units using similar A2L files.

## Requirements

- XCP driver in the control unit must support generic measuring

- A2L file with unique identifiers for generic measuring must be available

## Functionality

In the A2L file the generic measurement objects are provided with unique identifiers. When a connection to the control unit is established, these identifiers are requested. The control unit then returns the addresses for all identifiers available in the control unit. Afterwards, the usual XCP methods can be used for measuring and calibrating.

[...truncated for brevity...]

# Use Cases

.AMD/XCP » Use Cases

## Typical use cases for AMD

- Check the states of OS Tasks, Runnables, BSW, RTE and SWCs in the context of automated tests

- Identifying run time errors in the control unit

- Check the control unit configuration and version

### [AMDXCP] AMDXCP


# Option AMD/XCP

Standards – Bus Systems, Protocols, Databases » Option AMD/XCP

| X Calibration Protocol (XCP) / CAN Calibration Protocol (CCP) XCP and CCP are widely used calibration and measurement protocols that are standardized by the ASAM Working Group. XCP can be used on different bus systems, while CCP can be used only on CAN. Option AMD/XCP enables access to ECU-internal values and enables their usage in special test cases. In CANoe, ECU-internal signals are mapped as system variables and are thus available in all analysis windows, in CAPL, and in the Test Feature Set. CANoe acts as an XCP or CCP Master and can communicate simultaneously with several slaves. For Analysis you can decode XCP/CCP messages via the Protocol Observer. Supported Protocols and Bus Systems CCP XCP on CAN XCP on CAN FD XCP on Ethernet XCP on FlexRay XCP on LIN For CCP/XCP on CAN, XCP on FlexRay and XCP on LIN the respective bus options are required. | AUTOSAR Monitoring and Debugging (AMD) Effective with release 10 the Vector MICROSAR AUTOSAR stack offers the generation of A2L files for the BSW modules (Basic Software Components) and the SWC (Software Components) and thereby provides the symbolic information. You can read internal states and data flow out of a control unit and analyze it together with bus data. Option AMD/XCP uses the established XCP and CCP protocols to read the data from the control unit. |

| Quick Access New Features CAPL Reference Sample Configurations Feature Matrix FA

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [AMDXCP] HowToAddXCPCCPInTestAutomation


# How to Integrate XCP/CCP in your Test Automation

.AMD/XCP » How To » How to integrate XCP/CCP in your Test Automation

## Access to XCP Parameter Values during the Test

The XCP parameters are made available as system variables in CANoe. You can access the values using the standard system variable API in CANoe. An download in the control unit occurs on each write access.

## Changing the Configuration during the Test

You have the option of using the XCP CAPL functions to call XCP commands from CAPL test cases to control and configure the XCP connection. With CAPL functions xcpConnect, xcpDisconnect, and xcpIsconnected, you can establish and cancel the XCP connection or check whether the connection still exists during a test case.

With xcpActivate and xcpDeactivate, you can activate and deactivate individual measurement parameters in order, for example, to reduce the bus load and to read only the appropriate parameters for the test case.

With xcpUpload, you can trigger individual parameter updates in order, for example, to read a variable only once.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [AMDXCP] HowToDisplayChangeECUInternValues


# How to Display and Change ECU-internal Values in CANoe

.AMD/XCP » How To » How to Display and Change ECU-internal Values in CANoe

## Connection to CANoe

XCP/CCP parameters are accessible in CANoe via system variables. They are automatically created by the XCP/CCP configuration and use the reserved namespaces XCP and CCP. For each configured device a sub-namespace is created within the XCP or CCP namespace depending on the protocol type.

|  | Example CAPL sample for XCP system variables: @XCP::XcpSim::a0 = 4; XCP device 'XcpSim' with parameter a0 |

This means that the necessary system variables are available after creating the device and some parameters in the XCP/CCP Window. Thus you automatically have all means of the Test Feature Set that are available for system variables also available for parameters from XCP or CCP devices.

|  | Note System variables in CANoe must be valid CAPL identifiers and must not contain any periods.If parameter names in the A2L file contain periods or brackets, these are replaced by underscores for the system variable name, e.g., Wheel.FR -> Wheel_FR. |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [AMDXCP] ISystemDebugger


# iSYSTEM Debugger

.AMD/XCP » iSYSTEM Debugger

CANoe .AMD/XCP can use a hardware debugger from iSYSTEM to access an ECU. For this you have to activate the XCP plug-in in the winIDEA software (winIDEA 2012, version 9.12.78 or newer).

CANoe can control the debugger by using a local XCPonTCP connection. For this you need an A2L file that contains the parameters you want to measure.

The debugger implements an XCP slave, that can report modifications to variables to CANoe using the following DAQ events:

| DAQ Event Name | DAQ Event Number | Max. Sampling Rate [µs] |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Max_rate | 0 | Maximal, typically: 100 |
| 1 ms | 1 | 1'000 |
| 10 ms | 2 | 10'000 |
| 100 ms | 3 | 100'000 |
| 1 s | 4 | 1'000'000 |
| User defined | 5…255 | Maximal, typically: 100 |

The time stamps of the DAQ event of the debugger are evaluated by CANoe if the requirements are met in the A2L.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [AMDXCP] XCPCCPOverview


# XCP and CCP Functionality in CANoe

.AMD/XCP » XCP and CCP Functionality in CANoe

There are two options for linking XCP/CCP in CANoe:

## CANoe Option .AMD/XCP

- The XCP protocol (XCP on CAN, XCP on CAN FD, TCP, UDP, FlexRay and LIN) and the CCP protocol are supported. Here:

- Values can be written.

- Values can be read out via request/response. The cyclical polling time can be configured separately for each parameter.

- Values can be read out via the DAQ measurement (Data Acquisition Mode, free-running measurement data acquisition). The measurement mode can be configured separately for each parameter.Not available for XCP on LIN.

- Time stamps are used for the parameters measured via DAQ

- Real-time-capable coupling – The reading out and writing of values occur directly in CANoe. CANoe represents the XCP master here.

- The distributed mode and VN8900 are supported.

An A2L file is configured in CANoe for the .XCP option. The XCP or CCP parameters required during measurement in CANoe must be configured beforehand in the parameter list. A system variable for read and write operations are automatically available for every configured parameter. It can be used in test modules as usual. No CAPL code whatsoever is required.

The focus of the .XCP option is the testing of control units. Scalar value types (e.g. integer or float ), two-dimensional value blocks (arrays) and maps and curves are supported here.

|  | Note All value blocks are represented as one-dimensional ar

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [AMDXCP] XCPConfiguration


# XCP Configuration

.AMD/XCP » XCP/CCP Window » Configuration

All devices contained in this configuration that are configured for communication via XCP or CCP are displayed on the left side in the XCP/CCP Window.The respective settings/parameters are displayed on the right side.

## Add Device

To add another device you need a valid A2L file. You can open this file with in the toolbar.Alternatively, you can also import the entire XCP device configuration for an ECU. Select therefore the file type XCP Configuration (XCPCFG).

If the A2L file contains both an XCP and a CCP definition you must select the protocol type, here. If only one definition exists it will be selected automatically. If the A2L file contains different transport layers, e.g., FlexRay and CAN, you must select a transport layer. This selection cannot be changed afterwards.

|  | Note An A2L file can be added in CANoe using drag and drop. This operation creates a device and opens the XCP/CCP Window. |

Context Menu of the Device

Activate/Deactivate Device Activates/Deactivates the device for the following measurement. If the device is completely deactivated, you cannot re-activate it during the measurement; also XCP CAPL commands won't re-activate it.

Delete Device Deletes an device from the configuration. At the same time all system variables assigned to the device are also deleted.

Import Device Settings from CANape Imports CANape project files (CANape.ini) to take over XCP/CCP settings relevant for CAN

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [AMDXCP] XCPDeviceConfigurationDialog


# Device Configuration

.AMD/XCP » XCP/CCP Window » Device Configuration

If you have selected a device in the tree view on the left side, all XCP/CCP-relevant setting options are displayed, grouped according to subject, on the right side. You can change the settings or reset them to the default value with in the toolbar.

## General

- ECU Qualifier: Here you have to enter a unique ECU name. With this name the ECU is identified within CANoe.

- Active: Here you can activate/deactivate the device for the following measurement. If the device is completely deactivated, here, you cannot re-activate it during the measurement; also XCP CAPL commands won't re-activate it.You can also activate/deactivate the device via the context menu.

- Observer: Here you can activate/deactivate the protocol observer.

- Network: Select the network to which the ECU is connected.

- CAN FD: If the channel is operated in CAN FD mode, you can set here whether CAN or CAN FD messages are used for the XCP communication.

- Use Bitrate Switch: Here you can set whether the higher data rate should be used for CAN FD messages.

- CCP Version: Here you can switch between the CCP versions 2.0 and 2.1.

- TCP/IP Stack: Here you can select from a list of all TCP/IP stacks of the Ethernet simulation nodes via which the XCP connection should be established.

- Protocol: Here the protocol is displayed.In case of XCP on CAN, you can activate the CAN FD function and after that also the Use BRS check box for the CA

### [AMDXCP] XCPFAQ


# FAQ

.AMD/XCP » FAQ

|  | FAQ — General hints Using CANoe’s XCP option customers have regularly problems with the A2L file.In most of the cases the used A2L file does not fit to the XCP_SLAVE (ECU), because the file is not up to date/does not contain all required information.CANoe requires a 100% appropriate A2L file; CANape does not. CANape trusts the parameters and commands of the ECU. This means, (almost) any A2L file can be used in CANape and missing parameters and commands will be added automatically by CANape. So CANape can work with incomplete A2L files. Please consider to use an A2L file which fits to the ECU 100%. |

|  | FAQ — Not supported A2L parameters for CCP Following parameters are not supported in CANoe: ADDRESS_EXTENSION ACTION_SERVICE |

|  | FAQ — The Seed & Key DLL could not be loaded Cause: The DLL was linked with another DLL or a manifest, which could not be found on the target system.Solution: Link the DLL statically, do not create a manifest (Visual Studio settings under Linker and General). Cause: The DLL does not correspond to the checksum calculation API according to the ASAM Standard.Solution: Adapt the DLL according to the ASAM Standard. Cause: The DLL is an old checksum-DLL or an incompatible DLL.Solution: Old checksum-DLLs or incompatible DLLs are not supported. Cause: Inside the DLL a crash occurs. Due to this CANoe cannot load the DLL.Solution: A measurement is not possible with this DLLs. |

|  | FAQ — Upload command is locked (Seed & Key

### [AMDXCP] XCPMapsCurves


# Example for Maps & Curves

.AMD/XCP » Example for Maps and Curves

In CANoe the data types Maps and Curves are represented via Structs system variables. Each axis/dimension is assigned to a struct member.For maps maximum 3 dimensions are supported!

Maps and Curves are available since CANoe version 8.0 SP4.

|  | Example 1 KL2 is a Curve with 2x Shared Axis. The system variable gets 2 fields. The first field contains the interpolation points of the axis and the second field contains the data; see the following figure from the Symbol Explorer. Each axis/data field is defined as Array of Doubles. In the Trace Window the fields of the system variables are displayed one below the other. Code Example for an Upload with a CAPL Test Node in the Simulation Setup In the following example the Curve KL2 is read. For each axis or data array the values must be read in Array format. testcase TC_Upload_KL2(){ int i; double dataReceived[8]; int failed = RESULT_OK; sysGetVariableFloatArray("", "XCP::ECU_2::KL2.Curve2", dataReceived, 8); failed |= dataReceived[0] != 11; failed |= dataReceived[1] != 12; failed |= dataReceived[2] != 13; failed |= dataReceived[3] != 14; failed |= dataReceived[4] != 15; failed |= dataReceived[5] != 16; failed |= dataReceived[6] != 18; failed |= dataReceived[7] != 22; if(failed == RESULT_FAILED) { testStepFail("TC_Download_KL2: Wrong data received from XCP::ECU_2::KL2.Curve2."); return; } sysGetVariableFloatArray("", "XCP::ECU_2::KL2.Data", dataReceived, 8); fai

### [AMDXCP] XCPObserver


# Observer

.AMD/XCP » Observer

The Observer decodes all XCP/CCP protocol messages on CAN, FlexRay*, and Ethernet* (*=requires option CANoe .FlexRay or CANoe .Ethernet). If the Observer is active, the XCP/CCP command name is displayed in the Trace Window.

Responses to a command are represented in the form Ok:Command name if successful or ERR if an error occurred (for CCP currently also Err:Command name). In addition, the command or response parameters contained are displayed as sub-lines as well as in a separate column If you select a parameter, the corresponding bytes in the data column are highlighted.

XCP Configuration

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [AMDXCP] XCPonEthernet


# XCP on Ethernet

.AMD/XCP » XCP on Ethernet

CANoe .AMD/XCP supports XCP communication via UDP and TCP. This is possible both with and without the CANoe .Ethernet option. You can use the VN5610 or an Ethernet adapter of the PC as the network interface. Note that the Ethernet adapter of the RT computer is used in distributed mode because the real-time simulation is executed there. Analogously, when VN8900 is used, note that its Ethernet adapter will be used.

Below is a description of all variants with the relevant possible settings.

## CANoe .AMD/XCP without Option .Ethernet

## Ethernet Adapter of the Computer

The correct adapter is selected automatically via the Windows TCP/IP configuration. From the destination address, which is set in the XCP/CCP Window for the device, Windows determines the adapter that can communicate with the same subnet:

In this case, an adapter that is connected to subnet 192.168.1.* is searched for. If the ECU is correctly connected, you must check the Windows network settings if no communication is possible.

## Ethernet Adapter of the Computer in Distributed Mode

If CANoe is being used in distributed mode, the network settings of the RT computer are relevant and not those of the user computer. Use a remote desktop connection to check how the Windows network settings are configured. Ensure that the ECU is connected to the Ethernet adapter of the RT computer.

## VN8900

If you are using a VN8900, the ECU must be connected to its Ethernet ada

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [AMDXCP] XCPonFlexRay


# XCP on FlexRay

AMD/XCP » XCP on FlexRay

## Supported XCP Types in CANoe

CANoe uses both frames and PDUs, which must be defined in an AUTOSAR or FIBEX database, for the transmission of XCP data.

- In AUTOSAR databases that contain PDUs, only PDUs of the following type are used:

- XCP-PDU

- For databases that only contain frames, the following frame types are taken into account:

- XCP_PRE_CONFIGURED

- XCP_RUNTIME_CONFIGURED

In the static part (static part of schedule), only XCP_PRE_CONFIGURED types are used. The following rules apply to these:

- Every XCP slave has its exclusive PDUs

- No reconfiguration during runtime is possible

- Unique master <> slave assignment

- No slot multiplexing

In the dynamic part (dynamic part of schedule), both types are used. The following rules apply to these:

- XCP_PRE_CONFIGURED

- Preconfigured frames/PDUs

- Exclusive multiplexing > 1 per slot

- XCP_RUNTIME_CONFIGURED

- Frames/PDUs can be configured during runtime

- Initialization by the master

## Definition

This XCP type is defined differently depending on the network description format. For AUTOSAR files, the type is described by the <CATEGORY> XML element:

For FIBEX files with Version 3 and higher, the type is defined in the <PDU-TYPE> element:

For older FIBEX files in 2.x format, the type is defined in the <FRAME-TYPE> element:

## CANoe configuration

For an XCP measurement to be possible, the following requirements must be met:

- The name of the file (green mar

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [AMDXCP] XCPRequirementsDAQMeasurement


# Requirements for DAQ Measurement

.AMD/XCP » Requirements for DAQ Measurement

The XCP slave implementation in the control unit must support DAQ (Data Aquisition), and the A2L file must be appropriate for the control unit.

|  | Note Currently, static and dynamic DAQ lists are supported. To use dynamic DAQ lists with XCP, the ECU must implement the optional XCP commands for dynamic DAQ lists (see XCP Spec v1.0, http://www.asam.net or http://www.vector-worldwide.com/downloads/drivers/xcp.EXE – here you will find implementation examples (XCP and CCP) relating to ECUs and specification). |
