### [AMDXCP] XCPRequirementsECUTimeStampMeasurement


# Requirements for a Measurement with ECU Time Stamps

.AMD/XCP » Requirements for a Measurement with ECU Time Stamps

To use the ECU time stamp in the measurement, the control unit must support the sending of time stamps. The A2L file must contain the corresponding settings.

|  | Note CANoe .AMD/XCP does not provide the option of reading out the DAQ time stamp configurations from the control unit. The time stamp configurations must be configured correctly in the A2L file. |

|  | A2L Example for Time Stamp Configuration See also xcpSim.A2l: /begin TIMESTAMP_SUPPORTED 0x000A SIZE_DWORD UNIT_10US/end TIMESTAMP_SUPPORTED |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |

| Icon | Function |
| --- | --- |
|  | Adds an ECU to the configuration.You select an A2L file for this. |
|  | Deletes an ECU from the configuration.At the same time all system variables assigned to the device are also deleted. |

### [Analysis] AnalysisConcept


# Analysis with CANoe | Concept

CANoe » Analysis » Concept

|  | Glossary: »Analysis Displaying, interpreting, recording, and replaying of data (bus data, variables, and diagnostic parameters). |

| Overview |
| --- |
| Analysis in CANoe is based on data flow from the data source to the display or recording, in which the data can be additionally processed. The data flow is represented graphically in the Measurement Setup and can be configured there. |
| Receiving Data |
| Online or offline analysis is possible.The online data source is the simulated bus or the real bus that is connected by hardware (e.g., CANcardXL).The offline data source is a file containing recorded data. |
| Processing Data |
| To provide a clear representation of data, filters can be used to specify which data are to be allowed and which are to be explicitly filtered out. Filters can be active during or after the measurement and can filter data ranging from individual signals to channels of a whole bus system.Filtering is not necessary in some analysis windows, because only configured data are displayed there. Your own CAPL programs or MATLAB models can also be used for analysis functions. For example, a CAPL block can be used to filter data or to perform various arithmetic operations. Note Filters in the Measurement Setup Filters in the Measurement Setup are tailored to specific (superordinate) objects such as frames, PDUs, messages. Note that subsequent CAPL blocks usually address subobjects such as

### [Analysis] AnalysisFilterAndCAPLBlocks


# Filter and CAPL Blocks

CANoe » Analysis with CANoe » Function Overview » Filter and CAPL Blocks

The frames, which come as data streams from the network, are given as data-loaded events both to the Gatherer of the Signal Server and to the Measurement Setup of CANoe.

The purpose of the Gatherer is to read out the signal values contained in each incoming event, store them and make them centrally available to other components (e.g. the CAPL blocks in the Measurement Setup or the signal displays in panels) via the Signal Server. This allows access to the last transmitted signal value at any time in CANoe.

The events are typically passed on from block to block in the Measurement Setup. However, blocks can also omit the passing on of events to the following blocks and thus achieve a filter effect.

In CAPL programs, frame-based event handlers (e.g.: on message<message name>) refer to this event stream passed from block to block. If an event is filtered (i.e. not passed on) from a previous block, then a corresponding frame-based event handler is no longer called in the CAPL program.

Signal-based event handlers (e.g.: on signal_change <signal name>) and direct signal accesses in CAPL (e.g.: x = $<signal name>, see also page Concept of a Signal in CAPL) are operated by the central Signal Server and are therefore not affected by the filter effects of previous blocks.

A signal-based event handler is activated solely by the fact that the corresponding signal value in the Signal S

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Analysis] AnalysisLogData


# Log/Read Data

CANoe » Analysis with CANoe » Function Overview » Log/Read Data

For an analysis after the measurement, you can also record data in CANoe and replay it later.

CANoe supports two types of logging formats: message based and signal based.

## Message based

A message-based (frame based) format will store the traffic of bus systems and additional information about the bus communication. In general all bus events (messages) will be logged together with other information like statistics or even disturbances of the bus traffic. These logging files can be easily used for offline analyzing of network traffic or for replay on the bus with CANoe.

## Signal based

A signal-based format will store signal values that were extracted from messages transferred over the network. All information about the message itself and additional information about the network and communication will be lost. To log a signal-based file a description (database) for decoding the signal values has to be provided. Signal-based files cannot be directly replayed on the bus but can be easily used for signal analyzing in CANoe, CANape or third party tools.

| Logging Data | Reading In Data |
| --- | --- |
| Logging files in the Measurement Setup You can record the data stream in a logging file in the Measurement Setup. The file can be saved in ASCI, BLF or MDF format. For MOST other file formats are available.The logging file is represented in the Measurement Setup by a folder icon.You can integr

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Analysis] AnalysisReduceData


# Reduce Data

CANoe » Analysis with CANoe » Function Overview » Reduce Data

For a clear representation of data, you have the following options in CANoe:

| Filter in Measurement Setup |
| --- |
| In the Measurement Setup, you have various filters available that you use to specify which data are to be allowed in the analysis window and which data are to be explicitly blocked. For example, you can reduce your data by individual signals or by the data stream from whole channels. All filters can be used as stop and Pass Filter: Stop filter: The configured data are filtered out from the data stream; all other data are allowed by the filter. Pass filter: Only configured data are allowed by the filter; any other data are filtered out. Filter Overview of Measurement Setup Note Filter can be inserted in the analysis area and the real-time area. In the real-time area they optimize the throughput. Screenshot Short Description Channel Filter With a Channel Filter it is possible to completely block or open the channels of all registered bus systems (CAN, LIN, MOST, FlexRay). The Channel Filter appears in the Measurement Setup as a small block with a number of red and green lines. These lines symbolize the individual channels of a bus system and their momentary states. A green line means an open channel, while a broken red line indicates a blocked channel. Event Filter CAN Filter The CAN Filter lets you filter CAN messages. It has a number of advantages over standard filter: Special CAN

# CAPL Support

ARINC-825 » CAPL Support

Since ARINC-825 uses CAN for communication, all CAPL functions that are available for CAN can be used here.

In order to simplify access to parts of the CAN identifier in ARINC-825, an include file can be linked by the instruction:

includes { #include "CANaero/A825_3.cin"}

The file provides help functions for accessing parts of the CAN identifier and help functions for the calculation of the MIC.

### [ARINC825] ARINC825Concept


# ARINC-825 | Concept

ARINC-825 » Concept

| Overview |
| --- |
| CANoe ARINC-825 supports CAN-based protocols for avionics. The feature set is integrated in Option CAN. It supports the basic concepts of CANoe and extends them with avionics-specific functions. |
| Analysis |
| ARINC-825 supplements CANoe by adding analysis capabilities for the CAN-based protocols ARINC 825 (General Standardization of CAN) ARINC 826 (Data Loader) When these protocols are used in a CAN network, the protocol information can be shown decoded in the Trace Window. The individual bit fields are interpreted within the CAN identifier. This makes it much easier to obtain a clear overview in analysis. In addition, the CAN messages can be described in detail in a database. This makes it possible to access symbolic identifiers for messages and their contents within CANoe. |

### [ARINC825] ARINC825DBSupport


# Database Support

ARINC-825 » Database Support

CAN messages are described in a database (DBC format). The application data are then available as signals within <CANwin>. In addition, all network information can be represented symbolically.

The label is used as a unique key within the database. A new database can be created with CANdb++. The template CANaero_ARINC825.dbc must be used for this. Alternatively the Aerospace Message Editor may be used.

## Network Attributes

The database contains a network object that is described using the following attributes. These attributes are created automatically.

| Name | Data Type | Values | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| ProtocolType | String | Default: "ARINC825V3" The following values are allowed: "ARINC825" (specification prior to version 3) "ARINC825V3" (specification according to version 3) | This attribute value makes sure that a DBC file is interpreted as an ARINC825 network description. |
| A825RCIBitSize | Integer | 0…2 (default = 2) 0 = no RCI field used | This value defines the size of the RCI bit area for one-to-many (LCC = {EEC, NOC, UDC, FMC}) and peer-to-peer (LCC = {NSC, TMC}) messages. |

## Node Attributes

An attribute is also created for a node object.

| Name | Data Type | Values | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| CanEquipment | String | Default: "<unknown>" | The equipment name can be specified by the user. This name is displayed in the Equipment column in the Trace Window. Multiple nodes

|  | Definitions Client FID Client Function Code Identifier (Bit 19..25) Destination Address Unique address of the destination node Destination Port ID Intended port on destination side DOC Data Object Code (ARINC-825 CAN identifier: bit 2..15) FSB Functional Status Bit (bit 18) LCC Logical Channel Control (ARINC-825 CAN identifier: bit 26..28) Number Displayed Name Description 0 EEC Exception Event Channel 1 Reserved Reserved 2 NOC Normal Operation Channel 3 DMC Directed Message Channel 4 NSC Node Service Channel 5 UDC User-defined channel 6 TMC Test and Maintenance Channel 7 FMC CAN Base Frame Migration Channel LCL Local flag (bit 17) MIC Message Integrity Check (ARINC-825) NID Node ID (bit 0..15) The node ID combines the fields ServerFID, SID and RCI. PHSM Periodic Health Status Message (ARINC-825) PVT Private flag (bit 16) RCI Redundancy Channel Identifier (bit 0..1) SDL Software Data Loading (ARINC-826) Server FID Server Function Code Identifier (bit 9..15) SID Server Identifier (bit 2..8) SMT Service Message Type (Bit 18) Source Address Unique address of the source node Source Port ID Ephemeral port of the source node Source FID Source Function Code Identifier (bit 19..25) WKP Well Known Port | Client FID | Client Function Code Identifier (Bit 19..25) | Destination Address | Unique address of the destination node | Destination Port ID | Intended port on destination side

### [CAN] CAN


# Option CAN

Standards – Bus Systems, Protocols, Databases » Option CAN

|  | CAN (Controller Area Network) was developed by the Robert Bosch GmbH in the early eighties and was internationally standardized (ISO 11898) in 1994. CAN was specifically developed for the serial data exchange between electronic control units in automobiles but is also used for the realization of industrial microcontroller networks. CAN FD is a further development of the CAN bus system. It supports higher data rates (max. 8 Mbit/s) and up to 64 data bytes. Application area: Automotive engineering, automation engineering. |

### [CAN] CANandCANfdProtocol


# Protocol Specific Peculiarities (CAN/CAN FD)

CAN » Protocol Specific Peculiarities (CAN/CAN FD)

## Protocol-Specific Properties (CAN and CAN FD)

## What is the 'Bus Integration State'?

The CAN controller is in this state in the following situations:

- Start of communication

- After a Bus Off Recovery

- After a Protocol Exception Event

The CAN controller does not participate in communication in this state.

The CAN controller becomes active again in the network only when it has detected the Bus Idle condition.

## Where are the Time Stamps Recorded for Frames of CAN or CAN FD?

- For a received frame, the time stamp is recorded after the sixth EOF bit. For a recipient, a frame is valid when no error has occurred up to this position.

- For a sent frame, the time stamp is recorded after the seventh EOF bit. For a sender, a frame is valid when no error has occurred up to this position.

The different position for acceptance of a frame should ensure that a receive node can still signal an error in the sixth EOF bit to the sender in the seventh EOF bit. This causes the sender to send the frame again.

## How is the Frame Length Calculated?

The frame length is determined from the bit fields of the frame, the Stuff bits and the Intermission field (IFS). The IFS is added to the frame length because no other transmission can be started at this point. The IFS thus counts at the moment in which the network is occupied. (status Bus busy). This also affects the calculation of 

[...truncated for brevity...]

The Simulation Setup Assistant opens for creating a CANoe configuration. Select your database using the Database button. Database and bus are displayed. Go to the next page with Next >. From the available network nodes of the database, select the ones that are to be mapped in the CANoe configuration. In the simplest case, these are all nodes of the database. Select the desired nodes and transfer them with the arrow buttons. Go to the next page with Next >. You must now assign the channel to the configuration. Assign CAN channel 1 to the network by selecting CAN 1 and assigning with the arrow keys. Go to the next page with Next >. The result of the configuration is n

### [CAN] CANcreateDatabase


# Create CAN Database (CAN Communication Matrix)

CAN » Database (Communication Matrix)

The use of a CAN database is recommended for the development, analysis, and testing of CAN networks. This database contains the network architecture and the communication data of the distributed network nodes. For sending, it is customary to use an Interaction Layer (IL), which takes over the sending of frames based on the attributes of network nodes, frames, or signals in the communication matrix.

A CAN database is created in accordance with the CAN specifications with the following editors of Vector Informatik GmbH:

## AUTOSAR Explorer (file format ARXML)

Start CANoe and open the AUTOSAR Explorer using the Tools Ribbon Tab. This editor provides the following options:

- Create AUTOSAR CAN database

- View and analyze database

- Function for validation of the communication matrix according to the AUTOSAR schema:Menu path View|Schema Validation

|  | Cross Reference You can find more detailed information in the help of AUTOSAR Explorer. |

## CANdb++ Editor Standard (file format DBC)

Start CANoe and open CANdb++ using the Tools Ribbon Tab. This editor provides the following options:

- Create database

- Consistency check of certain CAN properties for CAN communication

- Menu path Options|Settings|Consistency Check

- Menu path File|Consistency Check

### [CANopen/CANopenTour] tourOverview


# Option CANoe.CANopen Tour

CANopen » Tour

If you are working with the CANopen option for the first time, the CANoe.CANopen Tour described in this section will help you. As part of the tour a CANopen network should be set up that comprises a CANopen master and two CANopen slaves. The master should monitor the slaves during ongoing operation by means of Node Guarding. The slaves represent two I/O devices with established communication relations. Whether and how the slaves are configured by the master during "boot-up" should not be relevant to this example.

You will now accomplish the following tasks step by step:

- Projecting the Network

- Simulation Creation

- Simulation

- Extension of the Configuration

Click Next to start the tour.

### [CANopen/CANopenTour] tourProjectingConnections


# Configure Process Data Objects

CANopen » Tour » Projecting » Configure Process Data Objects

Back

You will now set up communication relations between the slaves in the network. Select node 11 with the right mouse button and select the command Graphic Connection in the context menu. Then mark the second slave (ID 12) by selecting it with the left mouse key.

|  | Note In the dialog Graphical Connection, process data can be linked at will. To do this, select a piece of process data with the left mouse button. If a matching object for this object is present in the communication partner, it is displayed in black letters. Non-matching objects are inactive. |

In the filter area activate the filter Left node list and deselect the entries ID1 - Master and ID12 - I/O-Device 2. Analog to this activate the filter Right node list and deselect the entries ID1 - Master and ID11 - I/O-Device 1. Deselect the filter for Process data direction too.

Now select object 6000 Read Input 8-Bit in the left node list and open this object with a double click. Double click the sub object 6000sub01 Read Input 8-Bit1 and move the mouse cursor to object 6200 Write Output 8-Bit in the right node list. After a few seconds the object opens and you can select sub object 6200-2: Write Output 8-Bit1 as connection partner.

|  | Note Some objects are now marked with an exclamation mark. The reason for this is that node 12 does not support variable mapping. For the marked objects communication relations hav

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [CANopen/CANopenTour] tourProjectingGuarding


# Setu Up "Node Guarding"

CANopen » Tour » Projecting » Setu Up "Node Guarding"

Back

To set up the Node Guarding, select the master-enabled node Master with the right mouse button. Select the command Device Access from the context menu. In the Device Access dialog activate the CANopen Manager entry in the tree.

Proceed as follows to set up the Node Guarding:

| Dialog Element | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Button [Detail] | Opens a dialog to configure the object NMTStartup (1F80h). Make sure that both the control field Bit 0 and the control field Bit 1 have been activated. Then close the dialog via the [OK] button. |
| Button [Global] | Opens a dialog to configure the object Network List (1F81h). Activate the control field Bit 0. Then enter a Guard Time of 500ms and a Retry Factor of 3. Close the dialog via the [OK] button. |

Once these settings have been made, your entries are displayed in the list field Slave Assignment. Accept this parameter by activating the [Apply to Slaves] button. The values are then accepted in the object directories of the slaves. Settings are assigned to the CANopen master by clicking the [Übernehmen] button. Finally close the Device Access dialog using the [OK] button.

Next

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [CANopen/CANopenTour] tourProjectingNetworkStructure


# Specifying the Network Structure

CANopen » Tour » Projecting » Specifying the Network Structure

Back

First of all, the CANopen master should be set up. After a right click on the network nodes in the topology window, a context menu appears from which you can select the command Configuration. Make the following settings in the Node Configuration dialog:

| Dialog Element | Description | Value |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Input field Name | Enter the node name | Master |
| Input field Node-ID | Enter the network-wide clear node ID | 1 |

Then click the [Device Type…] button. The dialog to select an EDS file is opened. An EDS file is assigned to the network node via this dialog. Select the file vgenmst1.eds in the list field. Before this file is assigned to the network node, it should be checked by the EDS checker integrated in ProCANopen. To do this, click the [Check EDS] button. After checking, the column Chk should contain the value Ok. This means that the selected EDS file does not contain any warnings or errors.

The EDS file is accepted by clicking the [OK] button. Then close the node configuration dialog by clicking the [OK] button.

Now add two more nodes to the network using the command Edit | New Node. These network nodes should be configured in a similar way to the CANopen master. Please use the following values to configure the network nodes:

### [CANopen/CANopenTour] tourSimulation


# Simulation

CANopen » Tour » Simulation

Back

The simulation in CANoe can be started immediately. For this, use the Home ribbon tab|Start. The network nodes are switched to the "Operational" state by the master on the basis of the configuration created in ProCANopen and monitored by Node Guarding (have a look at the Trace Window).

The CANoe configuration contains three panels by means of which a test of the process data communication can take place. To do this, activate a switch on the panel of the node 11(I_O_Device_1). After a switch has been clicked, the lamp on the panel of node 12 (I_O_Device_2) is set according to the mapping made. The relevant PDO is displayed in the Trace Window.

This use case should illustrate how simply and quickly CANopen networks can be developed in ProCANopen and simulated using CANoe. If the complexity of the networks increases due to a larger number of devices, only the work required to configure the network grows. The fully automated generation process to create the simulation data is started.

Next

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [CANopen/CANopenTour] tourSimulationCreation


# Simulation Creation

CANopen » Tour » Simulation Creation

Back

For the network nodes in the project you have now made all of the settings that affect the framework conditions. If the hardware were physically available the configuration data would now be downloaded by ProCANopen . If the devices - as in this example - are not available, the network can be simulated by CANoe. CAPL programs and panels are needed for this that have to be integrated in the simulation environment of CANoe. The time-consuming compilation of CAPL models and node-specific panels is performed by the ProCANopen simulation generator.

### [CANopen/Nodelayer] callbackFunctions


# Event Functions (deprecated)

CANopen » CANopen Node Layer » Event Functions

The name of all event functions that are called from the node layer begins with the prefix coOn. The name and the signature of these functions must be adhered to. Otherwise errors may result. The node layer outputs a warning in the Write Window on measurement start if an event function is not defined.

The event functions serve the node layer in order to communicate to the CAPL program events that occur asynchronously. Any functions can be called in the event functions.

CANopen Node Layer CAPL Functions: Callbacks

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [CANopen/Nodelayer] canoeRTServer


# CANoe RT Server (deprecated)

CANopen » CANopen Node Layer » CANoe RT Server

To uncouple the real-time operation CANoe is divided into two parts.

- The simulation of the model is executed with the RT server, which is installed on a separate computer with Windows operating system.

- The evaluation part is typically executed on the user computer.

Furthermore CANoe is executed as distributed application if a VN8900 hardware interface or a VT system with a real-time module (VT6000 family) is used.

## Specifics on Generated CANopen Simulation Models

If you use CANopen simulation models that have been generated with the option CANoe.CANopen, you have to consider the following fact: depending on the configuration and functional range, the CANopen simulation models use additional files that have been made known to CANoe RT. For this CANoe uses a mechanism of so called User Files.

The following files must be made known to provide the correct function.

- DCF/EDS files

These files describe the object dictionary of the simulated CANopen model and are necessary to use the CAPL function coODCreateFromFile(). This function is used per default on generating the simulation models. ProCANopen stores these files in the directory of the corresponding ProCANopen project. The file extension is dcf.

- non volatile storage of configuration data of simulated nodes

The functionality of the non-volatile storage of configuration data of CANopen devices is defined in standard CiA® DSP301, v

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [CANopen/Nodelayer] CANopenNLOverview


# CANopen Node Layer (deprecated)

CANopen » Node Layer

Each node in the Simulation Setup can be assigned a node layer. The node layer makes the following functionality available to the node via function calls.

|  | Note The CANopen node layer is still supported for capability with CANoe versions up to and including 9.0 SP6. It was replaced by the CANopen Simulation Concept that is supported starting with CANoe 10.0. |

## Handling of the SDO Protocol as SDO Client

The node can access the object dictionary entries of other nodes on the bus. The user does not need to have any detailed protocol knowledge since the protocol is executed completely transparently via the node layer.

## Realization of the Object Dictionary

The Object Dictionary of the node is also realized in the node layer. The entries in the object dictionary can be created via a single function call. The user must only specify the EDS file or the DCF on the basis of which the object dictionary is created. Access from outside to the object dictionary is answered automatically by the SDO server integrated into the node layer. Each object dictionary entry can be assigned an Environment Variable. Via this variable, access to the current object value is possible.

- Offline Access This access mode is used as long as the node has not yet been started with coStartUp or after it has been stopped again with coShutDown. In this mode, the created objects can be accessed without limitation with the coSet.../coGet... fu

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [CANopen/Nodelayer] coNLinclude


# Include the CANopen Node Layer (deprecated)

CANopen » CANopen Node Layer » Include the CANopen Node Layer

So that a node can use the CANopen node layer, some settings must be made in the Simulation Setup and in the database.

- A node attribute with the name NodelayerModules of the type String must be added to the database.

- A node attribute with the name CO_ID of the type Integer must be added to the database.

- A node must be added to the database. For this node, the attribute NodelayerModules must be set to CANopenNL.DLL and the attribute CO_ID to the desired node ID.

- A node must be added to the simulation design. On the configuration dialog, the node from the database must be assigned.

Which node layers are actually used by a node can be checked in CANoe on the node configuration dialog on page Module.

|  | Note The CANopen node layer is still supported for capability with CANoe versions up to and including 9.0 SP6. It was replaced by the CANopen Simulation Concept that is supported starting with CANoe 10.0. |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [CANopen/Nodelayer] coNLinit


# Initialize the CANopen Node Layer (deprecated)

CANopen » CANopen Node Layer » Initialize the CANopen Node Layer

To initialize the node layer follow the next steps:

- Create the object dictionary: First an object dictionary must be created. This can happen in the CAPL functions on preStart or onStart

- Connect the object dictionary entries with environment variables: The entries in the object dictionary can be connected with environment variables with the CAPL call coODConnectEnvVar. Thus, for example, process data can be changed or output via a panel.

- Start the CANopen functionality: The CANopen functionality of the node layer can be started with the function coStartUp.

|  | Caution! After the call of the function coStartUp the node needs some time in order to start and be initialized completely. The start is completed if the event function coOnNodeChangedState was called with the value pre-operational! Not till then all data are completely initialized. |

|  | Note The CANopen node layer is still supported for capability with CANoe versions up to and including 9.0 SP6. It was replaced by the CANopen Simulation Concept that is supported starting with CANoe 10.0. |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [CANopen/Nodelayer] errorHandling


# Error Handling (deprecated)

CANopen » CANopen Node Layer » Error Handling

For the development of the interfaces, attention was paid to make sure that the error handling is the same for all functions. Each function except for the event functions has as last parameter a field of the type dword. The error code of the function is stored in index 0 of this field. This way it is possible for functions that return a value (e.g. coGetNodeId) to return the error code simultaneously.

The result of the last function call is stored internally and can be called up with coGetLastError. Furthermore, with this function the error can be read out as a string in plain text. If after a flawed function call there is a successful call, the error is deleted by the successful second call.

Many functions of the node layer work asynchronously. This means, the successful call of a function frequently does not mean simultaneously a successful execution of the action. For example, the error of a SDO transfer to another node is not reported directly with the return of the function coUpload. If an error should occur during the transfer, it is communicated with the function coOnError.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [CANopen/Nodelayer] layersettingservice


# Layer Setting Services (deprecated)

CANopen » CANopen Node Layer » Layer Setting Services

The layer setting services offer the opportunity to query the parameter node ID and baud rate via the bus and naturally also to change these. The implemented functionality corresponds to the functional scope of a LSS master. It must be noted that the functionality of a LSS slave is not available. Through the use of LSS it is possible to adjust a CANopen device with the functionality of a LSS slave to a network without having to use DIP switches or similar things for the specification of the baud rate and node ID.

For more detailed information about the LSS protocol, see /4/.

|  | Note A switching of the baud rate is not provided by the node layer. This setting can only be made in CANoe. |

CANopen Node Layer CAPL Functions: Layer Setting Services

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |

# Network Management (deprecated)

CANopen » CANopen Node Layer » Network Management

These functions can only be executed for a node that is also NMT master. For the functions defined here, the object 1F82 (RequestNMT) must exist in the object dictionary. The state of any other node on the network can only be returned if this node is monitored via guarding or heartbeat.

CANopen Node Layer CAPL Functions: Network Management

### [CANopen/Nodelayer] nodecontrolintro


# Node Control (deprecated)

CANopen » CANopen Node Layer » Node Control

The functions in this group handle the local control of the simulated ECU.

## Access to the Local Node State

The simulated CANopen ECU has a built-in status machine that knows the specifications of /2/ the states Operational, Pre-Operational, Reset Node, Reset Communication, and Stopped. With the functions coGetLocalState and coSetLocalState, the local state can be read and changed.

Each change of the local communication state is signaled by the event function coOnNodeChangedState. The corresponding action for this state change can be entered here.

|  | Note The CANopen node layer is still supported for capability with CANoe versions up to and including 9.0 SP6. It was replaced by the CANopen Simulation Concept that is supported starting with CANoe 10.0. |

## Start and Stop the Node Layer for a Node

After the node layer was started for a node, a boot-up is executed for this node. This boot-up can be configured largely via entries in the object dictionary. Of course these entries must already be created.

|  | Note A simulated ECU can also be started completely without object dictionary entries. In this case only the boot-up message is sent out. Access to the local object dictionary is possible, however it is always answered with an abort (object not available). |

|  | Note If no object 1200h is found at start up, the default SDO server (according to CiA301) is created. Furthermore all default SD

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [CANopen/Nodelayer/ObjectDictionary] AccessLocalOdOverview


# Access to the Local Object Dictionary (deprecated)

CANopen » CANopen Node Layer » Access to the Local Object Dictionary

Generally there are two possibilities for accessing entries in the local object dictionary:

- with the functions coODGet...()/coODGet...()

- via environment variables (putValue()/getValue()), after a connection with coODConnectEnvVar was established.

|  | Note The CANopen node layer is still supported for capability with CANoe versions up to and including 9.0 SP6. It was replaced by the CANopen Simulation Concept that is supported starting with CANoe 10.0. |

|  | Caution! In order to simplify handling for the user, the functions mentioned above work synchronously. This means, they only return if the data could be written or read. Based on the event character of CANoe, the simulation time only continues if an event occurs. Therefore, it is strongly advised that you not call these functions multiple times (e.g. in a loop) within an event, e.g. onTimer. Otherwise there can be time jumps in the simulation procedure. |

In order to simplify access to entries in the object dictionary, these can be connected with an environment variable via the function coODConnectEnvVar. Now access can occur via the functions putValue() and getValue().

|  | Caution! Environment variables must be created in a database. This database is then assigned to the CANoe configuration. For detailed information about environment variables see the CANoe online help /1/. |

A "mixing

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [CANopen/Nodelayer/ObjectDictionary] AccessOtherOdOverview


# Access to the Object Dictionary of other Nodes (deprecated)

CANopen » CANopen Node Layer » Access to the Object Dictionary of other Nodes

Frequently an application is permitted to access the object dictionary of other nodes. This is especially necessary if data should be accessed that is not mapped in a PDO (or cannot be mapped). The node layer makes a SDO client functionality available for this purpose. The user does not have to worry about the details of the SDO communication. Here the accesses always follow the same scheme:

- Read access via SDO

- Call of coUpload

- Reading out of the data with coThisGet...() in the event function coOnUploadResponse

- Write access via SDO

- Call of coDownload or coDownloadExpedited

- Successful completion of the transfer is signaled via coOnDownloadResponse

If during the transfer an error occurs, then the event handling is called in coOnError.

A SDO access to a node may only be executed if the previous SDO access is ended on the same node. Therefore it is not possible, e.g. to read out the mapping for a PDO in a loop. Several SDO accesses to various nodes can be initiated in parallel without limitations.

|  | Example int i;dword errCode[1];char errBuffer[256];dword index = 0x1600;for (i = 0; i < 9; i++) {coUpload(nodeID, index, i, 0, errCode); if (errCode[0] != 0) { coGetLastError(errBuffer, elCount(errBuffer)); write("Could not trigger SDO with coUpload...(%d): %#x - %s", nodeID, errCode[0], errBuffer); } else { write("SDO t

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [CANopen/Nodelayer/ObjectDictionary] CreationLocalOdOverview


# Generating the Local Object Dictionary (deprecated)

CANopen » CANopen Node Layer » Generating the Local Object Dictionary

The functions in this section enable the user to create an object dictionary. For a CANopen ECU, all parameters and data are present in an object dictionary. Each individual entry is addressed uniquely via an index (16 bit) and a subindex (8 bit). The significance of the individual object dictionary entries is already pre-defined via the communication profile (see /2/) and the device profile. Via PDOs, only data can be exchanged that is also present as entries in the object dictionary.

|  | Note The CANopen node layer is still supported for capability with CANoe versions up to and including 9.0 SP6. It was replaced by the CANopen Simulation Concept that is supported starting with CANoe 10.0. |

|  | Note The object dictionary can only be modified if the node was not yet started with coStartUp. |

Generally there are two possibilities for creating an object dictionary. In the simplest case, an EDS or a DCF is available for the CANopen ECU to be simulated. Then the object dictionary can be created via the function call coODCreateFromFile.

As an alternative, there is also the possibilities of creating object dictionary entries via individual function calls. When creating an entry, the following information is required:

- Index

- Subindex

- Data type of the object

- Permissible access types

- Initial value

The permissible access types are coded as

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [CANopen/Nodelayer/ObjectDictionary] ObjectDictionary


# Node Layer Object Dictionary (deprecated)

CANopen » CANopen Node Layer » Object Dictionary

The functionality of the node layer can be configured largely via existing object dictionary entries both for the master and for slaves. If entries do not exist, then functionalities can also be completely switched off. Nodes that are simulated via the node layer should naturally be as close to reality as possible. Therefore, this section describes the behavior of the node layer with respect to CANopen.

|  | Note The CANopen node layer is still supported for capability with CANoe versions up to and including 9.0 SP6. It was replaced by the CANopen Simulation Concept that is supported starting with CANoe 10.0. |

## General

The node layer can also be used if no objects are created in the object dictionary. All access to the object dictionary then returns the error "object does not exist." In most cases, at least one basic initialization of the object dictionary is necessary.

## Required Objects

Some objects are required according to /2/ in order to fulfill the CANopen conformity.

| Device Type |
| --- |
| Device type |
| Index | 0x1000 |
| Type | Unsigned32 |
| Description | This object contains the device profile number that is supported by this device. This object is required in order to support the defined boot-up in accordance with /3/. |

| Error Register |
| --- |
| General error register |
| Index | 0x1001 |
| Type | Unsigned8 |
| Description | This register depicts the 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [CANopen/Nodelayer/ObjectDictionary] ObjectDictionaryMaster


# Object Dictionary for Master (deprecated)

CANopen » CANopen Node Layer » Object Dictionary for Master

In order to configure the node layer as master, the object dictionary must also contain the following objects:

| Store Parameters (required for configuration manager) |
| --- |
| Storage of the parameters in non-volatile memory |
| Index | 0x1010 |
| Type | Array of Unsigned32 |
| Description | If the value 0x65766173 (ASCII "save") is written to a subindex, then the parameters are stored in a non-volatile memory and stored in binary fashion in a directory CO_Data in a file 'Nodename'.bin. The node layer only supports the storage of all parameters (subindex 0x01). |

| Restore Default Parameters (optional) |
| --- |
| Restore the default configuration of the node |
| Index | 0x1011 |
| Type | Array of Unsigned32 |
| Description | If the value 0x64616f6c (ASCII "load") is written to a subindex, then the default parameters are restored. The node layer only supports the restoration of all parameters (subindex 0x01). |

| Concised Configuration Storage (required for configuration manager) |
| --- |
| Storage of node configurations in concised format |
| Index | 0x1F22 |
| Type | Array of Domain |
| Description | With this object, the configuration of a node can be stored in concised format. The subindex corresponds to the node ID. On boot-up, the master transmits the configuration to the respective nodes. |

| NMT Startup (required) |
| --- |
| Defines the start behavior of

| Index | 0x1017 |

| --- |

### [CANopen/OptionCANopen] applicationProfiles


# Application Profiles

CANopen » Application Profiles

Additionally to the communication profile and a various number of device profiles, CANopen describes so called application profiles. At this not only device types are considered but also applications of different devices communicating over the bus.

Some of the applications are supported with adapted or additional functions by CANoe.CANopen and ProCANopen. Details of the support you can find in the online help of ProCANopen.

The following profiles are supported explicitly:

- CiA416 Door Environment

- CiA417 Lift Control Systems

- CiA447 Car Add-on Devices

## 447 Car Add-on Devices

This application profile deals with networking of control units for taxi and cars for administration and handicapped persons. Fundamental extensions to CiA302 are:

- Pre-defined virtual devices

- The value range of the node ID is restricted from 1..16. Devices can have a fix node ID or register dynamically via the LSS FastScan mechanism.

- PDOs and mappings are pre-defined.

- 15 SDO server and clients are defined for each device. This realizes a full meshed network. The COB-IDs differ from that defined in CiA302.

### Automatically Activation of CiA447 Support in the Option CANopen:

ProCANopen allows the setting for using an application profile.

If CiA® 447 is selected, all settings for using that application profile are enabled and used for the generation of a CANoe simulation or CANoe test configuration. If CANoe.CANopen is used 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [CANopen/OptionCANopen] canopenConcept


# CANopen with CANoe | Concept

CANopen » Concept

CANopen is a CAN-based communication protocol for the networking of devices from different manufacturers. A CANopen device contains a logical CANopen node represented by a unique node ID on the network and is characterized by an object directory in which process data and parameters are organized.

In the CANopen Configuration window, you can edit the contents of the object directory of all CANopen nodes of the network simultaneously and consistently with one another. A copy of the object directory in the form of a so-called device configuration file is created for this purpose. These files are generated in the _CANopen_<Configuration Name>\<Network Name> subfolder. Using the SDO protocol (service data object), the contents of the files can be compared to the object directory of the nodes. CANoe represents the entries in the object directory using system variables in the CANopen namespace. Access from CAPL or display in the Graphics Window is possible in this way, for example.

All CANopen specific functions which can be activated in the window for CANopen nodes are based on standardized entries from the so-called communication profile area of the object directory (index 0x1000 to 0x1FFF). Their functions are described in CiA documents DS 301 and DSP 302. For example, the heartbeat cycle time for the producer is configured in object 0x1017 subindex 0. If a network is selected in the Configurations view, the heartbeat configur

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [CANopen/OptionCANopen] canopenConfigurationPDOSRDO


# PDO/SRDO Connections

CANopen » CANopen Configuration » Window Elements » PDO/SRDO Connections

Ribbon: Simulation Tab|CANopen Configuration

## Functions

The PDO/SRDO Connections page provides an overview and configuration of the data exchange in a CANopen network and offers the following functions:

- Read and write the PDO/SRDO configuration of all nodes of the network.

- Overview which Tx PDO/SRDO of a node are connected to which Rx PDO/SRDO of other nodes of the network including the communication parameter settings of the PDOs/SRDOs and the mapping objects contained in the PDOs.

- Overview, which Tx mapping objects of a node are transferred via which CAN ID to Rx mapping objects of other nodes of the network.

- Consistency check of the object mappings including the data types.

- Overview of the available PDOs/SRDOs.

- Connecting Tx and Rx PDOs/SRDOs.

- Editing the communication parameters and mapping parameters.

- Calculation of the SRDO CRC checksums.

## Window Elements

The PDO/SRDO Connections page has a separate toolbar.

## PDO/SRDO View

Displays the PDOs or SRDOs. The Valid column shows the state of the connection:

| Symbol | Description |
| --- | --- |
|  | Tx and Rx connected, Valid Flags on set on both sides. |
|  | Only Tx Valid flag is set. |
|  | Only Rx Valid flag is set. |
|  | PDO/SRDO is not connected. |

The Mapping-Status column shows whether the mapping is correct or not , or whether it was not checked due to missing entries . Details ar

### View: Configurations

In the Configurations view, you can see all CAN networks and the CANopen nodes and node IDs contained within them. You can see the transmit, receive and safety PDOs (process data objects) of a node below the node. By selecting an element, you can make changes in the Details view.

| Selected Element | Available Pages | Description |

| Network | General | Name Name of the network from the Simulation Setup or name of the CAN channel. DBC only for configured node IDs When generating the database, only CAN frames of the nodes contained within the CANopen Configuration window are taken into account. If the option is deactivated, the standardized CAN frames of the maximum possible 127 nodes are entered. DBC for extended interpretation The generated database can also be used to interpret SDO commands. If you deactivate this option, the database will be considerably smaller and load faster. Folder for DBC and DCF files In this folder, all CANopen specific files are generated, such as the database and the device configuration files. You can copy the path of the folder, but you cannot change it. |

### [CANopen/OptionCANopen] canopenDataTypeDOMAIN


# Data Type DOMAIN

CANopen » Data Type DOMAIN

The data type DOMAIN is used to manage any number of data bytes in an object directory entry. This can be, for example, a firmware update, which is first transferred to the device via SDO download and is installed during the subsequent reset of the device.

This data type cannot be covered by a conventional system variable, because the data volume may be too large to be handled efficiently in CANoe. In the CANopen Configuration window you can therefore select for each Domain Data Object whether it is linked to CAPL callbacks or files.

When a file is selected, files are automatically created with the following naming scheme:

[Node]_[ID]_[Index]_[SubIndex]_domaindata_{current|init|default}.bin

When the file current or init is overwritten, a backup with the extension *.bin_Bak is created. In the CANopen Configuration window any file can be imported as a new init file. The file current contains the last observed value of the object e.g. after an SDO upload.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |

CANopen » CANopen Database

The network attribute ProtocolType is already defined and set with value CANopen. This attribute is used to activate the CANopen specific analysis functions.

### [CANopen/OptionCANopen] configTemplates


# Configuration Templates

CANopen » Configuration Templates

CANoe offers a configuration template to support you on creating new configurations. This template already defines CANopen specific settings.

- CANopen Database is assigned to the network

- CANopen specific interpretation in the Trace Window is activated

- CANopen Scanner is available in the Measurement Setup

- Interactive Generatorblock CANopen is available in the Simulation Setup

To create a new configuration based on the CANopen configuration template follow the next steps:

|  | Step by Step Procedure Start CANoe. Select File | New configuration.... In dialog Template select CANopen Template.tcn. Confirm you selection with [OK]. Finally the CANopen base configuration is created. |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [CANopen/OptionCANopen] extensionsProCANopen


# Simulation of CANopen Nodes

CANopen » Simulation of CANopen Nodes

Within the scope of the .CANopen option, CANoe offers the opportunity to use simulations of CANopen devices. The CANopen network to be simulated is set up and configured using the CANopen Configuration window. Then simulation nodes can then be generated automatically for each device in the Simulation Setup.

## CANopen Simulation Concept

With CANoe you can simulate both individual CANopen devices and complete networks. A device configuration file (*.dcf, *.xdc) containing the current configuration is used for each simulated device. If a simulation node is assigned to a CANopen device, the simulation node behaves like a real CANopen device and sends a boot-up message at the start of a measurement, for example.

## System Variables for the Object Directory

Each CANopen device has an object directory whose content is described in the device configuration file. Each object in the object directory is represented by a system variable in CANoe. These variables are created in the reserved CANopen namespace.

The system variables are structured by namespaces as follows:

- CANopen – network name – node name.All objects in the object directory are created with a flat structure in the namespace of the node. Complex objects are created in a separate namespace, which is derived from the name of the main object. The variable name corresponds to the name of the respective object. The comment for the system variable con

[...truncated for brevity...]

The option CANopen offers the possibility to set the baud rate and the bus timing register comfortably. For this, you must select Hardware ribbon tab|Network Hardware|CAN. On the dialog that appears, you can select the desired CANopen specific baud rate with CANopen settings.

### [CANopen/PlugIn] dlgNmtCommands


# Send NMT Commands

CANopen » Send NMT Commands

All nodes that are part of the network are listed in the list Network. The nodes that are described in the CAN database are listed first, followed by the nodes that are not described.

From this list you can choose the nodes that should be the receiver of the NMT command. Mark the node with the left mouse button for a single selection. If you want to choose several nodes, hold the key <CTRL> pressed and mark the wanted nodes with the left mouse button.

With the buttons [Operational], [Pre-Oper.], [Stop] and [Reset] you can send the appropriate NMT command immediately to the selected nodes.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [CANopen/PlugIn] dlgScanner


# CANopen Scanner

CANoe » Analysis Windows » CANopen Scanner

Ribbon|Analysis Tab|System Scanner|View / New

The CANopen scanner evaluates the CAN messages and displays the active nodes in a list. Additional node-specific information such as state, device name and master properties are also output.

## Using the CANopen Scanner

The CANopen scanner must be inserted into the Measurement Setup. For this, you must select the menu command Insert CANopen Scanner from the context menu of the Measurement Setup.

In the Measurement Setup a new analysis block with the label CO Scanner is now displayed. A double-click on the block opens the scanner window.

## Basic Principle

So that the scanner recognizes the nodes on the bus, the error control (CAN ID 1793..1919) and SDO messages (CAN ID 1409..1535) are evaluated.

If an error control message is received, the state of the node is displayed in the list. Furthermore, a colored icon is used. If only SDO messages are received or if the error control messages are missing for 10 seconds, then the icon is displayed in gray.

| Symbol | Description |
| --- | --- |
| (red) | Stopped |
| (yellow) | Pre-operational |
| (green) | Operational |
| (gray) | Only SDO messages are received by the node or the error control message was not sent for 10 seconds. |
| (gray) | The node has sent an emergency message. The error is still active. |

If one of the nodes transmits the message with CAN ID 113 (identification message of a master), then it is ma

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [CANopen/PlugIn/generatorblock] createMsgSequence


# Create and configure message sequences

CANopen » Interactive Generator » Create and configure message sequences

The tree view on the left side of the dialog contains both message templates and elements for flow control.

## Message Templates

Via drag and drop the message templates can be inserted either into the list of messages to be transmitted once (Sequence) or into the list of cyclical messages (Cyclic transmission). A double-click on a message template inserts the message into the Sequence list box. If the <Ctrl> key is pressed at the same time, the message is inserted into the cyclical transmission list.

Messages can also be inserted into the list of messages to be sent once or the list of messages to be sent cyclically by selecting the corresponding command from the local context menu of a message template (right-click on a message template).

### Configuration of Messages

After the message template is inserted into one of the list fields, a dialog for configuration of the message opens automatically. The settings on the configuration dialog vary depending on the type of message template.

The following message templates are available:

| Template | Service | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| SDO | SDO protocol | Executes a SDO download. Besides the specification if the transmission type (normal or block transfer) the transmitted data can be specified too. If block transfer is not selected the data length (0<data length<5 byte) is used to automatically switc

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [CANopen/PlugIn/generatorblock] generatorblockOverview


# CANopen Interactive Generator

CANopen » Interactive Generator

The CANopen Interactive Generator enables the creation of message sequences that can be sent once or cyclically. Messages can be selected from a list of CANopen specific message templates and combined into a sequence. The messages are configured via dialogs.

If the CANoe configuration has already been assigned a database, the messages described in the database are available for selection.

## Insert the CANopen Interactive Generator

The CANopen Interactive Generator can be inserted into the Simulation Setup. Right-click on the line that symbolizes the bus and select the Insert CANopen Interactive Generator entry. The CANopen Interactive Generator can be opened in the Simulation Setup with a double-click on the icon.

The messages are sent on the CAN channel that is assigned to the network in the System View.

## Message Sequences

- Creation and Configuration

- Sending

- Management

## Settings

A click of the [Configure...] button opens the CANopen IG settings dialog.

In the Pause between services (dec.) input field, it is possible to specify what minimum time must elapse between the transmission of two messages from the message sequence.

In the SDO Timeout (dec.) input field, the timeout for the SDO protocol is specified.

The value within the input field SDO Block size (dec.) determines the SDO block size to be used for SDO block upload transfers.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [CANopen/PlugIn/generatorblock] ManageMsgSequence


# Manage message sequences

CANopen » Interactive Generator » Manage message sequences

The sequence of the messages within the message sequence can be changed via drag and drop.

A double-click on a message in the list fields or the button [Configure...] opens the configuration dialog for the message.

Messages can be deleted, edited or activated/deactivated by selecting the corresponding command from the local context menu (right-click on a message). Deactivated messages remain in the list, but they are not sent. This status can also be switched by activating/deactivating the check box in the Type column.

Message sequences can be saved using the [Save...] button. Messages that are already saved are opened with the [Open...] button.

|  | Note If the database is changed with an open CANoe configuration, the list of message templates can be re-loaded with the [Refresh] button. |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [CANopen/PlugIn/generatorblock] sendMsgSequence


# Send message sequences

CANopen » Interactive Generator » Send message sequences

## One-time Sending of a Message Sequence

The one-time transmission of the message sequence is started with the [Execute...] button.

The first message of the sequence is sent immediately after the start. The distance after which each additional message is sent can be specified on the CANopen IG settings dialog.

## Cyclical Sending of Messages

The cyclical sending of the messages contained on the cyclical transmission list is started with the [Start] button. The cyclical transmission can also be started with the CYCLETX control element.

Insert the element for this at any point in the message sequence. For the control element, it is possible to specify whether the cyclical transmission should be started or stopped at this point.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |

### [CANopen/popups] dcf


# DCF

Device Configuration File

Device data is stored in a configuration file. An individual, unique file must be assigned to each network node. This file is called the device configuration file (DCF). The file format is specified in CiA306. It is derived from the EDS format and expanded with the concrete parameter values.

### [CANopen/popups] eds


# EDS

Electronic Data Sheet

CANopen Electronic Data Sheets (EDS) are device description files standardized by CiA® (CiA306). They make it possible to use manufacturer-independent project planning, configuration and analysis tools. A correct EDS file must be available in order to pass the CANopen conformance test.

# Test Sequence

Now have a look at the test configurator. It contains several test cases for each of the both nodes of your project. Some of them are already summed up in test groups. You can define more grouping if you want.

Save the test sequence in your project directory. To do this click the button [Save as...] and select a representative name for the XML file. This allows an easier mapping between file and test sequence later. For the further course of this instruction select the file name CheckOD and confirm it with [Save].

### [CANopen/test] testintroduction


# Testing with Option .CANopen

CANopen » Testing with Option .CANopen

After finishing the prototype system for CANopen devices, the necessary tests for the complete system are to built. In this phase CANoe supports you during the creation of tests as well as in logging and evaluation. The functionality of tests for a complete CANopen system contains several levels:

### Protocol Level

Tests at this level ensure that the CANopen specific communication protocols of the single devices in a complete system are implemented according to the specification.

### Communication Level

At this level the correct logical sequence of the (independent) protocol sequences is important. This becomes important on e.g. the configuration of PDOs. The description of PDO relevant entries in the object dictionary must follow a defined sequence.

### Applications Level

Applicative tests checks the relation between process data. To find out such relations, several requirements must be fulfilled. On the one hand it must be possible to exchange process data via PDO and on the other hand the system must be completely configured. So it is possible to check the state of a valve depending on temperature or pressure. Therefore the deep know-how of the user is necessary to create such tests.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [CANopen/TfsNodelayer] LSSTest


# LSS Tests

CANopen » Test Feature Set Node Layer »LSS Tests

To test the LSS functionality of a device you have the following functions available. Dependent on the device to be tested the functions can be arranged into groups.

## Testing LSS Slaves

The device to be testes is a LSS Slave. A request of a LSS Master is simulated and it is waited for the correct answer from the LSS Slave.

- coTfsLssSendSwitchStateModeGlobalRequest

- coTfsLssSendSwitchStateSelectiveSequence

- coTfsLssSendConfigureNodeIdRequest

- coTfsLssSendConfigureBitTimingRequest

- coTfsLssSendActivateBitParTimingRequest

- coTfsLssSendStoreConfigurationRequest

- coTfsLssSendInquireVendorIdRequest

- coTfsLssSendInquireProductCodeRequest

- coTfsLssSendInquireRevisionNoRequest

- coTfsLssSendInquireSerialNoRequest

- coTfsLssSendInquireNodeIdRequest

- coTfsLssSendIdentifyRemoteSlaveSequence

- coTfsLssWaitForIdentifySlaveResponse

- coTfsLssSendIdentifyNonConfRemoteSlaveRequest

- coTfsLssWaitForIdentifyNonConfSlaveResponse

- coTfsLssSendFastScanRequest

## Sequentially Testing of a LSS Master

The device to be tested is a LSS Master.

You can use these functions if the functionality of the LSS Master is known and you know the sequence of the LSS requests of the LSS Master. Within tests with these functions, the sequence of the requests must be kept.E.g. it is not possible to wait for a request A or B.

- coTfsLssWaitForSwitchStateModeGlobalRequest

- coTfsLssWaitforSwitchStateSelectiveSequence

- 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [CANopen/TfsNodelayer] Monitoring


# Monitoring

CANopen » Test Feature Set Node Layer » Monitoring

By using the monitoring functions you can monitor SDO and NMT messages that are sent between CANopen devices in the network. The functions check if SDO requests, SDO responses and NMT responses (Boot-up message) are processed and sent in a defines time span.

In contrast to the SDO timing functions messages that are not sent from CANopen test module are monitored too. Therefore the SDO timing functions are obsolete and should not be used any longer.

To monitor a node set the node ID of a single node (coTfsMonitorIncludeNodeId) or a range of node IDs (coTfsMonitorIncludeNodeIdRange). If you want to exclude nodes from monitoring, use the functions coTfsMonitorExcludeNodeId and coTfsMonitorExcludeNodeIdRange.

If you have added nodes to be monitored or excluded nodes from monitoring, you have to start the monitoring process with coTfsMonitorActivate. to take over theses changes.

You can stop the monitoring at any time with coTfsMonitorDeactivate. All values that are monitored by this time are added to the test report. These are the minimum, maximum and average response times of the nodes, the number of monitored events and the number of failed or late responses.

If the test module has writing access to an active test report, events are logged that have caused a violation of the defined time values (coTfsMonitorSetSdoTimeout and coTfsMonitorSetNmtTimeout). This is independent of other running tests.

CANopen Te

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [CANopen/TfsNodelayer] OdTests


# Object Dictionary Tests

CANopen » Test Feature Set Node Layer » Object Dictionary Tests

This test modules allows checking existent and hidden object dictionary entries.

A simple but not complete check can be done with coTfsODChkStdEntries. In this test only necessary and recommended objects are checked. Because the detailed device description is unknown, only the basis functionality of the device can be checked. A more detailed check is described in the following paragraph.

For checking the object dictionary the necessary parameter are saved in a internal list of the test module. This list is uses to realize the possible complexity of the test in CAPL. For a complete test the following functions are available:

- First the list should be reset with coTfsODClearAllEntries.

- The all entries should be added with coTfsODAddEntry. Because only one permissible value can defined for the function call, the user has the possibility to add more single entries with coTfsODAddOptEntryValue. A complete value range can be defined with coTfsODAddOptEntryValueRange.

- Depending on the device it is possible that the object dictionary contains objects, which only differ by the subindex. With coTfsODAddEntrySubIndexRange identical entries can be defined only differing by their subindex.

- After complete definition of the internal list, the functions coTfsODChk and coTfsODChkNotExist can access the elements. The first function checks if the given objects exist, executes a read or writ

[...truncated for brevity...]

# PDO Tests

CANopen » Test Feature Set Node Layer » PDO Tests

These test sequences allow getting the TPDO data of a CANopen device via different mechanisms:

- coTfsTPDOGetDataBySyncCyclic reads data via a cyclic sync

- coTfsTPDOGetDataByEventTimer reads data with a event timer mechanism

- coTfsTPDOGetDataByRTR reads data with object index and remote frame mechanism
