### [CANopen/TfsNodelayer] TfsNLOverview


# CANopen Test Feature Set Node Layer

CANopen » Test Feature Set Node Layer

## Overview

The Test Feature Set test module for CANopen can be assigned to any test module within CANoe. This test module makes available the following functionality:

- SDO protocol tests (support of expedited SDO Up/Downloads, segmented SDO Up/Downloads and SDO Block Up/Downloads)

- NMT tests

- Heartbeat tests

- Guarding tests

- Emergency tests

- SDO response time statistics

- User callbacks for sync messages/PDOs, heartbeat and callback events

- PDO Tests

- Object Dictionary Tests

- SDO Timing Tests

- LSS Tests

- Monitoring

## Test Functions

Most test functions are available in three different versions.

- Level 3 test functions can be implemented very easily and require only a little CANopen knowledge. With these tests, it is possible to check the absolute basic functionality of the DUT (Device Under Test) in the simplest way.

- Level 2 test functions, by contrast, offer the experienced user the setting of some more complex tests. General data access is possible here.

- Level 1 test functions enable the generation of CANopen protocol errors at any point and the exploitation of limit cases. The user is responsible for the communication here and can also write complete tests himself. Of course the mixing of the functions of the individual test levels is also possible.

## Test Control

The Test Control Functions make help functionality available to the user in order to describe t

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [CANopen/TfsNodelayer] timingdescription


# SDO Timing Tests

CANopen » Test Feature Set Node Layer » SDO Timing Tests

The test sequences of this test module allow the monitoring of the reaction times while SDO transfers of a device under test (DUT). The period of time between a SDO request and the SDO response is monitored. It is possible to monitor one or more nodes. It is always monitored the communication between the test module and the corresponding node.

A simple time test is available with the test sequence coTfsTimingSDOPollDevice. The object 1000h of the object dictionary is accessed several times, the result is added to the test report automatically.

Also the timing behavior can be monitored while other test sequences are executed. In this case the results are internal temporarily saved and added to the test report by request. With coTfsTimingSDOReset the results can be reset.

A monitoring is started (or resumed) with coTfsTimingSDOStart. coTfsTimingSDOStop stops the monitoring or pauses it.

|  | Note Instead of the functions coTfsTimingSDOStart, coTfsTimingSDOStop, coTfsTimingSDOReset and coTfsTimingSDOAddToReport you should use the monitoring functions. These functions offer a more complex functionality to monitor the bus traffic by watching messages that are not sent by the CANopen test module too. |

CANopen Test Feature Set Node Layer | CANopen Test Feature Set Level 1 Functions | CANopen Test Feature Set Level 2 Functions | CANopen Test Feature Set Level 3 Functions | Test Control Functions

| V

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [CANopen/TfsNodelayer/SDO] BlockSdoDownload


# SDO Block Download

CANopen » Test Feature Set Node Layer » SDO Block Download

The SDO block download is initialized with coTfsSDOBlockInit. The block size can be called with coTfsSDOGetBlockSize and CRC checksum support with coTfsSDOChkCrcSupport.

The individual block segments are sent using coTfsSDOBlockDownloadSegmentRequest. The last call of this function must wait for the confirmation (acknowledge) of the server and if necessary, re-transmit segments that have already been sent. A block can contain up to 127 individual segments. The confirmation also contains the block size desired by the server. This can be called up via coTfsSDOGetBlockSize.

As many blocks are transmitted as are necessary in order to transmit the desired data set.

Only in the last segment of the last block must the parameter isLastSegment of coTfsSDOBlockDownloadSegmentRequest be set to 1 in order to introduce the SDO block end phase.

The SDO block download is concluded with coTfsSDOBlockEnd. Here the CRC checksum is also transmitted that can be calculated with coTfsSDOCalcCrc.

The complete SDO block download can also be executed with the help of the command coTfsSDOBlockDownload.

CANopen Test Feature Set Node Layer | CANopen Test Feature Set Level 1 Functions | CANopen Test Feature Set Level 2 Functions | CANopen Test Feature Set Level 3 Functions | Test Control Functions | Segmented SDO Download | Segmented SDO Upload | Expedited SDO Download | Expedited SDO Upload | SDO Block Upload

| Ver

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [CANopen/TfsNodelayer/SDO] BlockSdoUpload


# SDO Block Upload

CANopen » Test Feature Set Node Layer » SDO Block Upload

The initialization phase for the SDO block upload consists of two calls of coTfsSDOBlockInit. With the first call, the client subcommand initiate upload request (cs = 0) is transmitted. Directly after the second call of this function (client subcommand = start upload (cs = 3)) the SDO server starts sending the first SDO block.

The call of coTfsSDOBlockUploadSegmentResponse must occur without temporal distance directly after the call of coTfsSDOBlockInit. Otherwise, it is very likely that not all data segments will be detected. With this function a block of this block is confirmed after receipt of all segments. The function must be called only once per SDO upload block and confirms the receipt of the individual segments.

The SDO block transfer is concluded with coTfsSDOBlockEnd. The possibly-transmitted CRC checksum can be checked with coTfsSDOCalcCrc.

The complete SDO block upload can also be executed with the help of the command coTfsSDOBlockUpload.

CANopen Test Feature Set Node Layer | CANopen Test Feature Set Level 1 Functions | CANopen Test Feature Set Level 2 Functions | CANopen Test Feature Set Level 3 Functions | Test Control Functions | Segmented SDO Download | Segmented SDO Upload | Expedited SDO Download | Expedited SDO Upload | SDO Block Download

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [CANopen/TfsNodelayer/SDO] ExpSdoDownload


# Expedited SDO Download

CANopen » Test Feature Set Node Layer » Expedited SDO Download

The expedited SDO download consists of a single request with the associated response message. The SDO client can transmit a maximum of 4 bytes of data to the SDO server.

Here the function coTfsSDOInit sends the request (with set expedited flag) together with the reference data and also processes the corresponding response message.

The complete procedure is also executed by the command coTfsSDODownload if this command is given a maximum of 4 bytes of data. Otherwise a segmented SDO Download is executed.

CANopen Test Feature Set Node Layer | CANopen Test Feature Set Level 1 Functions | CANopen Test Feature Set Level 2 Functions | CANopen Test Feature Set Level 3 Functions | Test Control Functions | Segmented SDO Download | Segmented SDO Upload | Expedited SDO Upload | SDO Block Download | SDO Block Upload

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [CANopen/TfsNodelayer/SDO] ExpSdoUpload


# Expedited SDO Upload

CANopen » Test Feature Set Node Layer » Expedited SDO Upload

The expedited SDO upload consists of a single request with the associated response message. The SDO server can transmit a maximum of 4 bytes of data to the SDO client.

The command coTfsSDOInit executes a query to the SDO server. If the data size is up to 4 bytes, then the SDO server already sends the reference data in the response.

The SDO server responds with the reference data if it does not exceed 4 bytes.

With more than 4 bytes reference data, the SDO server goes over to the segmented SDO Upload.

Instead of coTfsSDOInit an expedited transfer can be executed more easily with coTfsSDOUpload.

CANopen Test Feature Set Node Layer | CANopen Test Feature Set Level 1 Functions | CANopen Test Feature Set Level 2 Functions | CANopen Test Feature Set Level 3 Functions | Test Control Functions | Segmented SDO Download | Segmented SDO Upload | Expedited SDO Download | SDO Block Download | SDO Block Upload

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [CANopen/TfsNodelayer/SDO] SegSDODownload


# Segmented SDO Download

CANopen » Test Feature Set Node Layer » Segmented SDO Download

The segmented SDO download consists of the initialization phase and the actual data transmission. This transfer mode is used to transmit longer data blocks.

For the initiation of a segmented SDO transfer via coTfsSDOInit, the expedited Flag is not set. The function triggers the corresponding request and also processes the associated response message.

The actual data segments are sent with coTfsSDOSegmentRequest. It also handles the corresponding response messages of the server here.

In the last segment, the "completion flag" is set to 1 and thus the transfer is completed, whereby the user himself is responsible for the correct number of segments and data bytes.

A complete segmented download is also executed by the call of coTfsSDODownload if more than 4 bytes of data is transmitted to this command. Otherwise coTfsSDODownload executes an expedited SDO Download.

CANopen Test Feature Set Node Layer | CANopen Test Feature Set Level 1 Functions | CANopen Test Feature Set Level 2 Functions | CANopen Test Feature Set Level 3 Functions | Test Control Functions | Segmented SDO Upload | Expedited SDO Download | Expedited SDO Upload | SDO Block Download | SDO Block Upload

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [CANopen/TfsNodelayer/SDO] SegSdoUpload


# Segmented SDO Upload

CANopen » Test Feature Set Node Layer » Segmented SDO Upload

With a segmented SDO upload, any amount of reference data can be transmitted from a server to the client.

A segmented SDO upload is initiated, in that the SDO server has not set the expedited flag during the initialization phase.

With coTfsSDOInit the request of the client is generated and the corresponding response message is processed. The actual data segments are requested with coTfsSDOSegmentRequest. It also handles the corresponding response messages of the server.

With the last data segment, the SDO server sets the "completion flag" to the value 1.

The complete upload is also executed with the command coTfsSDOUpload if the target object provides more than 4 bytes of data and the SDO server supports this mode.

Otherwise an expedited SDO Upload is executed.

CANopen Test Feature Set Node Layer | CANopen Test Feature Set Level 1 Functions | CANopen Test Feature Set Level 2 Functions | CANopen Test Feature Set Level 3 Functions | Test Control Functions | Segmented SDO Download | Expedited SDO Download | Expedited SDO Upload | SDO Block Download | SDO Block Upload

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [CANopen/tips] tipsBackgroundBusload


# Background Busload

CANopen » Tips and Tricks » Background Busload

The CiA® Working Group on Performance Tests produced tables in CiA308 with which a reproducible reference background busload is specified. These tables can be written in an ASCII file using the clipboard. This can then be used as a replay. For this, additionally a code should be entered in the first line and the file should be configured as periodic in the replay block.

|  | Example base dec0.002380 1 387 Rx d 1 10.004759 1 643 Rx d 2 2 30.007139 1 899 Rx d 3 4 5 60.009519 1 392 Rx d 4 7 8 9 100.011898 1 648 Rx d 1 110.014278 1 904 Rx d 6 16 17 18 19 20 210.016657 1 387 Rx d 1 20.019037 1 643 Rx d 2 29 300.021417 1 899 Rx d 3 37 11 55........... |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [CANopen/tips] tipsDatabaseStorage


# Database Storage

CANopen » Tips and Tricks » Database Storage

ProCANopen stores the generated database both in the relevant project directory and in installation directory. One of two ways of working should be adhered to so that a correct database is always assigned in CANoe:

- In the case of strictly project-oriented work it is recommended to assign a CANoe configuration to the project and to indicate the project-specific database there.

- In the case of frequently changing networks and/or projects, changing the database directory may be annoying. Instead, the database in the installation directory can always be used. However, it is essential to make sure that when the project is changed, this is always loaded in ProCANopen and saved there again immediately. The database is always generated on this saving.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [CANopen/tips] tipsDeviceDebugging


# Debugging when Implementing an own Device

CANopen » Tips and Tricks » Debugging when Implementing an own Device

When developing own devices, one is usually presented with the task of "seeing" the first message transmitted by the device and then stimulating the device with messages. If this is successful, tests of the implemented device functions will follow step by step.

The first step here is always setting the correct baud rate. For this, a CANopen specific baud rate can be selected via the bus configuration dialog (Configuration | Hardware Configuration | CANopen settings).

A measurement is then started with the command Start | Start. After the CANopen device has been switched on, it should send a so-called BootUp Message. This can be observed in the Trace Window.

The CANopen Interactive Generator can then be used for stimulation. Here, single messages can be sent in a targeted fashion without any complex configuration.

To trigger further protocol-specific services, it is recommended to use ProCANopen now. If there is already an EDS file from the CANopen device, a corresponding node should be created in the ProCANopen project. Otherwise, you can start with the command Network | Generic Access without special configuration. The messages of individual services can be easily observed in the Trace Window.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [CANopen/tips] tipsOverview


# Tips and Tricks

CANopen » Tips and Tricks

This chapter describes some typical procedures. The methods for analysis and simulation are mainly described in the CANoe online help. Here, however, special features are displayed in connection with CANopen networks. A number of tips refer to the analysis functions that can also be used independently of simulation:

- Network Development and Analysis

- Debugging when Implementing an own Device

- Troubleshooting in a Network

- Database Storage

- Background Busload

- Write Window Notifications

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [CANopen/tips] tipsTroubleshooting


# Troubleshooting in a Network

CANopen » Tips and Tricks » Troubleshooting in a Network

In the case of investigations in a network, the clarity of the information obtained can be significantly increased by using the symbolic display. However, the symbolic display always presupposes the availability of a CAN database in the CANdb format. The following stages must be conducted to obtain this database.

First of all, launch ProCANopen , set the baud rate in Options | Settings, register ProCANopen in the network with Network | Connect and then conduct a scan with Network | Read in Network. ProCANopen searches all of the CANopen devices present in the network and displays them accordingly in the topology display. At the same time, the CANopen devices found are assigned the relevant EDS files. Obviously, this is subject to these EDS files being available and correct.

Then, the ProCANopen project is saved in a project directory. At the same time, a database with the name PRJDB.DBC is also created there. This database is now added to the current configuration in CANoe (database assigned to the project).

Now, accesses to the object directories of the existing CANopen devices can be easily executed with ProCANopen , such as reading fault histories, individual entries or the setting of values. The relevant message traffic can be tracked using the Trace Window.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [CANopen/TraceWindow] TraceWindowCANopen


# Trace Window

CANopen » Trace Window » CANopen

With the CANopen option, you can display more protocol-specific information in the Trace Window. Further you can add protocol-specific analysis filters.

The activation of these functions is controlled by various attributes within the databases. For more detailed information, please refer to the page Activation of CANopen Functionality.

The needed attributes are automatically written to the generated database on saving a ProCANopen project.

When the CANopen configuration template is used, a standard CANopen database is assigned. Accordingly, there is no expanded interpretation, and messages will be filtered and interpreted according to CiA 301.

## Further Information

- CANopen specific Columns

- CANopen Safety specific Columns

- Event Highlighting of CANopen Messages

- CANopen Analysis Filter

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [CANopen/TraceWindow] TraceWindowCANopenActivation


# Activation of CANopen Functionality

CANopen » Trace Window » CANopen » Activation of CANopen Functionality

The option CANopen offers the following extensions of the Trace Window:

- CANopen specific columns

- CANopen Safety specific columns

- CANopen analysis filter

For activation of these functions, at least one database within the configuration must contain an attribute with the name ProtocolType. This attribute must have the object type Network and the value type String.

The attribute can assume the following values:

| Extension | Value of ProtocolType |
| --- | --- |
| CANopen | CANopen |
| CANopen Safety | CANopenSafety |

|  | Note It is sufficient to set the attribute in one database to analyze all messages of the channel. |

## CANopen / CANopen Analysis Filter

The CANopen specific columns and the CANopen analysis filters are additionally configured by the CANopenApplProfile attribute. This attribute must have the object type Network and the value type Integer. The value of this attribute controls the internal CAN identifier calculation of the SDO channels, provided that no explicit description is found within the referenced DCF files.

The value of this attribute can assume the following values:

| Calculation of the CAN identifier according to | Value of CANopenApplProfile |
| --- | --- |
| CiA 301 | 301 |
| CiA 447 | 447 |

If this attribute is not defined, CiA 301 is adopted by default.

To enable expanded interpretation and correct filtering, additiona

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [CANopen/TraceWindow] TraceWindowCANopenAnalysisFilter


# Analysis Filter

CANopen » Trace Window » CANopen » Filter » Analysis Filter » CANopen

For selective evaluations, you use the so-called analysis filters. You can configure these either as a Stop Filter or a Pass Filter.

When the option CANopen is activated, you can filter for certain CANopen services instead of for individual CAN identifiers.

|  | Cross Reference Detailed information on the configuration and general filter methods of the analysis filters can be found under Trace Window: Filter. |

|  | Note You can add CANopen analysis filters using the context menu of the Trace Explorer. It is not possible to use drag and drop from the Trace Output Window or Symbol Explorer. |

## Requirements

The CANopen specific filters are only available under certain conditions:

- Basically the option CANopen in CANoe has to be active.

- Additionally, the database of the configuration has to contain certain attributes.

|  | Further information on the topic of activation Activating the CANopen Functionality |

## Filter Consistency

Avoid combining CANopen networks with different standards (e.g., CiA 301 or CiA 447). The filter always operates according to the first profile type found in a database and ignores subsequent databases that might possibly define a different profile type.

### Correct Sequence for SDO Transfer

The SDO transfer involves a transport protocol that requires certain message sequences. SDO protocol violations lead to the filter may not operate as expected.

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [CANopen/TraceWindow] TraceWindowCANopenHighlighting


# Event Highlighting

CANopen » Trace Window » CANopen » Event Highlighting

CANopen messages can be highlighted depending on different categories.

You can open the dialog to set the color via the context menu of the Trace Window with the entry Configuration|View|Standard Highlighting. Alternatively you can open this dialog via the Options dialog (Windows/Blocks|Trace Window) or the entry Trace Highlighting... in the menu Global Trace configuration of the Trace Window toolbar.

| Receive PDO (RPDO) 0 | first receive PDO |

| Receive PDO (RPDO) 1 | second receive PDO |

| Transmit PDO (TPDO) Others | other transmit PDO |
| Receive PDO (RPDO) Others | other receive PDO |
| Service Data Objects (SDO) |
| Default Client SDO (CSDO) (tx) | standard Client -> Server SDO |
| Default Server SDO (SSDO) (rx) | standard Server -> Client SDO |

| Server SDO

### [CANopen/TraceWindow] TraceWindowCANopenPrioritization


# Prioritization of the Extensions

CANopen » Trace Window » CANopen » Activation of CANopen Functionality » Prioritization of the Extensions

The interpretation of the messages displayed in the Trace Window is done with prioritization. The Trace Window extensions have the higher priority. If these extensions are not active or the message cannot be interpreted, the application fills the columns with the available information.

Per default the CANopen extension has higher priority than the CANopen Safety extension. This fact can be changed in the file can.INI. Section [EventHooks] defines the number of active extensions. Section [EventHook0], [EventHook1] etc define the priority and can have a single value only. The attribute is build with Name= followed by the file name of the extension. The highest priority has the active extension with the smallest numeric value.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [CANopen/writeWnd] writeWndCANopen


# Write Window

CANopen » Write Window

The CANopen specific analysis in the Trace Window is activated on measurement start depending on the assigned databases. The test is done for each channel independently. If the CANopen specific analysis is activated, a messages is output to the Write Window. It is differentiated if the activation is processed for all channels or for a selection of channels.

Furthermore an information is output on activation of CANopen Safety specific analysis if a DCF file is available for all nodes described in the databases. A complete CANopen Safety specific analysis is possible only with these configuration files.

Message in the Write Window

### [CAPL] CAPL


# CAPL

CANoe » CAPL

Communication Access Programming Language (CAPL) allows programming of network node models as well as special evaluation programs for individual applications.

The functional range of CANoe includes a CAPL compiler which compiles a created CAPL file with the extension *.CAN to an executable program file with the extension *.CBF.

CANoe also contains a CAPL Browser which you can use as an alternative to a normal text editor to create CAPL programs. The browser shows you the CAPL event procedures in structured form allowing you to create and modify CAPL programs in a user-friendly manner.

Instead of the Browser you can configure a different editor in the options.

Refer to the CAPL Browser help function for further information on CAPL.

File accesses from CAPL programs | CAPL-on-Board |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [CAPL] CAPLExportTable


# Description of the CAPL Export Table

CAPL » CAPL DLL » Export Table

Functions that have been created can be exported into CAPL code with the aid of a table function (CAPL_DLL_INFO_LIST).

The first row of the table contains version information. This row must be defined in the following manner:

{CDLL_VERSION_NAME, (CAPL_FARCALL)CDLL_VERSION, "", "", CAPL_DLL_CDECL, 0xabcd, CDLL_EXPORT },

## Information and Examples on the Functions

The description of a function for export has the following structure:

| Column | Description | Comment |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | Name of the function for export | — |
| 2 | Address of the function in the DLL | — |
| 3 | Name of the function category | — |
| 4 | Text that describes the function | — |
| 5 | Value type of the return parameter | — |
| 6 | Number of function parameters | When using CAPL_DLL_INFO3: up to 10 parameters CAPL_DLL_INFO4: up to 64 parameters |
| 7 | Value type of the function parameter | — |
| 8 | Dimensions of the function parameter | In order of the function parameters the parameters in octal format are characterized as follows: \000: Scalar\001: 1-dimensional Array\002: 2-dimensional Array |
| 9 | Name of the function parameter | — |

|  | Example: Using the Export Table void CAPLEXPORT far CAPLPASCAL appPut(unsigned long x{data = x;}{"dllPut", (CAPL_FARCALL)appPut, "CAPL_DLL","This function will save data from CAPL to DLL memory",'V', 1, "D", "\000", {"x"}}, Element Description Function name dllPut Function addr

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [CAPL] CAPLFileAccess


# File Access from CAPL Programs

CAPL » File Access

There is often the need to save variables or measurement parameters over multiple measurements. CAPL has file functions for this purpose.

The files used have the same structure as the widely used format of the INI files of MS-Windows.

File accesses were conceived for easy-of-use and security. Therefore, they are not very fast. They can be used in the on start{} event procedure or when a value has changed.

In order to assure real-time capability, the files being used must be loaded before the start of measurement.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [CAPL] CAPLIncludeNETDLL


# .NET DLL

CAPL » .NET DLL

From CAPL, it is possible to call functions which have been implemented in a separate .NET DLL. For this, the function must fulfill the following requirements:

- It must be defined as public in a public class

- It must be static

- Its name must be a valid CAPL identifier

- Neither the function nor its class must have generic parameters

- Its return type must be void, an integer type, bool or double

- All parameters must be integer types, bool, double, string or one-dimensional arrays from integer types or double.

The data type bool is mapped to DWORD in CAPL. The function is called in the same way as a CAPL function. The name, however, must be qualified with a class name and, if necessary, with a namespace.

|  | Example .NET Function (in .NET DLL) namespace MyNetHelpers { public class MyClass { public static Int32 SomeFunc(String s, out double d) { /* ... */ } }} Function Call in CAPL double d; long ret;ret = MyNetHelpers::MyClass::SomeFunc("Vector", d); |

It is not possible to look into the NET function from the debugger because CANoe does not compile the .NET DLL and the source files are not known to the debugger. Test and debug the .NET DLL with Visual Studio, ideally by means of a unit test outside of CANoe.

|  | Note The execution of .NET code always influences the real-time capability of the program. Especially during the first execution (by loading the IL code and just-in-time compilation) and during garbage collection, delays ma

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [CAPL] CAPLIncludeWindowsDLL


# CAPL DLL

CAPL » CAPL DLL

In CAPL programs you can call functions which you have implemented in your own C/C++ DLL. In doing so, the function from the DLL are exported through a function table.

|  | Note A 64-bit DLL is required for the CANoe Measurement Setup. The sample project also contains the code for this case. |

The CAPL DLL interface supports callback functions starting with CANoe version 5.0.

- Description of the CAPL Export Table

- Example of a C++ DLL for CAPL

- Search sequence for a Windows DLL

Whenever functions of a DLL are called in the real-time area (i.e. Simulation Setup), they run in a high-priority thread. This can affect measurement.

In order for the CAPL compiler and CAPL Browser to recognize the DLL, you must link it to the CAPL program.

To do this, proceed as follows:

- Enter the DLL in the Options dialog in CANoe. In this case, the DLL will be available to all CAPL programs.

- You can enter the DLL in the includes section of a CAPL program using the #pragma library command.In this case, it will only be available to this program.

|  | Note When linking your own DLLs in CAPL programs, all methods are available to you of accessing system resources (Memory management, hardware, etc). However, only experienced programmers should make use of this option due to the multitude of associated problems. We ask for your understanding that Vector is not able to provide any support for creating DLLs. |

CAPL DLL Sample Configuration | Switches: CAPL D

### [Car2x] c2xConceptDB


# Database Concept

Car2x » Database Concept

Databases in the areas Car2x/V2x/C-V2x are based on ASN.1. This description language for defining data structures is used by the standardization bodies to define the standards for application messages. Based on the ASN.1 files, ASNV database modules for the Car2x option are generated from them using the ASN.1 Import Wizard. This Vector specific format adds factors and units to the ASN.1 structures. This allows a better analysis in the Trace Window.

In addition, this approach offers great flexibility. As soon as new standards or ASN.1 files are available from the standardization bodies, they can be integrated immediately into the Car2x option.

|  | Cross Reference Add a Car2x Database Protocol Description Language ASN.1 Use Application Messages Show Physical Values with ASN.1 in Trace Window Import Application Messages |

Option .Car2x | See Also

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Car2x] c2xSecurityConcept


# Security Concept

Car2x » Security Concept

A public key method is used for secure Car2x communication. For this, certificates containing, among other things, the public keys are needed. The corresponding private keys are managed in the Car2x Certificate Manager.

With Car2x/V2x, a certificate confirms that its owner is in possession of the keys and rights contained in the certificate. For this, the certificate also contains, in addition to the necessary data, the identity of the certificate issuer as well as his digital signature that can be used to verify the correctness of the certificate.

The certificate issuer also identifies himself with a certificate signed by himself or by another issuer, who in turn identifies himself with a certificate. To ensure that two ITS stations that do not know each other can still trust each other, they must exchange their certificates (and, if required, the certificates of the certificate issuers) and trust the respective certificate issuers (or root certificates).

|  | Cross Reference Use Certificates Protocol overview of the supported security variants |

Option .Car2x

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |

### [Car2x] Car2xConcept


# Car2x for CANoe | Concept

Car2x for CANoe » Concept

| Overview |
| --- |
| CANoe Option Car2x supports the basic concepts of CANoe and extends them with Car2x-specific functions. For analysis of Car2x communication between vehicles and with the infrastructure, CANoe Option Car2x supports the IEEE 802.11p (WLAN) interface. Serving as the communication module is the VN4610 or alternatively radio modules of different manufacturers. CANoe supports the 802.11p radio adapters Cohda Radio MKx, NEC LinkBird V2, V3 and V4, DENSO WSU-01 and WSU-015, Delphi CRPU and Continental CCU MK2 and MK3. Data are exchanged between the radio adapters and CANoe via Ethernet. |
| Analysis |
| Option Car2x supports and extends the basic analysis of CANoe. Existing analysis windows are extended to interpret and analyze WLAN packets. Protocol information such as header and payload can be displayed in detail. Geographic coordinates that are received in a Car2x network can be comfortably displayed in a map too. The analysis of data can be made more comfortable by using filter. Ethernet packets that are not interesting for analysis can be filtered out of the data stream. |
| Simulation and Stimulation |
| With option Car2x for CANoe Car2x scenarios can be simulated and the device under test (DUT) can be stimulated. WLAN packets can easily be created, configured, and sent. Application payload written in ASN.1 can be interpreted too to access the data fields of the packets. This simplifies the simulati

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Car2x/asn1] asnApplMsgWithCAPL


# Process Application Messages with CAPL

Car2x » Procedures » Process Application Messages with CAPL

This example shows how you can create an application message as a packet with CAPL, fill it with data and send it. It is shown too how you can read and analyze an application message with a CAPL event handler.

## Create CAPL data structures and methods

Because the application message is described with ASN.1, the ASN.1 specification has to be imported to CANoe.

The following simple ASN.1 description is used for this example (transmitted in protocol BTP with destination port 1000):

SIMPLE-PDU-Descriptions {}DEFINITIONS AUTOMATIC TAGS ::=BEGINSimplePdu ::= SEQUENCE { -- header is mandatory header SimpleHeader, -- data is an optional field data SimpleData OPTIONAL}SimpleHeader ::= SEQUENCE { -- mandatory sample field id INTEGER(0..100), -- 7 bits -- optional sample field frequency INTEGER (0..255) OPTIONAL --units of 0.1 Hz}SimpleData ::= SEQUENCE { simpleEnum ENUMERATED { first (0), next (1), last (2) }, value INTEGER (0..900000000)}END

After the import the following CAPL files are available in folder Vector_CN_ASNV in the directory of your CANoe configuration:

- ASNV_Template_SimplePdu.cin

- ASNV_Template_BaseDatatypes.cin

## Include CAPL files

The file C2x_Basics.cin is an include file with help functions that is part of the CANoe.Car2x sample configurations. Include this file and the file ASNV_Template_SimplePdu.cin to your CAPL program as follows:

includes{ #incl

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Car2x/asn1] asnImporterTool


# ASN.1 Import Wizard

ASN.1 » ASN.1 Import Wizard

Windows Start Menu|CANoe|Tools|ASN.1 Import Wizard

To use an »ASN.1 based protocol in CANoe, you have to convert the ASN.1 module into a Car2x database using the tool ASN.1 Import Wizard. This database can then be added to a CANoe configuration.

The tool provides a graphic user interface to easily configure the settings. If you have configured all necessary settings, you can start the import.

## First configuration page

On this page you select the file with the ASN.1 module (ASN.1-Specification). If this module uses so-called imports (other ASN.1 modules), you have to add these modules to the selected one. Furthermore, you must select a directory in which the created files are to be saved.

The options of the area Options are used to send a packet with an ASN.1 based protocol. To receive that packet, the ASN.1 based protocol has to be imported with the same options.

- Encoding: Select the format of the fields of the ASN.1 based protocol. The following formats are supported

- Packet Encoding Rule (»PER) (unaligned)

- Basic Encoding Rule (»BER): The DER variant is used for coding, for decoding each BER variant is accepted.

- Underlying Protocol: Select the protocol that is used to transmit the ASN.1 based protocol.

- First and second selector: The function depends on the underlying protocol.

| Underlying Protocol | First Selector | Second Selector |
| --- | --- | --- |
| btp | – | BTP Destination Port |
| geo_cnh | 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Car2x/asn1] asnImportProcedure


# Import Application Messages

Car2x » Procedures » Import Application Messages

Windows Start Menu|CANoe|Tools|ASN.1 Import Wizard

To use ASN.1 described application messages (e.g. CAM/DENM (ETSI) or BSM/TIM (SAE)) in CANoe, you have to import them to a Car2x database using the ASN.1 Import Wizard.

|  | Note This import is necessary if you want to create a new database or extend an existing database with application messages. |

Follow the next steps to import application messages:

|  | Step by Step Procedure Open the tool Vector ASN.1 Import Wizard via the list on the help page Vector Tool Launch. On the first page enter the path to the ASN.1 module (ASN.1-Specification) and the destination directory. Note If the ASN.1 module refers to other ASN.1 modules you have to copy the content of the referenced ASN.1 modules to that ASN.1 module before the import. Select the procedure that is to be used for encoding the protocol fields (Encoding). Select the underlying protocol that is to be used the payload described with ASN.1 (Underlying Protocol). Define the conditions that the underlying protocol has to fulfill to use the imported protocol.E.g. a CAM (ASN.1 payload) can be transmitted with the underlying protocol BTP and the BTP destination port 1. Click [Next»] to switch to next page. Select the ASN.1 data type that describes the imported protocol. The name of this data type is used as protocol designator for the imported protocol. Click to [Import]. The protocol is importe

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Car2x/asn1] asnModuleSample


# Use Application Messages

Car2x » Procedures » Use Application Messages

The following example shows how to use ASN.1:

|  | Example ASN1Module DEFINITIONS AUTOMATIC TAGS ::= BEGINASN1Protocol ::= SEQUENCE { booleanField BOOLEAN OPTIONAL, bitStringField BIT STRING { bit1(0), bit2(1) }, sequenceField SEQUENCE { enumeratedField ENUMERATED { off (0), on (1) } }, arrayField SEQUENCE ( SIZE(0..10) ) OF SeperatlyDefinedChoice date DATE, classId MyClass.&id ({Class-Set}), classValue MyClass.&Value ({Class-Set}{@id}) }SeperatlyDefinedChoice ::= CHOICE { numberChoice INTEGER { namedNumber(123) }, ia5StringChoice IA5String}MY-CLASS ::= CLASS { &id ClassKey UNIQUE, &Value } WITH SYNTAX {&Value IDENTIFIED BY &id}ClassKey ::= INTEGER SIZE (0..255) { one(1), two(2) }classOne MY-CLASS ::= { INTEGER IDENTIFIED BY one }classTwo MY-CLASS ::= { BOOLEAN IDENTIFIED BY two } Class-Set MY-CLASS ::= { classOne | classTwo } END |

## Token designators of an ASN.1 protocol

To access the fields of an ASN.1 based protocol with the Packet API you have to derive the appropriate token designator from the ASN.1 module.

The following scenarios are possible, relating to the shown example:

- If ASN1Protocol is selected as highest data type then the token designator of a field is the field name.Referred to the example you could access the first protocol field with the token designator booleanField.

- If the protocol field is a SEQUENCE you can access the field by appending a point and the field name to t

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Car2x/asn1] asnPhysicalValuesInTraceWnd


# Show Physical Values with ASN.1 in Trace Window

Car2x » Procedures » Show physical values with ASN.1 in Trace Window

The ASN.1 standard does not allow to describe the physical interpretation of raw values that are transmitted as INTEGER. therefore the ASN.1 Import Wizard supports the following extension for the INTEGER data type so that the interpreted values can be shown in the Trace Window:

INTEGER PHYSICAL { FACTOR(f) OFFSET(o) UNIT("u") }

|  | Note This extension is no standard ASN.1 and is not supported by other ASN.1 tools. |

| Part | Type | Meaning | Default | Usage |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| f | floating-point number | specify the conversion from transmitted raw value to physical interpretation of the value [physical value] = [raw value] * f + o | 1.0 | optional |
| o | floating-point number | 0.0 | optional |
| u | string | specifies the physical unit | "" | optional |

|  | Example SEQUENCE{x INTEGER (0..100),y INTEGER PHYSICAL { FACTOR(0.1) OFFSET(-5) UNIT("m") } (0..100)} With transmitted raw values x=35 and y=35 the sequence is displayed as follows: x 35 35y -1.5m 35 |

Option .Car2x

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Car2x/asn1] asnUsePacketTypeDesignators


# Initializing Several Protocol Layers

Car2x » Procedures » Initializing Several Protocol Layers

You can initialize a packet with the wanted underlying protocols by using combinations of Packet Type Designators. The single packet type designators are intercombinable and can be assigned as strings to the functions C2xInitPacket() and C2xInitProtocol(). The pipe character (|) is used as separator between the single packet type designators. For each protocol header only one packet type designator is allowed.

## Packets based on GeoNetworking protocol according to ETSI EN 302 636

The following Packet Type Designators are available for these packets:

| Protocol Designator | Packet Type Designator | Default | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| <application message> | — | — | Initializes the packet as application message. |
| btp | BTP-A | — | Initializes the Basic Transport Protocol in the packet as BTP-A. |
| BTP-B | ✔ | Initializes the Basic Transport Protocol in the packet as BTP-B. |
| geo_eh | BEACON | — | Initializes the Extended Header of the packet as Beacon. |
| GUC | ✔ | Initializes the Extended Header of the packet as GeoUnicast. |
| GAC | — | Initializes the Extended Header of the packet as GeoAnycast. |
| GBC | — | Initializes the Extended Header of the packet as GeoBroadcast. |
| TSB-SHB | — | Initializes the Extended Header of the packet as Topologically Scoped Broadcast - Single Hop Broadcast. |
| TSB-MH | — | Initializes the Extended Header of the pack

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Car2x/basics] car2xFrameStructure80211p


# Structure of 802.11p Frames

Car2x » Structure of 802.11p Frames

## Packet Length

The packet length of a Car2x packet specifies the number of data in byte that are displayed in the Detail view of the Trace Window, starting from the WLAN header to the end of the payload. The Ethernet header that is generated and added to the packet due to easier analysis is included.

## Payload Length

The payload length specifies the number of data in bytes that are contained in the highest protocol that is interpreted by CANoe. For protocols that are supported by the option Car2x all bytes until packet end are included to the payload, independent of the length information that is specified in protocol header tokens.

|  | Car2x Application Data Application data, that are defined with ASN.1, are displayed separately if the corresponding ASN.1 module is available. |

Option .Car2x

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Car2x/basics] car2xFrameStructureCV2x


# Structure of C-V2x Frames

Car2x » Structure of C-V2x Frames

## Packet length

The packet length of a C-V2X packet comprises the number of data in bytes that are output in the Trace Window in the detail view, starting with the C-V2X Data Link Layer up to the end of the ASN.1 payload data. The C-V2X Data Link Layer is also counted.

## C-V2X Data Link Layer

To provide simplified access to the selected managed data in MAC, RLC (Radio Link Control) and PDCP (Packet Data Convergence Protocol) headers of the C-V2X packet, a C-V2X Data Link Layer is generated and inserted into the Car2x packet.

## Country specific payload

The value of the message family byte contained in the C-V2X layer determines according to which of the three possible C-V2X standards (Europe, US, China) the subsequent headers and payload data are structured. The structure of the headers is described in the protocol overview.

Option .Car2x

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Car2x/Hardware] c2xCV2xHardware


# C-V2x Hardware

Car2x » C-V2x Hardware

## C-V2x Communication

C-V2x here refers to the direct communication between traffic participants or traffic infrastructure units (RSUs) in accordance with 3GPP Release 14 Mode 4 (PC5). The following conditions apply:

- No cellular base stations are involved or necessary.

- A valid GNSS signal (fix) must be physically available for successful communication, since PC5 mode uses accurate GNSS time as a common time base for time-slot based resource assignment.

## C-V2x Hardware

No hardware developed by Vector exists for C-V2x communications. However, it is possible to connect a third-party C-V2X hardware with CANoe.

Currently the following hardware is supported: Autotalks Craton2 EVK CUT3 Revision F.

|  | Note Make sure that the hardware is Revision F, because only this version enables C-V2x communication using the CCSA radio settings (China). |

## Commissioning the C-V2x Hardware

The connection between CANoe and C-V2x hardware is made via Ethernet (UDPv4), using the computer's Ethernet network card.

In order for CANoe to communicate with C-V2x hardware, additional software (RMTPCD: Remote Packet Capture Daemon) must be installed on the device. More information can be found on this web page.

The configuration of the C-V2x hardware is done via the V2x Hardware Configuration dialog.

A detailed procedure for commissioning and configuration is described on the Connect a C-V2x Device help page.

Option Car2x

| Version 15© Vector

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Car2x/Hardware] c2xGNSSSimulatorVN4610


# GNSS Simulator and VN4610

Car2x » Position and Time » GNSS Simulator and VN4610

The VN4610 provides a GNSS channel. Instead of a GNSS receiver for receiving real data, a GNSS simulator can also be connected to the channel (e.g. in a HiL setup). This has two consequences:

- When GNSS logging files are replayed, the GNSS time stamps contained in the logging file are transmitted to the option Car2x via the GNSS channel of the VN4610. This timestamp is then used as the measurement start timestamp. If the GNSS logging files are in the past, all Car2x/V2x/C-V2x packets created with the option Car2x will also have a time stamp in the past.

- When GNSS logging files are replayed, the GNSS position data contained in the logging file are transmitted to the option Car2x via the GNSS channel of the VN4610. This position data is then stored in the GNSS system variables of the option Car2x and can be used in a variety of ways (e.g. in analysis windows or in CAPL).

Option Car2x

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Car2x/Hardware] c2xGNSSTimestampSourceAndUse


# GNSS Timestamp Source and Use

Car2x » Position and Time » GNSS Timestamp Source and Use

The time provided by a VN4610 via GNSS can be used in the Car2x option as a reference for the measurement time. How to set up a GNSS channel is described here.

The GNSS signals can either be received in real terms via the supplied GNSS receiver or imported via a GNSS simulator, for example.

If a GNSS time stamp is available and is used as a reference for the measurement time, this is displayed in the trace window with a note when the measurement is started:

This timestamp is then also used as a reference/base for the following:

- The CAPL function C2xGetITSTimestamp.

- Generation Timestamp for all EU/US/CN certificates.

- Geo-EH timestamp.

- Timestamps for EU application messages when the stack is enabled (e.g. CAM Generation Delta Time, DENM DetectionTime / ReferenceTime).

In addition, all GNSS signals and thus also the GNSS timestamp are made available as system variables and are thus globally available in the Car2x option (e.g. for further processing and use in CAPL, in panels or in analysis windows such as the graphics or data window).

Option Car2x

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Car2x/Hardware] c2xLeapSeconds


# Information about Leap Seconds

Car2x » Position and Time » Information about Leap Seconds

CANoe considers the following leap seconds (in relation to UTC±0) for the conversion between TAI and UTC:

| From | To | TAI-UTC | since 2004-01-01 | since 2010-01-01 |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| - | 2012-06-30 23:59:60 | 34 s | 2 s | 0 s |
| 2012-07-01 00:00:00 | 2015-06-30 23:59:60 | 35 s | 3 s | 1 s |
| 2015-07-01 00:00:00 | 2016-12-31 23:59:60 | 36 s | 4 s | 2 s |
| 2017-01-01 00:00:00 | – | 37 s | 5 s | 3 s |

Option .Car2x

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Car2x/Hardware] c2xPositionAndTime


# Position and Time

Car2x » Position and Time

If a VN4610 is available as hardware, there are various possibilities due to the GNSS connection for the position and time used in the option Car2x .

The time provided via GNSS can be used in the option Car2x as a reference for the measurement time. The GNSS signals can either be really received via the supplied GNSS receiver or imported via a GNSS simulator, for example.

The position data made available via GNSS can be used in the option Car2x in a variety of ways. Stored in system variables, you have access to it everywhere in the Car2x option, e.g. in CAPL code, in panels or in one of the analysis windows. You can also configure the sending behavior of stations in the Car2x Network Explorer to use the real position data in the Car2x frames. For more on these topics, see the cross-references below.

|  | Cross Reference Configure sending behavior Process GNSS Data GNSS timestamp source and use GNSS Simulator and VN4610 Analysis of timestamps Information about Leap Seconds |

Option .Car2x

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Car2x/Hardware] c2xTimestampAnalysis


# Timestamp Analysis

Car2x » Position and Time » Timestamp Analysis

In CANoe Car2x and the protocols used, a variety of different timestamps in different formats are used. The following two tables are intended to provide an overview.

## Timestamp of the EU/US/CN protocols

| Timestamp of the EU/US/CN protocols | Format | Intended use / description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| EU |
| GEO-EH Timestamp | 32 Bit uint Timestamp = TAI milliseconds since 01.01.2004 00:00:00.000 UTC Modulo 32 | Time at which the generation of the geographic position takes place. No complete absolute time stamp due to modulo operation. In the option Car2x, the display in the Trace Detail View is calculated back to the full time stamp based on the measurement start time. Is automatically set in the option Car2x in the CompletePacket. |
| Security Header - Generation Timestamp | 64 Bit uint Timestamp= TAI microseconds since 01.01.2004 00:00:00.000 UTC | Time at which the message using the certificate was generated. Is automatically set in the option Car2x in the CompletePacket. |
| Security Header - Start Timestamp | 32 Bit uint Timestamp = TAI seconds since 01.01.2004 00:00:00.000 UTC | Time from which the certificate is valid. Is created when generating of the certificate. The duration, which is also included in the certificate, can be used to determine the validity period of the certificate. |
| CAM Generation Delta Timestamp | 16 Bit uint Timestamp = TAI milliseconds since 01.01.2004 00:00:00.000 UTC

### [Car2x/Hardware/car2xHwConfig] car2xHwConfigPageSetup


# Network Hardware Configuration - Setup

Network Hardware » Ath » Setup

Ribbon: Hardware Tab|Network Hardware|Ath|Setup

| Ath | Setup | Signal Protocol |  |

You can set the communication parameter for an application channel on this page.

The dialog allows to configure the following parameters of the VN4610:

- Channel Type:This entry allows -depending on the country code- to preconfigure some parameters to suitable default values.

- Radio channel

- Band width

- Transmission power:See also transmission power restrictions applied in tolling areas

- Data rate

- Protocol Layout Defines whether EPD or LPD frames are used for transmission.The protocol type LPD is usually used for Car2x communication within the EU. In the USA, EPD is usually used. However, a correct protocol type must be used so that standard-compliant Car2x communication is possible.The protocol types EPD (EtherType protocol discrimination) and LPD (LLC protocol discrimination) allow the formal discrimination of the Car2x packets (IEEE 802.11p standard) from the other protocols and services of the IEEE 802 family. LPD and EPD headers define the structure of the beginning of a Car2x packet and thus how it is transmitted over the radio link:

- LPD Car2x header is 8 bytes long and uses Ethertype 0x8947 and additional LLC/SNAP/... fields.

- EPD Car2x header is 2 bytes long and uses Ethertype 0x88DC

- Tx-Antenna Allows selection of one of the two Tx-antennas on VN4610 device. The Auto setting is recommende

### [Car2x/Hardware/car2xHwConfig] car2xTransPowerRestriction


# Transmission Power Restrictions

Car2x » Network Hardware Configuration Setup Page » Transmission Power Restrictions

## Transmit power restrictions applied in tolling areas

In order to avoid interference between Car2x (DSRC) and ITS-G5A/B (tolling) radio communication, the coexistence rules (reduced transmit power) are applied at sending Car2x equipment (VN6410 device) as defined in ETSI TS 102 792. This applies to VN4610 devices operating in European mode only (see VN4610 country code).

The transmit power of the VN4610 device will be automatically restricted to 10 dBm if at least one of the following reasons apply:

- The GPS position of the VN4610 device cannot be determined due to missing valid GPS data. Thus it cannot be determined whether the VN4610 is within a radius of less than 250m from a known toll station or not.

- The current GPS position of the VN4610 device is closer than 250 m to the next known permanently installed tolling station (like tolling bridges on the highways).

- The current GPS position of the VN4610 is closer than 250 m to the toll station, which is described in a CAM frame received during measurement. The toll zone definitions are taken from the following optional elements of the CAM frame and used for the duration of the measurement.

If none of the above reasons exist, the VN4610 will automatically return to the previously used transmit power level, which is defined as follows:

- Directly after measurement start the transmit power is equ

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Car2x/Hardware/v2x] c2xV2XHwConfig


# V2X Hardware Configuration

Car2x » Procedures » Connect C-V2X Device » V2X Hardware Configuration

Ribbon: Hardware Tab|Channels|V2X Hardware

For a third party V2X device configuration you can assign the application channels used by CANoe (e.g. Ath 1) to V2X devices in this dialog. The V2X devices are connected to the computer via the local network and must be accessible by CANoe under an IP address.

## Available Devices

Each line of this list represents a device-interface combination. You can assign them to an application channel, which you select in the Application Channel column. This application channel is used to address or identify the WLAN interfaces within CANoe.

You can enter default values in the columns Radio Channel, Bandwidth, Tx Power and Data Rate. You can later apply these values for a channel in the Network Hardware Configuration dialog on the Setup page.

The GNSS Data column indicates whether the device should process GNSS data.

The LED in the Identifier column shows the status of the listed V2X devices. After a scan for V2X devices using the [Search Devices] button, you can recognize the status of the respective V2X device by the color of the LED.

| LED | Device Status |
| --- | --- |
| green | The V2X device is present and available. |
| black | The device is not available. |

## Buttons

| Button | Description |
| --- | --- |
| [Add...] | Use this button to open the Add Device dialog. In this dialog you can add a new V2X device to the list. |
|

### [Car2x/procedures/c2xProceduresAnalysis] c2xAnalyzeNewTraffic


# Analyze New Network Traffic

Car2x » Procedures » Analyze New Network Traffic

To analyze new network traffic, proceed as follows.

|  | Step by Step Procedure Connect a VN4610 and configure the hardware. See also the help page Operate a VN4610. Add a trace window to the measurement setup. Start the measurement in real mode. If frames are now received, they are displayed in the Trace Window: |

In the Trace Window, you also have the option of making various settings that simplify the analysis. These include the detail view, which gives you an insight into the frame content, or, for example, a change between continuous mode (each received frame is displayed under the previously received one) or fixed mode (only the most current frame per channel is displayed).

Option Car2x

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Car2x/procedures/c2xProceduresAnalysis] c2xLogReplayNetworkTraffic


# Log and Replay Network Traffic

Car2x » Procedures » Log and Replay Network Traffic

## Log Network Traffic

To log frames, proceed as follows.

|  | Step by Step Procedure Go to the measurement setup. Insert a logging block. With a right click you open the Logging File Configuration dialog. Configure the logging block.If necessary, specify the target path and the name of the logging file. At the start of the measurement, all frames you receive are recorded and saved in the logging file. Only BLF Frame Logging is supported as file format for CANoe .Car2x. |

Replay Network Traffic

If you want to replay or analyze this log file later, you have various options. Always deactivate your logging block before doing this so that the original logfile is not overwritten.

|  | Option 1: Replay of Logfiles Insert a replay block in the simulation setup. You are still in online mode, i.e. .Car2x will use the current system time as the time base and the simulation setup is still active. |

|  | Option 2: Replay of Logfiles in Offline Mode In offline mode, specify your previously recorded log file as the source. The offline mode gets its time base from the log file, the simulation setup is not active. In the offline mode you have the possibility to replay all frames immediately (as fast as possible) or as animation. For more information, see the Offline Mode help page. |

Option .Car2x

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |

Car2x » Procedures » Use Application Messages in Analysis Window

|  | Step by Step Procedure Add a Car2x database to a WLAN channel. Then create sender nodes in the Car2x database with the Car2x Network Explorer from which the application messages are to be sent. The available application messages are now displayed in the Symbol Explorer for each sender node. |

The messages are organized in a tree view and the elements represents the signals. These signals can be used in the Data and Graphics Window via drag and drop or copy and paste.

### [Car2x/procedures/c2xProceduresAnalysis] c2xUseStationManager


# Use the Car2x Station Manager

Car2x » Procedures » Use the Car2x Station Manager

In case you do not use the automatic procedure to merge ITS stations, there are different possibilities to merge them manually.

### Merge ITS Stations

You can merge individual ITS stations via drag and drop. All MAC/ HashedId8 pairs are added to the aimed station and the original station is deleted. One reason for the manual merge of ITS stations could be that you do not use the automatic assignment based on ITS station movement but still want to assign newly appearing station created by a certificate change to an already existing station. This is especially suited if you want to continue observing signals in the Data or Graphics Window since the assignment would be lost if a certificate change occurs. In case you assigned the new station wrongly, you may delete the station completely or remove individual MAC addresses/ HashedID8s (see below).

### Delete ITS Stations

In case you accidentally merged ITS stations that should not belong together, you have the possibility to delete such an ITS station. Either you select the station and then press <DEL> or you delete it via the context menu. The station and the MAC/ HashedId8 saved in it will disappear. In case one or various certificates of the former ITS station should reappear during further measurements, new ITS stations will be created. In case you accidentally added one or various MAC/ HashedId8 pairs to an ITS station, you have the pos

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Car2x/procedures/c2xProceduresConfig] c2xAddDatabase


# Add a Car2x Database

Car2x » Procedures » Add a Car2x Database

To use application messages (e.g. CAM/DENM (ETSI) or BSM/TIM (SAE)), you have to assign a Car2x database to a WLAN channel (e.g. ASNV_Configuration.xml). This database is a part of the sample configuration of the option Car2x and is contained in folder Vector_CN_ASNV.

If you want to create a new configuration it is recommendable to use a basic configuration in folder Car2x Demos/MoreExamples of the Car2x sample configurations and extend this configuration. To do this copy the folder Car2x_BasicConfiguration (ETSI) or V2x_BasicConfiguration (SAE) in the folder where you want to save your new configuration. The Car2x database and an example sender node are already contained in these sample configurations. To add further sender nodes to the database or modify existing sender nodes, open the database with the Car2x Network Explorer.

If a configuration does not contain a database yet, assign a database to a WLAN channel. Then open the database with the Car2x Network Explorer to create sender nodes.

If you want to extend an existing database or create a new one, follow the steps described here.

|  | Cross Reference How to assign a database to a channel is described here. |

### [Car2x/procedures/c2xProceduresHW] c2xConnectCV2XDevice


# Connect a C-V2X Device

Car2x » Procedures » Connect a C-V2X Device

## Activate the C-V2X Physical Layer

Communication via C-V2X (3GPP Rel. 14 Mode 4 (PC5)) uses its own frame format, which differs from IEEE 802.11p (DSRC). Therefore a switchover of the physical layer is necessary. Switching is done in the CANoe Options dialog under Bus Systems/Protocols on the Car2x page in the Physical Layer area via the Technologie list. The upper protocol layers (GeoNetworking, BTP, DSMP, ASN.1 Application Layer) are reused and are still described and parameterized in the database.

## C-V2X Hardware Configuration

If the measurement in CANoe is performed on the real bus instead of a simulated bus, a connected C-V2X device is required. The integration and assignment of C-V2X devices to application channels in CANoe (Ath1, Ath2, …) is done via the V2X Hardware Configuration dialog. If the communication interface of a C-V2X device is assigned to an application channel (e.g. Ath1), you can make other settings, such as radio channel, in the Network Hardware Configuration dialog for the selected application channel.

## GNSS

In addition to the C-V2X interface, the C-V2X devices provide the GNSS position data and the GNSS time source, which are available via the system variable GNSS::Ath<N>, where <N> is the number of the application channel.

## C-V2X RMTPCD (Remote Packet Capture Daemon)

To enable CANoe option .Car2x to communicate with a C-V2X device via a LAN interface of the CANoe c

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Car2x/procedures/c2xProceduresHW] c2xOperateVN4610


# Operate a VN4610

Procedures » Operate a VN4610

To put a VN4610 with CANoe .Car2x into operation, proceed as follows.

|  | Step by Step Procedure Install the appropriate driver from the Vector driver CD.This is the prerequisite to be able to put a VN4610 into operation. Connect the VN4610 to the computer/laptop via USB. Launch CANoe.Car2x. Click ribbon tab Hardware|Channels|Network Hardware in the menu bar.The Network Hardware Config dialog opens. Click [Driver...].The Vector Hardware Configuration dialog opens. Assign the channels of the VN4610 under Application|CANoe by right-clicking on the interface device channels. To provide an accurate UTC-based time base, a GNSS channel should always be configured in real mode. The CANoe reference timer is then automatically set to the corresponding module: Close the dialog.You return to the network hardware configuration dialog. Here you are shown whether the device is physically connected. In the Setup area you can set various communication parameters if necessary. Details can be found on the help page of the network hardware configuration dialog (help: Setup tab). Click [Ok].The dialog closes. Make sure you are in real mode. Start the measurement. |

With CANoe .Car2x you also have the possibility to change or read out transmission parameters at runtime. Use the CAPL functions C2xConfigureChannel, C2xSetSendParameters or C2xGetChannelConfiguration.

Option Car2x

| GeoidHeight | Double | – | m | Height of Geoid |

| VDOP | Double | 0..50 | – | Attenuation of the vertical accuracy (0.01). The smaller the VDOP-value, the better the geometry. |
| PDOP | Double | 0..50 | – | Attenuation of the accuracy of position (0.01). The smaller the PDOP-value , the better the geometry. |
| SatellitesInUse | Long | 0..255 | – | Number of used satellites |
| SatellitesInView | Long | 0..255 | – | Number of viewable satellites |
| Timestamp | Double | – | s | GNSS time |
| MagneticVariation | Double | -180..180 | ° | Declination: The angle between the direction to the Earth's Arctic magnetic pole and the geographic north direction. |
| Status | Long | 0..2 | – | No Fix (0) Fix (1) Differenti

### [Car2x/procedures/c2xProceduresSecurity] c2xReceiveSignedMessagesSH


# Receive and Analyze Signed Messages

Procedures » Use Security Header » Receive and Analyze Signed Messages

// The callback for the message receipt must be registered in the// Prestart function.on prestart{ C2xReceivePacket( "OnC2xPacket");}

// The callback is called when new messages are received.void OnC2xPacket( LONG channel, LONG dir, LONG radioChannel, LONG signalStrength, LONG signalQuality, LONG packet ){ long signatureStatus; long certHdl; char errorTxt[256]; byte hashedId8[8]; char signerTxt[256]; // Query the security status of the received message. signatureStatus = C2xSecPacketGetStatus(packet); switch (signatureStatus & 0xf000) // Filter out the main status { case 0x1000: // Signature cannot be checked due to missing information. write("signature unknown, message ignored"); return; case 0x2000: // Signature is invalid write("signature unknown, message ignored"); return; case 0x3000: // Signature is valid write("signature valid"); break; default: // Error, e.g., no Security Header in the message C2xGetLastErrorText(elcount(errorTxt), errorTxt); write("signature error \"%s\", message ignored", errorTxt); return; } // Certificate handle with which the message was signed certHdl = C2xSecPacketGetSignerHandle(packet) ; // Name that was assigned in the Car2x Certificate Manager (possibly empty) C2xSecCertificateGetName(certHdl, elcount(signerTxt), signerTxt); if (signerTxt[0] == 0) // No name assigned or name not stored in the Car2x Certificate Manager { // Hashed

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Car2x/procedures/c2xProceduresSecurity] c2xSendSignedMessagesSH


# Send Signed Messages

Procedures » Use Security Header » Send Signed Messages

## Example for ETSI TS 103 097

// Send function that is executed when the <s> key is pressedon key 's'{ long pktHdl; // Packet Handle long certHdl; // Certificate Handle certHdl = C2xSecCertificateGetHandle("name"); // "name" is the name assigned in the Car2x Certificate Manager. // It is also possible to reference a certificate using its // HashedId8 value, which is not recommended since it is // changed when a certificate is renewed, e.g., due to // expiration of the validity date, and the program code // would have to be changed. Instead, it is easier to // replace the certificate stored under the name in the // Car2x Certificate Manager. // Create a packet with Security Header // ("SH" in 2nd parameter). // (Here the document version ETSI TS 103 097 V1.1.1 of the standard is used.) pktHdl = C2xInitPacket("btp", "BTP-B|TSB-SHB|SH"); // Assign the certificate to the packet. C2xSecPacketSetSignerHandle(pktHdl, certHdl); // Specify the transmission method of the certificate // (HashedId8, certificate, or certificate chain) C2xSetTokenInt(pktHdl, "geo_sh", "signerType", 0); C2xSetTokenInt(pktHdl, "geo_sh", "securityProfile", 1); C2xSetTokenInt64(pktHdl, "geo_sh", "generationTime", C2xConvertTimeToMSSinceUTC(C2xGetITSTimestamp(), 1)*1000); // Additional Security Header fields can be set with the Packet API. // Coding and signing C2xCompletePacket(pktHdl); // Sending of the message C2xOutputPacket

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Car2x/procedures/c2xProceduresSecurity] c2xUseCertificates


# Use Certificates

Car2x » Procedures » Use Certificates

## Decode Certificates

Certificates transmitted in the Security Header can be decoded automatically in CANoe. The procedure is described on the page Use Security Header.

## Import/ Export Certificates

Certificates not transmitted in messages but needed for verification can be imported and also exported again in the Car2x Certificate Manager.

## Generate Certificates

If no certificates issued by a trustworthy certificate issuer are available, test certificates can be generated in the Car2x Certificate Manager. Since the test certificates are not signed by a trustworthy issuer, other ITS Stations will not trust certificates or messages signed by these test certificates.

## Analyze Certificates

The Car2x Certificate Explorer is used to explore all certificates known in CANoe, that is, the certificates configured in the Car2x Certificate Manager and the certificates received during the measurement. It shows the trustworthiness, the certificate hierarchy and the content of the certificates.

|  | More Security Procedures Create and manage certificates Analyse certificates Use Security Header Receive and Analyze Signed Messages Send Signed Messages Further Information Security-Concept Protocol overview of the supported security variants |

Option .Car2x

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Car2x/procedures/c2xProceduresSecurity] c2xUseSecurityHeader


# Use Security Header

Car2x » Procedures » Use Security Header

CANoe can automatically decode certificates transmitted in the Security Header.

|  | Cross Reference Receive and Analyze Signed Messages Send Signed Messages Use Certificates |

In order to evaluate the validity of the signed messages or in order to work with signed messages in CAPL, you must execute the following preparations.

- Open a configuration with at least one WLAN (Ath) channel.

- Check the used versions of the standards ETSI TS 103 097 and IEEE 1609.2, and adapt them if necessary.

- Enter the used certificates in the Car2x Certificate Manager. You can add existing certificates or create new test certificates. For the analysis, at least the root certificate must be present and marked as trustworthy. In addition, all certificates whose signatures are to be evaluated from measurement start must be present. To more easily identify pseudonym certificates in particular, a name can be assigned to each certificate that is then displayed in the analysis windows. Signatures of certificates not present can only be evaluated when the missing certificates are received and can be assigned to a root certificate. If signed messages are to be sent from CAPL, you must also enter the private key for the pseudonym certificates in the Car2x Certificate Manager. Observe the information on protection of private keys on the help page for the Car2x Certificate Manager. In addition, you should assign a name that can be use

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Car2x/procedures/c2xProceduresSimStim] c2xConfigureStackBehaviorCN


# Configure Stack Behavior (CN)

Car2x » Procedures » Configure Stack Behavior (CN)

## C-V2X Stack

C-V2X Stack means that the setting of certain fields in the frame as well as cycle time calculations are automatically taken over by CANoe. The C-V2X stack is currently only implemented for the BasicSafetyMessage application message according to Chinese standards CSAE-0053 (2020-09), GB/T 31024.3-2020.

## Configuration of the C-V2X Stack in the Car2x Network Explorer

How the C-V2X stack can be configured in the Car2x Network Explorer is described in Configuring the sending behavior.

## C-V2X Stack and Scenario Editor

When the C-V2X stack is activated, by default fields are set in the frame. By using scenarios, the frames can be further completed with dynamic fields such as Latitude, Longitude, Speed and Heading. If attributes are created and set in the Scenario Editor that were previously filled by the C-V2X stack, the data from the Scenario Editor is applied.

## C-V2X Stack and CAPL

When the C-V2X stack is activated, by default fields are set in the frame. By using CAPL, the frames can be further completed with dynamic fields such as Latitude, Longitude, Speed and Heading. This can be done e.g. in an OnPreTx CAPL callback. If fields are set in CAPL that were previously filled by the C-V2X stack, the data from CAPL is applied.

## V2X Stack - CAM

Overview of the filled fields in the frame:

| Protocol Layer | Field | Value |
| --- | --- | --- |
| C-V2X Data Link Layer 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Car2x/procedures/c2xProceduresSimStim] c2xConfigureStackBehaviorEU


# Configure Stack Behavior (EU)

Car2x » Procedures » Configure Stack Behavior (EU)

## V2X Stack

V2X Stack means that CANoe automatically takes care of setting certain fields in the frame and calculating cycle times. The V2X stack for the EU application messages CAM and DENM is currently implemented. The V2X stack is based on the Basic System Profile 1.5.2 of the Car2Car Communication Consortium.

## Configuration of the V2X Stack in the Car2x Network Explorer

How the V2X stack can be configured in the Car2x Network Explorer is described in Configuring the sending behavior.

## V2X Stack and Scenario Editor

When the V2X stack is activated, by default fields are set in the frame. By using scenarios, the frames can be further completed with dynamic fields such as Latitude, Longitude, Speed and Heading. If attributes are created and set in the Scenario Editor that were previously filled by the V2X stack, the data from the Scenario Editor is applied.

## V2X Stack and CAPL

When the V2X stack is activated, by default fields are set in the frame. By using CAPL, the frames can be further completed with dynamic fields such as Latitude, Longitude, Speed and Heading. This can be done e.g. in an OnPreTx CAPL callback. If fields are set in CAPL that were previously filled by the V2X stack, the data from CAPL is applied.

## V2X Stack - CAM

Overview of the filled fields in the frame:

| Protocol Layer | Field | Value |
| --- | --- | --- |
| GEO-BH | Version | 1 |
| Lifetime | 1 (On

### [Car2x/procedures/c2xProceduresSimStim] c2xIncludeCar2xIL


# Include the Car2x Interaction Layer

Simulate Car2x Nodes » Include the Car2x Interaction Layer

If you want to access the WLAN network from a CAPL program, you must include the Car2x Interaction Layer (Car2x IL) C2x_IL.dll. Afterwards, you can send and receive WLAN packets.

## Include the Car2x Interaction Layer

The Car2x Interaction Layer extends the functionality of a simulated node by adding CAPL functions. To make it possible for a node to use the extensions for WLAN, you must assign the Car2x IL as the component to the node in the Simulation Setup.

|  | Step by Step Procedure Open the node's configuration dialog from the nodes' context menu. Switch to page Components. Open the file selection dialog by clicking the [Add...] button. In the Exec32 directory of your CANoe installation, look for the file C2X_IL.dll. Close the dialog with [OK]. Now you can use the CAPL functions for Car2x. |

As an alternative, you can insert the Car2x IL using a database.

|  | Step by Step Procedure Add a Car2x database to your configuration (see Add Car2x Database). Insert a new node in the Simulation Setup. Select the node and open the context menu. Select the command Configuration. Select a network node from the database and click [OK]. The Car2x Interaction Layer is automatically included for this node. |

Option .Car2x

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Car2x/procedures/c2xProceduresSimStim] c2xSimStimITSStations


# Simulate and Stimulate ITS Stations

Car2x » Procedures » Simulate and Stimulate ITS Stations

To simulate and stimulate ITS stations, proceed as follows.

|  | Step by Step Procedure Create a new configuration for the simulation/stimulation of ITS stations. Depending on the standard to be used, we recommend that you use one of the supplied templates: Car2xTemplate EN 302 637 (2014) Car2xTemplate EN 302 637 (2018) V2xTemplate SAE J2735 (2010) V2xTemplate SAE J2735 (2016) Configure the database in the simulation setup with the Car2x Network Explorer: For example, change the simulation and stimulation behavior of the ITS stations. If necessary, add one or more application messages to a network node. The transmission behavior of these application messages can then be configured with the attributes (cyclic transmission, cycle time, use of a certificate, ...). Close the Car2x Network Explorer Now configure the simulation setup: Create a node in the simulation setup for each network node in the database. Then assign this node the network node from the database (DB node): The transmission behavior of the node from the simulation setup is now linked to the network node from the database. |

In the CAPL code from the templates different callback functions like OnPreTxApplMsg and OnRxApplMsg are implemented. The OnPreTxApplMsg function is called before a frame is sent. At this point you have the possibility to change the content of the frame with the Car2x Packet-API functions. The

### [Car2x/procedures/c2xProceduresSimStim] c2xSimulateNodesWithIL


# Simulate Car2x Nodes

Car2x » Procedures » Simulate Car2x Nodes

To simulate Car2x nodes, the Car2x Interaction Layer offers the following possibilities.

|  | Note Without the configuration of a network node of the database, you can only use the functions of the Car2x Interaction Layer if you included the Car2x Interaction Layer. |

## Database and Car2x IL API

For basic cyclic transmission of Car2x application messages, the Car2x Interaction Layer offers the possibility to configure both the protocol layers (including the Security Layer) and the transmission behavior in the database. By means of additional CAPL functions (Car2x IL API) you can influence the cycle time during the measurement, specifically activate and deactivate the transmission and change the values of individual elements of the application message. For this purpose, a callback function enables the update of the content of the application message immediately before the transmission.

## Generated CAPL Include Files (Car2x Network Explorer)

For the comfortable access to individual elements of the application messages to be sent and received, you can generate a CAPL include file for each application message providing specific data structures and methods by means of the Car2x Network Explorer. For access to the individual elements, the Car2x Network Explorer also generates the necessary CAPL code lines.

## Packet API

For access to the fields of the individual protocol layers, the Packet API offers exten
