### [Car2x/procedures/c2xProceduresTest] c2xTestECU


# Test a Car2x Control Unit

Car2x » Procedures » Test a Car2x Control Unit

With the optionCar2x you can test an ECU in many ways. Some possibilities are described below.

## Stimulation/analysis in HiL

|  | Option 1 - Stimulating the ECU with WLAN / C-V2x packets. With the option Car2x you can create scenarios using the Scenario Editor. The corresponding WLAN or C-V2x packets are generated from these scenarios. Using a VN4610 or third-party C-V2x hardware, these packets are sent to the ECU under test. Whether the processing of the data in the ECU was successful can be checked, for example, via CAN messages that the ECU places on the internal networks for further processing of the data. These CAN messages can be received via the CAN interface of the VN4610 and checked for correctness in a test environment. Car2x specific test functions facilitate the generation of tests at this point. A generated test report shows which tests were successful and which failed. Option 2 - Stimulating the ECU with CAN messages CAN messages are generated in the option Car2x and sent to the ECU via the VN4610 CAN interface. These CAN messages are intended to trigger the transmission of a specific WLAN / C-V2x packet, for example. Via the antenna of the VN4610 (or a third party hardware in case of C-V2x tests) it can be checked if the corresponding packet has been sent. A test environment can also automatically check whether the tests were successful. A generated test report shows which tests we

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Car2x/procedures/c2xProceduresTest] c2xTFSAPI


# Use Test Service Library

Car2x » Procedures » Use Test Service Library

The Car2x Test Service Library helps you to perform tests in the Car2x environment. For this purpose CAPL functions are provided to wait for receiving Car2x messages. An example configuration demonstrating the use of the API can be found here.

These functions can be used in test modules and TestUnits. An overview of the general test options in CANoe can be found here.

In the Car2x Test Service Library there are wait functions that wait for receiving single messages (C2xTestWaitForMessage). There are also wait functions that wait for a message to be received with a token in a certain range of values (C2xTestWaitForSignalInRange , C2xTestWaitForSignalMatch ). For all wait functions, there is an overload of the function that can be used to wait for messages from a particular station. The packets that trigger the wait functions can be accessed with the C2xTestGetWaitForMessage function. For all Car2x Test Service Library functions there is a code example in the help of the respective function.

You can also wait for receiving multiple messages. For this purpose there are corresponding C2xTestJoin functions for all C2xTestWait functions. The testWaitForAllJoinedEvents function can be used to wait for the reception of all jointed messages. The testWaitForAllJoinedEvents function is a cross-bus system function that can also be used to wait for messages from other bus systems. For example, a CAN message and

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Car2x/procedures/c2xProceduresTest] c2xUseTFSSampleConfig


# Use Test Service Library Sample Configuration

Car2x » Procedures » Use TFS Sample Configuration

The Test Service Library sample configuration demonstrates the use of the Car2x Test Service Library.

The functions of the Car2x Test Service Library are used in the configuration in a Test Unit. This contains a simple ECU test that tests the sending of a DENM message for EEBL (Emergency Electronic Brake Light) and a test that demonstrates the use of the Test Service Library in the scenario.

For the ECU test, the following is used:

- A CAN network with a CAN database containing a message for triggering an EEBL (Electronic Emergency Brake Light).

- A WLAN network containing an EU_ApplMsg database are used.

Within the test unit there are several test cases for the ECU test, that are all similar in structure. First a CAN message is sent. Depending on the transmitted values, a DENM message with the cause code for EEBL is then sent via a CAPL node simulating an ECU. In the individual test cases, the cause code of the EEBL is checked via different functions of the Car2x Test Service Library. Also, a test case list is used which executes a test case with multiple parameters.

For the test in which a scenario is used, first the scenario is started via the Scenario API. Then two test cases are executed. In the first test case, it is checked whether the station DUT sends both a CAM message and a DENM message with correct CauseCode. Here the C2xTestJoinMessage function of the Car2x 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Car2x/protocols] Action


# Action

Protocol Overview » Action

| Quick Access |
| --- |
| Protocol Information |
| Protocol Dependencies |
| Protocol and Packet Format |
| Header Format |

## Protocol Information

The frame body of the WLAN Management Frame with sub type Action contains specific information (see IEEE 802.11, IEEE 802.11p and IEEE 1609.12) that are represented by the Action protocol layer in CANoe.

## Protocol Dependencies [▲ top]

| Facility Layer |  |
| --- | --- |
| Network andTransport Layer | WSA |
| WSS |
| MAC Layer | Action |
| Physical Layer | WLAN |

If the type is set to Management and the sub type is set to Action in the Frame Control field of the WLAN header, the frame body of the Action frame is interpreted after the Sequence Control field. If the fields contain the values specified below, the following is interpreted as WAVE Security Services (WSS) layer and then a WAVE Service Advertisement (WSA) layer.

## Protocol and Packet Format [▲ top]

| Action Protocol |
| --- |
| Protocol Designator | action |
| Token | Token Designator | Offset [byte.bit] | Length [byte] |
| Header | header | 0 | 7 |
| Payload | data | 7 | n |

## Header Format [▲ top]

| Token | Token Designator | Offset[byte.bit] | Length[bit] | Packet API |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| C2xInitProtocol/C2xInitPacket | C2xCompletePacket |
| Category | category | 0 | 8 | 127 | – |
| category 127: vendor specific |
| Organization ID | organizationIdentifierTopNode | 1 | 40 | 0x0050C24A43 | – |
| Organiz

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Car2x/protocols] AIDandPSID


# AID and PSID

Car2x » Protocol Overview » AID and PSID

AID (Application Identifier) and PSID (Provider Service Identifier) are two names for the same ID. The term AID is used in the frame of ETSI GeoNetworking, the PSID is used in the frame of IEEE WAVE/DSRC. The IDs are unambiguous and are managed by the ISO. The complete list of AIDs and PSIDs can be found on the homepage of the ISO (http://standards.iso.org/iso/ts/17419/TS17419%20Assigned%20Numbers/TS17419_ITS-AID_AssignedNumbers.pdfview link address).

The IDs are mainly used for three purposes:

- The IDs show the content of higher protocol layers in a transport protocol

- By means of a PKI and with the help of certificates and the IDs the permissions of an ITS station is determined.

- A provider announces its own provided services via the IDs.

The following table shows frequently used AIDs and PSIDs with the assigned application messages.

| Dec | Hex | P-encoded | Name | Assigned Application Message | Domain |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 32 | 20 | 20 | Vehicle to vehicle safety and awareness | BSM - Basic Safety Message | IEEE WAVE/DSRC |
| 35 | 23 | 23 | WAVE Security Management |  | IEEE WAVE/DSRC |
| 36 | 24 | 24 | CA Basic Service | CAM - Cooperative Awareness Message | ETSI Geo Networking |
| 37 | 25 | 25 | DEN Basic Service | DENM -Decentralized Environmental Notification Message | ETSI Geo Networking |
| 38 | 26 | 26 | Misbehavior reporting for common applications |  | IEEE WAVE/DSRC |
| 131 |

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Car2x/protocols] ASN1


# Protocol Description Language ASN.1

Protocol Overview » Protocol Description Language ASN.1

## Protocol Information

»ASN.1 is a standardized description language to define the fields of a protocol and the data description of Car2x application messages. The definition is done in an ASN.1 module.

Application messages that are specified by the ETSI as CAM, DENM, SPat and TOPO or by the SAE as J2735 (BSM, TIM, ...) are described in ASN.1 in the Car2x environment.

## Protocol Dependencies

Application messages described with ASN.1 are transmitted with different protocols as their payload. Which protocol should be used for transmission must be defined during the import of the ASN.1 module.

## Protocol and Packet Format

The protocol designator is set during the import with the ASN.1 Import Wizard. The token designators are defined in the ASN.1 modules.

|  | Procedures With ASN.1 Process Application Messages with CAPL Import Application Messages Use Application Messages Show Physical Values with ASN.1 in Trace Window Initializing Several Protocol Layers |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |

## Protocol Information

In the C-V2X data link layer, the source and destination Layer-2 Identifiers are determined according to 3GPP TS 36.321 V14.11.0, Chapter 6.2.4 MAC header for SL-SCH.

## Protocol Dependencies [▲ top]

The data is interpreted as a C-V2X data link layer if the following conditions are met.

- The data frame is sent or received while the C-V2X PC5 physical layer is activated in CANoe Options dialog, subsection Bus Systems / Protocols|Car2x|Physical Layer|Technology

| Header | header | 0 | 6 |
| Payload | data | 6 | 0 |

| Token | Token Designator | Offset[byte.bit] | Length[bit] | Packet API |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| C2xInitProtocol/C2xInitPacket | C2xCompletePacket |
| Source Layer-2 identifier | srcl2id | 0 | 24 | - | - |
| Source Layer-2 identifier 0 .. 0xFFFFFE Source Layer-2 identifier of the sending C-V2X station 0xFFFFFF = Reserved |
| Destination Layer-2 identifier | destl2id | 24 | 24 | - | - |
| Destination Layer-2 identifier 0 .. 0xFFFFFE = Layer-2 identifier of the destination C-V2X station 0xFFFFFF = Broadcast (Default va

### [Car2x/protocols] protocolBTP


# Basic Transport Protocol

Protocol Overview » Basic Transport Protocol

| Quick Access |
| --- |
| Protocol Information |
| Protocol Dependencies |
| Protocol and Packet Format |
| Header Format |
| Packet API |

## Protocol Information

The Basic Transport Protocol (BTP) provides an end-to-end, connection-less transport service in »ITS ad hoc networks. Similar to UDP (User Datagram Protocol) it offers a minimal transport service, i.e. the non-guaranteed delivery of BTP-PDUs among BTP entities. It also allows protocol entities at the ITS facilities layer to directly access the services of the GeoNetworking (see ETSI EN 302 636-5-1).

## Protocol Dependencies [▲ top]

If Next Header = 1 or Next Header = 2 in the Common Network Header (CNH) the data are interpreted as Basic Transport Protocol.

## Protocol and Packet Format [▲ top]

| Basic Transport Protocol |
| --- |
| Protocol Designator | btp |
| Token | Token Designator | Offset [byte.bit] | Length [byte] |
| Header | header | 0 | 4 |
| Payload | data | 4 | 2218..2258 |

## Header Format [▲ top]

| Token | Token Designator | Offset[byte.bit] | Length[bit] | Packet API |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| C2xInitProtocol/C2xInitPacket | C2xCompletePacket |
| Destination Port | destination | 0 | 16 | 0 | - |
| destination port |
| Source Port | source | 2 | 16 | 0 | - |
| source port (available if Next Header field of Common Network Header is BTP-A) |
| Destination Port Info | destinationInfo | 2 | 16 | 0 | - |
| destinati

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Car2x/protocols] protocolDCC


# Decentralized Congestion Control (DCC)

Protocol Overview » Decentralized Congestion Control (DCC)

To avoid traffic overload over air a DCC mechanism is used as described by ETSI TS 102 687 V1.2.1 for the region EU. This approach limits the messages that can be transmitted within a given time period by enforcing a minimum time gap between consecutive messages. This minimum time gap depends on the measured busy ratio of the specific radio channel (CBR) and the so called Ton-time (how long the radio channel is blocked by this message). Table A.1 outlines the number of transmission opportunities when Ton < 1 milliseconds and Table A.2 provides values for when the Ton < 500 microseconds.

| State | CBR | Packet rate | Toff |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Relaxed | < 30 % | 10 Hz | 100 ms |
| Active 1 | 30 % to 39 % | 5 Hz | 200 ms |
| Active 2 | 40 % to 49 % | 2,5 Hz | 400 ms |
| Active 3 | 50 % to 60 % | 2 Hz | 500 ms |
| Restrictive | > 60 % | 1 Hz | 1 000 ms |

Table A.1: Mapping of CBR values to state and the currently allowed transmission opportunities per second when Ton is max 1 ms

| State | CBR | Packet rate | Toff |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Relaxed | < 30 % | 20 Hz | 50 ms |
| Active 1 | 30 % to 39 % | 10 Hz | 100 ms |
| Active 2 | 40 % to 49 % | 5 Hz | 200 ms |
| Active 3 | 50 % to 60 % | 4 Hz | 250 ms |
| Restrictive | > 60 % | 1 Hz | 1 000 ms |

Table A.2: Mapping of CBR values to state and the currently allowed transmission opportunities per second when Ton is 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Car2x/protocols] protocolEthernetCar2x


# Ethernet

Protocol Overview » Ethernet

| Quick Access |
| --- |
| Protocol Information |
| Protocol Dependencies |
| Protocol and Packet Format |
| Header Format |

## Protocol Information

The Ethernet header describes the source MAC address, destination MAC address, and the Ethernet type of the frame. If the Ethernet type is 0x0707 it is a C2C-CC GeoNetworking Header.

## Protocol Dependencies [▲ top]

For WLAN data frames the Ethernet protocol is based on the WLAN protocol.

## Protocol and Packet Format [▲ top]

| Ethernet |
| --- |
| Protocol Designator | eth |
| Token | Token Designator | Offset [byte.bit] | Length [byte] |
| Header | header | 0 | 14 |
| Payload | data | 14 | 2298 |

## Header Format [▲ top]

| Token | Token Designator | Offset[byte.bit] | Length[bit] | Packet API |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| C2xInitProtocol/C2xInitPacket | C2xCompletePacket |
| Destination | destination |  |  | FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF | - |
| the destination MAC address identifies the receiver of the WLAN frame |
| Source | source |  |  | 02:00:00:00:00:00 | - |
| the source MAC address identifies the sender of the WLAN frame |
| Ethernet Type | type |  |  | 0000h | - |
| type designator of the WLAN frame |

### [Car2x/protocols] ProtocolOverviewCN


# Protocol Overview China

Protocol Overview China

A large number of protocols used in the Chinese automotive sector are supported by CANoe. Therefore, the detail view of the Trace Window includes a detailed interpretation of the protocol information of each Car2x packet. In addition, programmatic access to protocol information of Car2x packets from CAPL nodes is possible.

## Graphical Representation

## Overview

| Layer | Protocol/Standard | Latest supported version |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Application Layer | CSAE-0053 | CSAE-0053 2020-09 |
| Security Layer | CN Sec | No final standard available |
| Transport Layer | DSMP | GB/T 31024.3-2020 |
| Network Layer |
| Data Link / Physical Layer | AL, C-V2x Layer, C-V2X Data Link Layer | 3GPP TS 24.386-2017 |

Option .Car2x | See Also

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |

# Protocol Overview Europe

Protocol Overview Europe

A large number of protocols used in the European automotive sector are supported by CANoe. Therefore, the detail view of the Trace Window includes a detailed interpretation of the protocol information of each Car2x packet. In addition, programmatic access to protocol information of Car2x packets from CAPL nodes is possible.

## Overview

| Layer | Protocol/Standard | Latest supported version |

| Security Layer | EU Sec | ETSI TS 103 097 V1.4.1 2020-10 |
| Transport Layer | BTP | ETSI EN 302 636-5-1 V2.1.0 2017-05 |

### [Car2x/protocols/GBT310243] ChinaSecurityHeader


# China Security Header

Protocol Overview » China Security Header

| Quick Access |
| --- |
| Protocol Information |
| Protocol Dependencies |
| Protocol and Packet Format |
| Trace Window |
| Header Format |
| Optional Tokens |

## Protocol Information

To protect the data of a C-V2X package, it can be secured by the protocol layer China Security Header. The CnSh layer consists of information about the signer, information about the payload, the payload itself and finally the signature (about the information about the payload and the payload itself).

## Protocol Dependencies [▲ top]

In the data frame with DSMP layer, the CnSh layer is interpreted if the first byte of the DSMP user data has the value 3. Then the application message is interpreted (if present).

## Trace Window [▲ top]

Since the data structure of the CnSh layer is unclear, a summary of the most important transmitted values is displayed in the detail view of the trace window under Summary. In some cases values are also displayed that do not appear in the message but are known to CANoe, such as the signature status or the certificate name. The complete transmitted data structure can be found under Message Tree. The user data is displayed interpreted according to the signature.

Invalid signatures are displayed in the Trace Window with an error symbol. If the signature of a message cannot be verified, a note appears in the Protocol Info column. This note remains even if a certificate is received at a later ti

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Car2x/protocols/GBT310243] protocolGBT310243AdaptionLayer


# Adaption Layer

Protocol Overview » Adaption Layer

| Quick Access |
| --- |
| Protocol Information |
| Protocol Dependencies |
| Protocol and Packet Format |
| Header Format |
| Packet API |

## Protocol Information

The Adaption Layer (GB/T 31024.3) is a network protocol that determines the subsequent network protocol (IPv4, IPv6 or DSMP).

## Protocol Dependencies [▲ top]

The data is interpreted as an adaptation layer if the following conditions are met

- In the C-V2X General Information Layer, the Message Family is set to four.

## Protocol and Packet Format [▲ top]

| Basic Transport Protocol |
| --- |
| Protocol Designator | al |
| Token | Token Designator | Offset [byte.bit] | Length [byte] |
| Header | header | 0 | 1 |
| Payload | data | 1 | n |

## Header Format [▲ top]

| Token | Token Designator | Offset[byte.bit] | Length[bit] | Packet API |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| C2xInitProtocol/C2xInitPacket | C2xCompletePacket |
| Protocol Type | protocolType | 0 | 8 | 4 | - |
| Used protocol type 0 = IPv6 4 = DSMP 5 = IPv4 |

## Packet API [▲ top]

A C-V2X layer is created during initialization. The Message Family of the C-V2X layer is set to four.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |

In the C-V2X layer, the Message Family is determined according to 3GPP TS 24.386 (2017-12).

## Protocol Dependencies [▲ top]

The data is interpreted as a C-V2X layer if the following conditions are met

- The Data is transferred in a C-V2X frame.

| Basic Transport Protocol |
| --- |

| Message Family 0 = Reserved 1 = US / IEEE WSMP 2 = ISO / CALM 3 = EU / ETSI GeoNet 4 = CH / GB/T DSMP 5 – 255 = Reserved |

## Packet API [▲ top]

During initialization, a C-V2X frame is created that differs from V2X frames among other things by the fact that no WLAN or ETH header is created.

## Protocol Information

The GeoNetworking protocol is a network layer protocol that provides packet routing in an ad hoc network. It makes use of geographical positions for packet transport. GeoNetworking supports the communication among individual »ITS stations as well as the distribution of packets in geographical area (see ETSI EN 302 636-4-1).

This protocol contains the Basic Header of the GeoNetworking protocol.

## Protocol Dependencies [▲ top]

If Ethertype=0x8947 in the Ethernet header, the data are interpreted as GeoNetworking protocol.

| C2xInitProtocol/C2xInitPacket | C2xCompletePacket |
| Version | version | 0 | 4 | 1 | — |
| version of the GeoNetworking protocol |
| Next Header | nextHeader | 0.4 | 4 | 0 | — |
| protocol of the next header 0: Any 1: Common Header 2: Secured Packet |
| Reserved | reserved | 1 | 8 | 0 | — |
| reserved |
| Lifetime | lifeTime | 2 | 8 | 0 | — |
| life

## Protocol Information

The GeoNetworking protocol is a network layer protocol that provides packet routing in an ad hoc network. It makes use of geographical positions for packet transport. GeoNetworking supports the communication among individual »ITS stations as well as the distribution of packets in geographical area (see ETSI EN 302 636-4-1).

This protocol contains the Common Header of the GeoNetworking protocol.

## Protocol Dependencies [▲ top]

The protocol order depends on the Next Header of the Basic Header. If the Next Header is 1, the Common Header is directly attached to the Basic Header. If the Next Header is 2, a Secured Packet protocol is initialized, which is then followed by the Common Header.

## Protocol Information

The GeoNetworking protocol is a network layer protocol that provides packet routing in an ad hoc network. It makes use of geographical positions for packet transport. GeoNetworking supports the communication among individual »ITS stations as well as the distribution of packets in geographical area (see ETSI EN 302 636-4-1).

This protocol contains the Extended Header of the GeoNetworking protocol.

| 4 | — | GeoBroadcast | SN + res + Source LPV + GeoAreaPos Lat + GeoAreaPos Long + Dist A + Dist B + angle + res |
| 5 | 0 | Topologically-scoped broadcast: Single Hop | Source LPV + Reserved |
| 5 | 1 | Topologically-scoped broadcast: Multi Hop | SN + res + Source LPV |
| 6 | 0 | Location service request | SN + res + Source LPV + Request GN Addr |
| 6 | 1 | Location service reply | SN + res + S

### [Car2x/protocols/geoNetworking302] protocolGeoNetworkingForwarding


# Forwarding

Protocol Overview » GeoNetworking: Forwarding

The EU protocol provides the possibility to forward application messages e.g. DENM. This means that a recipient can forward a message originally issued by another node (Originator Node). This way, the recipient becomes a Sender Node.

For such a message the following applies: the WLAN Source Address (Sender Node) is not equal to the GN Source Address (Originator Node). The GN Source Address corresponds to the address of the original Sender Node, whereas the WLAN Source Address comes from the forwarding node.

For this reason, it is possible to display the columns Originator Node and Sender Node Trace Window.

|  | Note Generally, forwarded messages are identified by the fact that the Remaining Hop Limit is smaller than the Max Hop Limit (geo_bh.remainingHopLimit < geo_ch.maxHopLimit). |

Option .Car2x

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Car2x/protocols/geoNetworking302] protocolGeoNetworkingSH


# Security Header

Protocol Overview » Security Header

| Quick Access |
| --- |
| Protocol Information |
| Trace Window |
| Protocol Dependencies |
| Protocol and Packet Format |
| Header Format for ETSI TS 103 097 V1.1.1 and ETSI TS 103 097 V1.2.1 |
| Header Format for ETSI TS 103 097 V1.3.1 |

## Protocol Information

To protect the data of a GeoNetworking packet, they can be transmitted in a secured packet (ETSI TS 103 097).

The secured packet follows the GeoNetworking Basic Header. It consists of the Security header, the data to be protected and a trailer that contains the signature. The payload to be protected always begins with the GeoNetworking Common Header, and optionally followed by the GeoNetworking Extended Header, Basic Transport Protocol and application message. The signed data begin with the security header and extend up to the end of the payload.

|  | Note Various document versions of the standard are supported. You can set which document version is used by CANoe in the area Car2x Security Layer (Options dialog). The document version used is displayed for the SH layer in the Detail view of the Trace Window. The functions of the packet APIs support only the version set in each case. Different protocol designators are available for the different versions. |

## Trace Window [▲ top]

Since the data structure of the security header is unclear, a summary of the most important transmitted values is shown under Summary in the Detail view of the Trace Window. Some

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Car2x/protocols/wave] protocolWavePeerToPeer


# WAVE Peer-to-Peer

Protocol Overview » WAVE Peer-to-Peer

| Quick Access |
| --- |
| Protocol Information |
| Protocol Dependencies |
| Protocol and Packet Format |
| Header Format |

## Protocol Information

The Peer-To-Peer protocol is part of IEEE 1609.2 and is used to exchange certificated lists. E.g. an unknown certificate that is needed for verification can be requested with the 1609.2-2016 field certLearningRequest. The requested certificate and all intermediate certificates up to the root certificate are transmitted with the Peer-To-Peer protocol then.

The Peer-To-Peer protocol can only be used with 1609.2 v3. Other 1609.2 versions are not supported.

## Protocol Dependencies [▲ top]

The Peer-To-Peer protocol is transmitted via WSMP. The WSMP field PSID must be set to value 136 (coded: 0x8008).

## Protocol and Packet Format [▲ top]

| Peer-To-Peer protocol |
| --- |
| Protocol Designator | wptp |

## Header Format [▲ top]

| Token | Token Designator | Length[bit] | Packet API |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Car2xInitProtocol/Car2xInitPacket | Car2xCompletePacket |
| Protocol Version | version | 8 | 1 | – |
| version of the protocol, must be set to 1 |
| Certificates | caCerts<N> | (N+1)*32 | – | – |
| list of the transmitted certificates In CAPL certificates are referenced via handles as they are used in the CAPL Security API. The list is built by setting the handles with C2xSetTokenInt. With C2xRemoveToken the handles are removed from the list. If a token caCerts

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Car2x/protocols/wave] protocolWaveSecurityServices


# WAVE Security Services

Protocol Overview » WAVE Security Services

| Quick Access |
| --- |
| Protocol Information |
| Protocol Dependencies |
| Trace Window |
| Protocol and Packet Format |
| Header Format |

## Protocol Information

In order to protect the data of a WAVE packet, these can be secured by the WAVE Security Services (IEEE 1609.2) protocol layer.

The WSS layer consists of information on the signer, information on the user data, the user data itself, and finally the signature (signature over the information of the user data and the user data itself).

|  | Notes Different document versions of the standard are supported. You can set which document version is used by CANoe in the area Car2x Security Layer (Options dialog). The document version used is displayed for the WSS layer in the Detail view of the Trace Window. The functions of the Packet API support only the version set in each case. Different protocol designators are available for the different versions. |

## Protocol Dependencies of 1609.3-2010 [▲ top]

It is possible to secure the payload data of a WAVE Short Message Protocol (WSMP) layer as well as the content of the WAVE Service Advertisement (WSA) layer. In the first case, the WSS layer is between the WSMP layer and the payload data, which consists mostly of a SAE J2735 message (BSM, TIM, etc.). In the second case, the WSS layer directly follows the WLAN header, and the WSA layer follows that.

| Facility Layer | Application Message |  |
| --- |

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Car2x/protocols/wave] protocolWaveWSA


# WAVE Services Advertisement

Protocol Overview » WAVE Services Advertisement

| Quick Access |
| --- |
| Protocol Information |
| Protocol Dependencies |
| Protocol and Packet Format |
| Header Format |

## Protocol Information

The WAVE Service Advertisement protocol (IEEE 1609.3) is a network protocol that provides information about services on other channels. After receipt of a WSA message, an ITS station is therefore always informed about which services are offered on which channel.

## Protocol Dependencies [▲ top]

The data are interpreted as WAVE Service Advertisement when the following conditions are met:

- A WLAN management frame with subtype "Action" is received (Frame Control = 0xD0xx).

- The frame body begins with the following values:

- Category Code = "Vendor Specific" (127)

- Organizationally Unique Identifier (OUI) = 0x0050C2 (IEEE Registration Authority)

- Individual Address Block (IAB) = 0x4A4 (IEEE P1609 WG)

- Management ID = 3 (IEEE 1609.3)

- Content Descriptor = 0x01 (WSA)

## Protocol and Packet Format [▲ top]

| WAVE Service Advertisement |
| --- |
| Protocol Designator | wsa |

## Header Format [▲ top]

Only the first start byte is fixed in the WAVE Service Advertisement protocol. All other fields are optional and can recur multiple times in some cases. For this reason, it is not possible to give an exact fixed definition of which field is located at which byte position. Byte positions are therefore not specified for optional fields and subfi

[...truncated for brevity...]

## Protocol Information

The WAVE Short Message Protocol (IEEE 1609.3 - 2016) is a network protocol that contains basic information for communication between individual ITS stations.

This protocol contains the Network Header of the WAVE Short Message Protocol.

## Protocol Dependencies [▲ top]

The data are interpreted as WAVE Short Message Protocol v3 if the following conditions are fulfilled

- An Ethernet packet with Ethertype = 0x88DC is received.

- The version field in the WSMP header has the value 3.

| C2xInitProtocol / C2xInitPacket | C2xCompletePacket |

| Used WSMP version |

| Indicates if optional fields are available |

### [Car2x/protocols/wave] protocolWaveWSMPv3TP


# WAVE Short Message Protocol v3 (2016) - Transport Header

Protocol Overview » WAVE Short Message Protocol v3 (2016) - Transport Header

| Quick Access |
| --- |
| Protocol Information |
| Protocol Dependencies |
| Protocol and Packet Format |
| Header Format |
| Packet API |

## Protocol Information

The WAVE Short Message Protocol (IEEE 1609.3 - 2016) is a network protocol that contains basic information for communication between individual ITS stations.

This protocol contains the Transport Header of the WAVE Short Message Protocol. This Header can never occur without a WSMP Network Header.

## Protocol Dependencies [▲ top]

The protocol interpretation depends on the Transport Protocol ID of the WSMP Network Header.

| Transport Protocol ID | Description |
| --- | --- |
| 0 | Address type PSID. No WAVE Information Elemente |
| 1 | Address type PSID. WAVE Information Elemente are available |
| 2 | Address type ITS Port Number. No WAVE Information Elements |
| 3 | Address type ITS Port Number. WAVE Information Elements available |

## Protocol and Packet Format [▲ top]

| WAVE Short Message Protokoll - Network |
| --- |
| Protocol Designator | wsmp_t |
| Token | Token Designator | Offset [byte.bit] | Length [byte] |
| Header | header | 0 | 2..10 |
| Payload | data | 2..10 | n |

## Header Format [▲ top]

## Transport Protocol ID 0 and Transport Protocol ID 1

| Token | Token Designator | Offset [byte.bit] | Length [bit] | Packet API |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| C2xI

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Car2x/windows] busStatisticWnd


# Bus Statistics Window

Car2x » Car2x Windows » Bus Statistics Window

The Bus Statistics Window shows WLAN specific data and values too. The following information is displayed:

| Statistic | Function |
| --- | --- |
| Rx Packets total [packetpkt] | number of completely received Car2x data packets. |
| Tx Packets total [pkt] | number of completely sent Car2x data packets. |
| Rx Bytes total [byte] | number of bytes in completely received Car2x data packets. |
| Tx Bytes total [byte] | number of bytes in completely sent Car2x data packets. |
| Rx Packet rate [pkt/s] | number of completely received Car2x data packets per second. |
| Tx Packet rate [pkt/s] | number of completely sent Car2x data packets per second. |
| Rx Byte rate [byte/s] | number of bytes per second in completely received Car2x data packets. |
| Tx Byte rate [byte/s] | number of bytes per second in completely sent Car2x data packets. |
| Collisions [total] | number of detected collisions while sending. |
| Errors [total] | number of erroneous or incomplete received packets (e.g. packets with format or checksum error). |

|  | Notes If lines (--) are shown in a row, the WLAN device doesn't supply this statistics information. For evaluation of byte count and byte rate, bytes that are available in CANoe are used only. The number of bytes that are transmitted between the WLAN device and CANoe typically differs from the number of bytes that are actually transmitted on the radio channel. Information about WLAN co

The following details are displayed:

| Column/ Symbol | Description |

| HashedId8 | HashedId8 of the certificate |
| Type | Certificate type (Root CA, Pseudonym CA, Pseudonym, etc.) |
| Signer HashedId8 | HashedId8 of the signature certificate |
| Status | The validity status assigned to the certificate by CANoe. The validity status is also indicated by the icon to the right of the certificate name: |
|  | certificate is valid |
|  | certificate is invalid |
|  | validity of the certificate is unknown |

|  | Note The certificates known at run-time are displayed in the Car2x Certificate Explorer. Certificates and their private keys that are to be known at the start of measurement are managed in the Car2x Certificate Manager. |

### [Car2x/windows/ScenarioEditor] ScenarioEditorApplicationEvents


# Application Events

Car2x » Windows » Scenario Editor » Application Events

## Application Events

With the help of the dialog different events can be created.

The right-hand side of the dialog describes concrete properties of the selected application event as well as the temporal extension (validity period) of the event in the scenario time:

- StartTime - Time in seconds when the event is activated (transmitted).

- Duration - Duration of the event in seconds. No specification means that the event is sent permanently and periodically active from the scenario time StartTime.

- The property groups DENM or BasicSafetyMessage describe individual components or behavioural characteristics of the application event from the perspective of the corresponding Car2x/V2x message.

The attributes (components) of the created application events are inserted into the corresponding attribute group Events of the selected station. For BSM events, only one attribute group is created; for DENM events, a separate attribute group is created for each message. The attribute groups must have a unique name and start with Events.

At runtime the attributes of the Car2x/V2x message can be read / changed in the corresponding OnPreTx() CAPL function.

### BSM (CN & US)

Additional, predefined attributes can be added to the attribute group in the Timeline after the event is created. The following attributes are available:

- VehicleEventsFlags

- PathHistory

- Events

- EventPosAndTime

- MessageCoun

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Car2x/windows/ScenarioEditor] ScenarioEditorFunctionality


# Functionality

Car2x » Windows » Scenario Editor » Functionality

Ribbon|Ribbon Tab Tools|Group Network Tools|Scenario Editor

## Use in CANoe

Scenarios created with the Scenario Editor can be loaded into CANoe via the Scenario Manager. You can open the Scenario Manager via Ribbon|Ribbon Tab Environment|Scenario Manager.

In the window of the Scenario Manager, the stations of the scenario are listed. Stations of the same name as a node of a loaded database are identified as the node. These stations are displayed with a yellow icon to the left and the preceding database name. Stations that have the same name as a GeoObject of a loaded ADAS database are displayed with a blue icon on the left side.

You can open the Scenario Editor via the button [Scenario Editor].

If a scenario was loaded via the Scenario Manager in the respective CANoe configuration, it is used when opening the scenario editor.

## Scenarios and CAPL with Car2x

The structure and time history of the attribute values of the stations in the scenario can be read with the following CAPL functions. With the help of the callback functions you can react to events in each station of the scenario and display further user-defined actions in the program.

The following requirements must be met to be able to use the values of the attributes of the stations specified in the scenario file as well as the scenario callback functions in CAPL.

- Scenario file must be loaded in the Scenario Manager

- The Car2x Interaction

### [Car2x/windows/StationManager] StationManager


# Car2x Station Manager

Windows » Car2x Station Manager

Ribbon|Ribbon Tab Environment|Group Car2x|Station Manager

| Screenshot | Description |
| --- | --- |
|  | The Car2x Station Manager is the central unit to manage ITS Stations. With the help of the Car2x Station Manager, received packets are assigned to ITS stations that are managed in CANoe. This way, CANoe.Car2x offers not only a packet based view but also an ITS station based view on the communication. To display data content (signals) of application messages in measurement windows, there must always exist a relation between the received packets and the nodes created in the database (ITS stations). The Car2x Station Manager provides the corresponding mapping to ensure that the data of the observed signals (e.g. in the data window) do come from the ITS station to be measured. When assigning, the Car2x Station Manager saves the MAC address and - if a Security Header is available - the HashedId8. This data is automatically assigned to the ITS stations through an intelligent tracking algorithm if the corresponding function is activated. It may as well be edited manually via the GUI. |
| Functionality and Control | Configuration |
| Operating Mode Procedures | User Interface Configuration |

Option .Car2x

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Car2x/windows/StationManager] StationManagerFunctionality


# Functionality

Car2x Station Manager » Functionality

In the secure Car2x communication, various road users and the infrastructure are distinguished through certificates (identification via HashedId8) or the MAC address. A road user or an ITS station carries out certificate changes at regular intervals. This changes the MAC address and the HashedId8 in the packets. After the certificate change, this can be regarded from that time on as a new communicating ITS station starting to send.

But since data such as speed and position from a certain ITS station (a node defined in the database) is to be monitored in CANoe (e.g. in the Data Window or Graphics Window), the defined signals of this ITS station would no longer be recognized as data of the assigned database node and would thus no longer be updated for it is considered as another station.

With the Car2x Station Manager, you may configure the assignment of certificates and MAC addresses to ITS stations or database nodes. You may determine which certificates (HashedId8) and MAC addresses belong to an ITS station and to which database node this station is to be assigned to. This ensures that the data of a station with a changed pseudonym is still assigned to the signals of a node configured in a measurement window. This way the measurement can continue without any extensive changes.

### Start of the Measurement

When first starting the measurement, packets will be received that are not yet assigned to a database node. Ther

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Car2x/windows/trace] traceWndCar2xConfig


# Configuration

Trace Window » Configuration

## General

### Fixed mode

In the fixed mode the messages are displayed using special criteria. The message data are updated only if the following data are available and identical (the protocol designator respectively the token designator is given in parenthesis):

- WLAN (wlan)

- Frame Control - Low Byte (frameControl)

- MAC address of the receiver (address1)

- MAC address of the sender (address2)

- Ethernet (eth)

- ethernet type (type)

- GeoNetworking: Common Header (geo_ch)

- protocol of the next layer (nextHeader)

- header type (ht)

- header subtype (hst)

- GeoNetworking: Extended Header (geo_eh)

- GeoNetworking address of the Long Position Vector (lpvGNAddr)

- GeoNetworking address of the Short Position Vector (spvGNAddr)

- GeoNetworking: Common Network Header (geo_cnh)

- protocol of the next layer (nextHeader)

- header type (ht)

- header subtype (hst)

- GeoNetworking address of the Long Position Vector (lpvNetAddr)

- GeoNetworking: Extended Header (geo_ext)

- GeoNetworking address of the Short Position Vector (spvNetAddr)

- Basic Transport Protocol (btp)

- sender port (source)

- destination port (destination)

- WAVE Short Message Protocol v2 (wsmp)

- with loaded database: DSRC Message ID of the J2735 application layer

- PS ID

- WAVE Element ID

- WAVE Short Message Protocol v3 Network Header (wsmp_n)

- with loaded database: DSRC Message ID of the J2735 application layer

- PS ID

- Transport Pro

### [Car2x/windows/trace] traceWndCar2xErrorIndication


# Error Indication

Trace Window » Error Indication

Several checks for different protocol layers are processed to detect violations against the protocols. A distinction is made between errors and warnings. In the case of warnings, the row will not be highlighted in the Trace Window, however, a warning sign will appear in the row. A warning message will be output in the column Protocol Info. In the Detail View of the Trace Window, the warning message will be output next to the corresponding field. If an error is detected, the row is highlighted in the Trace Window and marked with an error icon. In the column Protocol Info, an error message is output.

|  | Note Not all checks are processed when importing into the Trace Window. |

The configuration of the Protocol Analyzer is located in the Options dialog.

## The following checks are processed for the WLAN protocol layer:

| Notification | Description | Level |
| --- | --- | --- |
| WLAN Header |
| Frame Control unknown | The frame control is unequal to the known values for Car2x of 0x0800, 0x4800, 0x6000, 0x8800, 0xC800 or 0xD000. | Error |
| WLAN Address 2 should not be 00:00:00:00:00:00 | The WLAN address 2 should not be 0x00 everywhere. | Warning |

## The following checks are processed for the Ethernet protocol layer:

| Notification | Description | Level |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Ethernet Header |
| Source should not be 00:00:00:00:00:00 | The Source should not be 0x00 everywhere. | Warning |
| Source should be same as W

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Car2x/windows/trace] traceWndCar2xFilter


# Filters

Trace Window » Filters

## Analysis filters

You can use analysis filters for targeted analyses. You can configure these either as a pass filter or a stop filter.

When the Car2X option is used, the Source GN address is used as the filter criterion for the European ETSI protocols. This is defined in the extended header of the GeoNetworking protocol as Source Long Position Vector (Source LPV). For the American IEEE protocols the Source Address is used as the filter criterion that is defined in the WLAN header (Address 2). Additional filter criteria are the channel (e.g. Ath1) and the direction (Rx, Tx).

You can configure the stop and pass filters for the Car2x option as follows:

- via drag and drop from the Trace Output Window

- via drag and drop from the Detail view of the Map Window

If the analysis filters are defined via drag and drop from the Map Window, filtering by channel and direction does not occur.

|  | Cross Reference Detailed information on the general filter method of the analysis filters can be found here. |

Option .Car2x | See Also

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |

### [Car2x/windows/wlanPacketBuilder] wlanpbWndPacketinfo


# Packet Information

WLAN Packet Builder » User Interface » Packet Information

|  | Note If you use the option Ethernet too, you can additionally configure and send IP packets. The supported protocols are listed in the protocol feature matrix of the option Ethernet. |

Packets can be configured in the Packet Information window. Dependent on the detected protocol the packet will be interpreted.

In this window the following information is displayed:

| Column | Description |
| --- | --- |
| first | name of the field (e.g. Ether Type) |
| second | configurable value of the field Different representations of the values are possible depending on the interpretation. A MAC address is displayed in MAC address representation (hexadecimal numbers separated by colons (:)). An IP address is displayed in IP address representation (decimal values separated by dots (.)). For bit fields, the position within the bit field is highlighted. Otherwise the values are displayed in decimal or hexadecimal (0x) format. |
| third | position of the value in the frame (e.g. [0/6]: starts at byte 0, length=6 Bytes) If there are e.g. 3 bit, this column shows the start byte [15/1], the bit length in display in the interpretation column. The position results from the field order. |
| fourth | additional interpretation of the value if the protocol is supported The interpretation is automatically done based on the inserted data |

For an WLAN packet you can change the values Source (Source MAC address), De

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Car2x/windows/wlanPacketBuilder] wlanpbWndPacketlist


# Packet List

WLAN Packet Builder » User Interface » Packet List

In the packet list an overview of all packets and its information is listed. The following columns show you the packet information:

| Column | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Packet Description | description of the packet, which can be changed manually |
| Source | address of the sender |
| Destination | address of the receiver |
| Protocol | detected protocol of the packet |
| Packet length | length of the packet |
| Payload length | length of the payload |
| Channel | channel on which the packet is sent |

You can add new packets with [Add Packet]. With a click on the arrow you can insert a packet with the appropriate pre-configured protocol directly (e.g. a CNH datagram). With [Delete] you can remove packets from the list.

Within the list you can change the sequence of the packets to send via drag and drop. Existing packets you can duplicate with copy and paste.

You can also change the description and the packet length of a packet. Therefore you have to double-click with the left mouse button on the description in column Packet Description of a packet or in column Packet length to change the length. To change the length of the packet payload double click on column Payload length. The packet length is adapted automatically.

In the column Channel you have to select the channel on which the packet is send.

To send the packet finally, select it and click [Send].

If a packet is erroneous it is marked with .

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Car2x/windows/wlanPacketBuilder] wlanpbWndRawframe


# Raw Frame

WLAN Packet Builder » User Interface » Raw Frame

In the Raw Frame window all data bytes of the complete packet are shown and interpreted as hexadecimal and ASCII value.

To change the data bytes click on the byte position you want to change, then insert the hexadecimal value you want to insert. It is also possible to insert the value as ASCII character. Therefore click in the ASCII column and enter your values.

Changed values are highlighted in color. To commit the changed value press <Enter> or click in another window. To discard your input press <Esc>.

|  | Note Consider that manually inserted data are overwritten if you have selected <Actual channel> or <Adapter Ath<x>> for the MAC addresses in the configuration dialog. |

It can be selected how many bytes are displayed within one line (use list Bytes per line). It is also possible to change the length of the packet (use edit field Packet length).

You can use the comfortable copy/paste mechanism too. Therefore copy a stream of numbers in the clipboard, set the cursor to the position you want to insert it and paste it with the keys <CTRL>+<V>. That allows to insert raw data of a packet from Trace Window or other applications in the WLAN Packet Builder directly.

|  | Caution! If the length of the data packet exceeds the maximum length of 1514 bytes, the data that exceed the allowed length are highlighted and automatically truncated after a commit with <Enter>. |

If you want to avoid that copied data are a

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Car2x/windows/wlanPacketBuilder] wlanpbWndSettings


# Configuration

WLAN Packet Builder » Configuration

Ribbon|Simulation|Simulation Setup|Context of a bus line|Insert WLAN Packet Builder|Configuration...

## Default Settings

In this dialog you can specify the default values that should be used if a new packet is created and added to the packet list.

### Default Source MAC address and Default Destination MAC address

Here you can select the default MAC addresses from the following entries:

- <Actual channel>: The MAC-ID of the adapter is used that is assigned to the channel in the network containing the WLAN Packet Builder. If you change the assignment of adapter to channel, the MAC address of the packet is automatically changed too.

|  | Notes If you select this setting consider that manually inserted data are overwritten in the Raw Frame area. |

- <Config manual>: You can enter any MAC address.

|  | Note Enter the MAC address as six two-digit hexadecimal numbers separated by colons, e.g. the broadcast MAC address FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF. |

<Broadcast>: The broadcast MAC address (FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF) is used.

<Adapter Ath 1,2,...x>: The MAC-ID of the respective adapter is used.

### Default Source Port and Default Destination Port

In the entry field, you can set the default settings for the used ports.

Option .Car2x | See Also

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [CommunicationConcept] CC


# Communication Concept

CANoe » Communication Concept

| Main Challenges | Motivation |
| --- | --- |
|  | CANoe as simulation and test environment provides an uniform and cross-network communication concept. This communication concept enables application-related modeling of classic and new communication patterns. The communication concept provides consistent decoupling of logical communication on the application level from protocols of the network level. This means that CANoe provides a network-independent interface for application models and test scripts. Separately from this, the actual access or transmission is configured via a specific network. Comparison |
| Concept & Configuration | Programming |
| Concept Basics of the Communication Concept Service-Oriented Communication Binding Communication Objects Distributed Objects Configuration System And Communication Setup Stimulation Application Panel | Programming C# and CAPL Description how you can use the objects and the APIs of the communication concept in C# or in CAPL to create, for example application models or to test functionality. Vector Communication Description Language (vCDL) vCDL allows the CANoe communication concept to be extended by a tool for the simple and text-based configuration of communication objects. |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [CommunicationConcept] CCBinding


# Binding

CANoe » Communication Concept » Binding

## General

Binding is used to configure transmission between the communication endpoints of a communication object or the members of a distributed object. This means that binding is used to determine which protocol and which network must be used for transmission. Thus, binding creates transition between the virtual world of CANoe and the real word, for example in the form of a real ECU.

Complexity of binding varies depending on the underlying protocols and networks. Typically, binding for an Ethernet network is complex. Usually, protocol parameters for TCP/IP layers and the communication middleware used must be configured in this case. Finally, binding encapsulates this complexity on the network level and provides a uniform interface for application models and test scripts.

In CANoe, the so-called binding blocks enable binding. Communication objects are assigned to the binding blocks. These binding blocks transfer then communication between the endpoints of the communication object on the corresponding protocol and network.

Currently, CANoe offers different binding blocks which are described in the following sections.

## Abstract Binding

Abstract binding enables transmission between simulated endpoints within CANoe. Thus, abstract binding enables application-related communication between simulated components in CANoe without describing and simulating lower protocol levels.

Abstract binding is well suited, for example

[...truncated for brevity...]

CANoe » Communication Concept » Comparison

|  | Note All designations/functions which are not listed here remain with the new communication concept the same, for example Measurement Setup. |

| Previous Designation/Function | New Designation/Function | Short Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Database | Data Source | CANoe imports all databases (AUTOSAR, FIBEX) to a universal Vector data model format. Note The data in the data source is not automatically used by CANoe. To enable the use of participants/endpoints in CANoe (e.g. for simulation, analysis, display in the Symbol Explorer), you must first configure them in the System and Communication Setup (integrate into CANoe). |  | Note The data in the data source is not automatically used by CANoe. To enable the use of participants/endpoints in CANoe (e.g. for simulation, analysis, display in the Symbol Explorer), you must first configure them in the System and Communication Setup (integrate into CANoe). |
|  | Note The data in the data source is not automatically used by CANoe. To enable the use of participants/endpoints in CANoe (e.g. for simulation, analysis, display in the Symbol Explorer), you must first configure them in the System and Communication Setup (integrate into CANoe). |
| ECU/Node | Participants/Distributed Objects | Participants/distributed objects can be used to group communication endpoints that are logically associated; e.g. because they belong to the same ECU, or because they ar

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [CommunicationConcept] CCConcept


# Basics of the Communication Concept

CANoe » Communication Concept » Basics

The key of the CANoe communication concept is the strict separation of the communicating parties (the application parts) and the medium which is used for transmission. For this, CANoe provides a layer between the application and the transmission medium, the so-called middleware.

In the middleware, the required parameters are configured, for example endpoints for the communication partners or interfaces for the distributed objects. Based on the configured parameters, the objects communicate with their configured counterparts. This is done according to a configurable communication pattern. Typical patterns for communication objects are, for example point-to-point, broadcast or multicast connections. Typical patterns for distributed objects are Publish/Subscribe or Send/Receive. For communication objects (CO) or distributed objects (DO), all usual functions for analysis, test and simulation are available in CANoe:

- Interactive graphic interactions, such as Trace, Logging, Graphics Window, State Tracker, etc.

- Test modules and test scripts

- CAPL and C# interface

- MATLAB/Simulink

- …

The focus here is on the support of the service-oriented communication in the way it is required in modern vehicle architectures for application servers, for example for ADAS or modern infotainment systems. However, classic signal-oriented and PDU-oriented communication via broadcast is also supported.

A furthe

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [CommunicationConcept] CCDistributedObjects


# Distributed Objects

CANoe » Communication Concept » Distributed Objects

## General

Distributed Objects (DOs) are, besides communication objects (COs), a core feature of the communication concept. In CANoe they stand for various nodes, devices or functions that communicate with each other via interfaces.

The name distributed objects, especially the term object, is derived in the sense of object orientation: CANoe provides objects for the communication in a distributed system. This is also reflected in the syntax, which is strongly based on the object notation of C++ / C# / Java.

Analogous to the object-oriented design in software development, the interface of a distributed object defines a group of related members that can be used for communication. Distributed objects can correspond to one or several participants/devices/functions or also only a subset of these. The members of distributed objects are generally referred to as the communication endpoints of the distributed object. Note that these are not identical with the end points of the communication objects.

Various communication parameters can be set for each member of a distributed object, such as the

- Communication pattern (Shared Memory, Publish/Subscribe or Send/Receive).

- Bindings to a network (abstract binding, MQTT binding…)

- Behavior (e.g. AutoSubscribe at Publish/Subscribe)

- …

According to their communication direction (from the perspective of the distributed object), members are classified as p

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [CommunicationConcept] CCDOAttributes


# Pre-defined Attributes for Distibuted Objects

CANoe » Communication Concept » Distributed Objects » Pre-defined Attributes for Distibuted Objects

The following tables provide an overview of all predefined attributes for distributed objects. You cannot change the values of these attributes during the measurement. The symbol ✔ in the Interface column means that the attribute can only be set at interface level, which means that it cannot be overwritten by individual instances of distributed objects.

Note that an attribute ultimately always parametrizes one end point. If a value is set for the attribute on a superordinate object, this value is inherited by the end point, but can also be overwritten.

## General Attribute

These attributes can be used without specifying a namespace. They are not specific to a specific binding.

| Name | Interface | Data Type | Allowed Values | Default Value | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Binding | — | String | Abstract, MQTT | — | Selects the binding. |
| CommunicationPattern | ✔ | Enum | PublishSubscribe, SendReceive, SharedMemory | PublishSubscribe | Communication pattern of the communication endpoint. |
| HasAnnounceAPI | ✔ | Boolean |  | false | Specifies whether the communication endpoint must be published in the network (e.g. at a registry). Only for the communication pattern PublishSubscribe. |
| AutoAnnounce | ✔ | Enum | Never, OnConnect, OnFirstTransmit | OnConnect | Specifies whether to announce as soon as 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [CommunicationConcept] CCDOCAPLBinding


# CAPL Binding for Distributed Objects

CANoe » Communication Concept » CAPL Binding for Distributed Objects

A special variant of binding for distributed objects (DO) is to implement the transmission with CAPL itself. Therefore the binding attribute is set to CAPL and special functions and handlers are used to write the received values into the object or to send the values. The CAPL program is normally configured in the System and Communication Setup, in special cases in the Simulation Setup.

The CAPL binding can be used e.g. for prototypical implementations of a protocol, or to implement a special transmission for a few members. For a general protocol implementation it is rather not suitable, because a separate handler is needed for each DO member or interface member.

## Provided Data, Event and Field Member

To send the value you implement the handler on transmit_value <DO Member> or on transmit_any_value <Interface Member>. This handler is called as soon as the value should be transmitted. The concrete time depends especially on the value of the attribute TxTrigger of the member; the call is, however, in any case decoupled from other handlers. This means, for example, that if the member's value was set in an on timer handler and the TxTrigger attribute is set to OnUpdate, the on transmit_value handler is called in the same simulation step as the on timer handler, but only after the on timer handler has finished.

The value to be transmitted can be accessed in the handl

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [CommunicationConcept] CCDOCSharpBinding


# C# Binding for Distributed Objects

CANoe » Communication Concept » C# Binding for Distributed Objects

A special variant of binding for distributed objects (DO) is to implement the transmission with C# itself. Therefore the binding attribute is set to C# and special functions and handlers are used to write the received values into the object or to send the values. The C# program is normally configured in the System and Communication Setup, in special cases in the Simulation Setup.

The C# binding can be used e.g. for prototypical implementations of a protocol, or to implement a special transmission for a few members. For a general protocol implementation it is rather not suitable, because a separate handler is needed for each DO member or interface member.

## Provided Data, Event and Field Member

To send the value, use either the [OnTransmitValue] attribute on a method without parameters or the [OnTransmitAnyValue] attribute on a method that has an interface type as its only parameter. The method is called as soon as the value should be transmitted. The concrete time depends especially on the value of the attribute TxTrigger of the member; the call is, however, in any case decoupled from other handlers. This means, for example, that if the member's value was set in a Timer callback and the TxTrigger attribute is set to OnUpdate, the method marked with the attribute is called in the same simulation step as the Timer callback

## Consumed Data, Event and Field Member

### [CommunicationConcept] CCDODynamicObjectReferences


# Dynamic Objects and References

CANoe » Communication Concept » Distributed Objects » Dynamic Objects and References

Another feature of the communication concept are dynamically generated distributed objects. These can be generated at runtime via C# or CAPL, for which examples are shown below. To allow you to parametrize the generated objects before use, blueprints of dynamic objects are introduced. These provide methods and properties to set attribute values or configure virtual networks before they are used at object generation.

## Creating Dynamic Objects in CAPL

In CAPL there are singleton types for each vCDL interface (distObjInterface <Interface Name>), which are used to create and destroy dynamic objects that implement the respective interface or its reversal. The blueprint types (distObjBlueprint <Interface Name>) are also created at the interface singletons.

|  | Example // Example.vcdlversion 1.3;namespace SomeNamespace { interface SomeInterface { consumed data int32 SomeMember; } virtualnetwork SomeNetwork;} // Example.canvariables { using namespace _SystemAttributes;}on start { // Create object blueprints stack distObjBlueprint SomeInterface blueprint1 = SomeInterface.CreateObjectBlueprint(); stack distObjBlueprint reverse<SomeInterface> blueprint2 = SomeInterface.CreateReverseObjectBlueprint(); // Set attributes and virtual networks blueprint1.SetAttribute("_SystemAttributes::Binding", "Abstract"); blueprint1.SomeMember.SetAttribute("_SystemAttributes::Com

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [CommunicationConcept] CCDOPlaceholderInAttributes


# Placeholders in Attributes

CANoe » Communication Concept » Distributed Objects » Placeholders in Attributes

String attributes for distributed objects can contain placeholders that are replaced by the value of another attribute when the attribute value is used and queried.

This is especially useful if the value of the attribute should be similar for many objects or members. Then the value can be set with one or more placeholders, e.g. at the interface or at the object, while at the member only the value for the placeholder is set.

|  | Example For the MQTT binding, the topic is defined by attribute. Here you could have several distributed objects of type ClimateControl, one per room; and each of these objects receives temperature data from several sensors: namespace SmartHome{ interface ClimateControl { consumed data int64 Temperature1; consumed data int64 Temperature2; // ... } object LivingRoomClimateControl : ClimateControl; object OfficeClimateControl : ClimateControl; // ...} Often the MQTT topic is similar for all sensors, only room and sensor ID change. So you can define own attributes for these values: attribute string Room;attribute int32 SensorId; Then you define the topic at the interface with placeholders for all members: [MQTTBinding::Topic="rooms/{Room}/devID{SensorId:d}/Temperature"]interface ClimateControl The value of the new attributes can now be set for each object or member: [SensorId=1]consumed data int64 Temperature1;[Room="Office"]object OfficeCli

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [CommunicationConcept] CCMotivation


# Service-Oriented Communication

CANoe » Communication Concept » Service-Oriented Communication

The classic signal-based communication is increasingly supplemented by service-oriented communication patterns. The AUTOSAR Adaptive platform, for example consistently uses service-oriented approach. Service-oriented communication is often based on the TCP/IP protocol stack and uses communication middleware, for example SOME/IP. The transmitted network message, i.e. the Ethernet frame, and the actual application view drift here much more apart than in case of signal based communication via CAN. In addition, the service interfaces and the associated data structures are defined in a way which is detached from a specific network transmission or network topology.

The CANoe communication concept enables the use of the service interfaces directly as modeling artifact. The service interfaces support methods and events. Complex data types, used for example in the area of object detection, are supported directly. Endpoints which provide or use a service interface (providers and consumers) can be directly simulated in CANoe with communication objects or distributed objects.

## Dynamic Topologies

Static communication paths are increasingly supplemented by dynamic communication paths. Dynamic network topologies with time-varying communication paths are generated in particular by the communication of the backend services ("Connected Car"), but also by the communication in the immediate ve

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [CommunicationConcept] CCObjects


# Communication Objects

CANoe » Communication Concept » Communication Objects

## General

Besides distributed objects, communication objects (CO) are a core feature of the communication concept. They are the central configuration point of communication. Here, communication endpoints, communication type and pattern as well as transmission properties are set:

- The endpoints correspond to the participating communication partners.

- The communication type determines whether transmission must be signal based or service oriented.

- The communication pattern specifies whether it is a point-to-point or broadcast communication.

According to their role, the endpoints are divided into the sides sender/receiver for signal-based communication or provider/consumer for service-oriented communication. In addition, the service interface is set for service-oriented communication and the data type of the exchanged information units is set for signal-based communication. The configuration also specifies which endpoints must be operated by CANoe.

## Service Interfaces

Service interfaces describe service functions, service signals, service PDUs, etc. available in the service. For signal-based communication, the usual fundamental data types, such as glide ratio (float, double, etc.), integer or strings may be used. It is also possible to define and use complex structured data types.

Theoretically, for a communication object, connections between the endpoints are established according to 

[...truncated for brevity...]
