### [CommunicationConcept/Programming] CCPCOs


# API of the Different Communication Object Types

CANoe » Communication Concept » Programming » API of the Different Communication Object Types

Table of Contents

| Services |
| --- |
| Methods |
| Events and Fields |
| PDUs |
| Signals |

## Services [▲ back]

A service allows you to access individual elements (fields, events, methods and PDUs) with a '.' and their name.

You can query the state of a provider with ServiceState at the provider endpoint.

You can query the state of the connection between a consumer and a provider with ConnectionState both on the consumer side and on the provider side.

## Methods[▲ back]

The APIs of methods are described in detail on the Function Calls page.

## Events and Fields [▲ back]

## Values

You can access several values for events and fields in a service:

- The last received value on the consumer side, specifically for each provider that sends the value (consumer value).

- The last sent value or the value to be sent next on the provider side, specifically for each consumer to which the value is sent (send value).

- The last sent value or the value to be sent next on the provider side for all subscribed consumers. This value is also called provider value; there is a predefined subscription model for each simulated provider which uses this value. If data is written on the provider value, the subscription model distributes the new value to the send values for all consumers subscribed at that time.

The following picture illustrat

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [CommunicationConcept/Programming] CCPDOInterfaces


# Programming with DO Interfaces

CANoe » Communication Concept » Programming » Programming with DO Interfaces

Each distributed object has an interface, which in turn can be composed of sub-interfaces. If you want to use the same code for several similar objects, then you should use the interface of these objects in the code. This is especially necessary if objects are created dynamically, i.e. they cannot be known to the program code at all.

First of all, this means that you do not access the static objects directly in the runtime environment, but use variables and parameters whose data type is the interface of the objects.

|  | Example // Example.vcdlversion 1.3;namespace SomeNamespace{ interface SomeInterface { provided data int32 SomeMember; }} // Example.canvoid SetSomeMember(distObjRef SomeInterface obj, long val){ $obj.SomeMember = 42;} // Example.csvoid SetSomeMember(SomeNamespace.SomeInterface obj, Int32 val){ obj.SomeMember.Value = 42;} |

In the handlers, you do not react to events on specific objects, but to events on all objects with a specific interface.

In CAPL you use the handlers on any_... for this purpose. They work similarly to the specific handlers, except that you must specify an interface member for them, and in the handler you access the concrete object on which the event was triggered with this.object. For value handlers you access the value with this.value, for method handlers you access the calling context with this.context.

|  | Example // Ex

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [CommunicationConcept/Programming] CCPDOMembers


# Distributed Object Member

CANoe » Communication Concept » Programming » Distributed Object Member

## Members

Distributed objects can contain different kinds of members with some fixed direction. Like any member they can be accessed with the "." operator.

The names of distributes object members are case sensitive. In case of name conflicts with predefined members (e.g. distributed object member with name Path) an underscore will be appended. If the escaped name already exists, the escaping continues until the name is unique. This is done in such a way that a member always uses its real name if possible (i.e. if the members Path and Path_ exist, then Path will be mapped to Path__ (two underscores) and Path_ will stay the same).

The types of distributed object members are only used to provide certain methods and properties. The different kinds of members are described in the following sections.

| vCDL | version 1.2;namespace SomeNamespace{ [Binding="Abstract"] object SomeObject { internal data int32 SomeData; [CommunicationPattern="PublishSubscribe"] consumed event void SomeEvent; internal method int64 SomeMethod(int32 p1, inout int32 p2, out int32 p3); internal field string SomeField; } [Binding=”Abstract”] object OtherObject { [CommunicationPattern="PublishSubscribe", HasAnnounceAPI=true] provided event void SomeEvent; }} |
| --- | --- |
| CAPL/C# | // access the member “SomeMember” of the object “SomeObject” SomeObject.SomeData;SomeObject.SomeEvent;SomeObject.SomeMet

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [CommunicationConcept/Programming] CCPDOs


# Data Types for Distributed Objects

CANoe » Communication Concept » Programming » Data Types for Distributed Objects

## CAPL

Most distributed object types in CAPL can represent an invalid value. If variables of such types are not initialized on declaration, they are assigned to their invalid value. Some operations might also result in invalid values, e.g. if a cast or lookup fails. You can check the validity of a value by using the IsValid selector. Accessing members of an invalid value causes an error message in the Write Window and a returned default value (e.g. empty string). In test modules it will also cause an error in test system to be reported.

The types can be used for local variables, global variables and parameters. The types are not available for values of maps.

## Objects

Objects from vCDL have a distObjRef type in CAPL (distObjRef * or distObjRef <Interface Name>). The interface name can be partially qualified if it is unambiguous. The distObjRef types preserve the ordering of interfaces in the data model. Conversions to types higher in the hierarchy are implicit. Conversions to types lower in the hierarchy require an explicit cast, which will check the dynamic type to determine if the conversion is valid. If the conversion is invalid, the cast expression has an invalid value, but no CAPL runtime error is generated.

Statically declared objects can be referenced in CAPL through their qualified name. The name binding rules are the same as for communicatio

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [CommunicationConcept/Programming] CCPEndpoints


# Endpoint Selection in Communication Concept

CANoe » Communication Concept » Programming » Endpoint Selection in Communication Concept

Table of Contents

| Selection in Execution Units |
| --- |
| Default Selection (C# only) |
| API for Selection (C# only) |
| Direct Specification of Endpoints |
| Collective Endpoints |

## Selection in Execution Units [▲ back]

In C#, you often do not need to explicitly select the endpoints of a communication object, for example, because there is only one sender and one receiver. In the general case, a communication object can, however, have several endpoints on each side. In CAPL, you must always specify endpoints even if they are unambiguous.

With point-to-point communication, you must specify in the API from which endpoint to which endpoint communication takes place.

With broadcast communication, you must only specify the endpoint used by the code. For some values, however, you must additionally specify on which side the value should be read. For example, in case of an event, for each pair, consisting of a consumer and provider there is a value both on the consumer side (the last received value) and on the provider side (the value last sent to the consumer or the value to be sent next to the consumer). If you only want to measure transferred values between the endpoints, in particular if both endpoints are not simulated, there is also a measured value in the center.

In C#, selection per communication object and per execution unit i

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [CommunicationConcept/Programming] CCPExamples


# Programming Examples for the Communication Concept

CANoe » Communication Concept » Programming » Programming Examples for the Communication Concept

These short introductory examples show how to use communication objects and distributed objects in programming APIs in general.

## Using a Complex Signal

In the first example, a signal is set to adjust a mirror. As a data type, the signal has a structure with two elements for adjustment in x and y direction. After a waiting period, it is checked whether the status signal sent by the mirror has a new value.

| vCDL | namespace Mirrors{ struct MirrorPos { int32 x; int32 y; } [broadcast=true] signal MirrorPos CurrentMirrorPosition { sender Mirror; receiver CANoe; } [broadcast=true] signal MirrorPos ChangeMirrorPosition { sender CANoe; receiver Mirror; }} |
| --- | --- |
| C# | [TestCase]void TestMirror(){ // save the current (last received) value of the position var oldPos = new Mirrors.MirrorPos(CurrentMirrorPosition.Value); // set the adjustment signal: 5 degrees to the left // the signal is sent once if its value changes // (this is specified in the communication description) ChangeMirrorPosition.x = -50; // in tenths of a degree // wait for new position // after 200ms, the status signal should contain the new position Execution.Wait(200); Assert.IsTrue(CurrentMirrorPosition.x == oldPos.x.ImplValue - 50); Assert.IsTrue(CurrentMirrorPosition.y == oldPos.y.ImplValue);} |
| CAPL | testcase TestMirror(){ struct Mirrors::MirrorP

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [CommunicationConcept/Programming] CCPFunctions


# Function Calls

CANoe » Communication Concept » Programming » Function Calls

Table of Contents

| Consumer Side |
| --- |
| Provider Side |
| Internal DO Methods |

## Consumer Side [▲ back]

## Asynchronous Call

On the consumer, a function (in other words, a service method) is called with CallAsync(…). These calls are asynchronous, as the call returns before communication with the provider (and possibly return communication) has taken place. The CallAsync parameters are exactly the same as in- and inout-parameters of the function in the order specified when defining the function; in the case of inout-parameters, however, only the in value is relevant here.

CallAsync returns the so-called CallContext object (CCO). It can be used, for example to wait for an answer of the provider and then read the output parameters. In C#, you should call Dispose on it if it is no longer needed.

## Reaction to Answers

You can react to the answer of the provider with a handler or with a wait function. In C#, the handler is specified with the attribute [OnReturned(…)], in CAPL, it is specified with the keywords on fct_Returned.

| C# (CO) | [OnReturned(typeof(TrafficControllers.Car1.Bridge1.GetInfo))]public void GetInfoReturned(TrafficControllers.Car1.Bridge1.GetInfo.TransientCallContext ret){ Output.WriteLine("Return value: " + ret.Result);} |
| --- | --- |
| C# (DO) | [OnReturned(Mirrors.Controller.MemberIDs.AdjustLeft)]public voidOnReturnedAdjustLeft(Mirrors.Mirror_Types.Adjust.Transi

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [CommunicationConcept/Programming] CCPServiceDiscovery


# Service Discovery

CANoe » Communication Concept » Programming » Service Discovery

Table of Contents

| Auxiliary Models |
| --- |
| Direct Influence |
| Handlers |
| Wait Functions |

## Auxiliary Models [▲ back]

Auxiliary models are offered in CANoe to simplify the use of the complex Service Discovery sequence. They can be used to interact at the Service CO via special methods.

## Auxiliary Model Is Assigned to Provider Endpoints

The model reacts to first requests of consumers, takes care of Service Announcement and accepts connection request of consumers.

- The status of the simulated service provider is indicated in the ServiceState value which can assume values Unavailable, Initializing, and Available.

- You can use ProvideService() to say to the model that the service should be available.

- You can use ReleaseService() to say to the model that the service should not be available.

- You can use IsServiceProvided() to query the state of the model.

| C# | // On provider side for (uint i = 0; i < MirrorAdjustment.ProviderCount; ++i){ MirrorAdjustment.SelectProvider(i); if (MirrorAdjustment.IsSelectionValid) MirrorAdjustment.ProvideService();} |
| --- | --- |
| CAPL | dword i;for (i = 0; i < getNrOfCOProviders(MirrorAdjustment); ++i){ MirrorAdjustment.providerSide[i].ProvideService();} |

## Auxiliary Model Is Assigned to Consumer Endpoints and Exists Once per Provider

The model sends Service Discovery requests, reacts to answers of the desired provider and esta

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [CommunicationConcept/Programming] CCPValues


# Value Access in Communication Concept

CANoe » Communication Concept » Programmierung » Value Access in Communication Concept

Table of Contents

| General Access |
| --- |
| Reaction to Updates and Changes |
| Data Types for Values |
| Using Encodings |
| Predefined Enumeration Types |
| Overview of Available Values |

## General Access [▲ back]

In the simulation environment, there is a large number of values for the communication objects and distributed objects, depending on the type and number of endpoints and members: for example, the last received value of a service field or the value of a signal to be sent next. Generally, there are three categories of values. They are always accessed in the same way:

- Received values sent by a communication partner on the other side of the communication. These values are read only. If the values are transmitted on a real network or bus, they can be referred to as measured values.

- Data memory for the next transmission to a communication partner. These values are both readable and writable. The exact time and type of transmission depends on the configuration of the transmission behavior.

- Values which are not transmitted directly, but configure the simulation in CANoe or indicate a state adopted by a real or simulated object, e.g. whether an event is subscribed or not.

Overview of All Values According to CO Type

In C#, you access values with the value property at the corresponding object.

|  | Example C# (CO) // Value of a 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [CommunicationConcept/Programming] CCPVariables


# Reuse of Code with Variables and Parameters

CANoe » Communication Concept » Programming » Reuse of Code with Variables and Parameters

Table of Contents

| Data Types for Endpoints |
| --- |
| Initialization |
| Dereferencing |
| Changing the Referenced Endpoints |

## Data Types for Endpoints[▲ back]

You can save references to endpoints of communication objects in variables or parameters and transfer them to functions. The APIs provide data types for it. This allows you to program functions such that they can be used again and independently of the endpoints actually used.

The name of these data types consist of several components:

- Type of the communication object: service, method, event, field, PDU, signal.

- Type of the referenced endpoints, depending on the communication pattern and type. With point-to-point communication, both endpoints must often be referenced to address communication between the two endpoints. For other functionality (e.g. the state of a provider) and for broadcast communication, it is sufficient to reference only one endpoint.

- A Ref suffix to indicate that it is a reference

## Service Oriented Communication

| Referenced endpoints | Point-to-point | Broadcast |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Consumer and provider where the consumer side is referenced | consumed<Object type>Ref, e.g. consumedMethodRef, consumedServiceRef | N/A |
| Consumer and provider where the provider side is referenced | provided<Object type>Ref, e.g. providedEventRef, provided

### [Connectivity] ConnectivityDUT


# System Under Test (SUT)

CANoe » Connectivity » System Under Test (SUT)

## SUT

The SUT is the connected device to be tested which is to be connected with CANoe via MQTT broker in the CFS cloud.

The URI with which the SUT is to connect can be taken from the configuration dialog under the Endpoint item.

In order for the CFS cloud to identify the SUT, the SUT certificate must also be provided when establishing the TLS. An SUT can also be comprised of several different single devices which use the same SUT certificate.

## SUT example

The following example shows accessing of the MQTT broker in the CFS cloud by a connected SUT.

A Raspberry Pi with access to the Internet serves as the connected device in this case. An MQTT client is implemented on the device via JavaScript. NodeJS is used as the execution environment.

### JavaScript

// Imported namespaces const fs = require("fs");const mqtt = require("mqtt");//CFS URI from the brokerconst brokerAddress = <Endpoint>;//create mqtt clientvar mqttClient = mqtt.connect( brokerAddress, { key: fs.readFileSync(<SUT-KEY-PEM>), cert: fs.readFileSync(<SUT-CERT-PEM>), ca: fs.readFileSync(<SUT-CA-CERT>), rejectUnauthorized: true, });//suscribe to topic "rooms/living/devID00002/temperature"mqttClient.subscribe("rooms/living/devID00002/temperature");mqttClient.on("message", (topic, message) => { if (topic == "rooms/living/devID00002/temperature") { //double the temperature and return it as speed var newSpeed = parseFloat(message) * 2; 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Connectivity] ConnectivityIntroduction


# Use Cases and Certificates

CANoe » Connectivity » Use Cases and Certificates

## Getting Started

To use the cloud functionality of the Connectivity Features Services (CFS), a so-called owner certificate is required. This can be acquired through Support. No certificates are required for the local use case.

For this purpose, please contact Vector Support. Following this, you’ll receive an owner certificate.

For information on how you must configure CANoe, refer to Configuration: Connectivity.

## Use Cases

### What is the CFS for?

The CFS extends the connection functions of CANoe. With the CFS you currently have the possibility to connect Connected Devices to CANoe via the MQTT protocol, for example to simulate and test Internet-of-Things (IoT) use cases. This connection can be made either via a local broker (see use cases 2,3) or via the CFS cloud, which is operated by Vector (see use case 1). The CFS cloud provides each Vector customer with a dedicated MQTT broker, which can be used free of charge to a certain extent. (For details, see section Getting Started above).

### Use Case 1 – Connected Device Cloud Communication

A connected device connects to an MQTT broker run in the CFS cloud. If some sensors are not available, they can be simulated with CANoe in order to stimulate the connected device and check whether the response occurs in the form of a PUBLISH message, for example. This message can be received in CANoe and further processed as an application message.


[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Connectivity] ConnectivityIoTEnable


# IoT Enabler

CANoe » Connectivity » IoT Enabler

## Introduction

The VH4110 IoT Enabler enables the connection of CANoe to wireless end devices and sensors. Typical radio interfaces from the IoT environment are supported. The IoT Enabler has direct radio interfaces for WLAN and Bluetooth Low Energy. Other radio interfaces, such as ZigBee or Z-Wave, can be connected to the device in the form of commercially available USB sticks.

The IoT Enabler has an open and extensible firmware interface that you can extend with your own functions. These functions can then in turn be called from the CANoe simulation.

## First Steps

- Detailed information about the network interface and commissioning can be found in the manual of the VH4110 IoT Enabler.

- Configuration and control of the IoT Enabler network interface is performed via the CANoe System and Communication Setup and a corresponding vCDL description.

- The sample configuration IoT Enabler shows the use of the network interface based on a basic example. You can also use this sample configuration as a template for your own configurations.

## Connection via the Communication Setup

The VH4110 IoT Enabler is configured and controlled via the System and Communication Setup. The interfaces of the IoT Enabler are described in a vCDL file in the form of distributed objects. These contain functions that can be called by CANoe Simulation on the IoT Enabler. Furthermore, these interfaces contain events via which value changes of a c

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Connectivity] MQTTModelingDOs


# Distributed Objects (DOs)

CANoe » Connectivity » MQTT Protocol » Distributed Objects (DOs)

## MQTT Modeling with Distributed Objects

The modeling of an MQTT client in CANoe occurs according to the new communication concept and distributed objects (DOs).

The elements which can be used for modeling are listed in the following table:

| Designation | Possible Values | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Member types | Data, event | These member types are supported for MQTT in connection with distributed objects. |
| Data types | Unsigned integer, integer, double, float, UTF8 string, struct, union, array, bool, bytes | These data types are supported for MQTT in conjunction with distributed objects. |
| Attribute [Binding=<0>] | MQTT | Binds member to the MQTT binding. |
| Attribute [CommunicationPattern =<0>] | PublishSubscribe | MQTT is a publish/subscribe pattern, which is why only this value is permissible here. |
| Attribute [AutoConnect=<0>] | True, false | True – Automatic connection following the start of measurement. False – Manual connection following the start of measurement. Default: True |
| Attribute [AutoSubscribe=<0>] | True, false | True – Automatic subscribing with MQTT broker following connection. False – Manual subscribing to MQTT broker. Default: True |
| Attribute [ClientId=<0>] | String | Assignment of DO member to a client. (see exceptions under Supported Scope of the MQTT Protocol). |
| Attribute [Topic=<0>] | String | provided Member: topic name fo

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Connectivity] MQTTModelingDOsExamples


# Examples

CANoe » Connectivity » MQTT-Protokoll » Distributed Objects (DOs) » Examples

## MQTT Modeling with DOs - Example

## Data and Application Models

With the following example a simple client with a PUBLISH and a SUBSCRIBE can be modelled. For this purpose the model is represented in vCDL and an application model in both C# and CAPL.

### vCDL

version 1.2;namespace ClimateSimulation{ //---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- //Modulation of the Fan which is consuming the temperature and publish the rotation speed //---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- namespace FanSimulation { interface IFan { [Binding="MQTT"] [MQTTBinding::ClientId="CANoe"] [MQTTBinding::Topic="rooms/living/devLowerLeft/temperature"] consumed data int64 ConsumedTemperature; [Binding="MQTT"] [MQTTBinding::ClientId="CANoe"] [MQTTBinding::Topic="rooms/living/devFan/Speed"] provided data int64 ProvidedSpeed; } object Fan1 : IFan; } //------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ //Modulation of the temperature sensor //-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- namespace TemperatureSimulation { interface ITemperatureSensor { [Binding="MQTT"] [MQTTBinding::ClientId="CANoe"] [MQTTBinding::Topic="rooms/living/devLowerLeft/temperature"] provided data int64 ProvidedTemperature; } object TemperatureSensor : ITemperatureSensor

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Connectivity] MQTTProtocol


# MQTT Protocol

CANoe » Connectivity » MQTT Protocol

## Introduction

MQTT is a lightweight transport protocol which sits atop TCP/IP. It is primarily used in the Machine to Machine (M2M) and Internet of Things (IoT) environments.

The publish/subscribe pattern, which enables 1-n distribution of the messages, is used for communication. Here, distribution occurs solely over a server commonly also called an MQTT server.

### Topic

A topic serves as an ID for PUBLISH messages and as a filter for SUBSCRIBE messages to enable interest-based distribution of application messages between the clients.

### Client

Following establishment of the connection with MQTT broker, the client can send application messages via PUBLISH-messages and/or declare interest in specific application messages via SUBSCRIBE-messages.

### Broker

The main task of the broker is to distribute PUBLISH messages between clients. It also functions as a filter for the clients here, as only those PUBLISH messages for which the client has subscribed reach them.

|  | Note A detailed description can be found in the specifications of the Organization for the Advancement of Structured Information Standards (OASIS): Version 3.1.1 (from 2/25/20) http://docs.oasis-open.org/mqtt/mqtt/v3.1.1/mqtt-v3.1.1.html Version 5 (from 2/25/20) http://docs.oasis-open.org/mqtt/mqtt/v5.0/mqtt-v5.0.html |

## Example

In the following scenario, a sensor for temperature and a sensor for humidity connect as MQTT clients to an MQTT bro

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Debugger] DebuggerBreakPointWindow


# Debugger: Breakpoints

Debugger » Breakpoints

The Breakpoint area displays all breakpoints you have set in the Document view.

You can enable, disable, delete, and navigate to set breakpoints using the table view.

In the Name column the name of the program and the row of the breakpoint is displayed. The state of the breakpoint is visualized in the same way as in the Document view.

When a program reaches an active breakpoint during its execution, it is stopped. This breakpoint is highlighted in the overview (font: bold).

With Search you can search for breakpoints.

## Toolbar

| Symbol | Function |
| --- | --- |
|  | Removes an existing breakpoint. |
|  | Removes all breakpoints. |
|  | Activates all breakpoints. |
|  | Deactivates all breakpoints. |
|  | Navigates to the breakpoint in the Document view. |

## Context Menu

Remove Breakpoint Removes an existing breakpoint.

Enable/Disable Breakpoint Enables/disables a breakpoint

Break Test/Simulation In a test module or a test configuration you can select for a breakpoint, if the test module or the test configuration alone or also the simulation should be stopped.

Remove All Breakpoints Removes all breakpoints.

Disable All Breakpoints Disables all breakpoints.

Go to Source Code Navigates to the breakpoint in the Document view.

### [Debugger] DebuggerDebugging


# Debugger: Debugging

Debugger » Debugging

## Debug Mode

The debugger enables you to troubleshoot

- in Simulation Setup: in network nodes and test modules that were developed in CAPL and .NET.

- in Measurement Setup: in program nodes that were developed in CAPL.

- in Test Setup for Test Modules: in test modules that were developed in CAPL and .NET.

- in Test Setup for Test Units: in test units that were developed in CAPL and .NET.

Prior to starting a measurement, debug mode can be activated for as many programs as necessary. Each program, test module or test configuration is displayed in a separate Debugger Window. In simulated mode, debug mode remains active for all supported program types. For a measurement with real hardware, it is only activated for test modules test configurations because the simulation would have to be stopped for network nodes.

If debug mode is deactivated, no variable values are displayed for the node, the test module and the test configuration in the Debugger Window and the program cannot be stopped. This is indicated by the symbol.

If debug mode is active, you can set a breakpoint to the left of the line number with a mouse click. The breakpoint is red in network nodes and orange in test modules and test configurations.

In test modules and test configurations you can select for a breakpoint if the test module or test configuration alone or also the simulation should be stopped. According to the selection the breakpoint is displayed red w

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Debugger] DebuggerDocumentView


# Debugger: Document View

Debugger » Document View

Here, you see the open source code spread over individual pages.

If you have deactivated Debug mode via the toolbar , this is indicated with and a corresponding tooltip. If you have opened a program with a compiler error, this is also visualized with the same icon and a tooltip.

## Connection to program node

If you change a program outside of CANoe, the source code is automatically updated in the Debugger Window directly or at the latest after measurement stop. If a source code file no longer belongs to the main program, this file will be closed automatically.

If you remove a program node, test module or test configuration the associated Debugger Window is also removed.

## Breakpoints

If debug mode is active, you can set a breakpoint to the left of the line number in the document view with a mouse click. A program is interrupted at this breakpoint before it executes the command in the line in which the breakpoint was set. Breakpoints are marked in red for network nodes and orange or red in test modules / test configurations.

If a breakpoint cannot be reached, it is marked as not active by an open circle . Examples are

- Empty lines

- Comments

- Variable declarations

- Function definitions

You can insert only one breakpoint per line. If a line contains multiple commands, the breakpoint relates to the first command.

Clicking the breakpoint removes it without a prompt.

Breakpoints are retained even after the Deb

# Debugger: Variables

Debugger » Variables

The Variables area lists all variables contained in the program in a tabular form. In doing so, variables that are valid at the current breakpoint are black, and all others are grayed. Examples of grayed variables are local variables that can be used only in other sub-functions.

| Name | Name of the variable. |
| --- | --- |
| Value | Variable value at the time of a breakpoint or for a single step. |
| Data type | Data type of the variable. The full class name is displayed for objects. |
| Watch | Selection of whether or not the variable is to be displayed in the Watch area.The selection is saved in the configuration. |
| Function | Function or event handler in which the variable is declared and thus valid. For global CAPL variables, variables is displayed. |

When you double-click a variable, it will be highlighted in the Document view.

With Search you can search for variables.

## Value display

- Variables with simple (scalar) data types and variables of type char[] have one line each.

- Variables of type enumeration have one line each. Their value is displayed as an enumerator (if this type of variable exists for the value).

- Floating-point numbers are displayed in the value column. Integers are displayed as hexadecimal numbers if the global setting in CANoe is hexadecimal.

- Variables with array types (except char[]) can be expanded in the first line. There is a sub-line for each element in the array. Multi-dimensional 

[...truncated for brevity...]

# Administration of Desktops

Desktop concept » Administration

Desktops are administered by a context menu. This context menu appears when the mouse pointer is positioning over a page in desktop page and the right mouse button is pressed. The context menu consists of the following commands:

- Create new desktop

- Delete desktop

- Rename desktop

- Move desktop…

- Copy desktop…

- Activate desktop on configuration load

- Activate desktop on measurement start

- Activate desktop on measurement stop

- Desktop bar

- Desktop page

- Insert panel to configuration…

- Activate desktop

|  | Notes Effective with version 5.0 the position of all panels is stored in the configuration file (*.CFG). In earlier versions (before 5.0) the "attributes" open on configuration load open on measurement start open on measurement stop have been assigned to the panels directly.Effective with the desktop concept in version 5.0 these "attributes" are assigned to the desktop object. When loading an older configuration with panels in CANoe 5.0 desktops objects will be created — according to the "attributes" of the panels — that contain these "attributes". |

### [DesktopManagement] DesktopAdministrationDeleteDesktop


# Delete Desktop

Desktop concept » Delete Desktop

To delete a desktop select it in the desktop page and choose the Delete Desktop command in the desktop context menu.

- If a desktop to be deleted is empty it is deleted immediately.

- However, if there are opened windows on the desktop a message appears.

You must decide whether to close the windows or move them to the first desktop.

You may also decide whether window positions should be updated to the current window positions.

No windows are deleted when a desktop is deleted. If the user wishes to delete a window this is done by a separate function within the relevant blocks.

## General

With the Insert Panel… context menu command of the desktop you can insert panels to the configuration.

In the following file request dialog you can select the panel file. This panel is inserted in the current configuration and shown in the list of the configured panels.

Another quick and easy way to insert panels is to move them into CANoe's main window with drag and drop. This panel is inserted in the current configuration and shown in the list of the configured panels.

## ActiveX Panels

You can integrate external applications that you have implemented in the form of an ActiveX Control component directly in CANoe as panels. The programming languages available are all languages that are permitted by the Microsoft ActiveX interfaces implementation.

|  | Notes for (Visual Basic) Developers In general CANoe does not require any registration of the Top Level ActiveX Control to load it as a panel. However, this does not apply to other controls that are placed on the Top Level Control (this is the VB form). You should check for this, especially if you distribute configurations containing ActiveX panels. Consider that not every user has the necessary Administrator rights to install components on the computer (in other words to "register" them). In general you can assume that the so-called Common Windows Controls and Common Windows dialogs are available on every system that ActiveX components are. The same applies to V

### [DesktopManagement] DesktopConcept


# Desktop Concept

CANoe» Desktop Concept

It is usually the case that CANoe users have a large number of windows opened, and until now these windows have been organized on a single desktop. A new desktop concept is being introduced with CANoe Version 5.0. Multiple virtual CANoe desktops provide the space to organize windows more effectively.

Under the previous window management concept opened windows were organized within the CANoe program window. Panels supporting user-friendly control input and output are managed as independent objects in CANoe, and they are all arranged on the Windows desktop (outside of the CANoe program window). With CANoe Version 5.0 a new window management concept is being introduced, with the goal of giving the user increased flexibility in administering windows.

## Concept

The purpose of desktops is to organize windows for better clarity and comprehension. You can distribute your opened windows to any desired number of desktops and sort information for work processes and other information by topic.

- Each virtual desktop may provide any desired amount of information for viewing.

- It is possible to display identical information (identical windows) on different desktops simultaneously.

To achieve a better understanding of desktops, window management and their applications it is necessary to define certain concepts.

- In CANoe there are windows that are represented in the form of blocks in the Measurement Setup. Each block defines the properti

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [DesktopManagement] DesktopConceptPanelConfiguration


# Panel Configuration: Panels

Desktop concept » Panels » Add panels to configuration

Ribbon: Home Tab|Panels|Configuration

|  | Definition Panels Panels are graphical elements via which values of signals and variables can be changed and displayed using so-called controls. Referenced Panels Panels that are not assigned to the CANoe configuration and are configured using the Panel Control button. |

| Configuration | Programmed Panels | Settings |  |

### [DesktopManagement] DesktopConceptPanelConfigurationSettings


# Panel Configuration: Settings

Desktop concept » Panel Settings

Ribbon: Home Tab|Panels|Configuration

| Configuration | Programmed Panels | Settings |  |

On this page you can set if tooltips on panels and assigned Signal Generators should be displayed and you can set the window type for new configured panels.

## Behavior during measurement

Tooltips are used to support data interpretation on panels.

On this page you can set, if tooltips should be displayed during measurement or not.

Placing the mouse cursor over the signal in the element calls up a tooltip with the following information:

- Symbol Type ...shows whether a signal, environment variable or system variable is being displayed.

- Symbol Name ...shows the name of the symbol.

- Number of significant places In the case of a raw value, this shows the number of places needed to display the hexadecimal format.In the case of a physical value, the number of places needed to display the hexadecimal format is calculated depending on the conversion.

- Raw value / physical value ...shows whether it is a raw value or a physical value, and whether or not the symbol is signed.In the case of a physical value, the physical value range is also shown.

## Display Signal Generators

In order to use panels to automatically stimulate control units over an extended period, Signal Generators can be assigned to symbols on the panels; see detailed description on Support for Signal Generators in panels.

Here, you specify whether

### [DesktopManagement] DesktopManagementFurtherInformation


# Further Information

Desktop Concept » Further Information

## General

Functions and modes of behavior are identical for all desktops, regardless of whether they were created by the user or are standard desktops.

Desktop options and window management options are saved with the configuration.

## Desktop-Dependent Functions

Previously the window functions Open, Close and Change Position were executed for a window in the CANoe program window. With introduction of the desktop concept these window options become desktop-dependent and always relate to the active desktop.

When a block is added to the Measurement Setup the window associated with the block is opened in the active desktop.

Each window of a block or panel that is available to the user may be opened on any desktop, regardless of whether it has already been opened on other desktops.

## Desktop-Independent Functions

Configuring and deleting of windows (blocks) is desktop-independent. If a window is opened on various desktops and is then deleted, it no longer appears on any of the desktops. Changes made by configuring a window, e.g. inserting columns in the Trace window, appear on all desktops that have opened this window.

Toolbars, their contents and behaviors are desktop-independent in CANoe.

Administration of Desktops | Desktop Use Cases

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [DesktopManagement] DesktopManagementMoveWindows


# Desktop: Move Windows between Desktops

Desktop concept » Desktop: Move Windows between Desktops

The Move function allows the user to move windows from one desktop to another.

You just have to select a window, call up the window's context menu and choose the Move to option.A list of all desktops appears, and you select the target desktop.The window is then automatically moved to the selected desktop and is no longer visible on the active desktop.

If that window has already been opened on the target desktop a dialog appears which asks you whether the position settings should be updated.

- If you confirm with [Yes] the window position is updated on the target desktop.

- If you choose [No] then no changes are made for this window on the target desktop.

Administration of Desktops | Move/Copy Desktop | Keyboard Operation: Other Keys

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [DesktopManagement] DesktopUseCases


# Use Cases

Desktop Concept » Use Cases

Use cases are presented here to demonstrate the utility of the desktop and window management concepts. In doing so, you must differentiate between the model producer and the end user.

- A model producer constructs a test configuration. The producer groups the panels to be used according to topic/task areas. The producer also defines the selection of data windows and their configuration (contents and form).

- An end user utilizes the configuration constructed by a model producer to test specific topic/task areas.

## Examples of Configuring Desktops

- Desktops permit sorting of windows by topic.Data Windows — like Trace, Statistics and Graphics windows — might be maintained on one desktop.Another desktop might provide the Configuration windows for the Measurement Setup and Simulation Setup.

- Some users configure various bus systems, e.g. Powertrain Bus and Body Bus systems.The messages of each bus system are represented on separate desktops.

- A gateway sends signals from one bus to another.There is a desktop for each bus system.These signals may be displayed in a Graphics window for comparison purposes.Therefore it makes sense to display this Graphics window on both desktops.

- Some users utilize panels.Desktops are used to classify the panels as input panels and output panels.Other panels can be organized by the bus systems used.

- The window management concept makes it possible to save opened windows structurally.windows wh

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [DesktopManagement] PanelConversion


# Conversion of Panel Editor Panels

CANoe » Panels » Conversion of Panel Editor Panels

As of CANoe Version 9.0, the Panel Editor and panels created with the Panel Editor are no longer supported.

Old panels are converted automatically by opening them in the Panel Designer.

NM controls will be converted automatically since CANoe version 13.0.

## Conversion of Panels: CNP to XVP

If you have decided to convert, note the following:

- The converted panels are located in the same directory as the Panel Editor panels.

- The Panel Editor panels are removed from the configuration but the files are retained.

- The converted panels are added to the configuration.

- Panel Designer panels that reference Panel Editor panels (Panel Control Button) are overwritten and reference the corresponding converted panels.

- Check the converted panels since not all panel settings can be completely converted.

- After the conversion operation, store your configuration.

You will find information on the conversion operation in the Write Window.

During the conversion a report (<ConfigurationName>_PanelConversionLog.txt) is generated containing the following information:

- Date and time of the conversion

- Information on each panel that was converted (regardless of whether or not errors occurred)

- Listing of the Panel Designer panels that were added to the configuration (including referenced panels)

- Listing of the Panel Editor panels that were removed from the configuration (including r

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [DesktopManagement] ScreenLayout


# Screen Layout

Desktop Concept » Screen Layout

## Main Window

- Title bar

In the title bar are displayed:

- The file name of the configuration (*.cfg)

- The window name of a maximized MDI window

- The working mode (Simulated/Real)

On the left of the file name you can find the Quick Access Toolbar.

- Ribbon

The ribbon is located in the main window below the title bar. This is used for fast selection of important functions.

- Desktop tab

You use the tabs at the bottom of the main window to switch between the desktops. You can create desktops for your choice of topics and display your choice of windows inside them.

- Status bar

The status bar at the bottom edge of the main window displays information on the current configuration and measurement according to the situation:

- Load indicator

- Keyboard inputs during the active measurement

- Configuration of the key combinations

- Name of the database

- Write Window state indicator

- Animated state indicators

- Time period of the measurement

## Child Window

Below the toolbar is the content area of the main window. The main window can contain several child windows. There are various options for displaying these windows. (See Window Management for more information). Since many windows are often used in parallel, it is useful to distribute them among desktops structured by topic.

Ribbon | Window Management | Desktop Concept | Window Types | Desktop use cases

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |

During the measurement, the status bar and the ribbon of CANoe displays buttons that enable you to quickly access various functions. ## Ribbon

A click on the arrow symbol opens a submenu to control the different elements. Before the start of the measurement you can here activate/deactivate elements.

## Signal Generators and Signal Replay

You can start, stop, and pause Signal Generators during the measurement. The signal replay is only for display purposes and cannot be changed.

### [DesktopManagement] WindowManagement


# Window Management

CANoe» Window Management

## Context Menu of Windows

The context menu of the child windows provides not only the window types MDI, Standard and Docking but also other window management options:

- Show in Favorites With this command you can add windows and dialogs to the favorites on the Home ribbon tab.

- Copy Screenshot Thereby you can easily create a screenshot of a window (Graphics Window, statistic windows, Data Window, Trace Window, Write Window, Panel or State Tracker) and copy it to the clipboard.See also: Window Capture

- Move to You select the name of the desktop to which the window is to be moved in the submenu.

- Show on all Desktops Thereby the current window is made visible on all desktops.If the window wasn't so far visible on a certain desktop it will be shown at the same position as on the current desktop. Otherwise it keeps its position on other desktops.

For panels the following menu commands are additionally available:

- Edit... Opens the panel in the Panel Designer.

- Start Starts the measurement.

- Stop Stops the measurement.

- Signal Generators

## Ribbon

Depending on the window or panel some of menu commands listed above are also available via the ribbon.

If you move the mouse over a window or panel name in the ribbon, the [...] button is shown at the end of the line. If you then move the mouse over the button, a submenu is shown immediately.

### [DesktopManagement] WindowTypes


# Window Types

CANoe» Window Types

Various types are available for the windows, each of which defines a particular window behavior. You can change the window type via the context menu (opened by right-clicking the title bar).

## MDI Window

- standard form of newly displayed windows

- can only be displayed within the main window

## Standard Window

- can be positioned anywhere on the Windows desktop

- is independent from the main window and has its own button on the task bar

- can be attached to the main window

- can be maximized while the main window is minimized

## Docking Window

- can be docked to predefined positions in the main window using a drag and drop operation

- can be grouped with another docked window

- can be set to Auto-Hide mode

## Position Docking Windows

Click the title bar and drag the window to the desired position. The possible positions for an automatic layout are shown in a preview when dragging. Drag the mouse directly to a preview icon to dock the window there, e.g., at the left edge with this icon :

## Group Docking Windows

You can group several docked windows by dragging the windows directly one above the other. In this case, only one of the grouped windows is displayed at a time. You can call the others using the automatically generated pages. See below: Data and Trace pages:

## Auto-Hide Docking Windows

Docked windows can be switched to Auto-Hide mode using the Auto-Hide button (see above). In Auto-Hide mode, as soon as another 

[...truncated for brevity...]

CANoe » Diagnostics with CANoe | Concept

| General Information |
| --- |
| The Diagnostic Feature Set contains various features that are needed for developing, testing, and calibrating control units with or by means of diagnostics. The integrated diagnostic features allow both analysis as well as simulation of diagnostic services of a control unit. In addition to the use of specific diagnostic commands within CANoe (CAPL), external applications can easily access diagnostic objects and diagnostic parameters via the COM server. Besides CANoe, the Diagnostic Feature Set is also contained in Vector products CANape MC+D and CANdito. As a result, the same support is provided throughout the development process. |
| Use of CANoe in Development Phases |
| CANoe can be used in all phases of development and use of diagnostics in control units: Design of the diagnostic functionality (system simulation) Implementation of the diagnostic functionality in a control unit (remaining bus simulation) Diagnosing of ECUs with the test functionality integrated in CANoe Specification/integration/regression tests Analysis of the communication of real control units Troubleshooting Automatic creation of diagnostic test modules in connection with CANoe Option DiVa Description of tests in XML |

### [Diagnostics/AddressTypes] DiagnosticsAddressTypes


# Addressing Types

Diagnostics with CANoe » Functional Overview » Basics » Addressing Types

## Addressing a ECU — 1:1 Relationship

With physical addressing (physical request), a control unit is addressed by means of a CAN identifier sent by the tester. In other words, there is a 1:1 relationship between the tester and the control unit. The control unit that serves as receiver is known.

## Addressing Multiple ECUs — 1:n Relationship

CANoe enables multiple control units in a network to be diagnosed at once. The receiver does not have to be known.

An entire network with multiple diagnostic descriptions (CDD, ODX/PDX, MDX, Basic Diagnostics) can be accessed using Functional group requests (functional addressing).

## Functional Group Requests

In CANoe a diagnostic description can act as a functional diagnostic description. This means that a diagnostic description specifies certain services that can be received by multiple control units at the same (functional) ID and can be evaluated similarly (functional group).

If diagnostic services are sent on the basis of a functional diagnostic description (functional network requests), a single request for all control units is sent to the network using the functional ID stored in the diagnostic description. All control units answer to this functional ID.

The network nodes do not have to be configured using their own (specific) diagnostic description. The responses of the individual control units are interpreted with this specific

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Diagnostics/AddressTypes] DiagnosticsConfigFuncGroupRequests


# Functional Group Requests

Diagnostics with CANoe » Functional Overview » Configuration - Off-Board » Functional Group Requests

Ribbon: Diagnostics & XCP Tab|Diagnostics/ISO TP Configuration|Administrate Diagnostic Descriptions|Usage|Functional Group Request

To configure a functional diagnostic description in the relevant network you have to do the following steps:

|  | Open the Diagnostics/ISO-TP-Configuration dialog (Diagnostics & XCP ribbon tab). Select a node in the tree view left hand side (e.g. an ECU). In the Usage of the diagnostic description area select the Functional Group Request option. |

|  | Example An sample configuration that demonstrates the usage of functional diagnostic description files please refer to: UDS Diagnostic System Configuration. |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Diagnostics/Analysis] DiagnosticsDiagnosticsObjectsInCAPL


# Diagnostic Objects in CAPL

Diagnostics with CANoe » Functional Overview » Analysis » Diagnostic Objects in CAPL

CAPL programs that communicate with ECUs via diagnostic services will use DiagRequest and DiagResponse objects typically. The following operations can be performed on these objects:

- The value of parameters in the objects can be read and changed. This is possible in different modes. Details: DiagGetParameter, DiagSetParameter, etc.

- Objects can be sent, where a tester may only send request objects, and an ECU simulation may only send response objects. Details: DiagSendRequest, DiagSendResponse

- CAPL programs can react on the reception of a diagnostic object by implementing a matching handler function. Details: On diagRequest, On diagResponse

- CAPL programs can react on the send confirmation for a diagnostic request by implementing a matching callback function. Details: On diagRequestSent

- Tester programs can access a DiagResponse object via a DiagRequest object, if the former was received using the latter. Details: DiagGetRespParameter, etc.

- DiagRequest and DiagResponse objects can be used as arguments for CAPL functions. Details: Data Types for Function Parameters

## Declaration and Initialization

Diagnostic objects are declared and initialized as follows:

Syntax with ECU qualifier in DiagRequest/Response objects:

DiagRequest EcuQualifier.ServiceQualifier request;

DiagResponse EcuQualifier.ServiceQualifier response;

Obsolete syntax:

DiagReq

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Diagnostics/Analysis] DiagnosticsFeaturesAnlyzDisplay


# Filter and Display

Diagnostics with CANoe » Functional Overview » Analysis » Filter and Display

For the diagnostic interpretation in the Trace Window the ISO TP observer has to be configured: Configuration of the ISO TP observer.

|  | Example ISO TP Observer Use Case |

You can also add special fields to the Trace Window to display data sent and received on the ISO/OSEK transport protocol layer as well as on the diagnostic layer:

- Trace Window: Columns for ISO/OSEK TP

- Trace Window: Columns for Diagnostics

To verify the how robustly a control unit reacts to transport protocol layer errors, you can insert disturbances (Fault Injection) into the transport protocol. For more information, please see the CanTpFI_<xx> functions in Appendix: Fault Injection Support (OSEK TP).

## Filter Diagnostic Objects using CAPL Filter in the Measurement Setup

The ISO TP observer generates events that are being sent to the Measurement Setup. There they usually arrive in all branches; if several Trace Window have been configured the TP event will be displayed in all of them.

To show events only in selected Trace Windows, a CAPL filter node can be inserted before the window. In this node the following function must be implemented at which two different signatures are being accepted.

- In the first case the symbolic names of the sending node and the receiving node are handed over in the same way as they are shown in the Trace Window.

- In the second case these values are left out sin

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Diagnostics/Analysis] DiagnosticsScriptingNet


# Diagnostic Scripting in .NET

Diagnostics with CANoe » Functional Overview » Analysis » Diagnostic Scripting in .NET

Vector diagnostic scripting allows you to automate frequently recurring diagnostic processes using .NET scripts and to reuse them in other Vector diagnostic testers.

These .NET scripts can also be played as snippets in the macro recorder in CANoe. For this purpose, you must first configure the corresponding diagnostic description file.

## Diagnostic scripting library

- The Vector diagnostic scripting library enables you to

- Automate diagnostic sequences

- Generate, configure and send diagnostic requests

- Receive and interpret diagnostic responses

- Exchange diagnostic scripts between CANoe, CANape, CANdito and Indigo (beginning with CANoe 7.1. SP3, CANape 8.0, CANdito 4.0, Indigo 1.0 SP2).

It is also possible to create your own libraries.

Using .NET as a basis, you can

- Use any .NET programming environment to develop the scripts

- Use any .NET language

The API was developed for easy application in the diagnostic area. Additional details on the API can be found here.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Diagnostics/Analysis] DiagSendInteractive


# Send Interactively

Diagnostics with CANoe » Functional Overview» Analysis » Send Interactively

The Vector Diagnostic Feature Set includes various functions required for the development, testing and application of control units with/via diagnostics. These functions are part of CANoe but also part of the Vector products CANape and CANdito. So the complete development process is supported in identical manner.

The Diagnostic Feature Set provides interactive access to control unit diagnostic data and does not require programming. For this reason, this method is particularly suitable

- For getting started with diagnostics

- For getting to know the diagnostic interface of a new control unit

- For simple diagnostic sequences in which individual services are to be executed one after the other and the results evaluated directly by the user.

The most essential elements of the Diagnostic Feature Set for interactive diagnostic access are:

- Diagnostic Console In the Diagnostic Console diagnostic requests can be selected, parameterized and displayed with their responses.

- Fault Memory Window The Fault Memory Window offers a fast access to the fault memory of an ECU.

- Diagnostic Session Control With the Diagnostic Session Control you can switch interactively between the states of an ECU (Session-Switch, Security Access).

Once you have added to the configuration via the Diagnostics & XCP ribbon tab|Diagnostics/ISO TP Configuration|Network

- Diagnostic Descriptionwith CDD, OD

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Diagnostics/Analysis] DiagViaCOMServer


# Diagnostics via COM Server

Diagnostics with CANoe » Functional Overview» Analysis » Diagnostics via COM Server

To enable you to use diagnostic object access and analysis methods in other applications, CANoe lets you execute all diagnostic actions, e.g.,

- Generation, manipulation and sending of requests

- Receipt and evaluation of responses

- Symbolic access to parameters defined in the diagnostic description file (CDD, ODX/PDX, MDX)

via the COM Interface.

In this way, you can:

- Integrate ECU diagnostics into external tester applications, using all COM-compatible languages and development environments

- Create graphical controls tailored to your requirements for integration into CANoe. These control elements can contain complex control sequences or diagnostic analyses, enabling comfortable operation and reuse of sequences.

Due to the limited evaluation possibilities with regard to the time behavior of the diagnostic communication and the relatively slow real-time behavior of the COM server interface of MS Windows, the COM diagnostic functions are only suitable for simple diagnostic services, such as querying software statuses or serial numbers. For more complex diagnostic tests it is recommended to implement them in a test module or test unit. You can then start the test modules or test units via COM and evaluate their results via COM.

|  | Example In the directory of the UDS Diagnostic System Configuration in the folder C#\COM_Example\COM_Example (Open Folder)

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Diagnostics/BasicDiagnostics] BasicDiagnostics


# Basic Diagnostics

Diagnostics with CANoe » Functional Overview » Configuration - Off-Board » Basic Diagnostics

Using the basic diagnostics, you can exchange diagnostic information with the ECU also without a diagnostic description (CDD, ODX/PDX, MDX).

|  | Note Basic diagnostics is supported in CANalyzer pro and CANoe run/full as of version 7.2 SP3 and CANalyzer exp as of version 7.5. |

Depending on the protocol you want to use (KWP or UDS) select the corresponding basic diagnostic description.

For simple use cases, basic diagnostics is an alternative to the process-oriented approach using diagnostic description files.

## Supported Use Cases

- Description of simple diagnostic services (UDS & KWP) with the Basic Diagnostic Editor.

- Sending/receiving of defined requests/responses on CAN, LIN, FlexRay, K-Line and Ethernet (DoIP/HSFZ) by means of

- Diagnostic Console and

- CAPL/TFS

- Symbolic interpretation of basic diagnostic services as well as their parameters in the Trace Window.

Basic diagnostics thus represents an extension to the process-oriented approach with CANdela diagnostic descriptions for simple applications.

## Using Basic Diagnostics

In order to use basic diagnostics, you must add a basic diagnostic ECU to the diagnostic configuration.

Outside of the measurement, you can now define/modify the diagnostic services in the and commit them to the diagnostic console. Saving the configuration also commits these changes.

While measurement is stopped yo

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Diagnostics/BasicDiagnostics] BasicDiagnosticsEditorEditWindow


# Basic Diagnostic Editor: Edit Window

Diagnostics with CANoe » Functional Overview » Configuration - Off-Board » Basic Diagnostic Editor » Edit Window

## ECU

If you have selected an ECU node in the tree view, you can add or delete the Negative Response Codes (NRC) and edit them in the Edit window using Edit. Enter a hexadecimal value (1 byte) for the NRC and a symbolic text for this purpose. This text is then displayed in the Diagnostic Console or in the Trace Window if a negative response is received.

## Protocol Service Template

If you have selected a protocol service template in the tree view, all services defined based on this template are displayed. You can add or delete new services using the context menu. When you add a service, the new service is automatically displayed in the Edit window.

## Service

Once you have selected a service, you can concretely populate the service by setting the subfunction/local ID and editing the parameters of the request/response.

- Service Name Enter the name of the service in this input box. The name must be unique within the ECU and may only contain the characters [0 to 9, a to Z, _] and no spaces.

- Request PDU / Response PDU Hexadecimal display of the service PDUs.

- Synchronize subfunction/local ID This check box is used to keep the value of the subfunction between request and response synchronous. If you activate the check box, the value of the subfunction under Response is derived from the request and cannot be changed 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Diagnostics/BasicDiagnostics] BasicDiagnosticsEditorErrorMessages


# Basic Diagnostic Editor: Error Messages

Diagnostics with CANoe » Functional Overview » Configuration - Off-Board » Basic Diagnostic Editor » Error Messages

For consistent population, the Basic Diagnostic Editor immediately checks whether service or parameter names are being used twice or whether parameters overlap, for example, immediately after a field is left.

| Symbol | Description |
| --- | --- |
| / | An erroneous definition has occurred. The editor shows you the point where the error occurred and displays a popup help text. The other input options, changing to another dialog and committing changed definitions to the diagnostic console are disabled at this time. In this case, correct the corresponding value. After leaving the field, the message disappears and you can continue to enter data or commit the changes. |
|  | In the case of parameter overlapping within a service, the editor displays a warning message when a parameter field is left. When you commit new/changed definitions, the editor also informs you of the service in which parameter overlapping is occurring in the footer. In this case, correct the parameters of the corresponding service. After a successful correction, the message disappears and you can continue to enter data or commit the changes. Services whose parameters overlap are removed from the configuration when the configuration is saved. |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Diagnostics/BasicDiagnostics] BasicDiagnosticsEditorMenuBar


# Basic Diagnostic Editor: Menu

Diagnostics with CANoe » Functional Overview » Configuration - Off-Board » Basic Diagnostic Editor » Menu

Select ECU In this list, you select the basic diagnostic ECU which you would like to edit. The Basic Diagnostic Editor shows the definitions of all configured basic diagnostic ECUs in a window.

Configure ECUThis button opens the Diagnostics/ISO TP Configuration dialog.

File You can export the diagnostic service definitions from the currently selected basic diagnostic ECU to a file and import those definitions into another basic diagnostic ECU.

|  | Note In order to be able to import service definitions into a basic diagnostic ECU, its protocol type must match the one used in the export file. The import of UDS basic diagnostic services into a KWP ECU and vice-versa is not supported and will be rejected by the application. |

Edit Depending on the selected element in the service tree or inside a request/response definition, you can add or delete Negative Response Codes, Services or Request/Response Parameters.

CommitThis allows you to accept the changes to the population in the Diagnostic Console of the respective basic diagnostic ECU. Saving the CANoe/CANalyzer configuration also accepts these changes.

Diagnostic ConsoleThis button opens the Diagnostic Console.

Help Opens the help.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Diagnostics/BasicDiagnostics] BasicDiagnosticsEditorServiceTree


# Basic Diagnostic Editor: Tree View

Diagnostics with CANoe » Functional Overview » Configuration - Off-Board » Basic Diagnostic Editor » Tree View

This tree view represents all protocol service templates and the services of the currently selected basic diagnostic ECU based on it.

Create new services from a protocol service template or delete existing services via the Edit menu or the context menu. For this purpose, select the corresponding protocol service or a defined service of the desired protocol service in the tree view.

### [Diagnostics/Basics] DiagnosticsFeaturesBasicAddressSchemes


# Addressing Schemes

Diagnostics with CANoe » Functional Overview » Basics » Addressing Schemes

Different addressing schemes in diagnostics enable the boundary conditions of a use case to be flexibly met. For example, the limited availability of CAN IDs limit the selection of addressing schemes.

The following addressing schemes are supported:

## Normal Addressing

In the normal addressing addressing scheme, the CAN ID contains complete addressing information. All 8 data bytes can be used to exchange data between the tester and the control unit. Each control unit requires two CAN IDs: one for sending and one for receiving.

Advantage: It is possible to use 8 data bytes for the data exchange. Little effort for transport protocol information.

Disadvantage: Two CAN IDs per control unit are required.

## Extended Addressing

One CAN ID is used for all requests to all control units. The CAN ID comprises a base address and a source address. In extended addressing, the destination address is saved in the first data byte of the message. The destination address resolves the control unit to which the request is directed. Only 7 data bytes are available for the data exchange. In addition, one CAN ID per control unit is required for the response.

Advantage: Fewer CAN IDs are required.

Disadvantage: Only 7 data bytes can be used for the data exchange.

## Normal Fixed Addressing (29-bit CAN IDs only)

The CAN ID contains the complete addressing information (sender and receiver) in 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Diagnostics/Basics] DiagnosticsFeaturesBasicDiffBusSystems


# Different Bus Systems/Protocols

Diagnostics with CANoe » Functional Overview » Basics » Different Bus Systems/Protocols

## Supported Bus Systems

You can use CANoe to run ECU diagnostics on all the major automotive bus systems currently used for diagnostics:

- CAN For diagnostics with CAN, you can use ISO TP (OSEK TP (CAN Transport Protocol))or OEM-specific transport protocols (e.g. VW TP1.6/2.0).

- LIN A LIN TP (LinTP.DLL) is available for diagnostics on LIN Slaves. Details on diagnostics with LIN are available under LIN: How to simulate a diagnostic tester.Example for using the LIN transport protocol DLL in a diagnostic tester (LinTP.DLL):Sample Configuration LIN Diagnostic Tester (LINDiagnosticTester.CFG)

- MOST With CAPL gateways you can use the diagnostic features for MOST ECUs. Appropriate examples exist for the transport protocols MOST High Protocol (MHP) and AMS. They also regard the Diagnostic Adaptation Protocol (DAP).

- FlexRay Depending on the OEM, you can use a variety of transport protocols (e.g. AUTOSAR TP) for diagnostics with FlexRay.Details on diagnostics with FlexRay are available under FlexRay: How to use CANoe as a diagnostic tester or FlexRay transport protocol settings.

- Ethernet: DoIP/HSFZ/DoSoAd The implementation of the DoIP, HSFZ or SoAd protocol makes it possible to use CANoe to diagnose both individual ECUs that have a DoIP, HSFZ or SoAd interface for diagnostics and ECUs that are behind a gateway of DoIP/HSFZ to another network.

- K-L

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Diagnostics/Basics] DiagnosticsFeaturesBasicOffBoard


# Off-Board Diagnostics

Diagnostics with CANoe » Functional Overview » Basics » Off-Board Diagnostics

For error detection with off-board diagnostics, one or more control units are connected to an external diagnostic tester that executes the error detection functions (e.g., CANoe). The diagnostic communication occurs via a 1:1 connection between the tester and the control unit using standardized diagnostic protocols.

During active diagnostics, the tester and the control unit typically exchange large quantities of complex data. The interpretation of these data depends not only on the (vehicle) manufacturer but also on the model of the control unit, its year of manufacture, or even on the individual control unit itself.

## Working with Diagnostic Descriptions

The diagnostic data for a control unit are described in a standardized diagnostic description (CDD, ODX/PDX, MDX), taking into consideration the specification, control unit software, and test device parameterization.

The diagnostic description can be assigned to a CANoe configuration. This enables the sending of diagnostic requests as well as a diagnostic interpretation and the output of data with symbolic designators.

Based on the diagnostic descriptions, the Diagnostic Console contained in the Diagnostic Feature Set provides interactive access to all diagnostic requests. These can be selected, parameterized, and displayed with their response.

The Fault Memory Window enables you to read out the fault memory of a c

[...truncated for brevity...]

On level 1 of the tree view (see above) all assigned networks are listed (when available and configured in the simulation setup, with their diagnostic descriptions). ### [Diagnostics/DiagISOTPconfig/DiagDescr] DiagDescAdditional


# Settings Additional Descriptions

Diagnostics with CANoe » Functional Overview » Assignments »Settings Additional Descriptions

Ribbon: Diagnostics & XCP Tab|Diagnostics/ISO TP Configuration|Administrate Diagnostic Descriptions|Assignments|Additional Descriptions

An Additional Diagnostic Description is a diagnostics definition that is used to extend a so called Master Description. This can be used to add diagnostics services to a diagnostic description that cannot or shall not be changed, e.g. because it is provided by an OEM.

The Additional Diagnostic Descriptions share the communication channel of the Master Description and therefore no additional parameterization is required.

The Additional Diagnostic Descriptions can be used in the diagnostic windows (Diagnostic Console, Fault Memory Window and Diagnostic Session Control) as well as in CAPL and for interpretation of diagnostics messages by the Diagnostics Observer in the Trace Window.

## Add Additional Descriptions

Via the Add Additional Descriptions list you can add Additional Diagnostic Descriptions to an existing Master Description. Like for the Master Description, you may choose from:

- Diagnostic Description (CDD, ODX/PDX, MDX)

- Basic Diagnostic Description (UDS)

- Basic Diagnostic Description (KWP)

- Standard Diagnostic Description (CDD)

In the table you will see the Master Description and the Additional Diagnostic Descriptions attached to it. The descriptions are tried in top-down order when the data 

[...truncated for brevity...]

## Diagnostic Description

## ECU Qualifier

Here the ECU qualifier is displayed which will be used by CANoe to refer to the current diagnostic description. The qualifiers for each diagnostic description configured in a CANoe configuration must be unique.

When a description file is added, as default the ECU qualifier as defined in the description will be used. Yet it is also possible to manually overwrite this qualifier here. The qualifier name may only contain letters A…Z and a…z, numbers and underscores _.

Furthermore it is ensured that unique qualifiers are assigned to all ECUs.

|  | Note In particular this has effects on CAPL programs. When you configure the target ECU (DiagSetTarget or DiagInitECUSimulation) you have to specify the correct qualifier! It is also the name used in the Diagnostic Console, Diagnostic Session Control the Fault Memory Win

|  | Note This function makes it easy to assign the same diagnostic description file to multiple ECUs that support identical diagnostic services. ### [Diagnostics/DiagISOTPconfig/DiagDescr] DiagnosticsCDDHints

Diagnostics with CANoe » Functional Overview » Off Board Diagnostics» CDD Diagnostic Descriptions

Ribbon: Diagnostics & XCP Tab|Diagnostics/ISO TP Configuration|Administrate Diagnostic Descriptions|CDD Diagnostic Descriptions

## Converter

In some cases CANoe cannot read a CDD diagnostic description, if it comes from a CANdelaStudio version, that is not yet included in the current CANoe installation. Such newer CANdelaStudio diagnostic descriptions you can make readable for older CANdelaStudio versions with help of CANdela Diagnostic Converters (Persistors). However, only those features are supported, corresponding to the installed CANdelaStudio version. You can download the current converter from the page https://vector.com/persistors:

|  | Download and Install Converter Click the area with the information about Convertors for CANdela Diagnostic Descriptions. On bottom of the next page click the Download now button. Save the file on you computer. Unzip the saved ZIP file. Start the unzipped executable file. Follow the instructions. |

Pack these files into a ZIP archive.E.g. in Windows 7:- right click- Send To|Compressed (zipped) Folder Change the extension of the file from ZIP to PDX.
