### [Diagnostics/DiagISOTPconfig/DiagDescr] DiagnosticsDynamicDataIdentifiersUDS


# Dynamic Data Identifiers (UDS)

Diagnostics with CANoe » Functional Overview » Special » Dynamic Data Identifiers (UDS)

Ribbon: Diagnostics & XCP Tab|Diagnostics/ISO TP Configuration|Administrate Diagnostic Descriptions|Dynamic Data Identifiers (UDS)

## Definition and access to Dynamic DPIDs (only if ECU implements UDS):

Dynamically Defined Data Identifiers are used to create special services with "on the fly contents" in diagnostic testers. At runtime several already existing (static) DIDs or PIDs can be defined as dynamic contents of a DDPID using e.g. the Diagnostic Console.

The definition of a DDPID in UDS is done by e.g. sending a $2C 01 xx yy (xx = 0xF2/0xF3; yy= 0x00-0xFF) service request along with the necessary definition of the PIDs or DIDs to be contained to the ECU.

Once the DDPID is defined in the ECU, it can then be queried from CANoe using the respective ReadDataByIdentifier ($22) or ReadDataByPeriodicIdentifier ($2A) services, either from within CAPL or the Diagnostic Console.

|  | Note On reception of a DDPID, all therein contained PIDs/DIDs (and their symbolic definitions) will be shown in the Trace Window of CANoe. In order to display the contents of the received DIDs/PIDs in the Data Window or Graphics Window, you'll need to insert these single parameters one at a time from the diagnostic parameters' selection dialog. Their value will be updated in the respective window if either e.g. a PID is explicitly queried and received standalone or if it is

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Diagnostics/DiagISOTPconfig/DiagDescr] DiagnosticsMDXHints


# MDX Diagnostic Descriptions

Diagnostics with CANoe » Functional Overview » Off Board Diagnostics » MDX Diagnostic Descriptions

Ribbon: Diagnostics & XCP Tab|Diagnostics/ISO TP Configuration|Administrate Diagnostic Descriptions|MDX Diagnostic Descriptions

## Features and Versions

- Starting with CANoe version 7.6 SP3, diagnostic descriptions in MDX 3.0 format (Multiplex Diagnostic Exchange) are supported.

- Starting with CANoe version 8.1, diagnostic descriptions in MDX 2.0 format (Multiplex Diagnostic Exchange) are supported.

- Starting with CANoe version 9.0 SP3, diagnostic descriptions in MDX 4.0 format (Multiplex Diagnostic Exchange) are supported.

As with ODX/PDX and CDD diagnostic descriptions you can also display diagnostic classes and instances when using MDX diagnostic descriptions. This facilitates the usage of diagnostic services.

If more than one ECU are found in the selected MDX diagnostic description the Select ECU from diagnostic description dialog appears. In this dialog these ECUs are listed. Select the ECUs you want to add to your diagnostic configuration.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Diagnostics/DiagISOTPconfig/DiagDescr] DiagnosticsODXHints


# ODX/PDX Diagnostic Descriptions

Diagnostics with CANoe » Functional Overview » Off Board Diagnostics » ODX/PDX Diagnostic Descriptions

Ribbon: Diagnostics & XCP Tab|Diagnostics/ISO TP Configuration|Administrate Diagnostic Descriptions|ODX/PDX Diagnostic Descriptions

Diagnostic descriptions in ODX/PDX (Open Diagnostic Data Exchange/Packed ODX) format can be used as of CANoe version 6.0.

|  | Note for ODX versions ODX 2.0.1 has been supported since CANoe 6.0. ODX 2.2 has been supported since CANoe 7.1. |

All of the functions developed for CANdela descriptions are available; however, the following ODX format properties should be taken into account:

- ODX diagnostic descriptions can be spread over several files. For this reason, it is important to specify all of the required files, including, in particular, definition of communication parameters.

- An index file simplifies administration of these files. This index file is part of PDX packages (zip archives). A PDX package can be created according to the procedure described here.

- An ODX/PDX diagnostic description file can define the diagnostic functionality of several ECUs (but not "variants"). For this reason, the ECUs to be used must be specified explicitly.

- The contents of the ODX/PDX diagnostic description are converted to the CANdela schematic. This makes it possible to display diagnostic classes and instances, making the diagnostic services easier to use.

- If several responses are defined for one request, o

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Diagnostics/DiagISOTPconfig/DiagSettings] DiagDescDiagLayer


# Diagnostic Layer

Diagnostics with CANoe » Functional Overview » Configuration - Off-Board »Diagnostic Layer

Ribbon: Diagnostics & XCP Tab|Diagnostics/ISO TP Configuration|Administrate Diagnostic Descriptions|Assignments|Diagnostic Layer

Here you can set the behavior of the consoles and specify parameters for CAPL programs. These parameters can be reset during measurement in CAPL.

## Parameters from diagnostic description for transport and diagnostic layer

## Override manually

If the diagnostic description has an interface matching to the network, the default parameters from the interface of the diagnostic description are used. These parameters (see below) you can adjust manually, after activating the Override manually check box.

|  | Note Note that you cannot change this setting in the following cases: An interface using a transport protocol other than ISO TP is selected. The description is loaded on a non-CAN network. |

## Reset to Defaults

Via the [Reset to defaults] button, you can set back your manual changes (see above) to the parameters of the used diagnostic description.

## 'Tester Present' request

Many ECUs must receive such a request in order to remain in the (expanded) diagnostic session. Using this dialog settings can be changed, which control the sending of a Tester Present Service for the Diagnostic Console, the Fault Memory Window and such diagnostic testers, which do not use a CAPL callback interface for diagnostics.

|  | Note If within a tester 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Diagnostics/DiagISOTPconfig/DiagSettings/FR] DiagISOTPObserverFRTP


# FlexRay TP Observer

Diagnostics with CANoe » Functional Overview » Configuration - Off-Board » FlexRay TP Observer

Ribbon: Diagnostics & XCP Tab|Diagnostics/ISO TP Configuration|Additional Diagnostic Settings|FlexRay TP Observer

The FlexRay TP observer interprets messages sent over the FlexRay bus according to a specific Transport Protocol (TP) and diagnostic description and displays the results in the columns of the Trace Window.

Currently the following transport protocols are supported:

- ISO 10681-2 FlexRay Transport Protocol STD Version 2.2 2008-11-28

- AUTOSAR FlexRay Transport Layer V2.1.1 R2.1 Rev 0014

- BMW FlexRay Transport Protocol (2007-04-16 FlexRay_LH_Part3_SW)

|  | Note In order to be able to select the appropriate protocol variant, the appropriate FlexRay package (AUTOSAR FlexRay Support Package or BMW FlexRay TP Support Package) must be installed!If none is installed, then only the ISO 10681-2 FlexRay TP can be interpreted. |

## Performance

## Activate FlexRay TP Observer, enable diagnostic interpretation

Via this check box you can activate the FlexRay TP Observer and thus enable diagnostic interpretation in general for FlexRay.

|  | Note Only frames that are marked in the FIBEX database to be of any Diagnostics or TPL type are considered. |

## Use diagnostic settings instead of TP settings, if possible

If the TP is not configured sufficiently by the diagnostic description, you can define additional parameters here. If you activate this check 

[...truncated for brevity...]

| Name | The name of the parameter to display.You can adjust the display of the parameter name from the underlying diagnostic description (with or without a path, with or without an ECU qualifier) on the Name Display page (CANoeOptions dialog) in the Signals and Environment Variables area, which you can access via this button: . |
| Auto-Read | If you activate the check box in this column, the associated request is automatically sent with the cycle time setting after the measurement starts. |
| Cycle Time [ms] | The cycle time with which the request associated with a response parameter is sent.If multiple parameters are configured from the same response and different cycle times, the shortest cycle time wins. |
| Value | The value of the parameter from the associated response most recently received.You can use the button to send the individual request in question interactively in order to update the value and raw value of the parameter. |
| Unit | The unit of measurement associated with the parameter according to the definition from the diagnostic description. |
| Raw Value | The raw value of the parameter from the associated response most recently received (bytes in hexadecimal representation).You can use the button to send the individual request in ques

The display of the parameters in the right window area can be filtered by activating/deactivating the check box to the left of the associated request. |
|  | Switch on / Switch off Tester Present Tester present requests are sent regularly to the ECU if no other diagnostic communication between the tester and ECU has occurred for some time. Tester present is a message that is sent cyclically and maintains the communication with the ECU. If the filter area is switched on, you can use this button to switch on/off sending of tester present for the associated diagnostic description. This status also applies to other windows that are assigned to the same diagnostic description (e.g., the Fault Memory Window and Diagnostic Console). Note Tester present requests can only be sent if the ECU is online. |  | Note Tester present requests can only be sent if the ECU is online. |

### [Diagnostics/DoIP] DiagnosticsDoIP


# Diagnostics over Internet Protocol (DoIP)

Diagnostics with CANoe » Functional Overview » Basics » Bus Systems/Protocols » Diagnostics over Internet Protocol (DoIP)

|  | Caution! The Diagnostic over IP/HSFZ Diagnostic Access (as known as legacy DoIP access) is available due to compatibility reasons with CANoe versions up to and including 8.0. With this Diagnostic Access only Ethernet network adapters of the user computer can be used, accessing the VN5600 network interfaces is not possible. Additionally, only communication of the diagnostic layer is visible in the Trace Window, which makes debugging more difficult. With option .Ethernet, Eth channels should be used instead in order to benefit from the full DoIP/HSFZ functionality. Only by using the Eth channels it is possible to use VN5600 network interfaces and to observe the communication on all three layers (Ethernet frames, DoIP-PDUs and diagnostic messages) in the Trace Window. The Diagnostic over IP/HSFZ Diagnostic Access is deprecated and therefore should no longer be used. Vector reserves the right to remove this access in a future version from CANoe. |

|  | Cross Reference The current DoIP.DLL in CANoe supports also HSFZ (High-Speed-Fahrzeug-Zugang). You can switch between both types of Ethernet diagnostic communication. Use therefore the list in the Diagnostics/ISO TP Configuration dialog. |

Constant improvements in ECU functionality and memory capacity have resulted in the need for a higher capacity network co

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Diagnostics/DoIP] DiagnosticsDoIPActivationLine


# Activation Line

Diagnostics with CANoe » Functional Overview » Basics » Bus Systems/Protocols » DoIP » Activation Line

Starting with CANoe 9.0 SP3, the DoIP Activation Line can be activated automatically at the start of measurement , and deactivated at the end of measurement. The Activation Line is controlled by a writeable system variable of type long that has the possible values 0 (inactive) and 1 (active).

Suitable system variables you can configure automatically for Vector I/O hardware from the Vector I/O Configuration dialog, e.g. the standard DOUT port of a VN1630 or VN1640 interface.

If such a system variable is found in the configuration, you can select it on page DoIP/HSFZ Main Settings. At the start of measurement it will be set to 1, at the end of measurement it will be set to 0.

When the Activation Line is configured and a diagnostic request is sent from the DCW or FMW before the configured delay has passed after the start of measurement, the following error is printed in the control:DoIP Activation Line startup time did not pass yet - try again later.

From CAPL, the configured system variable and delay can be retrieved with the function DiagGetCommParameter and the following parameters:

- DoIP.ActivationLineStartuptime

- DoIP.ActivationLineSysvar

- DoIP.ActivationLineSysvarNamespace

In a CAPL test module the function TestWaitForDoIPActivationLineStartup can be called to make sure that the configured delay has passed.

DoIP/HSFZ Main Settings

## DoIP/HSFZ Behavior

## Protocol

The protocol version is used in the header of DoIP PDUs. Via this list you select a protocol version for Diagnostics.

- Diagnostics over IP (DoIP)

- Interpretation according to HSFZ (High-Speed-Fahrzeugzugang)

## Activate DoIP/HSFZ interpretation

If you activate this check box DoIP and HSFZ messages are interpreted according to the selected diagnostic description. Recognized service primitives are displayed in the Trace Window.

## Send 0xFF...

If you activate this check box, the DoIP tester will send 0xFF as protocol version in Vehicle Identification diagnostic requests and accept any DoIP protocol version supported by CANoe in the response. This setting is not relevant for HSFZ.

## Activate legacy DoIP access

If you activate this check box, you can use the legacy Diagnostic over IP (DoIP) access in your configuration. The DoIP access is deprecated, but can be used in case the option .Ethernet is not available.

|  | Note It is not recommended to use the legacy DoIP access in parallel with DoIP/HSFZ on Ethernet channels. |

### [Diagnostics/DoIP] DiagnosticsHSFZ


# HSFZ - High-Speed-Fahrzeug-Zugang

Diagnostics with CANoe » Functional Overview » Basics » Bus Systems/Protocols » DoIP » HSFZ - High-Speed-Fahrzeug-Zugang

|  | Caution! The Diagnostic over IP/HSFZ Diagnostic Access (as known as legacy DoIP access) is available due to compatibility reasons with CANoe versions up to and including 8.0. With this Diagnostic Access only Ethernet network adapters of the user computer can be used, accessing the VN5600 network interfaces is not possible. Additionally, only communication of the diagnostic layer is visible in the Trace Window, which makes debugging more difficult. With option .Ethernet, Eth channels should be used instead in order to benefit from the full DoIP/HSFZ functionality. Only by using the Eth channels it is possible to use VN5600 network interfaces and to observe the communication on all three layers (Ethernet frames, DoIP-PDUs and diagnostic messages) in the Trace Window. The Diagnostic over IP/HSFZ Diagnostic Access is deprecated and therefore should no longer be used. Vector reserves the right to remove this access in a future version from CANoe. |

The proprietary HSFZ (High-Speed-Fahrzeug-Zugang) protocol is a predecessor of the Diagnostics over Internet Protocol (DoIP). Therefore, the current implementation of HSFZ in CANoe is based on the DoIP.DLL by different parameterization of that DLL. You can switch between both flavors of Ethernet diagnostic communication in the Diagnostics/ISO TP Configuration dialog.

### [Diagnostics/ISOTPobserver] DiagISOTPObserver


# ISO TP Observer

Diagnostics with CANoe » Functional Overview » Basics » ISO TP Observer

The ISO TP observer interprets messages transmitted on the CAN bus in accordance with ISO Transport Protocol ISO/TF2 (ISO/DIS 15765-2 (2001-11-01)) and with any diagnostic descriptions (CDD, ODX/PDX, MDX) provided, displaying the interpreted message contents such as:

- Sender and receiver addresses of TP messages

- TP protocol information for each CAN message (First Frame, Consecutive Frame, Flow Control, etc.)

- IDs and name/qualifier of the diagnostics services

- Names/qualifiers/values of diagnostic parameters

in the Trace Window.

The ISO TP observer is an absolute prerequisite for diagnosis interpretation

## Interaction between the TP Observer and the Diagnostic Interpretation Settings

The observer is configured from the following sources:

- Settings in the dialog Diagnostics/ISO TP Configuration: Transport Layer

The values specified here are used for the interpretation for the corresponding diagnostic description file.

|  | Note The values in the diagnostic description file itself remain unchanged regardless of any manual modifications made in this dialog and its pages. You can restore the default values stored in the diagnostic description file to the dialog at any time by clicking the [Get Defaults] button. |

|  | Caution! Only if the parameters set here fit the actual transmission can the diagnosis data be interpreted! |

- From the assigned databases (.dbc files) 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Diagnostics/ISOTPobserver] DiagISOTPObserverAdvanced


# ISO TP Observer - Advanced

Diagnostics with CANoe » Functional Overview » Configuration - Off-Board »ISO TP Observer - Advanced

Ribbon: Diagnostics & XCP Tab|Diagnostics/ISO TP Configuration|Additional Diagnostic Settings|ISO TP Advanced

With the check boxes in this dialog you can select the messages that shall be displayed in the Trace Window:

## Messages displayed in the Trace Window

- Survey compliance of STmin (minimal separation time) A warning is issued when a Consecutive Frame (CF) is seen earlier than STmin after its predecessor. The first CF after the First Frame is not checked.

- Expect offset STMin before the first CF (consecutive frame) A warning is issued when the first Consecutive Frame is seen earlier than STmin after the Flow Control frame from the receiver.

- Supervise sequence number series A warning is issued when the sequence number in the Consecutive Frames (CF) is not correct. The first CF must have the sequence number configured in the observer INI file (cf. FirstSN), and no value may be repeated or skipped.

- Report frames unexpected according to protocol A warning is issued when the type of a protocol frame is unexpected at that time during a data transmission. For example:A Consecutive Frame (CF) without First Frame (FF).A FF or Single Frame (SF) while a transmission waits for a CF or Flow Control (FC).The protocol information in the frame is not known.

- A Consecutive Frame (CF) without First Frame (FF).

- A FF or Single Frame (SF) whil

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Diagnostics/ISOTPobserver] DiagISOTPObserverExamp


# ISO TP Observer Use Case

Diagnostics with CANoe » Functional Overview » Configuration - Off-Board »ISO TP Observer Use Case

- To allow use of the ISO TP observer to analyze messages in the Trace Window you can

- Assign databases to your configuration that define the ISO TP typical attributes for the network or the network nodes,

- Assign diagnostic description files (CDD, ODX), and

- Configure additional TP settings manually (see menu path Diagnostics/ISO TP Configuration...|Additional Diagnostic Settings|ISO TP Observer).

The TP parameters of all the above-mentioned setting options for the interpretation of TP traffic are used ("set union") as long as they are not contradictory.

|  | Note If the different configuration methods specify conflicting settings for TP parameters within the same network (e.g. different sending and receiving CAN IDs for the same network node in the database and the diagnostic description files) this can lead to unpredictable interpretation results. |

When using both databases and diagnostic description files as part of the same configuration, please take particular care to ensure that the corresponding TP parameters are compatible.

The most important attributes in connection with the database approach are:

- TpAddressingMode

- TpTxIdentifier

- TpRxIdentifier

- TpBaseAddress or TpNodeBaseAddress

- Diagnostic station address

|  | Note For a detailed description of the attributes and applicable attribute values, please refer to CANdb+

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Diagnostics/ISOTPobserver] DiagISOTPObserverOBMini


# ISO TP Observer via OBM.INI

Diagnostics with CANoe » Functional Overview » Special » ISO TP Observer via OBM.INI

| Shortcuts |
| --- |
| Configuration |  |
| Flow Control Messages | Timeouts |
| Zero Padding | Output Control |

## Configuration

The ISO TP observer uses the settings in OSEK_OBM.INI the configuration file to identify and interpret messages of the Transport Layer. The observer searches for the OSEK_OBM.INI file first in the directory where the configuration file is and second in the application data directory.

As long as you use only the address modes NormalFixed and/or Mixed the INI file doesn't need to contain any information – actually it doesn't need exist.

As long as you use only the address modes Normal and/or Extended you have to set the parameters standard address mode, base address and RxMask (see below).

|  | Notes The predefined default values can be replaced by attribute values from the database that is associated with the configuration if a node has been specified there and the transmitted data can be assigned to it. The parameter names in the INI file are not case sensitive; e.g. ADRMODE and AdrMode are both accepted as the same.The attribute names in the database are case sensitive. |

## Example of a configuration file OSEK_OBM.INI

[OSEK TP OBSERVER]// Address mode Normal=0/Extended=1ADRMODE=1// Global Default ValuesTpBaseAddress=0x600TpRxMask=0xff// Ignore connections between nodes that are not specified in the database?OnlyKnownNodes=

[...truncated for brevity...]

Diagnostics with CANoe » Functional Overview » Configuration - Off-Board » ISO TP Observer Settings

Ribbon: Diagnostics & XCP Tab|Diagnostics/ISO TP Configuration|Additional Diagnostic Settings|ISO TP Observer

The ISO TP Observer interprets messages sent over the CAN bus according to the ISO transport protocol ISO/TF2 (ISO/DIS 15765-2 (2001-11-01)) and displays the results in the Data columns of the Trace Window; see also CAPL Filter in the Measurement Setup.

Via the Activate ISO TP observer, enable diagnostics interpretation check box you can activate the ISO TP Observer and thus enable diagnostic interpretation.

|  | Note Since CANoe v5.2 only the integrated Observer is shipped. It completely replaces the Hook DLL that has formerly been shipped. |

## Performance

## Activate ISO TP observer, enable diagnostic interpretation

If you don't need diagnostic interpretation deactivate this check box to save processor capacity. In this case no diagnostic observer events are visible in the Trace Window.

## Detection of TP Messages

If the TP is not configured sufficiently in the database you can use additional parameters. If you activate Use additional TP parameters, you can select one of the following parameters:

- No Additional Detection Except the CAN identifiers that are defined in the used diagnostic descriptions no other identifiers are used to recognize TP messages.

- Normal Here you can additionally edit a Base address and an Rx mask.

-

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Diagnostics/J1939] DiagJ1939DynAddrClaim


# J1939 Address Claiming

Diagnostics with CANoe » Functional Overview » Basics » J1939 Address Claiming

## Concept

## UDS Diagnostics on J1939 Networks

The UDS diagnostics (ISO 14229) defined for other communication networks can also be used on J1939 networks. The CAN transport protocol (TP) according to ISO 15765 with NormalFixed addressing mode is used for this.

In this addressing mode, the sender and receiver addresses, each 8 bits wide, are coded in the CAN ID.

Diagnostics based on this pattern has already been possible in CANoe for several years.

## Dynamic Assignment of TP Addresses

A special feature of J1939 networks is the possibility to change TP addresses during operation. This can be necessary if another device is connected to the bus.

To prevent duplicate use of addresses, so-called address claiming is implemented. This requires that every node (several nodes may be active in one device) has a device identification, the Device Name, that is unique worldwide.

This name can be used to reassign the TP addresses of the node during runtime.

## Effects on CANoe

The use of address claiming has the following effects on diagnostics in CANoe:

- CANoe as a node in the network requires its own TP address.In order for this to be assigned, CANoe must have a Device Name device identification.In actuality, it is possible to specify various device identifications in CANoe for different purposes.

- Prior to sending each diagnostic request, CANoe must check whether th

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Diagnostics/J1939] DiagJ1939DynAddrClaimDeviceId


# J1939 Tester

Diagnostics with CANoe » Functional Overview » Configuration - Off-Board » J1939 Tester

Ribbon: Diagnostics & XCP Tab|Diagnostics/ISO TP Configuration|Administrate Diagnostic Descriptions|Assignments|J1939 Tester

## Initial Transport Protocol Address of J1939 Tester Device

Here you configure the name of the tester device.

## Settings for Address Claiming / J1939 Device Name

As soon you activate in the J1939 Transport Layer dialog under Address Claiming the Enable Address Claiming check box, there are overtaken the addresses you specify here in the J1939 Tester dialog under Settings for Address Claiming.

Here are provided the address fields that are described on the following page: Device Name

|  | Note CANoe cannot use any wildcards (see below) here because all fields must be defined. CANoe tries to get the initial TP address. In case of failing the search is repeated with following addresses. Wildcards A Device Name consists of 10 fields, one of which is defined as reserved and is therefore always 0. The remaining 9 fields are permanently assigned in a specific node. Typically, however, only the values of a subset of the fields, and not all of the fields, are known exactly when diagnostics are performed. Thus, for example, the function of a group of devices is the same, but never their Identity Number. In order to now deal with a different device identification (and thus different Device Names) in a CANoe configuration, fields can be masked in the con

[...truncated for brevity...]

Ribbon: Diagnostics & XCP Tab|Diagnostics/ISO TP Configuration|Administrate Diagnostic Descriptions|Assignments|J1939 Transport Layer

## Parameters from Diagnostic Description for transport and diagnostic layer

## Override manually

If the diagnostic description has an interface matching to the network, the default parameters from the interface of the diagnostic description are used. These parameters (see below) you can adjust manually, after activating the Override manually check box.

For basic diagnostic descriptions the parameters can be overwritten any time.

|  | Note Note that you cannot change this setting in the following cases: An interface using a transport protocol other than ISO TP is selected. The description is loaded on a non-CAN network. |

## Reset to Defaults

Via the [Reset to defaults] button, you can set back your manual changes (see above) to the parameters of the used diagnostic description.

## Addressing

The provided parameters are listed in a table. You can change the CAN ID and activate or deactivate the 29 bit identifier via the check box.

For the communication between testers and control units (ECUs), several "transport channels" are used:

- Physical requests The tester sends these to exactly one ECU.

- Physical responses The response to a physical request.

- Functional requests The tester sends these to a group of co

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Diagnostics/KLine] DiagnosticsKLine


# K-Line

Diagnostics with CANoe » Functional Overview » Basics » Bus Systems/Protocols » K-Line

## General

For diagnostics of ECUs on K-Line CANoe offers a built-in diagnostic channel according to ISO 14230-2 (Keyword 2000).

To access the ECU following Vector interfaces can be used:

- VN1611 (one K-Line channel)

- VN1630 (two K-Line channels)

- VN1640 (two K-Line channels)

- VN8970 (two K-Line channels)

- VN8972 (two K-Line channels)

As physical layer the K-Line capable LIN transceiver 7269 is used.

Alternatively you can use a serial port built-in to your computer and a Ser2K level converter from Vector attached to that serial port as hardware access the ECU. However, Vector recommends instead of the serial port to use the Vector interfaces listed above due to the higher timing accuracy. Some K-Line features are supported only with these interfaces.

|  | Notes on hardware using serial ports In order to meet timing requirements as good as possible – especially when performing diagnostics on FastInitialization ECUs – either a built-in computer serial port or a serial port connected to the computer via PCI/PCIe/PCMCIA/expressBus should be used. Common "off the shelf" USB/RS232 converters typically cannot provide the same performance and should not be used. Yet even when built-in serial ports are used, some restrictions may apply: Jitter in wake-up pattern timing / ECU does not react on wake-up pattern Adapt the K-Line parameters in the configuration of the diagnosti

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Diagnostics/KLine] DiagnosticsKLineConfiguration

Diagnostics with CANoe » Functional Overview » K-Line » Protocol Settings » K-Line Settings

Ribbon: Diagnostics & XCP Tab|Diagnostics/ISO TP Configuration|Administrate Diagnostic Descriptions|Assignments|K-Line Settings

## Configuration using Vector Interfaces

## K-Line Channel Configuration using Vector Interface

To use a K-Line channel please select a K-Line channel in the Options dialog under Measurement|General|Channel Usage.

Assign a K-Line application channel to the used hardware in the Vector Hardware Config dialog.

In the Diagnostics/ISO TP Configuration dialog now the Vector hardware based K-Line channels are visible - beside the COM interface - and can now be configured for diagnostics.

## Configuration of the K-Line ECU simulation using Vector Interface

To simulate a K-Line ECU please select a K-Line channel in the Options dialog under Measurement|General|Channel Usage.

Assign a K-Line application channel to the used hardware in the Vector Hardware Config dialog.

In the Diagnostics/ISO TP Configuration dialog now the Vector hardware based K-Line channels are visible - beside the COM interface - and can now be configured for the simulation.

|  | Note For the initialization of an ECU simulation, the ECU must be configured in the Diagnostics/ISO TP Configuration dialog. The initialization itself must be done with CAPL. |

## Configuration of the K-Line Monitoring using Vector Interface

To observe K-Line communication please select a K-L

### [Diagnostics/LIN] LINDiagnosticsSettingsDlg


# LIN Settings

LIN » Diagnostics with CANoe » Functional Overview » Protocol Settings » LIN Settings

Ribbon: Diagnostics & XCP Tab|Diagnostics/ISO TP Configuration|Diagnostic Descriptions|Assignments|LIN Settings

The LDF file contains LIN diagnostic data. This data is imported and used for the diagnostics. The values in the CDD file are ignored.

The majority of diagnostic values in the LDF file are optional. If a value is not present in the LDF file, a default value from the LIN 2.1 specification is used.

## Parameters from LDF File for LIN Settings and Diagnostic Layer

## Override manually

If the diagnostic description has an interface matching to the network, the default parameters from the interface of the diagnostic description are used. These parameters (see below) you can adjust manually, after activating the Override manually check box.

## Reset to Defaults

Via the [Reset to defaults] button, you can set back your manual changes (see above) to the parameters of the used diagnostic description.

## Transport Protocol

- STMin Minimum separation time (in ms) between two frames that the sender of a segmented message shall keep.

- N_As_timeout Time the sender of a frame waits for the send confirmation.

- N_Cr_timeout Time the receiver waits for a frame that is due from the sender.

- NAD LIN Node address, typically specified in the LDF file and assigned automatically with the selection of a network node.

### [Diagnostics/Special] DiagnosticsTips


# Tips & Tricks

Diagnostics with CANoe » Functional Overview » Special »Tips & Tricks

|  | FAQ – Use the same diagnostic description (CDD/ODX) multiple times for functionally identical control units If you want to use the same diagnostic description file (CDD/ODX) simultaneously for several control units - because, for example, the control units have the same diagnostic functionality but are addressed with different IDs - then you can add the diagnostic description to your configuration several times. To make the diagnostic description unique, you must modify for each entry in the Assigned Diagnostic Descriptions list at least the descriptor and the Protocol Settings. If you want to use the same diagnostic description to generate diagnostic responses in several simulation nodes, simply modify the bus or node assignment settings in the same way. |

|  | FAQ – Access symbolic diagnostic objects and diagnostic parameters from the CAPL browser If you are evaluating diagnostic requests or responses in CAPL, you can access the diagnostic objects and their associated parameters, which are stored in the corresponding diagnostic descriptions, from within the CAPL Browser via the context menu. Right-click either in the text editor of the CAPL Browser Window or in the global variable declaration and select the entries Diagnostic object from CANdela…Opens the Select Diagnostic Objects dialog. Diagnostic parameters from CANdela…Opens the Select Diagnostic Object Parameters dialog. |

|

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Diagnostics/Special] DiagSecurityDLLAccess


# Seed & Key DLL / Security Access

Diagnostics with CANoe » Functional Overview » Special » Seed & Key DLL / Security Access

|  | Note Starting with CANoe 7.1 or CANalyzer 8.0 it is possible to use a Seed & Key DLL without GenSAKey.DLL. |

The DiagGenerateKeyFromSeed and other functions encapsulate the concrete implementation of the key algorithm in the provider-dependent Seed & Key DLL. The Seed & Key DLL must be configured in the diagnostic configuration dialog (Diagnostics/ISO TP Configuration|Diagnostic Description Settings|Diagnostic Layer) for each diagnostic description that shall be used with DiagGenerateKeyFromSeed.

|  | Note The target has to be selected before with the DiagSetTarget function. |

The algorithm implemented in the DLL may be complex, or it may need to communicate with other network entities. Therefore it is possible that a computation needs more than a fraction of a milli-second to complete. In this case, calling DiagGenerateKeyFromSeed would stop the execution of the real-time measurement process, possibly causing problems like event loss and timeouts.

Therefore it is recommended to use the following functions:

- In a test module, TestWaitForGenerateKeyFromSeed will perform the same functionality, but allows waiting for the result.

- In simulation nodes, e.g. ECU simulations or event-controlled testers, using DiagStartGenerateKeyFromSeed and _Diag_GenerateKeyResult will prevent real-time problems by performing the calculation in the background

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Diagnostics/Test] DiagAutomatedTest


# Automated Testing of ECU Diagnostics

Diagnostics with CANoe » Functional Overview » Testing » Automated Testing of ECU Diagnostics

Control unit diagnostic functions can be tested with CANoe in the following ways:

- Functional testing of diagnostics using CAPL test cases (see Using diagnostic functions in test cases):You can use this to insert diagnostic commands into complex test runs and to add diagnostic commands to existing test cases or test modules. Doing so is particularly useful if testing a diagnostic function involves several complex steps.See UDS Diagnostic System Configuration.Example: Stimulate a physical input on the control unit; start a diagnostic routine with a request; end and read the results of a diagnostic routine with another request; evaluate the response dependent on an input signal.

- Testing diagnostics using the Diagnostics Service XML test function:If the expected response parameters for a diagnostic service are known and fixed (e.g., "Maximum response time for a request < 50ms"), you can use these to test a control unit diagnostic interface without having to program a test control. Numeric and symbolic parameters can be copied directly from the test specification to the test pattern (see also XML test modules structure).See CentralLockingSystem sample configuration.

- Testing fault memory using dedicated XML test patterns for using information from the fault memory for application tests:There are three XML test patterns (faultmem_clear, fau

### [Diagnostics/Test] DiagnosticsAndTesting


# Diagnostics and Testing

Diagnostics with CANoe » Functional Overview » Testing » Diagnostics and Testing

For test nodes and test modules, there are different methods for diagnostic communications with an ECU, depending on the bus system.

## CANoe Diagnostic Channel

If you are using diagnostic description files (CDD/ODX) or Basic Diagnostics, you can send data from a diagnostic tester to the ECU to be diagnosed via a diagnostic channel built directly into CANoe. The transport protocol parameters (e.g. CAN IDs, IP addresses of the tester and the ECU) and diagnostic layer parameters (e.g. P2/P2ext timings) needed for the configuration are either read from the diagnostic description file or use pre-defined values in the case of default interfaces. These parameters can be overwritten with custom values in the CANoe diagnostic configuration. In the case of diagnostics with FlexRay, Diagnostics over Internet Protocol (DoIP), High-Speed-Fahrzeug-Zugang (HSFZ) and diagnostics with LIN, such configuration data is drawn from network-specific databases (FIBEX, LDF) or from an INI file.

## Using a Transport Protocol DLL in the Tester

One or more transport protocols are available for the CAN, LIN, FlexRay and DoIP/HSFZ bus systems in the form of modeling libraries (DLL files). These can be integrated into test nodes manually (see Components page). The diagnostic layer of the tester is connected to the transport protocol layer via a CAPL Callback Interface (CCI). Sample implementat

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Diagnostics/Test] DiagnosticsAutomaticVariantIdentification


# Automatic Variant Identification

Diagnostics with CANoe » Functional Overview » Testing » Automatic Variant Identification

Diagnostic descriptions contain different variants of the control unit, which typically correspond to development stages or application scenarios. To check whether a suitable variant is available, you should identify the built-in variant during control unit diagnostics.

## Requirements

For the purpose of identifying the built-in variant, you need to enter identification characteristics in the diagnostic descriptions. If these characteristics are not entered, the process cannot be started.

## Special Features

Variant identification is performed in the built-in diagnostic channel; during this time, the channel will not be available for other types of communication. It is not recommended that you attempt to communicate with the control unit while identification is being performed, as this would violate the diagnostic protocol.

## Procedure

After selecting the control unit using the DiagSetTarget function, you can start identification of the built-in variant using DiagStartVariantIdentification. If the TestWaitForDiagVariantIdentificationCompleted function is active, a test module can wait for the process to be completed.Alternatively, you can use DiagGetIdentifiedVariant to determine whether the identification has already been completed. This is also possible for event-controlled test nodes. The identified variant can be compared with the configur

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Diagnostics/VariantCodingWindow] VariantCodingWindowColumns


# Columns

Diagnostics with CANoe » Functional Overview » Basics » Variant Coding Window » Columns

Ribbon|Diagnostics & XCP Tab|Variant Coding|Open Window

The columns in the Coding Data area have the following meaning:

| Column | Description |
| --- | --- |
| ECU/Coding Domain/Parameter | Name or qualifier of the respective ECU (/diagnostic description), coding domain or (sub-)parameter. By clicking on the arrow buttons you can expand or collapse nested data structures. |
| Specified Value | Specified value for the relevant parameter, is initialized with the default value stored in the diagnostic description and written to the connected ECU when writing. Differences between ECU value and specified value are highlighted in color. |
| ECU Value | Value read from the connected ECU during the last operation Read for the relevant parameter or value written during the last operation Write (if writing was successful according to the diagnostic response). |
| Unit | Unit belonging to the respective parameter, if defined in the diagnostic description. |
| Last Operation | Result of the last operation (read, write) performed. The following results are possible: —So far, no operation has been performed. Pending An operation is in the queue. Reading Operation Read was started and is not yet finished. Read Variant coding data was successfully read from the connected ECU. Writing Operation Write was started and is not yet finished. Written Variant coding data was successfully written t

Export file Use this input box to specify the save path to which the VVCD file should be exported.

Comment This input box allows you to enter a comment or description for the entire file, which will then be displayed during import.

Use selection from Variant Coding Data Window If you activate this check box (default), the same ECUs and domains are preselected in the tree view below as in the Selection column of the Variant Coding Window. In this case, you cannot change the selection.

If you deactivate the check box, you can change the selection of ECUs and domains in the tree view below.

The last setting made before the export is saved and restored the next time the dialog is opened.

Search This input box allows you to search and filter in the tree view of ECUs and domains.

Export Use this button to start the export.

Ribbon|Diagnostics & XCP Tab|Variant Coding|Open Window

| Symbol | Function |
| --- | --- |
|  | Read selected parameters Reads the variant coding data from the connected ECU and compares it with the specified values. |
|  | Read selected parameters and use their ECU values as new specified values Reads the variant coding data from the connected ECU. After successful reading, the ECU values read are transferred as new specified values. |
|  | Write selected parameters Writes the specified values for the variant coding data to the connected ECU. It is assumed that the write was successful if the corresponding write services return positive responses. |
|  | Write and read selected parameters Writes the specified values for the variant coding data to the connected ECU and then reads them back from the ECU to ensure that they have been written successfully. |
|  | Select parameters with differences Selects parameters with different values in the Selection column. Parameters with the same values are deselected. |
|  | Reset specified values to default values Writes the specified values for the variant coding data to the connected ECU and then reads them back from the ECU to ensure that they have been written successfully. |
|  | Import variant coding data Imports the desired variant coding data (specified values) from a VVCD file. |

### [Ethernet] EthernetConcept


# Ethernet for CANoe | Concept

Ethernet » Concept

| Overview |
| --- |
| The option .Ethernet supports the basic concepts of CANoe and extends them with Ethernet-specific functions. With Ethernet, categorization of protocols in layers is strongly pronounced. CANoe .Ethernet supports you with the analysis,simulation and test at all levels, providing multiple functions. |
| Link Layer |
| Ethernet frames are exchanged at this level. Ethernet switches guide an Ethernet frame to the addressed receiver using MAC addresses. Virtual networks (VLAN) can be used to form domains. CANoe processes Ethernet frames as Ethernet packet events. Via a network interface, for example VN5610, Ethernet packets can be received and also sent by an Ethernet link. CANoe allows you to evaluate an Ethernet packet from the Ethernet level to the application protocol. For example, all protocols of an Ethernet packet are displayed in the Detail View of the Trace Window. For stimulation, Ethernet packets with the required protocol information can be generated and sent. The Ethernet Packet Builder or CAPL can be used for this purpose. Programming The CAPL functions of the link layer can be used for analysis, stimulation, test and fault injection. For the simulation, CAPL functions of the internet and transport layer or the application layer should be used in order to make the generation of CAPL programs easier. on ethernetPacket can be used to receive and evaluate individual Ethernet packets. Selectors all

### [Ethernet] EthernetMigrationWizard


# Ethernet Migration Wizard

Ethernet » Ethernet Migration Wizard

VN Ethernet interfaces from firmware version 11.1 support network-based access. It is recommended to replace configuration using channel-based network access with configurations using network-based access.

When loading a channel-based CANoe configuration that is in Real Bus mode, a window appears from which you can start the migration wizard. Prerequisite is that a driver version 11.2 or higher is installed. You can also perform the migration later. However, the window appears every time a non-migrated configuration is loaded.

|  | Support/Knowledge Base If you have problems during or after the migration, please contact our Vector Support or use the Vector Knowlege Base (Category: CANalyzer/CANoe|Options|Ethernet (IP)|Migration Wizard). |

## Migration Wizard

The Migration Wizard helps you in 3 steps to migrate your channel-based CANoe and interface configuration to a network-based configuration.

On the Welcome to the Migration Wizard page, click [Next>] to begin the migration with step 1.

With [Cancel] you can cancel the migration at any step.

## Step 1: Select Device

In the first step, you must select the interfaces (devices) which provide the application channels needed by CANoe (e.g. VN5640 (001146) provides Eth1, VN5640 (006065) provides Eth2 and Eth3, ...).

The required application channels are displayed in Required tool application channels.

The Available Devices list displays the interfaces t

### [Ethernet] EthernetPortBasedNetworkAccess


# Network-based Access

Ethernet » Network-based Access

## New Concept for Ethernet VN Family since Firmware Version 11.1

Application note AN-IND-1-023_Ethernet_VN_Family_From_Firmware_Version_11.1.pdf describes how the concept behind the VN Ethernet interfaces was changed. These changes allow for greater flexibility in interface segmentation and in scaling both the number of network connectors and the simulation and analysis performance.

At the end of this page you will find two examples to illustrate the following terms.

## Requirement

To work with the VN Ethernet Interface, you need a network-based configuration

You can activate the network-based access with the Access network via Ethernet ports option in Options dialog or by using the network-based templates.

## Hardware Configuration

The following elements play a central role in the hardware configuration and are discussed from the CANoe perspective.

| Network | In CANoe, it is no longer necessary to define a separate Ethernet network for each switch, TAP or direct connection. It is now possible to combine all nodes that can communicate with each other via layer-2 (L2) switches into one network. Note The network definition for the network-based access is a L2 network (i.e. the broadcast domain). All nodes can communicate with each other via L2-switches (VLANs not considered). Give a meaningful name to an Ethernet network so that its use is unique. A network defined in CANoe and a network defined in the Ethernet

[...truncated for brevity...]

Ethernet » Port Configuration

Ribbon: Hardware Tab|Port Configuration

This dialog is only available with a loaded network-based configuration or with the activated Access network via Ethernet ports option in Options dialog. Here, you can configure

- which ports are to be measured

- the assignment of simulated ports to a hardware segment, which controls where the simulation participates in the Ethernet topology

## Views

The content of the dialog depends on

- If CANoe is already connected to the interface(s) that are configured with the required network(s), the Port Configuration dialog shows the ports and segments defined on the hardware in combination with the simulation ports defined in CANoe.

- If no interface is attached or if the required network cannot be found, you can configure manually.

## Configured Ports

All ports contained in the configuration are displayed here sorted by network and category in a tree view.

You can find a detailed description of the terms and example on the Network-based Access page.

## Network

All measurement and simulation ports of the available networks are displayed here.

## Context Menu

Optimize the Required Hardware Resources (Reduce Number of Used Ports) If you activate this option, CANoe optimizes the hardware resources used for the simulation.A detailed description can be found below at the simulation ports.

Activate All Measurement Ports Activates all measurement ports.

Deactivate All Measurement P

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Ethernet/Basics] BroadRReachSQI


# BroadR-Reach - SQI

Ethernet » BroadR-Reach - SQI

The SQI (Signal Quality Indicator) is a value, that is supported by BroadR-Reach hardware to describe the signal quality of this physical layer.

With channel-based access the SQI can have the following values representing the signal quality in ascending order:

- ErrorOccuring

- NoMargin

- Marginal

- Acceptable

- Good

- Excellent

With network-based access the signal quality is now represented by a plain value in range 0 to 7 according to the Open Alliance SIG (Advanced_PHY_features_for_automotive_Ethernet), were

- SQI=0 is the worst value and may indicate a link loss.

- SQI=7 is the best possible value.

The SQI value should increase/decrease monotonic with noise level and indicate in the data sheet at which level a BER<10-10 (better than 10-10) is achieved.

If CANoe is not able to determine any SQI values — e.g. in simulation mode or with an IEEE 802.3 connection — the text Not available is displayed for both modes, to signalize the missing value.

Bus Statistics Window: Ethernet | System Variables for Statistics (Ethernet)

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |

### [Ethernet/Basics] LengthDefinitions


# Length Definitions

Ethernet » Length Definitions

## Packet Length

The packet length specifies the number of data in bytes that are typically sent from the interface hardware to CANoe.The packet length depends on the network type.

For Ethernet the packet length includes the fields beginning at the destination MAC address to the end of the payload or the padding bytes. A packet length less than 60 byte are not allowed for Ethernet (Ref. IEEE Std 802.3TM-2008).

## Frame Length

The frame length includes the number of bytes of data contained in the Ethernet header, the Ethernet user data and the frame checksum. The frame length is 4 bytes longer than the packet length.

## Ethernet Size

|  | Note The Ethernet size is supported for the network type Ethernet only. |

The Ethernet size is specified by the packet length minus the Ethernet header. It might be called as payload length of the Ethernet packet also. The Ethernet header has a length of 14 or 18 bytes and includes the MAC addresses of sender and receiver, the field Ethertype and the field VLAN-Tag (if available).

## Payload Length

The payload length specifies the number of data in bytes that are contained in the highest protocol that is interpreted by CANoe. So the payload length always refers to the protocol that is displayed in the Protocol column of the Trace Window.

For application protocols with no own protocol header (e.g. SOME/IP), the payload length must be set to the same length as the previous protocol

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Ethernet/Basics] SyntaxIPv6Addresses


# Syntax of IPv6 Addresses

Ethernet » Syntax of IPv6 Addresses

## Valid IPv6 Addresses

- default notation containing only hexadecimal values in uppercase, without leading zeros and without zeros compressor

- 1080:0:0:0:8:800:200C:417A (a unicast address)

- FF01:0:0:0:0:0:0:101 (a multicast address)

- 0:0:0:0:0:0:0:1 (the loop back address)

- 0:0:0:0:0:0:0:0 (the unspecified address)

- hybrid notation mixing IPv6 hexadecimal values and IPv4 decimal values

- 0:0:0:0:0:0:13.1.68.3

- 0:0:0:0:0:FFFF:129.144.52.38

- with zeros compressor

- 1080::8:800:200C:417A (a unicast address)

- FF01::101 (a multicast address)

- ::1 (the loop back address)

- :: ( the unspecified address)

- ::13.1.68.3

- ::FFFF:129.144.52.38

- with blanks (spaces, tab characters etc.) that are discarded and ignored during parsing

- FF FF: :1 92.168.1.1 is interpreted as FFFF::192.168.1.1

- others without particular styles

- ::192

- 1:000::0ff:6f0f:7fff:8fff

- 1:000::0FFf:6f0f:7fff:8fff

- 000:00:0::0000:0.0.0.0

## Invalid IPv6 Addresses

| IPv6 Address | Reason for Invalidity |
| --- | --- |
| 1:FFF::hjff:6fff:7fff:8fff | The characters "h" and "j" are no valid hexadecimal digits |
| 1:FFF:::ff:6fff:7fff:8fff | There can be only one zero compressor in an IPv6 address |
| 1:FFFFF::ff:6fff:7fff:8fff | The value of the second block is bigger than two bytes |
| 1:FFFFF: | A colon separator cannot be alone at the beginning or the end of an address |
| ::19216811 | The dot separators are missi

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Ethernet/ILAUTOSAREth] ILAUTOSAREth


# AUTOSAR Eth Interaction Layer (AUTOSAR/IP IL)

Ethernet » AUTOSAR Eth Interaction Layer

This interaction layer involves a simulation of AUTOSAR PDUs that are to be transmitted via Ethernet. For example, the IL implements the send behavior of the connections for this. This includes the collection mechanism of AUTOSAR PDUs, so that multiple PDUs are transmitted together in one Ethernet frame. In addition, the interaction layer supports the service discovery in order to activate the transmission of PDUs and to negotiate the connection data. The interaction layer you find under the file name CANoeILNL_AUTOSAR_ETH.dll in the Exec32 directory of your CANoe installation.

## AUTOSAR PDUs with COM Signals

For AUTOSAR PDUs that contain COM signals, an additional interaction layer must be added in order to define the bus-independent portion of the send behavior. This second interaction layer might implement the cyclic sending of a PDU, for example. Without an additional IL, these PDUs are sent only at a signal change. A possible interaction layer for the bus-independent send behavior is supplied in the Exec32 directory. The name of this additional interaction layer is AsrPDUIL.dll.

## AUTOSAR in Connection with SOME/IP Messages

SOME/IP messages can be simulated without AUTOSAR mechanisms. The SOME/IP Interaction Layer is supplied for this. On the other hand, the SOME/IP messages can be transported using AUTOSAR PDUs, which is supported by the AUTOSAR Eth interaction layer. The m

[...truncated for brevity...]

|  | Step by Step Procedure Open the configuration dialog for the node from the context menu. Switch to the Components page. Open the file selection dialog with [Add...]. Select the file AVB_IL.DLL in the exec32 directory of your CANoe installation. Close the dialog with [OK]. Open the TCP/IP configuration dialog for the node from the context menu. In the TCP/IP Stack Selection area, activate the Individual TCP/IP stack instance option. Configure a unique MAC address for each channel.Optionally configure individual VLAN settings for a Talker if the default VLAN ID and PCP should not be used. Close the dialog with [OK]. After these steps you can use the functionality of the AVB Interaction Layer with the CAPL API. |

### [Ethernet/ILSomeIP] ILSomeIPBOM


# SOME/IP IL: BOM

Ethernet » SOME/IP IL » BOM

The Byte Order Mark at the start of a data flow serves as an identifier for defining the byte order and coding form in UCS/Unicode character strings.

For SOME/IP UTF8 and UTF16 strings, the following possibilities are available for working with BOM.

## UTF8

SomeIPGetValueString() returns the string without BOM.

SomeIPSetValueString() adds the BOM if you have not already taken it into consideration so that the message can be encoded in a valid manner.

|  | Note With SomeIPSetValueString(), a Configuration String of a Service Discovery option without BOM is set. See also Syntax for Predefined SD Access. |

## Possible Error Sources

|  | Example The UTF8 string param is written in the database with a fixed length of 96 bits (12 bytes), "Hello World" is to be set. SomeIpSetValueString(messageHandle,"param","Hello World"); // Transfer 11 characters leads to Error 46-0503, because it implicitly SomeIpSetValueString(messageHandle,"param","ï»¿Hello World"); // Transfer 14 characters corresponds and this has a length of more than 12 characters. The valid characters are nevertheless stored in the parameter so that the message can be sent. If the stored value in the string is now queried with SomeIpGetValueString, the value “Hello Wo” is returned (11 characters + null terminator). Error Elimination It is valid to increase the maximum length to 15 bytes or to shorten the string to a maximum length of 8 bytes (+ 3 bytes BOM + 1 byte n

### [Ethernet/ILSomeIP] ILSomeIPConfigFromDataBase


# SOME/IP IL: Configuration from Database

Ethernet » SOME/IP Interaction Layer » Configuration from Database

You can configure a node in the simulation setup automatically from the database if:

- The node was defined in a FIBEX database (since version 4.1) or AUTOSAR database (since version 4.2)

- The database was assigned to the Ethernet network

- A SomeIP_IL.DLL was assigned to the node as a component in the simulation setup

For an automatically configured node, all database objects (e.g., Service Instances, Eventgroups, Methods, Events, or Fields) are created and managed by the SOME/IP Interaction Layer (SOME/IP IL). CAPL code is no longer needed for this. Only the send behavior, for example, the cycle time of a SOME/IP service event notification, must be specified in CAPL (see also SomeIpSetProperty).

## How to configure a node automatically from a database

Configure the node (step 2-6)

Start the measurement. If a receiver (real or simulated) exists for the setup node, the information required within the scope of the service discovery is now exchanged.

A few database objects can define CAPL callbacks in order to set or accept values of received or sent SOME/IP messages. If a node is configured automatically from the database, the values of these callbacks are set to 0. In this case, for example, the appropriate response to a received Method query is sent automatically. The content of the response is initialized with 0 by default and returns the value E_OK. You 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Ethernet/ILSomeIP] ILSomeIPConsumeService


# SOME/IP IL: Consume a Service

Ethernet » SOME/IP IL » Consume a Service

variables{ char gMulticastIPStr[16] = "239.192.255.251"; dword gMulticastIP; LONG gClientID = 3; dword kUDP = 17; dword gLastEventValueReceived;}

on start{ dword appEndpointHandle; dword serviceHandle; dword consumedEventgroupHandle; dword consumedEventHandle; gMulticastIP = IpGetAddressAsNumber(gMulticastIPStr); appEndpointHandle = SomeIpOpenLocalApplicationEndpoint(kUDP, 30490); //create a consumed service instance serviceHandle = SomeIpCreateConsumedServiceInstance(appEndpointHandle, 257 /*serviceId*/, 54 /*instanceId*/); consumedEventgroupHandle = SomeIpAddConsumedEventgroup(serviceHandle, 1/*eventgroupId*/); consumedEventHandle = SomeIpCreateEventConsumer(serviceHandle, 32779/*eventId*/, "OnEventReceived");}

void OnEventReceived(dword eventHandle, dword messageHandle){ dword tmpValue; //"signal" must be a valid path according to the FIBEX database that is configured for the current bus tmpValue = SomeIpGetValueLong(messageHandle, “signal”); if (SomeIpGetLastError()!=0) { gLastEventValueReceived = tmpValue; }}

# SOME/IP IL: Offer a Service

Ethernet » SOME/IP Interaction Layer » Offer a Service

You can use the SOME/IP Interaction Layer to create a simulation node that offers a Service. To do this, you must assign the SOME/IP Interaction Layer to this node. Then you can configure the service in the CAPL node of the network node.

variables{ char gMulticastIPStr[16] = "239.192.255.251"; dword gMulticastIP; dword gProvidedEventHandle; LONG gClientID = 2; dword kUDP = 17; Timer gTimer; dword gNumber;}

on start{ dword appEndpointHandle; dword serviceHandle; dword providedEventgroupHandle; gMulticastIP = IpGetAddressAsNumber(gMulticastIPStr); appEndpointHandle = SomeIpOpenLocalApplicationEndpoint(kUDP, 30490);

//create a provided service instance serviceHandle = SomeIpCreateProvidedServiceInstance(appEndpointHandle, 257 /*serviceId*/, 54 /*instanceId*/); SomeIpSetProperty(serviceHandle, "SDCyclicOfferDelay", 1000); //service is announced every second SomeIpSetProperty(serviceHandle, "SDTTL", 3); //service offer is valid 3 seconds

//creates a provided event group providedEventgroupHandle = SomeIpAddProvidedEventGroup(serviceHandle, 1 /*eventgroupId*/); SomeIpSetProperty(providedEventgroupHandle, "MulticastIp" , gMulticastIP); //events are sent per multicast SomeIpSetProperty(providedEventgroupHandle, "MulticastPort", 30490);

//create provided event gProvidedEventHandle = SomeIpAddEvent(serviceHandle, 32779/*eventId*/, "OnPrepareEvent"); SomeIpAddEventToEventgroup(providedEventgroupH

### [Ethernet/ILSomeIP] ILSomeIPServiceSignals


# Qualifying SOME/IP Service Signals

Ethernet » Qualifying SOME/IP Service Signals

|  | Note Device parameters of a SOME/IP ECU that are transmitted by means of event notifications or field notifications will be referred to hereinafter as Service Signals. Service Signals always have a simple data type. Structure elements (for example, the root node of a complex data structure) are not considered to be Service Signals. |

The signal values of simulated SOME/IP devices can be accessed using the basic functions (SomeIpGetValue... and SomeIpSetValue...) of the SOME/IP interaction layer.A much more convenient way is to access the signal values using the $ notification contained in CAPL.

## Service Signal

A Service Signal is always addressed by a qualifier. This qualifier is formed as follows:

{message qualifier} + {Signal path}

## Message qualifier

Addresses the SOME/IP message in which the Service Signal is contained.It comprises the following components, separated by the :: separator:

- Database Name

- Package Name Packages can, in turn, contain packages in the database. In this case, the names are also separated from each other by ::.

- Service Name

- Major Version Numeric value. If the Major Version is specified, the Instance ID must also be specified.

- Instance ID Numeric value. If the Instance ID is specified, it must always be prefixed by the Major Version.

- Parameter Name Name of the parameter for Events, or name of the Field.

{DB Name}::{Package Name}::{S

### [Ethernet/ILSomeIP] ILSomeIPSimulationModel


# SOME/IP IL: SOME/IP Simulation Models

Ethernet » SOME/IP IL » Integration of SOME/IP simulation models

Services offered or consumed by SOME/IP ECUs and the attributes of these services (IP address, Service Signals, etc.) can be described in a standardized form. The FIBEX and AUTOSAR formats are used for this. The ECUs described in these databases can be simulated using CANoe as follows

|  | Note CANoe allows the simulation of SOME/IP ECUs even without a database. The SOME/IP interaction layer provides a comprehensive scope of functions for this. |

- Create a new CANoe configuration. Select Create Configuration… on File ribbon tab, and select the SOME-IP.tcn template.

- 2. Assign a SOME/IP database to the Ethernet network. Select the Databases entry in the System View of the Simulation Setup with the right mouse button, and select the Add… context menu command.Then select the desired SOME/IP database. Note You can add multiple nodes to your configuration simultaneously using the Import Wizard…context menu command. The wizard guides you stepwise through the configuration process. Once the wizard has been completed, you still have to assign the SOME/IP interaction layer to the network nodes. Please observe Note 2 below for this.

- Add network nodes in the Simulation Setup. In the Simulation Setup, select the line symbol with the right mouse button. Select the Insert Network Node context menu command.

- Assign network nodes from the database. Open the Node Configuration

### [Ethernet/ILSomeIP] ILSomeIPUseValueAccessAPI


# SOME/IP IL: Value Access API

Ethernet » SOME/IP Interaction Layer » Use Functions of Value Access API

Based on the following example, it is explained how you can use the Value Access API to access values of a SOME/IP message in the CAPL program of a simulated node.

The example was composed in a non-standardized notation. To actually use it in CANoe, the example would have to be transferred to a FIBEX database, and this database is then assigned to the network to which the simulated node is connected.

|  | Example EVENT onValueChanged(ComplexDatatype newValue)STRUCTURE ComplexDatatype { enumeratedField ENUMERATION { off = 0, on = 1 }, utf8String UTF8String, arrayField[0..100] UNION { numberChoice INT32, floatChoice FLOAT64 }} |

## Derive the Designator of an Event or a Method Call

To use the Value Access API to access a value of an event or of a method call that is contained in a SOME/IP message, you must derive the associated designator from the FIBEX database. The rules are oriented towards commonly used programming languages such as C.

The following cases occur in relation to the presented example:

- The parameters of an event or method call have a designator. In the presented example, the designator newValue is valid for the event onValueChanged.

- If a designator belongs to a STRUCTURE, then you can access their fields by appending a dot to the designator for this STRUCTURE followed by the field name. Therefore, in the example shown, the designator newValue.en

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Ethernet/NetIsolator] NetIsolator


# NetIsolator

Ethernet » NetIsolator

You can use the NetIsolator to isolate a network for exclusive use of CANoe. Right click on the tray icon select the entry Isolate Adapter and choose the network device you want to isolate. A red dot at the icon signalizes one isolated device at least.

E.g. you can configure your network interfaces so that:

- all incoming traffic will only be received by CANoe, not by any other Windows applications or services

- packets are sent from CANoe on this channel and its components only

|  | Note If you do not want to block windows applications as your browser or explorer from accessing the network, do not select the corresponding adapter for isolation. Sharing the adapter does not affect the functionality of CANoe. |

Exclusive Use of an Ethernet Interface

### [Ethernet/PacketBuilder] EthernetPacketBuilderConfig


# Configuration

Ethernet » Ethernet Packet Builder » Window » Configuration

## Default Settings

In this dialog you can specify the default values that should be used if a new packet is created and added to the packet list:

## Default Source MAC Address and Default Destination MAC Address

Here you can select the default MAC addresses from the following entries:

- <Current channel>

The MAC-ID of the adapter is used that is assigned to the channel entered for the packet (e.g. adapter MAC-ID of channel Eth 1).

|  | Note If you select this setting consider that manually inserted data are overwritten in the section Raw Frame. Enter the MAC address as six two-digit hexadecimal numbers separated by colons, e.g. the broadcast MAC address FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF. |

- <Config manual>

You can enter any MAC address.

- <Broadcast>

The broadcast MAC address (FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF) is used.

- <Adapter Eth <x>>

The MAC-ID of the adapter is used that is configured to channel Eth x.

|  | Note If you select this setting consider that manually inserted data are overwritten in the section Raw Frame. |

## Default Source Port / Default Destination Port

Here you select the default settings for the used ports.

## Default Source IPv4 Address / Default Destination IPv4 Address

Here you select the default settings for the IPv4 addresses.

|  | Note Enter the IP address in dot-decimal notation as four numbers in the range 0..255 separated by dots. |

## Default Source IPv6 Address / Default Dest

### [Ethernet/PacketBuilder] EthernetPacketBuilderPagePacketinfo


# Packet Information

Ethernet » Ethernet Packet Builder » Window » Packet Information

| Packet List | Packet Information | Raw Frame |  |

Packets can be configured in this area. Dependent on the detected protocol the packet will be interpreted. The supported protocols are listed in the protocol feature matrix.

In this window the following information is displayed:

| Column | Description |
| --- | --- |
| 1 | Name of the field (e.g. Ether Type) |
| 2 | Configurable value of the field Different representations of the values are possible depending on the interpretation. A MAC address is display in MAC address representation, the IP addresses as well. For bit fields, the position within the bit field is highlighted. Otherwise the values are displayed in decimal or hexadecimal (0x) format. |
| 3 | Position of the value in the frame (e.g. [0/6]: starts at byte 0, length=6 Bytes) If there are e.g. 3 bit, this column shows the start byte [15/1], the bit length in display in the interpretation column. The position results from the field order. |
| 4 | Additional interpretation of the value if the protocol is supported The interpretation is automatically done based on the inserted data |

For an Ethernet packet you can change the values Source (Source MAC address ), Destination (Destination MAC address ) and Ether Type. To set the Source MAC address and Target MAC address you can also use the MAC-ID of an available adapter.

### [Ethernet/PacketBuilder] EthernetPacketBuilderPagePacketlist


# Packet List

Ethernet » Ethernet Packet Builder » Window » Packet List

| Packet List | Packet Information | Raw Frame |  |

In the packet list an overview of all packets and its information is listed. The following columns show you the packet information:

| Column | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Packet Description | Description of the packet, which can be changed manually |
| Source | Address of the sender |
| Destination | Address of the receiver |
| Protocol | Detected protocol of the packet |
| Packet length | Length of the packet |
| Payload length | Length of the payload |
| For network-based access only: |
| Send to Port | Port on which the packet is sent |
| Reverse Direction | No Send direction from the selected port to a connected segment. Yes Send direction from the selected port away from the connected segment, e.g. towards an externally connected ECU respectively network or towards CANoe. |

You can add new packets with the button [Add Packet]. With a click on the arrow you can insert a packet with the appropriate pre-configured protocol directly (e.g. a UDP datagram). With the button [Delete] you can remove packets from the list.

Within the list you can change the sequence of the packets to send via drag and drop. Existing packets you can duplicate with <Ctrl>+<C> and <Ctrl>+<V>.

You can also change the description and the packet length of a packet. Therefore you have to double-click with the left mouse button on the description in column Packet Descriptio

### [Ethernet/PacketBuilder] EthernetPacketBuilderPageRawFrame


# Raw Frame

Ethernet » Ethernet Packet Builder » Window » Raw Frame

| Packet List | Packet Information | Raw Frame |  |

In this area all data bytes of the complete packet are shown and interpreted as hexadecimal and ASCII value.

To change the data bytes click on the byte position you want to change, then insert the hexadecimal value you want to insert. It is also possible to insert the value as ASCII character. Therefore click in the ASCII column and enter your values. With <Tab> you can switch between the hexadecimal field and the ASCII field. The byte position of the cursor persists.

Changed values are highlighted in color. To commit the changed value press <Enter> or click in another window. To discard your input press <ESC>.

|  | Note Consider that manually inserted data are overwritten if you have selected <Actual channel> or <Adapter Eth<x>> for the MAC addresses in the configuration dialog. |

It can be selected how many bytes are displayed within one line (use the Bytes per line list). It is also possible to change the length of the packet (use edit field Packet length).

You can use comfortable the copy/paste mechanism too. Therefore copy a stream of numbers in the clipboard, set the cursor to the position you want to insert it and paste it with the keys <CTRL>+<V>. That allows to insert raw data of a packet from Trace Window or other applications in the Ethernet Packet Builder directly.

|  | Caution! If the length of the data packet exceeds the maximum lengt

## Enabling Encrypted Communication via TLS for the Diagnostic Tester

|  | Step by Step Procedure Optional: By default, the diagnostic windows like Diagnostic Console, Fault Memory Window and Diagnostic Session Control will use the Shared CANoe Stack for communication. Open the Security Configuration dialog and change to the Stacks page. At the stack over which the diagnostics tester should communicate, enable the Use Security check box and select the desired security profile for the TLS communication. Close the dialog clicking the OK button. Open the Diagnostic/ISO TP Configuration dialog. At the network where the diagnostics tester should communicate, add a diagnostic description. Make sure that in the Usage of the Diagnostic Description area, the Diagnostics tester check box is activated. Switch to the DoIP/HSFZ Settings page for the diagnostic description

### [Ethernet/Procedures] ProgrammingEthernetOverview


# CAPL Function Overview

Ethernet » CAPL Function Overview

## Link Layer

The CAPL functions of the link layer can be used for analysis, stimulation, test and fault injection. For the simulation, CAPL functions of the internet and transport layers or the application layers should be used in order to make the generation of CAPL programs easier.

## Analysis and Simulation/Stimulation

on ethernetPacket can be used to receive and evaluate individual Ethernet packets. Selectors allow the protocol fields to be read. The ethernetPacket object is used to generate Ethernet packets and output( ethernetPacket ) is used to send them.

To generate a bus load, ethStartPacketGenerator can be used.

If AUTOSAR I-PDU are transmitted via Ethernet, ethernetPacket::PDUCount and ethernetPacket::GetPDU can be used to read a PDU.

The handler on ethernetStatus can inform a CAPL program if an Ethernet link is established or removed. The Eth object or the Ethernet statistics system variables can be used to query the Ethernet link status at any time.

## Test

In test configurations/modules, testWaitForEthernetPacket can be used to wait for specific Ethernet packets.

## Internet and Transport Layer

Using the TCP/IP-CAPL functions, UDP and TCP sockets can be generated and it is possible to communicate with them. The simulated node or the test module is always an active terminal station. To establish a connection, the IP addresses and port numbers of the local and remote endpoints must be specifi

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Ethernet/Procedures] ProgrammingEthernetPacket


# Access on Ethernet Packets in CAPL and .NET

Ethernet » Application scenarios » Access on Ethernet Packets in CAPL and .NET

Table of Contents

| General |
| --- |
| Ethernet packets |
| Protocols |
| Initialization |
| Protocol fields |
| Access to raw data |

## General [▲ back]

Within CAPL and .NET programs, you can access the individual Ethernet packets and their protocol information.

You can e.g. analyze whether a certain protocol is available in a packet, which is the value of a certain protocol field or which raw data are included in the packet.

You can modify packets, for example, by changing the values of protocol fields, manipulating raw data, adding protocols or rebuilding the entire protocol stack of the packet.

For stimulation, you can generate completely new packets, fill them with the desired protocols and data and send them afterwards.

- Functional overview for Ethernet packets (CAPL)

- Functional overview for Ethernet packets (.NET)

## Ethernet Packets [▲ back]

Ethernet packets are available as data types in CAPL and .NET and allow you the access to different properties of Ethernet packets.

You can read and modify the MAC address of the receiver, the MAC address of the sender, the Ethertype, possibly available VLAN tags as well as the raw payload.

The access to individual or several bytes of the payload in an Ethernet packet is possible in

- CAPL with the methods byte(x), char(x), word(x), int(x), dword(x), long(x), qword(x) and int64(x)

- .NET

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Ethernet/Procedures] StartCreateConfig


# How to Create an Ethernet Configuration
