### [Ethernet/Procedures] UseCaseAnalyze


# How to Analyze an Ethernet Network

Ethernet » Use Cases » How to Analyze an Ethernet Network

You analyze the Ethernet traffic of an Ethernet network via the Measurement Setup, since all Ethernet packets received by the Ethernet interface are guided through the Measurement Setup. In the Measurement Setup, various analysis windows and filters for evaluation are available to you.

You can also evaluate a recorded logging file in the offline mode.

|  | Note You can test step-by-step instructions on this page with the sample configuration EthernetBasic.cfg. |

## Ethernet Network Interface

Compared to other bus systems, you must pay attention to how the Ethernet interface is connected to the Ethernet network. Since Ethernet switches are usually used, Ethernet traffic varies on each Ethernet link. Therefore, pay attention to how and where the Ethernet interface is connected to analyze the desired Ethernet traffic.

Options available for the Vector Ethernet interfaces are described on the page How to Configure a Vector Ethernet Interface

## Interpretation of the Ethernet Packets

In CANoe, the Ethernet packets are interpreted as much as possible. The nested protocol headers in the Ethernet packet are evaluated. The protocol fields can be evaluated in the detail view of the Trace Window or in CAPL. A large number of protocols listed in the Protocol Overview are supported.

When interpreting protocols, CANoe identifies various errors, for example wrong checksums or incorrect l

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Ethernet/Procedures] UseCaseCheckEthernetLinkState


# How to Check the Ethernet Link State

Ethernet » Use Cases » How to Check the Ethernet Link State

To enable the transmission of Ethernet packets, a link between two participants must be established.

|  | Note If no Ethernet link is established, check settings in the Vector Hardware Config dialog (Windows start menu path: Control Panel|Vector Hardware). The bit rate or master/slave may be set incorrectly. |

## Bus Statistics Window

In the Bus Statistics Window, the Ethernet link status is displayed in the Connection status line. If Link up is displayed, the Ethernet link has been established successfully, the Ethernet packets can be exchanged.

You can use the Analysis ribbon tab to open an existing Bus Statistics Window or enter a new one.

## Trace Window

The Trace Window displays an info event () for each change of the Ethernet link status. In the Protocol info column, Link up or Link down with the current bit rate is displayed. The detailed view can be used to display further information on the event.

## Statistics System Variables

A statistics system variable is available for each configured Ethernet channel. It can be found in the _Statistics namespace, for example _Statistics::Eth1::ConnectionStatus.

These system variables can be used in the Data Window and Graphics Window and in panels.

|  | Step by Step Procedure: Checking the link status On the Environment ribbon tab, press the button to open the Symbol Explorer. In the Symbol Explorer, select the view fo

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Ethernet/Procedures] UseCaseLogEthernetData


# How to Log Ethernet Traffic

Ethernet » Use Cases » How to Log Ethernet Traffic

Ethernet, as the other bus systems, is recorded with the Logging Block. For an Ethernet channel, the Ethernet packets, link status, and erroneous Ethernet packets are recorded.

Events generated by the CANoe from Ethernet packets are not recorded in a logging file. During replay, CANoe generates these events from the Ethernet packets again. This applies, for example to the AUTOSAR PDU and SOME/IP events.

Compared to Windows network adapters, Vector Ethernet interfaces record additional information, such as frame duration and frame checksum. Make sure that the Extended Ethernet Logging is activated (Options dialog).

|  | Note If the Extended Ethernet Logging is used, the Ethernet packets can no longer be replayed with CANoe 9.0 SP2 and older. |

The following logging formats are supported for Ethernet:

- BLF All Ethernet bus events and the events of other bus systems are recorded. If the Extended Ethernet Logging is activated, additional information, such as frame duration and frame checksum is also recorded.

- ASCII The same information as with BLF is recorded. With Ethernet, however, the ASC logging files are significantly larger than with BLF.

- MF4 (frame logging)

- PCAP Only Ethernet packet events are recorded, other bus systems are not recorded. Besides, no information on direction and channels is recorded with the Ethernet packets.

- PCAPNG Only Ethernet packet events are recorded

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Ethernet/Procedures] UseCaseSimulation


# How to Simulate an Ethernet Network

Ethernet » Use Cases » How to Simulate an Ethernet Network

CANoe allows you to simulate one or several Ethernet nodes. The simulated nodes can also use other bus systems or be created as gateway nodes.

Typically, there is a real ECU which requires one or several Ethernet remote peers to implement or test embedded software.

## Simulating Ethernet Nodes without Database

If there is no database, the simulated nodes can be created in CANoe. The IP of the node must be configured for the TCP/IP Stack. This includes the MAC address, IPv4 or IPv6 addresses, and possibly the VLANs.

The simulation behavior can be implemented in CAPL or C#. The TCP/IP API is used to establish communication via UDP or TCP.

The application data must be generated by means of programming in CAPL/C#.

|  | Step by Step Procedure Open the Simulation Setup via the Simulation ribbon tab by pressing the button. Insert a new network node via the context menu of the network trunk. Open the TCP/IP Stack dialog via the context menu command TCP/IP Configuration... of the newly inserted block.Here you can see the IP configurations of all simulated nodes. The current network node is preselected. On the VLAN Configuration page, use [Add...] to create one or several VLANs and assign VLAN IDs. In the following steps, IP can be configured for each VLAN. For each Ethernet network to which the network node is assigned, there is a page with the IP Configuration.Specify a unique MA

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Ethernet/Procedures] UseCaseSimulationECUExtract


# How to Simulate an Ethernet Network with AUTOSAR ECU Extract

Ethernet » Use Cases » How to Simulate an Ethernet Network with AUTOSAR ECU Extract

In an AUTOSAR ECU extract, an individual ECU is defined. Usually, the ECU should be implemented or tested for this ECU extract. For the remaining bus simulation, however, remote peers for the ECU are required; these stations are not included in the ECU extract.

To enable remaining bus simulation for CANoe with an AUTOSAR ECU extract, an additional database node is created during reading. This node has the name of the ECU with the addition _SimulatedPeerNodes.

If an ARXML contains only one ECU, this ECU is identified as an ECU extract. CANoe collects all connections defined without remote peer and assigns them to the _SimulatedPeerNodes database node. IP addresses which lie within the same subnet as the addresses of the actual ECU are assigned to this node. Services consumed by the ECU are provided by the _SimulatedPeerNodes database node. Conversely, services provided by the ECU are consumed by the _SimulatedPeerNodes database node.

|  | Note IP addresses selected by the _SimulatedPeerNodes database node do not correspond to the IP addresses in the real network. To enable remaining bus simulation with the network settings of the real network, a complete ARXML is required. |

The remaining bus simulation with an AUTOSAR ECU extract is created as with a complete ARXML, see How to Simulate an Ethernet Network.

See Also

| Versi

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Ethernet/Procedures] UseCaseStimulation


# Stimulate Ethernet Packets

Ethernet » Use Cases » Stimulate Ethernet Packets

To establish functioning communication with Ethernet, usually it is necessary to send several Ethernet packets or to establish a connection to the receiver. To send a UDP packet, it is necessary to query the MAC address of the receiver beforehand using an ARP request.

Therefore, a TCP/IP Stack is usually used with Ethernet. The application lets the TCP/IP Stack handle the lower protocol layers and also generate and send individual Ethernet packets. Only application data is transferred to the TCP/IP Stack or written into the socket and the TCP/IP Stack ensures that the data is transmitted to the receiver.

During the development of a ECU, it is often helpful to send individual Ethernet packets and selectively influence certain protocol fields, for example during debugging or to generate erroneous Ethernet packets.

## Sending an Individual Ethernet Packet with Ethernet Packet Builder

The Ethernet Packet Builder can be used to create individual Ethernet packets in a table view and edit all protocol fields of a packet. User data can be edited in a hex view. The Ethernet packets can then be sent at the push of a button.

|  | Step by Step Procedure Open the Simulation Setup via the Simulation ribbon tab by pressing the button. Insert a new Ethernet Packet Builder via the context menu of the network trunk.You can also insert an Ethernet Packet Builder in the System View via the context menu of the 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Ethernet/Procedures] UseCaseTestEthernetECU


# How to Test an Ethernet ECU

Ethernet » Use Cases » How to Test an Ethernet ECU

With Ethernet, the basic concepts for test sequences are supported in CANoe. In tests, it is possible to affect the Ethernet communication at various protocol levels, for example to stimulate protocol errors.

## Link Layer

As in network nodes, CAPL and C# APIs in test units or test modules can be used to send and receive individual Ethernet packets. The status of the Ethernet link can be affected with ethSetLinkStatus. The status of the Ethernet link and the statistical values can be queried via the Eth object.

The Vector network interfaces provide a stress function which can be used to cyclically send an Ethernet packet. The ethStartPacketGenerator CAPL function controls this packet generator.

In test sequences, the testWaitForEthernetPacket CAPL function can be used to wait for certain Ethernet packets.

## Internet and Transport Layer

The TCP/IP API in CAPL and C# can be used in test units or test modules as in network nodes. In this case, the tester is the terminal station of a connection.

To make a fault injection with the TCP/IP Stack, the CAPL callbacks OnIpSendPrepare and OnIpReceivePrepare can be implemented. This allows you to change the protocol fields of Ethernet packets which are sent or received by the TCP/IP Stack.

## Application Layer

To test AUTOSAR PDUs, the general PDU function can also be used for Ethernet.

See Also

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Ethernet/Procedures] UseWindowsNetworkAdapters


# How to use the Ethernet interface of your computer

Ethernet » Use Cases » How to use the Ethernet interface of your computer

You can use Ethernet network adapters provided by Windows as measurement interfaces. Restrictions apply for this kind of use because functionalities like the precision of timestamps may vary dependent on the hardware. If you require constant, reproducible measurement precision, use network interfaces provided by Vector.

The appropriate driver for accessing Windows network adapters in CANoe is not installed with the default CANoe setup program. However, it can be installed separately from the following location: <InstallationDirectory>Installer Additional Components\Vector Network Driver\NetworkDriver.msi

Click Start Setup to install the driver.

## Using Windows Network Adapters in CANoe

|  | Step by Step Procedure Open the Network Hardware Configuration dialog via Hardware ribbon tab|Bus Hardware. If you select an Ethernet channel, for example Eth 1, information on the selected channel will be displayed on the right side. Close the dialog by clicking [OK]. |

## Using Windows Network Adapter Exclusively for CANoe

You can configure the network adapter of your system such that it will be exclusively used for CANoe.Ethernet. In this way, you prevent Windows or other applications from using this interface and interfering with your embedded network. At the same time, your network cannot interfere with the Windows system.

You can use NetIsolator to

### [Ethernet/Protocols] Protocol


# Protocol Overview

Ethernet » Protocol Overview

CANoe supports a large number of protocols used in the automotive industry. For this reason, the detailed view of the Trace Window includes a more comprehensive interpretation of the protocol information of each Ethernet packet. Moreover, the programmatic access to the protocol information of Ethernet packets from CAPL and .NET nodes is possible.

Supported IP protocols:

| Application Layer - Automotive | AUTOSAR PDU Multiplexing, DoIP, HSFZ, PDU Marshalling, SOME/IP, XCP, PGT, UDP NM |
| --- | --- |
| Application Layer - General | DHCPv4, DHCPv6, HTTP, NTP/SNTP, TFTP, SNMP, RTP, RTCP, HDCP Authentication Protocol, MP2T |
| Session Layer | TLS, DTLS |
| Transport Layer | TCP, UDP |
| Internet Layer | ICMPv4, ICMPv6, IGMP, IPv4, IPv6, IPsec Authentication Header, IPsec Encapsulating Security Payload |
| Link Layer | ARP/RARP, Ethernet, NDP, SRP, MAC Control |

Supported AVB/TSN protocols:

| Application Layer |  |  | HDCP Authentication Protocol |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Transport Layer | gPTP, AVTP | MP2T |  |
|  | RTP, RTCP |  |
|  | TCP, UDP |  |
| Network Layer |  | IPv4, IPv6 |  |
| Link Layer | ARP/RARP, Ethernet, NDP, SRP, MAC Control |

Supported AVTP formats and protocols:

| Streams | 61883_IIDC, MMA_STREAM, AAF, CVF, TSCF, SVF, RVF, VSF_STREAM, EF_STREAM |
| --- | --- |
| Alternative | CRF, AEF_CONTINUOUS, NTSCF, ESCF, EECF, AEF_DISCRETE |
| Control | ADP, AECP, ACMP, MAAP, EF_CONTROL |

Feature Matrix | Bus S

### [Ethernet/Protocols] ProtocolAAFPCM


# AAF PCM

Ethernet » Protocol Overview » AAF PCM

| Quick Access |
| --- |
| Protocol Information |
| Protocol Dependencies |
| Header Format |

## Protocol Information [▲ back]

The AAF PCM stream data encapsulation is designed to provide a mechanism for transporting pulse code modulated (PCM) encoded audio data over an AVTP network that is optimized for minimum overhead and maximum utilization of stream reservations. This format allows single or multiple channel audio streams with a variety of sample rates and sample sizes. AAF PCM is defined in IEEE 1722.

## Protocol Dependencies [▲ back]

| Application layer | AAFPCM |
| --- | --- |
| Transport layer | AVTP |
| Internet layer |
| Link layer | Ethernet |

## Header Format [▲ back]

| Protocol Field | Offset[byte.bit] | Length[bit] | Programming |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Designator | ethernetPacket.<protocol>.Init | ethernetPacket.CompletePacket |
| Nominal Sample Rate | 17 | 4 | Nsr | 0 | — |
| The Nsr field describes the sample rate of the PCM audio stream. The field shall be set to one of the values in the following table. Value Description 016 User specified 116 8 kHz 216 16 kHz 316 32 kHz 416 44.1 kHz 516 48 kHz 616 88.2 kHz 716 96 kHz 816 176.4 kHz 916 192 kHz A16 24 kHz B to F16 Reserved | Value | Description | 016 | User specified | 116 | 8 kHz | 216 | 16 kHz | 316 | 32 kHz | 416 | 44.1 kHz | 516 | 48 kHz | 616 | 88.2 kHz | 716 | 96 kHz | 816 | 176.4 kHz | 916 | 192 kHz | A16 | 24 kHz | B to F16 | Reserved |

## Protocol Information [▲ back]

AES Encrypted Format (AEF) stream is an encapsulation that provides a mechanism for carrying a payload encrypted with AES-SIV cryptography as defined in RFC 5297a. The same encapsulation is used by both AEF_CONTINUOUS and AEF_DISCRETE variants.

| Application layer | AEF |
| --- | --- |
| Transport layer | AVTP |
| Internet layer |
| Link layer | Ethernet |

## Header Format [▲ back]

This encapsulation uses the AVTP Common Header. With its initialization in CAPL or .NET the AEF_CONTINUOUS variant is used (Subtype field set to 110). For switching to AEF_DISCRETE set the Subtype field to 238.

| Protocol Field | Offset[byte.bit] | Length[bit] | Programming |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Designator | ethernetPacket.<protocol>.Init | ethernetPacket.CompletePacket |
| Enc | 1.4 | 4 | Enc | 0 | — |
| This field specifies the encryption format. The value of this field is defined in the following table. Value Name Description 016 AES_SIV AES-SIV encryption 1 to F16 — Reserved | Value | Name | Description | 016 | AES_SIV | AES-SIV encryption | 1 to F16 | — | Reserved |
| Value | Name | Description |
| 016 | AES_SIV | AES-SIV encryption |
| 1 to F16 | — | Reserved |
| Reserved | 2 | 8 | — | 0 | — |
| This is a reserved field. |

### [Ethernet/Protocols] ProtocolARP


# ARP/RARP

Ethernet » Protocol Overview » ARP/RARP

| Quick Access |
| --- |
| Protocol Information |
| Protocol Dependencies |
| Header Format |
| Protocol Validation |
| Programming |

## Protocol Information [▲ back]

The Address Resolution Protocol (ARP) is a network protocol that allows the assignment of network addresses (IP address) to hardware addresses (MAC Media Access Control address). It is mostly used for IPv4 addressing in Ethernet networks. For IPv6 networks the NDP protocol is used.

RARP (Reverse Address Resolution Protocol) offers the way vice versa: the assignment of MAC addresses to IP addresses. This allows the detection of IP addresses for devices that have no permanent MAC address.

The packet format is identical for both protocols.

## Protocol Dependencies [▲ back]

| Link layer | ARP | RARP |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Ethernet |

## Header Format for ARP [▲ back]

| Protocol Field | Offset[byte.bit] | Length[bit] | Programming |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Designator | ethernetPacket.<protocol>.Init | ethernetPacket.CompletePacket |
| Hardware type | 0 | 16 | hwType | 1 | - |
| Defines the used transmission medium: 1 - Ethernet |
| Protocol type | 2 | 16 | protType | 0800h | - |
| Type field numbers to distinguish between the used network protocols 0800h – IPv4 |
| Hardware size | 4 | 8 | hwSize | 6 | - |
| Number of bytes for hardware address 6 - Ethernet |
| Protocol size | 5 | 8 | protSize | 4 | - |
| Number of address bytes that are defined by high

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Ethernet/Protocols] ProtocolAUTOSARPDU


# AUTOSAR PDU

Ethernet » AUTOSAR PDU Overview

AUTOSAR PDUs are transmitted via Ethernet using UDP or TCP connections. With UDP, a PDU can be simultaneously sent to several receivers with multicast or broadcast. With Unicast, a PDU may be transmitted via several UDP or TCP connections.

SOME/IP messages are a special type of AUTOSAR PDUs. They use an extended protocol header and can be transmitted together with standard AUTOSAR PDUs via the same connection.

SOME/IP Service Discovery (SOME/IP-SD) can be used to enable the provider and consumer to establish a connection. But it is also possible to specify the connection statically via the database.

## Configuration

The UDP or TCP port number must be configured to enable CANoe to evaluate AUTOSAR PDUs. On the Bus Systems page (Options dialog), these settings can be found in the Protocol Identification area. The UDP and TCP ports which are used to transmit AUTOSAR PDUs can be entered here for the AUTOSAR PDU multiplexing protocol.

If SOME/IP is simultaneously transmitted via the same port numbers, these ports must also be entered into the SOME/IP protocol.

If an ARXML database is used, the port numbers are configured automatically.

## Analysis

CANoe evaluates the UDP and TCP transmissions which are identified as an AUTOSAR PDU multiplexing. IP or TCP fragmentation allows a large AUTOSAR PDU to be split into several Ethernet packets. An Ethernet packet can contain several small AUTOSAR PDUs.

The AUTOSAR PDUs are extracte

[...truncated for brevity...]

# AUTOSAR PDU Multiplexing

Ethernet » Protocol Overview » AUTOSAR PDU Multiplexing

## Protocol Information [▲ back]

With AUTOSAR PDU Multiplexing (AUTOSAR PDU Header Option), AUTOSAR PDUs can be transmitted via Ethernet. During this process, several PDUs can be transmitted as payload in the same packet.

CANoe offers further possibilities to use AUTOSAR PDU Multiplexing in Configurations.

## Protocol Dependencies [▲ back]

| Application layer | AutosarPduMux |
| --- | --- |
| Transport layer | UDP | TCP |
| Internet layer | IPv4, IPv6 |
| Link layer | Ethernet |

## Header Format

| Protocol Field | Offset[byte.bit] | Length] | Programming |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Designator | ethernetPacket.<protocol>.Init | ethernetPacket.CompletePacket |
| ID | 0 | 32 | — | 0 | — |
| ID of the AUTOSAR PDU. The payload can be interpreted on the basis of the PDU definition in the database. |
| Length | 4 | 32 | — |  | — |
| Length of the AUTOSAR PDU payload in bytes. |
| Payload | 8 | var. | — | — | — |
| Contains the current payload of the AUTOSAR PDU. The interpretation is defined in a database. |

## Protocol Validation [▲ back]

Ethernet packets containing the AUTOSAR PDU Multiplexing protocol are analyzed and checked for consistency. Observed irregularities are displayed in the Protocol Validation column of the Trace Window.

| Protocol Validat

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Ethernet/Protocols] ProtocolAUTOSARUDPNM


# AUTOSAR UDP NM

Ethernet » AUTOSAR UDP NM Overview

The AUTOSAR UDP network management protocol is used to switch ECUs from operating mode to sleep mode.

A AUTOSAR PDU is cyclically sent with broadcast via UDP to keep ECUs in the operating mode.

## Configuration

The UDP port numbers must be configured to enable CANoe to evaluate AUTOSAR NM. On the Bus Systems page (Options dialog), these settings can be found in the Protocol Identification area. The UDP port number can be entered here at UDP NM.

## Analysis

CANoe evaluates the UDP transmissions and extracts the NM PDU. They are shown, for example in the Trace Window as its own PDU events.

## Simulation

The AUTOSAR UDP network management IL (AsrUdpNm.dll) is available for simulation. This interaction layer (IL) manages the NM state of a simulated ECU and sends the NM PDUs. It must be configured together with AUTOSAR Eth IL (CANoeILNL_AUTOSAR_Eth.dll) (see also How to Simulate an Ethernet Network).

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Ethernet/Protocols] ProtocolAVBRTP


# AVB/RTP

Ethernet » AVB/RTP Overview

AVB is used to transmit audio and video data streams via Ethernet. It defines a transport protocol which is based directly on Ethernet.

With RTP, audio and video data streams are also transmitted, however, data is transmitted via UDP and TCP using IPv4 or IPv6.

## Configuration

Ethertype must be configured, to enable CANoe to evaluate AVB. On the Bus Systems page (Options dialog), these settings can be found in the Protocol Identification area. The Ethertype for the AVTP protocol can be defined here.

For RTP, the UDP and TCP port numbers must be configured in this dialog. The port numbers must be entered for the RTP and RTCP protocols.

On the Ethernet page of the same dialog, the RTP payload types used can be configured if they deviate from the standard definitions.

## Analysis

With RTP, the data can be distributed to several Ethernet packets by means of the IP and TCP fragmentation. Thus, CANoe evaluates the transmissions and extracts RTP and RTCP events which are shown in the Trace as its own lines.

For panels, the Media Stream Control is available. It can be added to the panels in the Panel Designer to show video and output audio.

## Simulation

The AVB interaction layer can be integrated for simulation. It can output the AVB and RTP data streams as a talker or receive data streams as a receiver. Audio or video files can be used as a source.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Ethernet/Protocols] ProtocolAVTP


# AVTP

Ethernet » Protocol Overview » AVTP

| Quick Access |
| --- |
| Protocol Information |
| Protocol Dependencies |
| Header Format |

## Protocol Information [▲ back]

The network protocol AVTP (Audio Video Transport Protocol) will facilitate interoperability between stations that stream time-sensitive audio and/or video across LANs providing time synchronization and latency/bandwidth services. AVTP is defined in IEEE 1722.

## Protocol Dependencies [▲ back]

| Application layer | AVTP |
| --- | --- |
| Transport layer |
| Internet layer |
| Link layer | Ethernet |

## Header Format for AVTP [▲ back]

### Common Header Format for AVTP

| Protocol Field | Offset[byte.bit] | Length[bit] | Programming |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Designator | ethernetPacket.<protocol>.Init | ethernetPacket.CompletePacket |
| Control Data Indicator | 0 | 1 | controlDataIndicator | 0 | — |
| Indicates whether this is a control (value is 1) or stream data AVTPDU (value is 0). |
| Subtype | 0 | 8 | Subtype | 0 | — |
| Identifies the protocol being carried by AVTP. ## Protocol Information [▲ back]

The MMA format stream format (MMA_STREAM) is defined by the MMA. MMA stands for the Musical Instrument Digital Interface (MIDI) Manufacturers Association, which is the publisher and authoritative source of MIDI specifications. The MMA publishes the MMA Payload Format Specification for AVTP, which defines the transport of MMA defined data over AVTP.

### [Ethernet/Protocols] ProtocolCATPGT


# CAT PGT

Ethernet » Protocol Overview » CAT PGT

| Fast Access |
| --- |
| Protocol information |
| HTTP PDU |
| PGT PDU |
| XML validation |
| PGT signals |
| Detail view |

## Protocol information [▲ back]

Cat PGT is a J1939-oriented protocol over Ethernet that enables a client (consumer) to request and receive parameter values (signals) from a corresponding server (provider) over a network.

## HTTP PDU [▲ back]

The communication between the client and server is essentially based on the HTTP protocol, using embedded XML documents to some extent (XHTML). Since these documents can get very large, multiple packets (frames) are often necessary in order to transfer a complete document.

However, since further processing is only possible on complete documents, the individual frames must be collected and assembled. If a document (request/response etc.) has been received completely, then, in addition to the frame events, a so-called HTTP PDU event is also generated with the collected content and displayed in the Trace Window with a PDU symbol .

|  | Note The size of HTTP PDUs is limited to 64 kB, i.e. larger documents are truncated. In this case, the PDU appears in the Trace Window with a yellow exclamation point, and a corresponding warning is displayed in the detail view. |

## PGT PDU [▲ back]

Many protocols are identified on the basis of so-called well-known ports. This typically also applies to HTTP (ports 80 and 8080) and can be specified in the Options dialog.

Howev

[...truncated for brevity...]

| This field is a 1-bit field used by a CRF Talker to indicate that there has been a change in the source of the media clock that is providing the samples used in the CRF stream. |
| Reserved | 1.5 | 1 | — | 0 | — |
| This is a reserved field. |
| Frame Sync | 1.6 | 1 | Fs | 0 | — |
| This field enables a clock to also convey a frame-synchonized clock. Usage of this field is defined per type under the CRF Data field. |

### [Ethernet/Protocols] ProtocolDTLS


# DTLS

Ethernet » Protocol Overview » DTLS

| Quick Access |
| --- |
| Protocol Information |
| Protocol Dependencies |
| Header Format |
| Programming |

## Protocol Information [▲ back]

DTLS (Datagram Transport Layer Security) offers the encryption of connectionless communication (via UDP). It specifies the four different message types Handshake, Change Cipher Spec, Alert and Application Data.

On the internet and transport layer, DTLS API offers the possibility to build up and terminate encrypted connections with CAPL or .NET programs.

A variant for connection-oriented communication (via TCP) is offered by TLS.

## Protocol Dependencies [▲ back]

| Application layer | DTLS |
| --- | --- |
| Transport layer | UDP |
| Internet layer | IPv4 | IPv6 |
| Link layer | Ethernet |

## Header-Format [▲ back]

| Protocol Field | Offset[Byte.Bit] | Length[Bit] | Programming |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Designator | ethernetPacket.<protocol>.Init | ethernetPacket.CompletePacket |
| Content Type | 0 | 8 | — | 0x16 (Handshake) | — |
| Identifies the type of the next header. |
| Protocol Version | 1 | 16 | — | 0xFEFD (DTLS 1.2) | — |
| Version of the DTLS protocol. The two bytes indicates major and minor version where the ones complement is used. |
| Epoch | 3 | 16 | — | 0 | — |
| Counter that is incremented at every cipher change. |
| Sequence Number | 5 | 48 | — | 0 | — |
| The sequence number of the DTLS record. |
| Length | 11 | 16 | — | 0 | — |
| Length of the DTLS payload. |

### [Ethernet/Protocols] ProtocolESCF


# ECC Signed Control Format (ESCF)

Ethernet » Protocol Overview » ECC Signed Control Format (ESCF)

| Quick Access |
| --- |
| Protocol Information |
| Protocol Dependencies |
| Header Format |

## Protocol Information [▲ back]

The ECC Signed Control Format (ESCF) is used to transport an encapsulated AVTPDU that is cryptographically signed with an Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC) algorithm as defined in IEEE Std 1363a™. The signing of an AVTPDU allows a Listener to verify the sender of the AVTPDU and also allows the Listener to verify the integrity of the encapsulated AVTPDU.

## Protocol Dependencies [▲ back]

| Application layer | ESCF |
| --- | --- |
| Transport layer | AVTP |
| Internet layer |
| Link layer | Ethernet |

### [Ethernet/Protocols] ProtocolEthernet


# Ethernet

Ethernet » Protocol Overview » Ethernet

| Quick Access |
| --- |
| Protocol Information |
| Protocol Dependencies |
| Header Format |
| Programming |

## Protocol Information [▲ back]

The data of an Ethernet Frame (MAC Media Access Control-SDU Service Data Unit) can contain further protocols and the payload. The data must have a minimum size of 46 bytes and a maximum size of 1500 bytes. If less data are available a padding is necessary, more data have to be fragmented.

Following the payload the checksum (CRC) is sent.

|  | Note Preamble and Start Frame Delimiter are not part of an Ethernet packet in CANoe and are not evaluated. Only with using VN5610, VN5610A or VN5640 the Ethernet checksum is displayed in the Detail view of the Trace Window and packets with incorrect checksum are displayed. If the IP network driver is used, packets with incorrect checksum will not be interpreted by CANoe. |

|  | Note With Vector hardware, e.g. VN5610, Ethernet packets with a length of 14 to 1600 bytes (including the Ethernet header) can be sent, which are thus outside the minimum or maximum lengths of 60 to 1518 bytes. |

## Protocol Dependencies [▲ back]

| Transport layer | AVTP | TCP, UDP | — |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Internet layer | IPv4 | IPv6 |
| Link layer | Ethernet | ARP |

## Header Format [▲ back]

| Protocol Field | Offset[byte.bit] | Length[bit] | Programming |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Designator | Default Value |
| Destination | 0 | 48 | CAPL: destinat

### [Ethernet/Protocols] ProtocolgPTP


# gPTP

Ethernet » gPTP Overview

gPTP as per IEEE 802.1AS is the generalized form of the IP based PTP protocol (IEEE 1588) and is used to establish a common time synchronization plane for all participants (end stations, bridges) on a time sensitive network built upon the data link layer.

The time synchronization is the basis for application protocols in the AVB/TSN domain and essential for their use.

See also AVB/RTP

## Configuration

The Ethertype must be configured to enable CANoe to evaluate gPTP traffic. The Ethertype can be defined for the gPTP protocol in the Options dialog (Protocol identification area)

## Analysis

CANoe dynamically observes gPTP message traffic, checks inconsistencies of related communication between end station or bridge ports and indicates them via the Protocol Validation column of the Trace Window.

## Simulation

The AVB Interaction Layer can be integrated for simulation. As a pre-requisite for the simulation of stream-related traffic it establishes the time-synchronization plane by managing port roles and doing necessary protocol communication.

For example it can be statically configured to operate as master or slave port. Without any initial configuration it selects its port role automatically by executing the Best Master Clock Algorithm (BMCA).

During the measurement it is keeping the relation between the (un-altered) simulation time and the (synchronized) gPTP time in each simulated node. It provides a compact function set for transla

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Ethernet/Protocols] ProtocolHDCP


# High-Bandwidth Digital Content Protection (HDCP) Authentication Protocol

Ethernet » Protocol Overview » High-Bandwidth Digital Content Protection (HDCP) Authentication Protocol

| Quick Access |
| --- |
| Protocol Information |
| Protocol Dependencies |

## Protocol Information [▲ back]

The High-Bandwidth Digital Content Protection (HDCP) authentication protocol enables an exchange between a HDCP transmitter and a HDCP receiver affirming to the transmitter that the receiver is authorized to receive HDCP content.

Therefor it uses the following stages:

- Authentication and Key Exchange (AKE)

- Locality Check

- Session Key Exchange (SKE)

- Authentication with Repeaters

## Protocol Dependencies [▲ back]

| Application layer | hdcpAuth |
| --- | --- |
| Transport layer | TCP |
| Internet layer | IPv4 | IPv6 |
| Link layer | Ethernet |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |

The High Speed Fahrzeugzugang (HSFZ) protocol is a proprietary protocol for a diagnostic access to a vehicle via Ethernet. It is the predecessor of DoIP.

CANoe offers further possibilities to use HSFZ in Configurations.

## Protocol Dependencies [▲ back]

## Protocol Validation [▲ back]

Ethernet packets containing the HFZ protocol are analyzed and checked for consistency. Observed irregularities are displayed in the Protocol Validation column of the Trace Window.

| Protocol Validation ID | Message | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| [DoIP-001] | HSFZ payload length is bigger than available data | The payload length specified in the header (Payload Length field) is bigger than the present packet. |
| [DoIP-002] | HSFZ packet length is too small. Must be at least 6 bytes | The present packet is too short. A HSFZ packet must consist of at least 6 bytes to be able to contain the header completely. |
| [DoIP-005] | Resync necessary to interpret data! Some data was not interpreted due to a length error in the previous PDU | A length error in a previous PDU prevents the interpretation of further data. |

### [Ethernet/Protocols] ProtocolHTTP


# HTTP

Ethernet » Protocol Overview » HTTP

| Quick Access |
| --- |
| Protocol Information |
| Protocol Dependencies |
| Header Format |
| Protocol Validation |
| Programming |

## Protocol Information [▲ back]

The Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) is a stateless protocol that is used for data exchange. Client and server communicate with request and response messages. For this the transport protocol TCP is often used.

CANoe offers further possibilities to use HTTP in Configurations.

## Protocol Dependencies [▲ back]

| Application layer | HTTP |
| --- | --- |
| Transport layer | TCP |
| Internet layer | IPv4 | IPv6 |
| Link layer | Ethernet |

## Header Format [▲ back]

The header of an HTTP message consists of an ASCII character string. The individual parts of the header are separated from each other by a carriage return and a line feed symbol (<CR><LF>, 0x0D 0x0A), making the structure of the header dynamically expandable.

| Protocol Field | String Format |
| --- | --- |
| Start line | Indicates the message type. A Client request has the format of a Request line, a Server response has the format of a Status line. |
| Request line | <method><SPACE><request-target><SPACE><HTTP-version><CR><LF> Contains the requested method, call-target, and the used HTTP version. Some standard methods are: Value Meaning RFC GET Requests the content of a resource. RFC7231 HEAD Requests only the meta information of a resource. RFC7231 POST Requests the processing of the transmitted cont

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Ethernet/Protocols] ProtocolHTTPOverview


# HTTP

Ethernet » HTTP Overview

HTTP is used to request data from a web server by a client. Web sockets can be used to establish a connection between the client and the server, allowing both sides to exchange messages.

## Configuration

The TCP port numbers must be configured to enable CANoe to evaluate HTTP. On the Bus Systems page (Options dialog), these settings can be found in the Protocol Identification area. The TCP ports for HTTP can be entered here.

## Analysis

CANoe evaluates TCP transmissions which are identified as HTTP. TCP fragmentation allows the HTTP requests or responses to be split into several Ethernet packets. The HTTP requests or responses are extracted from the Ethernet packets and shown, for example in the Trace Window as its own HTTP or WebSocket events.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Ethernet/Protocols] ProtocolICMPv4


# ICMPv4

Ethernet » Protocol Overview » ICMPv4

| Quick Access |
| --- |
| Protocol Information |
| Protocol Dependencies |
| Header Format |
| Protocol Validation |
| Programming |

## Protocol Information [▲ back]

The Internet Control Message Protocol (ICMP) is part of an IP implementation and it is used for controlling tasks. Applications that use ICMP are e.g. Ping and Traceroute.

With Ping you can check the IP communication between two computers. An Echo Request message is sent to a node which responds with an Echo Reply message.

With Traceroute you can observe the way of an IP packet through all routers. For this datagrams with manipulated Hop Limit are sent and the Time Exceeded message as well as the Destination Unreachable messages are evaluated.

## Protocol Dependencies [▲ back]

| Internet layer | ICMPv4 |
| --- | --- |
| IPv4 |
| Link layer | Ethernet |

## Header Format [▲ back]

| Protocol Field | Offset[byte.bit] | Length[bit] | Programming |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Designator | ethernetPacket.<protocol>.Init | ethernetPacket.CompletePacket |
| Type | 0 | 8 | Type | 8 | — |

### [Ethernet/Protocols] ProtocolICMPv6


# ICMPv6

Ethernet » Protocol Overview » ICMPv6

| Quick Access |
| --- |
| Protocol Information |
| Protocol Dependencies |
| Header Format |
| Protocol Validation |
| Programming |

## Protocol Information [▲ back]

The Internet Control Message Protocol (ICMP) is part of an IP implementation and it is used for controlling tasks. Applications that use ICMP are e.g. Ping and Traceroute.

With Ping you can check the IP communication between two computers. An Echo Request message is sent to a node which responds with an Echo Reply message.

With Traceroute you can observe the way of an IP packet through all routers. For this datagrams with manipulated Hop Limit are sent and the Time Exceeded message as well as the Destination Unreachable messages are evaluated.

## Protocol Dependencies [▲ back]

| Internet layer | NDP |
| --- | --- |
| ICMPv6 |
| IPv6 |
| Link layer | Ethernet |

## Header Format [▲ back]

| Protocol Field | Offset[byte.bit] | Length[bit] | Programming |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Designator | ethernetPacket.<protocol>.Init | ethernetPacket.CompletePacket |
| Type | 0 | 8 | type | 128 | — |
| ICMP type Value Packet Type Designator Name RFC 1 DestinationUnreachable Destination Unreachable RFC4443 2 PacketTooBig Packet Too Big RFC4443 3 TimeExceeded Time Exceeded RFC4443 4 ParameterProblem Parameter Problem RFC4443 100 — Private experimentation RFC4443 101 — Private experimentation RFC4443 127 — Reserved for expansion RFC4443 128 EchoRequest Echo Request RFC44

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Ethernet/Protocols] ProtocolIGMP


# IGMP

Ethernet » Protocol Overview » IGMP

| Quick Access |
| --- |
| Protocol Information |
| Protocol Dependencies |
| Header Format |
| Protocol Validation |
| Programming |

## Protocol Information [▲ back]

The Internet Group Management Protocol (IGMP) is used by IPv4 systems to report their IP multicast group memberships to neighboring multicast routers. It is required to be implemented by all hosts wishing to receive IP multicasts.

CANoe supports all three versions of IGMP.

## Protocol Dependencies [▲ back]

| Internet layer | IGMP |
| --- | --- |
| IPv4 |
| Link layer | Ethernet |

Besides IPv6 the IPv4 protocol (Internet Protocol Version 4) is the lowermost protocol of the internet protocol family respectively the TCP/IP model that is independent of the transmitting medium. With Ethernet IPv4 uses the Ethertype 0800.

### [Ethernet/Protocols] ProtocolMAAP


# MAAP

Ethernet » Protocol Overview » MAAP

| Quick Access |
| --- |
| Protocol Information |
| Protocol Dependencies |
| Header Format |

## Protocol Information [▲ back]

Multicast MAC addresses or locally administered unicast addresses are required by AVTP for the transmission of media streams. Because AVTP runs directly on the transport layer, there is no existing protocol to allocate multicast MAC addresses dynamically. MAAP (MAC Address Acquisition Protocol) is designed to provide a way to dynamically allocate the multicast MAC addresses needed by AVTP.

## Protocol Dependencies [▲ back]

| Application layer | MAAP |
| --- | --- |
| Transport layer | AVTP |
| Internet layer |
| Link layer | Ethernet |

## Header Format [▲ back]

This encapsulation uses the AVTP Common Header with the Subtype field set to 4.

| Version | 2 | 5 | — | — | — |
| Version of MAAP being used |
| Data Length | 2.5 | 11 | — | — | — |
| Length (in bytes) of the MAAP data fields, shall be set to 16 |
| Stream ID | 4 | 64 | — | — | — |
| Stream ID associated with the MAAP PDU |
| Requested Start Address | 12 | 48 | — | — | — |
| First address of 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Ethernet/Protocols] ProtocolMACControl


# MAC Control

Ethernet » Protocol Overview » MAC Control

| Quick Access |
| --- |
| Protocol Information |
| Protocol Dependencies |
| Header Format |

## Protocol Information [▲ back]

The MAC Control (or Ethernet Flow Control) protocol is used to limit the data flow of an Ethernet connection to protect it against data loss. A congested terminal station can signalize the remote station by sending a pause frame that this one should not send further packets for a certain time period. MAC Control uses the Ethertype 8808h.

|  | Note The Pause Frame is sent to the reserved multicast MAC address 01:80:C2:00:00:01. |

## Protocol Dependencies [▲ back]

| Link layer | MAC Control |
| --- | --- |
| Ethernet |

## Header Format [▲ back]

| Protocol Field | Offset[Byte.Bit] | Length[Bit] | Programming |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Designator | ethernetPacket.<protocol>.Init | ethernetPacket.CompletePacket |
| Op. Code | 0 | 16 | — | — | — |
| Operation Code, e.g. Pause Frame (0x0001). |
| Quanta | 2 | 16 | — | — | — |
| Length of the Pause Frames, measured in units of 512 bit times. |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Ethernet/Protocols] ProtocolMP2T


# MPEG-2 Transport Stream (MP2T)

Ethernet » Protocol Overview » MPEG-2 Transport Stream (MP2T)

| Quick Access |
| --- |
| Protocol Information |
| Protocol Dependencies |

## Protocol Information [▲ back]

| MPEG-2 Transport Stream is a standard digital container format for transmission and storage of audio, video, and Program and System Information Protocol (PSIP) data. It is used in broadcast systems such as DVB, ATSC and IPTV. Transport stream specifies a container format encapsulating packetized elementary streams, with error correction and synchronization pattern features for maintaining transmission integrity when the communication channel carrying the stream is degraded. Transport streams differ from the similarly-named MPEG program stream in several important ways: program streams are designed for reasonably reliable media, such as discs (like DVDs), while transport streams are designed for less reliable transmission, namely terrestrial or satellite broadcast. Further, a transport stream may carry multiple programs. Transport stream is specified in MPEG-2 Part 1, Systems, formally known as ISO/IEC standard 13818-1 or ITU-T Rec. H.222.0. |
| --- |
| Information taken from Wikipedia, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/MPEG_transport_stream |

Using the internet media type video/MP2T the format is standardized via transport over RTP.

## Protocol Dependencies [▲ back]

| Application layer | MP2T |
| --- | --- |
| Transport layer | RTP TCP/UDP |
| Internet layer | IPv4 | IP

### [Ethernet/Protocols] ProtocolNDP


# NDP

Ethernet » Protocol Overview » NDP

| Quick Access |
| --- |
| Protocol Information |
| Protocol Dependencies |
| Header Format |
| Programming |

## Protocol Information [▲ back]

The Neighbor Discovery Protocol (NDP) (RFC 2461) replaces the ARP protocol on using IPv6. It is based on ICMPv6. The protocol is used for the following application areas:

- Router Discovery: identify the available router within the network

- Prefix Discovery: set the address prefixes for local and remote nodes

- Parameter Discovery: set several parameters, e.g. MTU Maximum Transfer Unit or Hop limit

For this, the following message types are available:

| Type | Name | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 133 | Router Solicitation | A host can request the router to send a Router Advertisement message. |
| 134 | Router Advertisement | With this message the router announce themselves in the network. The message is sent from the router cyclically or is requested with a Router Solicitation message. |
| 135 | Neighbor Solicitation | A node can request the network address of another node with this message, or check if a node is still available. It can be checked too if an address is already assigned. |
| 136 | Neighbor Advertisement | This message is the answer to a Neighbor Solicitation message. A node can send this message too to announce a network address change. |
| 137 | Redirect Message | This message is used by the router to show a host a better way to a destination node. |
| 141 | Inver

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Ethernet/Protocols] ProtocolNTP


# NTP/SNTP

Ethernet » Protocol Overview » NTP/SNTP

| Quick Access |
| --- |
| Protocol Information |
| Protocol Dependencies |
| Header Format |
| Protocol Validation |
| Programming |

## Protocol Information [▲ back]

The Network Time Protocol (NTP) is used to synchronize clocks within computer systems with packet-based communication networks. Normally the connectionless transport protocol UDP is used. This was designed to get reliable time value of networks with variable latency of the packets.

In general SNTP (Simple Network Time Protocol) and NTP are similar protocols, that are used for different application areas. A Whereas NTP servers or clients ensure a high accuracy and avoids high time steps, SNTP is only useful for simple application without big requirements to accuracy and reliability.

## Protocol Dependencies [▲ back]

| Application layer | NTP |
| --- | --- |
| Transport layer | UDP |
| Internet layer | IPv4, IPv6 |
| Link layer | Ethernet |

| Warning of an impending leap second to be inserted or deleted in the last minute of the current day 0 - no warning1 - last minute of the month has 61 seconds2 - last minute of the month has 59 seconds3 - alarm condition (clock not synchronized) |
| Version number | 0.2 | 3 | Ver

### [Ethernet/Protocols] ProtocolNTSCF


# Non-Time-Synchronous Control Format (NTSCF)

Ethernet » Protocol Overview » Non-Time-Synchronous Control Format (NTSCF)

| Quick Access |
| --- |
| Protocol Information |
| Protocol Dependencies |

## Protocol Information [▲ back]

The AVTP Control Format encapsulations provide a transport alternative for control data where the reliable, bounded-, and low-latency delivery capabilities of TSN are desired. The Non-Time-Synchronous Control Format (NTSCF) is useful for delivering messages as efficiently and quickly as possible and where synchronization of control is not required. An example is the delivery of a stream of control messages from an automotive CAN bus, across an Ethernet “backbone,” to a diagnostic tool with the lowest latency and bandwidth as possible.

## Protocol Dependencies [▲ back]

| Application layer | NTSCF |
| --- | --- |
| Transport layer | AVTP |
| Internet layer |
| Link layer | Ethernet |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |

| Protocol Information |

## Protocol Information [▲ back]

PDU Marshalling is a proprietary protocol for the transmission of multiple application PDUs within an Ethernet frame.

|  | Note Only the first two protocol fields are supported. Creating or reading out of a single PDU is not possible. |

| Application layer | PDUMarshalling |

| Total Header Length | 1 | 16 | length | 0 | — |
| Length of the protocol fields described here plus the length of all following PDUs. |

## Programming [▲ back]

|  | Note During initialization of the PDU Marshalling protocol, the Packet API also sets the fields SourcePort and DestinationPort in the UDP header to a PDU Marshalling port number which has been configured by the user. |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Ethernet/Protocols] ProtocolPTP


# (Generalized) Precision Time Protocol ((g)PTP)

Ethernet » Protocol Overview » (Generalized) Precision Time Protocol ((g)PTP)

| Quick Access |
| --- |
| Protocol Information |
| Protocol Dependencies |
| Header Format |
| Protocol Validation |

## Protocol Information [▲ back]

The network protocol PTP (Precision Time Protocol) is used to synchronize time-consuming applications, eg. audio or video. PTP allows an accuracy in the range of nanoseconds. PTP is defined in IEEE 1588 and in a generalized form in IEEE 802.1AS (gPTP).

## Protocol Dependencies [▲ back]

| Application layer | PTP |
| --- | --- |
| Transport layer |
| Internet layer |
| Link layer | Ethernet |

| This value is 1. |
| Message Type | 0.4 | 4 | msgType | 11 | — |
| Note Dependent on msgType there are further specific tokens. Value Packet type designator name 0 sync Sync 2 pdelayReq PdelayReq 3 pdelayResp PdelayResp 8 followUp FollowUp 10 pdelayRespFollowUp PdelayRespFollowUp 11 announce Announce 12 signaling Signaling Note If no packet type designator is used during initialization of the Packet API (EthInitPacket or EthInitProtocol), the message type 11 (Announce message) is used. |  | Note Dependent on msgType there are further specific tok

| The value of the length field is 12. |
| Organization ID | 48 | 24 | signalingOrgId | 0x0080C2 | — |
| The value of organization ID is 00-80-C2. |
| Organization Sub Type | 51 | 24 | signalingOrgSubtype | 2 | — |
| The value of organization sub type is 2. |
| Link Delay Interval | 54 | 8 | linkDelayInterval | 0 | — |
| The value is the logarithm to base 2 of the mean time interval, desired by the port that sends this TLV, between successive Pdelay_Req messages sent by the port at the other end of the link. |
| Time Sync Interval | 55 | 8 | timeSyncInterval | 0 | — |
| The value is the logarithm to base 2 of the mean time interval, desired by the port that sends this TLV, between successive time synchronization event messages sent by the port at the other end of the link. |

### [Ethernet/Protocols] ProtocolRTCP


# RTCP

Ethernet » Protocol Overview » RTCP

| Quick Access |
| --- |
| Protocol Information |
| Protocol Dependencies |
| Header Format |
| Programming |

## Protocol Information [▲ back]

The RTP Control Protocol (RTCP) is used to control a data stream transmitted via RTP. RFC3550 is used to specify the five packet types Sender Report (SR), Receiver Report (RR), Source Description (SDES), Goodbye (BYE) and Application-Defined (APP).

CANoe offers further possibilities to use RTCP in Configurations.

## Protocol Dependencies [▲ back]

| Application layer | RTCP |
| --- | --- |
| Transport layer | UDP |
| Internet layer | IPv4 | IPv6 |
| Link layer | Ethernet |

## Header Format [▲ back]

| Protocol Field | Offset[Byte.Bit] | Length[Bit] | Programming |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Designator | ethernetPacket.<protocol>.Init | ethernetPacket.CompletePacket |
| Version | 0 | 2 | — | 1 | — |
| Version of the RTP and RTCP protocol. |
| Padding | 0.2 | 1 | — | 0 | — |
| If this bit is set, the payload is filled with additional octets at the end. In this case the last octet defines how many octets (the last one inclusive) must be ignored. |
| Subtype | 0.3 | 5 | — | 0 | — |
| Contains information about the number of data blocks in the packet (Reception Report Count for SR / RR, Source Count for SDES / BYE) or application-specific data (APP). |
| Payload Type | 1 | 8 | — | 0 | — |
| Packet type. In RFC3550 the decimal values 200 – 204 are specified for SR, RR, SDES, BYE and APP. |


[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Ethernet/Protocols] ProtocolSNMP


# SNMP

Ethernet » Protocol Overview » SNMP

| Quick Access |
| --- |
| Protocol Information |
| Protocol Dependencies |
| Header Format |
| Protocol Validation |
| Programming |

## Protocol Information [▲ back]

The SNMP-protocol (Simple Network Management Protocol) is a simple and very common protocol of the application layer to configure and monitor managed devices on IP-networks and was originally specified in the RFC 1067.

The communication happens typically between so called agents (client-side) and managers (server-side).The SNMP-protocol exists mainly in 3 different versions, v1, v2c and v3; v2c is currently only partially and v3 not supported here.The communication is connectionless via so called „well known UDP ports“

- 161 to query agents.

- 162 to send notifications (so called traps).

## Protocol Dependencies [▲ back]

| Application layer | SNMP |
| --- | --- |
| Transport layer | UDP |
| Internet layer | IPv4, IPv6 |
| Link layer | Ethernet |

## Header Format [▲ back]

The SNMP packets are described by ASN.1 and the protocol fields are encoded in the Type-Length-Value format. The SNMP protocol specifies the following packet types: GET-REQUEST, GETNEXT-REQUEST, GETBULK, SET-REQUEST, GET-RESPONSE and TRAP.

The packets (besides GETBULK and TRAP messages) are built up using the following schema :

| SNMP Packet Header | PDU Header | PDU Body |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Version Nummer | Community Name | Paket Type (Get, GetNext, …) | RequestID | Error Status | Err

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Ethernet/Protocols] ProtocolSOMEIP


# SOME/IP

Ethernet » SOME/IP Overview

The Scalable service-oriented MiddlewarE over IP (SOME/IP) can be used to implement communication in service-oriented networks. It provides events, fields, and remote procedure calls which are summarized with service interfaces.

Service instances are offered by a service provider and used by a service consumer. SOME/IP Service Discovery is used to enable the provider and consumer to establish a connection.

The SOME/IP serialization supports basic and complex data types and arrays.

SOME/IP is transmitted via UDP or TCP connections. With UDP, a SOME/IP message can be simultaneously sent to several receivers with multicast or broadcast. With Unicast, a SOME/IP message may be transmitted via several UDP or TCP connections.

With AUTOSAR, a SOME/IP message is a special type of AUTOSAR PDU which in comparison with AUTOSAR PDU has an extended protocol header.

## Configuration

The UDP or TCP port number must be configured to enable CANoe to evaluate SOME/IP. On the Bus Systems page (Options dialog), these settings can be found in the Protocol Identification area. Here you can enter UDP and TCP ports which are used to transmit SOME/IP.

If AUTOSAR PDUs are simultaneously transmitted via the same port numbers, these ports must also be entered into the AUTOSAR PDU multiplexing protocol.

If an ARXML or Fibex database is used, the port numbers are configured automatically.

## Analysis

CANoe evaluates the UDP and TCP transmissions which are 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Ethernet/Protocols] ProtocolSOMEIPContent


# SOME/IP

Ethernet » Protocol overview » SOME/IP

| Quick Access |
| --- |
| Protocol Information |
| Protocol Dependencies |
| Header Format |
| Protocol Validation |
| Programming |

## Protocol Information [▲ back]

The protocol Scalable service-Oriented MiddlewarE over IP (SOME/IP) is a service-oriented Ethernet protocol from the automotive industry. This protocol allows the individual control units or software components to offer so-called services (service provider) or to make use of services from other control units (consumer). It is possible, for example, to call methods, read or write field values or to subscribe to certain events on the occurrence of which the subscribers are informed.

The protocol Service Discovery (SOME/IP-SD) which is specified within the SOME/IP protocol is used for the communication of the available services of a provider or for a required service of a consumer as well as for subscribing or unsubscribing from SOME/IP services.

With the Transport Protocol (SOME/IP-TP), SOME/IP offers the possibility of a segmentation of large messages via UDP.

The SOME/IP Interaction Layer allows a simplified access.

CANoe offers additional possibilities to use SOME/IP in configurations.

## Protocol Dependencies [▲ back]

| Application layer | SOME/IP-SD |
| --- | --- |
| SOME/IP |
| Transport layer | TCP, UDP |
| Internet layer | IPv4, IPv6 |
| Link layer | Ethernet |

|  | Note The payload of a SOME/IP message on UDP is limited to a length of 0-1400 byt

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Ethernet/Protocols] ProtocolSRP


# SRP

Ethernet » Protocol Overview » SRP

| Quick Access |
| --- |
| Protocol Information |
| Protocol Dependencies |

## Protocol Information [▲ back]

The Stream Reservation Protocol (SRP) utilizes three signaling protocols MMRP, MVRP and MSRP to establish stream reservations across a bridged network. SRP is defined in IEEE 802.1Qat.

The Multiple MAC Registration Protocol (MMRP) is used optionally inside SRP to control the transmission of talker registrations in a bridged network. MMRP uses the Ethertype 88F6h.

The Multiple VLAN Registration Protocol (MVRP) is used within SRP by terminal stations and bridges to specify the affiliation to the VLAN from which a data stream is sent. This allows the transmission of the Data Frame Priority of the sender (talker) to the receivers (listeners) in tagged frames. MVRP does not allow data streams between bridge ports which are participants of the not tagged set of the associated VLAN-ID. MVRP uses the Ethertype 88F5h.

The Multiple Stream Registration Protocol (MSRP) within SRP is a signal protocol which allows terminal stations to reserve network resources and, therefore, to guarantee the transmission and the receipt of data streams in a network with the desired quality of service. The terminal stations are referred to as talkers (devices which generate data streams) and listeners (devices which consume data streams). MSRP uses the Ethertype 22EAh.

## Protocol Dependencies [▲ back]

| Link layer | MMRP | MVRP | MSRP |
| --- | --

[...truncated for brevity...]

The SDI Video Format (SVF) is designated to carry direct mappings of the commonly used SDI (Serial Data Interface) data streams. It is a primary objective of the SVF to allow equipment originally designed to use one or more of these SMPTE SDI interface standards to migrate to, or additionally support, TSN-based transport of the SDI payload, with a minimum degree of added complexity or transcoding.

### [Ethernet/Protocols] ProtocolTCP


# TCP

Ethernet » Protocol Overview » TCP

| Quick Access |
| --- |
| Protocol Information |
| Protocol Dependencies |
| Header Format |
| Protocol Validation |
| Programming |

## Protocol Information [▲ back]

TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) is a connection-oriented protocol and establishes bidirectional connections. There are three phases:

- establish the connection

- transmit data

- close connection

For each connection a Transmission Control Block (TCB Transmission Control Block) is available to observe the current state of the TCP connection. This block is created during the establishment phase, modified during the data transmission and deleted if the connection is closed.

The complete transport address is built of IP address and port number.

## Protocol Dependencies [▲ back]

| Transport layer | TCP |
| --- | --- |
| Internet layer | IPv4 | IPv6 |
| Link layer | Ethernet |

| Source port number |
| Destination Port | 2 | 16 | Destination | 0 | — |
| Destination port number |
| Sequence Number | 4 | 32 | Sequence | 0 | — |
| Sequence number of the first data byte of the TCP packet. If the SYN flag is set, this corresponds to the initialization sequence number. |
| Acknowledge Number | 8 | 32 | AckNumber | 0 | — |
| If the A

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Ethernet/Protocols] ProtocolTFTP


# TFTP

Ethernet » Protocol Overview » TFTP

| Quick Access |
| --- |
| Protocol Information |
| Protocol Dependencies |
| Header Format |
| Protocol Validation |
| Programming |

## Protocol Information

The TFTP (Trivial File Transfer Protocol) is a simple UDP-based file transfer protocol which supports the reading and writing of files. It is described by the RFC 1350.

It provides some standardized extensions (options) such as the block size (RFC 2348), FileTransferSize (RFC 2349) or TimeoutInterval (RFC 2349), but also a simple possibility to define further options (RFC 2347).

TFTP is packet-oriented and thus connectionless. The initiation is normally carried out via the well-known port 69, the further transmission, however, is done via the so-called Transfer Identifiers (TID) which correspond to the UDP ports of the opposite side and range from 1024 to 65535.

The data format depends on the respective Opcode, see below.

## Protocol Dependencies [▲ back]

| Application layer | TFTP |
| --- | --- |
| Transport layer | UDP |
| Internet layer | IPv4, IPv6 |
| Link layer | Ethernet |

## Header Format [▲ back]

| Protocol Field | Offset [byte.bit] | Length[bit] | Programming |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Designator | ethernetPacket.<protocol>.Init | ethernetPacket.CompletePacket |
| Opcode | 0 | 16 | Opcode | 4 (ACK) | — |
| Type of the TFTP packet. Value Packet type RFC 1 Read request (RRQ) RFC1350 2 Write request (WRQ RFC1350 3 Data (DATA) RFC1350 4 Acknowledgement (ACK)

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Ethernet/Protocols] ProtocolTLS


# TLS

Ethernet » Protocol Overview » TLS

| Quick Access |
| --- |
| Protocol Information |
| Protocol Dependencies |
| Header Format |

## Protocol Information [▲ back]

TLS (Transport Layer Security) offers an encryption of connection-oriented communication (via TCP). It specifies the four different message types Handshake, Change Cipher Spec, Alert and Application Data.

On the internet and transport layer, TLS API offers the possibility to build up and terminate encrypted connections with CAPL or .NET programs.

A variant for connectionless communication (via UDP) is offered by DTLS.

## Protocol Dependencies [▲ back]

| Application layer | TLS |
| --- | --- |
| Transport layer | TCP |
| Internet layer | IPv4 | IPv6 |

| Content Type | 0 | 8 | — | 0x16 (Handshake) | — |
| Identifies the type of the next header. |
| Protocol Version | 1 | 16 | — | 0x0303 (TLS 1.2) | — |
| Version of the TLS/SSL protocol. The two bytes indicate the major and minor version, starting at 0x0301 (TLS 1.0). |
| Length | 3 | 16 | — | 0 | — |
| Length of the TLS payload. |

### [Ethernet/Protocols] ProtocolTSCF


# Time-Synchronous Control Format (TSCF)

Ethernet » Protocol Overview » Time-Synchronous Control Format (TSCF)

| Quick Access |
| --- |
| Protocol Information |

## Protocol Information [▲ back]

The AVTP Control Format encapsulations provide a transport alternative for control data where the reliable, bounded-, and low-latency delivery capabilities of TSN are desired. For the Time-Synchronous Control Format (TSCF) this enables synchronous delivery of control messages and is useful in applications where synchronous behavior is required. An example is an entertainment event where lights, sound, video, pyrotechnic, and other components of the event need synchronization.

## Protocol Information [▲ back]

UDP (User Data Protocol) is a network protocol with minimal protocol header. It is a connectionless protocol and doesn't use data segmentation, that means the packet size in CANoe is restricted to the maximum Ethernet size. With a checksum transmission errors can be identified. With UDP a service on the target system can be addressed with port numbers.

| Optional checksum, composed of information of the IP Internet Protocol header, the UDP header and

### [Ethernet/SignalProtocolDLL] ProprietaryIPProtocols


# Proprietary IP Protocols

Ethernet » Proprietary IP Protocols

CANoe supports a large number of IP protocols which can be interpreted up to the signal level and thus used, for example in panels and Graphics Windows. SOME/IP, AUTOSAR PDU, DoIP, XCP and other protocols are supported directly.

There are several ways to enable the use of proprietary or manufacturer-specific protocols in CANoe.

## CAPL with System Variables

System variables can consist of individual values or be defined as structures. In the system variable dialog, you can define structures which will be shown, for example in the Trace Window as messages.

For a proprietary IP protocol, the protocol messages can now be defined as system variables. In CAPL, you must then evaluate the Ethernet packets, for example in on ethernetPacket and set the values of the system variables.

Then it is possible to work in the Analysis Windows with the values of the system variables.

|  | Cross Reference The EthernetBasicProprietaryProtocol example shows how you can interpret a proprietary IP protocol by means of system variables. |

## Signal Protocol DLL

You can create a signal protocol DLL and use signals in CANoe in combination with a DBC.

The signal protocol DLL is used for evaluation of Ethernet packet signals in combination with a DBC. It identifies the data area within the Ethernet packet which contains signals and determines the message identifier. A signal protocol DLL must be created by the user.

|  | Note A 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Ethernet/SignalProtocolDLL] SignalProtocolBasicAPI


# Signal Protocol DLL: Basic API

Ethernet » Proprietary IP Protocols » Signal Protocol DLL: Basic API

This API must be used to describe the signals in the data area of an Ethernet packet in a DBC. The DLL must determine at which offset within the Ethernet packet the data area starts and which message identifier from the database must interpret this data.

The basic API for signal protocols consists of a VSPHandlePacket function which must be implemented into a DLL. This function is called for each received packet. In the function, the packet must be evaluated and the data area of the signals must be determined. In addition, a message identifier must be generated. This message identifier is used to search for the corresponding message in the database (DBC).

## Restrictions

- The signals can only be defined using options provided by the DBC, for example fixed signal length and offset.

- Only one DBC message per Ethernet packet can be defined.

- The Ethernet packets may not be fragmented.

The transferred function pointer can be used to transfer the evaluated information (message identifier, offset, and length) to CANoe.

|  | Cross Reference An sample configuration with a signal protocol DLL and a C++ project is available in the Vector KnowledgeBase https://kb.vector.com. |

|  | Example VSPDEF(long) VSPHandlePacket(unsigned long long timestamp, // timestamp in [ns]unsigned short channel, // channel 1..nunsigned char destinationMacAddress[6], // destination MAC addressun

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Ethernet/SignalProtocolDLL] SignalProtocolExtendedAPI


# Signal Protocol: Extended API

Ethernet » Proprietary IP Protocols » Signal Protocol: Extended API

A signal protocol DLL which implements the extended API can be used to interpret proprietary IP protocols.

## Characteristics

- The messages and the signal must be described in one DBC. The signal protocol DLL determines the data area and the message identifier which can be used by the DBC to interpret the signals.

- The Protocol info trace column can be filled

- The Protocol trace column can display the designation of the IP protocol

- In the detail view of the Trace Window, the interpretation of an Ethernet packet can be supplemented.

## Restrictions

- The signals can only be defined using options provided by the DBC, for example fixed signal length and offset.

- Only one DBC message per Ethernet packet can be defined.

- The Ethernet packets may not be fragmented.

The sample project from the Vector KnowledgeBase https://kb.vector.com contains a C++ header file in which some interfaces are defined.

As an entry point, the DLL must implement the following functions:

STDAPI DllRegisterAddOn( NIPB::IAddOnRegistrar* registrar )STDAPI DllGetApiVersion( unsigned long /*out*/*major, unsigned long /*out*/*minor, unsigned long /*out*/*structVersion )

CANoe uses DllGetApiVersion to check whether the DLL is supported. Then DllRegisterAddOn transfers to the DLL an interface which can be used to register the protocols.

The following call registers a protocol with the vc-sig

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Ethernet/TCPIPNetworkSettings] PageAdvancedSettings


# Advanced Settings

Ethernet » TCP/IP Stack » Advanced Settings

Ribbon: Simulation Tab|TCP/IP Stack|Advanced Settings

| TCP/IP Stack | Advanced Settings | VLAN Configuration | Network Settings |  |

On this page the Tcp Delayed Acknowledge can be activated/deactivated.

This page is available for

- CANoe TCP/IP Stack with activated Use CANoe internal TCP/IP stack option

- Network nodes with activated Individual TCP/IP stack instance option

## TCP Settings

If this option is activated the TCP/IP Stack tries to send the ACK flag together with a possibly sent response. This should avoid that an acknowledgement is sent additionally to the response.With this behavior the amount of small packets in the network should be reduced.

You can find a detailed description of this behavior in RFC 1122, chapter 4.2.3.2 When to Send an ACK Segment.

The delayed acknowledge is activated by default.

## Routing

|  | Note This option is available if the following preconditions are complied with: The configuration contains at least two networks or a VLAN. At CANoe TCP/IP-Stack|TCP/IP Stack Selection you have selected option Use CANoe internal TCP/IP stack. At <node>|TCP/IP Stack Selection you have selected Individual TCP/IP stack instance and the node is assigned to two networks at least. |

If you activate option Enable routing the TCP/IP stack routes packets from one sub net to the other ones. Each assigned bus must be configured as a proprietary sub net.

| Version 15© Vector Informat

[...truncated for brevity...]

Ethernet » TCP/IP Stack » <network name> (Eth<channel number>)

| TCP/IP Stack | Advanced Settings | VLAN Configuration | Network Settings |  |

## Ethernet Settings

Enter the MAC address that is used as sender address for Ethernet packets sent from this network node. Consider that this MAC address can be used only once in the network.

|  | Note If a new network node is inserted a generated MAC address is assigned. Here it is ensured that this MAC address is not assigned in the simulated network yet. If the MAC address conflicts with the MAC-ID of a connected hardware, you have to change the MAC address. If a node from a FIBEX file is selected for the network node and the MAC address is defined there, the MAC address cannot be changed. |

- Use MAC-ID of Ethernet adapter Note This option is available only on selection of the CANoe network stack. If you use this option you don't need to configure the MAC address manually, it is allocated from the network adapter.

- MAC address Here you can manually enter the MAC address.

- IPv4 Enabled Here you enable IPv4 on the TCP/IP stack. This setting is enabled b

### [Ethernet/TCPIPNetworkSettings] PageStackSelection


# Stack Selection

Ethernet » TCP/IP Stack » Stack Selection

Ribbon: Simulation Tab|TCP/IP Stack|Stack Selection

| TCP/IP Stack | Advanced Settings | VLAN Configuration | Network Settings |  |

Several components use IP communication, e.g. DoIP, XCP and TCP/IP in CAPL. On this page you can configure the basic settings for the IP communication.

This page is available for

- CANoe TCP/IP Stack

- Network nodes

|  | Note You can change the settings only if at least one Ethernet channel is configured in the current configuration. If no Ethernet channel is configured the settings are deactivated. |

## TCP/IP Stack Selection

In this section you can select which TCP/IP stack CANoe or a network node uses for communication. You can select of the following options:

- Individual TCP/IP stack (only available for network nodes)

A proprietary TCP/IP stack is established for the network node. This stack is used for the IP communication of the TCP/IP API and DoIP functions in CAPL. The network node then communicates with an independent TCP/IP stack with its own IP and MAC address. You have to do these settings in the Ethernet settings for each assigned channel.

|  | Note Select an individual TCP/IP stack instance for the node if this node should represent a discrete node with its own TCP/IP stack in the network. However it is not possible to directly connect to the localhost of your computer. If a node from a FIBEX file is selected for the network node, the use of the individual TC

[...truncated for brevity...]

# VLAN Configuration

Ethernet » TCP/IP Stack » VLAN Configuration

Ribbon: Simulation Tab|TCP/IP Stack|VLAN Configuration

| TCP/IP Stack | Advanced Settings | VLAN Configuration | Network Settings |  |

On this page VLANs of the network notes are manages. The VLANs are sorted by channel and listed in the table with its ID and priority.

This page is available for

- CANoe TCP/IP Stack with activated Use CANoe internal TCP/IP stack option

- Network nodes with activated Individual TCP/IP stack instance option

## VLAN

| Column | Description |
| --- | --- |
| VLAN Name | Name of the VLAN, is automatically generated from channel number and VLAN ID |
| VLAN ID | ID of the VLAN, valid values: 1..4094 Note The ID can only used once per channel. |  | Note The ID can only used once per channel. |
|  | Note The ID can only used once per channel. |
| VLAN Priority | Priority of the VLAN, valid values: 0..7 |

Use [Add] to add a new VLAN and [Remove] to remove an existing VLAN.

The configuration pages for the IP addresses of the VLAN are shown below the appropriate channel. On these pages you can configure the IPv4 and IPv6 addresses.

|  | Note The configuration pages are shown only after the TCP/IP Stack dialog is reopened. If a node from a FIBEX file is selected for the network node, the VLAN settings of the associated network cannot be changed. |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Ethernet/TCPIPNetworkSettings] TCPIPConfigurationConflict


# TCP/IP Configuration Conflict

Ethernet » TCP/IP Configuration Conflict

Before every measurement start the current configuration is checked for IP and MAC address duplicates. The check refers to

- node addresses

- VLAN addresses that are assigned to the node

If one or several nodes use the TCP/IP stack of the operating system all addresses of the Windows stack are also checked.

If the check detects a conflict the TCP/IP Configuration Conflict dialog will be opened automatically.

| Column | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Status | Symbol indicates a current conflict. |
| Node | Affected node |
| Active | Here you can deactivate the affected node. |
| Conflicted IP Addresses | Display of the IP address that triggers the conflict. |
| Conflicted MAC Addresses | Display of the MAC address that triggers the conflict. |

With [OK] the measurement will be started and the conflicts will be ignored.

With [Cancel] the measurement start will be canceled.

With Don't show this conflict again this dialog will no longer be displayed at measurement start until the status of the displayed conflict will be changed.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |

For each network stack you can configure the following settings:
