### [FunctionBlocks/Trigger] TriggerModeEntire


# Trigger Mode: Entire Measurement

Trigger Configuration » Trigger Mode Entire Measurement

If you select this mode the entire measurement will be logged.

Therefore you cannot select any supplemental trigger conditions nor a time window.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [FunctionBlocks/Trigger] TriggerModeSingle


# Trigger Mode: Single Trigger

Trigger Configuration » Trigger Mode Single Trigger

In the Single Trigger trigger mode all those data occurring before and after a specific trigger is logged.

You can enter the necessary settings for pre-trigger and post-trigger times in the Time section, and the number of triggers you wish to log in the Advanced Options section.

|  | Example A fault keeps recurring in a CAN bus system and Error Frames are transmitted. To permit better analysis of what occurs in the time period before the CAN Error Frame you might set up the following configuration: Trigger mode: Single Trigger Trigger condition: User-defined Define a set of user-defined conditions that has only one predefined bus event: a CAN Error Frame(Predefined Events|CAN|Events|Error Frame). Time window – Pre-trigger time: 1000 ms No post-trigger time Measurement stop after: 1 trigger Buffer size: 10000 messages This is a way to narrow the area in which you need to troubleshoot. |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [FunctionBlocks/Trigger] TriggerModeTest


# Trigger Mode: Test Trigger

Trigger Configuration » Trigger Mode Test Trigger

In Test Trigger mode, you can here configure the time window for the logging of a execution period of a test element from the Test Feature Set. A test element can be a test configuration, a test unit, a test implementation entity, or a test case.

|  | Note The Test Trigger mode is only effective, when using test units (from vTESTstudio). For test modules, the Test Trigger mode is not applicable. |

The beginning of the corresponding test element triggers the start of logging and the end of the same test element triggers the stop of logging. The pre-trigger time refers to the start trigger and the post-trigger time to the stop trigger. If multiple test elements are executed at the same time, the trigger remains active until all test elements are finished, including their pre- and post-trigger times.

In addition, you can optionally specify a Verdict condition. In this way, a logging file will only be stored, if the verdict of the corresponding test element is equal to the specified one; otherwise, the logging file is deleted when the trigger is completed. If multiple verdicts are selected, the conditions are connected with a disjunction (OR). If multiple test elements are logged into a single file, the file is stored if at least one test element has one of the specified verdicts.

|  | Example You have two test configurations named Powertrain and Comfort and you want to log all events during bot

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [FunctionBlocks/Trigger] TriggerModeToggle


# Trigger Mode: Toggle Trigger

Trigger Configuration » Trigger Mode Toggle Trigger

In the Toggle Trigger trigger mode the time window is described by two sequential triggers (block start and block end triggers). The first trigger initiated during the measurement is a block start trigger, and the second is a block end trigger. There follows another block start trigger, etc. In the Toggle Trigger trigger mode the pre-trigger time refers to a block start trigger, and the post-trigger time to a block end trigger.

With Use combined toggle mode you can specify the same condition for block start and block end triggers.

You can enter the necessary settings for pre-trigger and post-trigger times in the Time section, and the number of triggers you wish to log in the Advanced Options section.

|  | Example Logging a time period between the two messages (or signals) Engine On and Engine Off To do this you have to select bus event and formulate a suitable Set of event based conditions… for the two signals. If you also wish to log the time period 1s before Engine On, you would enter 1000 ms in the Pre-trigger input box. |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [FunctionBlocks/Trigger] TriggerNoteDataLoss


# Data Loss During the Measurement

Trigger Block/Logging Block » Data Loss During Measurement

Please observe the following points so that no data losses occur during high system loading:

- Close applications that are running in background and place demands on system time.

- Select cyclic update for blocks in the Measurement Setup, or disable them entirely.

- Utilize filters to reduce data quantities.

- Avoid performing any unnecessary user actions during the measurement(e.g. don't move any windows, etc).

If a data loss occurs in spite of these measures, you have the program inform you of this by a message.

To do this, activate the Data lost message box check box in the Logging dialog.

After the end of measurement a dialog is then displayed. Lines in the logging file marked with a * as a special symbol have corrupted lines around them.

The symbol for the data loss disappears when the overload situation has ended.

Behavior Under High Load Conditions | Behavior Under Overload Conditions

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [FunctionBlocks/Trigger] TriggerNoteMessage


# Notes on Entering a Message ID

Trigger Configuration » Set of User-defined Conditions » Raw Message Condition » Message ID

ID specifies the identifier of a message.Maximum and minimum values are depending on the chosen bus system.The expected format of the number depends on the current global numbering format.

- You can explicitly specify a hexadecimal format by starting the number with the prefix 0x.

- In order to specify an extended identifier (CAN only) use a suffix x.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [FunctionBlocks/Trigger] TriggerNoteTime


# Notes on the Time Window

Trigger Configuration » Notes on the Time Window

- If you select the trigger condition Start and the Infinite post-trigger time check box, all data are logged from the beginning to the end of the measurement.This corresponds to the Entire Measurement trigger mode.

- If the trigger condition occurs so early that the pretrigger time cannot be satisfied since the measurement start, only the data that have occurred since the start can be saved.The pretrigger time must lie in the range of 0 to approx. 6 h.

- The post-trigger time defines how long data are still acquired and saved after the trigger condition occurs. If you activate the Infinite post-trigger check box, the data are logged until the measurement is stopped or until no more hard disk memory is available for the log file.

- Since the ring buffer has a limited size, a situation may arise where data are lost if the pretrigger and/or post-trigger times are too long. Then only the last data are logged until the post-trigger time elapses, and an error message is output.

Data Loss During the Measurement

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [FunctionBlocks/Trigger] TriggerTypeMessage


# Trigger condition: User-defined

Trigger Configuration » Trigger Condition User-defined

With the user-defined trigger condition triggering occurs when a configured set of user-defined conditions is satisfied. The set may include combinations of conditions which are based on a wide range of data (e.g. bus messages and their attributes, statistic events, values of environment variables, …)

To define these attributes press the [Define…] button.

The configured pre-trigger and post-trigger times define the logging time period for this trigger.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [FunctionBlocks/Trigger] TriggerTypeStart


# Trigger Condition: Start

Trigger Configuration » Trigger Condition Start

With the Start trigger condition, triggering - and thereby data logging - occurs immediately at the start of the measurement.

The entry of a pretrigger time is meaningless here and should be set to 0. The post-trigger time defines the duration of logging.

If you select the Start trigger condition and the Infinite post-trigger time check box, all data are logged from the beginning to the end of the measurement. This is equivalent to the Entire measurement trigger mode.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [FunctionBlocks/Trigger] TriggerTypeStop


# Trigger Condition: Stop

Trigger Configuration » Trigger Condition Stop

With the Stop trigger condition, triggering occurs at the end of the measurement.

There are two regular ways to stop a measurement:

- Manually By the relevant input from the user

- Controlled By a suitable CAPL program

The pretrigger time defines the logging time period. Entry of a post-trigger time is meaningless in this case, and it should be 0.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [FunctionBlocks/Trigger] TriggerUserDefined


# Set of user-defined conditions

Trigger Configuration » Set of User-defined Conditions

Here you can input the set of conditions used to search or trigger. This set can also be used as a global breakpoint in offline mode.

The set is organized in a tree form. Its node items are Groups and leaf items are Conditions.

- Groups are represented by their logical operators (AND/OR)

- Conditions are represented by a short textual description of the configured data.

The root of the tree is a predefined Group that cannot be removed.

## Groups

Each group specifies a logical operator, which is applied to its immediate children items. The available operators are AND and OR.

|  | Note The whole set complies with the requirements when its root group is satisfied. Only enabled groups and conditions participate in the evaluation of the set. The evaluation of a single condition depends on its classification (see discrete vs. continuous user-defined conditions). You can visually distinguish between the condition’s classifications according to their icons. Group with the logical operator OR is satisfied if at least one of its child conditions or groups complies with the requirements. Group with the logical operator AND is satisfied if all of its child conditions and groups comply with the requirements. It could never comply with the requirement if there are at least two user-defined conditions, which never can be fulfilled on one run-time event. Usually, such conditions are Discrete and

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [FunctionBlocks/Trigger] TriggerUserDefinedAttributes


# Attributes of User-defined Trigger Conditions

Trigger Configuration » Set of User-defined Conditions » Attributes of User-defined Trigger Conditions

The attributes of a user-defined condition depend on the bus system of the selected message or bus event.

## Common attributes

- Rx = receive

- Tx = transmit (send)

- TXRQ = transmission request (send request)

## Bus system specific attributes

### CAN

- Data = Data message

- Remote = Remote message

### J1939

- Source = Source address of the parameter group (PG)

- Destination = Destination address of the parameter group

- Priority = Priority of the parameter group

### MOST

- Function block = ID of the function block

- Function = ID of the function

- Operation type = Operation type of the message

- Source = Source address

- Destination = Destination address

- Instance ID

|  | Note Only messages of type normal are supported. Use a CAPL program or a MOST Filter to configure other types of messages. |

### AFDX

- Ethertype (only in dialog for raw frames): Here you can set the Ethernet Type of a message if only messages of a specific Ethernet Type should be triggered.

- ICMP (only in dialog for raw frames): Only ICMP messages are triggered.

- Non UDP (only in dialog for raw frames): Only non UDP messages are triggered.

- Rx: Only received messages are triggered.

- Tx: Only sent messages are triggered.

- Error Flags: Only messages with specific error properties are triggered. If no error flag is active onl

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [FunctionBlocks/Trigger] TriggerUserDefinedDiscreteContinuous


# Discrete vs. Continuous Conditions

Trigger Configuration » Set of User-defined Conditions » Discrete vs. Continuous Conditions

Each user-defined condition allows specifying

- a required run-time event

- its timing (optional) and

- its required data (if applicable).

We distinguish between discrete and continuous conditions according to the process of their evaluation.

Note, that user-defined condition passes evaluation on every incoming run-time event.

## Discrete condition

Discrete condition is reset before each new evaluation and becomes satisfied if the run-time event is the required run-time event with the required data (if applicable) and with the required timing (if configured).

The following types of conditions are discrete:

| Condition | Note |
| --- | --- |
| Symbolic Message Predefined Bus Event Raw Message | As long as there are no settings for Delay… or Timeout after…(see set of user-defined conditions: Timing) |
| Symbolic Signal Environment Variable Bus Statistics Signal | As long as there are no settings for Delay… or Timeout after…(see set of user-defined conditions: Timing) Mode of the required value(see set of user-defined conditions: Mode of the required value) Absolute with Discrete flag Consecutive Delta Change from … to … |

## Continuous condition

A Continuous condition is not reset before a new evaluation; nevertheless it becomes satisfied the same way as a Discrete condition.It is reset if the run-time event is the required run-time even

[...truncated for brevity...]

# Raw Message Condition

## Dialog: Raw condition pre-selection

Here you enter data required to create a new raw message condition.

## Dialog: Raw message condition

Here you enter the characteristics of a raw message condition – without having an associated database.

- Identifier Enter the logical operator and the message ID.

- Channel The channel on which the message/event is expected to be received.

- Number format This defines the format of all visible integer numbers; e.g. for entering the message ID.You can explicitly specify a hexadecimal format by starting the number with the prefix 0x.

- Data bytes

- Byte A serial number of bytes within a message (0…n); n depends on the selected bus system.

- Relation See Logical operators.

- Attributes See attributes of user-defined conditions.

### [FunctionBlocks/Trigger] TriggerUserDefinedTiming


# Timing

Trigger Configuration » Set of User-defined Conditions » Timing

Various time conditions can also be selected for a user-defined condition. The default configuration has no time condition.

## Timing of a user-defined condition

- None

- Timeout after: … cycle time(s)

You can enter any positive float number. The entered value must be equal to or greater than 0.01 milliseconds.

The length of a single cycle time is set in the message attribute CycleTime in the associated database. It is displayed as a static text below the edit box.

This timing type is unavailable if the message ID is not defined in any of the associated databases or its database does not have such an attribute; see below: user-defined condition with a timeout.

The resulting value of the timeout is stored in milliseconds (1 cycle time * N). The allowed minimum and maximum values of the timeout are the same as for timeout after … milliseconds.

- Timeout after: … ms The entered value must be equal to or greater than 0.01 milliseconds.See below: user-defined condition with a timeout.By configuring the Delay or Timeout after:…ms the user-defined condition is classified as Continuous.See Set of user-defined conditions:Discrete vs. continuous conditions for additional information.

- Delay…ms The feature specifies that after a successful evaluation the condition will be satisfied only after the required delay.By configuring the Delay or Timeout after:…ms the user-defined condition is classified as Co

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [General] 64Bit


# Notes on 64-Bit/32-Bit

CANoe » 64-Bit/32-Bit

## Overview

New in version 13.0:

- The CANoe application (and thus the analysis area with the graphical user interface) is now only available as a 64-bit variant for the user computer.

- The RT kernel (i.e. the real-time area) is available in a 64-bit variant and in a 32-bit variant for the RT hardware.

On the RT Setup page you will find an explanation of the operating modes and terms.

| Topic | 64-Bit RT Kernel | 32-Bit RT Kernel |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Deployment | Only for processors and operating systems in 64-bit architecture. | For processors and operating systems in 32-bit architecture or 64-bit architecture |
| Availability | The 64-bit variant is only available on the user computer. | For real-time execution in standalone mode or distributed mode; includes VN8900, VT6000 and RT Rack. The 32-bit variant is also available on the user computer. |
| Memory | The 64-bit variant can use more main memory; usually the complete built-in main memory. | The 32-bit variant can use a maximum of 4 GB main memory. The CAPL programs in the Simulation Setup of CANoe or test programs can therefore continue to use a maximum total of approx. 3 GB memory during real-time execution. |
| Use Cases | Applications that require a lot of main memory, especially large databases some CAPL analysis programs | When working with others who have older versions of CANoe. When working with VN8900.Exception: there are already all DLLs for both syst

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [General] AddOnDLLsAssigning


# Assigning of Modeling Libraries

CANoe » Libraries & Packages » Assigning of Modeling Libraries

## Introduction

The following describes how modeling libraries (interaction layer, transport protocol, network management, and customer-specific expansions) and test modules are integrated into a CANoe configuration.

The standard installation of CANoe contains a few sample configurations, into which such a DLL file has already been added; e.g. AUTOSAR network management for CAN and FlexRay.

|  | Note For OEM-specific add-ons, you install the add-on package beforehand. |

### Where are the DLL files located?

DLL files included in the standard delivery as well as DLL files installed with an add-on package can be found in the <CANoe installation directory>\Exec32 directory.

### Which version of the DLL file is currently installed?

You can find the version of the DLL loaded in CANoe on the File ribbon tab|Help|Details.

### Assigning DLL files

DLL files can be assigned for all nodes in the Simulation Setup are for individual nodes only. In the case of the test module, a DLL file can be assigned to an individual node in the test setup via the context menu.

There are various options for assigning a DLL file. These are presented in the following.

## Assigning a DLL File or Module Description File for CAN using the CANdb++ Editor

|  | Cross Reference See Embedding Modeling Libraries (CANdb++) for more information. |

In the CANdb database, you can store the value with the nam

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [General] CANoeCANalyzerCompatibility


# CANoe/CANalyzer Compatibility

CANoe » Compatibility

## General

CANoe/CANalyzer compatibility mode makes it possible to use configurations in the same way in both tools. This means that a configuration created in CANalyzer can be loaded directly in CANoe. Likewise, CANoe configurations can be loaded and used in CANalyzer. However, a configuration created in one tool and loaded in the other affects only the functions that are available in both tools. Functions that are not available are not loaded.

To prevent the original configuration from being corrupted, a configuration loaded in the other tool can only be saved under a new name (File|Save As…).

## CANalyzer Compatibility in CANoe

When a CANalyzer configuration is loaded in CANoe, only one block is loaded from the transmit branch. If there are several blocks, the first activated block is loaded. If all blocks are deactivated, the first block is loaded. On loading, the analysis branch of the Measurement Setup is preserved. This includes the database and channel assignments. A semantic analysis of CAPL programs is not carried out.

- The CANalyzer Measurement Setup and transmit branch are mapped to the CANoe Simulation and Measurement Setups.

- Note about CAPL programs

CAPL programs in the transmit branch typically have the following structure:

on message *{ output(this);}

Because CAPL programs are not validated when a CANalyzer configuration is loaded, this structure results in an infinite loop and should therefo

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [General] CANoeCANalyzerExtendedIdentifier


# CAN Extended Identifier

CANoe » CAN Extended Identifier

29 bit extended identifiers are displayed in CANoe with an appended x (example: 100x).

To configure for extended identifiers, e.g. in the Trace Window, in the transmit list of the Interactive Generator, or in the ID range of the Statistics Window, you must write the identifier (hexadecimal or decimal) in the appropriate text input field with the letter x as the last character. Every identifier that ends with an x is interpreted as an extended identifier.

In order to distinguish between normal and extended identifiers in CAPL programs, the most significant bit (bit 31, extended bit) is set to 1 for extended identifiers.

The isStdId and isExtId functions can be used to check whether the ID is in the standard format or extended format.

The mkExtId function can be used to generate an extended ID, and valOfId supplies the ID without extended bit.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [General] CANwinLoadConfigurationFromNetworkDrive


# Load Configuration from a Network Drive

CANoe » Load Configuration from a Network Drive

As of Version 6.0, CANoe uses Microsoft .NET Framework.

Because the preset security guidelines of the .NET Framework prohibit the execution of .NET programs/code on network drives (or from non-trusted sites), problems can occur when loading a configuration or components of a configuration from a network drive.

Problematic configurations are those that meet the following conditions:

- The configuration contains panels that were generated using the .NET Framework (e.g., C# or Visual Basic.NET) and are located on the network drive.The panels cannot be loaded from the network drive and displayed.

- The configuration contains a .NET test module that is located on the network drive.The measurement can be started but the .NET test module is not functioning properly.

- The configuration contains a .NET network node.When the measurement starts, an error message is displayed and the start of the measurement is canceled.

- The configuration contains .NET snippets.The measurement can be started but the snippets do not function during the measurement.

If you still want to start a configuration from a network drive, you can use caspol.EXE to adapt the security guideline for this network drive.

caspol.EXE can be found in <Windows-Verzeichnis>\Microsoft.NET\Framework\<Version>\caspol.EXE.

|  | Example Set the security settings of a folder (incl. all subfolders and the files therein) on a par

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [General] CANwinNETDiagnostics


# .NET Programming: Diagnostics

.NET Programming » Diagnostics

In order to support ECU diagnostics in various applications, the .Net library Vector.Diagnostics was introduced. Since it is part of CANoe, CANape and Indigo, you can exchange diagnostic procedures between these applications easily.

Please consider the following document: VDS_Library_QuickStart.PDF.

## Concept

For a simple implementation of communication with ECUs via diagnostics, the following areas are available:

- Selection of the ECU or a group of ECUs: Using the configured ECU qualifier, the Ecu object is retrieved.

- Creation of a request and setting of its parameters: At the ECU object a Request object can be created using the qualifier of a diagnostic service. The Request object provides Parameter objects whose values can be changed.

- Sending of a request and waiting until the request has been processed: After the Request object has been sent, the program will wait until it has been processed.

- Access to the responses received: The result of the sending contains the Response objects received, giving access to their Parameter objects.

|  | Notes In CANoe, the library Vector.Diagnostics is only available in test modules and snippets, since only they may wait for the processing of a request that has been sent. In order to write modules that work in all applications that provide the Vector.Diagnostics library, the following has to be kept in mind: modules should only import the libraries Vector.Di

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [General] CANwin_General_Installation-Directory


# Installation Directories

CANoe » Installation

## General

With CANoe 7.0, you can install user data and application data separately from program files. This means you can use CANoe as a default user even if you have chosen the Windows program folder as the CANoe installation folder.

You will need administrator rights for administrative tasks and installations (especially hardware installations and configuration of application channels in the Vector driver applet).

## Installation

The user data and application data are installed in different folders, depending on the selections made during installation.

## User Data

During installation, items such as sample configurations and templates are saved to the user data folder.

## Application Data

Settings for programs (e.g. CAN.ini) and add-on components are saved to the application data folder.

## Program Folder

The program files and add-on components are saved to the program folder. This folder also contains INI files that are read-only or that are changed only during administrative tasks (e.g. CAN driver configuration).

|  | Note You can open the user and application data folders using the Windows Explorer via General|File Locations in the Options dialog. |

Installation on a Network Drive

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [General] CANwin_Hints_for_SP_and_full_version


# Hints for Full and Service Pack Installation

CANoe » Hints for Full and Service Pack Installation

## Hints for Full Installation

- Several CANoe full installations can be installed on a computer in parallel.

- If a full version is installed on an existing CANoe directory, please be aware, that additional modules are compatible.

- For a full installation user-defined program settings are not restored, e.g. added CAPL DLLs from the Exec32 directory.

## Hints for Service Pack Installation

- A Service Pack is installed on an existing CANoe directory. Thereby user-defined program settings are restored. They are for example:

- Language settings

- Configured tools for several editors or viewers

- ZIP program for archiving used files

- Windows font settings

- Used CAPL DLLs

- Used HOOK DLLs

- Used CAN driver

- CANoe real time settings

- A backup is created for following files:

- CAN.ini => CAN.bak

- vector.ini => vector.bak

- candrv32.ini => candrv32.bak

- vsyschecks.ini => vsyschecks.bak

|  | Note If the Service Pack is installed as a silent installation all user-defined program settings are restored. |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [General] canwin_start_from_network_drive


# Installation on a Network Drive

CANoe » Installation on a Network Drive

If the CANoe application is installed and started on a network drive following problems can occur:

| Problem Description | General:Global settings are not stored. The additional tools (CAPL Compiler, CAPL Browser, Panel Designer, etc.) do not work or do not work proper. |
| --- | --- |
| Cause | CANoe uses the install directory (Exec32) to store files temporary. In case of you have no write access these misleading situation can occur. |
| Solution | Write access to the installation directory is needed. Please contact the system administrator. |

| Problem Description | COM server:Script or other programs which communicate with CANoe using the COM interface do not work. |
| --- | --- |
| Cause | The COM server is not registered on the local computer, if the installation program was not executed on the local computer. |
| Solution | Register the COM server on the local computer using following command line: canoe32.exe -regserver Note: Administrator rights on the local PC are necessary. |

| Problem Description | Help:The help cannot be opened. |
| --- | --- |
| Cause | The standard security settings of the Internet Explorer inhibit the display of the HTML based help, if it is located on a network drive. Detailed information can be found under: http://support.microsoft.com/kb/896358 http://support.microsoft.com/kb/896054 http://support.microsoft.com/kb/902225/DE/ |
| Solution | A workaround is to edit

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [General] cmn_canmasksetup


# Programming of Messages

CANoe » Programming of Messages

The 82C200 BASIC-CAN controller, the i82526 controller and the SJA1000 CAN controller as well as the NEC 72005 CAN controller control, by means of a mask, which messages can be transmitted and which can be received. It is sufficient to specify an acceptance code and an acceptance mask. If all messages should be received, the acceptance mask is set to hexadecimal FF; in this case the acceptance code is irrelevant.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [General] cmn_canoptions


# Card Options

CANoe » Card Options

It may be necessary to input various options depending on the existing hardware and driver equipment.

The last two menu commands of the board context menu are intended for this purpose.

When they are activated the user is presented with dialog windows for entering these options.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [General] cmn_cansetup


# Programming of Bus Parameters

CANoe » Programming of Bus Parameters

The data source in online mode is the CAN PC-card. Primarily it delivers messages received from the CAN bus together with the time of receipt. Furthermore, upon request it can also acknowledge transmit requests with the exact time of the request and provide transmitted messages with the exact time of transmission.

The card is initialized at the start of an online measurement. The user must enter parameters required for this purpose. Click this menu command to open a dialog for programming the card registers.

User control of the various dialogs and the meaning of the input boxes are explained in the Help function. When setting up a new measurement configuration the standard values are set automatically as a function of the controller type.

The two CAN controllers must be programmed separately to permit the support applications with independent bus systems with different speeds.

Programming of Messages | Card Options

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |

### [General] DatabaseLoadOldConfiguration


# Database Assignment When Loading Old Configurations

CANoe » Database Assignment When Loading Old Configurations

Ribbon: File Tab|Open

## Assigning Databases to Several Channels

Prior to Version 7.2 it was only possible to assign databases to either a single channel or to all channels of a bus system. For example, if three CAN channels were in use, a CAN database could be assigned to either CAN 1, CAN 2 or CAN 3, or to CAN All.

Version 7.2 offers a more precise way of assigning databases. You can now, for example, assign a database to CAN 1 and CAN 3 without assigning it to CAN 2. It is no longer possible to assign a database to CAN All, but you can still assign a database to all channels by assigning it to them individually.

When you load a configuration created with a version earlier than 7.2, in which a database is assigned to all channels, that database will remain assigned to all channels.

## Simulation Setup

Earlier versions already made it possible to assign a CAN database to a subset of channels in the Simulation Setup. When resolving ambiguities, however, the real assignment was still to CAN All. When you load such a configuration in CANoe versions 7.2 and later, the displayed Simulation Setup is corrected automatically so that the database is available in all CAN networks. If you don't want that database to be assigned to all of the networks, you can, of course, remove it from some of them.

If you don't want the correction to be made and want to keep the 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [General] dlg_consistent


# Consistency Check of Symbolic Data

CANoe » Consistency Check of Symbolic Data

In symbolic mode CAN messages are addressed exclusively using symbolic names from an associated database. For this the consistency of the database with the active configuration in the following situations is checked:

- At program start,

- When associating a new database

- When reactivating the main program after changing the database.

In this consistency test the symbolic names of all CAN messages used in the measurement configuration are compared to the names in the database. If a message name has changed in the database, an adaptation is made automatically. In doing so, an appropriate message appears on the screen. If neither the name or message ID were found in the database, an error message is generated. In this case the measurement cannot be started until after the user has removed the particular message from the configuration.

The output of additional inconsistency warnings or error messages can be suppressed by deactivating the appropriate check boxes in the report window. However, the consistency check and correction of inconsistent messages themselves are not influenced by this.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |

### [General] EnvironmentVariables


# Environment Variables

CANoe » Environment Variables

In contrast to system variables – the CAN signals – environment variables describe the behavior of network nodes with regard to external input and output signals.

CANoe differentiates between discrete and continuous variables, character strings and binary data.

- Discrete environment variables may be used to represent switch positions.

- Continuous environment variables might be used to represent variables such as temperature or engine speed.

- Character string variables can represent whole words or texts of any length.

- Binary data can be used to encode entire data fields.

## Select Environment Variables

|  | Note The creation of environment variables is no longer supported. Instead, use system variables directly in CANoe. Currently CANoe still supports the use of environment variables. This support will not be available in future versions. |

In this dialog you can select environment variables in the multiple selection list on the right side of the dialog.

The left side of the dialog is used to preselect environment variables which are displayed in the multiple selection list. In the upper left field you can check-off whether all those environment variables are to be displayed to which at least one of the selected nodes from the database has write or read access. In the two lower fields you check off whether all those environment variables are to be displayed to which at least one of the selected nodes from t

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [General] ErrorMessageRestoreDataAfterWinShutdown


# Restoring Unsaved Changes after Windows Shutdown or Malfunction

CANoe » Restoring Unsaved Changes after Windows Shutdown or Malfunction

Before this start of CANoe the tool had a malfunction or Windows was shut down while CANoe was open and the opened configuration contained unsaved changes.

To prevent these changes from being lost they were saved automatically in temporary recovery information files. See also Configuration: Save/Open.

|  | Notes Select [Yes] to restore the changes. You can then continue working with the version that existed before the malfunction or Windows shutdown. Select [No] to discard the changes and continue working with the last explicitly saved version of the configuration. The temporary recovery information will be deleted in both cases; you receive the prompt only once! If you are uncertain, select [Yes], and then save the configuration under a new name. |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [General] EventSorting


# Event Sorting

CANoe » Event Sorting

Since version 5.2, CANoe can sort events chronologically that it receives from different networks before they are processed. The time stamps of the events are used for sorting. An event in this context is, for example, a CAN message or an Error Frame.

It is important to sort events if the different networks are connected via well-synchronized hardware, but some hardware can only pass the events on to CANoe after a certain delay (this is the case, for example, for hardware connected via USB interface). Without sorting, an event with a higher time stamp would sometimes be processed before an event (from another network) with a lower time stamp, which can lead to problems in gateway nodes.

CANoe determines whether sorting is necessary based on the hardware and network configuration automatically. However, you can also configure or turn off the sorting yourself in the Options dialog. The latency time between the receipt of an event and its processing is somewhat lower without sorting.

## The Sorting Function

To sort the results, one of the channels is first selected as a reference channel. The reference channel must precisely generate and guarantee time stamps that events are delivered in short time intervals, since it determines the program-internal time.

Reference channel events are then delayed a certain period before processing. If an event from another channel arrives with a lower time stamp in this time, it can be processed befo

[...truncated for brevity...]

## CANoe/CANalyzer CANoeVariants at a Glance

| CANalyzer fun The fundamental variant is suitable for simple analysis, and it provides all interactive standard functions for this purpose. However, it does not offer programmability, diagnostic functions or user control panels. CANalyzer exp The expert variant is ideal for all standard applications, and it provides all functions and extensions without limitation. However, this variant does not support creating and executing CAPL programs. CANalyzer pro The professional variant provides all functions and extensions without limitations. It supports all use cases from simple observation of the bus traffic to complex analysis, stimulation and testing of heterogeneous systems. | CANoe pex The project execution variant provides a graphical user interface exclusively. Simulation, test cases and results are easily controlled without the need to specifically evaluate the underlying messages. CANoe run The runtime variant is suitable for users who want to quickly and easily test their ECU in interaction with a specified remaining bus simulation. Configurations cannot be changed, analysis functions are fully available and network nodes can be easily connected and disconnected. CANoe pro The professional variant is intended for users who want to take advantage of the full range of CANoe functions. Simulation models can be created with CAPL and .NET. Test cases are e

### [General] help_datei_path


# Saving File Paths

CANoe » Configuration » Save

When opening a file CANoe records the file path in the Registry. When the same data type is accessed at a later time the last opened path is automatically searched.

This path saving is performed for all relevant file types such as configurations (*.CFG), databases (*.DBC) and logging files (*.ASC).

Each installed version of CANoe saves its own paths to avoid dependencies.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |

# Loading Configurations with the Explorer

CANoe » Loading Configurations with the Explorer

It is possible to open the configuration files (CFG) in the Explorer automatically with CANoe. This option is located in the Options dialog.

## Manually linking the CANoe application with the extension CFG

You are advised to set up the automatic association of CFG files with the CANoe application using the CANoe option, since this association distinguishes between CANoe and CANalyzer configurations.

Another option is to link the extension CFG with the CANoe32.EXE application in Windows Explorer, so that the configurations is loaded directly via the Explorer. If CANoe is already running, you will get an error message when you try to open a CFG file via double-click, because the Explorer then attempts to start CANoe a second time.

|  | Note As of version 5.0 the DDE interface is no longer supported. For this reason, new data exchange implementations have to be based on the COM server. |

|  | Note for System State Starting with version 9.0 CANoe stores certain system parameters independent of the configuration file. These include, for example, the measurement index and the counter for the test execution. The file for the system parameters is created in the same directory as the CANoe configuration with the same name but with extension STCFG. If this file is present, it is automatically used in the CANoe configuration. If you copy or move a configuration, ensure that the file for t

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [General] HELP_INTERGATEPANEL


# Linking Panels

CANoe » Linking Panels

The Network node models which you have created and inserted in the simulation configuration can react to external events (e.g. activation of a switch). These events are described with the help of

- Environment Variables whose names and types (discrete/continuous) must first be defined in the database or

- or system variables.

You can interactively change the values of these variables during a measurement. To do this, you first create one or more control panels with the Panel Designer, whereby the control and display elements (switches, indicator lamps, etc.) must be linked to the associated variables.

To link the control panels into the configuration, you would first call the Panel Configuration (Home ribbon tab|Panel).

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [General] HELP_JITTER_DRIFT


# Drift and Jitter

CANoe » Drift and Jitter

The Drift and Jitter options change the behavior of timers that are used in a network node model.

Drift sees to it that the running times of all timers that are started in the specific node are lengthened or shortened by a fixed percentage.

With Jitter the timer running times are changed randomly. Here the user specifies the interval within which the fluctuation should lie. The fluctuation is thereby uniformly distributed.

The parameter values are input in the relevant text boxes in percent. The maximum bandwidth for this is from –100.00% to +100.00%.

If the supplemental attributes Drift and Jitter have been input in the database for this node, checking the option From CANdb causes the database values to be used.

- The key word for Drift in the database is: CANoeDrift.

- For Jitter the lower limit is defined by CANoeJitterMin,

- and the upper limit with CANoeJitterMax.

To learn how to define user-specific attributes in the database please refer to the CANdb++ Editor help function.

Settings for Drift and Jitter can also be changed directly from the specific network node model using the relevant CAPL functions. Please refer to CAPL Help regarding this.

The relevant functions are:

- Drift: setDrift, getDrift

- Jitter: setJitter, getJitterMax, getJitterMin

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [General] HELP_NETNODES


## Network nodes

System components with a specific functionality are called Network nodes or Controllers.This includes behavior with respect to input and output variables and messages to be received and transmitted.

The event-driven procedural language CAPL is available to the user for functional description of network nodes.For example, this allows the user to describe how, after the arrival of a message, the received data are to be processed, and how the result is to be output as a control value.

Network nodes are represented in the simulation configuration in CANoe.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |

### [General] HELP_ONLINE_GENERATOR_WITH_DATABASE


# Working with an Associated Database

CANoe » Working with an Associated Database

If there is a database associated to the CANoe configuration, messages from the database can be added to the transmit list. After clicking the [New] button, the Symbol Selection dialog is opened, with which messages can be selected symbolically. These are then added to the transmit list. Afterwards, signals of the messages can be configured individually in each message's signal list. As an alternative to the [New] button, double clicking the first blank line in the "Message Name" column with the left mouse button will also open the Symbol Selection dialog.

In the "Chip" column of message parameters you can configure the channel/chip over which the message is to be transmitted. This setting is taken from the database allocation to a channel. If the database is not allocated to a particular channel, "Chip 1" is configured as the default; it is then possible to change this setting.

Options can be preconfigured for messages and signals in the database. It is possible to define the cycle time for messages and signal values as database attributes and assign default values.

The attributes for messages are:

- CycleTime: Integer [ms].Explicit cycle time for the Interactive Generator.

- GenMsgCycleTime: Integer [ms].Database attribute generated by the CAPL Generator.

The attributes for physical signal values are:

- SignalDefault: Float [phys. signal value].Signal value for the Interactive Genera

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [General] HELP_STARTDELAY


# Start Delay

CANoe » Start Delay

The Start Delay is a time span after the start of measurement in which the particular node remains completely passive. It does not react to external influences, nor does it activate itself. It does not change its behavior and function like every other node until the time span has elapsed.

The active check box is used to activate the Start Delay for this node. In the text box behind it any desired time span between 0 and 999999 ms can be entered, and the node remains passive for this period of time.

If the supplemental Start Delay attribute is input for this node in the database, checking the option From CANdb will cause this value to be used.

The key word for Start Delay in the database is: CANoeStartDelay.

To learn how to define user-specific attributes in the database please refer to the CANdb++ Editor help function.

The Start Delay can also be set from the network node's CAPL program during the preStart phase. At the later run time it is possible to query this value. Please refer to Help for the getStartdelay and setStartdelay functions in the CAPL compiler regarding this.

|  | Note The Start Delay affects only the interaction layer, but not the network management. |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [General] HELP_WINDOWS_SEARCH_SEQUENCE


# Search sequence for a Windows DLL

CANoe » Search Sequence for a Windows DLL

At the start CANoe searches a DLL in the following sequence:

- In the CANoe EXEC32 directory (e.g. c:\canwin\exec32)

- In the active working directory

- In Windows system directory

- In Windows directory

- In the computer's active search path

For work with various user-defined DLLs it is recommended that the desired DLL be copied to the EXEC32 directory before starting CANoe (e.g. with batch files from various subdirectories). As an alternative, the variants of the user-defined DLLs can be saved in different working directories. Depending on the particular variant, CANoe must be started from the appropriate working directory. To accomplish this, a CANoe icon must be created for each variant in a group in the Program Manager, and for each icon the working directory in which the associated user-defined DLL is located must be defined.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [General] HISTOGRAMM_FKT


# Histogram Function

Measurement Setup » Frame Histogram » Configuration

The histogram functions make it possible to record statistical evaluations over multiple, consecutive time ranges. These evaluations may also originate from multiple measurements of the same configuration.

If the expanded statistical functions are activated in the configuration dialog of the Frame Histogram, an acquisition range is automatically created at the measurement start. Using the histogram function a new acquisition range can be started with Start Acquisition Range and ended with End Acquisition Range. An acquisition range is automatically ended when the measurement is stopped.

Besides manual control, CAPL functions are also available to start and end acquisition ranges:

- Start: startStatistcAcquisition

- Stop: stopStatistcAcquisition

- Test: isStatistcAcquisitionRunning

After one or more acquisition ranges have ended, the data can then be saved and/or output from the Histogram dialog.

For a detailed explanation of the use of CAPL functions for controlling acquisition ranges please refer to the Help function for the CAPL Compiler.

Frame Histogram

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |

### [General] KeyboardOperationMeasurement


# Keyboard Operations of the Windows during Measurement

CANoe » Keyboard Operations of the Windows during Measurement

Key combinations are used outside of a measurement for controlling window functions or for navigating in the windows.

During a measurement, key combinations are normally used to initiate keyboard actions, e.g., in CAPL programs, interactive generators, or Logging Blocks. The keyboard control of the window is then only possible to a very limited extent.

Via the status bar of CANoe, it is also possible to use the familiar key combinations for operation of the windows during the measurement.

## Button in the status bar

- : Default setting; Key combinations are used to initiate keyboard actions, e.g., in CAPL programs, interactive generators, or Logging Blocks.

- : With a click on you activate the possibility to use the familiar key combinations for operation of the windows during the measurement.

You can only activate and deactivate the control of the window functionality during an active measurement with a mouse click or with key combination <Ctrl> + <K>.

## Initiating Keyboard Actions (Default Setting)

With this setting, you can use the key combinations for user-defined actions in windows and program nodes.

Examples:

- Triggering of an action within the Interactive Generators

- Starting of a Logging Block

- Execution of a key action (e.g. on key) programmed within a CAPL program.

## Control of Window Functionality

With this setting, it is also 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [General] MacroRecorder


# Macro Recorder

CANoe » Automation Sequences » Macro Recorder

Macros permit reproducible and automated testing.

With the help of the Macro Recorder in CANoe macros can be made to record and replay user actions on panels and Diagnostic Consoles. Environment variable changes are also recorded while recording user actions on panels. If the actions of Diagnostic Console and the Fault Memory Window are recorded, then diagnostic data sent to the controller are included in the recording.

|  | Note Macros are being used to influence simulations (not analysis). Therefore macros can only be recorded and executed in online mode. |

Once you have started to record the macro every action you execute on the panels with the mouse, and every request sent via the Diagnostic Consoles is recorded. Furthermore, explicit opening and closing of the diagnostic channel are also recorded, and this affects sending of Tester Present requests. The Macro Recorder can also be used to replay the recorded macros.

Each macro is saved in a *.asc file. The ASCII format of the macro file conforms to ASCII Logging Format:

5.1543 Env_DoorWindow_up := 1

0.9326 Env_DoorWindow_up := 0

1.7398 Env_DoorWindow_down := 1

0.9893 Env_DoorWindow_down := 0

4.0247 1 Console::Console_2::Light = 0

0.9351 2 Console::Console_2::Light = 1

6.7700 L2 RightMirror::MirrorTemp::MirrorTempRight = 6

9.9156 DiagRequest[Vector_Door] 1A 90

You can open the macro file in a standard text editor and make changes to it (e.g. cha

### [General] MacroRecorderDiagnostics


# Macro Recorder: Diagnostics

Macro Recorder » Diagnostics

Diagnostic request sequences can be recorded and played back using the Macro Recorder. This lets you record frequently used control sequences for a control unit during an interactive diagnostic session and then repeat them in the same way e.g. at the touch of a button in the control panel.

### Use Cases

- Reading out a list of fault memory entries

- Initializing several diagnostic parameters on a control unit at the start of a session

- Switching a control unit to password-protected diagnostic mode in several steps

|  | Note In order to prevent the recording of desired control actions from being corrupted by test modules or other CAPL programs that may be running in the background, the Macro Recorder records only those diagnostic requests that are interactively sent to the Diagnostic Console and Fault Memory Window. |

To start recording diagnostic requests for a macro, select one of the two options listed below from the View|Automation Sequences dialog prior to starting the session:

- The Record Diagnostic Requests option, or

- The Record Diagnostic Requests and Panel Entries option.

Screenshot: Configuring the Macro Recorder for Diagnostics

You will find an example of how to use a diagnostic macro in the CANSystemDemo: Desktop Diagnostics sample configuration.

A simple C API for accessing services of CANoe is available in your application code. This API enables the sending and receiving of CAN and LIN frames, provides timer services and enables access to the system variables of CANoe. ### [General] RealTimeExecution


# Real Time Execution

CANoe » Real Time Execution

The following options for real-time execution are available to you.

- RT setup for standalone mode and distributed mode

- Mode

- Distributed Mode CANoe offers the opportunity to execute the real-time relevant simulation parts on an individual computer (RT server), that is, separated from the graphic interface (User Computer in distributed mode).

- Standalone Mode In standalone mode you can execute the real time specific portions of a configuration on an external device (e.g. VN8900) without a connection to the user computer.

- Hardware

- VN8900 The VN8900 combines the ease of use (plug & play, USB – as e.g. CANcaseXL, VN7600) with real-time capability. For that purpose in CANoe the real-time-relevant parts of the simulation- and test environment are executed on the VN8900 device (seeRT Setup). In CANalyzer the send branch and its blocks are executed on the VN8900 device. Thereby you achieve improvements with respect to latency times and precision of timers.

- VT6000The real time module has an embedded computer that runs the real time specific parts of CANoe tests and simulations in the VT System rack. The user sees the VT6000 as a pre-configured RT server. The user computer (in distributed mode) is connected to the VT6000 via Ethernet.

- Extended Realtime (ERT)With the feature Extended Real Time as a part of the Vector Tool Platform the latency and determinism of Vector tools like CANoe and CANape are improved.

- C

# Search Sequence for DLLs (CANoe only)

CANoe » Search sequence for DLLs (only CANoe) and Hooks

When the application starts, the modeling libraries (DLL files) will be searched in several paths.The search sequence is as follows:

## 1. Search relative to the path of the CANoe configuration in the following directories

- ./Nodes

- ./Models

- ./Exec32

- . (current directory)

## 2. Windows Search

- The directory from which the application was loaded

- The current directory

- Windows system directory SYSTEM32

- The Windows directory

- The directories that are listed in the PATH environment variable

## 3. Search relative to the path of the CANoe configuration in the following directories

- ../Exec32

- ../../Exec32

|  | Note Windows 7 performs a re-direction to the application directory prior to loading DLLs. If there is a DLL with the same name in the application directory (the Exec32 directory of the CANoe installation), this DLL (from the application directory) will be loaded regardless of any path settings or search sequences. |

### [General] Standards


# Standards – Bus Systems, Protocols, Databases

CANoe » Standards – Bus Systems, Protocols, Databases

## Bus Systems and Protocols

List of the most important supported bus systems and protocols:

| Bus System | Protocol | Short Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| CAN / CAN FD | CAN (Controller Area Network) was developed by the Robert Bosch GmbH in the early eighties and was internationally standardized (ISO 11898) in 1994. CAN was specifically developed for the serial data exchange between electronic control units in automobiles but is also used for the realization of industrial microcontroller networks. CAN FD is a further development of the CAN bus system. It supports higher data rates (max. 8 Mbit/s) and up to 64 data bytes. Application area: Automotive engineering, automation engineering. |
| J1939 (SAE J1939-11/-14/-15) | SAE J1939 is a communication protocol based on CAN for real time data exchange between electronic control units (ECUs) in the area of commercial vehicles. It was developed by the international Society of Automotive Engineers (SAE). Application area: Commercial vehicle engineering, railway engineering, agricultural engineering |
| ISO11783 | ISO 11783 is a communication protocol based on J1939 for agricultural engineering. Application area: Agricultural engineering |
| ISO 11992 | Truck & Trailer Interface |
| CANopen | The CANopen profile family specifies standardized communication mechanisms and device functionalities (EN 50325-4). CANopen is main

### [General] SymbolSelectionTextCompletion


# Symbol Selection: Text Completion

Visual Sequencer » Text Completion

## Symbol Selection with Text Completion

- An easy way of making entries in table cells is to use the list that appears for the cell.

- This list contains all values that are available for this cell.

- The upper part (Suggested Symbols) of the list shows your previous entered symbols. When using the list for the first time this part is hided.The lower part (All filtered Symbols) shows all available values.

- Your previous entry is used to filter the list (both parts).

## Symbol Selection with Selection Dialog

- Use in a table cell to open the Symbol Selection dialog and choose the desired symbol.

## Opening the List

- To open the list, start entering the name of the symbol (signal, environment variable or system variable) into the cell.The list then shows all available symbols that contain the text you have entered.

- Alternately, you can open the list by pressing <Alt>+<Arrow down>.

## Selection

- You can select the desired symbol by double-clicking it with the mouse, or

- by navigating to the symbol using the arrow keys and then pressing <Return>, <Enter> or <Tab> to make your selection.

- If you want to enter a value that is not in the list you can close the list with <ESC>.

## Hints

- The familiar icons that appear before the symbol name indicate the symbol type (signal, environment variable or system variable).

### [General] SynchronizationOfTimestamps


# Synchronization of Time Stamps

CANoe » Synchronization of Time Stamps

Time synchronization means the adjustment of hardware time stamps of events coming from different network interfaces each with a separate hardware clock. This is done to achieve that events virtually occurring at the same time, have the same time stamp, despite the fact that in real systems no two hardware clocks ever run at the same speed and have no offset.

Typically Vector interface hardware offers several channels, for connection to various bus systems such as CAN, LIN, FlexRay and Ethernet. When all channels are on one hardware interface, a time synchronization is not needed, because the time stamps for the events of the respective channels are derived from the same clock.

As soon as more than one hardware interface is used, a synchronization between the time stamps of events from different interfaces is necessary; because each interface has its own clock.

Vector offers two possibilities to synchronize the time stamps between different network interfaces.

- Software Time Synchronization A software algorithm synchronizes the time stamp of the events of each network interface to the computer.

- Hardware Time Synchronization A party line is used for signaling between all synchronized network interfaces to enable a precise synchronization. Not every interface supports this way of synchronization.

## Software Time Synchronization

A software algorithm synchronizes the time stamp of the events of

### [General] WorkingAsWindowsStandardUser


# Working as Windows Standard User without Admin Rights

CANoe » Working as Windows Standard User without Admin Rights

If you want to use CANoe as a standard Windows user without Administrator rights, please note the following:

- CANoe should be installed in a folder for which the user has full write permission.

- You can also install all add-ons and manually modify INI files in this folder.

The following problems may occur:

- Drivers: The Vector Hardware Configuration Dialog in the Control Panel must be opened once by someone with Administrator rights, e.g. when the Administrator carries out the hardware installation. This causes the path to be adapted in the Registry. After this you no longer need Administrator rights to modify the hardware configuration (e.g. to allocate application channels).

- COM server: If the user needs to switch between different CANoe versions, the COM server registration e.g. for model generation (Model Generation Wizard) will fail if the user does not have Administrator rights. If you are using only one single CANoe version, generation is not a problem because the COM server is registered during installation.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |

### [General/Licensing] LicenseCheck


# License Check

CANoe » Licensing » License Check

If no application license for CANoe is available on program start, configurations can be loaded and modified, but the program will run with limited feature set:

- Starting a measurement (online/offline) is not possible.

- Saving configurations is not possible.

- Saving test environments is not possible.

- Importing logging files in Graphics windows and Trace windows is not possible.

- Converting logging files in Graphics windows and Trace windows is not possible.

- Controlling the standalone mode is possible.

- Configuring the RT server is possible.

A View Only note is shown in the application window title and in the Information section of the Help page (File ribbon tab).

If you activate licenses during program execution, you can check them by one of the following methods:

- Opening the License Options dialog using the [Licensing] button in backstage view or Environment ribbon tab.

- Using Re-read Licenses (License Options dialog).

- Connection with an RT server which has license access.This method is the initial step after an installation of CANoe if no local license provider is used.

If a valid application license is available afterwards, a program restart is required.

|  | Cross Reference You can find an overview of procedures when starting the program without licenses here. |

### [General/MemoryLoadPerformance] HighBusloadBehavior


# Behavior under High Load Conditions

CANoe » Working at High Bus Loads » Behavior under High Load Conditions

At high bus load the computing power of your computer may – in some situations – be insufficient to simultaneously execute the more demanding evaluation and display functions (Frame Histogram with statistics report, Data and Graphics Windows with many signals, Trace Window. Therefore, to avoid the risk of data loss the program has mechanisms for detecting and handling load situations.

Thus, the analysis windows interrupt the display briefly in a load condition to make more computing resources available. However, in certain situations not all messages may be displayed in the window any longer during the measurement. Although not all messages are displayed any longer, no data are lost. After the measurement has ended, the complete set of information is available to you in the Trace window, Graphics window and in logging.

In addition to this automatic shutoff function, you can also manually optimize some analysis blocks.

It is impossible to predetermine an optimal configuration of the Measurement Setup for all situations. Cyclic updating indeed conserves computing time, but it also results in a worse display of information. Under some circumstances it may be sensible to completely disconnect unnecessary analysis branches of the Measurement Setup (Insert break command in the context menu of the Hot spot) or reduce the data volume at the entry to the Measurement Setu

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [General/MemoryLoadPerformance] HighBusloadWorking


# Working at High Bus Loads

CANoe » Working at High Bus Loads

An abridged version of the following tips can be read on help page Performance Problems - Causes and remedies.

At high bus loads the CAN messages registered by the computer card might not be displayed promptly any longer. However, in many cases the measurement window options can be optimized to prevent this data overrun:

- To free up more computing resources, deactivate all measurement windows that you do not need. To disable data flow into the measurement window, simply double click the left side of the Hot Spots before the particular analysis block. The block will then be shown in gray, and the window will be deactivated.

- For the Trace Window functions with a high computing power can be deactivated.

- Deactivate the statistics report in the Context Menu of the Frame Histogram if it is turned on and you don't need it.

- If you have configured a large number of signals in the Data Window you should select the Cyclic Symbol mode ("Configure timings" command in the Data Window's context menu and enter a cycle time of maximum 500 milliseconds. The window is then only updated cyclically and thereby demands less computing power.

- If you have configured a large number of signals in the Graphics Window you should select a large user-defined Refresh (200 ms to 2 sec) in the Measurement Configuration dialog ("Options" item in the context menu). This defines how often the Graphics Window display should be updated

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [General/MemoryLoadPerformance] LoadIndicators


# Load Indicators

CANoe » Working at High Bus Loads » Load Indicators

## Load Indicator

The load indicator (buffer fill status), located in the left corner of the status bar, can be used during the measurement to observe the loading of your system. It represents the ratio of arriving events to processed events (e.g. display in the Trace Window).

The length of the bar indicates the fill level of the queue which buffers the real-time area (e.g. CAN hardware) from the Measurement Setup (e.g. Trace, Logging).

If the ratio between arriving and processed events is relatively balanced, then the load indicator has a green color. However, if it is shown in red, significantly more events are arriving at that particular time than can be processed.

If the buffer fill level reaches an alarming limit, then this is a clear indication that the system is overloaded. This might even lead to loss of events.

By deactivating individual analysis windows an attempt can be made to relieve the system of loading. Please also refer to page Performance Problems.

|  | Edit If necessary you can disable the display of the buffer level with the help of the CAN.INI entry ShowMainQueue=0 in the [System] section. |

## Load Indicator Network

During distributed operation with a VN8900 device or an RT server the fill level of the queue on the RT server, that separates the real-time area from the network connection (USB or Ethernet), is shown under the normal load indicator.

When the fill level of this

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [General/MemoryLoadPerformance] MemoryInsufficient


# Message due to Insufficient Memory

CANoe » Working at High Bus Loads » Message due to Insufficient Memory

It can be generally stated that CANoe needs a certain amount of available physical memory for optimal operation. Available physical memory is that part of the memory that is not obtained with the help of a swap file.

## What leads to this message?

Effective with program version 3.2 the available physical memory is checked. As soon as the available memory falls below a predefined minimum the program outputs a warning/informational message.

## Memory requirements/installed memory

The amount of available physical memory depends on the actual processes that are running, i.e. it is not simply determined by CANoe. This will be different on every computer, since on some computers only CANoe is running, while on others additional applications may also be running. Consequently, no general conclusions may be drawn regarding the minimum installed memory.

## When, where and how does lack of memory have an impact?

- The warning message is normally only relevant when there are stringent requirements on timer accuracy during the simulation.

- It is not possible to deduce from the warning/information message alone that there will necessarily be poor or inaccurate simulation results. However, the risk of greater latency times is increased.

- Very big models/configurations can lead to delayed response times of panels and displays.

- Data loss should not be expected in cases o

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [General/MemoryLoadPerformance] MemoryInsuffMStop


# Critical Memory Shortage - Measurement Stopped

CANoe » Working at High Bus Loads » Critical Memory Shortage - Measurement Stopped

The measurement has been stopped due to a shortage of memory.

The virtual memory used by CANoe has a exceeded a critical limit. The measurement has been stopped to prevent irregular termination of the program in the event that additional memory would be needed but can no longer be made available by the operating system.

Save your configuration and restart CANoe if possible in order to prevent data loss.

CANoe contains mechanisms for preventing too much memory from being utilized during the measurement. However, these cannot anticipate every situation. For example, the saving of many frames of different sizes in the Data History can also lead to fragmentation in the Windows heap.

During the measurement, large amounts of memory may be used for the Data History and scope measurements or by CAPL programs in the Measurement Setup. You may therefore be able to avoid the situation in several ways:

- Use the 64-bit variant of CANoe.

- In the Data History settings, set the utilization of the hard disk either to No with short data history or Normal use.

- Check CAPL programs in the Measurement Setup to determine whether they use very large associative fields, for example.

- Reduce the data measured with the Scope option.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [General/MemoryLoadPerformance] MemoryShortage


# Low on Memory

CANoe » Working at High Bus Loads » Low on Memory

The Low on Memory dialog is displayed by CANoe at the start of the measurement if only a small amount of virtual memory is still available for the program.

## Background

The virtual memory is memory that the operating system (i.e., Windows) makes available to CANoe. All program data (e.g., DLLs), all configuration data (e.g., databases), and most measured data (e.g., for display in analysis windows) are stored in the virtual memory. If there is not enough virtual memory for all of the data, the program no longer functions correctly and crashes may occur, e.g., with error message bad_alloc. The size of the virtual memory is dependent on the operating system. CANoe normally receives up to 4 GB from a 64-bit operating system. However, if both the physical main memory of the computer is low and the Windows paging file is small, the virtual memory can also be smaller. CANoe does not use all of the virtual memory up to the maximum limit, but allows a certain safety margin of several hundred MB. The virtual memory must be differentiated from the main memory of the computer. On the one hand, a program can occupy a large amount of virtual memory, but use significantly less main memory, e.g., if the program has used data temporarily and deleted it again. On the other hand, if the main memory is smaller than the virtual memory, Windows may write parts of the virtual memory of a program to its paging file on the hard 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [General/MemoryLoadPerformance] OverloadBehavior


# Behavior under Overload Conditions

CANoe » Working at High Bus Loads » Behavior under Overload Conditions

In the course of a measurement, the PC card registers CAN messages on the bus and passes them via the paths designated in the data flow diagram to the evaluation and analysis blocks located at the far right. For this purpose, two program modules work closely together during a measurement. First the real-time library (CANRT.DLL) retrieves information arriving at the card and delivers it to a ring buffer. In a second step, these data are read out by CANoe and are evaluated in the Measurement Setup. If the rate of messages registered by the card is so high that the main Windows program cannot follow along in its processing, the ring buffer overflows. However, as a user you are informed of this data loss immediately.

The Trace window signals load situations through separate events/lines:

- Main Queue Load => Normal:Signals that a previously occurring load or overload situation (Main Queue Load => High and Main Queue Load => Data Lost) has been eliminated.

- Main Queue Load => High:Signals the time from which a load situation is detected. The Trace window interrupts the display of newly arriving events starting from this time. However, the events are stored in the buffer of the Trace window. As soon as the load situation is eliminated, the display resumes as usual.

- Main Queue Load => Data Lost:Signals the time from which a data loss came about due to the overload si

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [General/MemoryLoadPerformance] PerformanceMonitor


# Performance Monitor

Options » Measurement » Performance

Ribbon: File Tab|Options|Measurement|Performance

When the Performance Monitoring function is switched on, a set of performance-critical system parameters are monitored during runtime of the measurement. Through an analysis of these parameters, conclusions can be drawn about the area of the measurement where problems are being caused.

For each monitored parameter there is an internal system variable that is updated in a defined (parameter-specific) time interval. The system variables exist only as long as the Performance Monitoring function is switched on, so that the function itself does not negatively affect the performance. Individual parameters can be dependent on other settings and are therefore not necessarily visible immediately after the function is switched on.

All system variables of the Performance Monitoring function can be found in the CANoe namespace. Depending on whether a parameter mainly influences simulation or analysis, the namespace Simulation or Analysis is generated, in which the system variables are then defined.

| Variable | Interval | Type | Unit | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Analysis::CPUUsageTotal | 1 s | Int32 | % | CPUUsageTotal This parameter determines the relative usage of the CPU of the respective computer on which analysis and simulation is run. When analysis and simulation run on the same computer, both variables have identical values. |
| Simulation::CPUUsag

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [General/MemoryLoadPerformance] PerformanceOptimization


# Performance Optimization

Performance » Optimization

Ribbon: File Tab|Help|Performance|Optimization

This function executes a performance optimization and a report is generated in the Write Window. The report lists the configuration options used in the optimization and their previous and new values.

You can undo the changes at any time. Please note that only those values can be canceled which were not changed in specific configuration dialogs since the optimization.

The configuration analysis includes…

- fading is deactivated in new Trace Windows

- byte fading is deactivated in all Trace Windows

- event fading is deactivated in all Trace Windows

- during the measurement the automatic update of the Detail view is deactivated for all Trace Windows

- event duplication (A429) is deactivated

- event duplication (CAN) is deactivated

- event duplication (FlexRay) is deactivated

- the buffer size of the trigger (logging).

- the update mode of the Data Window.

- the display mode of the Graphics Window.

Load Indicators | Working at High Bus Loads | Performance Problems - Causes and Remedies
