### [General/MemoryLoadPerformance] PerformanceProblems


# Performance Problems - Causes and Remedies

CANoe » Working at High Bus Loads » Performance Problems - Causes and Remedies

It depends on the configuration of your computer whether active programs running in the background cause performance problems.

Some of these programs are not visible to the user. They can be reconstructed by using the Task Manager|Processes. You have to press <Ctrl> + <Alt> + <Del>. Also you should check your start-up folder.

For a detailed analysis of performance problems you can activate performance monitoring of critical system parameters in the global options.

You can examine the active configuration options with the function performance optimization.

If this does not lead to the desired results, you can resolve the following problems on your own:

## Configuration options

| Problem | Possible Causes | Remedies |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Time management is affected | animated or 3-dimensional mouse pointer | Deactivate the mouse pointer in: Control Panel|Mouse|Pointer. |
| Running measurement is affected, data loss | Screen saver | Deactivate the screen saver in: Control Panel|Display|screen saver. |
| Power Management | Deactivate the Power Management. |
| Slow real-time measurement | Windows animation, which can be observed when windows are maximized and minimized. | Deactivate this effect, for example, with the tool TWEAK UI, which you can download from the Microsoft homepage. With this tool you can deactivate the animation under: Effects|Gen

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Interfaces] ASAMMCD3


# ASAM MCD3: Overview

CANoe » Interfaces/Add-ons » ASAM MCD3

|  | New Technology available! Merely due to compatibility reasons ASAM MCD3 via CANape is currently still available. Please use CANoe option .AMD/XCP instead. |

ASAM MCD defines interfaces for communication with the control unit at the parameter level. In CANoe, the integration of this interface is based on the CANape integration.

## Configuration

MCD modules are defined in the Options dialog (External Programs|MCD A2L Files).

## Use of the MCD Functions with CANoe

You have to install CANape on your computer in order to make use of the ASAM MCD services.CANoe employs the MCD services (MCD server) provided by CANape.

The ASAM MCD functionality is integrated in CANoe in the form of CAPL functions.

MCD functions are partly synchronous (i.e. process-blocking). To avoid interruption of a running measurement, these functions have been moved to a separate thread, thus allowing the calls to be processed asynchronously. In practice this means that no CAPL function is capable of reading events directly from an ECU. The response to an MCD request is delivered via the MCDParamResponse() handler. This function is automatically as soon as when the response from the MCD server is available. The user implements this callback in CAPL.

|  | Example The response to the following request on envVar Env_Read{ MCDGetECUParam ( "MyModule", "b0", 1);} is returned to CAPL in the following response handler MCDParamResponse (char n

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Interfaces] ASAMMCD3SystemVariables


# ASAM MCD3: Access through System Variables

CANoe » Interfaces/Add-ons » ASAM MCD3 » System variables

## Access to MCD parameters through system variables

ECU parameters available through the integration of an MCD server are not directly accessible in XML test modules, Graphics or Data Window, etc. By mapping these parameters to system variables, they can be made available there, too.

The CAPL program Nodes\MCDSysvarMapper.CAN implements the mapping of control unit parameters to system variables for both read and write access. The necessary system variables are configured in the general configuration dialog for system variables, their names being equivalent to the names of the corresponding ECU parameters. For each parameter there is a system variable of the same name in the namespaces MCD::read and MCD::write, respectively. Note that due to the nature of the asynchronous access to the MCD server, values being written may not be visible immediately as read values but only after a short delay. In particular, read system variables are not updated spontaneously when the corresponding ECU parameter changes but only cyclically according to the programmed polling rate.

The CAPL Code from the demo may be reused in other configurations by making the following adaptations:

- Change the name of the MCD module (Variable gMcdModule)

- For each ECU parameter to be mapped:

- Define a system variable in namespace MCD::read

- Define a system variable in namespace MCD::write

- Add

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Interfaces] AutosarNMObserver


# AUTOSAR NM Observer

CANoe » AUTOSAR NM Observer

## Introduction

The AUTOSAR NM Observer enables you to perform an analysis (online and offline) of the protocol behavior of control units with respect to the AUTOSAR Network Management (NM) on CAN buses. This allows you to detect incorrect behavior at an early stage.

You can activate and configure the AUTOSAR NM Observer using the NM Configuration dialog.

## Method

## Overview

The AUTOSAR NM Observer observes the bus traffic (NM messages and application messages) and attempts to determine the current state of the control units from these observed messages. This determined state is written in system variables. You can use the State Tracker to visualize the corresponding states.

For certain events, status messages are generated (e.g., if an NM message is missing) and displayed in the Trace Window.

The system variables of the Observer are in the AUTOSAR_NM name space. This name space must not be used for your own system variables. When the Observer is activated, fixed system variables (per CAN network) that reflect the observed state are created automatically.After the configuration is saved, the system variables are displayed in the System Variables Configuration dialog.

The following instances and their states are observed:

Network/Bus

| System Variables |
| --- |
| NM Mode |
| State designation | Explanation |
| Requested | At least one node sends NM messages that request the bus. |
| Prepare Sleep | No node reque

### [Interfaces] CANstress


# CANstress Access

CANoe » Interfaces/Add-ons » CANstress Access

As of CANoe 5.0 it is possible to control CANstress via CAPL functions.

|  | Caution! For using CAPL functions for CANstress knowledge with handling CANstress is required. Please read the CANstress help or documentation.CANstress CAPL functions can only be executed in a test module. Within a normal net node the CAPL CANstress functionality can't be used. |

## Mode of Operation

Since CANoe Version 7.5 SP2, the communication between CANoe and CANstress devices takes place exclusive via DLLs that are part of CANoe. There is no longer access to any installed CANstress software.

## Conditions

In order to be able to use this functionality, the following conditions must be fulfilled:

- To use the CANstress_NL.DLL within a test module, this DLL must be specified as a module for the configuration of a test module (tab: Module)

- In certain cases, a configuration of the CANstress integration is required. This is described in more detail below.

Configuration

In certain cases, e.g., when multiple CANstress devices are used in parallel, access to CANstress devices must be configured. The configuration can be made via a page in the global options of CANoe. Open the CANstress page in the Options dialog (Extensions|CANstress). The display of the page may be somewhat delayed while CANstress devices are searched for.

If you have connected a CANstress device via an RS232 interface, you must set the proper COM port her

### [Interfaces] EtherCAT


# Option .For EtherCAT

CANoe » Extensions » Option For EtherCAT

The CANoe option .For EtherCAT® allows you to operate EtherCAT®-capable devices with CANoe. For this, CANoe provides an integrated EtherCAT® master certified by Beckhoff, which handles the communication with the connected EtherCAT® strings.

The sent and received data are available in CANoe as system variables and, thus, can be stimulated, visualized, recorded and evaluated conveniently.

CANoe supports the operation of up to two master instances. That's why you can operate a maximum number of two independent EtherCAT® strings in parallel with CANoe.

## Prerequisites

## License

To use the CANoe option .For EtherCAT®, the corresponding license must be available on your system.

## Network Adapter

For the CANoe option .For EtherCAT®, a network adapter of the type Intel® PRO/1000 is required (e.g. Intel I210 or Intel I219LM).

Overview of the network adapters which are currently supported:

- 82540EM

- 82541EI, 82541ER, 82541GI, 82541GI, 82541PI

- 82545GM

- 82546EB, 82546GB

- 82547EI, 82547GI

- 82572GI, 82572PI

- 82573, 82573E, 82573L

### [Interfaces] FDXProtocol


# FDX Protocol

Installer Additional Components, Extensions and Interfaces » FDX Protocol

Ribbon: Environment Tab|Tool Couplings|XIL API/FDX

CANoe FDX (Fast Data eXchange) is a protocol that can be used for easy, fast, and real-time data exchange between CANoe and other systems via an Ethernet connection. The protocol gives other systems read/write access to system variables, environment variables, and bus signals of CANoe. In addition, it is possible to send control commands (e.g., for starting and stopping the measurement) to CANoe or to receive status information via FDX.

The other system could be a HIL system in a test bench or a computer that is to display CANoe data, for example. The protocol is based on the widely used standard protocols UDP and TCP (on the basis of IPv4 or IPv6). In the configuration settings for the FDX protocol (Configuration: XIL API & FDX Protocol) you determine, which of these standard protocols is used as the transport layer for FDX.

To enable data exchange, you must specify in CANoe before start of the measurement which signals or variables are to be exchanged and the manner in which this is to occur (e.g., with regard to grouping). This happens with the help of one or more description files whose structure must follow a particular XML-based format. The files must be specified in CANoe in Options dialog (Extensions|FDX Protocol).

Details on the FDX protocol and on the format of the description files can be found in the user manual for the

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Interfaces] FDXProtocolCouplingCANoeInstances


# Coupling of two CANoe Instances using the FDX Protocol

Installer Additional Components, Extensions and Interfaces » FDX Protocol » Coupling of two CANoe Instances using the FDX Protocol

|  | Note The coupling of two CANoe instances requires the use of UDP (IPv4 or IPv6) as transport layer. |

The CANoe FDX protocol was originally designed in such a way that the communication always starts at the FDX client. This means that the FDX client sends a UDP datagram to CANoe first, whereupon CANoe sends as response one or multiple datagrams back to the sender. The necessary protocol settings that CANoe should use for this FDX client (e. g. IP address and port number of the FDX client, byte order, FDX version) are automatically derived from the datagram of the client. This means that the CANoe configuration remains largely free from the specific protocol settings of the FDX client.

If you want to couple two CANoe instances, this procedure does not work any more because now one of the CANoe instances must send the first UDP datagram and therefore needs the specific protocol settings of the remote station. In order to still establish a coupling of two CANoe instances the following CAPL functions have been added to CANoe:

- FDXClientHandleUdp

- FDXConfigureSequenceCounting

- FDXDisableFreeRunningMode

- FDXEnableFreeRunningMode

- FDXSetByteOrder

- FDXSetProtocolVersion

|  | Example The following CAPL example sets the protocol settings for a second CANoe instance and sends cyc

### [Interfaces] FMI


# Functional Mock-up Interface (FMI)

Installer Additional Components, Extensions and Interfaces » Functional Mock-up Interface (FMI)

Ribbon: Environment Tab|Tool Couplings|Functional Mock-up Interface

The Functional Mock-up Interface (FMI) defines a standardized interface for the coupling together of complex dynamic simulation models from various modeling tools (e.g., Simulink). One of its purposes is to enable and simplify validation and testing of the interaction of components within a simulated environment.

Data exchange via the FMI takes place using so-called Functional Mock-up Units (FMUs), which must be available in the form of a ZIP file (*.fmu extension). An FMU file is essentially an XML file with the variable definitions and a mathematical description of the model. This description can exist either in the form of C code or directly as executable binary files for the respective supported platforms.

An application can act as an FMU-Provider or as an FMU-Consumer:

- FMU-Provider: provides an FMU

- FMU-Consumer: incorporates other models

There are two FMU types:

- Co-Simulation The FMU file contains all components for autonomously performing the calculation steps of the model as a function of the simulation time. This also includes the solver needed for solving a system of differential equations, if necessary.

- Model Exchange An FMU of this type only provides the equations of the model via a C interface. If the FMU describes a system of differential equation

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Interfaces] FMIConfig


# Functional Mock-up Units

Functional Mock-up Interface (FMI) » Functional Mock-up Units

Ribbon: Environment Tab|Tool Couplings|Functional Mock-up Interface

| Import | Export |  |

## Import

On tab Import you can add, edit, and remove Functional Mock-up Units (FMUs) for the configuration. CANoe accesses the variables of the FMU data model using system variables. As a result, CANoe can control the data exchange with the FMU during a simulation.

### Toolbar

- With the Add FMU button, you add one or more FMUs.

- With the Remove FMU button, you remove previously selected FMUs from the configuration.

### Configured FMUs

All FMUs of the current configuration are listed in the area on the left. General information and configurable settings of the selected FMU are displayed on the right.

## General Settings

- Step size [s] In this input box, you specify the duration (in seconds) of a calculation step of the FMU that will be called periodically during the simulation.

- Activate debug outputs With this check box, you activate or deactivate the output of debug information during the simulation.

## General Information

- File This field shows the path to the file containing the FMU packet.

- Interface version This field shows the FMI version of the FMU.Versions 1.0 and 2.0 are supported.

- FMU type This field shows the type of the FMU. Only Co-Simulation is supported at present.

- GUID This field shows the GUID of the FMU.

## Model Variables

### [Interfaces] GMLAN


# GMLAN Extension

CANoe » Interfaces/Add-ons » GMLAN Extension

## GMLAN Extension in CANoe

## ID Format

GMLAN 3.0 uses a special format for message IDs:

- Solely extended IDs are used.

- The ID consists of the fields Priority, Parameter ID (PID) and Source Address.

If a bus has a database with GMLAN messages assigned to, no extended IDs of another type can be used on this bus. Messages with standard IDs are still allowed.

## GMLAN Database

If the UseGMParameterIDs bus attribute is defined, all messages of this database are interpreted as GMLAN messages.

## Trace Window

- The columns Priority and Source Address can be displayed. Therefore you have to select the GMLAN bus type in the Column Selection part of the Trace Window configuration. The GMLAN bus type is only available if a GMLAN database exists.

- The parameter ID (PID) is displayed in the ID column.

- In fixed (display) mode all messages with the same PID and source address are displayed in a single line.

## ASCII Log

In every line that contains a GMLAN message Priority, PID and Source Address are added at the end of this line.

## Message Filter

The table on the right side of the dialog shows Priority, PID and Source Address; Priority and Source Address can be changed there. You can enter either a value or the wildcard '*'.

## Interactive Generator

The display additionally contains the GMLAN bus type. Priority, PID and Source Address are displayed and can be changed.

## Visual Sequencer

If a GMLAN

### [Interfaces] GPIB


# GPIB

Connecting to External Hardware » GPIB

GPIB is a protocol for networking external instruments. These instruments include measuring devices, power supplies and function generators. This standard was originally developed by Hewlett Packard under the name HP-IB. Later HP-IB was renamed GPIB (General Purpose Interface Bus) by the IEEE Committee as. Other equivalent names are IEEE 488 and IEC 625.

## IEEE

Since the specification does not have anything to say about device functionality, almost any instrument can be controlled with IEEE 488. The specification only defines an interface; it describes how signals are read from the bus and written to the bus. The entire bus is controlled by a Controller. This controller is generally the GPIB card installed on the host computer.

## Listener, Talker and Controller

A GPIB device is defined by three functional device types (Listener, Talker, Controller). Some controllers contain more than one type. A spectrum analyzer, for example, expects commands to sample the frequency band, and it then reports the peak value to the Controller. It is therefore both a Listener and Talker.

- The specification allows a maximum of 15 devices per bus.

- A minimal system consists of one Controller and one Talker or Listener.

## Supported Devices

|  | Note The Vector real-time platforms (e.g. VN8900 and VT6000) support only the [National Instruments GPIB-USB-B] adapter. |

The GPIB interface of CANoe is based on the API defined by the NI-488.2

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Interfaces] Memorator


# KVASER Memorator Configuration

CANoe » Interfaces/Add-ons » Kvaser Memorator

There is an external program for the configuration of the KVASER Memorator LOGGER mode.

You can start it with the following menu path: Configuration|KVASER Memorator configuration...

You'll need the latest Memorator driver to have this menu command available. If you have installed the latest Memorator driver and this menu command is still not visible, you have to add the path of this configuration tool (MemoSetup.EXE) in the CAN.INI file:

[Memorator]MemoSetup=<full-path-to-memosetup.exe>

## Offline mode, Replay, Logging Export/Conversion: KVASER Memorator LOGGER Files

The KME format is supported in offline mode and in the Replay block.

Additionally it is available for logging export/conversion.

### [Interfaces] VectorLogger


# Vector Logger

CANoe » Interfaces/Add-ons » Vector Logger

Ribbon: Tools Tab|Vector Logger

| General |
| --- |
|  | You can use some loggers as bus interface devices in CANoe. The logger is connected to the CANoe computer via Ethernet connection. Once the measurement begins, the logger sends the data to CANoe. The data can then be analyzed in the Measurement Setup. You cannot send data. You can use the two modes described below. |
| Monitoring Mode | Signal Sampling Mode |
| In Monitoring Mode, the logger transfers all incoming data such as messages and Error Frames. During the measurement, the logger does not record any data in the ring buffer. Classifications are also continued during the measurement. | In Signal Sampling Mode, the logger transfers sampled signal values of predefined signals. Each signal is mapped onto a system variable that is created in the GLLogger namespace. During the measurement, the logger continues to record data in the ring buffer. |
| General |
| You can configure the data loggers of the GL1000, GL2000, GL3000, GL4000 and GL5000 series with the Vector Logger Configurator application.If installed, you can start the Vector Logger Configurator via the Tools ribbon tab. |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Interfaces/ASAMXILAPI] ASAMXILAPI


# ASAM XIL API

Installer Additional Components, Extensions and Interfaces » ASAM XIL API

Ribbon: Environment Tab|Tool Couplings|XIL API/FDX

## General

The ASAM XIL API is a standard that specifies software interfaces for access to test systems. This API allows different test systems to be programmed in a standardized manner. Furthermore, investments in test scripts remain protected even when a change is made from one test system to another.

- Starting with CANoe version 8.5, XIL API version 2.0.1 is supported.

- Starting with CANoe version 11.0 SP3, XIL API version 2.1.0 is supported (with the exception of the Diagnostic Port).

## Function Overview

CANoe supports ASAM XIL API starting with version 8.2. The following table provides an overview of the parts of the standard that are supported in the various CANoe versions. Currently unavailable functions may be realized in future CANoe versions.

| ASAM XIL API Port | Function of the Port | Supported in CANoe (since version) | Supported in CANoe4SW |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| MA port | Reading and writing of variables | 8.2 | ✔ |
| MA port | Signal generators | 8.2 | ✔ |
| MA port | Capturing | 8.5 | ✔ |
| Diagnostic port | Access diagnostic functionality of the ECUs | 10.0 | — |
| ECU M port | — | — | — |
| ECU C port | — | — | — |
| EES port | Electric error stimulation | 8.5 | — |
| Network port | — | — | — |

Information regarding the mode of functioning and commissioning of the respective ports can be found on th

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Interfaces/ASAMXILAPI] ASAMXILAPIDiagnosticPort


# Diagnostic Port

CANoe » Interfaces/Add-ons » ASAM XIL API » Diagnostic Port

## General

Starting with version 10.0, CANoe ASAM XIL API supports the diagnostic port.

This port allows you to access the diagnostic functionality of the ECUs using a standardized interface.

## Function Overview

With the exception of the functions IDiagPortConfig::getProjectName, IDIagPortCOnfig::getVehicleInfoTable, and IEcu::ExecuteJob, all functions of the XIL API diagnostic port are supported starting with CANoe 10.0.

## Configuration in CANoe

In order to use the CANoe diagnostic port, activate XIL API support in the Options dialog. The corresponding setting can be found under Expansions |XIL API & FDX protocol. In most cases you can keep the preassigned UDP Port 3030.

## Structure of the Diagnostic Port Configuration File

To configure the diagnostic port on the tester side, you need a special configuration file in XML format. This contains the following information:

- An obligatory connection Element.This element contains the IP address of the computer on which CANoe (more precisely: the RT part of CANoe) runs.

- An optional element for controlling the application.You can use this element in order to start CANoe automatically and load a configuration file once the diagnostic port is initialized.

The following example shows what this type of configuration file might look like:

|  | Example <DiagPort> <Connection ip="127.0.0.1" xilPort="3030"/> <ApplicationControl controlMode="COM

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Interfaces/ASAMXILAPI] ASAMXILAPIEESPort


# Electrical Error Stimulation Port

CANoe » Interfaces/Add-ons » ASAM XIL API » Electrical Error Stimulation Port

## General

Starting with version 8.5, CANoe supports the EES (Electrical Error Stimulation) port of the ASAM XIL API.

This port enables you to generate electrical errors using a standardized interface. A variety of possible error types, such as interrupts and short circuits, are available to you for this.

## Hardware Requirements

A Vector VT System is required to use the CANoe EES port. This must be equipped with at least one VT1004 or VT2004. Other VT modules are not supported at present. Each connected VT1004 or VT2004 provides the possibility of disturbing four different signals independently of one another.

## Function Overview

The internal structure of a VT1004 or VT2004 channel can be represented in a simplified way with a view to the XIL API EES port as follows:

The CANoe EES port thus supports the following error types:

- Short circuit of one or more signals to Gnd, UBatt, or one of two potentials.

- Interruption of one or more signals.

These errors can be enabled in two ways:

- Statically (i.e., permanently active)

- Dynamically (i.e., only active for a certain time)

The following errors are not supported:

- PWM-controlled errors (Loose Contact errors)

- Errors with resistance (Resistor errors)

- Errors between two signals (Pin 2 Pin errors)

- Errors with load (With Load errors)

A detailed description of the different error types can 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Interfaces/ASAMXILAPI] ASAMXILAPIModelAccessPort


# Model Access Port

CANoe » Interfaces/Add-ons » ASAM XIL API » Model Access Port

## General

Starting with version 8.2, CANoe supports the MA (Model Access) port of the ASAM XIL API.

This port enables you to read and write model variables using a standardized interface. In addition, the option exists to stimulate variables with Signal Generators or to record them.

## Function Overview

Starting with CANoe version 8.5, the complete scope of functions (reading, writing, Signal Generators, and capturing) of the XIL API MA port is supported.

## Configuration in CANoe

To use the CANoe MA port, activate XIL API support in the Options dialog. The corresponding setting can be found under Extensions|XIL API & FDX Protocol. In most cases you can keep the preassigned UDP Port 3030.

By default the LightRPC XIL API backend is used. If you wish to use the FDX backend instead, you need to enable FDX in this dialog, too. You also need to specify a corresponding FDX description file.

Details for creating an FDX description can be found in Section FDX Protocol.

## Structure of the MA Port Configuration File

To configure the MA port on the tester side, you need a special configuration file in XML format. This file contains the following information:

- A mandatory connection element.This holds the IP address of the computer on which CANoe (or more precisely the RT portion of CANoe) runs, as well as the XIL API port configured in CANoe (usually 3030).

- An optional application contr

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Interfaces/CANDisturbance] AnalogDisturbance


# Analog Disturbances

CAN Disturbance Interface » Analog Disturbances

You can configure various analog disturbances with the CAN Disturbance Interface.

Analog disturbances are configured with analog components. They are represented in CANoe by system variables and are located in the namespace of the corresponding CAN Disturbance Interface. The system variables contain value tables in which the possible values for a component can be set.

|  | Example The value for the resistor of CAN_H zu Vbat/GND is to be set to 498 Ohm. @CANDisturbanceInterface<DeviceID>::Resistors::CANh_S::Value = CANDisturbanceInterface<DeviceID>::Resistors::CANh_S::Value::_498Ohm; The value is configured directly in the device and is applied to all subsequent measurements. This approach applies to all analog components. |

|  | Caution! The CAN Disturbance Interface may be destroyed by using impermissible combinations of analog components. Please read the device manual before combining components. |

## Possible Disturbances

- Short-circuit CAN_H to GND / Vbat

- Short-circuit CAN_H to CAN_L

- Short-circuit CAN_L to GND / Vbat

- Additional capacitance between CAN_H and CAN_L

- Interchange of CAN_H and CAN_L

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |

### [Interfaces/CANDisturbance] CANDisturbanceFirstSteps


# First Steps

CAN Disturbance Interface » First Steps

|  | First Steps Information about the CAN Disturbance Interface VH6501 can be found in the VH6501 hardware manual. The CAN Disturbance Interface VH6501 has two connections for inserting into a CAN bus. Connect the CAN bus to be tested to one or both connections. Assign the CAN Disturbance Interface in the Application Channel Mapping dialog to a CANoe application channel. Activate the disturbance functions of the CAN Disturbance Interface in the Network Hardware Configuration dialog. The CANDisturbanceDemo sample configuration provides an overview of the functionality of the CAN Disturbance Interface. The CAN Disturbance Interface is controlled mainly via CAPL functions and CAPL classes. |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Interfaces/CANDisturbance] ConfigTrigger


# Configuring Triggers

CAN Disturbance Interface » Configuring Triggers

You can define various triggers with the CAN Disturbance Interface:

- Software Trigger

- Frame Trigger with and without trigger enable function

- External Trigger

- Error Frame Trigger

- Scope Trigger

- Inverted Trigger

- Send Direction Trigger

- Missing Bit Trigger

## Software Trigger [▲ back]

The software trigger can be activated with the CAPL function CanDisturbanceTriggerNow. This is implemented by sending a user-defined sequence or a frame-based sequence on the bus. It is sent as soon as the sequence is configured on the CAN Disturbance Interface.

CAPL Example

## Frame trigger with and without trigger enable function [▲ back]

Use a frame trigger when a user-defined or frame-based sequence should only be sent in case of a certain frame. The frame trigger can be configured with the CAPL function CanDisturbanceTriggerEnable. There are two variants of this function.

## Variant 1: Outputting user-defined sequences

Use this variant if you want to disturb a bit in a frame, for example.

|  | Example: Disturbing the acknowledge slots of a CAN frame Proceed as follows: Set the trigger position for the frame trigger to the CRC delimiter. Configure the frame trigger for a certain frame. Define a sequence that will send a recessive signal instead of a dominant signal. Configure the trigger for the CAN Disturbance Interface. CAPL example: CanDisturbanceTriggerEnable (frame and sequence) |

## Va

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Interfaces/CANDisturbance] DigitalDisturbance


# Digital Disturbances

CAN Disturbance Interface » Digital Disturbances

You can use the CAN Disturbance Interface to output dominant or recessive signals on the CAN/CAN-FD bus in order to generate digital disturbances. A distinction is made between user-defined sequences and frame-based sequences.

## User-Defined Sequences

User-defined sequences consist of multiple segments, each of which is defined by a time period and a bus level value. The bus level of a user-defined sequence can have any of the following values:

- Dominant level ("d")

- Recessive level ("r")

- Recessive level ("R") that can overdrive a dominant level from another bus-sharing unit

In CAPL, this sequence is represented by the data type (class) CanDisturbanceSequence. A user-defined sequence can have a length of up to 4096 segments. Each segment can have a length between 1 and 65535 FPGA ticks. One FPGA tick corresponds to 6.25 ns resulting in a maximum possible segment length of approx. 409 µs.

If you want to add a segment to a sequence, you have to use the AppendToSequence function of the CanDisturbanceSequence data type. This function expects the length and the value of the segment as parameters.

|  | Example A segment is to have a length of 2 µs and a dominant bus level: CanDisturbanceSequence.AppendToSequence(320, ‘d‘); This call adds a segment to the current sequence. |

If a user-defined sequence is not to be output immediately, you can combine it with frame triggers, external triggers or a

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Interfaces/CANDisturbance] SysVarDisturbance


# System Variables

CAN Disturbance Interface » System Variables

There are device-specific system variables containing information on the current configuration status, and device-independent system variables. The system variables are available only if you have activated the disturbance functionality in the Network Hardware Configuration dialog for at least one channel.

## Device-Independent

The device-independent system variables are enumerations with the respective possible values for trigger, trigger source, DIO channels, etc.

The system variables are located in the general namespace CanDisturbance::Enums.

All system variables in this namespace are read only and have the value Invalid(-1). The sole purpose of the variables is to act as an enumerator.

They are assigned to groups in the following table. The group defines what the system variable can be used for.

| Group | System variable | Type |
| --- | --- | --- |
| ExternalTrigger | DIOChannel | Type: int R/W: read only Value table: -1: Invalid 0: DIN0 1: DIN1 |
| DIOLevel | Type: int R/W: read only Value table: -1: Invalid 0: ActiveLow 1: ActiveHigh 2: FallingEdge 3: RisingEdge |
| ErrorFrameTrigger | ErrorFramePhase | Type: int R/W: read only Value table: -1: Invalid 0: ErrorFlag 1: ErrorDelimiter |
| FrameTrigger | FieldType | Type: int R/W: read only Value table: -1: Invalid 0: SOF 1: IDBase 2: IDExtended 3: RemoteBase 4: IDE 5: RemoteExtended 6: FDF 7: Reserved 8: BRS 9: ESI 10: DLC 11-74: DataByte0 – DataByte

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Interfaces/CAPLOnBoard] CAPLOnBoard


# CAPL code execution modes for improved real time characteristics

CANoe » CAPL code execution modes for improved real time characteristics

## General

To improve the real-time characteristics of a CANoe remaining bus simulation, you can use special execution modes for CAPL code on selected network interfaces.If a CAPL node is executed in one of these modes, a restricted set of CAPL functions is available for this node.

| Hardware | Mode |
| --- | --- |
| VN1630/VN1630A | CAPL-on-Board |
| VN1630 log | CAPL-on-Board |
| VN1640/VN1640A | CAPL-on-Board |
| VN3300 | CAPL-on-Board |
| VN3600 | CAPL-on-Board |
| VN7570 | CAPL-on-Board |
| VN7600 | CAPL-on-Board |

## Configuration

The CAPL node's configuration dialog contains an option to specify where the node is to be executed.The consistency check to make sure that the allocated buses are all connected to a single, appropriate hardware interface is not carried out until the measurement starts (real time mode).

## CAPL-on-Board

### Overview

The network nodes of a CANoe remaining bus simulation can be executed directly on a standard Vector network interface. This has the advantage of lower latency times, especially with USB devices. It thus provides a simple way to execute code under real time conditions.

### Differences in Comparison to Distributed Mode / VN8900 Standard Operation

- PC connectivity and power supply via USB

- More affordable than an RT box or rack

- The runtime environment offers fewer functions than 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Interfaces/CarMaker] CarMaker


# CarMaker Interface

Installer Additional Components, Extensions and Interfaces » CarMaker Interface

Ribbon: Environment Tab|Tool Couplings|CarMaker Interface

|  | Note To use the CarMaker Interface in CANoe, the CarMaker component must be installed. This component is included in the setup of CANoe. In the setup folder Installer Additional Components you find the corresponding setup files. Open Setup Folder |

The CarMaker interface consists of a configuration dialog and a modeling library (DLL file).

The DLL file must be configured as a component at a simulation node and provides special CAPL Functions for communication with CarMaker.

Using the configuration dialog you can create different groups of CarMaker Quantities. You can subscribe to these groups for monitoring using their names. During the measurement, you can switch between the configured groups, whereby only the last subscribed group is active.

You can also configure the cycle time for the periodic update of the internal state of the CarMaker interface in this dialog.

## Configuration

How to set up the data exchange between CANoe and CarMaker:

1. Select a CAPL network node in the Simulation Setup for the communication with the CarMaker interface. Add the CarMaker component (CarMakerControl.vmodule) from the Exec64 directory to this node and create a CAPL file.

2. Open the CarMaker configuration dialog and enter the host name or IP address of the CarMaker host computer and the name of the user running Car

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Interfaces/ExtendedRealTime] ERTNetworknodes


# ERT Network Nodes

Extended Real Time » ERT Network Nodes

ERT network nodes are executed in the Extended Real Time area of a supported Vector Hardware.

## Configuration

Before adding ERT network nodes to a CANoe configuration you must enable the option Activate ERT network nodes in the CANoe Options dialog|Performance|Extended Real Time (ERT). After that you can add ERT network nodes to the simulation setup e.g. via the context menu of the bus.

Configurations containing an ERT network node can only be executed under the following conditions:

- Execution on Vector Hardware that supports ERT

- The distributed mode must be activated

- Real Bus Mode must be activated

## Supported Features

ERT network nodes support following features:

- CAPL programs: If a CAPL program is attached to an ERT network node it is automatically compiled for and executed in ERT. For a list of supported CAPL features in ERT see CAPL in ERT.

- MATLAB/Simulink models: MATLAB DLLs compiled for ERT can be added in the node configuration dialog on the Components page.

- CANoe Interaction Layer: If a Network node from the database is configured, the Interaction Layer is automatically taken from the database. In the node configuration dialog on the Components page DLLs with CANoe Interaction Layer can also be added manually.

## Execution Concept

Every ERT network node runs as a real-time thread in the Extended Real Time part. The priorities of the threads can be controlled in the node configura

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Interfaces/ExtendedRealTime] ExtendedRealTime


# Extended Real Time (ERT)

Installer Additional Components, Extensions and Interfaces » Extended Real Time (ERT)

With the feature Extended Real Time as a part of the Vector Tool Platform the latency and determinism of Vector tools like CANoe and CANape are improved. To achieve this, the device is logically divided into two areas. A new area provides Extended Real Time in which predefined functions can be executed under real-time conditions.

## Configuration

Activate Extended Real Time via Home ribbon tab|Group Measurement|Vector Tool Platform|Tool Platform|General.

## Supported Hardware

Extended Real Time is supported by the Vector Hardware VN8912A, VT6051A, VN8911, VN8914 and RT Rack.

If ERT is enabled Vector Hardware uses one Ethernet adapter exclusively for ERT. See the IP settings in the Platform Manager to identify the correct adapter.

The Plug-in modules VN8970 and VN8972 as well as the interface card VN7572 are supported in Extended Real Time (CAN / CAN FD only).

## VT-System

If Extended Real Time is activated you benefit from an improved accuracy and higher sampling rates with VT6000-modules. See Extended Real Time (VT System) for further information.

## ERT Network Nodes

Custom user code (e.g. CAPL or MATLAB models) can be executed in Extended Real Time using ERT Network Nodes.

## Performance Monitor

To monitor the performance in Extended Real Time you can use the Performance Monitor.

## System Variables

System variables are supported in Extended Rea

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Interfaces/IOPiggy] IOPiggy


# IOpiggy

Connecting to External Hardware » IOpiggy

|  | The IOpiggy is a further piggyback module for the VN8900 and VN7570, complementing the CANpiggy and LINpiggy. This piggyback makes measuring and generating I/O signals simple and easy.Because it is integrated into the VN8900/VN7570, you don't need any additional hardware to measure and stimulate I/O signals. The IOpiggy has 8 digital inputs and outputs, 4 analog connections and one connection to capture and output PWM signals. For further technical information, see Technical Data or the user manual. In CANoe, I/O signals are manipulated and measured using system variables. This means that measurement values are readily available for analysis. |

### [Interfaces/IOPiggy] IOPiggyUseCases


# IOpiggy: Use Cases

IOpiggy » Use Cases

For the following examples, the Standard mode has been set for IOpiggy1.

|  | Example 1: Digital output on DOUT0 // Set High Level@sysvar::IO::IOPiggy1::DOUT0 = 1; ... // Set Low Level@sysvar::IO::IOPiggy1::DOUT0 = 0; |

|  | Example 2: Analog output on AOUT0 // Set Voltage to 2.5@sysvar::IO::IOPiggy1::AOUT0 = 2.5; |

|  | Example 3: PWM output // Set Output Frequency to 2000Hz@sysvar::IO::IOPiggy1::PWMOutFreq = 2000; // Set Duty Cycle to 75%@sysvar::IO::IOPiggy1::PWM = 75; |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Interfaces/MATLAB] MATLAB


# MATLAB Integration Package

Installer Additional Components, Extensions and Interfaces » MATLAB Integration Package

Ribbon: Environment Tab|Tool Couplings|MATLAB Integration Package

| General | Further Information |
| --- | --- |
| This Help describes the CANoe MATLAB Integration Package. It was designed to combine the strengths of both simulation tools. CANoe is a total network simulation tool designed to both model nodes on a network and the network that binds them. Traditionally the functionality of these network nodes are modeled using the CAPL language provided with CANoe. The purpose of this interface is to extend CANoe’s node modeling capability by adding the strength of the MATLAB/Simulink environment. Now models can be used to describe the functionality of a network node in CANoe. This allows CANoe to simulate nodes which are impossible or difficult to model using CAPL alone. Another feature would be to use a Simulink model to generate an outside stimulus to an existing CAPL node. | The MATLAB Integration Package provides different Execution Modes: Simulation Models Offline Mode Synchronized Mode Hardware-In-The-Loop (HIL) Mode Analysis Models Utilities Troubleshooting MATLAB Integration Package Version History AN-IND-1-007_Using_MATLAB_with_CANoe.pdf |
| Modeling Concepts | Setup |
| It is important to understand how the MATLAB Integration Package communicates with CANoe in order to easily design a model. It is easiest to think of the CANoe MATLAB Integration P

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Interfaces/MATLAB] MATLABExecutionModes


# Execution Modes

MATLAB Integration Package » Execution Modes

## Simulation Models

Simulations utilizing this interface can be operated in three different modes:

## 1. Offline Mode: Running a simulation in MATLAB/Simulink.

This is the offline simulation mode. It does not provide hardware access.

In this mode the simulation runs in the MATLAB/Simulink environment. CANoe operates in slave mode whereas MATLAB/Simulink is the simulation Master. This is the mode for non-real-time simulation. The simulation is controlled from the MATLAB/Simulink environment. CANoe's Slave mode is a special simulation mode whereby CANoe is simulating the (CAN) bus and takes its measurement time base from outside (here: from Simulink). This is especially useful in early design stages. Debugging features of the MATLAB environment can be used. Communication between MATLAB/Simulink and CANoe takes place over Microsoft DCOM for configuration and shared memory for data exchange. You cannot perform real-time simulation with this kind of simulation.

## 2. Synchronized Mode: Running a simulation in MATLAB/Simulink synchronized with CANoe

This is the synchronized simulation mode which provides real hardware access.

In this mode the simulation runs in the MATLAB/Simulink environment. In contrast to the Offline mode the CANoe time base is used in MATLAB/Simulink. Therefore the simulation runs in almost real-time (typical resolution is about 1ms depending on the model). It is necessary that MATLAB/Sim

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Interfaces/MATLAB] MATLABtroubleshooting


# Troubleshooting

MATLAB Integration Package » Troubleshooting

## Unusable CANoe GUI during offline simulation

It can happen that the CANoe user interface doesn’t update correctly or isn’t usable at all when operation in offline mode. If so, open the CAN.INI configuration file and verify that the following entry is set to zero:

[System] GUIThreadPriority=0

The default setting for this entry is –3 which lowers the priority of the CANoe GUI.

## Unable to compile the nlapml.CPP source file.

If the compilation of the source file fails with the following error:

error C2117: 'test_for_long_filename_init' : array bounds overflow

you’ve activated the export of CAPL functions in the code generation options and the file name of your model is longer than 44 characters. If you really need the CAPL functions save your model under a shorter name. If you don’t need the additional CAPL functions, deactivate the export in the CN code generation options.

## Model Advisor Checks not available

After installation of the CANoe MATLAB Integration Package the Simulink Model Advisor should contain a new folder named CANoe MATLAB Integration Checks below the By Product section. The CANoe checks are created by a file named sl_customization.m located in the folder $(My Documents)\Matlab\CANoe\Samples. Please make sure that this folder is included in the MATLAB path list (menu path File|Set Path). MATLAB updates all customizations if the command slcustomize is called. Please make sure that yo

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Interfaces/MATLAB] MATLAButilities


# Utilities for Configuration

MATLAB Integration Package » Utilities for Configuration

## Setup of signal and environment blocks

To specify database name, node name, message name and signal name of the Signal Input and Signal Output block you can choose from a list. To specify an system/environment variable name for a block you can also choose from a list of available variables.

A double-click on the Signal Input / Signal Output block will bring up the dialog.

## New Features in Version 7.0:

- Support of distributed object functions and structs in HIL mode.

- vModules can now be used in Communication Setup.

## New Features in Version 6.5:

- Support of XCP in MATLAB Release R2020a.

## New Features in Version 6.4:

- Support of distributed objects (data and events) in HIL mode.

- Model generation can now create a file of the type *.vmodule.

- Models can now be built as a 64-bit DLL.

- Arrays of type int and double supported in ERT.

- Support of Visual Studio 2019 in MT mode.

- Support of MATLAB Release R2020a.

- Support of system variables of the type Double Array and Integer Array in ERT DLLs.

## New Features in Version 6.3:

- Filter option to exclude blocks from parametrization.

- Export CAPL Functions option support for ERT.

- Support of Microsoft Visual Studio 2019.

- Support of MATLAB Release R2019b.

## New Features in Version 6.2:

- Support of parameterization in Extended Real Time DLLs.

- CAPL function that returns the name of the model.

- Support of MATLAB Release R2019a.

## New Features in Version 6.1:

- Support of c

### [Interfaces/MATLAB/CANoeCOlibrary] MATLABcoLib


# Communication Object Library

MATLAB Integration Package » Communication Object Library

The Communication Objects library is a collection of S-Functions blocks.

The library provides the following features:

| Communication Objects | Short Description of the Block |
| --- | --- |
| CO Function Answer | Can be used to define the behavior and data of an automatic function return. |
| CO Function Call | Is used to execute a function call. |
| CO Function Calling | Can be used to trigger Simulink subsystems on function call. |
| CO Function Return | Can be used to trigger Simulink subsystems on function return. |
| CO Latest Call | Is used to get the data of the latest function call. |
| CO Latest Return | Is used to get the data of the latest function return. |
| CO Signal Input | Is the basic input block for a communication object signal. |
| CO Signal Output | Is the basic output block for a communication object signal. |
| CO Signal Struct Input | Is used for input the signal of type Struct. |
| CO Signal Struct Output | Is used for output the signal of type Struct. |

## Union Data Type

A Union data type, as part of a signal struct, is represented in Simulink as a bus with all union elements. Additional, the first bus parameter represents the current union discriminator.

- a discriminator value of 0 means that none of the union elements is valid.

- a discriminator value larger 0 means that the according union element is valid.

This implicit discriminator is always av

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Interfaces/MATLAB/CANoeCOlibrary] MATLABcoLibFunctionAnswer


# CO Function Answer

MATLAB Integration Package » Communication Object Library » CO Function Answer

| Description | The CO Function Answer block can be used to define the behavior and data of an automatic function return. |
| --- | --- |
| Parameters | Data type Data type of the function answer. The data type must be a bus, which corresponds to the selected function data. The bus must contain elements for all input/output parameters, all output parameters, the function result, the server simulator mode and the return delay.The element for an input/output parameter must be a subbus with two elements: the parameter value and the behavior. The behavior can be set to 0 to return the data defined in parameter value element or to 1 to use the data which was set on function call.The server simulator mode can be set to 0 for an automatic function answer, to 1 for a manual function answer or to 2 to discard the function call. The return delay can be used to set a reply time in nanoseconds. Select function Specify function port from dialog. Create bus Generates a bus and necessary subbuses, which corresponds to the selected function port. |
| Availability | HIL (Singletasking) | HIL (Multitasking) | Offline / Synchronized | Simulation Models | AnalysisModels |
| • | • | • | • | • |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |

# CO Latest Call

MATLAB Integration Package » Communication Object Library » CO Latest Call

| Description | The CO Latest Call block is used to get the data of the latest function call. |
| --- | --- |
| Parameters | Sample Time The settings are according to Simulink semantics:0 = continuous sample time-1 = inherited sample timeContinuous sample time is set as default. If the Latest Call block is used in a triggered subsystem for example, the sample time must be set to inherited because only constant blocks or blocks with an inherited sample time are allowed in triggered subsystems. Data type Data type of the functional call. The data type must be a bus, which corresponds to the selected function call data. The bus must contain elements for all input parameters of the function, all input/output parameters and the request identifier. Select function Specify function port from dialog. Create bus Generates a bus and necessary subbuses, which corresponds to the selected function port. |
| Availability | HIL (Singletasking) | HIL (Multitasking) | Offline / Synchronized | Simulation Models | AnalysisModels |

| Description | The CO Latest Return block is used to get the data of the latest function return. |
| --- | --- |
| Parameters | Sample Time The settings are according to Simulink semantics:0 = continuous sample time-1 = inherited sample timeContinuous sample time is set as default. If the Latest Return block is used in a triggered subsystem for example, the sample time must be set to inherited because only constant blocks or blocks with an inherited sample time are allowed in triggered subsystems. Data type Data type of the function return. The data type must be a bus, which corresponds to the selected function data. The bus must contain elements for all output parameters of the function, the request identifier and the result value of the function. In case the result value is defined as void the bus must not contain a result value element. Select function Specify function port from dialog. Create bus Generates a bus and necessary subbuses, which corresponds to the selected function port. |

If the CO Signal Input block is used in a triggered subsystem for example, the sample time must be set to inherited because only constant blocks or blocks with an inherited sample time are allowed in triggered subsystems. Signal value Specifies whether the signal exists in the Simulink-model as a raw value, implementation value or physical value. Data type Data type of the Output port of the block. For the inherited data type, the data type is derived from the blocks connected to the block. For the DB type data type, the data type from the database is used and the Simulink data type that fits best is used. Select signal Specify signal from dialog. |

### [Interfaces/MATLAB/CANoeDOlibrary] MATLABdoLib


# Distributed Object Library

MATLAB Integration Package » Distributed Object Library

The Distributed Objects library is a collection of S-Functions blocks.

The library provides the following features:

| Distributed Objects | Short Description of the Block |
| --- | --- |
| DO Data Input | Is the basic input block for a DO data or event member. |
| DO Data Output | Is the basic output block for a DO data or event member. |
| DO Function Call | Can be used to execute a function call. |
| DO Function Calling | Can be used to trigger Simulink subsystems on function call. |
| DO Function Return | Can be used to trigger Simulink subsystems on function return. |
| DO Latest Call | Is used to get the data of the latest function call. |
| DO Latest Return | Is used to get the data of the latest function return. |
| DO Struct Input | Is used for input data of type Struct. |
| DO Struct Output | Is used for output data of type Struct. |

## Union Data Type

A Union data type, as part of a signal struct, is represented in Simulink as a bus with all union elements. Additional, the first bus parameter represents the current union discriminator.

- a discriminator value of 0 means that none of the union elements is valid.

- a discriminator value larger 0 means that the according union element is valid.

This implicit discriminator is always available regardless whether the struct contains an explicit discriminator value or not.

## String Data Type

A String data type is represented i

[...truncated for brevity...]

| --- | --- |
| Parameters | Sample Time Continuous sample time is set as default. If the DO Data Input block is used in a triggered subsystem for example, the sample time must be set to inherited because only constant blocks or blocks with an inherited sample time are allowed in triggered subsystems.The settings are according to Simulink semantics: 0 = continuous sample time -1 = inherited sample time Data member value Specifies whether the data member exists in the Simulink model as a raw value, implementation value or physical value. Data type Data type of the output port of the block. For the inherited data type, the data type is derived from the blocks connected to the block. Select data member Specify DO data member from the Symbol Selection dialog. |
| Availability | HIL (Singletasking) | HIL (Multitasking) | Offline / Synchronized | Simulation Models | AnalysisModels |

### [Interfaces/MATLAB/CANoeIOLibrary] MATLABioLib


# CANoe I/O Library

MATLAB Integration Package » CANoe I/O Library

The CANoe I/O library is a collection of S-Functions blocks.

The library provides the following features:

- Data I/O

- Trigger sources

- Event sinks

- Model execution control

- CANoe simulation control

- Calibration using the XCP Protocol

- Convenience functions for building models

| I/O Library | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Signal Input | The Signal Input block is the basic input block for a database signal. |
| Signal Output | The Signal Output block is the basic output block for a database signal. |
| Signal Block | This block allows the user to collect a number of Signal Input and Signal Output blocks into one subsystem and configure them by one step. |
| Bus Block | This block allows the user to connect to a Simulink signal bus. This eases the handling of many input and/or output signal. |
| Message Input | The Message Input block serves as input block for all signals of a database message. The values of the signals are output by the block either as a vector or as a Simulink signal bus. |
| Message Output | The Message Output block serves as output block for all signals of a database message. It contains the values of signals either as a vector or as a Simulink signal bus. |
| Simulation Step | The Simulation Step block is used for synchronization and data exchange between CANoe and Simulink. |
| Analysis Step | The Analysis Step block is used for synchronization and data exchange between CA

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Interfaces/MATLAB/CANoeIOLibrary] MATLABioLibAnalysisStep


# Analysis Step

MATLAB Integration Package » CANoe I/O Library » Analysis Step

|  | Note Please remind that no outputs except the System Variable Output blocks can be used together with this block. |  |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Description | The Analysis Step block is used for synchronization and data exchange between CANoe and Simulink. You must place this block in your model if the model should be used for analysis purposes. CANoe will start the simulation in Simulink when the model is configured in the Simulink Analysis Model settings and the measurement is started. |  |
| Parameters | — |  |

### [Interfaces/MATLAB/CANoeIOLibrary] MATLABioLibBusBlock


# Bus Block

MATLAB Integration Package » CANoe I/O Library » Bus Block

| Description | This block allows the user to connect to a Simulink signal bus. This eases the handling of many input and/or output signal. A MATLAB GUI supports the assignment of a Simulink bus signal to a specific CANoe signal. The Bus Block is a subsystem containing a Simulink bus creator and/or bus selector. By the usage of these blocks the bus signals are mapped to Signal Input and/or Signal Output blocks within the subsystem. |  |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Parameters | Add Signal Output Adds Signal Output blocks to the subsystem. Add Signal Input Adds Signal Input blocks to the subsystem. Delete All Removes all signals. Signal values Specifies whether the signal exists in the Simulink model as raw values or physical values. A change to the setting affects all signals configured in this block. Data type Data types of the individual signals in the bus signals. The only permissible data type here is inherited, i.e., the data types of the individual signals are derived from the connected blocks. |  |
| Availability | HIL (Singletasking) | HIL (Multitasking) | Offline / Synchronized | Simulation Models | Analysis Models |
| • | • | • | • | • |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |

### [Interfaces/MATLAB/CANoeIOLibrary] MATLABioLibConnectOpenPorts


# Connect Open Ports

MATLAB Integration Package » CANoe I/O Library » Connect Open Ports

| Description | The Connect Open Ports block is a convenience function for creating a model. If you place this block in your model and double-click on it, all open ports on the top level of the model will be connected to system variable input or output blocks (depending on whether it is an inport or outport). For each of these blocks, a new system variable with name <modelName>::<blockName>_<portNumber> is created in CANoe. Note that these system variables are not persistent, i.e., you have to export your CANoe configuration to an XML file, otherwise they are lost when you close the configuration, and running the model in Offline or Synchronized Mode will fail. On HIL mode, the system variables are stored in the created DLL, thus exporting them into an XML file is not necessary. Moreover, the functionality is equivalent to the option CreateSystem Variable blocks for unconnected ports in the CN code generation options, i.e., if that option is activated, no Connect Open Ports block is required. |  |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Parameters | — |  |
| Availability | HIL (Singletasking) | HIL (Multitasking) | Offline / Synchronized | Simulation Models | Analysis Models |
| • | • | • | • | • |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |

If the Environment Input block e.g. in a triggered subsystem, the sample time must be set to inherited because only constant blocks or blocks with inherited Sample Time are allowed in triggered subsystems. Data type Data type of the second Output port of the block. For the inherited data type, the data type is derived from the blocks connected to the block. For the DB type data type, the data type from the database is used and the Simulink data type that fits best is used. Select variable Specify environment variable from database. |  |
| Availability | HIL (Singletasking) | HIL (Multitasking) | Offline / Synchronized | Simulation Models | Analysis Models |

CAN Channel If the MATLAB model is attached to a gateway node in CANoe this setting defines the CAN channel on which the XCP communication takes place. # protobufOSI

| --- | --- | --- |
| Parameters | First Parameter (buffer) ByteArray containing the OSI3 sensor data serialized with Google Protocol Buffer. Second Parameter (BufferSize) Length of the buffer in byte. Third Parameter (UpdateFlag) The data will only be transferred if the update flag is set to 1. It is recommended to set the flag to 1 only if the data has actually changed. Fourth Parameter (DataType) The DataType indicates which message type the data to be read in is from. The possible values are: 0 = Sensor Data 1 = Ground Truth Data |  |

### [Interfaces/MATLAB/ModelingConcepts] MATLABmcApplication


# Application

MATLAB Integration Package » Modeling Concepts » Application

## Behavior

CAPL lets you define the basic application behavior of an ECU with special emphasis on its functional bus behavior.

## Interfaces

The application of an ECU has two major interfaces:

- The interface to bus signals

- The interface to peripheral I/Os like sensor and actuators. In CANoe the interface to I/Os is modeled using system variables.

ECUs with simple application logic (e.g. some truth tables, simple conditional behavior, simple calculations) are usually modeled directly in CAPL.

ECUs with complex application behavior may be modeled with tools like MATLAB/Simulink and Stateflow using the signal input and output blocks as interfaces between the application and interaction layers. System variable input and output blocks are used as interfaces to the sensors and actuators. An additional advantage of this approach is that application models can be used to generate code for the real target, too.

See Also

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Interfaces/MATLAB/ModelingConcepts] MATLABmcInteractionLayer


# Interaction Layer

MATLAB Integration Package » Modeling Concepts » Interaction Layer

## Introduction

An important part of the software located between an ECU's application layer and lower-level functions is the Interaction Layer (IL) with its Signal Interface. It is important because applications deal with named signals (bus signals) instead of their networked representation in bits and bytes of the data stream. This Interaction Layer performs mapping between the signals and their network representations.

## CANoe Interaction Layer

The Vector CANoe Interaction Layer (in short CANoe IL) provides a signal-oriented means of accessing the bus. The CANoe IL also performs mapping of signals to their send messages and controls the sending of these send messages as a function of the Send Model. Different Send Models are defined depending on the network type (OEM), and a special CANoe IL is provided to each of these Send Models.

It is possible to map model outputs and inputs directly to signals. This mapping causes the changed value of the model to be routed directly to the specific CANoe IL responsible for sending the value out and also the model’s input is stimulated with the changed value.

## Definition of Concepts

The Interaction Layer is a communication module responsible for separation of the low-level driver (a CAN Driver for example) which is dependent on the Data Link Layer and the application task which is independent of the underlying bus system.

Specifically th

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Interfaces/MATLAB/ModelingConcepts] MATLABmcMiddleware


# Middleware

MATLAB Integration Package » Modeling Concepts » Middleware

## Introduction

Apart from the application behavior of an ECU, there are parts of the software which are identical for each network node, such as interaction layers (IL), network management services (NM), diagnostic services and transport protocols (TP).

These layers are available as CANoe modeling libraries for all major OEMs.

NM and TP layers are available as CANoe DLLs; the IL is generated automatically as CAPL code. The IL and the modeling of the application are described in more detail in subsequent chapters.

## Definition of Concepts

Middleware is software that functions as a conversion or translation layer. It is also a consolidator and integrator. For decades custom-programmed middleware solutions have been developed to enable one application to communicate with another application that either runs on a different platform or comes from a different vendor or both. Today, a diverse group of products exists offering packaged middleware solutions.

See Also

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Interfaces/MATLAB/ModelingConcepts] MATLABmcNetworkManagementLayer


# Network Management Layer

MATLAB Integration Package » Modeling Concepts » Network Management Layer

## Definition of Concepts

The availability of the CAN bus is handled by network management. Typical features include:

- Identifying the network configuration at start-up

- Monitoring the network configuration while the bus system is running

- Synchronizing transition of all network nodes to bus sleep mode (power saving mode if CAN network is not needed)

- Controlling peripheral hardware (CAN Controller and Bus Transceiver)

- Providing network-relevant status information

- Error recovery after bus-off

Each ECU is identified by a unique station number and has a special CAN message identifier for exchanging network-relevant information. This message contains identification of the transmitting node (encoded in the CAN identifier), the address of the receiving node as well as the message type and additional sleep flags.

|  | Cross References for Network Management (NM) Network Management AUTOSAR NM Modeling Libraries (Standard Modeling Libraries) OSEK NM |

See Also

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Interfaces/MATLAB/ModelingConcepts] MATLABmcSoftwareLayers


# Software Layers of an ECU

MATLAB Integration Package » Modeling Concepts » Software Layers of an ECU

CANoe's Simulation Setup lets you define the network nodes for a given (CAN) network. The functional behavior of these nodes can be described in CAPL (Communication Access Programming Language) which is a C style programming language. CAPL was designed to facilitate the modeling of a network node's basic logic (behavior). CAPL allows you to directly integrate the specification of the CAN communication matrix into the programming syntax.

See Also

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Interfaces/MATLAB/ModelingConcepts] MATLABmcTransportLayer


# Transport Layer

MATLAB Integration Package » Modeling Concepts » Transport Layer

## Definition of Concepts

Some information to be transmitted over the CAN bus does not fit into a single data frame because the data length exceeds 8 bytes. In such cases the sender has to split up the data into several CAN messages with the same identifier. Additional information is necessary to re-assemble the data in the receiver. This is performed by the Transport Layer:

- Segmentation and reassembly of data that are larger than the underlying Data Link Layer

- Flow control for single messages

- Error recognition

A Transport Layer is not only needed for diagnosis purposes but also for any large data which must be exchanged between different nodes, e.g. text information to be displayed on a dashboard.

See Also | Transport Protocol

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Interfaces/MATLAB/Setup] MATLABSetupConfigTest


# Configuration and Test of CANoe Simulink Blocks

MATLAB Integration Package » Setup » Configuration and Test of CANoe Simulink Blocks

## Signal Block Configuration

CANoe signals can normally be identified by four parameters:

- Database name

- Node name (the node which sends the message)

- Message name (the message containing the signal)

- Signal name

By double clicking CANoe signal blocks a selection dialog appears containing all signals of the current CANoe configuration. The dialog also sets the parameters of the signal block so that it uses the currently selected signal.

## Test of CANoe Blocks

The CANoe MATLAB Integration Package provides custom checks for the Simulink Model Advisor. Please make sure that CANoe has opened a configuration containing all databases used by the model before running these checks. The Model Advisor will report all CANoe blocks which are not configured correctly.

By following the links of erroneous blocks in the Model Advisor report, Simulink opens the correct sub system and highlights the block. Using the Model Advisor it is very easy to find all CANoe blocks that won’t work properly with the current CANoe configuration.

The Model Advisor checks you can run automatically during compilation if you activate the Check CANoe block configuration at compilation check box in the CN code generation options page.

See Also

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Interfaces/MATLAB/Setup] MATLABSetupCustCalXCP


# Customizing for Calibration using XCP

MATLAB Integration Package » Setup » Customizing for Calibration using XCP

In this chapter all steps are described which are necessary to enable XCP calibration for a compiled model. It is assumed that the model uses the CANoe target cn.TLC.

|  | Note Either the calibration or the parameterization feature can be used but not both at the same time. |

## Simulink Settings

The CANoe MATLAB Integration Package requires the following settings of the Simulink model. Open the menu path Simulation | Configuration Parameters to open the settings dialog.

## Optimization

The Inline Parameters option on the Optimization page must be enabled. It is also recommended to disable the Block Reduction setting.

## Simulink Block Settings

All parameters must be defined as ASAP2.Parameter in the MATLAB Workspace to appear in the generated A2L file. If the model contains the XCP Calibration block all Workspace parameter referenced by the model will be converted to ASAP2.Parameter objects automatically during the code generation process.

## Code Generation

During the code generation process a calibration database file (A2L) is generated. It can be found in the code generation subfolder next to the MATLAB model:

MODEL_NAME_cn_rtw\MODEL_NAME.A2L

A calibration tool like CANape can use the Linker Address Map file to update the addresses in the A2L file after the model was built. The Map file is typically located in the Release folder:

MODEL_NAME_cn_

# Customizing for Interaction with CANoe

MATLAB Integration Package » Setup » Customizing for Interaction with CANoe

This page describes the steps which are necessary to run a MATLAB model together with a CANoe configuration. It is recommendable to start CANoe and open the desired configuration at first. If a new configuration should be created consider using the Model Generation Wizard (see Configuration Setup from Scratch). The reason is that CAN signals or environment variables can be selected easily in the Simulink model if a CANdb is present.

It may be helpful to copy the Simulink model file (MDL) into the same folder as the CANoe configuration (CFG file). If you want to use the generated DLL, create a subfolder named Exec32 for example. The Simulink Coder can be configured to copy the DLL into this folder.

## Simulink Settings

The CANoe MATLAB Integration Package needs some settings of the Simulink model which are described here. Open the menu path Simulation|Configuration Parameters to open the settings dialog.

|  | Note: Improvement performance when exchanging many signals If a large number of signals are to be exchanged between CANoe and the Simulink model, not for every signal should be used a Signal Input block or Signal Output block. Instead, the signals should be collected in one Signal block or a Bus block. This improves the performance of the data exchange between CANoe and the Simulink model. |

### [Interfaces/MATLAB/Setup] MATLABSetupCustMultitasking


# Customizing for Multitasking Environment

MATLAB Integration Package » Setup » Customizing for Multitasking Environment

In this page all steps are described which are necessary for running the Simulink model in multitasking mode. In this mode, each sample time of the model is a separate task. The generated DLL emulates a simple processor where these tasks can preempt each other.

## System Requirements

- MATLAB R2014a or newer is required.

- The only supported compilers are the Microsoft Visual C++ 2013 (12.0) Professional compiler, the Microsoft Visual C++ 2017 (15.0) Professional compiler and the Microsoft Visual C++ 2019 (16.0) Professional compiler. In MATLAB, type mex –setup to configure your compiler.

- DLLs built with Multitasking support require a CANoe version starting with 8.5.

## Simulink Settings

To activate the multitasking mode in a Simulink model, open the Configuration Parameters dialog of your Simulink Model, go to the Solver page, and set the following settings:

- Periodic sample time constraint: Specified

- Sample time properties: Set your sample times here

- MATLAB R2014a – R2016a: Tasking mode for periodic sample times: MultiTasking

- MATLAB R2016b or newer: Treat each discrete rate as separate task: on

For details about how to set the sample time properties, see the Simulink help. (You can open it with a right click on the item in the dialog, then click What’s This?).

## Behavior of the Multitasking Mode

A DLL built with the activated mul

[...truncated for brevity...]

The Inline Parameters option on the Optimization page defines which model parameters are accessible. If the option is deactivated, all parameters of every Simulink block will be accessible. Use workspace variables and assign them to block parameters. Activate the Inline parameters option and click the Configure button. Add all workspace variables which should be configurable after compilation. This is the recommended setting for optimal speed of the compiled model.

## Simulink Coder Settings

Select the C-API interface for data exchange on the Interface page:

Enable the options Export CAPL Functions and Enable Parameterization from CANoe on the CN code generation page:

Build the model with the new settings. The output folder will contain the model DLL and the parameterization file (named model

### [Interfaces/MATLAB/Setup] MATLABSetupIntroduction


# Introduction

MATLAB Integration Package » Setup » Introduction

## System Requirements

The CANoe MATLAB Integration Package requires the following minimum product versions:

- CANoe 7.5

- MATLAB® R2012b 64 bit

- Visual C++® 10.0

## Setup

You can start the setup for the MATLAB Integration Package with help of the following hyperlink: Open Setup Folder

## File Locations

After installation you should find the following path in your MATLAB environment: $(MATLABROOT)\rtw\c\canoe\devices

CANoe MATLAB Integration Package files (canoe\*.*) are located in the following directory:$(MATLABROOT)\rtw\c

The sample files (CANoe configurations and Simulink models) are located in the following directory: Open Directory

Here you find descriptions for the MATLAB sample configurations.

## MATLAB / Compiler Versions

MATLAB needs to be associated with your C compiler in order to generate the DLL files for the HIL mode. See your MATLAB Simulink Coder documentation for a correct setup of the compiler environment. Typically the command mex – setup (for MATLAB Version up to and including R2013b) or mex – setup C++ (starting with MATLAB Version R2014a) must be run from the command window. The following compilers are currently supported:

| 2010 (10.0), Professional | 2012 (11.0), Professional | 2013 (12.0), Professional | 2015 (14.0), Professional | 2017 (15.0), Professional | 2019 (16.0), Professional |
