### [Interfaces/MATLAB/Setup] MATLABSetupModelRef


# Model Referencing

MATLAB Integration Package » Setup » Model Referencing

## Introduction

Using MATLAB Version R14 or newer it is possible to use model references. Every model may contain one or more instances of a referenced model which in turn may also contain references to other models. The model block which realizes the referencing can be found in the Simulink library. This feature allows a modular development of Simulink models and might be used to separate CANoe input/output blocks from application blocks (see next chapter). The CANoe MATLAB Integration Package supports Model Referencing but some restrictions apply.

|  | Note When building large models with referenced models that are used in simulation nodes (i.e., in any ECU of the Simulation Setup), deactivate the setting Target 64 bit CANoe, even if you intend to use a 64 bit CANoe. The reason is that in a 64 bit CANoe, the simulation part still runs in 32 bit. As Simulink does not know whether a referenced model is built for a simulation or an analysis model, it has to build it in 32 bit and in 64 bit if the check box is activated. If the check box is deactivated, the referenced model will only be built in 32 bit. This can save a lot of build time for large models. |

## Use Case

The main focus of Model Referencing is reusing code without the need to copy it. Several models can reference the same sub model and might use several instances of it. One aspect of this technique is that it allows separating the app

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Interfaces/MATLAB/Setup] MATLABSetupStateflowCharts


# Stateflow Charts

MATLAB Integration Package » Setup » Stateflow Charts

## Highlighting of Stateflow States

The Simulink Model Viewer can highlight the current state of a stateflow chart in each simulation step. To enable this feature, the stateflow chart must be prepared in MATLAB as follows:

- Add either a local variable or an output variable to the stateflow chart.

- If a local variable is used, activate the Test point check box for this variable.

- If an output variable is used, activate the Test point of the output of the chart, and name it the same as the variable.

- In each state of the chart, set the variable to a unique value, and add a comment of the following form to the state:%VState{‘<varName>’, <value>}

- Activate Enable Simulink Signal Analysis and Generate CANoe Model Viewer description file in the CN code generation options.

|  | Note This feature is not compatible with XCP calibration, i.e., if XCP calibration is enabled, stateflow states cannot be highlighted. |

See Also

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Interfaces/MatrixSwitches] MatrixSwitches


# Switches: Introduction

Switches » Introduction

This topic describes the usage of the CAPL DLL pl_nise.DLL. This DLL enables CAPL programs to access switch devices through the National Instruments software Switch Executive.

The DLL is available as part of CANoe version 6.1 and higher.

| --- | --- |

| DUT | Device under test |

### [Interfaces/MatrixSwitches] MatrixSwitchesDebounceTime


# Switches: Switch Debounce Time

Switches » Switch Debounce Time

Most switches are relay based. This means after setting a route the switch requires a certain settling period. This time is called debounce time. Please note that all API functions return immediately, i.e. the functions do not wait till the debounce time expires. The API provides the function NISEIsDebounced() which can be used to check whether the switch settled in.

Typically, switches are accessed by CAPL code from CANoe test module. This allows you to use wait functions such as TestWaitForTimeout(). For more details on test modules and the corresponding functions please refer to CANoe Help.

|  | Example The following code excerpt shows how you can realize a wait function within a CANoe test module. long MainTest(){// …NISEConnect( …, …, …, 0);while ( !NISEIsDebounced( …, …)){WaitForTimeout( 1);}// …} |

Switches: Introduction | Switches: Switch Executive | Switches: Installation | Switches: Usage | Switches: CAPL DLL API

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Interfaces/MatrixSwitches] MatrixSwitchesInstallation


# Switches: Installation

Switches » Installation

In order to use the DLL you must install:

- MAX

- NI Switch Executive

- Switch and IVI driver

The first two items are available from NI, the latter one from your switch manufacturer of choice.

Since the DLL pl_nise.DLL is a CAPL DLL, it has to be integrated to CANoe like any other CAPL DLL. The CANoe help page CAPL-DLL explains how to do this.

The DLL pl_nise.DLL is located in the Exec32 directory of the CANoe installation. Please do not move the file to any other place.

After these steps you can use the DLL API in your CAPL program.

Switches: Introduction | Switches: Switch Executive | Switches: Usage | Switches: Debounce Time | Switches: CAPL DLL API

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Interfaces/MatrixSwitches] MatrixSwitchesSwitchExecutive


# Switches: Switch Executive

Switches » Switch Executive

Switch devices (or just switches) are commonly used in test environments. Typically, they are used to connect measurement devices to each other and with a DUT. Most switches are relay based devices.

The DLL pl_nise.DLL does not access switches directly, but through the National Instruments (NI) application Switch Executive (SE). NI SE provides a graphical user interface to configure switches. This GUI is available within the NI Measurement and Automation Explorer (MAX).

Please note that NI SE is not available for free. It must be purchased from NI.

NI SE works with all switches which have a so called IVI software interface. This covers most switches of NI and Pickering.

A switch configuration may consist of:

- Alias names for the switch channels (IOs of the switch)

- Names for special routes

- Connection exclusions (prevents unsafe connections like V+ to Gnd)

- Other definitions

The DLL pl_nise.DLL provides a CAPL API which allows to set the switch. A typical setting is to establish or disconnect a route between switch channels. The API of the DLL is very similar to the API which NI SE provides (described in [2]).

Switches: Introduction | Switches Installation | Switches: Usage | Switches: Debounce Time | Switches: CAPL DLL API

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Interfaces/MatrixSwitches] MatrixSwitchesUsage


# Switches: Usage

Switches » Usage

As mentioned above, switches can be configured in MAX. Please note that you must restart CANoe to pick up changes which has been set in MAX.

|  | Example The following CAPL code example shows how to call DLL API functions. char route[512];long handle[1];long gHandle;long ret;if((ret = NISEOpen( "MySwitch", gDevOptions, handle)) != 0){GetNISEError(gHandle); // handle error ......return;}gHandle = handle[0];strncpy(route, "myRoute", elcount(route));if((ret = NISEConnect(gHandle, route, 1, 0)) !=0){GetNISEError(gHandle); // handle error ...}if((ret = NISEConnect(gHandle, "[Kanal_c1_alias -> r6]", 1, 1)) !=0){GetNISEError(gHandle);}... void GetNISEError(long handle){long ret;char errorDesc[4096];if((ret=NISEGetError(handle, errorDesc, elcount(errorDesc) )) == 0){write("NISE Error: %s", errorDesc);}else{write("NISE Error: %d. (can't retrieve error string)", ret);}} |

Switches: Introduction | Switches: Switch Executive | Switches: Installation | Switches: Debounce Time | Switches: CAPL DLL API

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |

# National Instruments Devices

National Instruments Devices

## General

In CANoe you can use different I/O cards from National Instruments.

In doing so your actions can lead to serious personal injury or damage to property. For this reason only those persons may use this driver who understand the possible consequences of their actions with this driver or who have been specially trained to deal with this driver. Whenever other persons use this driver, Vector Informatik GmbH accepts no responsibility or liability beyond that of fixing bugs in the software / hardware or refunding the purchase price. |

|  | Note All product names in this document are either proprietary registered or unregistered trademarks. |

## Supported Devices And Features

Most of the National Instruments devices that can be addressed using the DAQmx driver interface and configured using National Instruments' Measurement and Automation Explorer (MAX) are supported.

## Installation And Configuration

To use National Instruments I/O cards the Measurement and Automation Explorer (MAX)has to be installed. Installation and configuration of I/O cards are described on page Configuration.

### [Interfaces/NationalInstruments] NIDevicesConfiguration


# Configuration

National Instruments Devices » Configuration

## Installation

You will need National Instruments Measurement and Automation Explorer (MAX) in order to configure devices and for use in CANoe. MAX an further required software components are available at NI; see NI-DAQmx Driver (http://www.ni.com/dataacquisition/nidaqmx.htm). When installing the NI-DAQmx driver you should use at least version 9.5.

The MAX can be used to configure both, the devices themselves and the measurement tasks for the particular devices. A task covers one or more ports that can be used with that task. Settings are made on a task-specific basis and affect all the relevant ports.

Please note that the supported types of acquisition and output will depend on the devices used. For more detailed information, refer to the description of the device in question.

The MAX can be used to assign names to tasks and the ports they contain. These names are used to identify and locate tasks and their ports at a later date.

|  | Step by Step Procedure: Installation Install the NI-DAQmx driver. Open the NI Measurement and Automation Explorer (NI MAX). Create one or more Measurement Tasks. Save your settings and close NI MAX. Start CANoe and open the Configuration dialog (Other in Hardware ribbon tab). Click on [I/O Device Drivers…]. Activate the pl_nidaqmx.dll I/O device driver and close the dialog with [OK]. Now the configuration dialog shows all available NI tasks and ports.You can now use these por

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Interfaces/PortLink] PortLink


# PortLink

Connecting to External Hardware » PortLink

| General |
| --- |
| PortLink is used to link CANoe environment variables to analog and digital I/O ports on I/O cards. The value of an environment variable is either read to a port or output by a port. |
| Glossary | Additional PortLink Topics |
| Device DLL A device DLL supports one or more I/O cards.In the Options dialog you can choose the device DLLs that should be used for the configuration. Device A device is a I/O card. Device Number Each device gets a device number.Device numbers are unique within a device DLL. DLL Identifier The internal name of the device DLL. Port Name The port name as used in the configuration of the I/O card. | I/O Cards Configuration Porting a PortLink configuration to another computer Notes on Performance Performance is very dependent on the type of I/O card and the number of ports used. It may take up to 300µs to read in an analog port. If several analog ports are being used, the user must make sure that the polling interval is not too short. |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Interfaces/PortLink] PortLinkConfiguration


# Configuration

PortLink » Configuration

Ribbon: Hardware Tab|Other

## Port List

Listed here are all ports existing in the computer. List entries consist of the following columns:

- Type Port type: Analog input (AI), analog output (AO), digital input (DI) or digital output (DO).

- Acquisition This column is only filled out for input ports and describes the acquisition type for the respective port.Possible acquisition modes:

- Polling Measurement values are acquired in an update interval to be specified.

- Event Driver triggers an event when changing an acquired measurement value.

- Stream Data from the driver is continuously acquired with a certain frequency and made available in data blocks.

- Port name The port's identifier

- Port # Number of the port on the card

- Device name Alias name of the card – the alias name is set in the DLL

- Device # Number of the card

- Sampling Sampling frequency for ports that support the acquisition mode Stream.

- Update [ms] Update interval for the port.For Polling ports the update interval can be set in ms. For Stream ports the update interval is derived from the configuration of the card and can't be changed.

## Sorting the List Entries

You can sort either list by clicking a column header.You can return the Port List to its standard sorting sequence by clicking the header of the DLL alias column.

## Exit Configuration Dialog

- No changes were made If no changes were made the configuration can only be exited by [Cancel].

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Interfaces/PortLink] PortLinkCopyConfiguration


# Porting a PortLink Configuration to Another PC

PortLink » Configuration » Porting a PortLink Configuration to Another PC

Two cases are differentiated here:

- An I/O card of the same type is installed in the PC

- If the card has the same Device Number then the PortLink configuration file can be accepted without changes. The user needs to copy the PortLink configuration file to a directory on the target computer – the name of the configuration file is shown in the PortLink Configuration dialog. If the file is copied to a different directory than the one on the original computer it must be loaded in the PortLink Configuration dialog, thereby relinking it to the CANoe configuration.

- If the Device Number is different it should be changed to the device number in the original configuration. With National Instruments cards the device number is set using the National Instruments "Measurement & Automation" tool. If the device number cannot be changed then a new PortLink configuration must be created.

- An I/O card of a different type is installed in the PC A new PortLink configuration must be created.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Interfaces/PortLink] PortLinkIOCards


# PortLink: I/O Cards

PortLink » I/O Cards

|  | Note In the Options dialog (Extensions|I/O Device Drivers) you can set the device DLLs that should be used for the configuration. |

At this time the following DLLs are available:

| DLL | Description |
| --- | --- |
| pl_nidaqmx.DLL | Supports most National Instruments I/O cards and data acquisition devices. |
| pl_redlab.DLL | Supports the Meilhaus RedLab device Family. |

## I/O Ports

Each I/O card has a number of ports. PortLink supports analog inputs and outputs and digital inputs or outputs.With some cards the number of ports may change when the card configuration is changed. For example, changing a port from Single Ended to Differential reduces the number of available analog ports by one-half.

- Analog I/Os The value range of an analog port depends on the I/O card and the port type.

- Digital I/Os Digital ports are always single-bit ports. With some cards the direction of the digital ports can be switched.

## Card Installation

Installation of an I/O card is very dependent on the card type. Therefore we refer you to the relevant manuals from your card producer. Usually it is necessary to install a card driver. For cards from National Instruments you must also install their "Measurement and Automation Explorer". Among other things this is used to set the device numbers of the cards.

## Device Identification

In PortLink an I/O card is referred to as a device.Each Device can be identified uniquely by its device numb

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Interfaces/RS232] RS232


# RS232 API

CANoe » Connecting to External Hardware » RS232 API

In CANoe you can control the RS232 interface via CAPL functions. If your computer does not have a built-in RS232 interface, you can use a USB to RS232 adapter. Be sure to install the corresponding adapter driver when doing so. You can also use other hardware interfaces with the RS232 API. The requirement for this is that Windows must recognize these serial hardware interfaces as serial COM ports. One example would be IrDA, as most are represented as a virtual COM port in Windows.

|  | Note Starting with CANoe 7.1 several RS232 CAPL functions have been declared deprecated. These functions have been fully removed with CANoe 12.0. Notes on using USB-RS232 Converter. |

## Differences between the current version and the obsolete RS232 CAPL API:

- You do not longer require a separate CAPL DLL, and hence the #pragma library CAPL command is not applicable.

- You do not longer require a v24.INI configuration file.

- Serial interfaces are detected automatically, i.e. manual action is no longer required (e.g. the entry of numbers in files).

- You can open all available ports from CAPL.

- Blocking write operations are obsolete.

- Reception of data blocks.

- Parallel sending and receiving.

- Error handling via callback handler.

## Changes if programs are to be ported:

- The CAPL DLL rs232vc.DLL must be removed (Options dialog).

- The CAPL DLL rs232vc.DLL may not be integrated in the includes section via #pragm

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Interfaces/RS232] RS232APILimitations


# RS232 API: Limitations

RS232 » Limitations

Note the following limitations:

- The latency setting of the COM port must be set to the lowest possible value for fast data reception. This reduces the delay to 1–2 ms when receiving serial data. You can change the setting for USB-RS232 converters in the Windows Device Manager. Open the properties of the COM port, choose the Port Settings page and click on [Advanced].

- There is no callback handler for changes to special signal lines.

- Control of break conditions is not possible.

RS232 API: Usage | RS232 API: Supported baud rates

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Interfaces/RS232] RS232Converter


# RS232: USB-RS232 Converter

RS232 » Converter

When working with VN8900 interface or real time modules (VT6000 family), it is possible to use serial ports on USB-RS232 converters (serial port). Supported chipsets are:

- Prolific (PL2303)

- FTDI (FT232)

|  | Note Vector has successfully tested several USB-RS232 converters which contained the chipsets mentioned above. Nevertheless in few cases failures while using such serial ports may occur – e.g. due to special hardware conditions on USB-RS232 converters. |

For further questions on this topic please contact Vector support.

RS232 API: Limitations | RS232 API: Supported baud rates | RS232 API: Usage

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Interfaces/RS232] RS232SupportedBaudRates


# RS232 API: Supported Baudrates

RS232 » Supported baud rates

The following baud rates are supported by the connected hardware and are entered in the RS232-standard:

- 300 bps

- 600 bps

- 1200 bps

- 2400 bps

- 4800 bps

- 9600 bps

- 19200 bps

- 38400 bps

- 57600 bps

- 115200 bps

The following baud rates are rather unusual and thus not always supported:

- 75 bps

- 110 bps

- 134.5 bps

- 150 bps

- 1800 bps

- 7200 bps

- 14400 bps

- 56K bps

- 128K bps

RS232 API: Limitations | RS232 API: Usage

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Interfaces/RS232] RS232Usage


# RS232 API: Usage

RS232 » Usage

When transmitting large amounts of data it is possible that the buffer of the receiver can be overrun before the receiver can process the data (overrun error). This is primary due to the used hardware:

- small receive buffer (e.g. 16 or 64 bytes)

- slow system CPU

- many other I/O accesses

- high system load

Windows typically uses an intermediate buffer with a size of 4 KByte, so that an overflow is quite unlikely when using CANoe. Nevertheless you should use a handshake for error-free transmission. The receiving hardware signals when its receive buffer is full. The sender then delays further sending until the receiving hardware again signals readiness to receive.

Hardware handshakes are recommended, as they work the fastest and most conveniently. Software handshakes use special characters that artificially limit the character set and thus the amount of data to be received.

If the receiving device does not offer handshake security, data quantities larger than the receive buffer size must not be transmitted.

RS232 API: Limitations | RS232 API: Supported baud rates

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Interfaces/VModule] VModule


# Module Description File (vmodule)

Setup and Extensions » Generic Interfaces » Module Description

Module description files (*.vmodule) contain file paths and metadata for modules. The following types of modules are supported:

- CAPL DLL

- Modeling library (NodeLayer DLL)

- C Library

In older versions of CANoe the implementation of a module (e.g. the path to a CAPL DLL) was entered directly into the configuration of CANoe. This makes it difficult to exchange configurations between different platforms (Windows 32-bit and 64-bit or Linux 64-bit), because the implementation is typically specific to one platform.

Starting with CANoe version 12.0 SP3 a module description file can therefore be entered in the configuration instead of the implementation. This module description file can contain the file paths to the implementations of different platforms.

Starting with CANoe version 13.0 a versioning with respect to the application (CANoe) can also be specified. This makes it possible to use different versions of a DLL in different CANoe versions or to define a minimum version of the application.

|  | Example of a module description file (*.vmodule) <?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?><Module xmlns:xsi=http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema-instance xsi:noNamespaceSchemaLocation="CANoe_vmodule_1.2.xsd"> <Name>CANoeILNLVector</Name> <Version>3.17.70</Version> <Application>CANoe</Application> <ModuleType>NodeLayer-DLL</ModuleType> <ModuleType>CAPL-DLL</ModuleType> <SupportedArchi

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Interfaces/VModule] VModuleFormat


# Format of the Module Description

Module Description » Format of the Module Description

The module description file is an XML document in UTF-8 format. A formal description (XML schema) is located in the <Path to the sample configurations>\Programming\vModule\Schema\CANoe_vmodule_1.4.xsd. Relative file paths within the XML document are relative to the module description files.

Many XML elements of the document allow an XML attribute called arch, which includes a list of supported platforms. This allows the effectiveness of the XML element to be limited to one or more platforms. The attribute is optional. If the attribute is not specified, the XML element is valid for all platforms supported by the module.Note: The elements of an XML list are separated by spaces.

The identifiers for the platforms are:

| Identifier | Platform |
| --- | --- |
| Linux-x64 | Linux with 64-bit computer architecture. |
| Windows-x64 | Windows with 64-bit computer architecture. |
| Windows-x86 | Windows with 32-bit computer architecture. |
| CaplOnBoard-ARM32 | CAPL-on-Board with 32-bit ARM architecture. |
| WEC-ERT | Windows Embedded Compact architecture for Extended Real Time. |

Many XML elements of the document allow the use of the version attributes minAppVersion and maxAppVersion. This can be used to create a restriction in relation to an CANoe version. If versioning is used, it is continuous and consistent. With the minAppVersion a certain minimum version can be required. The maxAppVers

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Interfaces/VModule] VModuleFormatCAPLDLL


# CAPL-DLL

Module Description » Format of the Module Description » CAPL-DLL

## XML Element CAPL-DLL

The CAPL-DLL XML element contains additional data for modules of type CAPL-DLL. This element duplicates the information of the CAPL Export Table and is used by the CAPL compiler during the translation of a CAPL program.

- For Windows-x86 and Windows-x64 platforms, this element is optional.If it is not specified, the CAPL compiler uses the CAPL Export Table contained in the Windows DLLs.

- This element is mandatory for the Linux-x64 platform, since the CAPL compiler cannot load Linux libraries and therefore has no access to the contained CAPL Export Table.

It contains the following XML elements:

| XML Element | Description |
| --- | --- |
| CDLL_VERSION | Version information identical to the version in the CAPL Export Table. |
| CallingConvention | Calling convention of the exported functions. Possible values: |
| Value | Short Description |
| CDECL | Corresponds to the setting CAPL_DLL_CDECL in the CAPL Export Table. |
| PASCAL | Corresponds to the setting CAPL_DLL_PASCAL in the CAPL Export Table. |
| CAN_MSG_VERSION | Version of the format for parameter transfer of CAN frames. Possible values: 0…7 Corresponds to the macros CAPL_DLL_CAN_MSG_VER_0 to CAPL_DLL_CAN_MSG_VER_7 in the CAPL Export Table. |
| LibraryVersion | Exported version number of the CAPL DLL. Corresponds to the exported variable caplDllLibraryVersion; see Version Control. |
| VersionCheckMessage | Text t

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Interfaces/VModule] VModuleFormatNodeLayerDLL


# NodeLayer-DLL

Module Description » Format of the Module Description » NodeLayer-DLL

The NodeLayer-DLL XML element contains additional data for modules of type NodeLayer-DLL and contains the following XML elements:

| XML Element | Description |
| --- | --- |
| NodeLayerConfig | File path |
| LoadOption | Determines the behavior when the modeling library should be loaded or unloaded. Possible values: |
| Value | Description |
| Default | Default behavior, identical to setting Permanent. |
| Permanent | Library is loaded when the configuration is opened and only unloaded when the configuration is closed. This setting offers the best performance. |
| Defer | Library is loaded at the start of measurement and unloaded at measurement end. In addition, when opening the configuration, the library is briefly loaded to request some information. This setting is well suited for developing libraries, since only the measurement has to be stopped to exchange the libraries, but the configuration does not have to be closed. |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Interfaces/VN0601] VN0601


# VN0601

Connecting to External Hardware » VN0601

| General |
| --- |
|  | The VN0601 interface module is connected to the computer via USB 2.0 and provides multiple ARINC-429 channels to the user. The device has four Tx channels and four Rx channels (channel 1..4: Tx; channel 5..8: Rx). The bit rate of the channels can be set separately to low-speed or high-speed. For the Rx channels, an automatic bit rate detection can also be activated. The parity calculation (generation/check) is also performed in the hardware. Please refer to the device manual for further information on setup and operation. The VN0601 has an analog input, two digital inputs and one digital output. For this reason you can easily measure and generate I/O signals; please see the manual for further technical details. Application area: Networking in avionics industry |
| Configuration |
| Vector I/O Configuration VN0601 Configuration |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Interfaces/VN1600] VN1630


# VN1630/VN1640

Connecting to External Hardware » VN1630/VN1640

| General |
| --- |
|  | The VN1600 interface product family is the successor of the proven CANcaseXL that can be used for a wide variety of applications. The devices are connected to the computer via USB. The VN1630 has two built-in CAN transceiver. Additionally you can assemble up to two CAN or LIN transceiver (CANpiggy/LINpiggy). The VN1640 has four piggy slots that can be assembled with CAN or LIN transceivers (CANpiggy/LINpiggy). Please refer to the device manual for further information on setup and operation (e.g. exchanging transceivers). VN1630 and VN1640 both have an analog input, two digital inputs and one digital output. For this reason you can easily measure and generate I/O signals; please see the manual for further technical details. CANoe uses system variables to manipulate and measure the I/O signals. Thus you can directly use the measurement values for deeper analysis. |
| Configuration |
| Vector I/O Configuration VN1600 Configuration |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Interfaces/VN4610] VN4610


# VN4610

CANoe » Network Interfaces » VN4610

## General

The VN4610 interface module is connected to the computer via USB 2.0 and provides its users 2 WLAN (W11p) channels and one GNSS channel.

W11p and GNSS can be operated independently of each other.

However, the two W11p radio channels are not completely independent of each other: While one W11p channel is transmitting, the other W11p channel cannot transmit or receive at this moment.

Further information on setup and startup can be found in the manual.

The VN4610 also has two CAN channels, one analog input, two digital inputs and two digital outputs. This allows you to easily measure and generate I/O signals. Further technical data can be found in the manual.

Application area: V2X communication

## Configuration

- Vector I/O-Configuration

- VN-I/O-Module

- CAN Configuration

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Interfaces/VN7570] VN7570


# VN7570/VN7572

Connecting to External Hardware » VN7570/VN7572

| General |
| --- |
|  | The interface cards VN7570 and VN7572 are a high performance PCI Express based network interfaces. They offer up to eight channels of which four may be assembled with CAN, LIN and up to one (VN7570)/two (VN7572) FlexRay transceivers. This makes the VN7570/VN7572 highly flexible and applicable for a wide variety of applications. The VN7570/VN7572 has four built-in CAN transceivers. The VN7570/VN7572 offers four additional slots that can be assembled with CAN, LIN and up to one (VN7570)/two (VN7572) FlexRay transceivers (CANpiggy/LINpiggy/FRpiggy). The VN7570/VN7572 has an IOpiggy slot. This allows assembling the proven Vector IOpiggy for simple measurement and generation of I/O signals. Please refer to the device manual for further information on setup and operation (e.g. exchanging transceivers). CANoe uses system variables to manipulate and measure the I/O signals. Thus you can directly use the measurement values for deeper analysis. |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Interfaces/VN8900] VN8900


# VN8900

Connecting to External Hardware » VN8900

| General |
| --- |
|  | The VN8900 combines the ease of use (plug & play, USB – as e.g. VN1600, VN7600) with real-time capability. For that purpose in CANoe the real-time-relevant parts of the simulation- and test environment are executed on the VN8900 device (distributed mode/standalone mode). In CANalyzer the send branch and its blocks are executed on the VN8900 device. Thereby you achieve improvements with respect to latency times and precision of timers. The device has four network interfaces that can be used to connect CAN, LIN or FlexRay transceivers (CANpiggy/LINpiggy). Additional network devices (e.g. with FlexRay support) can be connected to the VN8900 directly via the USB port. The device itself is also connected to the computer via USB/IP. The term VN8900 encompasses all units and variants of the device. The variants of the base unit (VN8910/VN8910A/VN8911/VN8912/VN8912A/VN8914) and of the plug-in module (VN8950/VN8970/VN8972) are distinguished from one another by means of the last two places in the VN number. In principle they do not differ in their usage — apart from minor exceptions. The processor in the device has enough performance capacity to enable the simulation environment (based on Windows) to process even the more demanding models with higher throughput volumes. Please refer to the device handbook for more information on how to set up, use and configure the VN8900 (e.g. swapping transceivers). |
| Ope

### [Interfaces/VN8900] VN8900LEDsKeypad


# VN8900: LEDs and Keypad

VN8900 » LEDs and Keypad

## LEDs

Both the VN8950 module and VN8970 module have LEDs on the front. The number of LEDs is dependent on the module type.

You can control the following LEDs during the measurement via CAPL:

- Channel LEDs on the front of the module.VN8950 module: Channels 1…4VN8970 module: Channels 1…4 and channels A and BVN8972 module: Channels 1…4 (FlexRay/CAN/LIN)

- Module LED on the front of the module

- LEDs S1 and S2 on an VN8900 with integrated keypad on the back of the device.

### CAPL functions for controlling the LEDs:

- With xlAcquireLed, the LEDs must be acquired for one use.

- With xlSetLed, the status of the LEDs can be changed.

- With XlReleaseLED, the effectively assigned LEDs are released again after their use.

If the LEDs were not yet assigned with xlAcquireLed or were already released again with xlReleaseLed, they indicate the bus load on the respective channels. An acquired LED does not indicate the bus load of the channel.

## Keypad

- The VN8910/VN8910A/VN8911 devices have the keypad on the back of the device.

- The VN8912/VN8912A/VN8914 devices have the keypad on the front of the device.

The following keys can be available:

- Start/Stop key Starts and stops the measurement in interactive mode and in standalone mode.

- <F2>, <F3>, and <F4>Behaves within a measurement according to the definition via the on key event procedure. Outside of the measurement, the keys have no function.

For the <F2>, <F3>,

### [Interfaces/VN8900] VN8900ReplaceModule


# VN8900: Replacing a Hardware Module

VN8900 » Replacing a Hardware Module

|  | Changing hardware modules When a VN8900 module is replaced by a different one (e.g. replacement of a VN8950 module by a VN8970 module), some steps are required to ensure correct interworking of CANoe and the VN8900 device. The detailed actions to be done depend on the versions of the operating system image and CANoe installed on the VN8900 device, but in general the following steps are required: Update software on the VN8900 device This step is only necessary if the software version on the VN8900 device is different from the version installed on the computer.Details on performing a software update. Replace the old VN8900 module by the new one To replace the VN8900 module by a new one, follow the steps below: If not already done, unplug the power supply from the VN8900 device. Loosen the screws of the VN8900 module, located on the front panel. Pull the VN8900 module from the VN8900 device (you can drag it by holding the screws) Push in the new VN8900 module into the VN8900 device. Pay attention to the correct orientation of the module. When you completely pushed in the new module, tighten the screws located on the front panel. Plug in the power supply again. Update the VN8900 driver on the computer This step is only necessary if the current VN8900 driver on the computer does not support the new module. Download the driver package from the Vector homepage and execute the setup program. In the sel

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Interfaces/VN8900] VN8900Storage


# VN8900: Storage

VN8900 » Storage

The internal storage in VN8900 devices is divided into two partitions – the system partition and the data partition.

## System Partition

The system partition comprises the operating system files and driver files as well as the runtime environment for CANoe (RT kernel), including all required components.

In addition, all custom DLL files used in a configuration (e.g. CAPL DLLs) as well as the user files are loaded into a specific directory on the system partition. This is applicable for the interactive mode and when a standalone configuration is loaded (extraction of the relevant files from a RTCFG file).

## Data Partition

The available memory on the data partition is used for the following files:

- Logging files

- Test report files

- Standalone configurations (RTCFG files)

- Files of further applications that are possibly executed on the device (e.g. vFlash)

- Temporary filesIn particular, these files are generated during software or driver updates and when unpacking compressed diagnostic descriptions.

## Deleting Files from Data Partition

If there is not enough memory available when updating a driver or software, a message appears, and the user is asked if the existing logging or test report files should be deleted.

In logging and test report settings in standalone mode, you can configure that old logging or test report files will be deleted automatically when a certain memory threshold on the data partition has been exceede

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Interfaces/VN8900] VN8900Troubleshooting


# VN8900: Troubleshooting

VN8900 » Troubleshooting

|  | Differing Timestamps Activate the Hardware Synchronization to avoid differing time stamps between the VN8900 and connected USB network interfaces. |

|  | USB-RS232 Converter When working with VN8900 interface, it is possible to use serial ports on USB-RS232 converters. |

|  | Vector Keyman License Dongle You can use a Keyman license dongle directly attached to the VN8900 device which is mainly useful in the standalone mode use case. This might require installation of the corresponding driver on the device (see Driver Update). A Keyman license dongle connected to the VN8900 cannot be used outside standalone mode. For this purpose, the Keyman license dongle must be connected to the computer. |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Interfaces/VN8900] VN8900USBRS232


# USB-RS232/RS485 Adapter

VN8900 » USB-RS232/RS485 Adapter

## General

The VN8900 supports USB-RS232/RS485 adapters that are based on one of the following ICs:

- Prolific PL232

- FTDI FT232

## VN8910A

Effective with VN8900 image version 3.2.2, the adapters provide their serial interfaces as COM3 and COM4. This depends on the VN8900 USB connection at which the respective adapter is connected:

- Upper USB connection: COM3

- Lower USB connection: COM4

## VN8911

The adapters make their serial interfaces available as COM3 to COM4 to the VN8911. This is dependent on the type of the adapter as well as the USB connection of the VN8911 to which the respective adapter is connected.

- Prolific PL232:

- USB 1: COM3

- USB 2: COM4

- FTDI FT232:

- COM3 for all USB connections (when only one adapter is used)

## VN8912/VN8912A

With VN8912/VN8912A the adapters provide their serial interfaces as COM3 to COM6. This depends on the type of the adapter and on the VN8912/VN8912A USB connection at which the respective adapter is connected:

- Prolific PL232:

- USB 1: COM3

- USB 2: COM4

- USB 3: COM5

- USB 4: COM6

- FTDI FT232:

- COM3 for all USB connections (while using one single adapter)

## VN8914

With VN8914 the adapters provide their serial interfaces as COM3 to COM5. This depends on the type of the adapter and on the VN8914 USB connection at which the respective adapter is connected:

- Prolific PL232:

- USB 1: COM3

- USB 2: COM4

- USB 3: COM5

- FTDI FT232:

- COM3

| Simulation and Stimulation |
| Option ISO11783 allows to simulate ISO11783 nodes (ECUs). The ISO11783 Interaction Layer enables you to realize a complex simulation of an ISOBUS implementation including the communication to a Virtual Terminal, to a Task Controller, to a File Server and/or to a TIM Server/TIM Client with little programming effort. ### [ISO11783] startpageISO11783


# Option ISO11783

Standards – Bus Systems, Protocols, Databases » Option ISO11783

|  | Option ISO11783 is based on Option J1939. ISO Standard 11783 describes CAN communications in open networks for the agricultural equipment industry (ISOBUS). Special support of this standard by CANoe allows the user to utilize, analyze and simulate the complex communication structures very easily and efficiently. CANoe Option ISO11783 provides all functions necessary to accomplish this, thereby giving the user the means to develop CAN relevant portions of a project with just one tool. |

### [ISO11783/FileServer] FSOverview


# File Server (FS)

ISO11783 » File Server (FS)

| General |
| --- |
| The File Server in an ISO11783 network is designed for use by a tractor or self-propelled implement to provide file access such as read and write operations. CANoe Option ISO11783 offers the whole functionality of such a File Server. The File Server Interaction Layer can be used to expand a simulation node of an ISOBUS network into a file server. It offers extensive possibilities for fault injection and can be controlled from a test unit. The File Server Window can be used to setup a ready to use File Server instance within a few steps without additional CAPL programming. The window displays connected clients, opened files and read/write operations as well as a log file with detailed information about the communication. |
| File Server Window |
| is immediately ready for use no possibility for fault injection has the graphical user interface is controlled by the CANoe user | File Server Window |
| File Server Interaction Layer |
| has to be complemented by a CAPL program extensive possibilities for the fault injections has a programming interface (CAPL) is controlled by a CAPL program can be completely controlled from the test unit | File Server IL |

### [ISO11783/FileServerWindow] FSWindow


# File Server Window

## Insert the File Server Window [▲ back]

To add a file server window to the Simulation Setup, open the context menu of the channel in the Simulation Setup with a right click on the bus. Then click on the context menu command Insert ISO11783 File Server. This adds a node to the Simulation Setup. With a double click on the node to open the File Server Window.

## Layout of the File Server Window [▲ back]

The file server window is divided into two areas. On the left side you will find the three headings Configuration, Clients and Information, via which you can display the respective pages on the right. The selected element is highlighted in blue.

You will also find the Connection button with a plug icon at the bottom left. The symbol indicates whether the interaction layer is switched on or off. You can toggle the two states by clicking the button.

### Configuration

### Properties:

- Version The version of the File Server (1-4).

- Root directory The root directory containing all folders and files to be accessed by the server. The path is relative to the CANoe configuration.

- Primary volume The primary volume is always the first connected removable media, i.e. the current direc

### [ISO11783/InteractiveTaskController] ITC


# Interactive Task Controller

ISO11783 » Interactive Task Controller

The Interactive Task Controller can be used to access the process data dictionary of an implement. The process variables can be set or read out. The process variables are selected symbolically after the device description has been transferred by the implement. When a task is started, the process variables of the default treatment zone are transmitted to the implement and the peer control assignments are set.

This online video tutorial gives you an overview of the most important functions of the Interactive Task Controller:Video Tutorial: ISO11783 ITC Overview.

Here the use of the Interactive Task Controller in passive (listen-only) mode is described:Video Tutorial: ISO11783 Interactive Task Controller in Passive Mode.

The support of peer control is described here:Video Tutorial: ISO11783 Peer Control with Interactive Task Controller.

The window of the Interactive Task Controller is divided in two parts. The left side shows an overview about the available information. The device for which a device description is available are listed here. The view on the right side depends on the selected item.

There are several views available:

- Devices overview

- Device view

- Task overview

- Task view

- Configuration

The expandable information view Information below shows the messages issued by the Interactive Task Controller. When selecting a line in the list and pressing <F1> the online help will show a de

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [ISO11783/InteractiveTaskController] ITCDeviceName


# Device name

ISO11783 » Interactive Task Controller » Configuration » Device name

The 64-bit device name of an ECU consists of the following individual segments:

| Segment | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Arbitrary Address Capable (AAC) | represents the arbitrary address capable flag |
| Industry Group | represents the industry group the device belongs to |
| Vehicle System Instance | represents the instance of the vehicle system |
| Vehicle System | represents the vehicle system |
| Function | represents the function |
| Function Instance | represents the function instance If the check box Dynamic is activated, the Interactive Task Controller checks before registering to the network, if the selected Function Instance is already used by another Interactive Task Controller or Data Logger. In that case the Interactive Task Controller changes its Function Instance to an unused value. |
| ECU Instance | represents the ECU instance |
| Manufacturer Code | represents the manufacturer code of the device |
| Identity Number | represents the identity number |

See Also

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [ISO11783/InteractiveTaskController] ITCDeviceOverview


# Device overview of the Interactive Task Controller

ISO11783 » Interactive Task Controller » Device overview

If you select the entry Devices in the tree view of the Interactive Task Controller the right side shows the device overview.

The table contains all devices for which a device description is available. An implement must transfer its device description before it is listed here. In the table the designator of the device and the address are shown.

The icon in front of the device name shows the state of the device:

| Icon | Device State |
| --- | --- |
|  | The object pool is activated. |
|  | The object pool is not activated. |
|  | The object pool is deactivated because the connection to the implement had been lost. |

You can delete the stored device descriptions of all devices with command Remove all devices in the context menu. With command Remove device you delete a single device description.

With command Export device description you can store the process data dictionary of a device in a XML file. With Import device description you can load such a XLM file and add the device description to a process data dictionary.

See Also

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [ISO11783/InteractiveTaskController] ITCDeviceView


# Device view of the Interactive Task Controller

ISO11783 » Interactive Task Controller » Device view

The device view shows the process data dictionary of the selected device. The process data dictionary is displayed as defined in the device description.

In the input box at the top you can enter a term or a DDI, which is used for filtering the displayed elements. Use the [X] button to undo the filtering.

|  | Note The [Get], [Set] and Cmd buttons are only available for process data and always disabled in passive mode. |

With the [Get] and [Set] buttons you can send a read or write command to the implement. For read-only process data only the [Get] button is displayed.

The [Cmd] button opens a dialog where you can send measurement commands to the client.

The Measurement Commands column lists the measurement commands sent. If a task is started or stopped, this column is emptied

With a double click or the <F2> key you can edit a value in the currently selected row. It is also possible to enter the first digit to start editing. The [Set] button sends the new value to the process data dictionary of the implement. Use the <F3> keyto open the dialog for sending the measurement commands for the currently selected line.

The different entries of the process data dictionary are distinguished with the following icons:

| Icon | Process Data Dictionary Element |
| --- | --- |
|  | Device Element |
|  | Device Process Data |
|  | Device Property |

See Also

| Version 15© Vector 

[...truncated for brevity...]

# Insert Interactive Task Controller

ISO11783 » Interactive Task Controller » Insert

The Interactive Task Controller is inserted in the simulation structure. To do this, the menu command Insert Interactive Task Controller is selected in the context menu. Then, a new block is inserted which you can open withe a double mouse click.

### [ISO11783/InteractiveTaskController] ITCMeasurementCommandDialog


# Measurement Command Dialog

ISO11783 » Interactive Task Controller » Device view » Measurement Command Dialog (ISO11783 ITC)

Over this dialog you can send different measurement commands ton the Task Controller Client. Enter therefor an interval or a threshold for the selected process data element an then click the Send button.

After closing the dialog the sent measurement commands are displayed in the Device view.

Starting with the Task Controller version 4 the Interactive Task Controller evaluates, if an acknowledgment (PDACK) was received from the client for the sent command.

| Task Controller version | Here you can select if the Interactive Task Controller is a version 1, 2, 3 or 4 Task Controller. |
| --- | --- |
| Task Controller Capability |
| Support Version Request | If you activate this option, the response for the Version Request message is enabled. If you deactivate the option, a negative acknowledge is sent. |
| Support Structure Label Request | If you activate this option, the response for the Structure Label Request message is enabled. The implement can activate a device description stored in the Task Controller without anew transmission. If you deactivate the option, a negative acknowledge is sent. The device description must sent to the Task Controller again. |
| Support Localization Label Request | If you activate this option, the response for the Localization Label Request message is enabled. If you deactivate the option, a negative acknowledge is sent. |
| Boot time | Here you set the maximum number of seconds until the first Task Controller status message A value of 255 (default value) means that this information is not available. This value is used in the Version Message if the Task Controller version is 3 or higher. |
| Supports TC-BAS, TC-GEO, peer control assignment, TC-SC | These

### [ISO11783/InteractiveTaskController] ITCSettingsTaskfileMatrix


# Settings Matrix Task File

ISO11783 » Interactive Task Controller » Configuration » Task File

| Task file is specified | Load Task File | Reset task data | Store data to Task File | Functionality |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| X | On configuration loading and on each measurement start | — | On each measurement stop | Simulates a standard ITC operating mode. A transmitted device description is automatically available on the Interactive Task Controller and don't has to be transmitted again. Due to all data are stored in the specified task file on measurement stop these data can be checked for consistency. |
| X | On configuration loading and on each measurement start | — | Never | Simulates an ITC operating mode which resets the Interactive Task Controller to its default state on each measurement start. Device descriptions that might be transmitted from an implement are overwritten with the specified task file. |
| — | — | On configuration loading and on each measurement start | — | Simulates an ITC operating mode which resets the Interactive Task Controller to its default state on each measurement start. Device descriptions that might be transmitted from an implement are discarded. |

### [ISO11783/InteractiveTaskController] ITCTaskView


# Task view of the Interactive Task Controller

ISO11783 » Interactive Task Controller » Task view

The task view shows the available tasks. You can modify these tasks (Treatment zones, peer control assignments and process data), create new tasks and delete existing tasks. During measurement you can send the values of a treatment zone or a single process value to the implement with the [Set] buttons.

To show the properties of a task select an entry below the entry Tasks in the tree view. On the right side of the dialog the properties of the selected task are shown.

To create a new task open the context menu of the entry Tasks in the tree view and select the menu command Add Task. A new task is created for which you can set the designator, the default treatment zone, further treatment zones and peer control assignments.

To delete a task, select the task and open the context menu. Select the entry Remove, the task will be deleted.

## Information

In this section the designator is shown and you can select the default treatment zone (Default Zone). This is applied automatically when a task is started.

## Treatment Zones

In this section you can modify and delete existing treatment zones and its process data, as well as create new treatment zones and process data.

To create and delete treatment zones and process data the following buttons are available:

| Button | Description |
| --- | --- |
|  | creates a new treatment zone |
|  | creates a new entry for the selected trea

### [ISO11783/InteractiveTaskController] TCOverview


# Task Controller (TC)

ISO11783 » Task Controller (TC)

| Allgemein |
| --- |
| In an ISO11783 network one of the important components is the Task Controller (TC). CANoe Option .ISO11783 offers everything necessary for the simulation of such a Task Controller. This includes, i.e., simple access to the process data of an implement. Beyond that for implement control various tasks can be defined which can be activated as needed. You can also set up the peer control assignments. If available CANoe can import standardized device description files based on XML to query implement information or receive it directly from the implement via CAN. The user can choose between two variants: A Task Controller Interaction Layer (TC_IL) implemented as a modeling library. An easy to use Interactive Task Controller window (ITC). ITC and TC_IL can also run passively on the network. This has the same benefits, such as the detection of compatibility issues in an active network or during logging file replay. |
| Interactive Task Contoller (ITC) |
| is immediately ready for use offers no possibility for fault injection has the graphical user interface is controlled by the CANoe user | Interactive Task Controller |
| Task Controller (TC) Interaction Layer |
| has to be complemented by a CAPL program offers extensive possibilities for fault injection has a programming interface (CAPL) is controlled by a CAPL program can be completely controlled from the test unit | Task Controller IL |

| Version 15©

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [ISO11783/ISO11783IL] ISO11783ILConfig


# Setting up the ISO11783 Interaction Layer

ISO11783 » ISO11783 Interaction Layer » Setting up the ISO11783 Interaction Layer

## Setting up the ISO11783 Interaction Layer

The ISO11783 Interaction Layer (ISO11783 IL) is configured via the database.

Follow the next steps to activate using the ISO1783 IL by a node.

|  | Define a node in the database. For this node set all NmJ1939xxx attributes for the designated device name. Set value Iso11783_IL.dll to the node attribute NodeLayerModules. Add additional send messages if required. If the ISO11783 IL should communicate with a Virtual Terminal: Configure the node as described here: Configuration If the ISO11783 IL should communicate with a Task Controller or a Data Logger: Configure the node as described here: Configuration If the ISO11783 IL has to communicate with a TIM Server or TIM Client: Configure the node as described here: Configuration (TIM Component - ISO11783) |

## ISO11783 Network Management

The ISO11783 IL automatically performs address claiming at start-up. For this purpose the IL uses the device name that is defined in the database with the NmJ1939xxx node attributes. More information about network management and behavior in the event of address conflicts can be found on the following page: J1939/ISO11783 Interaction Layer Network Management.

## Using the ISO11783 Interaction Layer in vTESTstudio

The CAPL functions of the ISO11783 Interaction Layer are not available in vTESTstudio by default. To use it in 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [ISO11783/ISO11783IL] ISO11783ILOverview


# ISO11783 Interaction Layer

ISO11783 » ISO11783 Interaction Layer

The ISO11783 Interaction Layer (short ISO11783 IL) is a library for quick and easy modeling of ECUs on the ISOBUS. In addition to functions for signal-based access to the ISOBUS (similar to those of the J1939 Interaction Layer), the ISO11783 IL offers many ways to simulate the entire communication between a simulated ISO11783 ECU and a virtual terminal, Task Controller or data logger in a very simple way. The simulation of a TIM Server or TIM Client is also possible with a few CAPL code lines. In addition, the ISO11783 IL simplifies simulation of an auxiliary input ECU considerably.

## Functionality

- Signal-based access to the ISOBUS

- Sending of periodic messages

- Sending of messages when setting and changing a signal

- Sending messages in response to a request (ISO 11783-3)

- Support of Address Claiming (ISO 11783-5)

- Support of transport protocols

- Data Transfer (ISO 11783-3)

- Extended Data Transfer (ISO11783-6)

- Fast-Packet

- Communication with a virtual terminal and one or more auxiliary ECUs (Object Pool component)

- Automatic read-in of an object pool and sending to a virtual terminal

- System variables, which are automatically generated from the object pool, offer a simplified interface both for communication between the simulated node and a virtual terminal and for interfacing to an auxiliary ECU or simulating it

- Object Pool API offers additional ways to influence the communic

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [ISO11783/ISO11783IL] ISO11783ILUsage


# Use the ISO11783 Interaction Layer

ISO11783 » ISO11783 Interaction Layer » Use the ISO11783 Interaction Layer

| Page Content:ISO11783 Interaction Layer (ISO11783 IL) | Further Pages |
| --- | --- |
| ISO11783 IL Behavior | ISO11783 Set Implementation Version | ISO11783 IL CAPL Functions |
| ISO11783 IL Activate Functions | Fault Injection | ISO11783 Diagnostics |

## ISO11783 IL Behavior [▲ back]

The ISO11783 IL automatically registers on the network after measurement start with the Address-Claimed message.

If configured, the object pool and process files are read in and transmitted to a Virtual Terminal or a Task Controller. TIM components must first be configured more precisely in CAPL in order to read in certificates and keys and then start initialization and authentication with another TIM ECU.

If the ISO11783 IL is not to be started together with the measurement start of CANoe, you can call the function ISO11783IL_ControlInit in the event handler on preStart. The IL can then be started at a later time with the function ISO11782IL_ControlStart.

## ISO11783 Set Implementation Version [▲ back]

The implementation versions of the individual components can be set as follows:

- With the database attributes ISO11783PDDVersion and ISO11783IOPVersion. (Object pool component and process data component only).

- With the Component Configuration dialog of the node in the Simulation Setup. (Object pool and process data component only).

- with CAPL functions Iso11783IL_OPSe

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [ISO11783/ISO11783IL/ILObjectPoolComponent] ILOPCommObjPoolAndVT


# Changing and Transmitting Object Values

ISO11783 » ISO11783 Interaction Layer » Object Pool Component » Changing and Transmitting Object Values

Values of objects from the object pool can be changed either via the automatically generated system variables or via the object pool API and transferred to the Virtual Terminal.

|  | Note System variables are only created automatically if the object pool file is defined in the database (attribute ISO11783IOPFilename) or in the Component Configuration dialog of the node. |

## Changing Object Values Using System Variables

Some objects in the object pool that are used for user inputs or displaying values (e.g. output number, input number, number variable, etc.) have a special attribute value. For these objects, the ISO11783 IL offers various ways to change the values and react to changes.

A system variable OBX00XYZ_value is prepared for the attribute. Each change to the system variables is transmitted to the relevant object in the object pool of the virtual terminal with Change Numeric Value command or Change String Value command. Simultaneously, the CAPL callback Iso11783IL_OPOnValueChange is called.

## Changing Object Values Using Object Pool API

In the CAPL program, the Iso11783IL_OPSetNumericValue or Iso11783IL_OPSetStringValue is called. This changes the new value in the object pool and sends it to the relevant system variable. The Change Numeric Value command or Change String Value command is used to send the new value t

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [ISO11783/ISO11783IL/ILObjectPoolComponent] ILOPConfiguration


# Configuration

ISO11783 » ISO11783 Interaction Layer » Object Pool Component » Configuration

After setting up the ISO11783 IL, perform the following additional steps in the database:

- Add the message VT21 (PGN = EC00h) in the Tx list of the node.

- Set the node attributes ISO11783IOPFilename and ISO11783IOPVersion.

| Attribute Name | Attribute Typ | Value Type | Value Range | Default | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| ISO11783IOPFilename | Node | String | — | empty | Relative or absolute path to the file with the object pool, e.g. IOP/node1.iop. |
| ISO11783IOPVersion | Node | Enum | 0..5 | 3 | Version of the ISO11783 specification (part 6) supported by the interaction layer. |

Alternatively, the object pool file and the version can also be specified in the Component Configuration dialog of the node in the Simulation Setup or at runtime using the CAPL commands Iso11783IL_OPLoad and Iso11783IL_OPSetProperty.

|  | Cross Reference For other database attributes that are provided by J1939, see Overview of J1939 database attributes. |

See Also

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |

### [ISO11783/ISO11783IL/ILObjectPoolComponent] ILOPStates


# State Graph

ISO11783 » ISO11783 Interaction Layer » Object Pool Component » State Graph

The following diagram shows the states and transitions of the object pool component. With each state change, the CAPL callback function Iso11783IL_OPOnStateChange is called.

The current state can be polled with the CAPL function Iso11783IL_OPGetState.

| State | Description |
| --- | --- |
| 1 | During the measurement start, the object pool API is in this state. It is automatically switched over to state 2. |
| 2 | The object pool API is not active. Only the functions Iso11783IL_OPActivate or Iso11783IL_OPLoad can be called. If the Object Pool Component is activated (with Iso11783IL_OPActivate), it is changed to state 3. |
| 3 | The object pool API waits until Address Claiming has been successfully executed, and the node has a valid address. |
| 4 | The object pool API waits for the Parameter Group status from the virtual terminal. When this is received, a switch is made to state 5 and an initialization is performed with the virtual terminal. |
| 5 | The initialization is performed with the virtual terminal. The following actions are performed: Working set is identified Command Get Memory is executed Command Get Number Of Soft Keys is executed Command Get Text Font Data is executed Command Get Hardware is executed After this phase has been successfully completed, a switch is made to the state 6 or 7. In case of error, a switch is made back to state 2 (inactive). |
| 6 | If a version 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [ISO11783/ISO11783IL/ILObjectPoolComponent] ILOPSupportAuxiliaryControl


# Auxiliary Control Support

ISO11783 » ISO11783 Interaction Layer » Object Pool Component » Auxiliary Control Support

The concept of the Auxiliary Control (consisting of auxiliary inputs and auxiliary functions) is a component of the ISO11783 specification and is described in Part 6, Appendix.

- Auxiliary inputs describe various physical input elements, such as switches, sliders or joysticks. These elements are used to accept user inputs directly (i.e. without virtual terminal) and route them to one or more nodes. It makes sense to use Auxiliary Inputs if, for example, user control is not convenient enough via a virtual terminal or if, for ergonomic reasons, it needs to be implemented differently (e.g. compare a virtual joystick to a physical joystick).

- Auxiliary functions are the counterparts to the specific auxiliary inputs.

Currently, 15 different types of auxiliary inputs and 15 types of auxiliary functions are defined. For each auxiliary input type there is one auxiliary function type.

## Auxiliary Input Objects

Each assigned auxiliary input object transmits its state to the ISO11783 Interaction Layer via the automatically provided system variables (generated by ISO11783 IL). For some auxiliary input types (Type ID 0, 1, 2, 3 and 4), one system variable with the designation value1 is provided, for all others (Type ID 5 to 14), two system variables with the designations value1 and value2 are provided. The system variable value1 (and if available - also value2) s

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [ISO11783/ISO11783IL/ILObjectPoolComponent] ILOPSysVariables


# Elements of the Object Pool and System Variables

ISO11783 » ISO11783 Interaction Layer » Object Pool Component » Elements of the Object Pool and System Variables

To clarify the relationship between object pool objects and system variables, we take as an example an object pool, as it is displayed in the virtual terminal, and system variables that are generated for the object pool.

|  | Note System variables are only created automatically if the object pool file is defined in the database (attribute ISO11783IOPFilename) or in the Component Configuration dialog of the node. |

The generated system variables are grouped by specific criteria: all system variables that describe the various attributes of an object from the object pool are generated in the same namespace. The identifier for the namespace is always constructed according to the following pattern:

„OBJ“ + ObjectID + „_“ + Object type

|  | Examples OBJ00001_workingSet OBJ00050_fontAtribute OBJ00010_numberVariable |

In turn, all object namespaces are assigned the „OP“ identifier. This is organized under the namespace with the name of the node from the database (e.g. „Loader“).

The ISO11783 IL monitors the generated system variables throughout the simulation. As soon as a system variable changes in value (e.g. from a panel or by a direct user input), this change is immediately sent to the virtual terminal with a VT message. In this way, the user can modify the display of the virtual terminal without having to wri

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [ISO11783/ISO11783IL/ILObjectPoolComponent] ILOPTransmitAttrValuesToVT


# Changing and Transmitting Object Attributes

ISO11783 » ISO11783 Interaction Layer » Object Pool Component » Changing and Transmitting Object Attributes

Attribute values of objects from the object pool can be changed either via the generated system variables or via the object pool API and transferred to the Virtual Terminal.

The ISO11783 specification, Part 6, defines two methods for sending a new attribute value from one simulated node to a virtual terminal.

- By the Change Attribute Command method (VT function AFh)

This method can only be used for attributes for which an attribute ID is defined.

- By a specialized instruction such as:

- Change Background Color (VT function A7h)

- Change Numeric Value (VT function A8h)

- Change Active Mask (VT function ADh)

- Change Soft Key Mask (VT function AEh)

- Change String Value (VT function B3h) etc.

For some attributes, only one method may be used, while for others either method may be used.

### System Variables

If only one method is allowed for an attribute, this method is used by the ISO11783 IL. If both methods are permitted, then the ISO11783 IL uses the specialized instruction and not the Change Attribute Command method.

### Object Pool API

If an attribute has an Attrbute ID defined, you can change the attribute value using the CAPL function CAPL-Funktion Iso11783IL_OPSetAttribute.

There are also several CAPL functions available that start with Iso11783IL_OPChange... that allow you to change certain attribute

[...truncated for brevity...]

ISO11783 » ISO11783 Interaction Layer » TIM Component

| General | Function List |
| --- | --- |
| The TIM (Tractor Implement Management) component is a part of the ISO11783 Interaction Layer (ISO11783 IL) and is needed to implement communication between a TIM server and a TIM client or to test a TIM server or client. If the demonstration mode of the TIM component is not active, different certificates and an additional authentication DLL (AuthLib.dll) are required for the communication, which are not delivered with CANoe. | Authentication Library Test Support Configuration Fault Injection Client States Server States |
| Setting Up a TIM Client | Setting Up a TIM Server |

Set node attribute NodeLayerModules to value Iso11783_IL.dll. Add following messages to the Tx list of the node: Address Claimed (PGN 0xEE00) TIM21 (PGN 0x2400) AUTH21 (PGN 0x6F00) ) ISOBUS Compliance Certification (ICC, PGN 0xFD42) Product Identification Information (PII, PGN 0xFC8D) Add a new network node to the Simulation Setup in CANoe, open the configuration dialog of the network node and choose the TIM client node of the database. Assign a CAPL file to the network node. After configuration of the TIM client you can connect the TIM client to a TIM server with the function Iso11783IL_TIMConnectToServer. |

### [ISO11783/ISO11783IL/TIMComponent] TIMComponentConfiguration


# Configuration

ISO11783 » ISO11783 Interaction Layer » TIM Component » Configuration

Before starting the simulated node (TIM client or TIM server) you must configure it.

To do this, perform the following additional steps in the database after setting up ISO11783 IL:

- Add the messages TIM12 (PGN 2300h) and AUTH12 (PGN 7000h) to the Tx list of the TIM server node.

- Add the messages TIM21 (PGN 2400h) and AUTH21 (PGN 6F00h) to the Tx list of the TIM client node.

You must also define the following in the CAPL program of the node:

| Properties | CAPL Functions | Server or Client |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Certificates, CRLs and private key | Iso11783IL_TIMAddCertificate | Server and client |
| Iso11783IL_TIMSetCRL | Server and client |
| Iso11783IL_TIMSetPrivateKey | Server and client |
| Compliance certification information | Iso11783IL_TIMSetSupportedFacility | Server |
| Iso11783IL_TIMSetRequiredFacility | Client |
| Iso11783IL_TIMAssignFunction | Client, optional |
| TIM function facilities | Iso11783IL_TIMSetProductIdentification | Server and client |
| Brand and model of the TIM participant | Iso11783IL_TIMSetComplianceCertification | Server and client |
| Compliance certification information | Iso11783IL_TIMSetProperty | Server and client, optional |

After starting the TIM server component with Iso11783IL_TIMActivateServer the simulated node sends its status notification, exchanges certificates with TIM clients and verifies them.

After starting the TIM client compo

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [ISO11783/ISO11783IL/TIMComponent] TIMComponentFaultInjection


# Fault Injections

ISO11783 » ISO11783 Interaction Layer » TIM Component » Fault Injections

You can influence the communication between a simulated TIM participant with a TIM counterpart in various ways. E.g. you can use invalid certificate files or you can modify the TIM server status notification (with CAPL function Iso11783IL_TIMSetServerStatus). Furthermore, you can influence the standard communication procedure between the client and server.

### Influence the Standard Procedure

Sometimes it is necessary to influence the standard procedure: certain steps should be skipped or executed in a different order, e.g. to inject an error or to avoid an implementation error.

The states of the internal status machine of a TIM server/client are defined on page Server States and Client States. In the standard run, the simulated node runs through all its states automatically. However, the user has the option of only allowing the process flow up to a certain state. When the state is reached, the simulated node stops (except for the status notification – it is still sent), and the user can intervene manually: e.g. after a pause to resume the automatic sequence, to send one or more notifications or to resume the automatic sequence with another state.

### Graphic: Straightforward vs. controlled running:

### A state change for a client/server connection looks like this:

|  | Description | Example for the TIM Client |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | In state X, an event occurs that leads 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [ISO11783/ISO11783NL] ISO11783NLOverview


# ISO11783 Node Layer

ISO11783 » ISO11783 Node Layer

The ISO11783 Node Layer simplifies the simulation of a ISO11783 node with CAPL. The ISO11783 Node Layer offers the following functionality:

- Address Claiming

- Node table

- Sending and reception of Parameter Groups

- Transport protocols

- Is used by generated CAPL programs

Furthermore the following APIs are available:

- Object Pool API: Manages the communication with a Virtual Terminal and transmits an object pool file (IOP) automatically to the Virtual Terminal.

- Process data API: Administers a device description file (ISO11783-10) and creates a process data dictionary. This is automatically transmitted to the Task Controller.

|  | Cross Reference The sample ISO11783SystemDemo shows the use of the ISO11783 Node Layer. |

## Include the Node Layer

To use the communication node layer with a node in the Simulation Setup, it must be included. The node layer ISO11783DLL.DLL must be assigned to the node attributeNodeLayerModules.

## Using the Node Layer

The following example shows how the ISO11783 node layer is used in a CAPL program:

|  | Example dword ecuHandle;char deviceName[] = { 0x01, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00, 0x00 };ecuHandle = Iso11783CreateECU( "default", deviceName );Iso11783ECUGoOnline( ecuHandle, 0 );// ...char data[8];// send parameter groupIso11783TxReqPG( ecuHandle , 0xF001, 0xFF, 4, 8, data );// ...Iso11783DestroyECU( ecuHandle ); |

Function overview

| Version 15© Vector Informatik G

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [ISO11783/ISO11783NL/ObjectPoolAPI] OPOverview


# Object Pool API

ISO11783 » ISO11783 Node Layer » Object Pool API

The Object Pool API of the ISO11783 Node Layer manages the object pool which is needed for the user interface on a Virtual Terminal. It is part of the ISO11783 Node Layer and controlled with CAPL functions. The Object Pool API requires an object pool file (IOP).

## Features

- Connection Management with the Virtual Terminal (Initialization procedure, Maintenance message)

- Load an object pool file (IOP)

- Transmit the object pool to the Virtual Terminal

- Access on the local object pool

- Sending commands to the Virtual Terminal

- Handle messages from the Virtual Terminal

- Bind object values from the object pool to environment variables

- Management of auxiliary inputs and functions

|  | Cross Reference The sample ISO11783SystemDemo shows the use of the ISO11783 Node Layer. |

ISO11783 Node Layer | Object Pool API - CAPL Overview | State graph of the Object Pool API | Object Pool API - Usage

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [ISO11783/ISO11783NL/ObjectPoolAPI] opUsage


# Object Pool API - Usage

ISO11783 » ISO11783 Node Layer » Process Data API » Usage

The initialization is done in CAPL before or after Iso11783ECUGoOnline. First an object pool filed must be loaded and activated. Vice versa is also possible, first activate, the load. The following example show the necessary CAPL calls to connect to the Virtual Terminal and transmit the object pool.

|  | Example handle = Iso11783CreateECU( gECUName );Iso11783ECUGoOnline(handle, gECUAddress );Iso11783OPLoad( handle, "ObjectPool.iop" );Iso11783OPActivate( handle, "pool001" ); |

After loading the object pool file, the object pool must be activated with the CAPL function Iso11783OPActivate. Then the Object Pool API waits for a status message from the Virtual Terminal. After that the initialization procedure starts and the object pool is transmitted to the Virtual Terminal. As long as the Object Pool API is active the maintenance message is sent. If the connection to the Virtual Terminal is lost, the Object Pool API waits for a status message again.

Environment variables can be connected to objects (variable, input or output objects) in the object pool. The environment variables must be defined in the database and must have a appropriate type.

|  | Example Iso11783OPConnectEnvVar( handle, 1000, EnvVar_Value ); |

A change on a environment variable triggers a Change Value command with the value of the environment variable. If a Change Value Response is received, the new value is put to the en

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [ISO11783/procedures] GNSSReceiverSimulation


# Simulate GNSS Receiver

ISO11783 » Simulate GNSS Receiver

The options .J1939 and .ISO11783 offer you two ways to simulate a GNSS receiver and thus generate J1939 or ISO11738 messages with position data.

With the GNSS Simulator you can drive predefined routes with an adjustable speed with just a few clicks.

If you want to define more complicated distances or set the speed depending on other sizes, you can use the GNSS Nodelayer. With this you can set waypoints or course in CAPL and adjust the speed.

### [ISO11783/procedures] ISO11783ProtocolActivation


# ISO11783 Protocol Activation

ISO11783 » ISO11783 Protocol Activation

To activate the ISO11783-specific functions, at least one database within the configuration must contain an attribute with the name ProtocolType and the value ISO11783. This attribute must have the object type Network and the value type String.

|  | Note In the directory of the sample configurations, in the Database folder (Open Folder), there is the database ISO11783.dbc, in which the ProtocolType attribute is already correctly defined. This database also contains all ISO11783-relevant messages. |
