### [J1939/j1939basics] j1939CrcAndCounter


# J1939 Functional Safety – Messages With Integrated Checksum and Counter

J1939 » J1939 Basics » J1939 Functional Safety – Messages With Integrated Checksum and Counter

The J1939 Digital Annex specifies checksums and message counters for more than 200 messages (as of March 2021) to detect error states of the ECUs involved. The message counter and the checksum are part of the user data of a PG. Each time a message is transmitted, the message counter is incremented by one. Since the checksum is calculated from the user data, the checksum consequently also changes with each correct transmission, regardless of whether other signals have changed or not. However, if the message counter is not increased during a subsequent transmission, or if the checksum does not match the user data, the receiver can assume an error condition of the sender.

The Digital Annex describes five different calculation rules for the checksums. Two of them are based on a typical CRC (Cyclic Redundancy Counter) calculation with eight-bit length. The other three calculations use simple addition, bit shift and bit mask. In CANoe, the calculation rules are identified by a capital letter (A-E) and stored in the message database. Six possible positions within the message are specified for the message counter. We identify them with lowercase letters a-f. The following tables show the common combinations and positions of checksums and message counters, as well as exemplary names of messages using this combinati

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [J1939/j1939basics] j1939DeviceName


# Device Name

J1939 » J1939 Basics » Device Name

The 64-bit device name of a J1939 ECU consists of the following individual segments:

| Segment | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Arbitrary Address Capable (AAC) | represents the arbitrary address capable flag |
| Industry Group | represents the industry group the device belongs to |
| Vehicle System Instance | represents the instance of the vehicle system |
| Vehicle System | represents the vehicle system |
| Function | represents the function |
| Function Instance | represents the function instance |
| ECU Instance | represents the ECU instance |
| Manufacturer Code | represents the manufacturer code of the device |
| Identity Number | represents the identity number |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [J1939/j1939basics] j1939ExtCanId


# Extended CAN Identifier

CANoe » J1939 » J1939 Basics » Extended CAN Identifier

The extended CAN identifier used for J1939 receives, in addition to the PGN also the priority, source address, and DataPage information. This is depicted schematically below:

| 3 bit | 1 bit | 1 bit | 8 bit | 8 bit | 8 bit |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Priority | EDP | DP | PDU Format | PDU Specific | Source |
|  | PGN |  |

If the value of PDU format < 240 (peer-to-peer, PDU Format 1), then PDU Specific receives the recipient address of the Parameter Group. The GLOBAL address (255) can also be entered as a valid recipient address.

If the value of PDU format >= 240 (broadcast, PDU Format 2), then PDU format together with PDU specific forms the PGN.

J1939 basics | J1939 Parameter Group | J1939 PGN

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [J1939/j1939basics] j1939FunctionalSafety


# Functional Safety J1939-76

J1939 » J1939 Basics » Functional Safety J1939-76

## Introduction

The SAE J1939-76 standard (SAE J1939 Functional Safety Communications Protocol) describes a J1939 extension for the transmission of safety-critical data. This is achieved by sending an additional message (Safety Header Message, SHM) before the message containing the critical data (Safety Data Message, SDM). SHM and SDM then form a Safety Data Group (SDG).

THe SHM contains the following information:

- a sequence counter (per SDG, 5 Bit)

- the CRC over the data of the SDM (32 Bit)

- the inverted CAN ID of the SDM (ohne Priorität, 26 Bit)

The sender (Producer) generates the SHM and SDM and sends them in the correct order and in compliance with the time constraints. The receiver (Consumer) is responsible for checking the sequence and time constraints as well as the integrity of the transmitted data.

The following times must be observed when transferring the SDG:

| SRVT | Safety-Relevant Validation Time | Time between a SHM and a SDM |
| --- | --- | --- |
| SCT | Safety Cycle Time | Time between continuous SDM instances |

The time base for the calculation of SRVTMaximum and SCTMaximum is derived from the cycle time (Transmission Rate) in the SAE J1939 PG, which is to be transmitted as SDM.

The calculation of the maximum cycle time between two SDM instances is defined as follows:

| SDG Timing Basis | SCTMaximum |
| --- | --- |
| ≤ 200 ms | 150 % of the timing basis |
| > 200

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [J1939/j1939basics] j1939NMT


# Network Management

CANoe » J1939 » J1939 Basics » Network Management

On a J1939 network, each ECU has a unique address. The address may exist only once. The device address is represented by an 8-bit value. This is also coded in the CAN identifier. The device address is contained in each sent PGN. In J1939, 255 device addresses are defined, whereby two addresses have a special meaning:

| Address | Description |
| --- | --- |
| 0..253 | Valid addresses for communication |
| 254 | NULL address A device has no valid address. |
| 255 | GLOBAL address. For global addressing for specific Parameter Groups or for the transport protocols. |

Each ECU has a predefined address. Another identification characteristic of an ECU is the device name. This is a 64-bit value that serves to describe the functionality of a device and also provides information about the manufacturer.

## Address Claiming

Before a device may participate in sending on a network, it must log onto the network with a valid address. For this, the predefined device address together with the device name is sent to the network in the boot sequence of an ECU. This procedure is called Address Claiming. The AddressClaim Parameter Group handles the sending of this information. If the predefined address of an ECU is already present on the network, the device name serves as a prioritizing aid. Here, the device has the higher priority whose 64-bit device name has a smaller numeric value.

|  | Cross Reference The sample con

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [J1939/j1939basics] j1939PGandPGN


# Parameter Groups and PGNs

CANoe » J1939 » J1939 Basics » Parameter Groups and PGNs (J1939)

## J1939 Parameter Group (PG)

A J1939 Parameter Group (PG) is a set of data parameters which are always transferred together as a logical unit (equivalent to a CAN frame, which contains a set of CAN signals). It represents an atomic data burst with which electronic ECUs exchange information. Most parameter groups contain one protocol data unit (PDU) each from the application layer – e.g. motor/gearbox data or diagnostics data – e.g. active Diagnostic Trouble Codes. However, there are also parameter groups which support the basic service of the J1939 protocol, such as PG Request, Transport Protocol and Address Claim.

Each Parameter Group is uniquely identified by a so-called Parameter Group number (PGN). Parameter groups with the same PGN are interpreted in the same manner and, in fact, independently of their sender and receiver (that is, from their source and destination address). The definition of the parameter groups and the parameters contained within them can be found in the J1939 Digital Annex or in one of the corresponding J1939 subdocuments, e.g. J1939-21, J1939-71, J1939-73, J1939-75, J1939-76 and J1939-81.

The length of a Parameter Group is not limited to the length of a CAN frame. A Parameter Group typically has a length of between 8 bytes and 1,785 bytes. Parameter groups with more than 8 bytes require a transport protocol for transfer.

## Interpretation of the CAN I

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [J1939/j1939basics] j1939SPACE


# SPACE

J1939 » J1939 Basics » SPACE

During the object-oriented memory access the SPACE specifies how the object ID is to be interpreted. J1939 actually specifies only the SPACE for SPNs (value 0). That means that the SPN (Suspect Parameter Number) is identical with the object ID. The values 1..127 are reserved for future use by the SAE, the values 128..255 can be used user-specific.

In CANoe objects are defined in a CAN database (DBC) by using signals. With the signal attribute SPN the SPN of the signal and the ID for SPACE 0 are set.

## User-defined SPACE Values

To use a user-defined SPACE you have to define the node attribute J1939SpaceSigAttr (value type String) in the CAN database. Then you can define the supported SPACEs for each node by using this node attribute.

For this you define signal attributes (value type Integer) in the following syntax:

<name of first signal attribute>=<number of first SPACE> <name of second signal attribute>=<number of second SPACE> etc.

Now you can define the object ID within the appropriate SPACE for each signal by setting the attribute value.

|  | Example With the following example values you define an object with object ID 1 within the SPACE 128 of the device. Object type Attribute name Value type Default value Samle value Node J1939SpaceSigAttr String EngineSpace=128 Signal EngineSpace Integer 0 1 | Object type | Attribute name | Value type | Default value | Samle value | Node | J1939SpaceSigAttr | String |  | EngineSpace=128 |

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [J1939/j1939basics] j1939TP


# Transport Protocol

CANoe » J1939 » J1939 Basics » Transport Protocol

If Parameter Groups with more than 8 data bytes should be sent or received, the data content must be distributed across several CAN messages. This is handled, as is Address Claiming, by specially-reserved Parameter Groups. These are the TP_CM PG, a command for transmitting the PG context (data length and PGN to be transmitted) and TP_DT this PG contains the actual use data and a sequence number.

For the sequence number, there is an 8-bit value available; a maximum of 255 sequences can be transmitted per protocol. So 255 * 7 = 1785 data bytes can be transmitted with the J1939 transport protocols.

Adjusted to both procedures peer-to-peer and broadcast, two different transport protocols are supported, broadcast and peer-to-peer too. To use the transport protocols in CANoe/CANalyzer , these must be activated first.

## BAM Transport Protocol

For the global transmission of Parameter Groups, the Broadcast Announce Message (BAM) transport protocol is used. Here a BAM transmission with the TP_CM "BAM" is announced and then the data packets are sent at a minimum distance of 50ms. No confirmation from the recipient is sent.

## CMDT transport protocol

For the specific transmission of Parameter Groups the Connection Mode Data Transfer (CMDT) protocol is used. Here, other than for the BAM protocol, control mechanisms are used by the recipient for the connection during the transmission. With CMDT, the sender ini

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [J1939/j1939basics] j1939Usage


# Activate the J1939 Protocol

CANoe » J1939» Activate the J1939 Protocol

The J1939 expansions can only be used on a J1939 bus (CANoe) or channel (CANalyzer). Therefore, a bus or channel must first be assigned a J1939 database in order to be able to use

- the J1939 transport protocols and

- the J1939-specific interpretation on the Trace Window

## Activation

To activate the J1939 protocol, a J1939 database must be used. By creating the network attribute ProtocolType with the value J1939 a database (CANdb) becomes a J1939 database.

CANoe/CANalyzer can receive and send the J1939 transport protocols CMDT and BAM. The transport protocols must first be activated in CANoe/CANalyzer. With a newly created configuration, the transport protocols are not active.

This attribute has been created for the included database J1939.dbc. This database can be used in individual configurations.

|  | Cross Reference The sample configuration TransportProtocolTransportProtocol demonstrates how the J1939 transport protocol is used in CANoe. |

J1939 Parameter Group | J1939 PGN

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [J1939/j1939IL] j1939ILActivateFeatures


# Activation of J1939 IL Functions

J1939 » J1939 Interaction Layer » Activation of J1939 IL Functions

The default configuration of the J1939 IL does not use network management (Address Claiming) and does not respond to request with a negative acknowledgment, if a message is not supported. This can be enabled by assign several messages.

### Use Network Management

To enable the Address Claiming it must be activated first. If it is not activated the J1939 IL send its messages from a fix configured address. To learn how to activate network management, see the following page: J1939/ISO11783 Interaction Layer Network Management.

### Use Acknowledgment

To enable the acknowledgment of the J1939 IL, the acknowledgment Parameter Group (E800h) must be assigned to the node as Tx message. If the J1939 IL finds this message in the Tx list of the node and the requested PGN is not in the Tx list of this node, the request will be responded with a negative acknowledgment.

J1939 Interaction Layer | Function Overview

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [J1939/j1939IL] j1939ILConfigureDB


# Configure Database

J1939 » J1939 Interaction Layer » Configure Database

The J1939 IL is configured through the assigned database. The node must be defined in the database.

The J1939 IL must be assigned to the node. For this the node attribute NodeLayerModules must have the value J1939_IL.DLL. The J1939 IL is implemented as DLL, which is located in the exec32 directory of the CANoe installation.

Furthermore the address must be defined, which the J1939 IL should use as source address. The address must be defined with the node attribute NmStationAddress.

|  | Note The input field Address in the node configuration dialog of the CANdb++ Editor contains the value of the node attribute NmStationAddress. |

Each message which should be sent by a node with the J1939 IL, must be assigned to the node as Tx message. Furthermore the send type must be configured and depending on the send type more attributes must be defined.

In principle the transmission behavior of all messages is influenced by the send types of the message and all contained signals. The results of all send types are prioritized in the following order beginning with the highest priority:

- spontaneous transmission

- fast cyclic transmission

- cyclic transmission

- no transmission

| Attribute Name | Attribute Type | Value Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| GenMsgILSupport | Message | Enum | message is handled by the J1939 IL No: J1939 IL ignores this message Yes: J1939 IL handles this message |


[...truncated for brevity...]

### [J1939/j1939IL] j1939ILConnectEnvvarsSignals


# Connect Environment Variables with Signals (deprecated)

J1939 » J1939 Interaction Layer » Connect Environment Variables with Signals (deprecated)

The J1939 IL can connect environment variables with signals. If the value of an environment variable is changed, the new value is also written to the connect signal. The message of the signal is sent depending the configured send type.

The environment variables must be defined by the user.

The connection between the signal and the environment variable is made with the signal attribute GenSigEVName. This attribute must contain the name of the environment variable.

The default value of the attribute can contain the following placeholders:

- @Nodename

- @Msgname

- @Signame

If the default value contains these placeholders, the value of GenSigEVName need not to be set. The J1939 IL creates the name of the environment variable with these placeholders. The environment variable must be defined according the used naming schema.

|  | Note The creation of environment variables is no longer supported. Instead, use system variables directly in CANoe. Currently CANoe still supports the use of environment variables. This support will not be available in future versions. |

J1939 Interaction Layer

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [J1939/j1939IL] j1939ILCreateTxMsg


# Setup a Tx message

J1939 » J1939 Interaction Layer » Setup a Tx message

This page explains how to setup Tx message for the J1939 Interaction Layer. The Tx messages of a node are sent by the J1939 IL. The signal value can be changed via CAPL or panels.

To setup Tx messages, the node must already be configured for the J1939 IL, see setup a node with the J1939 IL.

|  | To send messages with the J1939 IL, the messages must be assigned to the node as Tx message in the CANdb++ Editor. Messages can be assigned with drag and drop in the CANdb++ Editor. The send type of the message must be configured. For that the message attribute GenMsgSendType must be edited. Depending on the send type more message and signal attributes must be configured. |

## Setup Cyclic Messages

|  | For cyclic messages the attribute GenMsgSendType must be set to cyclic. The node attribute GetMsgCycleTime must contain the cycle time in milliseconds. If the J1939 IL is started, it begins with sending of the cyclic messages after the Address Claiming. The attribute GenMsgStartDelayTime defines a start delay in milliseconds. If a cyclic message contains a OnChange or OnWrite signal, the message can be triggered before reaching the cycle time. |

## Send Messages on Signal Change

|  | To send a message on change of a signal value, the attribute GenMsgSendType must be set to noMsgSendType. The signal attribute GenSigSendType of the signal, which should trigger sending, must be set to OnChange. The message 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [J1939/j1939IL] j1939ILDiagnostics


# J1939 Diagnostics

J1939 » J1939 Interaction Layer » J1939 Diagnostics

In order for the J1939 IL to send diagnostic messages automatically, first activate J1939 diagnostic support via the J1939ILActivateDiagnosticsSupport function. Via CAPL you can activate or deactivate errors which are then communicated via the J1939 diagnostic messages (DM1 and DM2).

Once diagnostic support has been activated, the J1939 IL begins cyclically sending the message DM1 (Active Diagnostic Trouble Codes, PGN 0xFECA). If you activate an error (Diagnostic Trouble Code, DTC) with the function J1939ILActivateDTC, this DTC will be contained in message DM1 in the future. To prevent the DTC from being reported, you can deactivate it with the J1939ILDeactivateDTC function. deaktivieren. The DTC then moves to the list of previously active errors, which can be queried via message DM2 (Previously Active Diagnostic Trouble Codes, PGN 0xFECB).

By a J1939 request of the message DM3 (Diagnostic Data Clear/Reset of Previously Active DTCs, PGN 0xFECC) and DM11 (Diagnostic Data Clear/Reset for Active DTCs, PGN 0xFED3) you can reset errors.

When diagnostic support is enabled, J1939 requests for messages DM6, DM12, DM23, DM27, DM28, DM35, DM41, DM42, DM43, DM44, DM45, DM46, DM47, DM48, DM49, DM50, DM51, DM52 are also answered automatically. With the functions J1939ILAddDTC and J1939ILRemoveDTC you can define the contents of these diagnostic messages.

The description of the corresponding CAPL functions you fi

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [J1939/j1939IL] j1939ILEnvVars


# Environment Variables (deprecated)

J1939 » J1939 Interaction Layer » Environment Variables (deprecated)

The J1939 IL sets some environment variables with the current state and the current address. The activation of the J1939 IL and change of the address can be done via environment variables. These environment variables are optional and they need not be defined.

| Environment Variable | Function |
| --- | --- |
| Env<nodename>_ILAddress | Current address of the J1939 IL. Setting this environment variable causes the J1939 IL to change the source address. |
| Env<nodeame>_ILEnable | Shows, if the J1939 IL is active. 1 = Active 0 = Not active Setting this environment variable activates or deactivates the J1939 IL. |
| Env<nodename>_ILState | Contains the current state of the J1939 IL as String: Initialized Claiming Active Stopped Suspended |

The environment variables must be defined by the user. The respective node name must be entered in the environment variable.

|  | Note The creation of environment variables is no longer supported. Instead, use system variables directly in CANoe. Currently CANoe still supports the use of environment variables. This support will not be available in future versions. |

J1939 Interaction Layer

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [J1939/j1939IL] j1939ILErrorDetection


# Error Detection

J1939 » J1939 Interaction Layer » Error Detection

In some cases, it is necessary to take measures to detect interference from transmitting ECUs.

The J1939 DigitalAnnex defines a large number of messages that also contain a checksum and a message counter in the user data.

The J1939 Interaction Layer supports you in automatically setting the message counter and the checksum for these messages.

To use this functionality you have to configure the database.

- Make sure that the FsJ1939UseCrcAndCounter attribute is defined correctly.

- Define the calculation formula in the form [A-E][a-f] (e.g. Bc) for each message that the Interaction Layer is to provide with the checksum and message counter. Take the calculation rules from the table below. You can combine (with the exception of overlaps) the calculation of the checksum and the position of the message counter as you wish.

|  | Note CANoe has already supported the calculation of checksums in earlier versions. However, the function was only available to PGs with PGN 0, 1024, 2816, 61469 and 61483. You can still use this functionality. However, if you add the FsJ1939UseCrcAndCounter message attribute to your database, only the new implementation will be used. The following applies: only for those messages where the attribute has a value in the form [A-E][a-f], checksum and message counter are set. |

The following rules for calculating the checksum are available. The checksum rule is always defined with a c

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [J1939/j1939IL] j1939ILFaultInjection


# Fault Injection

J1939 » J1939 Interaction Layer » Fault Injection

With test modules you can influence the behavior of the J1939 Interaction Layer to inject errors. So you can produce communication errors within a simulation of the remaining bus. The SUT (System Under Test) must react on that errors.

The J1939 Interaction Layer must be assigned to one or more nodes in the Simulation Setup and sends the corresponding Parameter Groups. The signal values can be changed within test modules.

Within a test module the following CAPL functions are available to influence the behavior of the J1939 Interaction Layer:

| Function | Short Description |
| --- | --- |
| TestDisableMsg | disables the transmission of a Parameter Group |
| TestEnableMsg | enables the transmission of a Parameter Group |
| TestEnableMsgAllTx | enables all Tx Parameter Groups |
| TestDisableMsgAllTx | disables all Tx Parameter Groups |
| TestSetMsgEvent | stimulates the transmission of a non-cyclic Parameter Group |
| TestSetMsgCycleTime | changes the cycle time and enables the cyclic transmission of a Parameter Group |
| TestResetMsgCycleTime | resets the cycle time and the send type to the values defined in the database |
| TestSetMsgDlc | changes the DLC of a Parameter Group |
| TestResetMsgDlc | resets the DLC of a Parameter Group to the value defined in the database |
| TestResetAllFaultInjections | resets all values to the initial values defined in the database |

|  | Note The CAPL functions uses Par

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [J1939/j1939IL] j1939ILFunctionalSafety


# Functional Safety

J1939 » J1939 Interaction Layer » Functional Safety

The J1939 functional safety communication protocol according to J1939-76 improves the reliability and safety of the transmission of critical data. For this purpose, an additional message (Safety Header Message, SHM) is sent before the actual data message (Safety Data Message, SDM). The SHM contains information about the SDM as well as a sequence number. SHM and SDM then form a Safety Data Group (SDG).

In order for a message to be preceded by a SHM, configure it to be sent from a node. Also set the message attribute FsJ1939NeedsSHM in the database to the value Yes so that it is treated as an SDM. The contents of the SHM (information about the SDM, CRC, sequence number) are automatically determined by the J1939 IL when it is sent.

|  | Note The DLC of the SDM must not be larger than 8 bytes. |

At the time of transmission of the SDG, the SHM is sent first. The J1939 IL uses the same J1939 priority as the SDM. The SDM is then sent one millisecond later.

With the help of the FsJ1939Delay attribute you can increase the distance between SHM and SDM to values up to 1000 ms. The value of FsJ1939Delay must be smaller than half the value of the attribute GenMsgDelayTime (which describes the smallest possible distance between two consecutive messages).

|  | Note If the J1939 IL has to send a SDM with a DLC larger than 8 bytes during a measurement, no preceding SHM is sent and a corresponding error notificatio

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [J1939/j1939IL] j1939ILOverview


# Interaction Layer

J1939 » Interaction Layer

The J1939 Interaction Layer (J1939 IL) provides signal oriented access to the J1939 bus. The mapping of the signals on the Tx messages is done accordingly to the configuration in the used database. The J1939 IL manages the Tx messages, Rx messages are handled directly by CANoe and can be processed in CAPL.

The J1939 IL must be assigned to a node in the Simulation Setup. The J1939 IL transmits the Tx messages which are assigned to the node in the database. With attribute the transmission properties of the Tx message can be configured.

The signal values can be modified via CAPL, Panels or environment variables. The J1939 IL send the message of the signal according the configured send type. It is also possible to modify the signal value of any node in the Simulation Setup (which is using the J1939 IL) from a test module (J1939ILSetSignal). A test module can control several nodes. A device under test (DUT) can be supplied with messages/signals by this mechanism.

The J1939 Interaction Layer provides the following features:

- sending of cyclic messages

- sending of messages on change/write of a mapped signal

- signals can be connected to environment variables

- sending of messages on request (SAE J1939-21)

- support of Address Claiming (SAE J1939-81)

- support of transport protocols (SAE J1939-21, BAM, RTS/CTS)

- support of J1939 diagnostics (SAE J1939-73)

- support of Functional Safety (SAE J1939-76)

## Remote Control of

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [J1939/j1939IL] j1939ILStates


# States

J1939 » J1939 Interaction Layer » States

Depending on the state of the J1939 IL different actions are performed.

| State | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Initialized | At the start of measurement the J1939 IL is in this state. It switches automatically to state Claiming, if the function J1939ILControlInit does not suppress this behavior. |
| Claiming | During Address Claiming phase the J1939 IL is in this state. No messages are sent and the J1939 IL reacts only on Address Claiming messages. After successfully claiming an address, the state is changed to Active. |
| Active | The J1939 IL is active. Messages are sent according the configured send types. |
| Stopped | The J1939 IL is deactivated. No messages are sent and signals cannot be set. On change to this state a Cannot Claim Address message is sent. The J1939 IL changes to this state if, for example, a conflict occurs during address claiming, the advanced address claiming support is not activated and the J1939 IL loses address arbitration. How the behavior of the simulated node can be influenced after a lost address claim depends on the node attribute NmJ1939AAC and is described here. |
| Suspended | No messages are sent in this state. |

The following figure shows the state chart of the J1939 IL:

| State Transition | Description |
| --- | --- |
| 1 | At the start of measurement or when the function J1939ILControlStart is called, the J1939 IL is activated and Address Claiming is started. If NMT is not activat

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [J1939/j1939IL] j1939ILSystemVars


# System Variables

J1939 » J1939 Interaction Layer » System Variables

The J1939 IL creates three system variables for each node, the shows the current state and address of the J1939 IL.

- ILAddress

- ILState

- ILEnable

These system variables can be used to request and change the address and the state of the J1939 IL. Each system variable can be found in the namespace <NetworkName>::<NodeName>, e.g. J1939TestBus::Node1.

| System Variable | Function |
| --- | --- |
| ILAddress | displays the current address If this system variable is changed, the J1939 IL is triggered to use the new address. |
| ILEnable | displays if the J1939 IL is active 1: active 0: passive If the initial value is set to 0, the node will remain in passive state after measurement start. If the current value is set to 0 resp. 1 during the measurement, the node will change its state to passive resp. active. |
| ILState | displays the current state of the J1939 IL -1: Uninitialized 0: Initialized 1: Claiming 2: Active 3: Stopped 4: Suspended |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [J1939/j1939IL] j1939ILUsage


# Setup a Node

J1939 » J1939 Interaction Layer » Setup a Node

This section shows the steps, which are necessary to use the J1939 Interaction Layer with a node in the Simulation Setup. The J1939 IL is implemented as node layer and extends the features of a node. There a more CAPL functions available, but it is also possible to use the J1939 IL without CAPL programming.

|  | First there must be setup a node in the CANdb++ Editor. The easiest way is to use the database J1939.dbc or J1939base.dbc, which is delivered with CANoe, as base. These databases contains all necessary attributes.The databases are located in the directory Database of the J1939 examples. The J1939 IL must be assigned to the node. For this the node attribute NodeLayerModules must be set to the value J1939_IL.DLL. The attribute can be edited in the node configuration dialog of the CANdb++ Editor on the Attribute page. The address of the node must be defined. The address must be entered in the Address field in the node configuration dialog of the CANdb++ Editor. As alternative, the node attribute NmStationAddress can be edited. Additionally the 64 bit device name of the node should be defined for the Address Claiming. For this several NmJ1939-Attribute must be defined. These attributes are available on the Attribute page of the node configuration dialog of the CANdb++ Editor. The node can now be inserted into the Simulation Setup of CANoe. To create a new configuration the Model Generation Wizard can be use

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [J1939/j1939IL] j1939ILUsePanels


# Panels

J1939 » J1939 Interaction Layer » Panels

This page explains how to modify signal in panel, which are assigned to message of the J1939 IL.

The Tx messages must already be configured, see Setup Tx messages.

|  | Edit field, sliders, switches and buttons can be inserted into panels. In the configuration dialog of theses panels, signals can be assigned. A signal, which is transmitted with J1993 IL, can be assigned to a control. If the signal value is changed during the measurement with the control, the J1993 IL sends the message depending the configured send type of the signal. It is also possible to use a Message Control. In the configuration dialog of this control, the node which uses the J1993 IL must be assigned. The control shows all Tx message of the node. If the signal values are modified in the control, the J1993 IL send the message of the signal depending the configured send type. |

J1939 Interaction Layer | Activate Address Claiming

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [J1939/j1939tsl] j1939tslChecks


# Checks

J1939 » J1939 Test Service Library » Checks (J1939)

Checks observe and validate procedures in the network. A check must be setup in a CAPL or XML test module and runs in parallel to test cases. If a check detects a violation of an observed condition, a message is written to the test report and the test verdict is set to failed.

In CAPL a check can be configured to call a callback function or trigger a wait function, if a violation of an observed condition is detected.

Checks are passive, this means no messages are sent or environment variables are modified by the check.

| J1939 Checks | XML | CAPL |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Request Check | j1939_request_violation | J1939TestChkCreate_RequestViolation |
| Cycle Time | j1939_cycletime_rel_violation j1939_cycletime_abs_violation | J1939TestChkCreate_RelCycleTimeViolation J1939TestChkCreate_AbsCycleTimeViolation |
| BAM | j1939_BAM_violation | J1939TestChkCreate_BAMViolation |
| RTS/CTS | j1939_RTS_CTS_violation | J1939TestChkCreate_RTSCTSViolation |
| Address Claiming | j1939_ACL_violation | J1939TestChkCreate_ACLViolation |
| Message Distance | — | J1939TestChkCreate_PGDistanceViolation |
| Signal Value | — | J1939TestChkCreate_SignalRangeViolation |
| Rules Specific | j1939_rules_specific | — |
| Messages Known | j1939_messages_known | — |

## J1939-82 Compliance Checks (2008)

These checks are used for test cases of the J1939 Compliance specification. As a first step, tests of "Data Link Transmit Tests – General" 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [J1939/j1939tsl] j1939tslNmt


# Network Management Functions

J1939 » J1939 Test Service Library » Network Management Functions

The J1939 Test Service Library supports several function for the Network Management.

A Test Module, which uses the J1939 Test Service Library, manages a table with all active ECUs in the network. This table contains the 64-Bit Device Name and the addresses. With the NMT functions the addresses and device names can be queried.

On test start the function J1939TestNmtRefresh should be called. A Request for Address Claim is sent and the NMT table is updated.

To receive a notification, when the NMT table is modified, the callback function J1939TestOnNMTChange can be used.

CAPL Function Overview

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [J1939/j1939tsl] j1939tslOverview


# Test Service Library

J1939 » Test Service Library

The J1939 Test Service Library (short J1939 TSL) extends the Test Feature Set with J1939 specific functions. The functions can be used in CAPL and XML test modules.

In difference to the standard functions of the Test Feature Set (TSL), the J1939 TSL uses parameter groups and PGN. The functions of the standard Test Feature Set can also be used with J1939, but J1939 transport protocols are not supported and complete extended CAN identifiers are used. This means for example, if the priority is changed, the standard TSL may not recognize the message.

| More Information |
| --- |
| J1939 Test Pattern J1939 Checks J1939 Stimulus J1939 Wait Functions J1939 Network Management |

|  | Cross Reference The examples in the directory Open Folder show the usage of the J1939 Test Service Library. |

CAPL Function Overview | Test Service Library XML

| Diagnostic | j1939_wait_for_dm_with_spn j1939_wait_for_dm_without_spn | J1939TestWaitForDMWithSPN J1939TestWaitForDMWithoutSPN J1939TestJoinDMWithSPNEvent J1939TestJoinDMWithoutSPNEvent |

### [J1939/j1939tsl] j1939tslStimulus


# Stimulus

J1939 » J1939 Test Service Library » Stimulus

The following Stimuli are available:

- Toggle Stimulus: a signal toggles between two values.

- CSV Stimulus: signal values are read from a CSV file

|  | The Setup of a Stimulus is Done with these Steps: Create a Stimulus with a J1939TestStmCreate function Start the Stimulus with J1939TestStmControlStart After the test the Stimulus can be stopped with J1939TestStmControlStop. Resume the Stimulus with the Start function. Destroy the Stimulus with J1939TestStmControlDestroy. |

|  | Note The Stimuli of the J1939 Test Service Library must be used with the J1939TestStm functions. The control functions of the CAN Test Service Library cannot be used for J1939 Stimuli. |

CAPL Function Overview

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [J1939/j1939tsl] j1939tslWait


# Wait Functions

J1939 » J1939 Test Service Library » Wait Functions

Wait functions can be used to interrupt the execution of a CAPL program. The execution is resumed at that point, if an event occurs. The test can be performed step by step. This makes the CAPL programs easier. The wait functions of the J1939 Test Service Library interrupts the CAPL programs until the specified Parameter Group is received or a timeout occurs.

The following example waits for the Parameter Group EEC1 from the source address 0 and verifies the signal EngineSpeed.

|  | Example long lret;pg EEC1 rxPG;lret = J1939TestWaitForPG( 0, 0xfe, EEC1, 500 );switch(lret) { case 1: if (J1939TestGetWaitEventPGData(engParam ) == 0) { if (rxPG.EngineSpeed.phys < 100) { TestStepFail( "TestCase 1", "Wrong Signal Value"); } } else { TestStepFail( "TestCase 1", "Get event data failed"); } break; default: TestStepFail( "TestCase 1", "timeout or constrain violation "); break;} |

CAPL Function Overview

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |

# Commanded Address

J1939 » J1939 Test Service Library » Test Pattern » Commanded Address

| Name | J1939 Commanded Address Test Pattern |
| --- | --- |
| Description | This test pattern can validate the behavior of a node, if it receives a commanded address Parameter Group (FED8h). The pattern send a commanded address message and waits until the node responds with a ACL message from the new address. Following validations are done during this test pattern: the ACL message must be received before timeout. the ACL Parameter Group must be sent global. the DLC of the ACL Parameter Group must be 8. the default timeout is 250ms. |
| CAPL Functions | J1939TestCommandedAddress |
| XML Elements | j1939_commanded_address |

J1939 » J1939 Test Service Library » Test Pattern » Command/Response

| Name | J1939 Command/Response Test Pattern |
| --- | --- |
| Description | This test pattern can validate the behavior, if a node responds to a command message with a diagnostic response. The command and response can be configured. The test module sends the command message. Following validations are done during this test pattern: the command Parameter Group is send from the test module. the node must send the diagnostic response before timeout. Optionally the signal values can be validated. |
| CAPL Functions | J1939TestCommand |
| XML Elements | j1939_command_response |

### [J1939/j1939tsl/pattern] j1939tslPatternConflictACL


# Address Claiming Conflict

J1939 » J1939 Test Service Library » Test Pattern » Address Claiming Conflict

| Name | J1939 Address Claiming Conflict Test Pattern |
| --- | --- |
| Description | This test pattern validates the reaction of a node during an Address Claiming conflict. The test module ends an ACL message with a higher or lower priority than the DUT. The DUT must send an Address Claim message with the old address (if priority is higher ) or a Cannot Claim Address message (if priority is lower). Following validations are done during this test pattern: An ACL message with the source address of the DUT is sent from the test module. The priority level of the device name is increased or decrease by one. The DUT must respond with an ACL message before time-out. Depending on the priority of the device name, the DUT must send the ACL message from its old address or the Null-address (FEh). Instead the Null-address the DUT can use a new address. |
| CAPL Functions | J1939TestConflictACL |
| XML Elements | j1939_conflict_ACL |
| Test Module Manager | This pattern is available for network nodes with the Address Claiming (ACL) Parameter Group (PGN EE00h) in the Tx list. |

ACL Check

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [J1939/j1939tsl/pattern] j1939tslPatternDiagnostic


# Diagnostic

J1939 » J1939 Test Service Library » Test Pattern » Diagnostic

| Name | J1939 Diagnostic Test Pattern for SPN |
| --- | --- |
| Description | This test pattern can be used to check error codes in diagnostic messages. It is possible to wait for a definable J1939 diagnostic Parameter Group which contains a defined Suspect Parameter Number (SPN). The diagnostic Parameter Group with the SPN must be received in a definable time period (time-out). The following J1939 diagnostic Parameter Groups are supported: DM1, DM2, DM6, DM12, DM23, DM27, DM28, DM41, DM42, DM43, DM44, DM45, DM46, DM47, DM48, DM49, DM50, DM51, DM52 |
| CAPL Functions | J1939TestWaitForDMWithSPN | J1939TestWaitForDMWithoutSPN |
| XML Elements | J1939 Wait for DM with SPN | J1939 Wait for DM without SPN |
| Test Module Manager | - |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [J1939/j1939tsl/pattern] j1939tslPatternRequestACL


# Request Address Claiming

J1939 » J1939 Test Service Library » Test Pattern » Request Address Claiming

| Name | J1939 Request Address Claiming Test Pattern |
| --- | --- |
| Description | This test pattern validates, if a request for address claim is responded. The pattern send a request for ACL and waits for the ACL message (EE00h) from the configured nodes. Following validations are done during this test pattern: a global request for ACL (EA00h) must be responded by all nodes with the ACL Parameter Group (EE00h). a specific request for ACL must only be responded by the addressed node. the ACL Parameter Group must always be send global. the DLC of the ACL Parameter Group must be 8. the default time-out is 250ms. reserved bit of ACL Parameter Group must be 0. |
| CAPL Functions | J1939TestRequestACL |
| XML Elements | j1939_request_ACL |
| Test Module Manager | This pattern is available for network nodes with the Address Claiming (ACL) Parameter Group (PGN EE00h) in the Tx list. |

ACL Check

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [J1939/j1939tsl/pattern] j1939tslPatternRequestResponse


# Request/Response

J1939 » J1939 Test Service Library » Test Pattern » Request/Response

| Name | J1939 Request/Response Test Pattern |
| --- | --- |
| Description | This test pattern is suitable for validating the behavior of a node, if it receives a J1939 request (EA00h). The request Parameter Group is sent be the test module. Then it waits for the response. The response must be sent before timeout. The response can be the requested message or the acknowledgment Parameter Group (E800h). Should the request be observer only passive, the Request Check can be used. The XML test pattern is able to validate the signal values of the response. The request test pattern differs between global request, which are addressed to all nodes and specific request which are addressed to a single node. Depending on this the response must be sent global or specific. Global Requests Requested PGN DLC Transport Protocol Response Global DLC ≤ 8 - Global Specific DLC ≤ 8 - Global Global DLC > 8 BAM Global Specific DLC > 8 BAM Global Specific Requests Requested PGN DLC Transport Protocol Response Global DLC ≤ 8 - Global Specific DLC ≤ 8 - Specific Global DLC > 8 RTS/CTS Specific Specific DLC > 8 RTS/CTS Specific | Requested PGN | DLC | Transport Protocol | Response | Global | DLC ≤ 8 | - | Global | Specific | DLC ≤ 8 | - | Global | Global | DLC > 8 | BAM | Global | Specific | DLC > 8 | BAM | Global | Requested PGN | DLC | Transport Protocol | Response | Global | DLC ≤ 8 | - | Global | Specific | DL

### [J1939/j1939tsl/xml] j1939tslnode


# XML - j1939_node

J1939 » J1939 Test Service Library » XML overview

| Syntax | <j1939_node name="" aac="" ig="" sys="" sysi="" fct="" fcti="" ecui="" mc="" in="" address=""/> |
| --- | --- |
| Attributes | name node name from the database - the attributes NmStationAddress and NmJ1939xxx for the J1939 device name must be defined address address of the node, overwrites NmStationAddress definition from database aac Arbitrary Address Capable, overwrites definition from database ig Industry Group, overwrites definition from database sys Vehicle System, overwrites definition from database sysi Vehicle System Instance, overwrites definition from database fct Function, overwrites definition from database fcti Function Instance, overwrites definition from database ecui ECU Instance, overwrites definition from database mc Manufacturer Code, overwrites definition from database in Identity Number, overwrites definition from database |
| Elements | - |
| Description | Identifies a J1939 node. If the name attribute is specified, the device name and the address is taken from the node attributes in the database. The components of the 64-bit device name can be specified optionally and overwrite the values from the database. |
|  | Example <j1939_node name="EMS"/><j1939_node aac="1" ig="0" sys="4" sysi="0" fct="128" fcti="0" ecui="0" mc="1861" in="20060913"/> |

### [J1939/j1939tsl/xml] j1939tslpg


# XML - j1939_pg

J1939 » J1939 Test Service Library » XML Overview

| Syntax | <j1939_pg id="" sendNode="" receiveNode="" priority="" dlc="" bus="" dir=""> <signal><signal> </j1939_pg> |
| --- | --- |
| Attributes | id message name from database or PGN sendNode send node from database or sender addressoptional, if source address is defined in message receiveNode receive node from database or receive addressoptional, if destination address is defined in message priority priority of the message, overwrite the priority from database dlc DLC of the message, overwrite the DLC from database bus bus on which the message is sent/received dir direction of the message (rx/tx) |
| Elements | signal - 0..n byte - 0..n |
| Description | Identifies a J1939 Parameter Group. If the attribute idis specified, the device name and the address are taken from the node attributes in the database. |
|  | Example <j1939_pg id="EEC1" sendNode="Engine" receiveNode="CruiseControl" priority="6" dlc="8" bus="J1939_4" dir="tx"> <signal id="EngSpeed">1200</signal></j1939_pg> |

### [J1939/j1939tsl/xml] j1939tslsignal


# XML - signal

J1939 » J1939 Test Service Library » XML Overview

| Syntax | <signal name="">Value</signal> |
| --- | --- |
| Attributes | name signal name from database Value value of the signal |
| Elements | If <signal> is used within a Tx message, no sub elements are allowed. If <signal> is used within a Rx message the following sub elements (see table below) are allowed: eq, nq, gt, ge, lt, le |
| Description | Defines the value of a J1939 signal. This element can only be embedded in a j1939_pg element. |
|  | Example <signal name="EngSpeed">1200</signal> |

## Sub Elements

| Syntax | <eq>Value</eq> equal <ne>Value</ne> not equal <gt>Value</gt> greater than <ge>Value</ge> greater or equal <lt>Value</lt> lower than <le>Value</le> lower or equal |
| --- | --- |
| Attributes | Value value to compare with |
| Elements | - |
| Description | With these compare element, it is possible to compare the received signal value, with an expected value. If non of these elements is specified, <eq> is used. |
|  | Example <signal id="EngSpeed">1200</signal> same as<signal id="EngSpeed"><eq>1200</eq></signal> |

### [J1939/nmeabasics] fastPacketCAPL


# Fast Packet Protocol (CAPL J1939)

J1939 » Fast Packet Protocol (CAPL J1939)

The support for maritime applications offers functionality to analyze and simulate the Fast Packet Protocol, so CANoe can send and receive this transport protocol.

In order to recognize the Fast Packet Protocol, the message attribute SingleFrame within the CAN database must be set to 0. If this attribute is missing or not set to 0 and the DLC is greater than 8, CANoe select a suitable transport protocol according to the destination address (broadcast: BAM / peer-to-peer: CMDT).

## Use

The Fast Packet Protocol can transfer 1 to 223 bytes. Because in CAPL the packets and the complete Parameter Group are received, a special handling of the messages is necessary.

The on pg function is called for a single packet with DLC 8 and the complete Parameter Group with DLC 1..223. In general only the complete message is needed. For Parameter Groups with DLC > 8 the CAPL function getThisMessage must be used. This function copies a received message, which is transferred with a transport protocol, into a variable. For message which does not use a transport protocol the DLC is set to 0.

|  | Example In the example the Parameter Group ConfigurationInformation is received. The Parameter Group must be defined in the database with a DLC > 8. The DLC of the variable rxPG is > 0, if the complete message is received. on pg ConfigurationInformation{ pg ConfigurationInformation rxPG; getThisMessage( rxPG, this.DLC ); 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [J1939/nmeabasics] nmeaExtensions


# Extensions for Maritime Applications

J1939 » Extensions for Maritime Applications

## GNSS Monitor

The GNSS (Global Navigation Satellite System) monitor is used for graphical and numeric display of degrees of longitude and latitude as well as to specify the height of a GNSS message. In addition, this data can be logged with other information in the protocol.

## GNSS Simulator

The GNSS Simulator can be used to simulate a GNSS receiver. To do this, a geometric figure or a position file is used to determine a path that will be traced by the virtual GNSS receiver when the simulation is started. GNSS positions thereby generated are transferred cyclically with configurable messages.

## GNSS Node Layer

The GNSS node layer can be used to simulate a GNSS receiver. To do this, different CAPL functions are used to determine a path that will be traced by the virtual GNSS receiver when the simulation is started. GNSS positions thereby generated are transferred cyclically with configurable messages.

## Fast Packet Protocol

The Fast Packet Protocol is supported for analysis and simulation. Therefore it is necessary to define which Parameter Groups are transmitted with Fast Packet Protocol. For this purpose the message attribute SingleFrame is used in the database. If the attribute value is set to 0 for a Parameter Group, the Fast Packet Protocol is used for this Parameter Group.

This is considered for the Trace Window interpretation as well as for the signal access in the Graphi

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [J1939/procedures] createJ1939configuration


# How to create a configuration for J1939

J1939 » How to create a configuration for J1939

|  | To Create a J1939 Configuration, follow the next Steps: Create a new configuration via File ribbon tab|New|Create Configuration. Select the J1939 template and activate the Use Wizard check box. Confirm with [OK]. Press button [Database…] and load the default J1939 database in the directory Open Folder or a manual created J1939 database. Press button [Continue]. Select the nodes in list Available nodes and add them to the Assigned nodes list with [>>]. Press button [Continue]. Select the CAN channel in list Available channels and add them to the Assigned channels list with [>>]. Press button [Continue]. Press [Finish] to finish the creation of the configuration. The configuration assistant has now done the following things: Nodes are assigned to the network. The database is assigned to the used CAN channel. The communication is configured as defined in the database. The Trace Window is configured for J1939. The Measurement Setup contains a J1939 Network Scanner and a J1939 DTC Monitor. |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [J1939/procedures] defineJ1939nodes


# Define a J1939 Node

CANoe » J1939 » Define a J1939 Database » Define a J1939 Node

For the definition of a J1939 node, parameters for the network management can be specified and send and receive Parameter Groups can be assigned. In contrast to a standard CAN node, a J1939 node has additional attributes. These attributes are necessary for the code generation.

A J1939 node has

- a node address in the range from 0 to 253

- and a 64-bit device name (device name)

These properties can be set via node attributes.

|  | Note In the SAE J1939 specification, the terms node and ECU are used in another way than in the CANdb. In the SAE J1939 specification, the ECU respectively the Controller Application (CA) has an address and a 64-bit device name; in the CANdb, by contrast, the node has the properties. |

The node address is set with the node attribute NmStationAddress (see table below). For a node with network management, the preferred address is set here. The node address may not be used multiple times in the database.

| Attribute Definition | Value |
| --- | --- |
| Attribute type | Node |
| Name | NmStationAddress |
| Type | Integer |
| Value | 0 .. 254 |

For the network management the device name is also required. This consists of a 64-bit value that is composed of several components. With the device name a node can be identified uniquely in the network, even if the node changes its address at run-time. The following node attributes are used for this.

| Attribute Name | 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [J1939/procedures] j1939logging


# Replay Block and Offline Mode

J1939 » Replay Block and Offline Mode

J1939 Parameter Groups are recorded as normal CAN messages. The Replay Block and the offline mode replay CAN message with extended CAN identifier as Parameter Groups.

## Transport Protocol

There is a feature, if transport protocol Parameter Groups are recorded in an ASC logging file. The assembled Parameter Groups with a DLC greater than 8 are stored as Parameter Groups in the logging file. The lines are marked with J1939TP.

The several Parameter Groups of a transport protocol transmission are written additionally to the logging file, if they are not filtered by a filter.

## J1939 Interpretation

A comment line is inserted before each line of a J1939 Parameter Group in an ASC logging. This comment line contains the source address, destination address and the PGN of the J1939 Parameter Group. The symbolic display must be activated in the Home ribbon tab to use the J1939 interpretation.

## Replay

J1939 Parameter Groups with DLC greater than 8 which a contained in a logging file are ignored on replay. The transport protocol Parameter Groups are handled and an assembled Parameter Group is created.

## Import in Trace Window

When importing an ASC logging file into the Trace Window, also the Parameter Groups with a DLC greater than 8 are read. An interpretation of the transport protocol (as in the Replay Block) is not done.

J1939 basics

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [J1939/procedures] SendReceiveJ1939Frames


# Send and Receive J1939 Frames

J1939 » Send and Receive J1939 Frames

J1939 Frame

A new frame class named J1939Frame was added to .NET-API. It derives from CANFrame and thus has all the properties of this class.

Additionally the following properties can be set and requested:

- Characteristic

- DA

- DA_TP

- DLCEx

- DP

- EDP

- PF

- PGN

- Priority

- PS

- SA

If the properties are used as write (set), the bit-fields are set directly in the CANId.

## Sending J1939 Frames

The sending is shown here in a NET MeasurementScript by means of an example. The length (DLC) for the J1939Frame can be set directly in the constructor or via the property DLCEx.

|  | Example [OnKey('s')]public void SendJ1939Frame(char c){ J1939Frame frame = new J1939Frame(0xCFEF7F3, 10); frame.PGN = 0xFEF7; frame.SA = 0xF3; frame.Priority = 3; frame.DA_TP = 0xFF; var data = new byte[] { 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0xFF, 0xFF, 0, 0 }; frame.Bytes = data; frame.Send();} |

But the frames are also generated for the used J1939 database, these are then derived from the class J1939Frame.

|  | Example [OnKey('s')]public void SendBatteryPotentialSwitched (char c){ var frame = new NetworkDB.Frames.VEP1_ACS(); frame.BatteryPotentialSwitched.Value = 2; frame.ElectricalPotential.Value = 10; frame.Send();} |

In addition to the parameterless method Send() , which transmits on the first channel, the SendTo(channel) method can be used to transmit on a specific channel.

## Reception of J1939 Frames

To receive frames 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [J1939/windows] datawndconfigurationj1939


# Data Window

J1939 » Data Window

The configuration of the Data Window occurs as in standard mode. Instead of messages, Parameter Groups are selected.

## Address Input

If a signal from a J1939 Parameter Group is displayed in the Data Window, attention must be paid whether there are several transmitters of this Parameter Group.

If the address of the Parameter Group is not defined (as is the case for Parameter Groups of the standard database J1939.dbc), the signal is only displayed correctly if there is a transmitter. If the signal is sent by several transmitters, the Data Window always displays the last value received. The result is a "jumping" signal in the Data Window that always switches between the values of the two transmitters.

To receive a correct signal flow, the sender address must be defined. For this, click Edit... on the context menu of a signal. Now the transmitter address can be entered on the signal dialog after the database connection was interrupted.

## Signal Interpretation

The value of a J1939 signal is interpreted according the SigType database attribute.

The Data Window can display string values. The display of the line must be changed to Text or Symbolic and the SigType attribute must be set to a string type. As physical value the number of characters are displayed.

For a PGN or DTC signal type the Parameter Group name or signal name from the database is displayed.

## J1939 Indicator

In addition, an indicator can be activated on the context m

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [J1939/windows] graphicwndsignalselectionj1939


# Graphics Window

J1939 » Graphics Window

The configuration of the Graphics Window occurs as in standard mode. Instead of messages, Parameter Groups are selected.

If a signal from a J1939 Parameter Group is displayed in the Graphics Window, attention must be paid whether there are several transmitters of this Parameter Group.

If the address of the Parameter Group is not defined (as is the case for Parameter Groups of the standard database J1939.dbc), the signal is only displayed correctly if there is a transmitter. If the signal is sent by several transmitters, the Graphics Window always displays the last value received. The result is a "jumping" signal in the Graphics Window as you can see in the following image in the upper signal flow.

To receive a correct signal flow, the sender address must be defined. For this, on the signal configuration dialog of the Graphics Window, a signal must be selected and modify called up. After the database connection was interrupted, it is possible to enter the sender address on the signal dialog. The signal is then displayed as the two lower signal flows in the picture above.

## J1939 Indicator

Within the configuration dialog of the Graphics Window you can define if the value range of J1939 signals is considered during measurement. For this activate the check box in the column J1939. Afterwards signal values detected as Not available are not shown in the signal trace. the curve contains the last valid signal value.

To specify the s

[...truncated for brevity...]

# Statistic Window

J1939 » Statistic Window

The Statistics Window can display the number of messages depending on the CAN IDs as a histogram.

On the context menu of the Statistics Window, click Configure view.... Under range setting, the X-axis of the histogram can now be set.

| ID range | X axis shows the 29-Bit-CAN ID. |
| PGN range | X axis shows the Parameter Group number. |
| SA range | X axis shows the source address. |

### [LibrariesPackages] AddOnDLLs


# Function- and Modeling Libraries

CANoe » Function- and Modeling Libraries

## Introduction

The functionality of CANoe can be further expanded by supplemental ModelingLibraries (DLLs).

Since not all expansions are needed for every task, this can reduce the complexity of the main program.

CANoe supports the following types of DLLs:

## CAPL DLL

These DLLs directly expand the functional scope of CAPL and permit implementation of complex tasks in a programming language other than CAPL, e.g. C or C++.

To activate such a DLL it must be entered in the CAPL DLL list in the Options dialog (Programming|CAPL DLL).

|  | Further Information Configuration: CAPL DLL Linking a Window DLL in CAPL |

## Modeling Libraries for Simulation

This DLL expands the Functionality of a special single simulated Node, e.g.:

- Interaction Layer (IL)

- Network Management (NM)

- Transport Protocol (TP)

Assigning of Simulation and Test Modules | Overview of Libraries and Packages |Overview of default Sample Configurations

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [LibrariesPackages] AddOnDLLsAssigning


# Assigning Modeling Libraries

CANoe » Libraries & Packages » Assigning Modeling Libraries

## Introduction

The following describes how modeling libraries (interaction layer, transport protocol, network management, and customer-specific expansions) and test modules are integrated into a CANoe configuration.

The standard installation of CANoe contains a few sample configurations, into which such a DLL file has already been added; e.g. AUTOSAR network management for CAN and FlexRay.

|  | Note For OEM-specific add-ons, you install the add-on package beforehand. |

### Where are the DLL files located?

DLL files included in the standard delivery as well as DLL files installed with an add-on package can be found in the <CANoe installation directory>\Exec32 directory.

### Which version of the DLL file is currently installed?

You can find the version of the DLL loaded in CANoe on the File ribbon tab|Help|Details.

### Assigning DLL files

DLL files can be assigned for all nodes in the Simulation Setup are for individual nodes only. In the case of the test module, a DLL file can be assigned to an individual node in the test setup via the context menu.

There are various options for assigning a DLL file. These are presented in the following.

## Assigning a DLL File or Module Description File for CAN using the CANdb++ Editor

### [LibrariesPackages/AUTOSARpduIL] AUTOSARpduILFeatures


# Supported Features

CANoe » Libraries & Packages » AUTOSAR PDU IL » Supported Features

| Quick Access |
| --- |
| Global System States | Message Counter | Update Bit |
| Sending Model | CRC Value | — |

## Global System States [▲ back]

There exist two global system states that are handled by the AUTOSAR IL DLL:

## Ignition

The Ignition state is represented by the global system variable sysvar::_ILControl::Ignition, which can be set to 0 (off) or 1 (on).

- When the Ignition is on, then the IL will prevent an attached NM DLL at the same node to release the bus.

- If Ignition is switch from off to on, then the IL will request the bus at an attached NM DLL at the same node.

- Otherwise, when the Ignition is going off, then the attached NM DLL at the same node will release the bus after the NM sleep timer expires (see Usage of the Interaction Layer).

|  | Notes When an NM DLL at the same node releases the bus, then the IL DLL will be set to waiting (see Interaction Layer State Model). Thus, the IL stops sending. When an NM DLL at the same node requests the bus (possibly due to switching the Ignition to on), then the IL DLL will go to running (see Interaction Layer State Model). Thus, the IL starts sending. |

The Ignition state can also be controlled by the CAPL functions ARILSetIgnitionState and ARILFaultInjectionSetIgnitionState.

## Power

The Power state is represented by the global system variable sysvar::_ILControl::Power, which can be set to 0 (off) or 1 (on).

-

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [LibrariesPackages/AUTOSARpduIL] AUTOSARpduILReturnCodes


# Return Codes

CANoe » Libraries & Packages » AUTOSAR PDU IL » Return Codes

It is not necessary to check the error codes of the API functions. In case of an error the errors are additionally output to the Write Window.

| Numeric Value (long) | Description |
| --- | --- |
| 0 | Request processed |
| Warnings | Are not reported to the Write Window. |
| -1 | IL is in a state that ignores the given request: State graph of the IL (Link). |
| -2 | Allowed value range of a signal exceeded. (The exceeded value is transmitted to the bus nevertheless). |
| Configuration Errors | Are reported to Write Window. Indicate a fundamental problem that must be solved before usage. |
| -50 | Node layer is not active. Presumably it is deactivated in the node’s configuration dialog. |
| -51 | API function not supported. |
| -52 | The node is connected to another bus like the CAN Bus. |
| Dynamic Errors | Are reported to Write Window. The supplied data is not consumed; therefore it is not needed to set signal value back. |
| -100 | Signal/message not found in DB, or not mapped to the node. |
| -101 | Maximum possible value range exceeded. (There is no transmission to the bus). |
| -102 | A network management-, diagnostic, or transport protocol signal was set. IL declines such types of signals, because it is not intended to connect these layers to the IL. |
| -103 | Invalid value supplied (for all types of requests). |
| -104 | General error for invalid requests that are not described furthermor

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [LibrariesPackages/AUTOSARpduIL] AUTOSARpduILStateModel


# Interaction Layer State Model

CANoe » Libraries & Packages » AUTOSAR PDU IL » Interaction Layer State Model

If the IL is simulated (default), it knows the following states. Please refer the Callback Interface functions for details how to traverse the state graph.

- If the IL is not simulated, the described functions have no consequences to the IL.

- If the IL changes from SimulationOn to SimulationOff, the IL is stopped.

- If the IL changes from SimulationOf to SimulationOn, the IL is started.

### [LibrariesPackages/AUTOSARpduIL] AUTOSARpduILUsage


# Usage of the Interaction Layer

CANoe » Libraries & Packages » AUTOSAR PDU IL » Usage of the Interaction Layer

If you have fully described the transmission behavior of the nodes, messages and signals in the database (DBC or AUTOSAR), there are two options for building a simulation model using the interaction layer, one automated and one manual procedure.

## Automated

The Model Generation Wizard generates a CANoe configuration based on the database and the selected Interaction Layer, adds the corresponding network nodes to the Simulation Setup and creates operating and display panels so that the remaining bus simulation can be operated.

## Manual

If you already have a configuration and want to operate the simulation model or individual network nodes with the CANoe interaction layer, you can now assign the Interaction Layer to the corresponding network nodes manually.

- Assigning Complete Databases

- Editing Nodes in the Simulation Setup

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [LibrariesPackages/AUTOSARpduIL] AUTOSARpduILWriteWindowNotification


# Write Window Notifications

CANoe » Libraries & Packages » AUTOSAR PDU IL » Write Window Notifications

Here some outputs to the Write Window are described:

## SignalHandle invalid

This signal is not known in the node scope of the IL. Reasons:

- Send node of the associated PDU is not the node the IL is responsible for.

- Associated PDU was identified as a diagnostic, NM, XCP or TP message.

## MessageHandle invalid

This PDU is not known in the node scope of the IL. Reasons:

- Send node of the PDU is not the node the IL is responsible for.

- PDU was identified as a diagnostic, NM, XCP or TP message.

## Message for (un)changed signal is not sent

A set signal request doesn’t cause an output of the associated PDU to the bus. Reasons:

- IL inactive.

- Event timing of PDU not defined or disabled.

## Sending of PDU forced

Only a notification that the PDU was sent although the send type had prevented a sending. Reasons:

- Usage of ARILSetPDUEvent or ARILSetEvent in CAPL.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |

### [LibrariesPackages/ModelGeneration] ModelGenerationWizardPanels


# Generated Panels

Model Generation Wizard » Panels

The following panels will be created at the generation of a CANoe configuration with the Model Generation Wizard. The creation of the panels depend on the panel settings.

On the help page you find information about the handling of these panels.

## Main Panel (with CANoe IL)

The Main Panel displays all nodes of the configuration. The node buttons allow opening the new output or input panels or the node panels from CANoe. The output and input panels shows message buttons in order to open the Message Panel with all signals of the message and appropriate disturbance functions. The Main Panel establishes also the possibility to change the ignition status in order to start the remaining bus simulation. The columns with the buttons for the Tx/Rx signals and the node panels are optional. Their availability depends on the selected panel settings for the generation process.

## Global States

Ignition (KL15) For all ECUs the status of the KL15 can be used to control the sending of the simulation node. The ILs can be started and stopped depending on the status of the clamp 15. If enabled all nodes start sending and NM nodes set a network request. This switch is the recommended switch to start and stop the remaining bus simulation.

Power (Kl30) Sets the global state of the clamp 30.

### [LibrariesPackages/VectorILCAN] VectorILCANBasics


# Basics - CAN Interaction Layer

CANoe » Libraries & Packages » CANoe IL CAN » Basics - CAN Interaction Layer

## Concept

Various timings for a Message can be defined based on a database description. The CANoe interaction layer controls the transmission accordingly.

Data updates are possible at any time. However, the data transfer takes place only at the times allowed by the Interaction Layer State Model.

## Timing Types

The transmit method is defined by the message send type and the send types of the signals placed on the message. The message transmit behavior additionally includes the times for cyclic transmissions, transmit delays, signal inactive values, etc. that are used to control the message transmissions.

The following send types are supported by the CANoe interaction layer:

## Basic Send Types

| Send Type | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Cyclic | The message is sent at a fixed cycle time independent of signal values. |
| OnChange | The message is sent when a changed signal value is written to the message. |
| OnWrite | The message is sent when the value of a signal is rewritten to the message. |
| IfActive | The message is sent at a fast cycle time if a signal of the message is set to an active value. As soon as all values are inactive, the message is no longer sent. |

## Combined Send Types

| Send Type | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Cyclic and OnChange | The message is sent cyclically. If a modified signal value is written to the message, it is sent addi

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [LibrariesPackages/VectorILCAN] VectorILCANDatabaseSettingsASR3


# Database Settings AUTOSAR 3

CANoe » Libraries & Packages » CANoe IL CAN » Database Settings AUTOSAR 3

|  | Note These settings are only valid for AUTOSAR 3 databases in conjunction with the CANoe interaction layer. AUTOSAR 4 databases must use AUTOSAR PDU IL. |

The transmission behavior of messages and signals sent with the CANoe interaction layer are described in the database. In AUTOSAR databases the send types are defined in a different way to DBC databases. The CANoe interaction layer supports a subset of all possible send types which can be described with the AUTOSAR format:

| General |
| --- |
| Minimum Delay | Deﬁnes the Minimum Delay Time (MDT) between successive transmissions of this I-PDU in seconds. |
| Transmission Mode True Timing |
| Cyclic Timing | Time Offset | This attribute speciﬁes the time until ﬁrst transmission of this I-PDU. |
| Time Period | Period of the repetition of cyclic transmissions. |
| Event Controlled Timing | Repetition Period | The repetition Period speciﬁes the time in seconds that elapses before the PDU can be sent the next time (Minimum repeat gap between two pdus). The repetition Period is optional in case that no repetitions are conﬁgured. |
| Number of Repetititons | Deﬁnes the number of repetitions for the Event transmission mode. |
| Transmission Mode False Timing |
| Cyclic Timing | Time Offset | This attribute speciﬁes the time until ﬁrst transmission of this I-PDU. |
| Time Period | Period of the repetition of cyclic trans

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [LibrariesPackages/VectorILCAN] VectorILCANDatabaseSettingsDBC


# Database Settings DBC

CANoe » Libraries & Packages » CANoe IL CAN » Database Settings DBC

The transmission behavior of messages and signals sent with the CANoe interaction layer are described in the database. The CANoe interaction layer models the transmission behavior of the remaining bus simulation at runtime using the database attributes. The database attributes must fulfill certain requirements to reproduce the transmission behavior.

## Node Attributes

| Name | Type | Values | Default | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| NodeLayerModules | String | For the CANoe interaction layer CANoeILNLVector.dll and possibly also an OSEK/AUTOSAR-NM-DLL. | — | Defines the modules the node needs in the simulation model automatically. This assignment can be done manually in the node setup too. |

## Message Attributes

| Name | Type | Values | Default | Description |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| GenMsgILSupport | Enum | 0: No 1: Yes | Yes | Set to Yes if the message is handled by the interaction layer. |
| GenMsgSendType | Enum | 0: Cyclic 1: NotUsed 2: NotUsed 3: NotUsed 4: NotUsed 5: NotUsed 6: NotUsed 7: IfActive 8: NoMsgSendType 9: NotUsed 10: NotUsed 11: NotUsed | Cyclic | Defines the message related send type. This attribute together with the send types of the signals placed on the message define the overall transmit behavior of the message. |
| GenMsgCycleTime | Integer | 0 — 65535 | 0 | Cycle time of periodic messages [ms]. |
| GenMsgCycleTimeFast | Integ

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [LibrariesPackages/VectorILCAN] VectorILCANErrorCodes


# Return Codes

CANoe » Libraries & Packages » CANoe IL CAN » Return Codes

It is not necessary to check the error codes of the API functions. In case of an error the errors are additionally output to the Write Window.

| Numeric Value (long) | Description |
| --- | --- |
| 0 | Request processed |
| Warnings | Are not reported to the Write Window. |
| -1 | IL is in a state that ignores the given request: State graph of the IL (Link). |
| -2 | Allowed value range of a signal exceeded. (The exceeded value is transmitted to the bus nevertheless). |
| Configuration Errors | Are reported to Write Window. Indicate a fundamental problem that must be solved before usage. |
| -50 | Node layer is not active. Presumably it is deactivated in the node’s configuration dialog. |
| -51 | API function not supported. |
| -52 | The node is connected to another bus like the CAN Bus. |
| Dynamic Errors | Are reported to Write Window. The supplied data is not consumed; therefore it is not needed to set signal value back. |
| -100 | Signal/message not found in DB, or not mapped to the node. |
| -101 | Maximum possible value range exceeded. (There is no transmission to the bus). |
| -102 | A network management-, diagnostic, or transport protocol signal was set. IL declines such types of signals, because it is not intended to connect these layers to the IL. |
| -103 | Invalid value supplied (for all types of requests). |
| -104 | General error for invalid requests that are not described furthermore.

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [LibrariesPackages/VectorILCAN] VectorILcanOSEKNM


# Communication with OSEK-NM

CANoe » Libraries & Packages » CANoe IL CAN » Communication with OSEK-NM

The communication between CANoe interaction layer and OSEK-NM does not require any CAPL code and runs automatically.

If the OSEK-NM is not to be used, the entry dmoseknm.dll and OSEKNM01.dll respectively has to be removed from the database attribute NodeLayerModules. This module is then no longer loaded. In certain cases you may want to have control over the communication. This can be achieved by means of the concept described here.

## CAPL Callbacks Are Used for Communication Between IL and NM

These callbacks are not used with the test functions ILSetMsgEvent and ILSetEvent.

- Callbacks of the OSEK network management:

The names of these callbacks are specified in the INI file dmoseknm.ini.This is the default setting:

[NAMES] CALLBACKFunc1 = apBusSleep CALLBACKFunc2 = apCanSleep CALLBACKFunc3 = apCanNormal CALLBACKFunc4 = apCanWakeUp CALLBACKFunc5 = apCanOff CALLBACKFunc6 = apCanOn

- The callbacks apBusSleep, apCanSleep and apCanNormal are called if they have been defined.

- If the callback apCanWakeUp is defined the application has to react to it accordingly.If this callback is not defined the communication between CANoe interaction layer and OSEK-NM is controlled automatically.

- If the callbacks apCanOff and apCanOn are defined the application must react to them accordingly, i.e. either with the CAPL functions CanOffline / CanOnline or via the CANoe interaction

[...truncated for brevity...]

| Cyclic | CT | X Time Period Time Offset | — | — |
| ET | — | — |

| Cyclic and OnWrite | CT | Time Period Time Offset | — | Always |
| ET | RepetitionPeriod NumberOfRepetitions | — |
| Cyclic and IfActive | CT | Time Period Time Offset | Time Period Time Offset | MaskedNewDiffersX* Signal Mask X (Inactive Value) |
| ET | — | — |

- CT = Cyclic Timing

### [LibrariesPackages/VectorILCAN] VectorILCANStateModel


# Interaction Layer State Model

CANoe » Libraries & Packages » CANoe IL CAN » Interaction Layer State Model

If the IL is simulated (default), it knows the following states. Please refer the Callback Interface functions for details how to traverse the state graph.

- If the IL is not simulated, the described functions have no consequences to the IL.

- If the IL changes from SimulationOn to SimulationOff, the IL is stopped.

- If the IL changes from SimulationOf to SimulationOn, the IL is started.
