### [LibrariesPackages/VectorILCAN] VectorILCANUsage


# Usage of the Interaction Layer

CANoe » Libraries & Packages » CANoe IL CAN » Usage of the Interaction Layer

If you have fully described the transmission behavior of the nodes, messages and signals in the database (DBC or AUTOSAR), there are two options for building a simulation model using the interaction layer, one automated and one manual procedure.

## Automated

The Model Generation Wizard generates a CANoe configuration based on the database and the selected Interaction Layer, adds the corresponding network nodes to the Simulation Setup and creates operating and display panels so that the remaining bus simulation can be operated.

## Manual

If you already have a configuration and want to operate the simulation model or individual network nodes with the CANoe interaction layer, you can now assign the Interaction Layer to the corresponding network nodes manually.

- Assigning Complete Databases

- Editing Nodes in the Simulation Setup

### [LIN] LIN


# Option LIN

Standards – Bus Systems, Protocols, Databases » Option LIN

|  | LIN (Local Interconnect Network) is a serial communication system that is used for distributed electronic systems, usually for sensors, in automobiles. An example of an application is the networking within a door or seat. Typically, a number of physically separated local LIN networks are connected with a CAN network. Application Areas: Automotive engineering (body electronics bus, e.g. regulation of the interior lighting of a car). |

### [LIN] LIN20_Reconfiguration_Simulation


# Simulation of the LIN2.x-Reconfiguration

LIN » Simulation of the LIN 2.x Reconfiguration

When simulating a LIN2.x Slave, Slave responses which follow reconfiguration commands will be automatically answered. Please note that between the Master request containing the reconfiguration command and the Slave response no other frame should be sent. On the successful completion of an AssignFrameID command (LIN 2.0) or an AssignFrameIdRange command (LIN 2.1), the configured frame is activated and will be answered by the LIN hardware. By default Slaves with non-volatile memory are simulated. This means that at measurement start all the Slave’s frames are activated.

In order to simulate a Slave with volatile memory, you will need to extend the Slave’s CAPL program as follows:

- Call in on preStart the function LinResetSlave() in order to deactivate all frames configured for this Slave and to reset its NAD at measurement start.

- Call in on linSleepModeEvent the function LinResetSlave(). In this case, the modelled Slave will lose its configuration on receiving a sleep command.

LinResetSlave() has the same affect as calling the functions LinResetNAD() and LinDeactivateResps(). It can also be used at any other time during the measurement, in order to simulate the reset of a Slave. Individual frames can also be activate/deactivated using the function LINSetRespCounter(). To activate all frames of a Slave, you can use the function LinActivateResps() at any time during the measuremen

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [LIN] LINCommunication


# LIN Communication (Network Errors and Network Events)

LIN » LIN Communication » Network Errors and Network Events

## LIN Communication

The LIN protocol is implemented in software, in general. As a low-cost protocol, its implementation is on the same controller as the application and it dispenses with explicit error detection and handling.

For analysis of the LIN communication, however, detailed interpretation of the communication behavior is essential. To detect any network errors and the network status, CANoe provides an extensive event structure and a unique interpretation.

## LIN Network Errors

| Error Type | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Send Error | A send error occurs when no Slave responds to a send request from the Master (internal or external). A send error event includes information about the send identifier. Note Send errors for the diagnostic frame SlaveResp (Frame Id = 0x3d) and for event-triggered frames do not violate the LIN protocol. |  | Note Send errors for the diagnostic frame SlaveResp (Frame Id = 0x3d) and for event-triggered frames do not violate the LIN protocol. |
|  | Note Send errors for the diagnostic frame SlaveResp (Frame Id = 0x3d) and for event-triggered frames do not violate the LIN protocol. |
| Receive Error | A receiving error may have a wide variety of causes. An external Master can cause a receiving error by transmitting synch break that is too short or assigning an incorrect parity to the frame identifier. A receiving error can 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [LIN] LIN_FSM_Usage


# LIN FSM

LIN » FSM

This is a special feature which is mostly needed for strict timing requirements. Normally the methods for Simulating LIN Slaves should be sufficient for most purposes.

Using Finite State Machine(s) it is possible to define frame responses and to automatically react on message data. Up to 64 FSM(s) (Finite State Machine)can be defined.

The following function calls have to be made in the on preStart event procedure and be enclosed by LINInitBegin() / LINInitEnd().

A finite State Machine (FSM) must first be activated by calling LINSlSimulate(). At the same time, the state machine identifier is defined.

The number of states for the FSM can be set using LINSlFSMSetGlobal(). The states of the FSM are configured individually.

The configuration of a state always starts with LINSIFSMSetStBegin() and concludes with LINSlFSMSetStEnd().

The FSM can be configured with LINSlFSMSetStSFUp() that it's reaction on a header request is a change of its state.

LINSlFSMSetStMFUp() can be used to react on message data. The message data on the bus will be masked with the specified mask and compared to the specified result. If the comparison is successful, the state machine proceeds to another state.

Finally LINSlFSMSetStTO() can be used to define how long the FSM may remain in a given state. After this time, the FSM sends a slave timeout and changes to a defined state.

|  | Note Using a FSM and the automatic configuration of LIN frames in the database for a given ident

### [LIN] LIN_LIN-Channel_Baudrate


# Configuring the Baud Rate of a LIN Channel

LIN » Configuration » Configuring the Baud Rate of a LIN Channel

The initial baud rate for a LIN channel can be set in several ways. If more than one method is configured, the actual value used depends on the following priority order from highest to lowest:

- the value set by the CAPL function LINInitSetBaseBaud()

- the value of Network Hardware Configuration dialog (Overwrite DB Settings and Baud rate) configured for the LIN channel

- the LinSpeedDefinition network attribute of the LIN database associated with the LIN channel

If the Master mode of the LIN hardware is activated, the actual baud rate is the same as the initial one. Otherwise the initial baud rate is used as an initial value for synchronizing with an external »master clock. The actual baud rate however must not deviate by more than ±15 % from the initial value.

How to create a LIN Description File | How to create a CANoe.LIN Configuration | Master mode of the LIN hardware | LIN: Channel Configuration

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |

### [LIN] SFS_LIN_Overview


# Stress Feature Set

LIN » Stress Feature Set

Using the XL-hardware family, you can use the Stress Feature Set for LIN to stimulate protocol errors:

- Invalid parity ID/sync byte/checksum

- Invalid sync break/sync delimiter

- Short message error/no response/collision

- Transmission of arbitrary bit streams (also as a response)

Additionally, you can modify LIN-protocol during a measurement:

- Synch break length/synch delimiter length

- Interframe space length/interbyte space length

- Baud rate

- Master scheduling jitter

CAPL functions of the LIN Option |LIN Disturbance Feature Set

### [LIN/HowTos] LINHardwareMasterMode


# Master Mode of the LIN Hardware

LIN » Master Mode of the LIN Hardware

## Working modes

The LIN network interface features two working modes:

- In master mode, the LIN network interface is able to output message headers on the network.

- Headers sent by an external master are responded to in slave mode.

The selection of the working mode of a LIN channel has the following individual effects:

## Slave mode:

- The LIN network interface responds to received LIN headers if a response to the LIN ID contained within the header has been configured. A LIN response is configured at the start of a measurement with the initial value defined in the LDF if an LDF is used. During the measurement, the LIN response data can be modified at any time with linUpdateResponse or by calling the output (linFrame) general function with linFrame.RTR=0.

- The network interface measures the baud rate of the sync byte of each header received to then adapt the transmission baud rate to the measured receive baud rate. For this reason, a LIN header should not be sent on a LIN channel of the network interface in slave mode (for example using the Bitstream functions; see linSendBitStream). In this case, the LIN channel measures the baud rate of the header which it itself had sent. As the measurement is subject to quantization and cannot be exact, the baud rate begins to drift until it exits the tolerance range (15%).

- The CAPL functions for direct transmission of LIN headers (linTransmitHeader or 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [LIN/HowTos] LINHowtoConformanceTest


# How to Test Conformance of my LIN Node

LIN » How to Test Conformance of my LIN Node

## LIN Slave Conformance Tester

- Add a Test Environment to your configuration.

- Select Insert LIN Slave Conformance Tester in the context menu of the Test Environment.

- Open the Conformance Tester's configuration dialog.

- Select the bus on which you want to test a slave node.

- Select the slave node to be tested.

- Select the data source (LDF or NCF) which should be used to parametrize the test specification.

- Perform further settings if wanted (e.g filename for report or test specification)

- Leave the dialog via the button Generate XML. A XML specification will be generated which is parameterized with the selected data source.

- If you test a real slave node, answer the popup message if all nodes on the bus should be deactivated with yes.

- If you want to simulate the test deactivate all nodes except the tested one.

- Open the Test Module Execution dialog (if not already opened) with a double click on the Conformance Tester in the Test Environment.

- Start the measurement; Start the test within the Test Module Execution dialog.

- When the tests are finished the test report can be opened with the Test Module Execution dialog.

- Conformance Tests can be performed for the following protocol versions: LIN1.3, LIN 2.0 and SAE J2602.

- There are also tests to perform a LIN 2.0 Conformance Test without Reconfiguration. These tests are a development of Vector Informatik corp

[...truncated for brevity...]

# How to Manipulate LIN Signals

|  | Using CAPL signal API Create a LIN configuration. Select a network node (e.g. DWF_Left) in the Simulation Setup (Simulation ribbon tab|Simulation Setup) and choose Configuration… from the context menu. Enter a file name for your CAPL program and then press [Edit] to start the CAPL Browser. Now use the CAPL function SetSignal to manipulate the LIN signal FrontLeftSwitchStatus.To get the signal's latest value use the function GetSignal.Here is a simple example assuming the LDF database (e.g. door.ldf) defines a signal DWFL_WinPos: on key ’+’{ int val; val=$DWFL_WinPos; // reading signal $DWFL_WinPos=val+1; // writing signal} Now save and compile the CAPL program e.g. by pressing <F9> and start the measurement. Open the Trace Window (Analysis ribbon tab|Trace) and expand the corresponding LIN frame (e.g. DWFL_WinPos).On pressing the key <+>, the signal should now increase its value by one. |

|  | Using the CAPL function "output()" You can also update signals using the CAPL function output. Using this method, you can update multiple frame signals simultaneously. To try out this method, add a new event handler for the key<-> to your CAPL program with the following code: on key ’-’{ linFrame DWFL_WinPos myframe; int val; val=myframe.FWL_WinPos; myframe.FWL_WinPos=val-1; output(myframe);} Now save and recompile your CAPL program and start the measurement again. With key<-> you should be

### [LIN/HowTos] LIN_Control_LIN-Master-Scheduler


# How to Control a LIN Master's Scheduler

LIN » How to Control a LIN Master's Scheduler

### Using the Interactive Master

|  | Step by Step Procedure Create a LIN configuration. In the Simulation Setup (Simulation ribbon tab|Simulation Setup) insert an Interactive Master block using the context menu. Open the Interactive Master block e.g. per double-click. Configure per context menu, which schedule the Interactive Master should start on measurement start. Per default the first schedule defined in the LDF will be started. Start the measurement and change the schedules as desired interactively. |

## Using CAPL

|  | Step by Step Procedure Create a LIN configuration Select the Master network node in the Simulation Setup (Simulation ribbon tab|Simulation Setup) and choose Configuration… from the context menu. Enter a file name for your CAPL program and then press [Edit] to start the CAPL Browser. Now use the CAPL function LINChangeSchedTable to determine when a schedule table change should take place. For example to change to the second schedule table in the LDF on pressing key <2> insert the following: on key ’2’ { LINChangeSchedTable(1); // Index starts with zero } Now compile the CAPL program e.g. by pressing <F9> and start the measurement. On pressing the key <2> the Master's scheduler should change to the second schedule defined in your LDF (e.g. Table1) in LDF example door.ldf. |

# How to Create a CANoe/CANalyzer.LIN Configuration

LIN » How to create a CANoe.LIN Configuration

To create a LIN configuration using a LDF, simply follow the next steps.

|  | Step by Step Procedure Create a new configuration using File ribbon tab|New|Create Configuration. Select the LIN template and activate Use Wizard. Now press [Database]. Open your LDF, e.g. Door.LDF Open Folder. Press [Continue]. Select those nodes to be simulated from the Available Nodes list. Assign them to the Assigned Nodes list and press [Continue]. Select one of the LIN channels from the Available Channels list. Assign it to the Assigned Channels list and press [Continue]. To complete your configuration press [Finish]. The configuration wizard has now automatically added the Master and Slave nodes to your LIN network and configured the communication according to your LDF. |

### [LIN/HowTos] LIN_Create_LDF


# How to Create a LIN Description File

LIN » How to Create a LIN Description File

## LIN Database

Although not required, it is highly recommended that you use a LIN database when developing, analyzing or testing LIN networks. The LIN database is described as a LIN description file (LDF) using the LIN configuration language as defined in the LIN specification.

## LDF Explorer

If you do not already have a LDF, you can easily create one using the LDF Explorer utility provided with CANoe.LIN. You can start the LDF Explorer from the Windows Start menu path (Start|Vector CANwin|Tools|LDF Explorer - LIN).

With the LDF Explorer you can also view and analyze your LDFs by double-clicking an LDF either in the Windows Explorer or by opening LDF Explorer in CANoe.LIN Tools ribbon tab.

## Add schedule tables

To add schedule tables to your LDF, you can use the LDF Explorer, which can also be accessed from the Windows Start menu path (Start|Program Files|CANwin|Tools|LDF Explorer) or directly from within CANoe.LIN e.g. via File|Open LDF Explorer or the toolbar icon .

|  | Note Example LDFs can be found in the LIN sample configuration subdirectories e.g. Open Folder |

How to Create a CANoe.LIN Configuration

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [LIN/HowTos] LIN_create_ncf


# How to Create a Node Capability File (LIN)

LIN » More How to's ... » How to Create a Node Capability File

## Creation of an NCF

if you need a NCF File create one using the LDF Explorer utility provided with CANoe.LIN. You can start the LDF Explorer from the Windows Start menu path (Start|Program Files|Vector LDF Explorer).

The LDF Explorer provides also the capability to generate an NCF out of an LDF.

How to Create a CANoe.LIN Configuration

# How to Log and Replay LIN Traffic (LIN)

LIN » How to Log and Replay LIN Traffic

## Log

You can log LIN traffic by activating the connection to the Logging block in the Measurement Setup. Double-click this block to open its configuration dialog.

## Analysis

To analyze a logging file offline, use the offline mode and configure the block in Measurement Setup (click the context menu command Configuration…) to use your logging file.

## Replay

Alternatively you replay a logging file by inserting and configuring a LIN Replay Block in the Simulation Setup.

### [LIN/HowTos] LIN_Howto_Analyze_Network


# How to Analyze a LIN Network

LIN » How to analyze a LIN Network

## How to analyze a LIN Network

If you have a real LIN system connected to your LIN hardware, using the created configuration with the appropriate LDF, you can now press start button to observe the LIN traffic in the Trace Window (Analysis ribbon tab|Trace). The Trace Window not only displays valid LIN frames, but all types of LIN bus events and errors.

By expanding a LIN frame, you can view its signals as defined in the LDF associated to this LIN channel. Additional LIN-specific columns can be added to the Trace Window using its configuration dialog.

To view the LIN statistics, open the LIN Statistics Window via double-click on the LIN Statistics Window in the Measurement Setup or via the Analysis ribbon tab|LIN Statistics.

How to Create a CANoe.LIN Configuration |How to Create a LIN Description File | LIN Communication (Network Errors and Network Events)

### [LIN/HowTos] LIN_Howto_Gateway_CAN_LIN


# How to simulate a CAN-LIN Gateway

LIN » More How to's ... » How to Simulate a CAN-LIN Gateway

A CAN-LIN gateway works on the basis of a common network node (gateway node) on a CAN and a LIN network. The network node shall have the same name in both databases. The network node in the Simulation Setup shall reference this node. The node contains a CAPL program for both networks, which routs messages between these two busses.

To configure a CAN-LIN gateway, please do following.

## Create a configuration

Create a configuration containing a CAN and a LIN network. Assign a CAN database for the CAN network and a LIN database (LDF file) for the LIN network.

## Configuration of the CAN network

- Add a node to the CAN database, e.g. with the name Gateway.

- Add a node to the CAN network in the Simulation etup. The database and the network node in the Simulation Setup has to be connected. Please assign the Gateway node from the database to the network node in the Simulation Setup using node context menu Configuration…

- Add an empty CAPL program to the gateway node.

## Configuration of the LIN network

- Add a master node to the LIN database named as Gateway.

|  | Notes In a LIN network the master node has to be the Gateway node. While the gateway node contains the same name in both networks the node is automatically identified as gateway node. |

- Copy the gateway node from the CAN network and add it to the LIN network using context menu Paste Shortcut. With this action 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [LIN/HowTos] LIN_Howto_send_LIN_Frames


# How to Send a LIN Frame

LIN » How to Send a LIN Frame

## Sending of Reconfiguration Commands

Normally, reconfiguration commands are defined in a separate table. Headers defined in this table are only sent once after a power-on or a reset. This behavior can be simulated in CANoe/CANalyzer. By using the CAPL-function LINChangeSchedTable in on preStart, it is possible to start with any table. In order to change to another table after having sent all the headers of the first one, the CAPL-function LINChangeSchedTable should be called again in on preStart. This time with the ID of the table to be used after reconfiguration and with the third parameter set to –1. This third parameter simply means that the schedule table change should take place on completion of the currently configured table.

|  | Note When sending reconfiguration commands, the transmission data for the frame 0x3C (MasterReq) will be changed permanently. |

## Sending of Diagnostic Frames

If diagnostic frames are defined in Free Format, then it is possible to specify the transmission data in the LDF-file. In this case, the transmission data are automatically configured during sending of the table.

|  | Note When sending diagnostic frames in Free Format, the transmission data for the frame 0x3C (MasterReq) will be changed permanently. |

## Sending of Event Triggered Frames

If a collision occurs while sending an Event Triggered Frame (e.g. receive error with invalid ID or checksum error), then the internal

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [LIN/HowTos] LIN_Howto_Simulate_Diagnostics


# How to Simulate a LIN Diagnostic Tester

LIN » More How to's ... » How to Simulate a LIN Diagnostic Tester

## To simulate a diagnostic tester on a LIN bus perform the following steps:

- Add a diagnostic description or a basic diagnostic device to your configuration.

- For that purpose open the diagnostic configuration dialog via Configuration|Diagnostics/ISO TP Configuration…

- Assign a diagnostic description file or a basic diagnostic device to your LIN bus (represented with an own tab). You may also add an generic diagnostic description which is shipped with your installation.

- After adding a diagnostic description file or a basic diagnostic device the dialog Diagnostic Description Settings opens.

- Name your control unit in the ECU control.

- Select the slave node under test via the Function/Node control.

- The slave node must be described in an LDF file which is assigned to the configuration and must have assigned a unique NAD.

- When leaving the dialog the Diagnostic Consoles are opened. The consoles allow to send diagnostic services directly.

- Add the master node in the Simulation Setup. This is necessary to set the LIN hardware into master mode and to start the scheduling.

- Instead of the Diagnostic Consoles it is also possible to use a test module and to send diagnostic requests via CAPL. If you work with a basic diagnostic device configure your diagnostic services via the Basic Diagnostic Editor.

### [LIN/HowTos] LIN_Show_Signals


# How to View LIN Signals

LIN » How to View LIN Signals

To view frame signals numerically, either open an existing Data Window (Analysis ribbon tab|Data) or create a new one in the Measurement Setup (Analysis ribbon tab|Measurement Setup).

You can add signals to this window in one of the following ways:

- Via context menu commandAdd signals

- Per drag and drop from the Symbol Explorer (Analysis ribbon tab|Symbol Explorer)

- Per drag and drop from the Trace Window (Analysis ribbon tab|Trace).

To view frame signals graphically, either open an existing Graphics Window (Analysis ribbon tab|Graphics) or create a new one in the Measurement Setup (Analysis ribbon tab|Measurement Setup). You can add signals in the same way as for the Data Window.

Signals can also be viewed by creating your own panels using the Panel Editor utility (Tools ribbon tab|Panel Designer).

|  | Step by Step Procedure Select the Input/Output box and insert it via drag &v drop on the panel. Select a LIN signal in the Properties Window. Now save your panel (File|Save). To configure CANoe to use this panel, select Home ribbon tab|Panel Configuration and use the [Add…] button to add the panel to your configuration. To display your panel on the currently active desktop, press the button [Display] and then close the dialog with [OK]. After measurement start it should now be possible to view the signal’s value using the Input/Output Box. |

### [LIN/HowTos] LIN_Simulating_LIN-Master


# How to Simulate a LIN Master

LIN » More How to's ... » How to Simulate a LIN Master

## General

On a LIN network, a bus node (network node) has to act as the LIN master. The LIN master is responsible for transmitting LIN headers on the LIN network which are responded to by the LIN slaves. The cyclical, time-controlled transmission of LIN headers is called scheduling. Accordingly, the hardware or software component which executes scheduling is called a scheduler.

In CANoe, an internal scheduler is set up through the assignment of a network node as an LDF master node. This scheduler supports all LIN frame types. For LIN 2.0 and LIN 2.1, reconfiguration commands, diagnostic messages and headers for event triggered frames and sporadic frames are also transmitted.

In CANoe, the assignment of a node as an LDF master is done in the node configuration dialog of the Simulation Setup.

## Methods

The following methods for the simulation of a LIN Master are available:

## 1. Using schedule tables

- Use a LIN database to configure a Master node and its schedule tables

- Assign the LIN database to a LIN channel

- Configure the master nodes as follows:

- Create a network node on the suitable channel and assign the master node from the database to it in the configuration dialog.

|  | Note This method automatically activates the Master mode of the LIN hardware. |

- The LIN Interactive Master can also be used. You can switch between tables during the measurement with this. In CA

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [LIN/HowTos] LIN_Simulating_LIN-Slaves


# How to Simulate LIN Slaves

LIN » More How to's ... » How to Simulate LIN Slaves

You can use the following three methods to simulate LIN Slaves:

## 1. Using LIN database settings

- Create a LIN database defining the Slave node(s) and signals to be published

- Assign the LIN database to a LIN channel

- Configure CANoe to use the Slave node(s) defined in the database: associate the Slave node(s) with network node(s) in the simulation setup

- On measurement start, the signal values of the Slave’s responses are automatically set according to the initial values defined in the LIN database

## 2. Using simple CAPL APIs

The CAPL function output() can be used to change the transmission data of any frame.

|  | Note This method overwrites the initial signal values defined in the LIN database(s). |

## 3. Using an Interactive Generator

- Define an Interactive Generator in the simulation setup

- Select LIN frame(s) and choose the Response option in Frame column

### [LIN/HowTos] LIN_Simulating_Multi_Channel_Master


# How to Simulate a Multi-Channel Master (LIN)

LIN » More How to's ... » How to Simulate a Multi Channel Master

|  | Note The sample configuration System Configuration (LIN) contains GWI and GWE as multi-channel master nodes. |

To create a configuration with a multi-channel master node, please do as follows:

## Define the multi-channel master in the Simulation Setup

- Create all LIN buses in the Simulation Setup.

- Create a CAPL node on one of the LIN buses. Add the name of the master node from the LDF file in the CANdb Name field of the network node's configuration dialog.

- Assign all needed LIN buses to the network node using the Buses tab of the configuration dialog.

## The Bus Context - Working with multiple LIN channels in CAPL

Modeling a LIN master in CAPL the used bus interface (channel) has to be unique. To facilitate this distinction, a bus context is placed in the CAPL program by the runtime environment. This context identifies the bus, on which an action has to be done. CAPL functions are used to set and access the bus context.

## Define the Bus Context in CAPL

In following the steps are described to constitute the bus context.

- Step 1: Define Variables

Variables are used to realize the bus context in CAPL. For every LIN bus a variable has to be defined to represent the corresponding LIN bus.

|  | Example variables{dword gDoorLeft_context = 0; dword gDoorRight_context = 0;dword gRoofUnit_context = 0;char gDoorLeft[32] = "DoorLeft" ;char gDoorRight[

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [LIN/LINSlaveConformanceTest] LINSCTIUTInitialization


# IUT Initialization Settings

LIN Slave Conformance Test » IUT Initialization Settings

Test Setup for Test Modules|Slave Conformance Test Module|Configuration…|IUT Initialization…

There are several possibilities to reset a slave node. It is also possible to specify the times needed for a reset.

## Reset Type (prior to each test case)

- HW-reset using VH11xx This is the recommended reset method and conforms to the LIN conformance test specification. The hardware reset is performed using Vector's test hardware VH1100 or VH1150/VH1160.

- HW-reset using VT7001 The hardware reset is performed using VT System power module VT7001.

- HW-reset manually The hardware reset must be performed manually. Each test case will wait for confirmation of the manual reset.

- SW-Reset (waiting for BusIdle)Only a software reset is performed by waiting for the time specified for bus idle timeout (see separate edit box) to occur prior to each test case.

- No reset No hardware reset is performed. This method can be used for initial tests if no hardware reset is easily possible.

## Reset Parameters

- Power off time Time the IUT needs for an hardware reset. For this time period the IUT supply is disconnected.

- Wait for BusIdle time Time the bus has to be idle, so that the IUT performs an software reset.

- IUT init time Time the IUT needs to start up after a reset.

- Supply voltage Supply voltage of the IUT.

## VT7001 Settings

- Module name Name of the VT7001 module to be used. With auto

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [LIN/LINSlaveConformanceTest] LINSCTIUTSettings1x


# IUT Settings LIN 1.x

LIN Slave Conformance Test » IUT Settings LIN 1.x

Test Setup for Test Modules|Slave Conformance Test Module|Configuration...|IUT Settings...

IUT settings not specified in the database can be configured here.

## Common Settings

- Supports interbyte >= 1 bit This check box should only be deactivated, if the IUT's interbyte space is not always larger than or equal to 1 bit. All test cases requiring interbyte space will be deactivated, in order to prevent inconsistent test results.

- Sends wake-up signal This check box should only be deactivated, if the IUT is unable to send a wake-up sequence. All test cases for wake-up sequences will be deactivated.

- Wake-up delimiter Wake-up delimiter length (in bit times) expected by IUT. Default value is the allowed minimum: 4 bit times

## Diagnostic Service

The data of IUT-specific diagnostic request and response should be defined here. The request is transmitted via MasterRequest frame (0x3C) and the response is expected via SlaveResponse frame (0x3D).

### [LIN/LINSlaveConformanceTest] LINSCTIUTSettingsSAEJ2602


# IUT Settings SAE J2602

LIN Slave Conformance Tests » IUT Settings SAE J2602

Test Setup for Test Modules|Slave Conformance Test Module|Configuration…|IUT Settings…

Here you can configure IUT settings that are not specified in the database.

## Common Settings

- Sends wake-up signal Please deactivate this option only if the IUT is unable to send a wakeup sequence. All test cases for wakeup sequences will be deactivated.

- Deviant Initial Node Address (NAD) Initial node address value is a mandatory parameter for performing conformance tests. Here it is possible to set it or to override the value defined in the assigned database.

- Supports autobauding Please activate this option only if the IUT has an autobauding clock.

- Supports hardware DNN selection Please activate this option only if the IUT supports DNN selection via hardware, e.g. using a deep switch.

- Supports software DNN selection Please activate this option only if the IUT supports DNN selection via software, i.e. if the DNN can be programmed somehow.

## Configuration Services

- Supports AssignNAD (0xB0) service Please deactivate this option only if the IUT does not support this optional service.

- Supports AssignFrameID (0xB1) service Please deactivate this option only if the IUT does not support this optional service.

### [LIN/LINSlaveConformanceTest] LINSCTModule


# LIN Slave Conformance Test

LIN » Slave Conformance Test Module

Test Environment Context Menu: Insert LIN Slave Conformance Tester

## General

This special test module allows the conformance of a Slave node (IUT) to be easily tested using a CANoe configuration. The slave conformance test module can be inserted in the Test Setup using the context menu. The test module generates a XML test specification for the selected slave node. The generic test cases are implemented in CAPL libraries, which are automatically assigned to the test module. The test cases can be controlled via the test module's execution window. It is currently possible to perform slave conformance tests for the LIN protocol versions 1.x, 2.x and SAE J2602. The Vector hardware VN1611 is currently not supported.

|  | Notes for LIN 2.1 You can perform all tests (except for the sampling point test 3.17) with the XL-hardware. However, 3 tests cannot be performed exactly as specified using the XL: Variation of Length of break field low phase (Test 3.2) Variation of Header Length (Test 3.11) Bit rate tolerance (Test 3.13 and 3.14) These tests are marked with a warning in the test report. With the CANCardXLe and VN modules (VN8900, VN1600) all tests (including the sampling point test 3.17) can be performed strictly according to the LIN2.1 conformance test specification. |

|  | Notes on Test Coverage The slave conformance test module supports tests for layer 2 and 3. Physical tests and EMC tests are not supporte

[...truncated for brevity...]

## On test case fail

- Add Trace snapshot to the report After each failed test case a Trace snapshot is added to the report.See test report: window capture. for further information on trace snapshots in the test report.

- Name of the Trace Window Name of the Trace Window from which the snapshots are taken.

- Trigger Scope hardware (Scope option is required)USB connection to the Scope hardware will be established automatically on starting the tests. After that on each failed test case the Scope hardware will be triggered automatically.

- Add Scope snapshot to report Scope Window snapshot will be added to the report each time the Scope hardware triggers.

## Use Scope Option for Verification (only J2602)

Test cases of the J2602 Conformance Tests requiring manual verification can be performed using the Scope option. The scope will be triggered at the correct time if manual verification is needed.

This is possible for the following test cases:

- 7.4.1.4.* (Recessive input voltage threshold)

- 7.4.1.6.* (Dominant input voltage threshold)

## Deactivate internal Master Resistor (only J2602)

Some test cases of the J2602 Conformance Tests require the use of an external network load. With this option, the internal Master Resistor can be deactivated during these test cases so i

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [LIN/Protocol_Versions] Cooling


# COOLING Protocol (LIN)

LIN » Protocol » COOLING

## Introduction

This document describes the results of an analysis made by Vector according to the COOLING-specification Cool_lin_1.0._F (released 2003-10-07) using CANoe 5.0 SP3 with the LIN option.

## Database

An LDF database is not suitable for defining COOLING networks, since for each frame only one sender/publisher can be defined. Vector's DBC format, however, permits the definition of multiple senders/publishers and also allows the additional COOLING attributes (e.g. node address) to be defined. Vector has therefore prepared a DBC database example for COOLING customers. If necessary, this database can be edited by customers using the CANdb++ Editor provided with CANoe.

## Bus Monitoring

Using a DBC database for COOLING, all COOLING frames can be monitored and displayed symbolically in the Trace Window.

- Address Filtering Instead of displaying all COOLING frames in the Trace Window, it can be useful to display the frames for each COOLING slave in separate Trace Windows. This can be achieved by using a very simple CAPL program, which filters the COOLING frames according to their first data byte i.e. the slave’s address. An sample configuration has been created for COOLING, which demonstrates how to do this. Please note that the filter cannot currently be used for this purpose.

- Addressing Service The current version of the COOLING specification permits long synch breaks for auto-addressing COOLING-slaves. For t

[...truncated for brevity...]

# Option MOST

Standards – Bus Systems, Protocols, Databases » Option MOST

|  | MOST (Media Oriented Systems Transport) is a versatile, high-performance multimedia network technology based on synchronous data communication. ### [MOST] MOSTElectricalControlLine


# Electrical Control Line - ECL

MOST » Electrical Control Line

If the MOST ring is interrupted, the individual MOST control units in the vehicle are no longer available via MOST. Thus, it is hardly possible to localize the interruption of the ring.

The Electrical Control Line (ECL), which is specially designated for MOST150 systems, serves to additionally connect all MOST control units in a star topology.

Via this connection, together with a simple serial protocol, the ECL makes it possible to query the status of the individual control units independently from MOST and thus to localize the interruption.

Besides this improved Ring Break Diagnosis (RBD), the ECL is also used (depending on the OEM) for the stimulation of other tests as well as for waking up the ECUs.

## ECL Support

CANoe, in conjunction with VN2640, supports the ECL by means of multiple features:

- The VN2640 is equipped with an ECL port which is used for directly capturing the ECL signal or for stimulating the line.

The ECL port of the VN2640 is galvanically isolated. For this reason, please ensure that the supply voltage (Vbat) and GND are connected to this port. A voltage source might be required for lab operation; the power supply in the vehicle must be realized via the same voltage source as that of the control units since the signal thresholds of the ECL-input are derived from this voltage. (For further information on the ECL port wiring, please refer to the VN2640 manual).

- Line status display

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [MOST] MOSTSimulationMode


# Simulation Mode

MOST » Simulation Mode

CANoe offers a simulation mode in which CAPL nodes access a simulated bus instead of the real bus. With .MOST the operation in simulation mode approaches that of a real MOST ring. Properties that are considered include: Sequence of devices in the ring, message addressing and retries when transmission fails. Some properties are not simulated such as arbitration or behavior of the ring when a node is connected/disconnected. This mode is very useful, e.g. to start sample configurations supplied with CANoe.MOST or to subject user-created models to initial testing without the connected hardware.

- Each channel in the Simulation Setup represents a MOST device. Device settings such as Master/ Slave/ Spy or node addresses are taken from the Hardware Configuration Dialog.

- The channels are interconnected in a ring shape by a virtual cable. The sequence follows the channel numbering. A Timing Master is either configured in a device, or else the first device to be configured as a Slave assumes this task. This allows the user to switch over – with little effort –from operation with a real bus to simulated operation resuming with the previously simulated nodes.

- Messages are routed around the ring from the sender over the consecutive nodes until it reaches the receiver, and from there it continues until it arrives back at the Sender. Message addressing is considered in this process. For example, retries are executed if a message is sent to 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [MOST] MOSTTestConcept


# Test Concept

MOST » Test Concept

CANoe supports testing of MOST systems with a bundle of specialized XML test patterns, checks and CAPL functions:

## MOST specific XML Patterns

| Test Pattern | Description |
| --- | --- |
| FBlocksExistence | This pattern checks the existence of FBlocks in a device to be tested. |
| FunctionsInFBlock | This pattern checks the existence of functions in an FBlock. |
| IsProperty | This pattern detects whether a given function address is a property or not. |
| NetBlockComplete | This pattern checks the completeness of the NetBlock of a device. |
| Replay | In the Replay test pattern a Replay file is played back. |
| Request/Response | In the Request/Response test pattern, a message or message sequence is sent and a corresponding response or several logically interconnected responses are awaited. |

## MOST specific Checks

| Check | Description | Availability |
| --- | --- | --- |
| CAPL | XML |
| MOST Error Messages | Monitors the occurrence of MOST error messages (OpType 0xF or 0x9). | • | • |
| MOST Light & Lock Observation | Monitors the occurrence of LightLock events. | • | • |
| MOST NetState Observation | Monitors the occurrence of NetState events. | • | • |
| MOST Property Protocol Observation | Monitors compliance with the MOST protocol for a specified property. | • | • |
| MOST Method Protocol Observation | Monitors compliance with the MOST protocol for a specified method. | • | — |

## MOST specific CAPL Functions

| CAPL Funct

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [MOST] most_filter_possibilities


# Filter

MOST » Filter

CANoe.MOST offers a range of possibilities for filtering the data displayed in the Trace Window or recorded in a logging file. This page helps you select an appropriate filtering option based on a series of typical use cases.

## Use cases:

### Observation only, possibly with simulation (e.g. via IG) as a secondary requirement

- Set up node/spy filtering in the Hardware Configuration dialog so that the Analysis branch only shows spy messages. If you don't want this, you can add a MOST application filter to the test setup instead. Filtering takes place via insertion of a line that simply selects the direction attribute between node and spy messages. All other attributes are left with wildcard "*" for this.

- Configure the MOST filters in the Trace Window configuration so that all CMS messages, but only segmented AMS messages, are displayed. This results in the most compact Trace Window display.

### Restriction to messages from one bus system

Insert a channel filter in the test setup

### Restriction to messages from one device, function block or use case

Insert a MOST application filter in the test setup and configure it using wildcards (*)

### Computer with low performance capacity or with many bus systems attached

- If access to an asynchronous channel is not needed, you should deactivate this feature in the Hardware Configuration dialog.

- If the MOST FBlock Monitor is not needed, you can switch off the corresponding service in the Hardwar

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [MOST] option_most_timestamps


# Timestamps

MOST » Timestamps

The resolution of the time stamp in CANoe depends on which bus interface is being used. For Vector hardware, it is typically 1 µs. The resolution displayed in the Trace Window can be set in the Options dialog. In CAPL, the selector time and most CAPL functions deliver a resolution of 10 µs.

All events received prior to time synchronization being able to calculate a valid offset between the CAN bus and MOST ring are suppressed. It is for this reason that the first MOST frames displayed in a Trace Window may show a time stamp which predates the start of measurement by a few seconds.

Here you can find detailed information about synchronized time stamps.

## Timestamps in COM

For all MOST events a 64 bit time stamp is delivered as two 32 bit Signed Integers (timeHigh, time). The value determines the time in 10 µs. A time stamp with the value 100000 means, for example, that the according event exactly occurred one second after measurement start.

Although the signatures are Signed Integer, both parts of the time stamp have to be interpreted as Unsigned values. In client languages that don't have Unsigned Integer types (e.g. Visual Basic) time stamps > 5.96 hours need a special treatment.

One possibility to process large time stamps is to save them as a Double value (or similar) internally in the client. By doing so, you have to take care that there may occur rounding problems especially with Double values and large time stamps.

|  | Example T

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [MOST] option_most_time_synchronization


# Time Synchronization Accuracy

MOST » Timestamps » Time Synchronization Accuracy

We recommend that you use hardware based time synchronization to measure gateway throughput times. This is very precise and eliminates the offset of clocks on different bus interfaces virtually completely.

Click here to find out more.

### [MOST/MOST150] MOST150


# MOST150

MOST » MOST150

CANoe version 7.1 SP4 and newer supports MOST150.

Besides meeting the special requirements of the MOST150 system, Vector also took the needs of experienced MOST25 users into consideration, and training effort is kept very low thanks to the unchanged user interface. For the most part, the programming interface has been kept identical, too. The tool accepts existing simulations and test scripts. However some differences in the user interface and the programming interface have to be considered.

## Hardware Interfaces

Starting with version 7.5 SP2, CANoe supports the Vector interface VN2640. It is connected to the computer by means of USB 2.0 and requires an appropriate, high-performance driver developed by Vector. The MOST option supports MOST25 (VN2610), MOST50 and MOST150 (VN2640) with the same activation.

In MOST150 networks with optical physical layer the Vector interface VN2640 and K2L’s OptoLyzer G2 3150o and OptoLyzer G2 3150o Production are applicable. Coaxial networks using simplex (unidirectional) transmission can be accessed with the same interfaces if an additional converter module (e.g. MOST150 C-O Converter of K2L) is joined up in circuit. Starting with version 8.5, CANoe supports coaxial networks with duplex (bidirectional) transmission and the interface OptoLyzer MOCCA compact 150c of K2L.

OptoLyzer G2 3150o, OptoLyzer G2 3150o Production and OptoLyzer MOCCA compact 150c require a license for the OptoLyzer Integration Package (OIP

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [MOST/MOST150] MOST150DifferencesToMOST25


# Differences to MOST25

MOST » MOST150 » Differences to MOST25

## Setup: Node & Spy

VN2640 simultaneously supports the node and spy on the same channel with comparable time stamps and latencies for both event sources.

For technical reasons, even though the OptoLyzer G2 3150o supports node and spy at the same time, it is strongly recommended to assign these two event sources (node & spy) to separate MOST channels of CANoe. Therefore two MOST channels can use the same OptoLyzer device.

## Timing Mode

Switching from Slave to non-static Master does not wake the network automatically. To start the network use action wake-up of MOST State Window or call CAPL function MostWakeup.

## Control channel

- Control Messages now can have up to 45 user data bytes

When existing CAPL code evaluates the DLC of mostMessages unprecedented high values may occur. CAPL code working on mostAmsMessages is safe, as these messages have not changed.

Currently, sending on the control channel only supports messages with a value of TelLen in the data area which corresponds to the real length of the CMS message. Thus, sending erroneous CMS messages with invalid TelLen values is not possible.

- No CMS acknowledgments for segmented AMS transmissions

With MOST25 each CMS frame of a segmented AMS transmission is either received by the node or reported as Tx acknowledgment to the node; this also applies to the MOST150 Interface VN2640. With VN2640, both the node mode and spy mode supply a complete pi

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [MOST/MOST50] MOST50


# MOST50

MOST » MOST50 Overview

CANoe version 7.2 SP3 and newer supports MOST50.

Besides meeting the special requirements of the MOST50 system, Vector also took the needs of experienced MOST25 users into consideration, and training effort is kept very low thanks to the unchanged user interface. For the most part, the programming interface has been kept identical, too. The tool accepts existing simulations and test scripts. However some differences in the user interface and the programming interface have to be considered.

## Hardware Interfaces

CANoe supports OptoLyzer MOCCA compact 50e, OptoLyzer G2 3050e and OptoLyzer G2 3050e Production from K2L to access the MOST50 network. OptoLyzer MOCCA compact 50e is connected over USB to the computer. The required drives are part of the delivery of K2L. OptoLyzer G2 3050e and OptoLyzer G2 3050e Production are connected over Ethernet. Beside a standard Ethernet network adapter, no additional drivers or software packages need to be installed.

To use the interfaces, a license for the OptoLyzer Integration Package (OIP) from Vector is needed. Please contact Vector’s sales department for a quotation.

## Features

This version of MOST50 support allows access to the control channel and packet channel as node or spy. Many practice-proven analysis features are offered, such as the Audio Routing, Trace Windows, Data Windows and Graphics Windows , and the MOST FBlock Monitor. The Interactive Generator allows a flexible way to stimulate t

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [MOST/MOST50] MOST50DifferencesToMOST25


# Differences to MOST25

MOST » MOST50 » Differences to MOST25

## Light & Lock, resp. MOST signal

With MOST50, data is transmitted via electrical lines. However, just as with the optical MOST25 and MOST150 systems, MOST50 also binds the individual devices into a ring using point-to-point connections. In the case of MOST50, as with the others, this ring contains exactly one selected device that acts as the timing master and MOST signal source. All the other devices synchronize to this MOST signal. This signal can, just like the light in the case of MOST25, either be present or not. The light/no light terminology is retained in CANoe/CANalyzer, which means that the MOST signal is on or off. In addition, slave devices can either be synchronized to the timing master (Lock) or not (Unlock). These cases are handled in the same way for all most systems.

## Setup: Node & Spy

For technical reasons, even though the OptoLyzer G2 3050e supports node and spy at the same time, we strongly recommend that you assign these two event sources (node/spy) to separate MOST channels in CANoe. In this way, two MOST channels can use the same OptoLyzer device.

The OptoLyzer G2 3050e does not currently support the software synchronization of spy channel time stamps with time stamps of other channels. If you need to synchronize the spy, you can connect a sync line (see Installation OptoLyzer G2 3050e) to the OptoLyzer and the bus interfaces of the other channels.

## Current limitations of the Opt

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [MOST/MOSTHighProtocol] MOSTHighProtocolAutomaticMHPReceiver


# Automatic MHP Receiver

MOST » MOST High Protocol » Automatic MHP Receiver

Introduction

The automatic MHP receiver serves as a simple data sink (DSI) for incoming connections. It implements the receiver-side MOST High Protocol (MHP). The protocol parameters are set for this in such a way that as many incoming connections as possible are accepted regardless of the requirements specified for the transmission by the sender.

## Use cases

- Stimulation For the query of an FBlock property transmitted via MHP, the Get command with the MOST IG is sent via the control channel. The status response is received by means of MHP. For this purpose a receiver (DSI) must send a series of acknowledgments during the transmission. The automatic MHP receiver ensures that this occurs without the need for further configuration or programming effort.

- Simulation During simulation of MHP-capable FBlocks or FBlock shadows, the automatic MHP receiver takes over the control of the MHP protocol without also having to simulate the receiver.

- Test In addition, when control units are tested, often the only thing that matters is that a device under test (DUT) can respond to special requests with an MHP transmission and successfully transmit its data. As long as the data contents are of secondary interest, the automatic receiver can control the MHP connection without having to add an MHP simulation to the test script.

## Data analysis

The received data are not saved. However, they are visible in 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [MOST/MOSTHighProtocol] MOSTHighProtocolOverview


# MOST High Protocol

MOST » MOST High Protocol

The packet channel is suitable for transmission of large amounts of data, as it allows transmission of packets up to 1014 bytes (MOST150: 1524 bytes) in size. The MOST High Protocol (MHP) makes it possible to generate directional connections for data transmission with error recognition mechanisms.

The MHP Observer and Combiner is used to analyze MHP data transmissions and detect protocol violations.

The automatic MHP receiver can be activated to receive all incoming MHP connections automatically, and to transmit all necessary acknowledgments to the data source.

The MOST High DLL can be used to simulate MHP connection senders and receivers. This DLL can also be used in the Test Feature Set to create tests using MOST High data transmissions.

- Observer and Combiner: Observe MHP Connections

- Automatic MHP Receiver: MHP Data Sink Simulation

- MHP DLL: Simulation of MHP Sender and Receiver

- Test and Fault Injection of MHP Connections

## Overview of the MOST High Protocol usage

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [MOST/MOSTHighProtocol] MOSTHighProtocolSimulation


# Simulation of MHP Sender and Receiver

MOST » MOST High Protocol » Simulation of MHP Sender and Receiver

MOST High connection senders and receivers are easy to program in CAPL using the MOST High DLL. The DLL has to first be assigned to a network node in the Simulation Setup. To do this, right-click the desired node in the Simulation Setup and choose "Configuration..." from the context menu. Add the DLL (MOST_MHP.DLL) on the "Module" tab. The DLL is located in the Exec32 folder of the MOST High demo.

Here you can find more information about the MOST_MHP.DLL.

The required functions, which are used in the following examples, are then available in CAPL.

Sender

A port has to be created for each outgoing MOST High connection. Each MHP port can only handle exactly one connection at a time.

variables{ long gTxPort; long gDestAdr; long gDestFBlockID; long gDestInstID; long gDestFktID; long gDestOpType;}

on prestart{ gTxPort = MH_CreateTxPort(""); gDestAdr = 0x171; gDestFBlockID = 0x52; gDestInstID = 0x01; gDestFktID = 0xE01; gDestOpType = 0x0C;}

A connection has to be established with the receiver in order to start a data transmission. There are several ways to do this. The simplest way is to pass the name of the file or byte array to be transmitted in the same function call.

on key 'f'{ char filename[255] = "c:\\MHP_Data.TXT"; MH_ReqTrans(gTxPort, gDestAdr, gDestFBlockID, gDestInstID, gDestFktID, gDestOpType, filename);}

on key 's'{ byte data[200]; MH_ReqTrans(gTxPort, 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [MOST/MOSTHighProtocol] MOSTHighProtocolTestAndFaultInjection


# Test and Fault Injection of MHP Connections

MOST » MOST High Protocol » Test and Fault Injection of MHP Connections

The MOST High DLL (MOST_MHP.DLL) can also be used in CAPL test modules. In this case it has to be added to the test node configuration on the Module page. The DLL is located in the Exec32 folder of the MOST High demo. The MOST High functions described below can then be used in the test module.

variables{ long gTxPort;}

testcase TC_MHP(){ byte data[200]; gTxPort = MH_CreateTxPort(""); MH_ReqTrans(gTxPort,0x171, 0x52, //Navigation 0x01, //InstanceID 0xE01, //FunctionID 0xC, //OpType data, elcount(data)); //wait for reply of the receiver if (TestWaitForMostPkt(“Navigation.Waypoints.Status(START CONNECTION)”, 1000) > 0) TestStepPass("1", "Receiver accepts connection request"); else TestStepFail("1", "Receiver didn’t reply");}

|  | Note The TestWaitForMostPkt function can be used independently of the MOST High DLL to wait for MOST High packets as a way of testing MHP connections. |

## Fault Injection

The MOST High DLL has a set of CAPL functions and callbacks that can manipulate messages before they are transmitted or even suppress transmission altogether. Similarly, received messages can also be manipulated or discarded before they reach the MOST High DLL. The fault injection option has to be explicitly activated before any of the individual functions can be used.

on key 'f'{ MH_FI_Active(1);}

long MH_FI_IndTxPreSend(dword handle){ if (MH_FI_GetHighCmd()

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [MOST/MOSTHowTos] MOSTHowToAnalyzeMOSTSystem


# MOST: How to Analyze a MOST System

MOST » How to... » How to Analyze the MOST Ring

After measurement start you can monitor the MOST data traffic in the Trace Window (Analysis ribbon tab|Trace), if

- a real MOST system is connected to your MOST hardware

- a suitable function catalog is assigned to your configuration

The Trace Window displays not only the valid MOST messages but all types of MOST bus events and errors.

If the Trace Window is paused, you can expand a MOST message to see how the parameters of the message are defined in the function catalog. Using the configuration dialog, you can add additional MOST specific columns to the Trace Window.

## Analysis Windows

For MOST there is a set of analysis windows that provide a quick overview of the structure and status of the connected MOST system, without significant configuration effort.

- MOST State Window Displays the current status of the MOST Ring and the main settings of the connected hardware interface.

- MOST System Viewer Displays the monitored structure of the MOST ring and allows a ring scan when the bypass is open.

- MOST Audio Routing Window Displays the current assignment of the synchronous channels. Through selection of a connection label followed by demute, normal audio channels can be listened to with headphones at the MOST interface.

- MOST Central Registry Window Displays the content of the registry based on the monitored ring scans.

- MOST FBlock Monitor Displays all properties of the appl

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [MOST/MOSTHowTos] MOSTHowToConfigureHardware


# MOST: How to Configure Hardware

MOST » How to... » How to Configure Hardware

To create a MOST configuration, simply follow these steps:

|  | Step by Step Procedure Create a new configuration using File ribbon tab|New|Create Configuration. Select the MOST, MOST50 or MOST150 template – depending on the speedgrades of your MOST system. Select the required number of MOST channels in Options dialog (Measurement|General|Channel Usage). Note about MOST50 and MOST150 When using OptoLyzer G2 for MOST50 and MOST150, for technical reasons Vector strongly recommends placing the node and the spy of a connected device on separate channels. Select the hardware to be used (Hardware ribbon tab|Network Hardware). Note about using an OptoLyzer G2 for MOST50 and MOST150 Enter the IP address of the OptoLyzer on the Interface page. If it is already connected, you can determine its IP address at the push of a button. In addition, select the network adapter to which the OptoLyzer is connected. Configure the hardware settings, such as hardware mode, node addresses, or additional services for each channel. Notes Observation If you only want to observe the MOST system activate Bypass, Control-Spy and Asynchroner Spy, if needed. Thereby the MOST interface remains invisible in the MOST ring, it can observe all messages but cannot send any messages. Stimulation If you additionally want to stimulate the MOST system with messages leave Bypass inactive and choose Slave and adequate values for the addre

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [MOST/MOSTHowTos] MOSTHowToFindInterestedMessagesInTraceWindow


# MOST: How to find messages of interest in the Trace Window

MOST » How to... » MOST: How to find messages of interest in the Trace Window

Text Search

The text search entry field can be found in the toolbar of the Trace Window.

You can search for any character strings (e.g., including portions of time stamps); column-overlapping texts cannot be specified.

When the measurement is stopped, you can search the content in the forward direction with <F3> and in the backward direction with <SHIFT>+<F3>.

## Quick Find

The context menu for messages provides the Quick Find command for a corresponding message (e.g., Jump to Next Status Message).

When the measurement is stopped, you can use the following key combinations for the quick find function:

| Function | Key Combination |
| --- | --- |
| Command | F12 |
| First command |  |
| Previous report | <Shift>+<F12> |
| Next report | <Ctrl>+<F12> |

## Filtering

With the filter function in the Trace Window you can incrementally reduce the number of messages displayed to just the messages of interest.

## Highlight Messages

The context menu offers the option to assign color to messages. In this way, you can highlight all messages of the same type, which makes them easier to locate.

MOST Filtering Possibilities

### [MOST/MOSTHowTos] MOSTHowToLogAnayzeMOSTCommunication


# MOST: How to Log and Analyze MOST Communication

MOST » How to... » How to Log and Analyze MOST Communication

You can log MOST data traffic by activating the connection to the Logging block in the Measurement Setup (View|Measurement Setup menu path).

With a double-click on the Logging block you open the Configuration dialog.

To analyze a protocol file offline, use

- Import to Trace Window

Switch to a Trace Window via the menu path View|Trace.

Stop the measurement or pause the Trace Window.

Right-click to open the context menu, and select the menu path Import/Export|Import…

|  | Note Alternatively, you can also use a drag-and-drop operation to import a logging file from Explorer to a Trace Window. |

- Offline mode

Switch to offline mode (Standard toolbar), and configure the block in the Measurement Setup (context menu command Configuration...).

You can now start the measurement, and the logging file will be imported into the Measurement Setup.

With the help of a CAPL node in the Measurement Setup, you can stop the measurement with the Stop() command, for example, when certain situations occur (message sequences, time expiration, parameter values, etc.). At this point, single step mode is active, which allows you to analyze the existing content of the Trace Window and to import the rest of the logging file, if required, by restarting the measurement.

|  | Note Note that the hardware settings are used to decide whether AMS and MHP combiners are to import your ass

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [MOST/MOSTHowTos] MOSTHowToReceieveMessagesPackages


# MOST: How To Receive Messages and Packages (MDP & MEP)

MOST » How to... » How To Receive Messages and Packages (MDP & MEP)

You can receive Messages and packages with CAPL:

|  | Example: Receiving a message on mostAMSMessage NetBlock.DeviceInfo.Get{ // report message variable mostAmsMessage NetBlock.DeviceInfo.Status msg; // initialize report message // (e.g. with source address of the sender of DeviceInfo.Get mostPrepareReport(this, msg); // user specific code to fill parameters of the message // ... // send message output(msg);} |

|  | Example: Receiving a packet (MDP) OnMostPkt(long pktdatalen){ BYTE buffer[1100]; MostPktGetData(buffer, pktdatalen); // user specific code to analyze parameters of the packet // ...} |

|  | Example: Receiving an Ethernet packet (MEP) - only with MOST150 available OnMostEthPkt(long pktDataLen){ BYTE buffer[1506]; MostEthPktGetData (buffer, pktdatalen); // user specific code to analyze parameters of the ethernet packet // ...} |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |

You can import a logging file back to a real ring by inserting and configuring a MOST Replay Block in the Simulation Setup (View|Simulation Setup).

### [MOST/MOSTHowTos] MOSTHowToSendMessagesPackages


# MOST: How To Send Messages and Packages (MDP & MEP)

MOST » How to... » How To Send Messages and Packages (MDP & MEP)

You can send MOST messages and packages as follows:

## MOST Interactive Generator (MOST IG)

The MOST Interactive Generator (MOST IG) offers the fastest way to send MOST messages or packets. The configuration user interface for entry of messages can be opened via the Simulation ribbon tab|MOST IG. To insert packets in the send list, use the drop down menu of the button [New].

|  | Notes If the Simulation ribbon tab does not offer MOST IG, this means that the configuration does not contain such a Generator. To insert a new block, the measurement must be stopped and the MOST IG must be inserted into the Simulation Setup as a block. The Interactive Generator does not support sending of Ethernet packets (MEP). |

## CAPL

Alternatively, CAPL nodes can be used to send messages or packets, which is especially useful if messages have to be sent in reaction to events on the bus, e.g. as response to other messages.

As preparation a network node has to be inserted in the Simulation Setup and a CAPL file has to be assigned to this node.

|  | Example: Sending a message on keyboard hit (It would also be possible to achieve this exact behavior through suitable configuration with the MOST IG.): on key ’m’{ // channel, destination address, message specification, instance id mostAmsOutput(1, 0x100,”NetBlock.DeviceInfo.Get(0x00)”, 0x00);} |

|  | Example: Sending a pack

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [MOST/MOSTHowTos] MOSTHowToStimulateMOSTSystem


# MOST: How To Stimulate a MOST System

MOST » How to... » How to Stimulate a MOST System

|  | Note You can only stimulate your MOST system with messages or packages, if the MOST interface has opened the bypass. If the bypass is closed (pure spy operation) sending of messages is not possible. |

You can stimulate a MOST system as follows:

## StressWindow

The Stress Window offers various options for stimulating the MOST system.

The configuration user interface for selecting and setting the stress mode can be opened via the Simulation ribbon tab|MOST Stress.

MOST messages or packets can then be sent cyclically in order to stress the MOST system. In addition, unlock cycles or ring interruption phases can be created. Both stimulation methods also works in the pure spy operation.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [MOST/MOSTHowTos] MOSTHowToTransmitMOSTAnalysis


# MOST: How to Transmit MOST Analysis

MOST » How to... » How to Transmit MOST Analysis

If the analysis is available as a logging file and …

- …the addressee also uses Vector programs

Simply forward the logging file. The addressee can view your recorded messages in offline mode or by import to the Trace Window. For the logging format, Vector recommends BLF (very compact and performant) or ASC (can be read and, for example, edited via a script).

- …the addressee uses programs of third-party providers

Convert the logging file to a format that is supported by the program of the third-party provider (e.g., OP2 or IMG). The ASC format defined by Vector is currently supported by a few programs.

You can perform the conversion using the Export context menu command of the Logging Block file symbol in the Measurement Setup. Alternatively, you can read in the logging file in offline mode – if, for example, the content is still to be filtered – and use the Logging Block to record in the target format.

- …the addressee does not use a program

You can convert small logging files to HTML format. You can perform the conversion using the Export context menu command of the Logging Block file symbol in the Measurement Setup. In the Export dialog, select XML as the target format. The disassembly and the assembled events of the AMS and MHP combiners are retained. Likewise, the typical highlighting of MOST messages is retained.

|  | Caution! For large logging files, this operation can tak

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [MOST/MOSTHowTos] MOSTHowToWakeUpMOSTSytem


# MOST: How to Wake Up the MOST System

MOST » How to... » How to Wake Up the MOST System

If your MOST interface does not indicate a light (e.g., in the State Window) despite being connected correctly to the MOST ring, it is possible that your MOST ring has entered sleep mode following a shutdown.

In the MOST State Window, you can wake-up the MOST ring by selecting the Wakeup command in the context menu or by double-clicking the light symbol.

|  | Note The MOST systems of some OEMs can be woken only by selected control units. If this is the case, the corresponding device must be operated. Other systems cannot be woken at all via MOST; in this case, additional signals must be applied to control units, e.g., via CAN. If necessary, the MOST system must be kept constantly awake by suitable measures, e.g., terminal 15. |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [MOST/MOSTIsochronousChannels] MOSTIsochronousChannelOverview


# Isochronous Channels

MOST » Isochronous Channels

One of the main use cases for a MOST150 System is the broadcasting of video data between devices such as a DVB-T tuner, a DSP or a Display. For this purpose the concept of isochronous channels was introduced.

## Properties and Use Cases of Isochronous Channels

- Streams with varying bandwidth requirements

- granted maximal bandwidth

- Broadcasting:

- encoded video data

- TV channels

- maps of navigation systems

Figure 1: Overview of access to isochronous Channels

Streaming of Isochronous Channels

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [MOST/MOSTIsochronousChannels] MOSTIsochronousChannelStreaming


# Isochronous Channel Streaming

MOST » Isochronous Channel Streaming

Figure 1: Block diagram streaming of the isochronous channels

## Features of the Streaming via USB

- 1:1 streaming of isochronous data on the hard disk drive or to an IP Port

- direct recording of TS files

- online statistics and metadata of the stream

- the isochronous data is transmitted unchanged from the MOST ring to the computer

- efficient implementation on driver level for low CPU pollution

## Use Cases

- recording of video TS stream for offline analysis

- online analysis of isochronous data

- online streaming to a media player over the network

There are two universal streaming DLLs provided with your CANoe installation which can be used to record or replay isochronous data. You find additional information in the document MOST_SyncStreaming_Documentation.pdf.

Isonchronous Channel: Overview

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [MOST/MOSTSynchronousChannels] MOSTSynchronousChannelAnalogAudio


# Access to Analog Audio Channels (Line In/ Headphone Out)

MOST » Access to Analog Audio Channels (Line In/ Headphone Out)

Analog audio data can be fed into and out of CANoe. With MOST, the term "routing" is commonly used to describe the transfer of audio data into and out of a MOST network. External audio devices can be connected via the MOST Line In/Out interface (e.g. VN2610 or VN2640). Stereo audio data are transmitted into and out of the MOST network at 4 bytes each, whereby each audio channel uses 2 bytes:

| Left audio channel | Right audio channel |
| --- | --- |
| MSB | LSB | MSB | LSB |

The routing can be done interactively via the MOST Audio Routing Window. The procedure is explained for each use case.

## Alternatively the routing can be done in CAPL.

Therefore the functions MostSetSyncAudio(), MostSetSyncMute() and MostSetSyncVolume() are available.

## MOST25 with VN2610

In CAPL, you have to specify individual synchronous channels for routing in the MostSetSyncAudio() function. The following applies in this case:

|  | Example Routing audio data from synchronous channels 0x04..0x07 to Line Out. long syncChannels[4]; syncChannels[0] = 0x04; // MSB left audio channel syncChannels[1] = 0x05; // LSB left audio channel syncChannels[2] = 0x06; // MSB right audio channel syncChannels[3] = 0x07; // LSB right audio channel // activate routing of Line Out MostSetSyncAudio(mostGetChannel(), syncChannels, 1, 1); The mapping of the individual bytes (MSB left, LSB left,

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [MOST/MOSTSynchronousChannels] MOSTSynchronousChannelDigitalAudio


# Access to Digital Audio Channels (S/PDIF In and Out)

MOST » Access to Digital Audio Channels (S/PDIF In and Out)

With CANoe and the VN2610 or VN2640 digital audio data can be fed in and out. With MOST, the term "routing" is commonly used to describe the transfer of audio data into and out of a MOST network. External S/PDIF capable devices can be connected to the VN2610's or VN2640's S/PDIF interface. S/PDIF data are transmitted into and out of the MOST network via the VN2610 or VN2640 at 8 bytes each, whereby 4 bytes are used per audio channel:

| Left audio channel | Right audio channel |
| --- | --- |
| MSB | LSB | AUX | VUCP | MSB | LSB | AUX | VUCP |

CAPL offers the following functions for routing digital audio data: MostSetSyncSpdif(), MostSetSyncSpdifLock() and MostSetSyncSpdifMode().

## MOST25 with VN2610

In CAPL, you have to specify individual synchronous channels for routing in the MostSetSyncSpdif() function. The following applies in this case:

|  | Example Routing S/PDIF data from synchronous channels 0x04..0x0B to S/PDIF Out. long syncChannels[8]; syncChannels[0] = 0x04; // MSB left audio channel syncChannels[1] = 0x05; // LSB left audio channel syncChannels[2] = 0x06; // AUX left audio channel syncChannels[3] = 0x07; // VUCP left audio channel syncChannels[4] = 0x08; // MSB right audio channel syncChannels[5] = 0x09; // LSB right audio channel syncChannels[6] = 0x0A; // AUX right audio channel syncChannels[7] = 0x0B; // VUCP right audio channel // activa

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [MOST/MOSTSynchronousChannels] MOSTSynchronousChannelOverview


# Synchronous Channels

MOST » Synchronous Channels

One of the main use cases for a MOST system is transferring audio data between different ECUs like audio disc player, digital signal processing units and amplifier. Thus the major part of the bandwidth in a MOST ring is reserved for the synchronous data area which is divided into several synchronous channels carrying e.g. audio streams.

There are several ways to access the synchronous data area withCANoe and the MOST interface (see fig. 1):

- LineIn/Headphone out: Access to analog audio channels (MOST25: VN2610, MOST150: VN2640)

- S/PDIF In and Out: Access to digital audio channels (MOST25: VN2610, MOST150: VN2640)

- CANoe via USB 2.0: Access to all synchronous channels at the same time (MOST25: VN2610, MOST150: VN2640)

Figure 1: Overview of access to synchronous channels.

Streaming of Synchronous Channels | Line In/ Headphone Out | S/PDIF In and Out

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [MOST/MOSTSynchronousChannels] MOSTSynchronousChannelStreaming


# Synchronous Channel Streaming

MOST » Synchronous Channel Streaming

With CANoe and the VN2610 for MOST25 or VN2640 for MOST150 it is possible to access all synchronous channels in parallel via USB 2.0.

Figure 1: Block diagram of the synchronous streaming

## Features of the Streaming via USB

- Streaming can be done in both directions from and to the MOST ring simultaneously

- MOST25: The synchronous streaming via USB allows access to all synchronous channels in parallel. Depending on the configuration of the synchronous boundary control (SBC) this means up to 60 channels.

- MOST150: The access to the streaming data is always achieved by connection labels. Up to 8 labels with at maximum 152 Bytes per frame can be streamed from the ring to the computer.

- The data of the synchronous area of MOST is transmitted without any modification to the computer and vice versa.

- Optionally, time stamps and status information can be added to the streaming data, when streaming from the MOST ring to the computer.

- Latency times and buffer sizes can be customized for each use case.

## Use Cases

- Recording and replaying of synchronous data from and to the MOST ring.

- Online analysis of the synchronous data to detect test signals, to determine the activity status on certain channels (mute detection) or to determine the delay between commands on the control channel and actions on the synchronous channels.

- CODEC algorithms can be integrated in the streaming framework for both 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [MOST/MOST_Hardware] MOSTHardwareConfiguration


# MOST Hardware Configuration

MOST Network Hardware » MOST

Ribbon: Hardware Tab|Network Hardware|MOST

The Network Hardware Configuration dialog contains three areas for each activated MOST channel:

## MOST

### HW Type

Select the desired hardware type and speed grade (MOST50 or MOST150) for each MOST channel.

### Additional Services

Here you can set which services should be activated in CANoe:

- None

CANoe behaves entirely passive; all bus accesses must be performed by the user or be initiated by simulation nodes or Replay Blocks.

- AMS

The AMS Service is activated; fragmented transmissions may be performed, and when they are received the individual control messages of a segmented transmission are reassembled into an AMS message. CANoe continues to remain essentially passive; only segmented transmission errors are communicated to the sending node per the MOST specification.

AMS must also be activated if the AMS Combiner should be active in offline mode or when importing logging files into a Trace Window.

- AMS+AS

Besides AMS the Application Socket (AS) is also activated. Depending on the node mode that is set for the specific channel, Master or Slave, CANoe is now active in the sense of NetServices.As a Master node the integrated MOST NetworkMaster polls existing function blocks of the connected devices and propagates the configuration status.Configured as a Slave the CANoe Application Socket answers requests from a MOST NetworkMaster via the integrated NetBloc

[...truncated for brevity...]

The OptoLyzer G2 is addressed on the following Ethernet ports. A potentially installed firewall must not block these ports. Spy: 27998 Node: 27999 The computer automatically assigns the ports. However, if specific ports are used the file CAN.INI has to be adapted. If necessary please remove this software. |

If you want to synchronize the OptoLyzer time stamps of the spy channel with the time stamps of any Vector hardware interfaces that are connected in parallel, you will need a sync line. To do this, use the SyncCable to connect the OptoLyzer's Trigger Extension Box and the sync input of the Vector hardware (in the case of CANcardXL, to a SyncBox XL).
