# NM Configuration

AUTOSAR NM Observer » Configuration

Ribbon: Simulation Tab|Simulation Setup|Network Management

In this dialog, you can activate the AUTOSAR NM Observer and make settings for it. The AUTOSAR NM Observer is only available for CAN buses. It is deactivated by default.

## Networks

On the left side of the dialog, you see an overview of the networks integrated in the configuration for which the AUTOSAR NM Observer can be activated.

## Settings CAN1/AUTOSAR NM

### Enable AUTOSAR NM Observer

You can use this option to activate or deactivate the AUTOSAR NM Observer on the network. To do so, select AUTOSAR NM under the desired network and select the check box.

### Configured Nodes

If you have activated the AUTOSAR NM Observer for a network, you can select the nodes to be monitored here. The nodes contained in the database are displayed in the list.

### [Open Main INI File] / [Open Local INI file]

The AUTOSAR NM Observer obtains its settings from the same INI files that are used for AUTOSAR NM simulation (see the Technical Reference: AUTOSAR NM CAN 3.3.0).

You can use the buttons to open the INI files in a text editor. The buttons are only active if a corresponding file is available.

|  | Note In order for the changes to be applied to the INI files, the configuration must be reloaded. |

### Create and configure State Tracker on close

If you have selected this option, a new State Tracker containing all relevant system variables of the activated AUTOSAR

### [Ribbon/StimulationSimulation] SecurityConfiguration


# Security Configuration

CANoe » Security Configuration

Ribbon: Simulation Tab|Security Configuration

In this dialog, you can define which Security Profiles shall be used by CANoe. There are different security use cases like Secured Onboard Communication (SecOC), Diagnostics, Backend Access, TLS and TLS observation and IPSec. Depending on the security use case, you have to assign a Security Profile to networks, stacks or the TLS Observer.

You can create and manage Security Profiles in the Vector Security Manager. Only the ID of the profile is stored in the CANoe configuration. At no time the data of the profile is exported to CANoe.

## Search in Security Profile

In all Security Profile drop down lists, you can search for Security Profiles by selecting the drop down list and entering a search text. All profiles including the entered string are shown in the profile list. All profiles that are not relevant will be hidden.

## Toolbar

- Remove Security Profile Use this button to remove the selected Security Profile assignment.

- Refresh List of Security Profiles Synchronizes the list of available Security Profiles with the Vector Security Manager.

- Open Security Manager Use this button to open the Vector Security Manager.The Security Profiles are managed and configured in this tool.

## SecOC and Diagnostics

In this tab, you can assign Security Profiles to networks of the simulation setup. The Security Profile is used for SecOC, Diagnostics and Backend Access.

The li

### [Ribbon/Window] ActivateMDIWindows


# More Windows

Ribbon » Window » Activate MDI Windows

Ribbon: Layout Tab|Activate Window|More Windows

You can use the Windows dialog to activate an MDI Window.

All opened MDI Windows are shown in the dialog. Within this list, the windows can be sorted in ascending or descending order by name using .You can also input a window name in order to navigate to a window.

Only one window can be selected.

## Keyboard Operations

| Function Description | Key Combination |
| --- | --- |
| Scroll one line down | <↓> |
| Scroll one line up | <↑> |
| Scroll one page down | <Page ↓> |
| Scroll one page up | <Page ↑> |
| Scroll to the top of the dialog | <Pos 1> |
| Scroll to the bottom of the dialog | <End> |

## Acceptance of the Window Selection

- Double-clicking a window in the list activates this window and closes the dialog.

- After activating/deactivating, you must close the dialog with [OK] so that the selected windows will be opened or closed.

- [Cancel] closes the dialog without accepting your window selection.

### [RTSetup] RTSetupLinux


# RT Setup with Linux

RT Setup » RT Setup with Linux

## Overview

From CANoe 13.0 the RT kernel (i.e. the real time part) is also available in a Linux variant that can be used for the operation with CANoe4SW Server Edition.

| Subject | Linux RT Kernel |
| --- | --- |
| Operation | Execution of CANoe4SW Server Edition configurations Only for x64 architectures compatible with Intel |
| Availability | The Linux variant is available for the following distributions: Ubuntu 18.04 CentOS 7.5 |
| Use case | Can be easily automated in combination with CANoe4Server via a command line interface For applications that require a lot of main memory |

## Installation

Linux RT is currently only provided on request.

If you have any questions, please contact the Vector Support.

## Supported Features

Currently the following selection of features is available:

- Only simulated mode is supported (real hardware is not accessible)

- Bussystems: CAN and Ethernet buses are supported

- CAPL programs with a limited set of functions

- System variables are accessible

## Binary extensions

Also under Linux it is possible to integrate your own developed extensions. However, these must be compiled as SOs (Shared Objects) and integrated via module description files.

If you have any questions, please contact the Vector Support.

## Integration of modeling libraries

Please note that if you integrate modeling libraries, the Linux variant must be referenced via a module description file (*.vmodule

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [RTSetup] RTSetupOverview


# RT Setup

Setup and Extensions » RT Setup

The tool is divided into two components for decoupling the real-time operation, the real-time area and the analysis area. The RT kernel is the execution environment for the real-time part of the configuration. RT server is the role of the device running the RT kernel in distributed mode.

The term RT hardware covers the devices

- RT computer

- RT Rack

- VN8900

- VT6000

The following operating modes are available for RT hardware:

## Standard operation without RT hardware

In standard operation without RT hardware, the real-time relevant parts are executed together with CANoe on the user computer.

## Interface Mode

In interface mode, the real-time relevant parts are not executed on the VN8900, but as in normal mode together with CANoe on the user computer.

## Distributed Mode

CANoe offers the possibility to execute the real-time relevant simulation parts on a separate computer (RT server), i.e. separate from the graphical user interface (user computer).

## Standalone Mode

In standalone mode you can execute the real time specific portions of a configuration on an external device (e.g. VN8900) without a connection to the user computer.

Multi CANoe Mode

Multi CANoe allows several computers with CANoe to be connected and synchronized.

| RT Mode / Device | Standard operation without RT hardware | Interface Mode | Distributed Mode | Standalone Mode |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| VN1600 | ✔ | — | — | — |
| VN4610 | ✔ | 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [RTSetup/DistributedMode] DistributedModeConcept


# Distributed Mode

RT Setup » Distributed ModeVN8900 » Distributed Mode

| Introduction |
| --- |
| CANoe offers the opportunity to execute the real-time relevant simulation parts on an individual computer (RT server) that is separated from the graphical user interface (user computer in distributed mode). Thus on the one hand, the total performance of the system can be expanded more easily if necessary; on the other hand, influences above all of the graphic system on latency times and timer precisions are prevented. The configuration of the simulation and the evaluation then occur on the usual workstation computer, for example, while the simulation runs on an individual computer/ device (distributed mode). |
| Architecture |
| In distributed mode, CANoe is divided into two components that are executed on different computers / devices in order to uncouple of the real-time operation. Real-time part on RT server The RT server is executed on an individual computer/device with the Microsoft Windows operating system. Different types of devices can be used as RT servers: VN8900 (but not VN8910/VN8910A) VT6000 RT Rack Standard computer (after the corresponding installation) In addition to the CANoe execution environment, the Vector Tool Platform (VTP) must be installed on the RT server. This allows access to the RT server in CANoe or in the Vector Platform Manager, e.g. to update the execution environment of CANoe on the RT server or to change device settings (e.g. network settings

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [RTSetup/DistributedMode] DistributedModeConfigRequirements


# Configuration Requirements

Distributed Mode » Configuration Requirements

When CANoe is used in a distributed environment with decoupled real time operations (e.g. on a VN8900), the same models and configurations are used that are normally used for execution on a computer, and consequently the same program properties are generally available.

There are, however, a few restrictions that apply when a configuration is used in distributed mode:

- Synchronization of program files

|  | Note If you want to use a C library or a CAPL DLL in distributed mode on a RT server (e.g. with a VN8900 or an RT Rack, please mind that the DLL doesn't contain dependencies to other DLLs that are not part of the Windows Embedded Images on the device. Particularly debug versions cannot be executed if they contain dependencies to dynamic debug libraries. |

- CANoe automatically transfers modeling libraries (DLL files incl. the corresponding INI files), CAPL DLLs, C-Libraries and .NET assemblies to the RT server prior to the start of the measurement.

- If DLLs are changed during an active connection – e.g. due to re-compiling a MATLAB model – the updated DLLs are automatically transferred on measurement start.

- Generally you do not need to add or delete files manually on the RT server.

- CAPL access to user files

- Files that should be read by a CAPL program in the Simulation Setup or in the Test Setup for Test Modules need to be transferred from the user computer to the VN8900 or the RT co

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [RTSetup/DistributedMode] DistributedModeConfigurationOperation


# Configuration and Operation

Distributed Mode » Configuration and Operation

The operation of the distributed mode is very similar to the standard operation.

## Preparation:

- Both computers used must be set up appropriately. The RT server must be a device with an installed Vector Tool Platform. On devices shipped by Vector (VN8900, VT6000, RT Rack) the Vector Tool Platform is either already pre-installed or can be installed in the context of a driver update. Alternatively, you can also operate any computer as an RT server. Please refer to the instructions for installing the Vector Tool Platform on a standard computer.

- In addition, make sure that the configuration satisfies the currently existing limitations.

## Configuration of the RT server

Start CANoe on the user computer. The configuration of a device/computer as RT server now takes place in the dialog under Home ribbon tab|Group Measurement|Vector Tool Platform. First select the device on the Connection page. Then select on the page CANoe/CANalyzer|Distributed Mode that this device should be used as RT server.

## Configuration of the hardware channels

In CANoe, the channels and the hardware are set and programmed on the usual dialogs. However, the [Driver] button on the hardware dialog leads to the driver settings of the local computer (user computer) and not to the settings of the RT server (where in the case of VN8900 devices connected via USB, local settings may be synchronized to the device). The channel 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [RTSetup/DistributedMode] DistributedModeHardwareConfiguration


# Distributed Mode: Hint for Hardware Configuration

Distributed Mode » Hint Vector Hardware Configuration for Distributed Mode

Changes on the local Vector Driver Applet for local Vector Hardware affect only the user computer and not the RT server. If you like to change Vector hardware configuration of the RT server, you have to change them on the RT server itself.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [RTSetup/DistributedMode] DistributedModeTroubleshooting


# Distributed Mode: Troubleshooting

Distributed Mode » Troubleshooting

|  | Establishing a Connection The following things can cause a failure to connect to the configured RT server ( e.g. VN8900 , VT6000, RT Rack): The device/computer is not turned on or connected to the LAN or via USB. The device/computer is switched off or not connected to a USB port of the user computer or to the LAN. The simulation environment version on the RT server does not correspond to the CANoe version on the local computer.A software update can solve this problem. The simulation environment on the RT server is running in standalone mode. The simulation environment on the device is currently in use by another process (e.g. another instance of CANoe). The RT kernel on the device is currently not running. You can start the RT kernel via Home ribbon tab|Group Measurement|Vector Tool Platform|Tool Platform|Applications. A general connection error has occurred.A possible reason for this is that the device was detached from the computer or switched off during the attempt to establish a connection to it.This problem may also arise if there is no suitable or up-to-date driver on the RT server.A driver update can solve this problem. The simulation environment on the RT server is not running.To solve this problem switch off the RT server and switch it on, again. If the problem isn't solved update the CANoe software on the device (see software update). Is the problem still isn't solved please contact our s

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [RTSetup/InterfaceMode] InterfaceModeConcept


# Interface Mode

RT Setup » Interface ModeVN8900 » Interface Mode

| Introduction |
| --- |
| In interface mode, the real-time relevant parts are not executed on the VN8900 but together with CANoe on the user computer. The VN8900 is used like a standard network interface (see VN1640). In this respect, the interface mode corresponds to the standard operation of CANoe on a single computer, but in the context of VN8900 devices, which can also be operated in distributed mode or standalone mode, it is rather the exception. |
| Architecture |
| Real-time Area The real-time area is located on the user computer. Actions such as control and test execution, real-time simulation and model execution, measurement, analysis, logging, bypassing are performed on the user's computer. The details correspond to the standard operation. Analysis Area The analysis area is located on the user computer. The details correspond to the standard operation. |
| Schema | Use Cases |
|  | The interface mode can be useful in the following use cases: The properties of the CANoe configuration do not allow operation in distributed mode (see Requirements for Configurations ). This is especially true in the case of dependencies to operating system components or other applications that are not available in the embedded image of the VN8900. You have a device of type VN8910 or VN8910A. Since CANoe 11.0 this device is only supported in interface mode. In addition to the VN8900, you want to connect further network 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [RTSetup/InterfaceMode] InterfaceModeConfiguration


# Configuration and Operation

Interface Mode » Configuration and Operation

A VN8900 device is used in interface mode when the following requirements are met:

- You have assigned application channels that are used in the CANoe configuration to hardware channels of the VN8900. The channel assignment for the interface mode is set in the Vector Hardware Configuration on the computer. The channel assignment on the device, which you can change under Home ribbon tab|Group Measurement|Vector Tool Platform|Tool Platform|Channel Mapping page, has no effect in interface mode.

- CANoe is not configured for operation in distributed mode, i.e. no RT server is configured under Home ribbon tab|Group Measurement|Vector Tool Platform|CANoe/CANalyzer|Distributed Mode.

## Notes

- The channel assignment for the interface mode is set in the Vector Hardware Configuration on the computer. The channel assignment on the device, which you can change under Home ribbon tab|Group Measurement|Vector Tool Platform|Tool Platform|Channel Mapping page, has no effect in interface mode.

- Since CANoe 12.0, an assignment of all channels used in the CANoe configuration to a VN8900 no longer causes it to be used automatically in distributed mode.

In order to prevent unintentional use in interface mode (with the above-mentioned effects, e.g. with regard to real-time characteristics), CANoe performs a check at various points before a VN8900 connected via USB is used in interface mode, which could also be ope

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [RTSetup/StandaloneMode] StandaloneModeConcept


# Standalone Mode

RT Setup » Standalone ModeVN8900 » Standalone Mode

| Introduction |
| --- |
| In standalone mode you can execute the real time specific portions of a configuration on an external device (e.g. VN8900) without a connection to the user computer. |
| Architecture |
| Real-time Area In standalone mode, you can operate an external device without your user computer. You can load the simulation and test setup onto the device and execute it there. You can set up the device so that measurement begins as soon as it is switched on. You can also interact using a keypad on the device (e.g. to stop and start measurement). As an alternative you can export the real time specific part of a configuration in a standalone configuration file (RTCFG). You can load it later on the selected or an identical device using CANoe or the Standalone Manager. Analysis Area Evaluation-specific portions of the configuration (in particular, any blocks defined in the Measurement Setup) are not included in measurements carried out in standalone mode. You can log data and analyze it later. |
| Schema | Use Cases |
|  | Only one standalone license is available. The user interface of CANoe is not available. Example: Use of the interface during a test drive Test bench use case (automated (HiL) tests without user interaction) |
| Further Topics |
| Configuration Requirements Configuration and Operation Error Messages Using I/O Hardware Vector Platform Manager |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik Gmb

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [RTSetup/StandaloneMode] StandaloneModeConfigRequirements


# Configuration Requirements

Standalone Mode » Configuration Requirements

Configurations that have their real time specific portions loaded onto a VN8900 or an RT server for execution in standalone mode are subject to the following limitations.

The following functions are supported:

- Simulation nodes and test modules in the Simulation Setup and in Test Environments for Test Modules.as well as Test Units.

- Diagnostic functions and XCP functions.

- Configurations that use CAN, LIN, MOST, FlexRay, K-Line or Ethernet channels.

- Configurations using IOpiggy.

- The use of modeling libraries, CAPL DLLs and C libraries.

- The execution of Visual Sequencer programs.

- The Replay Block. You can use a source file in BLF format.

Please note the following limitations:

- The use of simulated bus mode while in Standalone Mode doesn't make much sense and is therefore not supported.In standalone mode only execution in online mode is supported.

- You cannot download a configuration where files with the same name are loaded from different folders.Workaround: rename the file(s) to make the file names unique, and change the configuration to use the new file names.

If the configuration contains a function that is not supported, a corresponding error message is displayed during the download/export. Some of the functions that are not supported are automatically deactivated when the standalone configuration is created. In this case only a warning appears in the Write Window on downl

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [RTSetup/StandaloneMode] StandaloneModeConfiguration


# Configuration and Operation

Standalone Mode » Configuration and Operation

You can access the configuration and control for standalone mode in CANoe via Home ribbon tab|Group Measurement|Vector Tool Platform|CANoe/CANalyzer|Standalone Mode.

CANoe is shipped with accessories called Standalone Manager and Vector Platform Manager that you can also install on any other computer. You can use it to control standalone mode on any VN8900 devices connected to that computer.

|  | Note An installation package for the Standalone Manager can be found in the CANoe installation directory in the subfolder Installer Additional Components\StandaloneUtilities and as a download package on the Vector website. The Vector Platform Manager is delivered as part of a Vector driver package (available for download on the Vector website). |

To set up standalone mode, please take the following steps:

|  | Configuration of the Standalone Mode Check whether the configuration currently opened in CANoe is suitable for standalone mode (see Configuration Requirements). Open the Vector Tool Platform. Select the device to be configured (see Device Selection). Download the configuration to the device (see Device Configuration). Activate standalone mode on the device (see Device State). Start the standalone measurement (see Device State). |

|  | Note For information about errors, see Error Messages. |

As an alternative you can export the real time specific part of a configuration in a standalone configura

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [RTSetup/StandaloneMode] StandaloneModeErrorMessages


# Error Messages

Standalone-Mode » Error Messages

The following error messages can occur while using standalone mode:

## Downloading the Standalone Configuration

- The configuration uses functions that are not supported in standalone mode. To set up standalone mode, deactivate the specified functions in your configuration, if possible (see also Configuration Requirements).

- An error occurred during compilation. As is the case with a normal measurement start, the programs used in the Simulation Setup are compiled, if necessary, prior to download. Check the specified program and correct the error.

- Hardware and application channel check has failed. Check whether the network hardware on the device is connected and the application channels are assigned correctly. Also take note of the output in the Write Window.

- A suitable license is not available on the device. Check the license installed on the device.

- An error occurred while saving the configuration to the device. The device may not have sufficient disk space available for the configuration.

- An error occurred while reading files appended to the configuration. When a standalone configuration is downloaded or exported, the following are provided with the generated standalone configuration file:

- CAPL DLLs,

- Modeling libraries (DLL files incl. associated INI files),

- User files configured for access in CAPL

- diagnostic descriptions

An error occurred while reading one of these files from its original mem

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [RTSetup/StandaloneMode] StandaloneModeUseOfIOHardware


# Using I/O Hardware

Standalone Mode » Using I/O Hardware

Starting with CANoe 7.6 SP3, I/O hardware can be used in standalone mode. Vector hardware that has been activated via the I/O Configuration dialog can thus be used without any configuration. For hardware that is addressed via Portlink, you must still copy the associated configuration files (pl_x.INI) to the standalone platform. You must save these files there in the directory of the application data.

In order for a configuration with I/O hardware to be performed correctly in standalone mode, you must connect the required hardware to the standalone platform. In addition, you must install the required drivers and configure the I/O hardware there.

Before you perform a configuration in standalone mode, you should start the configuration in distributed mode. In this way, you can more easily trace and eliminate possible errors with the use of I/O hardware. Should errors occur in standalone mode, additional information can found in the Notifications page of the Standalone dialog of the user computer, if necessary.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |

### [SCOPE] ScopeAnalysis


# Analysis of Measurement Values

Scope » Analysis

You can use the Diagram and the Trace View to analyze your data.

- The Diagram displays the measurement values and logical interpretation graphically.

- The Trace View lists all the occurring bus events along with their protocol components.

- The global windows synchronization of CANoe works unidirectional from Scope-Trace to the CANoe Trace Window.

- The two views are synchronized.

- The most recently captured data is automatically displayed in both views.

- You can switch this automatic display off in the Diagram configuration.

- You can also select other datasets for analysis on the Measurements (Scope) page.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [SCOPE] ScopeBitMaskDefinition


# Bitmask Definition

Scope » Layout » Eye Diagram » Bitmask Definition

Click on (selection via ) of the Eye Diagram's toolbar in the Scope Window to open the Bitmask Definition dialog.

In addition to the eye diagram you can also analyze the eye diagram. To do this, a bitmask must be defined.

## Appearance

The appearance of the bitmask can be changed using four parameters. Changes to a data point (tuple) are visible with immediate effect in an eye diagram, which has been created before.

- Line width/Line colorSpecify the line width and a line color for the bitmask.

- Symbol/Symbol colorSpecify a symbol and a color for the data points (tuples) of the bitmask.

- Alpha-Blending/Background colorSpecify the Alpha-Blending for the background color.

## Data Point Definition

A bitmask can be defined using 6 data points (tuples) for CAN bus systems and 10 tuples for FlexRay systems.

To add more data points you can edit the XML import file and then import it via the [Import] button.

A tuple is defined as {nominal bit time[%]; voltage[mV]}. Changes to a tuple are visible with immediate effect in an eye diagram, which has been created before.

- Time [%] The nominal bit time (NBT) always depends on the current baud rate for the respective measurements.Valid values: 0 ... 100 %

- Voltage [mV] The voltage which must not be fall down or must not be exceeded during nominal bit time.

|  | Example: Bit mask with green lines and red data points (tuples) |

## Import / Export

All

### [SCOPE] ScopeCANstressTrigger


# Triggering with CANstress

Scope » Triggering with CANstress

Devices and Trigges Page|Scope Device|Context Menu|Add Trigger Condition|CANstress – External Trigger

## Preconditions

You need a T connector cable to connect the Trigger Out channel of the CANstress device to the Sync channel of the Vector hardware interface and to the Ext. channel of the scope device.

## CANstress Trigger Conditions

You can choose among the following trigger conditions to work with Vector's CANstress hardware device:

- SOF

- EOF

- Bit field (e.g. ID field)

- Error Frame

- Special bit sequences for disturbance

|  | Cross Reference The CANstress hardware documentation and the CANstress Online Help provide detailed information. |

## Activate CANstress Trigger Conditions in CANoe

You have to define a special I/O trigger condition for CANstress hardware on the Devices and Triggers (Scope) page of the Scope Window. This trigger condition activates all hardware trigger conditions (see above) defined with CANstress; see page I/O Trigger for details.

When a CANoe measurement is running you have to activate the configured trigger condition by pressing the Activate trigger button . If you want to trigger repetitive times you should switch to Repeat in the toolbar of the Scope Window.

|  | Note If you've activated the CANstress I/O trigger, you have to deactivate or remove all other triggers before you can start a scope measurement. |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |

### [SCOPE] ScopeConfigurationColors


# Colors

Scope » Configuration » Colors

Diagram|Context Menu|Configuration

Here, you configure the colors for the diagram. For Signals and Messages, a separate page is available containing different functions in some cases.

With the [Set Defaults] button, you reset the settings of the dialog to the default settings.

|  | Notes For the signals, the Use custom colors default check box is available. Check box is activated: User defined colors are saved in the CAN.INI independent of the configuration. The Set Defaults button causes a reset to the colors saved by the user in the CAN.INI. Check box is deactivated: User defined colors are saved together with the configuration in the persistence. The Set Defaults button resets everything to the settings of the persistence that is dependent on the configuration. |

Only when you confirm the dialog with [OK] will the settings be applied to the diagram.

## Signals

### Line Color

Click with the left mouse button to open the selection list for the color of the respective signal.

Click with the right mouse button to open the context menu for the respective signal.

- With the Load custom default color context menu command, you set the color that you saved with the above-mentioned context menu command.

### [SCOPE] ScopeConfigurationDevices


# Devices

Scope » Configuration » Devices

Diagram|Context Menu|Configuration

## Device List

The device list contains the assignment of device names (e.g. Scope_0), device types and the serial numbers.

The serial number is only shown during an established connection. When configuring several Scopes, you have to check (and adjust) the serial numbers manually.

The assignments of the devices (Scope_ID to serial number) is done via the Serial selection menu.

With [Ping] you can force the LED of particular oscilloscopes to flash to verify the device assignment.

### [SCOPE] ScopeConfigurationDiagram


# Diagram

Scope » Configuration » Diagram

Diagram|Context Menu|Configuration

## Diagram Settings

Use Show logical interpretation as cursor tooltip to activate/deactivate the display of logical data at the cursor.

Use Insert multiple signals with an optimal Y-offset to specify whether you want to display multiple signals on a common Y-axis or layered over one another.

Use Show grid to turn the grid on or off.When the grid is visible, you can also configure the Y-Raster.

You can set the Fitting type to All (default) or to Triggered event. When selecting the Triggered event, only the bus event that caused the trigger condition will be displayed.

## Time Axis

You can set the X-axis unit to Auto, Microseconds, Milliseconds, Seconds or Minutes.If you choose Auto, the unit is automatically adapted to the unit value of the time stamp at runtime.

If you activate Smart label, large time stamps are replaced by an offset.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [SCOPE] ScopeConfigurationGeneral


# General

Scope » Configuration » General

Diagram|Context Menu|Configuration

## Trigger Activation

Use the Activate trigger on measurement start option to activate/deactivate automatic start of the Scope trigger at the start of CANoe measurement.

## Captured Data

Use User-defined buffer size to specify a buffer size for the data you acquire. When the configured buffer size is reached, you have to delete one or more datasets before you can resume data acquisition.If you do not activate this option, the buffer size is set automatically in line with the amount of memory currently available.

Use Ignore Scope channels without trigger to specify whether or not to discard data from Scope channels that do not have trigger events.This option is helpful if you want to minimize the required memory.

Use Clear on measurement start to specify whether all data captured during previous CANoe measurement (i.e. the contents of the Measurements (Scope) page) should be deleted.To avoid losing data that you still need, you can export it into a file on your hard disk (see Measurements (Scope) page).

Use Automatically display the latest data in diagram to specify whether the most recently captured data should be output automatically in the diagram.If you do not activate this option, the contents of the diagram are not updated when data is acquired.

Use Ring buffer mode to specify whether or not to apply the FIFO (First In First Out) based dataset management. The FIFO based dataset manage

Diagram|Context Menu|Configuration

This is where you configure the diagram signals.

- When loading FlexRay data, FR_BP and FR_BM signals can be optionally deactivated.

Here you can select how to display the Difference Signal in the diagram. Selecting the other option, the signal is shown inverted.

You can return to the default settings by clicking [Set Defaults].

### [SCOPE] ScopeDiagram


# Diagram

Scope » Layout » Diagram

## General

The diagram shows the following information:

- Signal curves

- The chart control has a multi pane concept. Analog and digital signals are shown in separate panes (see Scope Configuration dialog).

- Highlight information based on chronological values as displayed in the Trace View.

- The ground bar with corresponding ground levels.

- The time axis

- You can set the time unit and the display of time values in the Scope Configuration dialog.

- The grid

- Y grid (in mV) is configurable,

- X grid is implicitly changed depending on the X zoom factor.

- Trigger time-stamp information regarding

- trigger request (trigger condition has been recognized)

- trigger acknowledge (trigger pulse has been created)

|  | Note The difference between these two time stamps depends on the trigger settings. Choose Pre-Trigger, if you don't want to trigger during the pre-trigger stage. With this setting no trigger condition will be evaluated during pre-trigger time. The trigger acknowledge follows immediately after the trigger request. The setting of Min. Post-Trigger enables evaluating the trigger condition within the pre-trigger stage. Here the time difference between the two trigger time stamp values may add up to the pre-trigger stage length.Triggering within the pre-trigger stage is currently supported by the Scope hardware type 4227. |

## Analysis the Diagram

### [SCOPE] ScopeDigitalTrigger


# Digital Trigger

Scope » Triggers » Digital Trigger

Devices and Triggers Page|Context Menu|Add Trigger Condition|Digital Trigger…

## Requirements

- The MSO-Trigger can be used with all scopes of PicoScope 6000 series. For example the device PS6403E-034 provided by Vector.

- A special trigger cable (Y-cable, same for I/O Trigger) is required. For details contact Vector Support.The cable connections are presented in the Scope manual.

## Adding Digital Trigger

If a corresponding scope device is integrated and configured on the Devices and Triggers (Scope) page, a digital trigger condition can be added using the context menu of the scope device.

## Configuring a Digital Trigger

In the Digital Channel and Trigger Condition Configuration dialog you can configure digital ports and setup trigger conditions on each digital channel contributed to a port.

## Ports Configuration

The digital port configuration is equal to Hardware Configuration for scopes.

## Digital Trigger Configuration

Here you configure all digital channel capabilities for a selected port. (Port 0 or Port1). The port settings Threshold and Hysteresis are common for each digital channel. The entire configuration of a digital port is a complex trigger condition where channels D0 to D7 are logically ORed to each other.

### Channel

Fix logical channel names (D0…D7).

### Label

User defined description.

### Enable Trigger

Activates or deactivate trigger

### Trigger Condition

Trigger on levels (High (1

### [SCOPE] ScopeEyeDiagram


# Eye Diagram

Scope » Layout » Eye Diagram

## Analysis Requirements

- Supported bus systems:

- CAN and CAN FD (starting with CANoe 9.0)

- FlexRay (starting with CANoe 9.0 SP2)

- LIN (starting with CANoe 9.0 SP4)

- Analysis of single frames that can be selected in the Trace view.

- Analysis of multiple frames filtered by frame type (CAN, CAN FD, FlexRay, LIN), application channel control unit (ECU) or bit fields.

- Analysis of data imports starting with CANoe version 8.5.

|  | Note When preparing an eye diagram, the number of sample points has to be observed. If this is chosen too low for a bit, a maximum deviation may occur in positive or negative direction of the sampling time. During the creation of the eye diagram, an edge slope measurement is carried out. This edge slope measurement cannot be done if there are not enough of sampling points. The legend of the Eye Diagram reveals whether the edge slope measurement has been successful. If the edge slope measurement has not been successful, no time will be displayed for the falling and rising edges. |

## Analysis Restrictions

In the following cases an analysis is not possible or faulty. In all these cases an error notification will be displayed.

- General

- Unsupported bus systems.

- Incomplete recorded frames.

- Old data imports from CANoe version < 8.5.

- Sampling rate of the scope is too low.

- Failed compensation of the time drift between a CAN hardware and the PicoScope.

- CAN

- Frames where the domi

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [SCOPE] ScopeFirstSteps


# First Steps

Scope » First Steps

|  | First Steps with Scope Please take note of the requirements set out in the manual (Doc\Scope_Manual_EN.PDF). Once the requirements for recording with Scope are met, you can set up the hardware connections; please refer to the manual Doc\Scope_Manual_EN.PDF. You can now open Scope via Analysis Ribbon Tab|Scope. In the Devices and Triggers (Scope) page, create a Scope hardware element for each piece of Scope hardware you are using. One Scope hardware element is already present by default. In the Scope Hardware Configuration dialog, configure the Scope hardware type and Scope channels mapping to correspond to the hardware connections. The data acquisition settings are generated automatically by default. In the status bar you can see the values currently in use. If the automatic values are not appropriate, you can enter your own acquisition settings. Click in the toolbar to create a connection to the Scope hardware and start the measurement. The Scope hardware is now live and can be triggered. Following successful triggering, the data you acquire is saved to the Measurements (Scope).page. You can now analyze the measurement values. |

|  | Note Hardware-based time synchronization is not possible with Scope hardware because the sync line is reserved for transmission of the trigger signal. |

# Hardware Configuration

In the Scope Hardware Configuration dialog you configure each single scope device, that is connected.

You can also open the dialog with a double click on a Scope device on the Devices and Triggers (Scope) page.

## Active Configuration

The user can add, delete, undo and redo custom settings for selected device. When choosing another device type for current setting the active configuration is transferred to selected device. For example, the channel mapping of a 4-channel scope is transferred to the first 4-channels of an 8-channel scope.

## Scope

## Device Type

Select the Scope hardware type from the list.

When using PicoScope devices of 5000 series you can adjust the voltage measurement range resolution.

|  | Note When using PicoScope 6403E-34 voltage resolution is 8-bit and not adjustable. In addition to the real Scope hardware you can you can use a virtual Scope in simulation mode and real mode. You can configure it like a real oscilloscope. Currently only the CAN bus system is supported. See also Bit Mask Analysis (CAN - Scope) sample configuration. |

## Voltage Resolution

The resolution of voltage measurement range can be adjusted. The following settings are available: 8 Bit and 12 Bit. If a higher resolution is used, the adjustment of minimum sampling interval will be restricted.

### [SCOPE] ScopeImportExport


# Data Import/Export

Scope » Import/Export

Measurement Page|Context Menu|Import/Export

You can import (multi-selection support) and export data via the context menu on the Measurements (Scope) page.

Available import formats for offline analysis:

- CSF

- CSFX Recommended export format since CANoe 9.0 SP4.The CSFX format increases the load/save performance and reduces the file size. In addition the measurement data can also be stored in a higher resolution which depends on configured voltage range resolution of the scope device. Note In CANoe versions older than 9.0 SP4 the CSF format must still be used.

Available export formats:

- CSF (binary format, exports the full measurement),

- MAT (Matlab format) and

- CSV (ASCII format)

Export of raw measurement data in MAT (MATLAB format) or CSV (ASCII format) is also possible in the Diagram.

You can make the following settings in the Trigger Condition Configuration dialog:

## Select Signal Source

Here you must select a logical signal source that has been assigned to a physical oscilloscope channel beforehand in the device configuration.

Logical signal sources are either assigned bus signals (e.g., CAN_H, CAN_L, LIN, FlexRay_BP, Flexray_BM) or originate from any external signal source.

External signals are assigned as logical I/O channels initially to an oscilloscope channel.Thus, trigger conditions can also be defined for I/O signals.

You can assign a maximum of 4 logical I/O channels to an oscilloscope with 4 physical channels.You can define only one trigger condition for each signal source. If no signal source is selected, the trigger condition is invalid.

### [SCOPE] ScopeMarker


# Marker

Scope » Marker

In the Scope Window, you can set markers for marking significant points of a measurement. Each marker has a name and a defined time stamp.

Using the context menu of the marker bar you can:

- set new markers (alternatively by a double click on the marker bar)

- edit markers

- navigate to markers

- delete markers

- Show and hide markers

## Features

- You can define a separate set of markers for each measurement.

- For the voltage characteristics of a measurement, you can define any number of markers.

- The markers are contained in the scope data export and can be produced again at any time.

- You can change the names of the markers. When you create a new marker, it receives a default name.

- You can position the markers anywhere in the diagram of the Scope Window with the mouse.

- You can provide any marker with a comment.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [SCOPE] ScopeMemoryManagement


# Memory Management

Scope » Memory Management

The amount of memory needed for captured data depends on

- the number of sample points determined by the combination of acquisition duration and sampling duration,

- the number of used BNC channels.

For more information about configuration options, please refer to the Scope Configuration dialog.

Please note that the data acquisition process can use a lot of memory.You can limit the amount of memory used for data acquisition in the Scope Configuration dialog. When an acquisition process reaches the memory limit, you cannot acquire any further data. To continue, you will first need to delete existing datasets.

### [SCOPE] ScopeMultiScopeOperation


# Multi-Scope-Operation

Scope » Multi-Scope-Operation

Effective with version 7.6 SP4 the Scope option supports Multi-Scope-Operation.

### Features

- Operation with up to four oscilloscopes supported.

- Different sampling rates per oscilloscope configurable.

- One data measurement log contains the acquisition of all Scopes.

### Restrictions for Multi-Scope-Operation

- All activated oscilloscope devices have to be of the same type.

- All Scopes are controlled via the same Vector hardware interface; a common trigger interface is used.

- The user has to assign the Scope hardware serial number to the Scope-ID manually during the initial operation.

### [SCOPE] ScopePhysicalLayerTestCAN


# Physical Layer Test for CAN

Scope » Physical Layer Test » CAN

The signal quality of a CAN high-speed channel can be verified with the bit mask analysis.

The bit mask defines an area through which the signal must not cut. If portions of the signal are located within the bit mask, the Scope signals this as a bit mask violation.

## Functions/Characteristics

- The bit mask analysis is integrated in the Scope option.

- The bit mask analysis is performed with the differential CAN signal.

- The bit mask analysis is possible with classic CAN and with CAN FD.

- The differential CAN signal is analyzed according to the CAN specification. The synchronization occurs at recessive-dominant edges, taking into consideration the configured synchronization jump width.

- The bit masks can be defined by the user.

- The analysis is controlled by a user-programmable CAPL test node.

- A complete CAN message from the SOF to the EOF can be analyzed. However it is also possible to limit the analysis to individual message segments.

- The variety of trigger possibilities enables automated testing of a specific message, all messages of a send node, or any other combination of messages.

- The result of the analysis is written in a test report.

- In addition to the written test evaluation, the test report can contain screenshots of the Scope diagram. It is also possible to display the utilized bit masks.

## Configuration

- Connect a CAN channel of a Vector hardware interface to the CAN bu

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [SCOPE] ScopePhysicalLayerTestFlexRay


# Physical Layer Test for FlexRay

Scope » Physical Layer Test » FlexRay

The signal quality of a FlexRay cluster can be verified with the bit mask analysis.

The bit mask defines an area through which the signal must not cut. If portions of the signal are located within the bit mask, the Scope signals this as a bit mask violation.

## Functions / Characteristics

- The bit mask analysis is integrated in the .Scope option.

- The bit mask analysis is performed with the differential FlexRay signal.

- The differential FlexRay signal is analyzed according to the FlexRay EPL Specification V2.1.

- The bit masks can be defined by the user.

- The analysis is controlled by a user-programmable CAPL test node.

- The send messages of an ECU that were recorded with the Scope are analyzed.

- The analysis can be limited to specific message types:

- All messages

- Messages in key slots

- All messages of the static segment

- All messages of the dynamic segment

- The result of the analysis is written in a test report.

- In addition to the written test evaluation, the test report can contain screenshots of the Scope diagram. It is also possible to display the utilized bit masks.

## Configuration

- Connect a FlexRay channel of a Vector hardware interface to the FlexRay cluster to be analyzed.

- Connect the PicoScope channels to the FlexRay cluster with the probe cable.

- Connect the hardware trigger connection of the Vector hardware interface to the trigger input of the PicoScop

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [SCOPE] ScopeTrigger


# Triggers

Scope » Triggers » CAN Frame / CAN Any Error Type

Devices and Triggers Page|Context Menu|Add Trigger Condition|CAN Frame…/CAN Any Error Type

## Prerequisites

To enable triggering, you need

- a running measurement

- a connection to a Scope device, and

- an activated trigger (toolbar: ) to use predefined trigger conditions.

## Trigger Types

You can use the following trigger types individually or in combination; the first trigger that occurs "wins":

- Manual triggering Click (Trigger now) in the toolbar to trigger immediately.

- Trigger on predefined condition To trigger in reaction to a bus event, you need a suitable trigger condition (see Devices and Triggers (Scope) page).A trigger condition is based on specific event types and event parameters.You can create as many trigger conditions as you like, all of which are linked with an OR operator.

- Trigger via CAPL You can use the scopeTriggerNow() CAPL function to trigger via a CAPL program.This lets you trigger on e.g. signal or system values.

## Trigger Modes

You can switch the trigger mode at any time via the toolbar:

- Single The trigger is not reactivated after it is triggered; all preconfigured trigger conditions are also deactivated.

- Repeat The trigger is automatically reactivated after triggering; all preconfigured trigger conditions remain active.The blind time in Repeat mode is at least 100 ms.

## Trigger Conditions

You can define trigger conditions for CAN, CAN FD, LIN and FlexRay.

### [SCOPE] ScopeViewTrace


# Trace View

Scope » Layout » Trace View

In the Trace View you can view all the bus events that occur during data acquisition here. Bus events originate from the hardware interface, not the Scope device.

You can open/close the Trace View via a splitter below the diagram.

You can also view bus event fields, depending on the event type. These fields are shown as sub-elements of a given bus event.

When you click on a bus event or sub-element, the diagram display automatically scrolls to the start of that event. A double-click on the bus event or sub-element automatically fits the diagram to match the start of the event.

- Time Timestamp of the bus event.In the case of sub-elements, this is the time stamp of the start of the field.

- Event Type/Field Bus event type.In the case of sub-elements, this is the name of the field.

- Duration Duration of the bus event.In the case of sub-elements, this is the duration of the field.

- Data Payload bytes of the bus event.In the case of sub-elements, this is the numerical field value (only displayed if present).

- Channel Application channel of the bus event.

- ID Frame ID at bus event (only displayed if present).

- Event Name Symbolic name at bus event (only displayed if present).

- Capture ID Unique ID of the captured dataset.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [SCOPE] ScopeVTSystemTrigger


# Triggering with VT System

Scope » Triggering with VT System

Devices and Triggers Page|Context Menu|Add Trigger Condition|IO Trigger…

## Preconditions

You need a T connector cable which is connected to the Sync channel of VT System. On the double ended side the cable is connected to the scope device on the Ext. and Gen. inputs. See labels on connectors.

## Activate VT System Trigger Conditions in CANoe

You have to define a special I/O trigger condition for VT System hardware on the Devices and Triggers (Scope) page of the Scope Window. This trigger condition allows you to trigger physical signals of the VT System. E.g. sensor signals based on PSI5 or SENT protocol; see page I/O Trigger for details.

When a CANoe measurement is running you have to activate the configured trigger condition by pressing the Activate trigger toolbar button . If you want to trigger repetitive times you should switch to Repeat in the toolbar of the Scope Window.

### [Security] SecurityBackendStatusDisplay


# Backend Status Display

Security » Backend Status Display

Some OEM security packages can communicate with an OEM backend. This is usually necessary for authentication in diagnostic communication.

The backend status display provides information about the current connection status of the respective backend.

As soon as the security functionality is activated via the security configuration and the security profile has a backend connection, an info text with the relevant information appears in the status bar. The content is updated every 10s.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Security] SecurityConcept


# Concept

Security » Concept

## Security Concept with Sources and Tool Connections

## CANoe Node Layer

- Callback handler, called before transmission starts and after reception of a secured PDU.

- CAPL functions for MAC creation and verification

- Callback handler to influence the security calculation in CAPL and to be informed about the results of the security calculation

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Security] SecuritySecOC


# Secured Onboard Communication

Security » Secured Onboard Communication

## Message Authentication Code

To protect important data related to authenticity and integrity, secured PDUs have a Message Authentication Code (MAC) which can be verified by a receiver.

A symmetric key will be needed to calculate the authenticator (MAC). The key must be known at sender and receiver side.

## Freshness Simulation

The security extension requires a valid freshness value in addition to the key.

The freshness value is a replay protection and is used to calculate the MAC during creation and checking. How the freshness value is distributed to participating nodes in the network is OEM-specific. These specific variants are provided via security sources. The Vector Security Manager can thus receive the freshness value and make it available for generating and checking MACs.

Additionally to this the QueryFreshness CAPL callback provides the possibility to set the freshness in a CAPL node.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Security] SecuritySetup


# Setup

Security » Setup

This page describes the necessary steps to configure the security in CANoe.

## Configuration

|  | Note A corresponding AUTOSAR database is needed. At least Secured-I-PDUs must be available; see Security Support. |

In the Vector Security Manager user interface, the desired profile template must be selected and a concrete security profile must be created. The profile has to be configured by entering the parameter of the profile, e.g. Data ID key pairs.

## Setup of the Simulation Model

## Security Profile Mapping

CANoe needs a mapping between the network and the security profile to be applied on this channel. This mapping can be done in the Security Configuration dialog.

The dialog can be accessed from the Simulation ribbon tab.

## Simulation Setup

In the Simulation Setup all nodes to be simulated must be inserted and must have the database node context.

The node layer SecMgrCANoeClient.dll must be added to relevant simulation nodes:

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Security] SecuritySupport


# Security Support

Security » Security Support

|  | Note Starting with CANoe version 10.0 SP3 security functionality is available. The security functionality is part of CANoe standard feature set and therefore the functions are still available. |

## General

The CANoe security features are mainly:

- Creation of a Message Authentication Code

- Verification of a Message Authentication Code

The features are based on AUTOSAR 4.2.2.

## Database Attributes

|  | Note The minimum AUTOSAR database version is AUTOSAR 4.2.2. |

Only PDUs of the type Secured-I-PDU are relevant for the security support.

Each Secured-I-PDU has a set of attributes. Currently the following attributes are evaluated:

| Attribute | Description |
| --- | --- |
| AuthInfoTx Length | Length of the authenticator (e.g. CMAC) in bit |
| DataID | ID for key assignment |
| Freshness Value Tx Length | Length of the transmitted Freshness in bit |
| Freshness Value Length | Length of complete Freshness value. |

## Automatic Validation of Secured PDUs

With the reception of secured PDUs the authenticator is automatically validated. The result is displayed in the Trace Window with overlay icons. Additionally the state can be checked in CAPL.

## Access to SecOC Data

Additional data fields like AuthInfo and Freshness of a secured PDU are offered as separate signals:

This way, the secured info can be used in all signal based windows. The signals have the pattern <PDUName>_AuthInfo and <PDUName>_Freshness.

## CA

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Security] SecurityTLS


# Transport Layer Security (TLS)

Security »Transport Layer Security (TLS)

The communication between two endpoints can be secured with TLS. It allows to setup up a secured communication between client and server. CANoe can take part in a TLS communication and simulate the TLS server and/or the TLS client. CANoe can also replay TLS recordings. The TLS Observer can try to decrypt the TLS communication. TLS is only supported together with CANoe option .Ethernet. There is a sample configuration available which demonstrates the use of TLS: TCP Chat (CAPL) with TLS encryption.

## Simulation of a TLS Endpoint in CANoe

Every simulation node of an Ethernet network can be used to simulate a TLS endpoint (Client or Server). The UDP or TCP Socket must be opened in CAPL or C#. Refer to .Net and CAPL.

Refer to the following CAPL functions:

- tlsAuthenticateAsClient

- tlsAuthenticateAsServer

A TLS communication can only be established if a Security Profile is assigned to the TCP/IP Stack of the Simulation Node. The TCP/IP Stack Settings are listed in the dialog TCP/IP Stack.

The assignment of the Security Profile to the TCP/IP Stack is done in the dialog Security Configuration on the tab Stacks.

Every Security Profile of type PKI (Public Key Infrastructure) can be assigned to a stack and be used for TLS. The Security Profiles are configured in the Vector Security Manager.

## Security Profile Configuration

The PKI Security Profile to be used for TLS must define certain TLS parame

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Security] SecurityTLSMasterSecret


# TLS Master Secret

Security » TLS Master Secret

The system-defined system variable _Security::TLSMasterSecret is used by CANoe to store the MasterSecret and the ClientRandom in the Trace Window as a system variable, so that the logging file can be decrypted during replay. For this CANoe must be either TLS client or TLS server.

The system variable _Security::TLSMasterSecret can also be used to decrypt a trace by setting the system variable _Security::TLSMasterSecret to the correct value. This way the master secret can also be read and set via diagnostics or a third way in CANoe.

## TLSMasterSecretStruct

- Key Contains the Client Random and the MasterSecret. The Client Random is used to detect the correct TLS connection because the Client Random is transmitted unencrypted in the TLS handshake message Client Hello.

- Type Specifies the two types ByteStreamMasterSecret and NSSKeyLogMasterSecret.

| Type | Value | Example Key |
| --- | --- | --- |
| ByteStreamMasterSecret | 1 | 5C0560F326F158413…294F0AF127E9…24C |
| NSSKeyLogMasterSecret | 2 | CLIENT_RANDOM 5C0560F326F158413…29 4F0AF127E9…24C |

|  | Example on start{ sysBeginVariableStructUpdate("_Security", "TLSMasterSecret"); // Syntax of NSS Key Log Format: <Label><space><ClientRandom Value><space><MasterSecret Value> // Example: CLIENT_RANDOM 5C0560F326F158413…29 4F0AF127E9…24C sysSetVariableString("_Security", "TLSMasterSecret.Key", "CLIENT_RANDOM " *** YOUR CLIENT RANDOM VALUE*** " " *** YOUR MASTER SECRET VALUE ***

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Sensor] Sensor


# Option Sensor

CANoe » Option Sensor

|  | Option Sensor offers you a variety of applications concerning the analysis, simulation and testing of sensors and ECUs. Among others, it includes the following functions: Logging and analysis of the communication between ECUs and sensors. Simulation of sensors for testing the functions of your ECUs. Simulation of ECUs for testing sensors. Option Sensor is seamlessly integrated into the scope of functions of CANoe: Visualize the sensor data in the numerous analysis windows of CANoe. Among others, the following windows are available: the Trace Window, the Graphics Window, the State Tracker and the Data Window. The communication between ECUs and sensors can be logged through the logging functions of CANoe. The communication can be analyzed offline at a later time or played back in real time. The precise time stamps allow you to correlate sensor data with data of other bus systems, such as CAN or LIN, or I/O data from VT System, without problems. Create simulation data for your simulated sensors or ECUs using Signal Generators, panels or through the flexible programming with CAPL. Use the numerous functions of the Test Feature Set to easily create comprehensive tests for your ECUs and sensors and to execute them with CANoe. |

| Quick Access New Functions CAPL Reference Sample Configurations Feature Matrix Basics Required Licenses Configuration Dialog Error Codes Hardware The operation of the Option .Sensor requires an appropriate har

### [Sensor] SensorConfiguration


# Configuration

Option .Sensor » Configuration

Ribbon|Hardware Tab|Protocol Configuration

|  | The Option .Sensor can be configured in the Sensor Configuration dialog. This dialog allows you to create different types of channels in which sensors and ECUs can be configured. Depending on the use case, you can also select which devices are currently connected to the hardware interface and which ones shall be simulated through CANoe. |

## Adding a Sensor Channel

Via menu pathChannels|Add Channel it is possible to add a new channel (PSI, SENT or SPI). Alternatively, via menu pathChannels|Add Channel from Template the channel can also be selected from a library of pre-created user-specific configurations in the Add Channel from Template dialog.

## Configuring a Sensor Channel

The configured channels together with the respective elements are displayed in the Sensor Channels column on the left side of the navigation tree.

Further functions are available in the context menu of the various elements, such as adding further sub-elements, deleting elements or copying and pasting elements.

## Configuring Elements of the Sensor Channel

The settings of the selected elements are displayed on the right side.

- Configuration of the PSI5 Sensor Channel

- Configuration of the SENT Sensor Channel

- Configuration of the SPI Sensor Channel

- Configuration of the UART, RS-485 and LVDS Sensor Channel

- Configuration of the I2C Sensor Channel

The settings area on the right side consist

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Sensor] SensorConfigurationAddChannel


# Add Channel from Template

Option .Sensor » Configuration

Ribbon|Hardware Tab|Protocol Configuration|Configuration|New from Template...Ribbon|Hardware Tab|Protocol Configuration|Channels|Add Channel from Template...

In the Add Channels from Template dialog you can select on the left side the sensor protocol. Dependent on the protocol selection the following settings are displayed on the right side of the dialog.

## Templates

- Use case

- ECU testing (sensor simulation) If you select this use case, a channel with real ECU and simulated sensor is created.Use case: Test of an existing ECU.

- Sensor testing (ECU simulation) If you select this use case, a channel with simulated ECU and real sensor is created.Use case: Test of an existing sensor.

- Application Here you can select the application you want to work in (e.g. Airbag, Chassis and Safety).

- Template Dependent on the selected settings above you can select here the template you want to load.

## Template Description

Here you can see a short description of the currently selected template.

Configuration of the PSI5 Sensor Channel | Configuration of the SENT Sensor Channel | Configuration of the SPI Sensor Channel | Configuration of the UART Sensor Channel

### [Sensor] SensorConfigurationDataSource


# Stimulation Data Source Browser

Option .Sensor » Configuration

Ribbon|Hardware Tab|Protocol Configuration

In the Stimulation Data Source Browser dialog you can select the replay data source for the currently selected sensor.

When you open the dialog, the BLF file configured for the sensor’s channel will be automatically scanned. Any suitable system variables will be listed under Available Sensors.

The system variables have to meet the following criteria:

- They must contain sensor frames

- They must be in the right namespace (e.g. SENSOR::PSI5, if you are using the PSI5 protocol)

By clicking on any of the found sensors you can confirm your selection and close the dialog.

Configuration of the PSI5 Sensor Channel | Configuration of the SENT Sensor Channel | Configuration of the SPI Sensor Channel | Configuration of the UART Sensor Channel

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |

| I2C channel settings Assigned hardware channel Specifies which physical hardware channel (e.g. a VT2710 channel) is assigned to this sensor channel. If no channels have been configured, the sensor channel will be automatically deactivated at the start of the measurement. Bit rate Specifies the bit rate of the communication on this channel. Generate low level events When this option is activated, additional system variable events will be generated. This is especially useful when analyzing the exact timing on the bus (e.g. clock stretching).Note: enabling this option may greatly increase the CANoe event load. Generate raw frame events When this option is activated, CANoe will generate additional raw frame system variable events for every transmitted I2C frame. Voltage level high Determines the high voltage level on the I2C signal lines. Depending on the hardware interface used, this setting must be made elsewhere (e.g. in

### [Sensor] SensorConfigurationIssues


# Configuration Issues

Option .Sensor » Configuration

When you apply any changes made in the Sensor Configuration dialog, an automated plausibility check will be performed. Potentials issues will be reported as warnings or errors, depending on their severity.

Each of these issues acts as a hyperlink. Thus clicking any of the issues will take you directly to the corresponding configuration element.

Warnings may be ignored by clicking [Ignore]. This will apply the changes and allow you to start the measurement. If your configuration contains errors, this option is not available. To permanently ignore warnings, activate Report warnings only once.

Please note that ignoring warnings is not recommended and may lead to unexpected behavior during the measurement.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |

| Element | Description/Configuration |
| --- | --- |
| Network | PSI5 Settings No configuration settings are required. |
| Channel | A channel describes the interconnection between an ECU and one or more PSI5 sensors. All the settings that apply to the elements within a channel are configured on this level (e.g. communication mode or baud rate). |
| PSI5 Channel Settings Assigned hardware channel Specifies which physical hardware channel (e.g. a VT2710 piggy channel) is assigned to this sensor channel. In case no channels have been configured, the sensor channel will be automatically deactivated at the start of the measurement. Communication mode Specifies which communication mode must be used for this channel. Details relative to the different modes can be found in the PSI5 specification. Bit rate Specifies the bit rate used by this channel. The standard values are 125 or 189 kbps. Different values can also be entered. Cycle time Specifies the cycle time with which this channel works. ### [Sensor] SensorConfigurationPSI5InitialDataConfig


# Initialization Data Configuration

Option .Sensor » Configuration » PSI5 Sensor Channel » Initialization Data Configuration

Ribbon|Hardware Tab|Protocol Configuration|PSI5|Initialization Data Content

In the Initialization Data Configuration dialog you can specify which initialization data are sent by the simulated sensor in this time slot.

## General Settings

- Data length This value specifies how many nibbles (4 bit) are sent as initialization data. This setting determines which settings are available in the following sections.

- Repeat block Specifies how often each block (i.e. pair of ID and value) must be repeated. In the PSI5 specification this value is referred to as k times.

## Contents

- Protocol revision Specifies which protocol revision is reported in the initialization data.

- Manufacturer Specifies the manufacturer of the sensor. The codes set by the PSI5 standard are also available for the selection. The setting user-defined allows you to use an own manufacturer code.

- Sensor type Specifies the type of sensor. Detailed information on this value can be found in the PSI5 specification.

- Sensor parameter Specifies the sensor parameters. Detailed information on this value can be found in the PSI5 specification.

- Sensor code (sensor manufacturer) Specifies the sensor code of the sensor manufacturer. Detailed information on this value can be found in the PSI5 specification.

- Sensor code (vehicle manufacturer) Specifies the sensor code of the vehicle 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Sensor] SensorErrorCodes


# Error Codes

Option .Sensor » Error Codes

With the Option .Sensor the errors can be reported via different system variables (such as ChannelError or SensorError). All the errors together with a description are listed in the following table.

## General Error Codes

| Error Code | Error Message | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | Unknown Error | An unknown error occurred for which no details have been provided. |
| 100 | Invalid checksum in serial message | The checksum of a received serial message was invalid. |
| 101 | Invalid format of serial message | The format of a received serial message was invalid. This happens for example when the bits 7, 13 or 18 of an extended serial message weren't 0. |

## PSI5 Error Codes

| Error Code | Error Message | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 200 | Resuming operation | If no more errors are detected on the bus, the simulated ECU or sensor will start to function again. |
| 201 | Dispatching of received frame failed | A received frame couldn't have been assigned to a sensor or a time slot. This happens when a frame lies outside all configured time slots. |
| 202 | Frame started before time slot | A received frame couldn't have been assigned to a sensor or a time slot, but the frame started before the time slot. |
| 203 | Frame ended after time slot | A received frame couldn't have been assigned to a sensor or a time slot, but the frame ended after the time slot. |
| 204 | Illegal edge detected | An unexpected error has been

### [Sensor] SensorI2CProtocol


# I2C Protocol

Option .Sensor » I2C Protocol

The Inter-Integrated Circuit Interface (I2C) is a synchronous serial data bus interface for data exchange between at least a master and one or several slaves. Multi-master configurations are permitted, but they are not supported by CANoe. The I2C interface is usually used for networking different parts of an electronic circuit.

ON THIS PAGE:

| Transmission Rates |
| --- |
| Signal Lines and Data Transmission Start Condition Bit Transmission Stop Condition |
| Communication Protocol Addressing R/W bit Acknowledge |
| Clock Stretching |

## Transmission Rates [▲ back]

The following table shows the transmission modes defined for the I2C, together with the respective maximum transmission rates:

| Mode | Max. Transmission Rate |
| --- | --- |
| Standard Mode (Sm) | 0.1 Mbit/s |
| Fast Mode (Fm) | 0.4 Mbit/s |
| Fast Mode Plus (Fm+) | 1.0 Mbit/s |
| High Speed Mode (Hs-mode) | 3.4 Mbit/s |

## Signal Lines and Data Transmission [▲ back]

The data connection between all bus participants is provided only via two lines:

| Abbreviation | Name | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| SCL | Serial Clock Line | Clock line to control the communication process |
| SDA | Serial Data Line | Line for data transmission (bidirectionally) |

## Start Condition

To generate a start condition, the master switches the SDA from High to Low, while the SCL is set to High.

## Bit Transmission

To transmit a bit, the clock line SCL must be set to High. I

[...truncated for brevity...]

# Required Licenses to Use Option .Sensor

Option .Sensor » Licenses

In general, you can always configure and edit all channels. However, you need the corresponding licenses to start the measurement procedure.

You CANoe can display your licenses in the Options dialog.

The SPI, UART and I2C protocols are included in the CANoe basic license. This allows you to

- Configure channels

- Edit channels

- Start measurements

Required hardware: VT System module VT2710

## License for the .Sensor Option

The PSI5 and SENT protocols are included in the license for the .Sensor option. This allows you to

- Configure channels

- Edit channels

- Start measurements

Required hardware: VT System module VT2710

## Standalone Basic License

The Standalone Basic license required for the standalone mode includes the license for the .Sensor option. This allows you to

- Start measurements

Required hardware: VT6000 - Realtime modules and VT2710 module

### [Sensor] SensorPSI5Protocol


# PSI5 Protocol

Option .Sensor » PSI5 Protocol

PSI5 (Peripheral Sensor Interface 5) is an open digital current interface for sensors. The interface is equipped with two lines supplied through the sensors. PSI5 is characterized by a high EMC safety and low radiated emissions. The communication is two-way: the data can also be sent from the ECU to the sensor.

ON THIS PAGE:

| Bus Topologies |
| --- |
| Operating Modes |
| Sensor to ECU Communication Frame Structure Error Detection Data Interpretation Serial Messages Initialization Data |
| ECU to Sensor Communication |

## Bus Topologies [▲ back]

Several sensors - up to a maximum of six per bus - can be connected to an ECU in different topologies. The topologies can also be mixed.

| Point-to-Point | Parallel Mode |
| --- | --- |
| The point-to-point topology connects several sensors to the ECU individually: | In the parallel mode the sensors are connected to the ECU in parallel: |
| Universal Bus Mode | Daisy Chain |
| In the universal bus mode several sensors are connected to the ECU through a not exactly known wiring for a synchronous data transmission. The wiring is considered as a black box: | The daisy chain topolgy connects the sensors one after the other: |

## Operating Modes [▲ back]

Depending on the topology and on the use case, PSI5 supports different operating modes. At the moment, CANoe supports the following modes:

- Asynchronous The asynchronous mode is only possible with a point-to-point connection. The 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Sensor] SensorSENTProtocol


# SENT Protocol

Option .Sensor » SENT Protocol

SENT (Single Edge Nibble Transmission) is a digital voltage interface between a sensor and an ECU. The connection is one-way: the data are only transferred from the sensor to the ECU. SENT is intended to allow you to implement sensors in the most cost effective manner. This is possible thanks to a reduced pin number, a simple protocol and low timing requirements.

ON THIS PAGE:

| Signal Lines |
| --- |
| Sensor to ECU Communication Frame Structure Nibble Structure Usage of the Pause Serial Messages |

## Signal Lines [▲ back]

A SENT sensor is usually equipped with only three connections:

- supply voltage (5V)

- a signal line

- GND

The sensor is connected to the corresponding pins on the ECU.

The high/low signal levels are clearly defined: the minimum voltage for the high level is 4.1 V; for the low level the voltage must be inferior to 0.5 V.

## Sensor to ECU Communication [▲ back]

## Frame Structure [▲ back]

As soon as the supply voltage is applied to the sensor, the sensor will begin to cyclically transmit his data. The data are transmitted in nibbles (4-bit) whose value is codified using the length between two falling edges. The sent frames have always the same structure that must be known to the ECU.

A frame starts with the sync pulse (56 ticks) followed by the status and communication nibbles. These are then followed by 1 to 6 data nibbles (12-162 ticks) and by the CRC checksum (12-27 ticks). At the end of the 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Sensor] SensorSPIProtocol


# SPI Protocol

Option .Sensor » SPI Protocol

SPI (Serial Peripheral Interface) is a synchronous serial data bus interface for data exchange between a master and one or several slaves. The interface is usually used to exchange data between an ECU (master) and various sensors (e.g. temperature, pressure or acceleration sensors) and actuators (e.g. digital-to-analog converters).

ON THIS PAGE:

| Signal Lines |
| --- |
| Bus Topologies |
| Operating Modes |
| Communication |

## Signal Lines [▲ back]

The data connection between master and slave is provided via the following signal lines:

| Abbreviation | Name | Description | Direction |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| SCLK | Serial Clock | Clock line of the master | Master -> slave |
| MOSI | Master Output, Slave Input | This line is the data output of the master | Master -> slave |
| MISO | Master Input, Slave Output | This line is the data input of the master | Slave -> Master |
| CS | Chip Select | Mostly low-active signal which can be used by the master to directly address individual slaves | Master -> slave |

## Bus Topologies [▲ back]

Several sensors in different topologies can be connected to an ECU. Different topologies can also be mixed.

| Star | Daisy chain |
| --- | --- |
| In the star connection, all slaves are connected to the signal lines SCKL, MISO and MOSI in parallel. For each slave, the master has a separate chip select line which can be used to address the slave individually: | In the daisy chain mode, the

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Sensor] SensorUARTProtocol


# UART Protocol

Option .Sensor » UART Protocol

The UART protocol (Universal Asynchronous Receiver Transmitter) is an asynchronous serial protocol for data transmission between two network nodes or more. It can be operated on diverse physical interfaces like RS-232, RS-485, RS-422 or LVDS.

ON THIS PAGE:

| Bus Topologies and Signal Lines Serial Interface RS-485 or RS-422 Interface LVDS Interface Frame Structure |

## Bus Topologies and Signal Lines [▲ back]

Depending on the used physical interface, different bus topologies and signal lines are used.

## Serial Interface [▲ back]

This simple interface can often be found in microcontrollers. It allows a full duplex communication between two network nodes. The data communication is usually done via two signal lines that link together the Rx and Tx pins of both nodes.

Since the data transmission is asynchronous, no additional clock line is necessary. Instead a receiver synchronizes itself via the start and stop bits of the frames.

In this interface, the logic levels are implemented by different tensions to the common ground. TTL/LVTTL levels (0 V for a logical 0, 5 V/3.3 V for a logical 1) are often used.

## RS-485 or RS-422 Interface [▲ back]

The RS-485 and RS-422 interfaces have been developed for the serial high-speed data transmission over long distances. They allow to operate up to 32 network nodes on one bus and are widely used in industrial applications. A differential twisted pair to which all nodes of the bus ar

[...truncated for brevity...]
