## Variables and their Members [▲ back]

- Error

- RawFrameStart

- RawFrameRawFrameAddress

- AddressTypes Indicates if a 7 bit or a 10 bit address was transmitted.

- SlaveAddress Contains the current slave address.

- ReadWriteBit Indicates if the following data bytes are written or read by the master.

- Acknowledge1 This member contains the acknowledge of the first address byte.

- Acknowledge2 This member contains the acknowledge of the second address byte. This value is only relevant when a 10 bit address was transmitted.

- RawFrameWriteData

- NumBytes Number of written bytes.

- RawData An array containing the written bytes.

- Acknowledges An array containing the acknowledges for each byte.

- RawFrameReadData

- NumBytes Number

[...truncated for brevity...]

## Variables and their Members [▲ back]

- ChannelError

- ErrorCode Provides information about the type of error.

- FrameLength If a frame is linked to this error, this member contains the length of the frame (if available). If there is no frame, this member is 0.

- Frame If a frame is linked to this error, this member contains the data of the frame (if available). As the exact structure of the frame is not known, the data are presented as byte arrays (Data1 to Data4).

- SyncPulse

- Duration Indicates the length of the sync pulse in nanoseconds.

- CycleTime Indicates the time passed from the last s

| Simulating a Sensor Configuration of the Sensor Simulation Starting Behavior of the Sensor Simulation Behavior of the Sensor in Case of a Voltage Loss Sending of Simulation Data |

- Data Contains the value of the nibbles in the data region.

- StartOfFrameTime Contains the time stamp at which the frame begins. Measured in nanoseconds, relative to the end of the sync pulse.

- FrameLength Contains the frame length in bits.

- TickLength Contains the measured clock tick length of the frame.

- PauseLength Contains the number of pause ticks of the frame. If there was no pause, this value is 0.

- Data The value of the data bits.

- DataBitCount The number of data bits in the frame.

- Parity The value of the parity bit (optional).

- StopBitCount The number of stop bits in the frame.

- StartOfFrameTime Start time of the frame in ns.

- Frame_TX

- Data The value of the data bits.

- DataBitCount The number of data bits in the frame.

- Parity The value of the parity bit (optional).

- StopBitCount The number of stop bits in the frame.

- StartOfFrameTime Start time of the frame in ns.

- Frame_Stim

- Data The value of the data bits.

### [SIL] SIL


# Software in the Loop

CANoe » Software in the Loop

Virtual execution environments on the computer, in virtual machines or in the cloud enable testing of software for any target OS based on Windows or Linux, e.g. Ubuntu, CentOS, SUSE.

A SIL adapter is generated based on a functional system interface description of the application under test and allows access to the SUT on a pure software level.

- SIL Adapter (Concept)

- Sample Configurations Software in the Loop

- SIL Adapter Basic

### [SIL] SILAdapter


# SIL Adapter

Software in the Loop » SIL Adapter

## Overview

|  | Step by Step Procedure: Workflow for SIL-Tests Describe the functional system interfaces of the software under test as Distributed Objects (DO). For these distributed objects a "SIL Adapter" for data exchange with CANoe is generated. The adapter is provided as a set of source files and libraries. Instrument the SUT source code using the SIL Adapter in the development environment. When you start the instrumented SUT in a virtual execution environment, you can read and write the values of the software under test from CANoe. |

## Description of the Functional System Interfaces

You can define physical environments and software environments to stimulate the application under test and verify its behavior.

You define this environment using distributed objects (DO); either with the graphical UI of the Model Editor or in text form with the vCDL Editor.

To create the SIL Adapter, you have to set the binding of distributed objects (DO) to SilAdapter.

|  | Example: vCDL file version 1.2;namespace RoomTemperatureControl{ [Binding="SilAdapter"] interface ISensor { provided data double Temperature; } [Binding="SilAdapter"] interface IHeating { consumed data bool IsCooling; consumed data bool IsHeating; } object Sensor1 : ISensor; object Sensor2 : ISensor; object Sensor3 : ISensor; object Heating : IHeating;} |

## System Setup in CANoe4SW

You can import a vCDL file into CANoe via System And Communication Setup. Afte

### [SimulationStimulation] ConfigurationRemainingBusSimulation


# Configuration of a Remaining Bus Simulation

CANoe » Simulation/Stimulation with CANoe » Function Overview » Configuration of a Remaining Bus Simulation

|  | Glossary Simulation A simulation includes all software components that can occur in the real control unit and the basic transmission behavior of the control unit. The control unit behavior is thus fully simulated. Remaining bus simulation When a real control unit is tested, the communication of the other control units in the network design is reproduced in a remaining bus simulation. Transmission models Transmission models describe the transmission behavior of messages and signals. Transmission models are integrated in the configuration as databases (e.g., DBC or XML files) The transmission is carried out, e.g., via a schedule table or the interaction layer. |

On the following pages you can find Step by Step instructions for the configuration of a remaining bus simulation.

For the creation of a remaining bus simulation with CAN in this example the interaction layer and the Model Generation Wizard are used. The interaction layer (IL) takes over the transmitting of messages. Therefore function libraries (DLLs), which must be integrated in the network node, are used and the corresponding attributes in the database have to be set. In the case of LIN and FlexRay, the schedule table takes over the transmitting of messages.

- CAN bus: How to simulate a CAN network (IL)

- LIN bus: How to simulate a LIN network

- FlexRay

### [SimulationStimulation] NetworkManagement


# Network Management

CANoe » Simulation/Stimulation with CANoe » Function Overview » Network Management

The network management (NM) is responsible for the changeover of control units from standard operating mode to sleep mode and monitors the availability of the individual network nodes.

Each simulated node that is to participate in network management must be marked in the database by corresponding node attributes. Network management messages are transmitted exclusively by the NM.DLL.

CANoe provides for the network management different modeling libraries (DLL files).

In the online help you can fin further information here:

- OEM-specific modeling libraries

- LIN network management

- OSEK network management

- CANopen Network Management

You can also find further information about network management in the document library (DOC installation folder).

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |

# Simulation/Stimulation with CANoe | Concept

CANoe » Concept » Simulation/Stimulation with CANoe

|  | Glossary: »Simulation / »Stimulation Simulation A simulation includes all software components that can occur in the real control unit and the basic transmission behavior of the control unit. The control unit behavior is thus fully simulated. Remaining bus simulation When a real control unit is tested, the communication of the other control units in the network design is reproduced in a remaining bus simulation. Stimulation The purpose of stimulation is to induce reactions of control units in the network. Here, for example, messages only may simply be sent, or signal values set and their corresponding message sent. Transmission models Transmission models describe the transmission behavior of messages and signals. Transmission models are integrated in the configuration as databases (e.g. DBC or XML files). The transmission is carried out, e.g. via a schedule table or the interaction layer. |

| Overview |

| CANoe supports the three phases of the development process, ranging from simulation of the entire network and remaining bus simulation to analysis of a real network. This requires all control units in the network to be integrated as network nodes in the Simulation Setup. The bus communication is defined via integrated databases (e.g., DBC, XML). ### [SmartCharging] SCCCSListener


# VH5110 CCS Listener

SmartCharging » VH5110 CCS Listener

## Listening Strategy

The CCS Listener offers two ways to establish a PLC (Power Line Communication) connection between a Charge Point and a Vehicle. The first way requires a direct connection to the CP (Control Pilot) as well as the PE (Protective Earth) lines of the Charging Plug. By using this way, the PWM signal can be measured.

The second way is more passive because it uses inductive coupling, usually a negative side effect of the PLC. By using this way, the PWM signal cannot be measured.

For being able to see any IPv6/UDP/TCP based communication, like SECC Discovery or V2G, the CCS Listener must monitor the SLAC protocol run already from the beginning. The SLAC protocol is responsible for establishing a network connection between the Vehicle and the Charge Point.

The CCS Listener is just forwarding all the traffic between the Vehicle and the Charge Point to the Host computer. It is neither capable of decrypting TLS secured communication nor can it be used to send any traffic on the network listened in on.

## Messages

The following table contains all messages provided by the CCS Listener.

| Message | Type | ID | Type | ID | Active/Passive |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| UnassociatedSta | Ind | 201 | — | — | Passive |
| CcAssoc | Req | 203 | Cnf | 204 | Passive |
| CcSetTeiMap | Ind | 205 | — | — | Passive |
| CmEncryptedPayload | Ind | 207 | — | — | Passive |
| CmStaIndentify | Ind | 209 | Rsp 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [SmartCharging] SCNonSupportedFunctions


# Non Supported Functions

SmartCharging » Non Supported Functions (Smart Charging)

|  | Note Not all features of the protocol are implemented. Refer to Supported Protocol Versions for an overview of the currently supported protocol versions. The limitations indicated below apply additionally and to all implemented protocol versions. |

In contrast to ISO 15118 / DIN 70121, the following functions are not supported:

| Function | Description |
| --- | --- |
| ChargingProfile | The charging profile sent by the vehicle is not yet applied. Nevertheless, it is validated against the PMax schedule by the charge point. |
| PMax schedule | The charge point’s PMax schedule is not yet adapted to the vehicle’s departure time (see [V2G2-303], [V2G2-304] resp. [V2G-DC-559]). If defined in the XML configuration file, it is sent exactly in its present form. If not, a schedule is generated to match exactly the ChargingProfile. |
| Certificates and Security | The vehicle’s GenChallenge field is not yet compared against the sent GenChallenge by the charge point. The SECC certificate chain (used for TLS) is not yet verified by the vehicle (see [V2G2-875]). The OEM provisioning certificate chain (part of CertificateInstallationReq) is not yet verified by the charge point. The scope of the signatures is not yet verified, i.e. if the correct elements were signed. Vehicle certificates received via CertificateInstallationRes or CertificateUpdateRes are not persistent. They are only used during the

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [SmartCharging] SCOverview


# Overview DIN-70121/ISO-15118

SmartCharging » Overview DIN-70121/ISO-15118

The ISO/IEC TC 22/SC 3 N - Road vehicles — Vehicle-to-Grid Communication Interface specification defines the communication protocol between a vehicle and a charge point.

This document describes the scope of functions which implements the aforementioned specification and related simulation modules.

It consists of the following components:

- Charge point simulation module (SCC_ChargePoint.dll)

- Vehicle simulation module (SCC_Vehicle.dll)

- CANoe Observer for display of SCC events in the Trace Window

## Charge point module

The charge point simulation module is responsible for processing the following tasks:

- Handling of the SLAC protocol for EVSEs

- Receipt of SCC Requests and automatic sending of SCC Responses

- Simulation of charge point statuses (e.g. EVSEStatus)

- Sending of arbitrary SCC and SLAC messages

## Vehicle module

The vehicle simulation module is responsible for processing the following tasks:

- Handling of the SLAC protocol for EVs

- Receipt of SCC Responses and automatic sending of SCC Requests

- Simulation of vehicle statuses (e.g., EVStatus)

- Sending of arbitrary SCC and SLAC messages

## Monitor module

The Monitor DLL is a passive module that tracks SCC communication. In contrast to the Observer module, it is a Nodelayer DLL that provides direct access to the data via system variables. Use for:

- Man-in-the-middle analysis of a running SCC connection

- Offline

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [SmartCharging] SCSimulationOverview


# Simulation Overview

SmartCharging » Simulation Overview (Smart Charging)

## Simulation Modes and States

### Simulation Basics

There are three main use cases:

- Active simulation mode: A charge point or vehicle is simulated automatically, using the internal state machine and data from an XML configuration file

- Passive simulation mode: A charge point or vehicle tester is implemented by sending Vehicle2Grid and/or SLAC message manually, using CAPL functions and data provided

- Monitoring: No simulation takes place. Instead, the communication between two existing SCC modules is observed using the SCC Monitor and/or the SCC Observer.

### State Diagram

In the first two cases, the simulation DLL SCC_ChargePoint.DLL or SCC_Vehicle.DLL is run.

The simulation DLLs can be in one of the following states. CAPL functions for state transitions are denoted next to the state transition arrows.

### Active Mode

Active simulation mode is a straightforward mode of simulation, where minimal intervention from CAPL is necessary. In this mode, the DLL runs a state machine that conforms to one of the supported specifications, thus providing a counterpart to an exist vehicle or charge point implementation.

All necessary data and CAPL implementation to run an active simulation is provided as part of the sample configurations.

### Passive Mode

Passive simulation mode allows running a DLL without starting the SCC state machine. This results in a simulation which does not sent SCC messa

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [SmartCharging] SCSupportedProtocolVersions


# Protocols

SmartCharging » Protocols

## Supported Protocol Versions

### Mode of Functioning

The node layer DLLs can be operated with various versions of the Vehicle2Grid communication protocol. Such a version consists of an XML schema, defined by a namespace string, and a two digit version number. It is negotiated between vehicle and charge point using the SupportedAppProtocol message pair (Protocol handshake). If the vehicle node layer is part of the simulation, the desired protocol version can be configured using the vehicle‘s configuration file, or selected online via the CAPL interface. If the charge point node layer is part of the simulation, it will accept the SupportedAppProtocol request if namespace and version are one of the entries listed below.

|  | Note When switching protocol versions online, switching of XML configuration section will most likely be necessary because of different parameter formats. In this case, please refer to the function LoadV2GConfig . |

### Protocol Versions

The following versions are available:

- ISO 15118

- DIN 70121

## Basic Protocol Layers

| Communication layers | SCC-relevant norms (examples) | Vector support |
| --- | --- | --- |
| V2G Application | ISO 15118, DIN 70121 | CANoe .SmartCharging (V2G communication messages, EXI, V2GTP and SLAC control) |
| EXI/XML | ISO 15118, DIN 70121, W3C EXI Profile |
| V2G TP | ISO 15118, DIN 70121 |
| TLS (optional) | — | CANoe .Ethernet (TCP, UDP, IPv6, TLS) |
| TCP/IP | IPv6, UDP |
|

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [SmartCharging] SCTestFunctions


# Test Functions

SmartCharging » Test Functions (Smart Charging)

## Overview

## Test Support

Test functions provide means to influence the SCC protocol outside the bounds of the regular protocol flow. The functions are always available, indifferent to the protocol’s state machine, or if a protocol instance is even running. This means in particular that test functions can be used in passive mode (refer to Simulation Modes and States).

At the moment, all supported test functions are related to the free (e.g. state-machine unrelated) sending of messages. Free Sending of SLAC Messages describes the sending of SLAC messages (MME frames), while Free Sending of SCC Messages describes the sending of SECC Discovery and V2G messages.

|  | Note Only one of the protocols (SLAC, SDP, V2G) can be used at the same time. Preparing a new message will discard a previously prepared message, regardless of its protocol type. |

|  | Step by Step Procedure To send an message using the test functions, there are three steps: Create the respective message using one of the Create-functions from Free Sending of SLAC Messages or Free Sending of SCC Messages. If desired and available, set further parameters using the function SCC_SetOptionalParameter() resp. SCC_SLAC_SetAdditionalParameter(). Send the prepared message using SCC_SendPreparedMessage(). For V2G messages that support signatures, you can choose to send the message with or without signature. |

## Free Sending of SLAC Messages

## Overv

### [SmartCharging] SCTroubleshooting


# Troubleshooting

SmartCharging » Troubleshooting

This page lists frequent issues when setting up a CCS configuration.

## First steps

In case of any issues with a CCS simulation, please check the following first:

- Has the simulation DLL been started correctly, i.e. using SCC_StartSimulation()? Is it possibly in passive mode?

- Has the XML configuration been loaded? Check the Write window for a message saying Could not load configuration. An invalid XML configuration file is a common cause for various issues, refer to Simulation Overview.

- Are any other error messages printed to the Write window?

## IP Configuration

### Which of the three stacks should I use?

- It is highly recommended to use the node / individual network stack.

### The TCP connection doesn't work in a reliable way.

- This issue is only known for Windows network adapters. Try reconfiguring or re-enabling the adapter. If you use the node / individual stack, disable all services except Vector Network Driver.

### How to configure multiple CCS nodes with the same simulation DLL, but different XML configurations?

- Two different networks can be used, this results in two different XML configuration files. Another possibility is to use several sections in the file and load a different one for each node using CAPL. Please note the IP address is not a necessary parameter in the XML file.

### After moving the node to a different network, the V2G communication no longer works.

- The IP configuration is

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [SmartCharging] SCUsageofMonitorDLL


# Usage of Monitor DLL

SmartCharging » Usage of Monitor DLL (Smart Charging)

## Mode of Operation

### Distinction from SCC Observer

The monitor DLL operates similar to the SCC observer. It tracks SCC messages on the selected bus, assembles fragments, and provides the data for analysis. The difference is that while the SCC observer serves for data extraction to the Trace window, the monitor provides direct access to the data inside the messages.

|  | FAQ Please note that due to technical limitations, a data object from an SCC message provided by the SCC observer cannot be used like a regular signal, e.g. a CAN signal. In particular, it cannot be dragged to other CANoe windows, and its value cannot be access via CAPL code. The SCC monitor is designed to fill this gap. |

### Adding the monitor

The SCC monitor is not active by default. Every SCC configuration can be extended by the monitor DLL in a similar way to adding a new simulation module.

|  | Step by Step Procedure Open the Simulation Setup window Insert a new node Right click on the node and choose “Configuration…” In the Components tab, choose “Add…” and select the SCC_Monitor.DLL from the Exec32 folder of your CANoe installation |

|  | Note No configuration is necessary at the moment, nor is there an INI or XML configuration file for the SCC monitor. |

## Use Cases

Usage of the SCC Monitor is recommended for the following applications:

### Man-In-The-Middle

To track the communication between two real remot

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [SmartCharging] SCVisualizationInCANoe


# Visualization in CANoe

SmartCharging » Visualization in CANoe (Smart Charging)

## Overview

### SCC Observer

The integrated SCC Observer enables a clear representation of SCC messages in the Trace Window.

### Toggling Observer Events

You can activate and deactivate the display and/or storage of SCC messages, if required. To do so, choose the Predefined Filters|Observer|SCC Observer option in the Trace Window.

In case that you want to anything but SCC events displayed, deactivate the predefined filter Ethernet events. It does not influence the SCC observer events.

### Advanced filtering

You can choose to display/store specific SCC events only. To do this, click the predefined filter SCC Observer:

The meaning of these specific filters is as follows:

- V2G events: Vehicle2Grid and SECC Discovery messages (resp TCP and UDP)

- SLAC events: All handled MME messages except VS_PL_Lnk_Status

- Polling events: VS_PL_Lnk_Status.Req and VS_PL_Lnk_Status.Cnf

## Representation of Messages

### SCC messages

SCC messages are represented with the same message name as they have in the XML representation. The return type is added for response messages. In addition, the following information is available:

- The Protocol column displays the sub-protocol (sdp for SECC Discovery Protocol, v2g for Vehicle2Grid TP).

- The Protocol Information column displays protocol version and Session ID.

In addition, all information regarding the underlying TCP packet can be displayed as usual.

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [SmartCharging/SCProcedures] SCCConfigurationVehicleSCCNodes


# Configuration of Vehicle SCC Nodes

SmartCharging » Procedures » Configuration of Vehicle SCC Nodes (Smart Charging)

## General information

An SCC entity is simulated by assigning one of the node layer DLLs to a network node of an Ethernet network. The node then reacts automatically to incoming SCC messages according to its internal state machine. Its addresses, protocol configuration and the data to be sent and the must be configured by the user using one or more XML configuration files.

The parameters available are described below for the charge point, respectively for the vehicle.

## Configuration of a vehicle

The vehicle parameters are configured using an XML file. The meaning of each parameter is listed below.

|  | Example XML example files accompany the supplied sample configuration. You can use them as the basis for your own configuration. They contain the most relevant parameters out of the list below. |

The configuration file must be named according to the schema [Network name]_SCC_Vehicle.xml and be located in one of the following directories:

- Directory of the vehicle DLL used (e.g., CANoe/Exec32)

- Directory of the loaded CANoe configuration

Thus, different parameters can be defined for various networks of a CANoe configuration. In addition, multiple configurations can also be stored in the same file. You achieve this by integrating several PEVConfiguration elements, which must each have their own unique ID. Within the simulation, you can switch betw

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [SmartCharging/SCProcedures] SCCertificateHandling


# Certificate Handling

SmartCharging » Certificate Handling

A simulation of SmartCharging communication according to ISO 15118 with PnC mode needs access to public key infrastructure. Depending on the simulated node (vehicle or charge point), and the required features, one or more certificates and their keys must be available. These are configured using the Vector Security Manager and its PKI Security Source.

## PKI Structure

A complete sample PKI according to ISO 15118 is provided with the CANoe installation. The naming and structure is chosen according to ISO 15118-2:2014 Annex E. The SmartCharging sample configurations are preconfigured to use this PKI for TLS and application layer security (XML signatures and encrypted key transfer).

## Configuration of Certificates

In order to configure different certificates than those provided, it is recommended to first create a new PKI security profile in the Vector Security Manager (please refer to the tool documentation for more information). Please make sure that private keys are available for all owned certificates of the simulated participant.

The new profile must then be assigned for the TCP/IP stack of the respective node using the Security Configuration dialog.

Moreover, the SmartCharging nodelayer must be aware of the updated certificates. Its XML configuration has to match the certificate names as given by the Name field in the Security Manager, to enable the application layer to map the certificates to the require

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [SmartCharging/SCProcedures] SCConfigurationChargePointSCCNodes


# Configuration of Charge Point SCC Nodes

SmartCharging » Procedures » Configuration of Charge Point SCC Nodes

## General information

An SCC entity is simulated by assigning one of the node layer DLLs to a network node of an Ethernet network. The node then reacts automatically to incoming SCC messages according to its internal state machine. Its addresses, protocol configuration and the data to be sent must be configured by the user using one or more XML configuration files.

The parameters available are described below for the charge point, respectively for the vehicle.

## Configuration of a Charge Point

The charge point parameters are configured using an XML file. The meaning of each parameter is listed below.

|  | Example XML example files accompany the supplied sample configuration. You can use them as the basis for your own configuration. They contain the most relevant parameters out of the following list. |

The configuration file must be named according to the schema [Network name]_SCC_ChargePoint.xml and be located in one of the following directories:

- Directory of the charge point DLL used (e.g., CANoe/Exec32)

- Directory of the loaded CANoe configuration

Thus, different parameters can be defined for various networks of a CANoe configuration. In addition, multiple configurations can also be stored in the same file. You achieve this by integrating several EVSEConfiguration elements, which must each have their own unique ID. Within the simulation, you can sw

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [SmartCharging/SCProcedures] SCCreatingSCCConfig


# Creating an SCC Configuration

SmartCharging » Procedures » Creating an SCC Configuration (Smart Charging)

## Example setup

This section uses an example to describe the creation of an SCC simulation. In this example, a charge point simulation will be set up in such a way that it can communicate with a real vehicle control unit.

|  | Note Configuring a vehicle simulation can be done in a similar way. |

To set up a charge point simulation, follow these steps:

|  | Step by Step Procedure Open CANoe and create a new configuration. Choose the Ethernet template. Open the Configuration|Network Hardware… dialog, and choose a suitable Ethernet adapter. Insert a new network node in the Ethernet network. Open the network node configuration for the created node. Under Components, insert the SCC_ChargePoint.dll file, which is located in the Exec32 directory of your CANoe installation. Close the node configuration dialog. Open the TCP/IP configuration dialog via Simulation|TCP/IP Stacks… and select the EVSE node. In the section Stack Selection, choose Individual TCP/IP stack instance.Next, select the page Ethernet and configure a valid, link-local IPv6 address (starting with FE80:) and MAC address. Make sure that these are not being used by any other adapter on your system. Save the configuration to a suitable directory. Create a file named Ethernet_SCC_ChargePoint.xml in the same directory.Open the file with a text editor and enter the following (actual content will be added in a 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [SmartCharging/SCProcedures] SCFirstSteps


# First Steps

SmartCharging » First Steps (Smart Charging)

## General information

SCC protocol nodes are simulated by assigning one or more of the supplied node layer DLLs to nodes in the Simulation Setup of CANoe. The following DLLs exist:

- Charge point: SCC_ChargePoint.dll

- Vehicle: SCC_Vehicle.dll

If one of the SCC node layers is linked to a CANoe node and set to active mode, the node automatically simulates the corresponding protocol node, i.e., it reacts to SCC Requests or SCC Responses with the appropriate messages. A user interaction is not necessary for this. However, the behavior of the simulation can be influenced via CAPL.

|  | Cross Reference Each node layer can also be used in passive mode as a test library, which is described on the page Test Functions. |

## Ethernet configuration

Every SCC node must have a unique IPv6 address and MAC address. The method used to configure these depends on the IP network stack used.

- Node network stack: IP and MAC addresses are configured in the Simulation Setup. The nodes share a real adapter.

- CANoe network stack: IP and MAC addresses are configured in the Options dialog of CANoe. The nodes share a real adapter.

- Windows protocol stack: Every simulated node requires its own real network adapter and is referenced via its IP and MAC addresses. The adapters should have a fixed IP address.

|  | FAQ Usage of the individual stack instance (node network stack) is highly recommended. |

|  | Cross Reference For furth

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [SmartCharging/SCProcedures] SCHardwareConfiguration


# Hardware Configuration

SmartCharging » Procedures » Hardware Configuration

## HomePlug Green PHY Configuration Tool

In the next step, the HomePlug Green PHY chip should be configured according to the scenario. When using a VT7870, this can be done with the Vector GreenPHY Configurator tool.

## No SLAC usage

If your real ECU is connected directly via Ethernet (no HomePlug Green PHY chip is used), this step can be skipped.

To configure the HomePlug Green PHY chip of a VT7870, follow these steps:

|  | Step by Step Procedure Connect the RJ45 connector on the VT7870 with a network adapter of the computer. Open the Vector GreenPHY Configurator. Wait until the scan is completed. Flash the firmware to the current delivery version, if necessary. Configure the chip to act as EVSE (or as EV respectively). Start the measurement in real mode. The charge point node now answers SLAC (Ethernet) requests from the external device with corresponding response messages. Also, a UDP message pair (SECC Discovery) is exchanged. |

|  | Cross Reference For a detailed description of the configuration tool and its functionality, see the help document which can be opened using the tool’s Help button. |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [SmartCharging/SCProcedures] SCStartingV2GCommunication


# Starting the V2G Communication

SmartCharging » Procedures » Starting the V2G Communication (Smart Charging)

## Advancing to the V2G phase

With the steps in the previous sections, the basics configuration has been finished. It is now possible to initiate communication, starting with the SLAC process.

However,to reach the higher layer of communication (Vehicle2Grid TP), which controls the actual charging progress, additional steps may need to be performed. One reason is that the progression from the SLAC phase to the SECC Discovery phase depends on the successful establishment of a PLC link between the two HomePlug Green PHY chips, as denoted by the variable link status.

## Link status handling (Charge Point)

When setting up a charge point, as described in the previous steps, the link status is needed only for information purposes, or to implement custom handling. Polling, as described below, may be implemented but the simulation itself does not react on the result. However, no further messages will be received until the link is successfully established.

## Link status handling (Vehicle)

When settings up vehicle, the simulation DLL needs to be notified about the link establishment. Once it is aware that link status = 1, it will start sending the SECC Discovery Request. One of the following methods must be implemented.

## Polling

Using link status polling, an existing HomePlug Green PHY chip can be queried automatically for the link status.

- In the configuration f

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Test] TestCasePreconditions


# Preconditions

Test » Preconditions

Test modules and test cases may contain sections with preconditions and abort conditions.

## Preconditions

Preconditions are checked before the test execution starts.

- If the precondition is not fulfilled, the test group/case will not be executed — this includes the associated sections of preparation and finalization. The verdict will not be altered.

- If the precondition is fulfilled, the test group/case will be executed.

The preparation step is shows up as a separate section in the test report.

Test cases that haven't been executed are marked as skipped in the test report and are displayed deactivated in the Execution dialog.

## Implementation

- CAPL The implementation has to be coded by the user.

- .NET The implementation has to be coded by the user.

- XML See XML file – precondition and break condition.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |

Testing in terms of "does my CAPL program what I want it to do" can be found under the keyword CAPL Programming. Testing in the diagnostic tester region can be found under the keyword Diagnostics. |

### [Test] TestServiceLibrary


# Test Service Library (TSL)

Test » Test Service Library

| CANoe can be used to monitor message cycle times, the reaction time that an ECU needs to send an answering message upon receipt of a message, the validity of signal values in messages etc. You can make deductions about the quality of the control devices being tested using test result statistics, such as the number of deviations reported during the test period. CANoe makes special functions (checks) available for these tests, which are consolidated in the Test Service Library. They can be used in CAPL and XML test modules as well as in test units. Test specification details are partially drawn from the CANdb++ database, in which data such as target values can be stored. Thanks to this functionality, a minimal amount of test code is needed. The Test Service Library also includes functions which make it easier to stimulate control devices (stimulus functions). It is possible, for example, to use these functions to generate different signal forms. |

| Basics/Concept Introduction to the Test Service Library (TSL) Functionality of TSL functions Glossary | Basics/Concept | Introduction to the Test Service Library (TSL) Functionality of TSL functions Glossary | Implementation TSL in XML test modules TSL in CAPL test modules TSL in CAPL simulation nodes Precision of the timers | Implementation | TSL in XML test modules TSL in CAPL test modules TSL in CAPL simulation nodes Precision of the timers | Checks Overview of checks

### [Test] TestTips


# Tips & Tricks

Test » Tips&Tricks

|  | Tip:Editor for XML test modules The editor for XML test modules can be chosen in the Options dialog. Tip:Editor for .NET test modules The editor for .NET test modules can be chosen in the Options dialog. Tip:Subsequent generation of HTML test reports from XML test reports Test reports in the XML format can subsequently be converted into HTML. For that purpose the XSLT processor has to be called manually on command line level. sabcmd <XSLT File> <XML File> <HTML File> Thereby the HTML file is generated from the given XML file by using the XSLT stylesheet.Example: sabcmd file://report\onepage.xslt file:///d:\project\report.XML file:///d:\project\report.html The sabcmd application (the XSLT processor) is located in the exec32 directory of your CANoe installation.Please take care of the correct path entries – be sure to use "file://" for relative and "file:///" for absolute paths. There are also complex XSLT stylesheets such as extendedNavigation that entail further operations in addition to the basic XSLT transformations, as for example copying additionally needed files. For further information and hints on how to use these stylesheets manually, please refer to the text file of the same name which is located in the directory of the stylesheet and the comments in the first lines of the stylesheets. Tip:Avoiding rounding errors When working with floating-point numbers (e.g. with according signals) you always have to work with relation com

### [Test] TestTroubleshooting


# Troubleshooting

Test » Troubleshooting

- Warning in the Write Window: "Test group was not closed – please use the function TestGroupEnd."A test group was opened in the test module via the TestGroupBegin function, but not closed via TestGroupEnd. The opened test group will be closed automatically at the end of the test module.

- Warning in the Write Window: "Report information block missing for transfer of information pair with TestReportAddMiscInfo."An attempt was made to write an information pair to the report with TestReportAddMiscInfo, but no supplemental information block was created in which the information pair could be stored. Therefore a suitable supplemental information block was created automatically.

- DTD not foundThe DTD file specified in an XML file should normally be located in the same directory as the XML file. If this is not the case, the DTD will not be found when the XML is read, which will lead to an error, for example when attempting to load an XML test module or when converting an XML logging file.

- Failure of tests due to rounding errorsFloating-point values are sometimes used, especially for the representation of physical measurement values. If floating-point values are transmitted as signals or generated from signals in the database on the basis of computational specifications that demand floating-point values, there are likely to be rounding errors. For this reason, such values should never be tested for equality to another value, but alway

[...truncated for brevity...]

# Variants

Test » Variants

A single test module may contain multiple variants of a test. Hence there's no need to create multiple test modules for ECUs that only differ in details. For each variant you can define the test cases that have to be executed and how the test cases differ in detail.

## Implementation

- CAPL The implementation has to be coded by the user.

- .NET The implementation has to be coded by the user.

- XML See XML test module variants.

Modularization

### [Test/TestFeatureSet] TFSCompatibilityCANoe50


# Compatibility with CANoe v5.0

Test Feature Set » Compatibility

With CANoe version 5.1, a few changes were made to the behavior of the test functionality that are not completely backward-compatible with CANoe version 5.0:

- Constraint injuries no longer abort the current wait point; instead, they are only reported in the report and cause a failure of the test case (verdict fail).

In most cases these changes have no effects on existing CAPL test programs. However, should these be based on the abortion of wait conditions for constraint injuries, the default behavior can be converted to the CANoe 5.0 behavior with the following entry in the CAN.INI file:

[TestFeatureSet]ConstraintViolationDefaultMode=4

We recommend the conversion of existing programs to the new behavior since thus queries for constraint injuries after all wait conditions are no longer necessary and the programs thus become simpler.

Constraints and conditions (CAPL)

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Test/TestFeatureSet] TFSConstraintsConditions


# Constraints and Conditions

Test Feature Set » Constraints and Conditions

Using constraints and conditions you can check the adherence to conditions in a particular time interval. These checks occur parallel to the execution of the test case.

- Constraints are used to monitor the test environment/configuration. They can ensure that the test environment/configuration does not assume a state during the test that hinders correct test execution and thus leads to a possibly-incorrect test result.

- Conditions are used to check the system to be tested during the test procedure. This must often adhere to conditions that may not be injured during the test procedure. If these conditions are injured, then there is a failure of the test item and the test fails. See also Testing with conditions.

There is no functional difference between constraints and conditions regarding the verdict.

Any number of constraints and conditions for parallel monitoring may be used.

- In XML test modules always complete test cases, test groups or test modules are monitored.

- In CAPL test modules/test units Constraints and Conditions may be added and be removed from the active monitoring. This can occur for several test cases together on the level of the test module/test unit or within test cases. In the latter case, however, the monitoring is ended automatically with the end of the test case.

If a constraint or condition is injured, this is noted in the test report and it causes failure of the cu

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Test/TestFeatureSet] TFSIntroduction


# Introduction to Testing with CANoe

Test Feature Set » Introduction

You can implement existing test scenarios that are usually sequential with a linear flow. This can be done for test modules (CAPL/.NET/XML) and test units. The former purely event-oriented execution paradigm has been expanded to include a sequential execution model for test modules / test units. The test procedures of the individual test modules / test units are executed in parallel, while the flow within each test module / test unit is executed sequentially.

Each test module/test unit has a beginning (start of the test sequence) and an end (end of the test procedure). Outside of the test run time, there is the singular initialization phase on prestart that will be called on start of measurement. All other handlers will be called only, if the test module/test unit is currently running. Test modules, test units, and whole test configurations may be executed several times in succession during a measurement. The other familiar event procedures, which previously informed a CAPL node about the start and end of the measurement (on start, on end), are unnecessary in test module / test unit, because their functionality can be added simply to the test module sequence itself and are therefore unavailable.

The variables are set to their initial states at the beginning of the test module / test unit. The contents of a variable cannot — by definition — be transferred from one test procedure to another. Even variable

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Test/TestFeatureSet] TFSJenkinsTestController


# Jenkins as CANoe Test Controller

Test » Test Feature Set (TFS) » Jenkins as CANoe Test Controller

»Jenkins is a famous continuous integration and delivery platform.

Using the CANoe COM Interface you are able to integrate CANoe jobs into your Jenkins infrastructure.

The following text provides basic information and hints on how to do so. It also provides a simple step-by-step example.

|  | Important Note For licensing reasons, the test setup described here is only permitted when testing real hardware! |

This page focuses on Jenkins. But the general ideas are applicable to other continuous integration systems. Since the tool integration is based on simple operating system mechanisms, you can easily adapt the approach to other tools.

## Integration Strategies

For several use cases a binary decision might be sufficient. This can be achieved by a simple batch script returning errorlevel information. This information can be used to decide whether the Jenkins job was successful. You can specify additional files produced by the simulation such as test reports and logging files to get a detailed view of the simulation result. The general result as well as the additional files can be directly accessed through the Jenkins web interface.

If your simulation is running tests, Jenkins provides a more specific view on JUnit test results. To benefit from this feature you can provide a simple transformation from CANoe test results to JUnit test results.

The following sections prov

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Test/TestFeatureSet] TFSSignalOrientedAccess


# Signal Oriented Access

Test Feature Set » Signal Oriented Access

CANoe offers different ways to access network communications via signal abstraction.The following overview illustrates the different access options:

## Receive Direction

- Signals can be displayed directly in panels; for this purpose, no more environment variables are required

- In XML test modules signal values can be accessed directly from several test functions, control functions and checks.

- In CAPL test modules, signal values are queried and/or compared with test functions as compared to a specified interval.If the test result (verdict) should be influenced automatically, then special test functions can be used.

- GetSignal

- CheckSignalInRange

- CheckSignalMatch

- TestValidateSignalInRange

- TestValidateSignalOutsideRange

- TestValidateSignalMatch

- In simulation nodes, signal values can be queried directly and compared with a specified interval.

- GetSignal

- CheckSignalInRange

- CheckSignalMatch

## Send Direction

Signals can be set directly by some sources (panels, test modules), insofar as "signal drivers" are available for these. Currently CANoe interaction layer or CAPL callbacks can be used as signal drivers.

- The CANoe interaction layer can be used as signal driver for all Tx signals of a node. For this use, the included CANoe interaction layer component is available.

- A CAPL callback can be used as signal driver for a single signal. This callback has to be registered as si

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Test/TestFeatureSet] TFSStandaloneMode


# Test Feature Set: Standalone Mode

Test » Test Feature Set (TFS) » Standalone Mode

## Notes on Standalone Operation

Test Modules in the Simulation Setup as well as Test Units can also be used in standalone mode with certain functional restrictions.

The following features are currently not supported:

- User dialogs for confirmation and entries

- Replay functionality

- SysCall functionality

- Window Capture

Execution options that cause pausing the test module are deactivated due to the fact that no execution dialog is available.

It is recommended to use the CAPL function TestAcquireStatusLED to view the verdict and the current status of the test module.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Test/TestFeatureSet] TFSTestModuleConfiguration


# Test Module Configuration Dialog

Test Feature Set » Test Module » Configuration

Context Menu: Configuration…

You open the XML Test Modul Configuration dialog via the Test Setup for Test Modules window with a right click on a test module and the context menu command Configuration....

Here you can configure a test module in the Simulation Setup or in the Test Setup for Test Modules.

## Common

Enter the name of the test module into the Module name field. To prevent mistakes, <<default>> is set as the default module name. Click [File…] to open a file request dialog in which you can select the file (CAPL or XML) that describes the functionality of the test module.

Use [Edit] to open the CAPL browser or XML editor with which you can edit the selected program.

All CAPL programs must be compiled before a measurement is started. You can do this either by clicking [Compile] or by selecting Compile All Nodes on the Environment Ribbon Tab.

This is not necessary for XML files. Use [Compile] to start the integrated XML parser, to read the XML file or to display potential errors.

Use the State on/State off options to activate/deactivate the test module. In the test setup, you can use the check box in front of each line to do this.

Use Start to define when the test module is to be started:

- Immediately after start of measurement.

- When the defined environment variable switches to a value unequal to 0 during measurement.

- When the defined system variable switches to a valu

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Test/TestFeatureSet] TFSTestModuleExecution


# Test Module Execution

Test Feature Set » Test Module » Execution

Test modules can be started interactively or automatically:

- The following variants for starting test modules are suitable for automated execution:

- Starting the test module via "Start measurement"

- Starting the test module with a modified environment variable

- Starting the test module via key combination

- Starting the test module as part of a sequence of test modules in the test setup

These variants can be specified in the Configuration dialog or by the placement of the test module in the test setup.

- The following variants for starting test modules are suitable for interactive execution:

- In the Execution dialog (recommended).The Execution dialog is opened by double-clicking on the test module or via the test module context menu.

- In the Simulation Setup: via the button on the module.

- In the test setup: via the context menu.

In the Execution dialog for XML test modules you can specify which test cases are to be executed.

|  | Note The execution of a test module is documented in the Write Window(e.g., the start and end of the execution of a test case; test case and test module results). The start and end of test case are also displayed in the Trace Window.This enables you to quickly gain an overview of which activities belonged to a test case. The execution of a test module is documented in the Test Report.The test report can be configured separately for each test module. |

Test Modu

### [Test/TestFeatureSet] TFSTestModuleExecutionDialog


# Test Module Execution Dialog

Test Feature Set » Test Module » Execution » Execution Dialog

Context Menu: Execution…

You can use this dialog to control the execution of test modules.The dialog can be accessed via the test module's context menu or by double-clicking the test module.

As long as the test module is not executed the XML test module provides a context menu to select the test cases to be executed. It contains the possibility to invert the current selection as well as the possibility to select all test cases with result passed or failed. The actions refer to the currently selected element. So you can easily choose e.g. all failed test cases of a test group of the previous execution. The current selection outside this context of the selected element remains untouched.

|  | Note The execution dialogs for CAPL and XML test modules differ slightly, because the test modules formulated in CAPL contain a CAPL routine (MainTest) which controls the execution. For this reason, it is not possible to manually select the test cases to be executed. Furthermore for XML test modules with variants an additional box is shown on top of the Test Cases page where you can select the active variant. |

## Context Menu

- Expand all The selected branch (with sub-branches) will be expanded.

- Collapse all The selected branch (with sub-branches) will be collapsed.

- Selection

- Select all "failed" test cases All failed test cases of the selected branch (with sub-branches) will be se

### [Test/TestFeatureSet] TFSTestModuleExecutionDialogOptions


# Test Module Execution Dialog Options

Test Feature Set » Test module » Execution » Execution Dialog » Options

Context Menu: Execution…

You can set various options for execution of test modules here:

## Test Execution

- A test module can be executed several times in succession (XML test modules only).You can define the number and duration of the test runs. If you restrict the duration to a certain amount of time (days, hours, minutes) the current test run will be executed completely even if the specified duration already ended.Regardless of repetitive execution only one test report will be generated for all test runs, in which the single test sequences are marked.If a test module – that has been configured for repetitive execution – has finished, the verdict remains unchanged if a complete test run has been done. This means that effective with the 2nd test run a test module may be stopped manually without getting the verdict failed.

- Execution in random order (XML test modules only).With this option the test cases will be executed in random order instead of a sequential processing.The structure within the test module remains as it is given by the test group. This means that randomizing the test cases only happens within a test group. Therefore Preparation and Completion sections remain effective for the same part of the test module. Furthermore all test cases activated in the execution dialog will be executed exactly once.In the test report all test cases will appear 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Test/TestFeatureSet] TFSTestModuleExecutionSequential


# Test Module: Sequential Execution of Several Test Modules

Test Feature Set » Test Module » Sequential Execution of Several Test Modules

Context Menu of a Test Environment: Test Module: Sequential Execution of Several Test Modules

As a rule, tests that belong together should be stored in a single test module. The test cases for different test areas can be arranged hierarchically as test groups within the test module. The test cases in a test module are executed sequentially and the results are recorded in a test report.

## Starting Several Test Modules

In certain cases, there may be test modules that cannot be combined in a single test module. To avoid having to start each test module separately, CANoe offers the option of storing test modules in a given folder and, within certain limits, treating them as a unit.

By selecting Execute test modules sequentially from the context menu for this folder in the test setup it is possible to start the execution of all the test modules during a measurement. In this case, all the test modules in this folder are executed sequentially according to the order in which they are sorted in the folder. Deactivated test modules (check box in test setup) are skipped.

|  | Important Note This menu command is only available when the measurement is running. Furthermore, the folder must contain only CAPL and XML test modules and must not include any subfolders. |

The individual test modules in this folder remain independent of each other. Th

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Test/TestFeatureSet] TFSTestModuleGenerationCANdb


# Generate Test Modules With CANdb++

Test Feature Set » Generate Test Modules With CANdb++

You can start the test module generator from CANdb++:

- For all nodes of the database:CANdb++ menu path: File|Plug-Ins|Test Generation for whole Database (Screenshot)

- For selected nodes:context menu of the selected node in CANdb++: Test Generation for selected Nodes (Screenshot)

## Generating Test Modules from CANdb++ Network Files

Tests can automatically be extracted from network files. Some of these tests act global for all nodes, others are set up specific to selected nodes.

In the test module generator you can select either to generate the tests in an XML test module or in a CAPL test module. Depending on the use case the generated file can directly be assigned to a corresponding XML or CAPL test module. In some cases even a partial usage of the generated test module might be useful.

The following CANdb++ attributes are used for generating the test modules:

- GenMsgSendType

- GenMsgCycleTime

- GenMsgDelayTime

Global acting tests:

- Checking whether all messages received in the network are defined in the database

- Checking whether Error Frames occur

For the messages sent by the selected nodes:

- Checking the minimum allowed send distanceIs only generated if the message attribute GenMsgDelayTime is defined and not equal 0.

- Checking the maximum allowed send distanceThis test will only be generated if a cycle time GenMsgCycleTime is defined as not equal 0.This che

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Test/TestFeatureSet] TFSTestModuleSetup


# Creating a Test Module

Test Feature Set » Test Module

A test module (XML, .NET or CAPL) is inserted into the Test Setup for Test Modules via the context menu. By default, the test module is assigned to all buses (see notes on multibus environment). You can assign the test module to specific buses in the test module configuration dialog.

Test modules can also be inserted into the Simulation Setup via the context menu of the simulated bus. However, it is preferable to insert test modules during test setup, in order to keep a clear separation between tests and simulations.

Special configuration options are available for test modules. These can be viewed and edited using the test module's context menu.

The execution of test modules can be controlled using a dialog, which can be accessed via the test module's context menu or by double-clicking the test module.

Schematic representation for test integration:

## Editing Test Modules

In the Options dialog (External Programs|Tools), you specify which program is to be used as the XML editor. In many cases, the test modules are generated in XML by a generator or by a generator from existing specifications. Nevertheless, it makes sense to enter an XML editor so that the XML files can still be changed manually.

You can then open the XML file in the selected editor using the context menu of an XML test module.

Structure of XML test modules | Structure of CAPL test modules | Configuration of the test report | Configuration of th

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Test/TestFeatureSet] TFSTestWithConditions


# Test with Conditions

Test Feature Set » Conditions

The functions of the Test Feature Set for CANoe permit the setup of tests according to different test strategies. The conditions are conceived as aids for tests according to the following pattern: for the system to be tested, conditions are formulated that the system must adhere to at all times (or at least in a defined period of time). These conditions are monitored parallel to the execution of the test procedure. In the test procedure, a series of actions are executed (for example, a type "vehicle cycle"). The test fails if the condition was injured at any time during the test execution.

With this type of test it is not necessarily required that test conditions are queried actively in the test sequences. The test cycles can be programmed as pure system usage (stimulus), while the test conditions to be checked are formulated as conditions.

|  | Example A transmission control system sends a signal cyclically that communicates the gear used. The cycle time of the signal may be subject to only small fluctuations even under load; larger fluctuations indicate a problem in the control unit. For the test, a condition is set that describes the reliable cycle time of the relevant status message. The cycle time must adhere to the limits specified in the condition at all times. cycleCheck = ChkStart_MsgRelCycleTimeViolation (GearStatus, 0.8, 1.2)TestAddCondition (cycleCheck); For the actual test execution, various sequences are 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Test/TestFeatureSet] TFSWait


# Wait Points

Test Feature Set » Wait Points

## CAPL

Typically stimuli for the SUT are generated within the test case followed by waiting for the reactions. The expanded CANoe execution model for testing permits real waiting for the most important events:

- Receipt of a message with a specified ID

- Writing of an environment variable with a specified name

- Signaling of a user-defined text event

- Signaling of events from the supplied TestServiceLibrary

- Signaling of events from other Node Layer Modules

- Expiration of a time period, and

- AND / OR combinations of the above conditions

A wait point is exited (resume) once the specified condition or a timeout occurs.

The reason for a "resume" (i.e. the event that occurred) can be found in the return value of the wait instruction. If a message initiated the resume, the initiating message may be called in CAPL from immediately after the wait point until just before another wait point is executed.

## .NET

As in CAPL, it is possible to wait for various events in .NET test modules. There are wait points for:

- chronological conditions

- bus signals, environment variables and system variablesA value condition and timeout can also be specified.

- custom event procedures with and without timeouts

- composite conditions composed of the above two

|  | Note In .NET, wait points are resolved as soon as they are called if the condition is already met at that point in time. |

|  | Example of a custom wait point. So-call

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Test/TestModules/CAPL] CAPLConstraintsConditions


# Constraints and Conditions

Test Feature Set » Constraints and conditions » CAPL

Constraints and conditions are conditions, that are simultaneously being checked during the test sequence.

Any number of constraints and conditions for parallel monitoring may be added and they may be removed in any sequence from the active monitoring. This can occur for several test cases together on the level of the test module or within test cases. In the latter case, however, the monitoring is ended automatically with the end of the test case.

If a constraint or condition is injured, this is noted in the test log and it causes failure of the current test case (and thus failure of the entire test module). However, the test is not aborted. The other test cases of a test module are executed.

The state of the active constraints and conditions, that is whether or not these have already recognized an error, can be queried at any time in the test case with the help of the function TestCheckConstraint/TestCheckCondition. This way, it is possible to react purposefully to the failure of particular constraints/conditions should this be necessary sometime in exceptional situations.

Constraints and conditions can be defined easily with the functions (checks) of the Test Service Library.

|  | Example checkID = ChkStart_MsgRelCycleTimeViolation (StatusMsg, 0.8, 1.2);TestAddConstraint (checkID);..// cycle time of message StatusMsg will be checkedTestRemoveConstraint (checkID); |

Constraints and con

[...truncated for brevity...]

# Test Feature Set Sample Code

Test Feature Set» CAPL » Sample Code

MainTest(){

// ----- add information to test report

TestModuleTitle("Sample Tests"); // change test module title and add descriptionTestModuleDescription("This test module calls some test cases to demonstrate ");TestModuleDescription("the test features of CANoe.");TestReportAddSUTInfo("Serial No.", "A012345BC"); // write info about SUT into reportTestReportAddSUTInfo("Manufactured", "2003-10-02");TestReportAddEngineerInfo("Test Engineer", "S. Grey"); // add info about engineer to report

// ----- call test case

ConfigureSUT_Testmode();}testcase ConfigureSUT_Testmode(){long lRet; // return value for the Test...() functionsMessage ModeStatusRequest lRequest; // create message container for the requestMessage 0x0 lMsg; // create a message container for the responsdword lCheckCycleTimeId; // id for the cycle time check

// ----- add information to test report

TestCaseDescription("Test case is used to check the ability of the SUT to switch ");TestCaseDescription("into test mode."); // add description to test case

// ----- define cycle time accuracy and add condition

lCheckCycleTimeId = ChkStart_MsgRelCycleTimeViolation(ECUStatusMsg, 0.8, 1.2); TestAddCondition(lCheckCycleTimeId);

// ----- test sequence

lRequest.sig = 1; // perform stimulus that results in a high CPU load for the ECUoutput(lRequest);TestStepPass("010.1", "Request sent to the SUT, sig = 1"); // create entry in report file

lRet = TestWait

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Test/TestModules/CAPL] CAPLTestModuleStructure


# CAPL Test Module Structure

Test Feature Set » CAPL » Test Module

A test module consists of:

- Any desired number of test cases, and

- test flow control

The execution of test cases is typically recorded by test steps. These test steps help the user to understand the test sequence. It is advisable to subdivide test cases into finely differentiated test steps.

Test cases are qualified by the test case keyword in CAPL. Test cases cannot be called by other test cases.

Test flow control is implemented in the central CAPL function MainTest. In the simplest case this flow control merely consists of calls of test cases to be executed.

The TestControl and TestCases nodes may be accessed from the tree view of the CAPL Browser.

The PreStart handler of a test module is called once upon measurement start and can be used to configure hardware devices.

|  | Note Upon test module start all CAPL variables are reset. Settings done in the PreStart step of a test module are lost and should be done in the main function instead. |

Alternatively you can create test modules in XML.

Creating a test module | Test module configuration | Test module: XML structure | Glossary

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Test/TestModules/NET] NETCheck


# .NET Checks

Test Feature Set » .NET Checks

Checks in .NET test modules are used to monitor certain criteria during a test sequence. Checks can be implemented as constraints, conditions and now also as observations. In contrast to constraints or conditions, observations cannot influence the verdict of a test module. Aside from this, the test report contains the same exact information.

Checks are typically implemented for an entire test module or for individual test cases. However, they can also be activated/deactivated during a test sequence using method calls.

The Vector.CANoe.TFS.ValueCheck class is used to monitor the value criteria of bus signals, environment variables and system variables. It is parameterized directly with the parameters to be monitored, and if needed, the value conditions and check type.

[TestCase("Title", "Description")]public void ObserveLightState(){ ICheck lightsOn = new ValueCheck<LightState.OnOff>(CheckType.Condition, 1); // Now the condition is activated lightsOn.Activate(); // Wait for a specified time and observe the criterion Vector.CANoe.Threading.Execution.Wait(4000); // Now the condition is deactivated lightsOn.Deactivate();}

The Vector.CANoe.TFS.Check class is used to monitor custom criteria. It is parameterized with the event procedure, and if needed, the check type. The same monitoring as shown above can be achieved using the following code.

[Criterion][OnChange(typeof(LightState.OnOff))]public bool AreLightsOn(){ return LightSt

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Test/TestModules/NET] NETDebugging


# .NET Debugging

Test » Test Feature Set » .NET Debugging

## Overview

The .NET debugging support integrated in CANoe helps you debug test modules that contain .NET code. It simplifies the process by automating actions that you need to execute frequently. This includes starting Visual Studio, loading relevant source files, and starting and initializing the debugger. For remote debugging, this also includes starting the remote debugging monitor on the RT System and automatically starting up Visual Studio with the correct user rights.

In the Options dialog you can configure .NET debugging.

## Notes on Remote Debugging

If you're using an RT System (such as the VT6000), you need to use remote debugging instead of normal debugging. The process is the same as for local debugging, except that you need to take a number of steps at the start to set up the system. These are described in VT6000: Remote Debugging.

### Installing the Remote Debugging Monitor

To enable automatic starting of the RT System's remote debugging monitor via CANoe, you have to ensure that the remote debugging monitor is in the correct folder. The folder name is:

- D:\Tmp\msvsmon\2003\ for Visual Studio 2003

- D:\Tmp\msvsmon\2005\ for Visual Studio 2005

- …

This means you can install different versions of the remote debugging monitor in parallel if you are debugging with several Visual Studio versions.

### Location of test module source files

Because a local version of Visual Studio needs to run unde

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Test/TestModules/NET] NETTestModule


# .NET Test Module

Test Feature Set » .NET Test Module

CANoe comes with two kinds of test modules:

- Unstructured modules: Test cases are not shown in the Test Execution dialog until the module is running.These modules use the TestModule base class and implement the Main() function.

- Structured modules: The structure of the test module is analyzed during compilation, and the test groups and test cases are displayed in the Test Execution dialog.The big advantage here is that you can deactivate test cases and test groups before running the module.Structured modules use the StructuredTestModule base class and implement the StructuredMain() function.

To program a .NET test module, you need to specify a .NET file as the source file for the test module. If this file does not exist yet and you then access the Edit function, a compilable file containing an unstructured .NET test module skeleton is automatically created. The recommended programming language is C#.

To create a structured module, replace TestModule with StructuredTestModule and Main with StructuredMain().

//Skeleton of a .NET test module using System;using Vector.Tools;using Vector.CANoe.Runtime;using Vector.CANoe.Threading;using Vector.Diagnostics;using Vector.Scripting.UI;using Vector.CANoe.TFS;using NetworkDB;public class test : TestModule{ public override void Main() { }}

Detailed information about the .NET test module structure can be found here.

|  | Note For the migration from .NET2 API to .NET4 API th

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Test/TestModules/NET] NETTestModuleCallCAPLTestCaseFunction


# Call CAPL Test Case / CAPL Test Function

Test Feature Set » .NET » CAPL Test Cases / CAPL Test Functions

You can assign CAPL files to the .NET test module as libraries in the Test module configuration dialog. All CAPL test functions and test cases can be called from within the .NET test module.

|  | Note All CAPL libraries under the Libraries page in Test module configuration dialog will be added as references to the VS project, as long as they were configured before the creation of the project file. If you added a test library you need to add the reference manually in your .NET solution. Alternatively (if you have configured a .NET source file in CANoe) you can delete the solution and project files – they will be regenerated with correct references when you start editing the .NET source from CANoe. |

Not all CAPL features are available in .NET (this especially concerns bus system specifics) but with CAPL test libraries you can easily access the complete CAPL functionality.

CAPL libraries that are assigned to the .NET test module are structured like CAPL test modules, but they must not contain a MainTest() routine. Event procedures are only called during runtime of the .NET test module. Thereby all event procedures of the assigned CAPL libraries are considered. There is no certain test flow and – like in CAPL test modules – the event procedures on start and on stop are not available.

CAPL test cases and test functions may use parameter types "char, char[], byte, int,

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Test/TestModules/NET] NETTestModuleStructure


# .NET Test Module Structure

Test Feature Set » .NET test module » Structure

Every .NET test module consists of several test cases and one test sequence control. The Main or StructuredMain function constitutes the test sequence control. In the simplest case, this merely consists of several test case calls. The test cases are always executed sequentially.

Test case execution is typically documented by means of test steps. The Vector.CANoe.TFS.Report class offers several functions for this purpose. Test steps can influence the verdict of a test module and, as with CAPL and XML test modules, are included in the report.

Test cases can be grouped together by arranging them into test groups. The test groups themselves can contain other test groups. These test groups can only be used for the test sequence control, and not within test cases.

Test cases are identified in .NET test modules by means of a method attribute; i.e., this attribute can be used to declare any function as a test case.

[TestCase("Title", "Description")]public void MyTestCase(){ // do something}

The following makes it possible to use one test case several times with different titles and descriptions:

[TestCase()]public void MyTestCase(String title, String description){ Vector.CANoe.TFS.Report.TestCaseTitle(title); Vector.CANoe.TFS.Report.TestCaseDescription(description); // do something}

## Special characteristics of structured test modules

The structure of a test module can be analyzed by executing a 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Test/TestModules/NET] NETTestPattern


# .NET Test Pattern

Test Feature Set » .NET Test Pattern

It is possible to declare a test pattern in .NET test modules. Attributes are used to parametrize the test pattern and a method used to execute it repeatedly at different times.

Test patterns can only be executed within test cases. A test pattern can influence the verdict of a test module.

Parameter semantics are specified within a test pattern by means of an attribute.

public class MyStateChange : StateChange{ public MyStateChange() { Wait = 100; } [Input(typeof(LightSwitch.OnOff))] public double lightSwitch = 1; [Expected(typeof(LightState.OnOff), Relation.Equal)] public double lightState = 1;}

The Input attribute is used to set a parameter to a specific value.

The Expected attribute is used to check whether a parameter contains a defined value.

General Notes on .NET Programming | Event Procedures | .NET API Functional Description | .NET Checks

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Test/TestModules/XML] XMLConstraintsConditions


# Constraints and Conditions

Test Feature Set » Constraints and Conditions » XML Test Module

Constraints and conditions are conditions that are checked parallel to the execution of a test case, all test cases of a test group or a complete test module. These conditions are specified in the test module, in the test group or in the test case in one block apiece:

<constraints> List of test conditions</constraints>

<conditions> List of test conditions</conditions>

You can use the test conditions of the Test Service Library.

Structure of the XML file | Notes on writing XML Files | XML sample file

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Test/TestModules/XML] XMLDeclareParameters


# Declaration and Transfer of CAPL Test Case and Test Function Parameters

Test Features » XML » Declaration and Transfer of CAPL Test Case and Test Function Parameters

The usage of CAPL test cases and CAPL test functions is described on the following pages:

- Call CAPL test cases

- Call CAPL test functions

Here you can find some examples for the declaration and transfer of parameters:

|  | Integers (byte, word, dword, int, long, qword, int64) Note: All CAPL integer types are referred to as int in XML. testcase TC1(long value){ $signal = value; // the value of the signal is set to value} <capltestcase name="TC1"> <caplparam type="int" name="value">50</caplparam></capltestcase> testfunction TF1(long value){ $signal = value; // the value of the signal is set to value} <capltestfunction name="TF1"> <caplparam type="int" name="value">60</caplparam></capltestfunction> |

|  | Floating point numbers (float, double) Note: All CAPL floating point types are referred to as float in XML. testcase TC2(float value){ $signal = value; // the value of the signal is set to value} <capltestcase name="TC2"> <caplparam type="float" name="value">44.4</caplparam></capltestcase> testfunction TF2(float value){ $signal = value; // the value of the signal is set to value} <capltestfunction name="TF2"> <caplparam type="float" name="value">55.5</caplparam></capltestfunction> |

|  | String (char []) testcase TC3(char title[]){ TestCaseTitle("TC3", title); // ...} <capltestcase name="TC3"> <caplpar

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Test/TestModules/XML] XMLFileCallCAPLTestCase


# Call CAPL Test Case

Test Feature Set » XML » CAPL Test Cases

You can assign CAPL files to the XML test module as libraries in the Test Module Configuration dialog. Test functions called from the XML test module can be implemented in these CAPL files.

The CAPL test case libraries assigned here are structured like CAPL test modules, but they must not contain a MainTest routine. The call of event procedures is only performed during the runtime of the XML test module to which the CAPL file is assigned to as a library. Thereby all event procedures of the assigned CAPL libraries are considered. There is no independent test course and – like in CAPL test modules – the event procedures on start and on stop are not available. The test cases contained therein can only be called from the XML test module.

The user of such an XML test module will not see the difference between test cases that are implemented in CAPL or XML. CAPL test cases can, in this case, also be deactivated via the user interface.

|  | Example A test case programmed in CAPL is referenced in an XML test module in the following manner: <capltestcase name="CAPL name of CAPL test case" title="Displayed name of the CAPL test case" ident="ID of the test case, e.g. number"> <caplparam name="Parameter name" type="float|int|string|signal|envvar|sysvar">Parameter value</caplparam> ...</capltestcase> The name attribute indicates the test case name, used in the CAPL program. The title attribute is optional; with this attr

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Test/TestModules/XML] XMLFileCallCAPLTestFunction


# Call CAPL Test Function

Test Feature Set » XML » Call CAPL Test Functions

In the test module configuration dialog, CAPL files can be assigned to the XML test module as libraries. Test functions called from the XML test module can be implemented in these CAPL files.

Event procedures are only called during the runtime of the XML test module to which this CAPL file has been assigned as a library. All event procedures are taken into account in the assigned CAPL libraries. There is no dedicated test run; also, like in CAPL test modules, the on start and on stop event procedures are not available. The included test functions can only be called by the XML test module.
