Like test patterns, CAPL test functions are used within a <testcase> or in the <preparation> or <completion> sections. A test function programmed in CAPL is referenced in an XML test module as follows:

<testcase title=" Name of test case" ident="ID of test case"> ... <capltestfunction name="CAPL name of test function" title="Display name for CAPL test function"> <caplparam name="Parameter name" type="float|int|string|signal|envvar|sysvar">Parameter value</caplparam> </capltestfunction> ...</testcase>

- The name attribute indicates the name of the test function used in the CAPL program.

- The title attribute is optional; it can be used to give the test function a title which is used on the user interface and in the log instead of the CAPL name. This can be particularly useful if the same CAPL test function is 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Test/TestModules/XML] XMLFileCallNETTestCase


# Call .NET Test Case

Test Feature Set » XML » .NET Test Cases

In the test module configuration dialog, .NET assemblies can be added to the XML test module via the Modules tab. The assemblies contain test cases that can be called from the XML test module.

The test cases are defined as in .NET test modules, except that no Main function is required.

|  | Example using System;using Vector.Tools;using Vector.CANoe.Runtime;using Vector.CANoe.Threading;using Vector.CANoe.TFS;using NetworkDB;public class NETTestCaseLibrary{ [TestCase] public void SetSignal<T>(Double d) where T : Signal { RuntimeObject<T>.Value = d; }} |

In order to create the .NET test case libraries, you have to reference different assemblies. You need to include all the assemblies that are also required to develop complete test modules.

Event procedures are only active as long as the test case is active.

For the users of this type of XML test module there is no apparent difference between test cases in XML and .NET, as the .NET test cases can also be deactivated in the user interface.

The above test case programmed in .NET is referenced in an XML test module as follows:

<nettestcase name="SetSignal" title="TC 1" assembly="TestCaseLibrary" class=" NETTestCaseLibrary "> <netparam type="signal" name="T"> <cansignal name="WindowPosition" /> </netparam> <netparam type="float" name="d">1.1</netparam></nettestcase>

- The name attribute indicates the name of the test case used in the .NET program.

- The title 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Test/TestModules/XML] XMLFileCallNETTestFunction


# Call .NET Test Function

Test Feature Set » XML » Call .NET Test Function

In the test module configuration dialog, you can add .NET assemblies to the XML test module via the Modules tab. The assemblies contain test functions that can be called from the XML test module.

|  | How to Define Test Functions in .NET Test Modules: using System;using Vector.Tools;using Vector.CANoe.Runtime;using Vector.CANoe.Threading;using Vector.CANoe.TFS;using NetworkDB; public class NETTestFunctionLibrary{ [TestFunction] public void SetSignal<S>(int d) where S : Signal { RuntimeObject<S>.Value = d; }} |

In order to create the .NET test libraries, you need to reference the different assemblies.You need to include all the assemblies that are required to develop pure .NET test modules.

|  | How to Reference the Test Function Programmed in .NET Above in an XML Test Module: <testcase title="TC2" ident="ID2"> <nettestfunction name="SetSignal" title="Close the window"> <netparam name="S" type="signal"> <cansignal name="CyclicSignal" /> <netparam> <netparam type="int" name="d">0</netparam> </nettestfunction></testcase> The name attribute indicates the name of the test function used in the .NET program. The title attribute is mandatory.It can be used to give the test function a title for use in the user interface and the protocol. The class and assembly attributes are optional.They can be used to provide a unique description of the test function. Whole numbers, floating-point numbers, character str

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Test/TestModules/XML] XMLFileParameters


# Parameters

Test Feature Set » XML » Parameters

In the definition of checks (for constraints and conditions), variables, test functions and control functions, various value specifications and the description of messages, signals, and environment variables are required. These are always defined in the same way.

## Signals and Signal Values Are Defined As Follows:

<cansignal name="Signal name" msg="Message name" node="Node name" bus="Bus name">Value</cansignal><linsignal name="Signal name" msg="Message name" node="Node name" bus="Bus name">Value</linsignal><flexraysignal name="Signal name" frame="Frame name" node="Node name" bus="Bus name">Value</flexraysignal>

The specification of the associated message name, the node name or the bus name is only required if the signal name cannot be resolved uniquely by the incorporated CANdb databases.

The value itself can be specified by a corresponding number, variable, or an enum value. If sensible, when setting a signal value no relation is permitted, of course, the value can be described by a relation that is described more precisely by additional XML elements:

|  | Note Instead of Number an enumeration value of the database can be used. All given signal values are always physical values.Physical value = raw value * factor + offsetThe values for factor and offset are defined in the database. The signals, messages and environment variables stated as parameters actually have to be described in the related databases.This applies e

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Test/TestModules/XML] XMLFilePreconditionAssert


# Precondition and Break Condition

Test Feature Set » XML » Precondition and Break Condition

Test modules and test cases may contain a section on precondition and break condition.

## Precondition

The preconditions are checked before test execution.

If the preconditions are not fulfilled the test group or the test case respectively is not executed — including corresponding preparation and finalization step.

|  | Note The verdict is not changed. |

If the preconditions are fulfilled the test group or the test case respectively is executed. The preparation step is displayed as separate section in the test report.

Not executed test cases are visualized in the test report as skipped, as well as they were not activated in the Execution dialog.

The precondition is optional and can be implemented as following:

<precondition>CAN/LIN/FlexRay signal, environment variable, system variable, <vareval>, <and>, <or> or <xor> element</precondition>

It checks either a single value or it checks a list of values grouped by a conjunction.

## Break Condition

For the test sequence a special control function is used to abort the test case or the whole test module. As well as the precondition a single value or a list of values grouped by a conjunction can be checked thereby.

<assure terminate="testcase/testmodule" title="…"> CAN/LIN/FlexRay signal, environment variable, system variable, <vareval>, <and>, <or> or <xor> element</assure>

The title attribute has to be defined; the terminat

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Test/TestModules/XML] XMLFilePreparationFinalization


# Preparation and Finalization

Test Feature Set » XML » Preparation and Finalization

The test module, each test group and each test case may contain a section on preparation and finalization. Both are optional and can be implemented independently of one another.

<preparation> List of comments, variable definitions, system variables definitions, test functions and control functions</preparation>

<completion> List of comments, test functions and control functions</completion>

The variable definitions, test functions and control functions in the preparation section are always executed before the actual contents of the test module, test group or test case. Constraints and conditions defined in the same context (e.g., conditions defined in the same test case) will not apply yet. However, constraints and conditions defined in the surrounding contest (e.g., constraints defined for the parent test group) will apply.

|  | Note Variables can only be implemented in a preparation section, as they can only be defined and described there. Only system variables created in an XML test module can be changed by the XML test module. System variables can only be created in a preparatory step. |

The test functions and control functions in a finalization section are processed after the respective test module, test group or test case has been processed. Constraints and conditions that have been implemented in the same context will not be monitored at the same time as the preparation section

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Test/TestModules/XML] XMLFileStructure


# XML Test Module Structure

Test Feature Set » XML » Structure

The structure of the XML test module's XML file is formally described by an XML Schema, which is located in the CANoe installation directory as Exec32/testmodule.xsd.

Precisely one test module is described in an XML file. The file has the following basic structure:

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="iso-8859-1" standalone="yes"?><testmodule xmlns:xsi="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema-instance" xmlns="http://www.vector-informatik.de/CANoe/TestModule/1.15" xsi:schemaLocation="http://www.vector-informatik.de/CANoe/TestModule/1.15 testmodule.xsd" title="Test module title" version="Test module version"> <description>Test module description (optional)</description> Declaration of the variants (optional) Information about the test engineer, test setup and SUT for the test report (optional) Global definitions (optional) Preparation (optional) Definition of constraints (optional) Definition of conditions (optional) List of test groups and test cases Finalization (optional)</testmodule>

The schema declaration assigns the schema file testmodule.xsd to the XML file. This makes it possible to validate the XML file with an XML editor, in order to check that the file is complete and uses correct syntax. If you reference the Schema file testmodule.xsd without giving a path (see example above), the Schema file must be in the same directory as the XML file. You can copy the Schema file from the Exec32 directory of your CANoe insta

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Test/TestModules/XML] XMLFileTestCase


# Test Cases

Test Feature Set » XML » Test Cases

Test cases represent the central execution unit in test modules.There are two types of test cases:

- Test cases defined in XML:A sequence of Test Functions and Control Functions is being processed.For modeling the test series with the same sequence but different parameters you can use parameterized test cases.

- Test cases programmed in CAPL:The XML test module calls a CAPL test case (with parameter values) that has been programmed in CAPL.

|  | An XML test case has the following basic structure: <testcase ident="Identification of the test case, e.g. number" title="Title of the test case" version="Optional version name";> <description>Description of the test case (optional)</description> List of information blocks for the test report Precondition (optional) Preparation (optional) Definition of constraints (optional) Definition of conditions (optional) List of comments, test functions and control functions Finalization (optional)</testcase> |

The description of the test module by the <description> element is adopted into the test report, which can be created automatically while executing test modules. To obtain line breaks (in form of <br /> tags) in this description in CANoe Test Report Viewer or the HTML browser, "\n" may be inserted at any place in the text.

The list of information blocks for the test report corresponds to the information lines in the test module, however here only information blocks and extended info

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Test/TestModules/XML] XMLFileTestCaseParametrized


# Parameterized Test Cases

Test Feature Set » XML » Test Cases » Parameterized Test Cases

Test cases parameterized in XML can be used when a test module is to be used repeatedly for identical tests where the sequence of test functions/control functions executed does not vary, but the data used does (e.g. for checking different signals or comparisons with different must-values).

## Template For Parameterized Test Cases

A parametrizable test case consists of a test case template that specifies the sequence of test functions/control function to be executed along with a set of parameters (paramdef) to be used in the sequence. These templates for test cases are defined at the start of the test module in the optional templates section:

<testmodule title="Testmodul Title"> Test module preparation etc. <templates> <testcasetemplate name="Name of the test case template" version="optional version description"> <paramdef name="Name of the parameter" default="default value (optional)"/> Contents are identical to XML test cases </testcasetemplate> </templates> Test groups and test cases</testmodule>

Parameters defined via paramdef can be used at any given point in the test sequence, both as the content of XML elements and in their attributes. The parameter name is enclosed by $ signs in this case, as shown here:

<statecheck wait="100" title="check signal"> <expected> <cansignal name="$t2-signame$"> <eq>$t2-sigvalue$</eq> </cansignal> </expected></statecheck>

|  | Note Parameter n

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Test/TestModules/XML] XMLFileTestGroup


# Test Groups

Test Feature Set » XML » Test Groups

Test groups handle the logical grouping of test cases. Like test modules they contain some additional information that must essentially be forwarded to the test report.

A test group has the following basic structure:

<testgroup ident="identifier of the test group" title="title of the test group"> <description>Description of the Test Group (optional)</description> List of Information blocks for the test reportPrecondition (optional) Preparation (optional) Definition of constraints (optional) Definition of conditions (optional) List of test groups and test cases Finalization (optional)</testgroup>

The description of the test module by the <description> element is adopted into the test report, which can be created automatically while executing test modules. To obtain line breaks (in form of <br /> tags) in this description in CANoe Test Report Viewer or the HTML browser, "\n" may be inserted at any place in the text.

The list of information blocks for the test report corresponds to the information lines in the test module, however here only information blocks and extended info lines are permitted.

<miscinfo title="Title of the information block"> <info> <name>Name of a name/value pair in the information block</name> <description>Value specification for this name/value pair</description> </info> <info> ... </info></miscinfo>

<extendedinfo type="html">Character string to be taken over unchanged into the test report</exten

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Test/TestModules/XML] XMLTestModuleStructure


# XML Test Module Structure

Test Feature Set » XML » Structure

The use of text modules in XML is particularly expedient when a large number of test cases is to be generated from existing data. Since the XML files essentially contain the test parameters, the generators must simply convert the existing data into the required XML form. The fact that XML is a well-known and commonly used format, for which a large number of editors are available, is helpful in this regard.

The significant differences between the description of test modules in XML and in CAPL, in addition to the language used, are:

- In CAPL, test modules are formulated procedurally. That is, how a test case should be executed is programmed. This is very flexible, and in principle permits the definition of any given test procedure. Although the CAPL language is task-oriented and is generally quite simple, some programming experience is required to create more complex tests.

- Test modules are formulated descriptively in XML. That is, the XML file essentially contains information about what should be tested. The actual test procedures, that is the "how", are specified via test functions and control functions. The XML file contains the specifications required for the test functions/control functions, e.g., initial and target values, etc. The XML file therefore essentially parametrizes the tests.

The creation of a test module in XML is therefore easier, since the tester must only specify the required parameters

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Test/TestModules/XML] XMLTestModuleVariants


# Variants

Test Feature Set » XML » Variants

A single XML test module may contain several variants of one test. This makes it unnecessary, for example, to create separate XML files for ECUs that differ only in minor ways. For each variant, you can define which test cases are executed and how the test cases differ from one another.

## Declaration and use of variants in test modules

To use variants in a test module, you must first declare the variants at the start of the XML file; there are no "default" variants.

|  | Example 1 A certain test case of an existing test module shall only be executed in one variant, all other test cases always. This test case is now labeled as variant A. All other test cases don't need a variant label. Therefore the variants attribute has only to be added to this single test case. However, two variants have to be created: variant A, in which the test case is executed, and variant B, that includes all test cases without a particular label. In this case the test module doesn't need to use variant label B since this variant describes the "default" for all unlabeled test cases. <testmodule title="Test" version="1.0"> <variants> <variant name="A">Test with special test case</variant> <variant name="B">Test without special test case</variant> </variants> ... <testcase ident="72.1" title="Special test case" variants="A"> ... </testcase> ... <testcase ident="80.3" title="Other test cases"> ... </testcase> ...</testmodule> Example 2 <testmodule title=

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Test/TestModules/XML/StructuringElements] SECAPLTestCase


# CAPL Test Case

Structuring Elements » CAPL Test Case

| Name | CAPL Test Case |
| --- | --- |
| Description | Allows to reference test cases programmed in CAPL in a XML test module. |
| Remarks | CAPL test cases parametrize and invoke test cases that are implemented in CAPL test case libraries. CAPL files can be assigned to the XML test module as libraries in CANoe's test module configuration dialog. These CAPL files contain tests which can be called from within the XML test module. The CAPL test case libraries assigned here are structured like CAPL test modules, but they must not contain a MainTest routine. The call of event procedures is only performed during the run-time of the XML test module to which the CAPL file is assigned to as a library. Thereby all event procedures of the assigned CAPL libraries are considered. There is no independent test course and — like in CAPL test modules — the event procedures on start, on prestart and on stop are not available. The test cases contained therein can only be called from the XML test module. The user of such an XML test module does not see any difference between test cases that just a list of test pattern parameterizations in XML and test cases that are implemented in CAPL. CAPL test cases can, in this case, also be deactivated via the user interface. |
| Syntax | <capltestcase name=" CAPL name of CAPL test case" title=" Displayed name of the CAPL test case" ident=" ID of the test case, e.g. number" variants="A" > <caplpara

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Test/TestModules/XML/StructuringElements] SENETTestCase


# .NET Test Case

Structuring Elements » .NET Test Case

| Name | .NET Test Case |
| --- | --- |
| Description | Allows to reference test cases programmed in .NET in an XML test module. |
| Remarks | They allow to parametrize and invoke test cases that are programmed in .NET. In CANoe's Test Module Configuration dialog, .NET assemblies can be added to the XML test module via the Modules tab. The assemblies contain test cases that can be called from the XML test module. The test cases are defined as in .NET test modules, except that no Main function is required. For the users of this type of XML test module there is no apparent difference between test cases in XML and .NET, as the .NET test cases can also be deactivated in the user interface. |
| Syntax | <nettestcase name="SimpleTest" title="Title" title=" Displayed name of the CAPL test case" class=" NETTestCaseLibrary" assembly="TestCaseLibrary" " variants="A" > <netparam name="ParameterName" type="float|int|string|signal|envvar|sysvar"> Parameter Value </netparam></nettestcase> |
| Attributes | name (mandatory)The name attribute indicates the name of the test case used in the .NET program. title (optional)The attribute can be used to give the test case a title for use in the user interface and the protocol.This is particularly useful when the same .NET test case is called several times with different parameters from an XML test module. ident (optional)This attribute is used to assign a unique ID to a test case. This ID is

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Test/TestModules/XML/StructuringElements] SETestCase


# Test Case

Structuring Elements » Test Case

| Name | Test Case |
| --- | --- |
| Description | Test cases contain the actual instructions for test execution. |
| Remarks | Test cases contain the parametrization the of a test sequence. Test cases are conceptionally intended to work independently of other test cases. Test cases may be passed (pass) or failed (fail). This simple, two-value test result is called a verdict. A test case is considered passed if all specified validation steps have been done successfully. If any of the validation steps fails, the whole test case is failed. Generally the test case is finished after a verdict failed occurs, and the subsequent test cases will be started. Subsequent tests might run just fine with no reported errors and an explicit pass in the test report. A clear rule: Once an error has occurred during the execution of a test case, then the test case is failed. Details of validation parameters and measured values are reported automatically. Within XML test cases, some special sections can be defined:The Preparation section is intended for preparing steps that are not the actual part of the test case itself.The Constraint/Condition section contains parametrization of checks, that supervise the System Under Test or the test environment in parallel to test sequence.The Sequence part contains the actual part of the test case. Normally it contains a list of test function/control function parametrization.The Completion section will be proce

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Test/TestModules/XML/StructuringElements] SETestCaseInstance


# Test Case Instance

Structuring Elements » Test Case Instance

| Name | Test Case Instance |
| --- | --- |
| Description | A test case template can be used to create as many instances as desired; these instances can then be used at any given point in a test module just like normal XML test cases. A separate set of parameter values is specified for each testcaseinstance. |
| Remarks | A test case template can be reused multiply. Each usage of a test case template can parametrize the test case template. Such a usage is called test case instance. These instances can then be used at any given point in a test module just like normal XML test cases. A separate set of parameter values is specified for each testcaseinstance. Just like an XML or CAPL test case, each templateinstance contains a title and an ID, and can optionally be assigned to a variant. For all parameters defined in the test case template using paramdef, you can use paramvalue to specify the individual value to be used in a particular instance. If no individual value is given for a parameter, the default value (default) is taken from the template definition. It is obligatory to specify a concrete value for each instance where a default value is not defined for that parameter in the template definition. Parameter values are used in a text-based manner, i.e., exactly as written in the XML file. It is not possible to nest other elements in the parameter values. A testcaseinstance works just like a normal XML or CAPL 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Test/TestModules/XML/StructuringElements] SETestCaseTemplate


# Test Case Template

Structuring Elements » Test Case Template

| Name | Test Case Template |
| --- | --- |
| Description | Test case templates are generic test cases, which define the test case content and contain the parameter place holder. |
| Remarks | Test case templates are generic test cases with the possibility to define arguments for certain parameters. These templates for test cases are defined at the start of the test module in the optional templates section. Test case template parameters defined via paramdef in the test case template can be used at any given point in the test sequence, both as the content of XML elements and in their attributes. The parameter name is enclosed by $ signs. It is recommended to add parameters using drag and drop whereby the enclosed $ qualifier is added automatically. |
| Syntax | <templates> <testcasetemplate name="Name of the test case template" version="optional version description"> <paramdef name="Name of the parameter" default="default value (optional)"/> Contents are identical to XML Test Cases </testcasetemplate></templates> |
| Attributes | name (mandatory)Name of the test case template version (optional)Version description |
| See Also | — |
|  | Example <templates> <testcasetemplate name="StaticOpenClose"> <paramdef name="Waiting Time" default="3000"/> <paramdef name="UpDown" default="KeyDown"/> <paramdef name="Sig Window Motion" default="currently opening"/> <statechange wait="$Waiting Time$" title="Start opening/closin

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Test/TestModules/XML/StructuringElements] SETestGroup


# Test Group

Structuring Elements » Test Group

| Name | Test Group |
| --- | --- |
| Description | Test groups provide structure to the test cases of a test module. A test module may contain test groups, but it may also contain test cases directly. Test groups contain test cases or other test groups. That is, test groups may be embedded but they may not overlap. The assignment of executed test cases to test groups in XML test modules is determined by the structure of the XML file. For the most part test groups affect presentation aspects of the test report. |
| Remarks | Test groups are used to structure test cases. Like headings of chapters in a book, the test groups may be created hierarchically, whereby the executed test cases would be the leaves on the tree of test groups. The executed test cases will be structured in the test report automatically according to the defined test groups. |
| Syntax | <testgroup ident=" ID of the test group, e.g. number " title="Title of test group" variants="A" ></testgroup> |
| Attributes | title (mandatory)Title of the test group. ident (optional)Identification of the test group. variants (optional)Specifies the variants in which the test group is executed. |
| See Also | — |
|  | Example <testgroup title="Test static requirements of the SUT"></testgroup> |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Test/TestModules/XML/StructuringElements] SETestModule


# Test Module

Structuring Elements » Test Module

| Name | Test Module |
| --- | --- |
| Description | A test module is a test sequence for testing control units and networks using specific test functions in CANoe. |
| Remarks | CANoe supports the testing of control units and networks using test modules. A test module encompasses a test sequence of specific test functions. Using these functions, test modules can be created to verify individual development steps, to inspect prototypes or to implement regression and conformity tests. Test modules can be created with the Test Automation Editor and are executable in CANoe in the Simulation or Test Setup at any time during a measurement. XML test modules contain a sequence of test parameterization of prepared test functions and control functions. They can contain additionally parameterization of CAPL test functions, CAPL test cases or .NET test cases. An XML test module consists of: any number of test cases, that may be grouped in test groups. The execution of a test module is documented in the Write Window (e.g. start and end of a test case and results of test cases and test modules). The result of a test module execution is written the test report. |
| Syntax | <testmodule title="Title of test module " version="1.0" xmlns="Namespace of test module (as html)" xmlns:xsi="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema-instance" xsi:schemaLocation="Namespace with assigned schema-file"></testmodule> |
| Attributes | title (mandatory)Title of the

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Test/TestModules/XML/StructuringElements] StructuringElements


# Structuring Elements

Structuring Elements (XML)

| Structure Element | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Test Module | Test modules are used to specify and verify individual development steps, inspect prototypes or implement regression and conformity tests. They consist of a number of test cases. |
| Test Group | A test group serves exclusively as a grouping element for structuring of the execution tree. It contains no implementation logic. It can, in turn, contain other test groups or elements, such as test cases. |
| Test Case | Test cases contain the actual instructions for the test execution. |
| CAPL Test Case | Test cases programmed in CAPL can be referenced in XML test modules. |
| .NET Test Case | Test cases programmed in .NET can be referenced in XML test modules. |
| Test Case Template | Test case templates are generic test cases, which define the test case content and contain the parameter place holder. |
| Test Case Instance | A test case instance is derived from a test case template and filled with parameter values. |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Test/TestReport] TestReport


# Test Report

Test » Test Report

CANoe writes the test report to a file while the test is being implemented. This file contains all information of the test report in a structured, machine-readable form.

In the global CANoe options, you select the file format for reports:

- CANoe Test Report Viewer (recommended)Using CANoe Test Report Viewer you can visualize and search test reports intuitively.Several possibilities for filter, grouping and navigation as well as user-defined views provide easy analysis of test reports.

- XML/HTML Format (previous format)XML/HTML output format allows creating test reports in XML format. Using an XSLT stylesheet file transforms the XML test reports to HTML. Thus you can adjust the look of your test report to your needs.

## Test Units

On the Configure a test configuration page…

- …in the Test Report section, you receive additional information about the configuration of the test report.

- …in the Test Report Filter section, various classes of test report information can be deactivated. These will then be ignored in CANoe and not included in the XML test report. Accordingly, this information also does not appear in the HTML test report generated from the XML test report.

## Overall Test Report Or Separate Test Report For Each Test Unit

In the configuration dialog of the test configuration, you can specify whether an overall test report for all test units or separate test reports for each individual test unit shall be generated. The test

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Test/TestReport] TestReportExternalReferences


# External Test Report References

Test Report » External References

On the level of test modules, test units, test groups and test cases you can add references to external resources to the report. In the test report the link appears under the (optional) title. The following types of references are supported by CANoe and labeled appropriate in the report:

- URL an URL that links to any external resource and can be opened in a browser.

- DOORS an URL that links to a requirement in the DOORS Requirements Management Tool.

- eASEE an URL that links to an object in eASEE.

This functionality can be used both in XML test modules and in CAPL test modules / test units.

- The appropriate CAPL function is TestReportAddExternalRef.

- The appropriate element in XML test modules is externalref.

|  | Note URLs Open the resource you want to link to in your browser, copy the URL from the browser's address field and paste it in your test module. DOORS (effective with DOORS version 8.1) You can both link to single requirements and to modules in DOORS: choose Copy URL in the context menu of the module/requirement to copy the DOORS URL in the clipboard. Alternatively you can find the URL in the properties dialog of the module/requirement. The browser forwards DOORS links to the DOORS front end, therefore for opening the URL the DOORS client software has to be installed on the computer on which the report is viewed (the client is not necessary for executing test or for creating a report).

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Test/TestReport] TestReportFieldCodes


# Report Field Codes

Test Report » Report Field Codes

Toolbar of the Test Configuration Window: Configuration...

In the Report area of the configuration dialog for a test configuration you find the Field Codes button. With this button you can insert in the file path different field codes that will generate dynamic report file paths. At the end of the test the field codes will be replaced with their evaluated values and the final string will represent the report path.

The example text will give you a hint on how the final report file path will look like. Some of the field code samples may contain already the final values, like the field code {ConfigName}, but most of them will be evaluated during the test run.

|  | Note CANoe creates the resulting report path provided that the specified path is accessible and the user has write access. The report path may contain field codes to specify folders. Moreover the system variable field code may be used for creating dynamic sub paths. Field codes may be used also using the CAPL function testReportFileName. See Restrictions for limitations when using report field codes. |

## System

| Field Name | Field Code | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| User Name | {Username} | Windows log-in name. |
| Computer Name | {Computername} | Name of the computer. |
| Local System Time | {LocalTime[|Format]} | Current local system time. The default of the time stamp format can be set in the global options. In the field code you can overwrite th

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Test/TestReport] TestReportOutputFormat


# Test Report Output Format

Test Report » Output Format

Ribbon: File Tab|Options|General|Test Feature Set

CANoe writes the test report to a file while the test is being implemented. This file contains all information of the test report in a structured, machine-readable form.

In the global CANoe options, you select the file format for reports:

- CANoe Test Report Viewer (recommended)Using CANoe Test Report Viewer you can visualize and search test reports intuitively.Several possibilities for filter, grouping and navigation as well as user-defined views provide easy analysis of test reports.

- XML/HTML Format (previous format)XML/HTML output format allows creating test reports in XML format. Using an XSLT stylesheet file transforms the XML test reports to HTML. Thus you can adjust the look of your test report to your needs.

## CANoe Test Report Viewer

Using CANoe Test Report Viewer you can intuitively view and search test reports. This output format is stored in a *.vtestreport file and can be viewed with CANoe Test Report Viewer installed with CANoe. There you can use certain filters, grouping and navigation features as well as user-defined searches. The CANoe Test Report Viewer provides the following features:

- Queries CANoe Test Report Viewer enables query definitions on the complete data of the test report. Even complex and nested queries can easily be defined. The defined queries can be saved and re-used later; e.g. a list of all test cases that contain a partic

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Test/TestReport] TestReportWindowCapture


# Window Capture

Test Report » Window Capture

The Window Capture feature lets you capture the content of many analysis windows (AFDX VL Statistics Window, CAN Statistics Window, Data Window, FlexRay Statistics Window, Frame Histogram, Graphics Window, LIN Statistics Window, Scope Window, State Tracker, Test Trace Window, Trace Window, Video Window) and also panels during a test and integrate such window captures into the test report. This means you can view – in the test report – e.g. an image of the signals observed in the Graphics Window at the time the test was run.

This feature can be used in both XML and CAPL test modules/units.

- The relevant CAPL function is TestReportAddWindowCapture.

- The relevant pattern in XML test modules is windowcapture.

|  | Note In order for window capture to work, CANoe must be visible in the foreground and must not be obscured by other applications. Only those areas that are visible on the screen are captured successfully. It works best if you maximize the main CANoe window. Windows are not captured if you disable the output of comments and details in the test module Report Filter configuration. If you use default names for the windows to be captured, the capture may fail if you are running the test in a CANoe installation that is set to a different language. This is because the default names are adapted to the GUI language on loading. As a result, the windows are not found in the test module/unit under the specified name. If you wan

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Test/TestReport] TestReportXSLTStylesheets


# XSLT Stylesheets

Test Report » XSLT Stylesheets

If you selected the XML/HTML test report format in the global CANoe options, you can define in the configuration dialog of test modules, test configurations and test units whether the XML report shall be transformed to HTML using an XSLT stylesheet file.

## XSLT Stylesheet Overview

| Stylesheet | Short Description |
| --- | --- |
| onepage.xslt | Default stylesheet which creates one single page for the complete test report. |
| multipage.xslt | Stylesheet that creates an overview page which links to one additional page for each test case or other structuring element. |
| multicycle.xslt | Stylesheet that creates an overview page which links to one page for each complete execution cycle. Useful for tests that have been run using the multiple execution option. |
| extendedNavigation.xslt | Stylesheet that creates a report with multiple frames which contain an overview and additional pages for each test case or other structuring element. In addition, it displays logging files which were written during the test. Navigation from time stamps in the report to corresponding logged events is possible. |
| xunit.xslt | Provides semantic information on test case level (without any details) in xUnit format. Used for integration purposes with test management systems that are able to import xUnit reports. This stylesheet can be found within the Central Locking System sample configuration. |
| merge.xslt | Used internally by CANoe! Merg

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Test/TestReport] TFSTestReportConfiguration


# Test Report: Configuration

Test Feature Set » Test Report » Configuration

The test report is activated from the test module's configuration dialog. The filename of the test report file and the XSLT stylesheet used to generate the HTML test report are also specified there. The test report is then re-generated with each execution of the test module. If a file already exists it is overwritten. Alternatively, you can enable automatic extension of the test report filename. If you do so, a sequential number is appended to the filename.

If you want to used a different XSLT stylesheet once, you can select the XSLT file in the execution dialog in the Test Report menu and used it for an immediate transformation of the last generated XML file. After the transformation has finished, the former XSLT stylesheet will be used again.

Suitable CAPL commands in the test program or appropriate XML file entries can be used to include additional information in the test report (that is, in addition to the automatically generated information).

At first, all of this information is written to the test report in XML format. When the test report is transformed to HTML format, the stylesheet can be used to reduce the amount of information included. Several definitions are included at the beginning of the existing default stylesheet, onepage.xslt (located in the CANoe installation directory under Exec32/Report). These can be edited easily to adapt them to your individual requirements.

Different c

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Test/TestReport] TFSTestReportConfigurationDialog


# Test Report: Configuration Dialog

Test Feature Set » Test Report for Test Modules » Configuration

Context Menu of a Test Environment: Test Report: Configuration Dialog

You open this dialog via the Test Setup for Test Modules with a right click on a test environment element and the context menu command Configure Test Report.

When performing a sequential execution of several test modules in the test setup a test report is generated for each test module. These reports can then be merged to a common test report. Here you can configure this merged test report.

Enter the name of the test report in the XML test report input box. You can open a file request dialog using [...]. Existing files will be overwritten during the merge of the reports. Activating the Increment file name of test report automatically check box the file name is extended by a four digit number. E.g., report.XML will be generated as report0001.XML. This number is generated automatically; therefore existing files will not be overwritten.

Via the next check box Create HTML test report using the following XSLT stylesheet you can specify that an HTML test report is also to be generated in HTML format. The XML report file is transformed into an HTML report file after the test has been performed. This HTML test report has the same file name as the XML test report, except that the extension is HTML instead of XML.

If you don't want to use the standard transformation script, you can select a different XSLT style

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Test/TestServiceLibrary] TSLAnalyzingMeasurementResults


# Test Service Library: Analyzing the Measurement Results

Test Service Library » Analysis

Checks are usually used as constraints or conditions in test modules/units. The check results thus automatically flow into the test result, and detailed information is automatically included in the test report. A manual reaction to any violation of the check conditions is therefore not necessary in this case.

## Querying the check state (in CAPL)

The state of a check may be queried using appropriate CAPL commands. The query returns the following values:

- How often the event occurred since the start of the initialization

- You can query statistical values like the average, minimum, maximum and median values for event-free measurement periods.

- It is possible to obtain more information on the last event that occurred, e.g., the ID of the observed message (if initialized using a range), the actual time observed, the signal value that generated the event, etc.

TSL in XML test modules | TSL in CAPL test modules/ units | TSL in CAPL simulation nodes

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Test/TestServiceLibrary] TSLCAPLSimulationNodeSampleCode


# Test Service Library: Sample Code (CAPL Simulation Node)

Test Service Library » Sample Code

variables{ dword mCheckId = 0; // Remember the id of the currently running check const int cTextBufferLength = 512; // For the textual status query dword mMinDist = 1000; // Initial minimal distance between the // occurrence of Ref-Msg and Obs-Msg in ms dword mMaxDist = 6000; // Initial maximal distance between the // occurrence of Ref-Msg and Obs-Msg in ms}

on preStart{ ChkConfig_Init ("user"); // Initialize the test functionality // Now the concrete checks are setup (and commonly started) // The connected database contains the following messages: // «RefMsg» and «ObsMsg» SetupCheck();}

Callback (dword aCheckId){ long lEventMsgId = 0; char lEventMsgName[cTextBufferLength]; long lQueryResultOnMsgName = 0; long lTime = 0; long lStatNumEvents = 0; lQueryResultOnMsgName = ChkQuery_EventMessageName (aCheckId, lEventMsgName, cTextBufferLength); lEventMsgId = ChkQuery_EventMessageId (aCheckId); lTime = ChkQuery_EventInterval (aCheckId); if(lTime < 0) { // In this case, the message distance was too big. // Refer the error codes of this query // Let's resetup the check more liberal on the max distance mMaxDist = mMaxDist * 1.1; } else { // In the case, the message distance was too short. // Let's resetup the check more liberal on the min distance mMinDist = mMinDist * 0.9; } write("Callback for MsgDist: Msg %s(%d) had interval %d ", lEventMsgName, lEventMsgId, lTime); lStatNumEvents = C

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Test/TestServiceLibrary] TSLCAPLTestModuleSampleCode


# Test Service Library: Sample Code (CAPL Test Module)

Test Service Library » Sample Code

testcase LoadTest (){ dword chkId; dword stmId; // create condition to supervise cycle time of SUT chkId = ChkStart_MsgAbsCycleTimeViolation (MsgSpeed, 80, 120); TestAddCondition (chkId); // create stimulus function to generate ramp StmCreate_Ramp (Msg, Msg::RampSig, -10, 10, 5, 300, 200, 100, 400); // start ramp for 4000 ms StmControl_Start (stmId); TestWaitForTimeout (4000); StmControl_Stop (stmId); // stop condition supervision TestRemoveCondition (chkId); // optional: delete used check and stimulus function StmControl_Destroy (stmId); ChkControl_Destroy (chkId);}

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Test/TestServiceLibrary] TSLIntroduction


# Test Service Library: Introduction

Test Service Library » Introduction

The Test Service Library contains a collection of prepared test functions (checks) and value generation functions (stimuli) which make it easier to assemble tests. It is based on the basic functions included in the Test Feature Set (TFS). It makes special functions available that assist with solving specific, and possibly very special, test problems effectively and efficiently.

- Checks Check functions run parallel to the defined test procedure or simulation node and continuously test whether certain conditions are being met. Any violation of a check condition detected by the check is reported via a callback function. In test modules/units, the checks are implemented as constraints or conditions. Feedback is not necessary in this case. The checks influence the test results directly in this case, and the execution is logged in the test report.

- Stimulus Generators These commands are used to generate a specific series of values for a signal or an environment variable, which are used to stimulate a device. The stimulus generators create value series which are applied to signals or environment variables cyclically. The cycle time sets the sampling rate for the generated value series and thus the rate of change of the drain. The specified value series are repeated periodically, so that a value series of any desired length can be created. The time required to execute one value series until it is repeated

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Test/TestServiceLibrary] TSLPrecisionOfTheTimers


# Timer Precision

Test Service Library » Timer

The Test Service Library use the standard timers of CANoe.

- The internal precision of the timers is nanoseconds.

- The standard precision for the CAPL API is milliseconds.

To set a higher precision for the CAPL API the function ChkConfig_SetPrecision can be used. This function can change the precision between 1ms and 1 ns.

|  | Note This does not affect the checks and stimuli that were created before changing the precision. |

All time stamps that are given to the Test Service Library by the CAPL API have the unit of the current precision.

## Checks

- The create and start functions of the specific checks contain time information.

- The statistical queries always return the time information with the precision that was used to create the check.With another function ChkQuery_Precision it is possible to convert the time information to the standard precision (unit = milliseconds).

## Stimulus Generators:

Only the create functions of the stimuli contain time stamps.

|  | Sample Code – Precision of the Timers ChkConfig_SetPrecision(5); // set unit to 10^-5, i.e. 10usdword lCheckId = ChkStart...(...)ChkConfig_SetPrecision(6); // set unit to 10^-6, i.e. 1us // time in unit of specific check: double lTimeInPrecision = // precision is 10^-5ChkQuery_EventInterval(lCheckId); // time in standard unit milliseconds: double lTimeInMilliSeconds = // precision in millisecondsChkQuery_Precision(lCheckId) * ChkQuery_EventInterval(lCheck

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Test/TestServiceLibrary] TSLUsageCAPLSimulationNodes


# Test Service Library: TSL in CAPL Simulation Nodes

Test Service Library » CAPL » Simulation Nodes

Test Service Library functions can also be used in CAPL simulation nodes. However, it is simpler to use these functions in test modules/units. For this reason, it is advisable to use test modules/units to implement tests and use the Test Service Library functions.

## Configuration of Checks and Stimulus Generators

When Test Service Library functions are used in a simulation node, the Test Service Library must always be initialized by calling up ChkConfig_Init in the "on prestart" event procedure.

Checks and stimulus generators must always be created and parameterized before usage.

Checks and stimulus generators can already be created in "on prestart" or "on start". They can then be started, when they are needed, via the control functions.

## Starting and Controlling

The control functions are used to activate, deactivate, reset or delete a check or a generator. The testing or generation takes place in the background, in parallel to the continued processing of the simulation node.

Once a check or generator has been created, it can be used repeatedly during test execution, e.g., it can be started and stopped repeatedly. However, a check or stimulus generator which has already been started cannot be started again without being stopped first. You can also create several similar checks and use them simultaneously. A check or generator is identified by a unique number (ID), 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Test/TestServiceLibrary] TSLUsageCAPLTestModules


# Test Service Library in CAPL Test Modules

Test Service Library » CAPL » Test Modules

## Checks

Checks are used as constraints or conditions in test modules/units. The checks must merely be created, parameterized and activated for this purpose, and activated/deactivated as a constraint/condition:

dword id;id = ChkStart_MsgAbsCycleTimeViolation (MsgSpeed, 80, 120);TestAddConstraint (id);...TestRemoveConstraint (id);ChkControl_Destroy (id);

It is not necessary to use callback functions to react to errors or to control the check via a CAPL function, as constraints and conditions automatically influence the test result and document it in the test report.

Once a check or generator has been created, it can be used repeatedly during test execution, e.g., it can be started and stopped repeatedly. However, a check or stimulus generator which has already been started cannot be started again without being stopped first. It is generally advisable to use a ChkControl_Reset call prior to reuse as a constraint or condition. You can also create several similar checks and use them simultaneously. A check or generator is identified by a unique number (ID), which is returned when the check or generator is created.

In principle, it is possible to use a check in a CAPL test module in the same way as in a CAPL simulation node. However, this is generally more time-consuming and is not recommended.

## Stimulus Generators

Stimulus generators, like checks, must be created and parameterized 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Test/TestServiceLibrary] TSLUsageXMLTestModules


# Test Service Library in XML Test Modules

Test Service Library » XML » Test Modules

Test Service Library checks are available as constraints and conditions in XML test modules. The required checks are configured in an XML description in the constraints or conditions section of the test module, test group or test case.

|  | Example <constraints> <cycletime_rel min="80" max="120"> <canmsg id="StatusMsg" /> </cycletime_rel></constraints> |

The starting and deletion of checks, the analysis of check results and the transmission of information into the test report all take place automatically. Additional functions for controlling checks are not needed in XML test modules.

Stimulus generators are implemented in XML test modules via test functions. The generator is created and started in the test function. It is not necessary to explicitly delete the generator.

|  | Example <stimulate_ramp title="Speed ramp" cycle="2" duration="100" timeUp="5" timeDown="5" timeLow="10" timeHigh="10"> <envvar name="EvSpeed" /></stimulate_ramp> |

Additional control functions are also not needed in this case.

Test Feature Set (TFS) | Structure of XML test modules | Constraints and conditions (XML) | Sample File (XML test module)

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Test/TestServiceLibrary] TSLWorkingWithTSLFunctions


# Test Service Library: Functionality of TSL Functions

Test Service Library » TSL Functions

## General functional requirements

The TSL functions work in the following phases:

- Initialization The program set up checks and stimulus generators by specifying the parameters for the checks and generators as well as the way that events are to be reported. This can take place at any given point during the measurement. In CAPL simulation nodes, for example, this would not just take place in the "on start" function.

- Events As soon as the check conditions are violated, a specified reaction is triggered; e.g., a callback function is called or the test module/unit result is set to fail and a corresponding message is written to the test report.

- Status The status of the check can be queried at any point in a CAPL program. The status can also be converted to a string for post processing.

- Stop/Restart The check or stimulus is deactivated/continued.

|  | Note If a check or a stimulus generator is no longer required or is to be replaced by a different one, you must first delete the old one and then generate a new check or stimulus generator. The TSL may be used at any point in a CAPL simulation node or CAPL test module/unit. |

TSL in XML test modules | TSL in CAPL test modules | TSL in CAPL simulation nodes

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Test/TestServiceLibrary] TSLXMLTestModuleSampleFile


# Test Service Library: Sample File (XML Test Module)

Test Service Library » Sample File

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?><testmodule title="ECU Test" version="1.0" xmlns:xsi="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema-instance" xmlns="http://www.vector-informatik.de/CANoe/TestModule/1.15" xsi:schemaLocation="http://www.vector-informatik.de/CANoe/TestModule/1.15 testmodule.xsd"> <testcase ident="TC1" title="Stimulate and check cycle time"> <conditions> <cycletime_abs min="100" max="500"> <canmsg id="StatusMsg" /> </cycletime_abs> </conditions> <initialize title="Select level 2" wait="1500"> <cansignal name="Level">2</cansignal> </initialize> <stimulate_ramp title="Ramp signal on signal ControlSig" cycle="10" duration="5000" timeUp="20" timeDown="20" timeHigh="100" timeLow="100"> <cansignal id="ControlSig" /> </stimulate_ramp> </testcase></testmodule>

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [Test/TestUnits] TU


# Test Units

Test » Test Units

In vTESTstudio, you can implement tests with the help of CAPL and C# files as well as in tabular and graphic forms. In so doing, the different test languages can be easily combined. The executable entity programmed in vTESTstudio is called a test unit. It comprises all files required for the test. A test unit created in vTESTstudio has the file extension *.VTUEXE. This can be configured and executed in CANoe.

A test unit is configured within a test configuration. A CANoe configuration can contain any number of test configurations. A test configuration, in turn, can contain 1…n configured test units. All test units within a test configuration are executed sequentially one after the other.

A test configuration can be tested either automatically at the start of the measurement or interactively by the user. A started test configuration normally terminates at the end of the test run or when forced by the user or at the end of the measurement.

The assignment of a test unit to buses happens already in vTESTstudio. A test unit is assigned automatically to all buses configured in the vTESTstudio project.

The user can activate or deactivate the test configuration in the Test Setup for Test Units before the start of the measurement (setting can be recognized from the corresponding check box). A deactivated test configuration cannot be started during the measurement. The automatic start function is also disabled.

Each execution of a test configurati

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Test/TestUnits] TUExecution


# Executing Test Units

Test » Test Units » Executing Test Units

Context Menu of the test configuration: Execute Test Units

Test configurations with one or more test units can be started interactively or with an automated start:

- For automated execution, the following start options are appropriate and can be specified in the configuration dialog of the test configuration:

- Start of test configuration at the start of the measurement

- Start of test configuration when an environment variable changes

- Start of test configuration at the press of a button

- An interactive execution of the test configuration is possible in the execution dialog. To open this execution dialog, double-click the test configuration in the Test Setup for Test Units or select it using the context menu of the test configuration.

You can select which test cases and test sequences are to be executed in the execution dialog.

You can monitor the test execution of a test configuration in the Test Trace Window. All test steps and actions are displayed there while the test is running.Additionally two »system variables will be generated automatically for each test configuration, which can be used to query and show the current execution status and the current »verdict:

| Variable Name | Values |
| --- | --- |
| RunState | 0|Not Running 1|Running 2|Finished |
| VerdictSummary | 0|None 1|Passed 2|Inconclusive 3|Failed 4|Error in Test System |

|  | Note The execution of a test configuration is represent

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [Test/TestUnits] TUTestSystemVariables


# Test System Variables

Test » Test Units » Test System Variables

A test unit that has been configured in CANoe automatically provides this CANoe configuration with its test system variables. To ensure the unambiguousness of the test system variables, CANoe automatically generates a namespace for every configured test unit. These namespaces contain the corresponding test system variables. The namespace consists of two parts: the name of the test configuration and the name of the test unit. In CANoe the test system variables can be used in the manner as other system variables.

If name conflicts occur between system variables that have been defined in CANoe and test system variables from test units, you'll get according error messages. In order to configure test units successfully you have to solve the these conflicts.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [vCDL/Concept] vCDLConcept


# Vector Communication Description Language (vCDL)

CANoe » vCDL

The acronym vCDL stands for Vector Communication Description Language and is a domain-specific language (DSLDomain Specific Language) for the description of communication objects in CANoe.

vCDL allows the CANoe communication concept to be extended by a tool for the simple and text-based configuration of communication objects. Communication objects and their respective parameters are defined in a syntax similar to the most common programming languages. A primary design goal of the language is to be able to map simple constructs with few language elements. If complex configurations are required, an extended syntax allows definitions to be supplemented by attributes (similar to the attributes commonly used in C#).

|  | Example: Definition of a service with fields and methods in vCDL version 1.0;namespace Audio{ service CDPlayer { consumer TouchScreen, Dashboard; provider Console; field uint16 NumberOfTracks { get; } field uint16 CurrentTrack; // get, set and notify implied void NextTrack(); void PrevTrack(); binding Abstract; }} |

## Overview

| Chapter | Question |
| --- | --- |
| Motivation and Delimitation | What should I use vCDL for? |
| Structure and Syntax | What is the structure and syntax of vCDL? |
| Integration in CANoe | How is vCDL integrated in CANoe? |
| Functional Scope | What functions are provided by vCDL? |
| vCDL Editor | Which editor should I use to edit vCDL files? |
| Language (only avai

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [vCDL/Concept] vCDLFunctionalScope


# Functional Scope

CANoe » vCDL » Functional Scope

In the current CANoe release the complete functional scope of vCDL is not available.

## Restrictions

- Communication objects can not be configured with PDU bindingNetwork connection of communication objects, defines the communication stack and its parameters..

- For editing vCDL files it is recommended to use the provided IDE, because only there correct syntax highlighting, autocompletion and error display are available at programming time.

- No objects from an external data source (e.g. an AUTOSAR extract) can be referenced.

## New in Version 1.1 (12.0 SP3)

- E2E protection

- PDU timings

- Secure onboard communication

- Signal groups

## New in Version 1.2 (13.0)

- Distributed objects (DOs)

- Virtual networks

- New distributed object interfaces

- New enumeration attribute value and custom attributes

- Changed comment syntax

- New supported attributes for SOME/IP binding: mac (SOME/IP endpoint) and stringTermination (global)

## New in Version 1.3 (14)

- DO references and DO container

- Initial values for distributes objects

- Constants and literals also for complex data types

- New possibilities for the definition of network communication

## New in Version 1.4 (15)

- Preprocessor for conditional compilation

- Initial values at DO interfaces

- More options for initializing DO containers

- Attributes returnDelay and serverSimulatorMode for DO methods and DO fields

| Version 15© Vector Informatik Gmb

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [vCDL/Concept] vCDLIntegration


# Integration in CANoe

CANoe » vCDL » Integration in CANoe

The integration of vCDL in CANoe is carried out in the same way as for other data sources. vCDL files can be selected and imported via the Data Sources dialog. It is not necessary to select all dependencies – the roots of the dependency graphs are sufficient. It is recommended to create a hierarchy between source files, for example communication objects, data types and bindingsNetwork connection of communication objects, defines the communication stack and its parameters.. In particular, if the binding is defined in a completely separate way, it can be exchanged more easily: either by adapting an import statement for the binding or vice versa, if the binding source file serves as anchor for the import into CANoe and imports the definition of communication objects and data types implicitly. This separation is particularly convenient when, for example, an abstract binding is used at first and then it is necessary to switch to a concrete binding.

When importing data sources by means of implicit imports, it must be noted that conflicts may arise if the same file is imported several times via the dependency graphs. In this case, the files directly imported into CANoe must be imported as a group.

Once import has been successfully completed, the defined communication objects are available for further editing in the Model Editor and for the use in application models.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [vCDL/Concept] vCDLMotivation


# Motivation and Delimitation

CANoe » vCDL » Motivation

The cross-network communication concept of CANoe offers the possibility to import communication objects, distributed objects, or just data types and interfaces from different data sources. These are generated in different steps of the process and can be incorrect and incomplete:

- When commissioning an ECU, no database may be present or data may be missing that are only required to simulate the network.

- During the development of software components or individual algorithms, the communication structure is still undefined.

- The network design is incorrect or incomplete and the databases generated therefrom are patchy.

- Different versions of databases with incompatible definitions do not allow a network to be simulated or partly put into operation.

- An automated test requires additional communication objects that are not part of the system design.

These gaps or errors in the definition either mean that information must be added via the respective data source (which can lead to a lengthy process) or that changes must be made in the test / simulation tool. In the latter case, the CANoe data sources can be extended and corrected using the Model Editor or by importing vCDL. The Communication Object Editor is particularly suitable for changing parameters and bindingsNetwork connection of communication objects, defines the communication stack and its parameters. of existing communication objects, while the import of

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [vCDL/Concept] vCDLSyntax


# Structure and Syntax

CANoe » vCDL » Structure and Syntax

The structure of vCDL is similar to the most common programming languages, except that vCDL is a pure declarative language and therefore does not include any program flow.

## Communication Objects

The basic objective of this language is to define communication objects that can be identified by a unique name. In the case of service-oriented architecture, these are services that can include events, fields, methods, and PDUs. In signal-based communication, signals and PDUs are available as independent communication objects.

signal uint16 Speed{ sender WheelFL, WheelFR, WheelBL, WheelBR; receiver TractionControl;}

## Data Types

Defining communication objects requires a description of the data to be communicated in the form of primitive and complex data types.

## Supported Data Types

| Primitive Data Types | Complex Data Types |
| --- | --- |
| Strings | Enumerations |
| Boolean | Bit fields |
| Integer numbers | Arrays |
| Float numbers | Lists |
| Time | Structures |
|  | Unions |

Type alias definitions allow application-specific names to be assigned to the defined data types; furthermore, properties for serialization and simulation can be added by means of data-specific attributes.

const int32 AbsZero = -273;[min=AbsZero, max=1000, unit="C"]typedef Temperature = int32;typedef History = array<Temperature, 100>;

## Namespaces

As for conventional programming languages, defined communication objects are organi

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [vCDL/Language] vCDLAbstractBinding


# Abstract Binding

CANoe » vCDL » Language » Abstract Binding

Abstract binding allows to use defined objects in simulation-only scenarios, i.e. without binding objects to concrete network hardware. All properties of the simulation model are defined through attributes on language elements. Some attributes are shared with other bindings. Attributes specific to the abstract binding generally use the namespace Abstract. The abstract binding is available for communication objects and distributed objects.

## Communication Objects

A binding statement is started with the keyword binding followed by the binding name. With this the communication object uses the declared binding and will accept corresponding attributes. These attributes then do not need to be qualified with the binding namespace.

|  | Example: Service with inline abstract binding. [delay=100us, subscribedEvents=(FuelInjectionFailure, OverTemperature)]service EngineWatchdog{ binding Abstract; field bool OverTemperature { get; notify; } field bool OilPressureLow { get; notify; } event bool FuelInjectionFailure; event bool ElectricalFailure; field bool SingleWire { get; notify; } provider Engine; consumer Dashboard, Logger; [delay=10ms, subscribedEvents=this.ElectricalFailure] refine this.Dashboard.Engine; [subscribedConsumers=(this.Dashboard, this.Logger)] refine this.Engine.ElectricalFailure;} |

In this example the default transmission delay and static event subscription for all ports is defined at service level a

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [vCDL/Language] vCDLArrayDataTypes


# Array Data Types

CANoe » vCDL » Language » Array Data Types

Array data types are declared using the array keyword, specifying the array’s base data type and size in angle brackets.

|  | Example: Array alias definition. typedef FixArray = array<float, 25>; |

Arrays are generally unnamed data types and can be inlined in any declaration expecting a data type (e.g. function parameters, fields, events, signals, …). A named type can be declared as an alias.

|  | Example: Field declaration with array data type. service CDPlayer{ field array<uint16, 50> TrackHistory;} |

## Supported Attributes

Arrays support the following attributes:

| Name | Type | Default | Semantics |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| byteOrder | String | "Intel" | Specifies whether values and length fields are serialized in Motorola or Intel byte order. Complex array element types may override this and serialize in a different byte order. |
| fixLayout | Bool | false | Specifies whether the data type has a fix binary layout (requires all members to have a fix layout data type). |

Array attribute placement is determined by the statement containing the array declaration.

Attributes for the array element data type may be placed before or after the data type.

Language Conventions

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [vCDL/Language] vCDLAttributeRefinements


# Attribute Refinements

CANoe » vCDL » Language » Attribute Refinements

To be able to split the pure communication description from binding specific settings or for overriding attribute settings (e.g. per endpoint) the refine keyword allows to reference any named objects and members or endpoints of those. The statement itself does not carry further semantic but allows to redefine or add attributes to the referenced entity. In case a combination of endpoint and member is referenced then the refined attributes are only applied for that combination, thus allowing to specify individual attribute values per endpoint and member.

|  | Example: Service adding attributes to members declared in the implemented service interface. namespace Multimedia{ namespace Audio { interface IAudioSource { field uint16 NumberOfTracks { get; } field uint16 CurrentTrack { get; set; notify; } void NextTrack(); void PrevTrack(); } } [inParamsRepr="Raw", txValueRepr="Impl"] service CDPlayer : Audio::IAudioSource { [inParamsRepr="Impl"] consumer Console; [inParamsRepr="Default", getterID=1] refine NumberOfTracks; [inParamsRepr="Raw"] refine Console.NumberOfTracks; }} |

Refinements may appear globally, on namespace level or within complex data types, within communication objects or within distributes objects. The latter can only refine members within the communication object/distributes object. If there is a member within the communication object/distributes object with the same name as objects outsid

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [vCDL/Language] vCDLAttributes


# Attributes

CANoe » vCDL » Language » Attributes

vCDL allows to specify attributes for core language declarations, which provide extended configuration information and are by nature optional. Syntactically attributes are a comma separated list of key-value pairs enclosed in square brackets [...]. Valid attribute keys are predefined identifiers, optionally qualified by one or more namespaces, where the list of valid identifiers is individually defined for each language element. Most predefined attributes are not defined within any namespace. However, binding specific attributes, either for communication- or distributed objects, have the binding type as namespace. For some attributes, mostly for those with boolean type, the value is optional. Besides predefined attributes you can define your own attributes (see below).

Attribute values are expressions over primitive type operands (bool, string, integer, float), references to named elements, primitive type names, encoding formulas or homogeneous collections of these. Each attribute has a specific data type associated, which constrains the possible values. The expected data types are documented in the reference to the corresponding language element.

| Attributes | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Boolean attribute value | Expression evaluating to true or false. The language supports a shortcut notation, where only the attribute key (without the equal sign) is given, implying the value true. The boolean type supports literals t

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [vCDL/Language] vCDLBitFields


# Bit Fields

CANoe » vCDL » Language » Bit Fields

Bit fields are declared using the bitfields keyword followed by an identifier and a body definition in curly braces. The body definition contains groups of values, which each define a bitmask for the contained set of values. A group is started with the keyword group, followed by an optional mask expression and another body in curly braces, which defines the mapping of values to textual representation. The semantics of a group imply that at any point in time only one value from the group can be stored in the underlying data type. The mask for setting these values can be calculated from the bitwise OR operation on all values in a group if not explicitly specified. Multiple groups must have non-overlapping masks, such that values from these groups do not interfere with each other.

|  | Example: Bit field data type encoding some flags. bitfields Speakers{ [name="Speakers"] group 0x1F { "FL" where x == 1 << 0 [display="Front Left"], "FR" where x == 1 << 1 [display="Front Right"], "RL" where x == 1 << 2 [display="Rear Left"], "RR" where x == 1 << 3 [display="Rear Right"], "CT" where x == 1 << 4 [display="Subwoofer"], "All" where x == 0x1F, }}[bitFields=Speakers]event uint8 SpeakersEnabled; |

Similar to encodings the bit field declaration does not imply a named data type but is referenced by integer type elements as a semantic attribute (bitFields) which also allows merging multiple bitfield definitions.

Bitfields must be decla

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [vCDL/Language] vCDLBytesDataTypes


# Bytes Data Type

CANoe » vCDL » Language » Bytes Data Type

The bytes data type is used to store or transfer an opaque sequence of bytes. The number of bytes can vary with each value. In contrast to a list of bytes (which could be declared with list<uint8>), it is not possible to access an individual element (a single byte) in the programming APIs directly or to react on updates or changes of individual elements with a handler. On the other hand, it is possible to copy all bytes directly into and out of a byte value (which does not work with a list because it also contains internal control data).

|  | Example: Byte sequence as a data member of a distributed object. object SomeObject{ consumed data bytes SomeData;} |

## Supported Attributes

The bytes data type does not support any attributes.

Language Conventions

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [vCDL/Language] vCDLCharacterSet


# Character Set

CANoe » vCDL » Language » Character Set

The vCDL parser processes input as UTF-16 encoded characters. The mechanism to read source files into strings takes care of the encoding of those files. UTF-16 encoded files are detected by the corresponding BOM and the byte order shall be reversed if necessary. If no known BOM is found, single byte encoding is assumed and the file is scanned for a specific statement indicating the used encoding:

#pragma encoding UTF-7

If the encoding is found and known, the byte stream from the file is accordingly transformed before parsing. Otherwise UTF-8 encoding is assumed and the same transformation is applied.

All identifiers must consist of non-special characters (letters, digits and underscore) and must not start with a digit. Only in string literals the use of special characters is allowed.

Language Conventions

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [vCDL/Language] vCDLComments


# Comments

CANoe » vCDL » Language » Comments

Comments are supported as line style and block comments similar to C++ and C#. A line comment starts with a double slash // and stops before the next newline character. Block comments start with the token /* and end with the next */ token. These comments are meant for authors of t he vCDL file only and are usually discarded by components that import this file. However, managed line comments starting with /// or //! and managed block comments starting with /** or /*! (both closing with */) are tried to be kept by the importer and can be used for documentation of the data model represented in the vCDL file.

|  | Note If you have used comments in vCDL files prior to version v1.2 and still want them to be visible after import into CANoe, you must update all of them to managed comments! |

If the closing */ for block comments is missing, then the source text is malformed and importing it will show an error. Block comments cannot be nested, i.e. any text containing /* within a block comment shall not be considered yet another block comment start, but simple text within the outer block comment.

Comments are associated with specific statements by vicinity rules. Statements are either delimited by a semicolon or by a closing curly brace in case of a block statement. Each comment is associated with the current or following statement except if a comment directly follows a statement delimiter on the same line (ignoring whitespace), in wh

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [vCDL/Language] vCDLConstantDeclarations


# Constant Declarations

CANoe » vCDL » Language » Constant Declarations

Constant values of primitive and string data types can be defined for use in expressions or as attribute values.
