The syntax for constants is similar to C++ and other programming languages starting with the const keyword followed by a data type, a name and the assignment of the constant value (which can be an expression with constant value operands).

|  | Example: Constant integer value declaration used as attribute value. const int32 AbsZero = -273;[min=AbsZero, max=1000, unit="C"]typedef Temperature = int32; |

Constants must be declared at namespace level, i.e. they cannot be nested into complex types, interfaces or services.

## Supported Attributes

There are no specific attributes for constant declarations.

Attributes for the used data type can be placed before the const keyword or before the statement delimiter.

Language Conventions

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [vCDL/Language] vCDLConventions


# Conventions

CANoe » vCDL » Language » Conventions

The following table lists the font attributes used for specific language elements of vCDL:

| Element | Font | Example |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Keyword | Blue monospace | service |
| Data Type | Dark blue monospace | uint16 |
| String Literal | Dark red monospace | "string" |
| Numeric Literal | Orange monospace | 2.0e-10 |
| Comment | Green monospace | // comment |
| Attribute Key | Gray monospace, italics | unit |
| Binding Namespace | Gray monospace, italics | Abstract |
| Encoding Formula Variable | Brown monospace, italics | x |
| Separators | Black monospace | .: |
| Operators | Black monospace | +-*/ |
| Brackets | Black monospace | {}()<> |
| Identifiers | Black monospace | Engine |

Although we strongly recommend using the vCDL Editor, you can get almost the same syntax highlighting by importing the language definition file (vCDL_1.3.xml) into Notepad++. However, this gives you the highlighting only without any advanced features such as code completion.

The documentation of individual language elements lists supported attributes each with the required value data types. In case a specific attribute’s value data type is a collection type, the element type of the collection is noted in square brackets [] if ordered list semantics are implied and in curly braces {} if set semantics are implied.

The language is case sensitive regarding all keywords, attribute keys, binding namespaces, primitive data types and the enc

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [vCDL/Language] vCDLDistributedObjects


# Distributed Objects

CANoe » vCDL » Language » Distributed Objects

Distributed objects (DOs) are declared similarly to services. The keyword object is followed by the name and the body containing members and refinements. Declaring the members within the distributed object body creates an implicit, anonymous distributed object interface. If an explicit distributed object interface shall be used, it is referenced after the distributed object name separated by a colon. It is possible to assign multiple DO interfaces to a distributed object in vCDL. If this feature is used, the interfaces are implicitly aggregated into a new DO interface. This mechanism also allows the distributed object to define new members which are then added to the implicit DO interface. Distributed objects are always declared on namespace level.

interface DOInterface{ internal data Struct Member;}DOInterface DistributedObject;reverse<DOInterface> ReversedDO;[virtualNetwork=Network1];refine ReversedDO.Member;object Inlined{ internal data Struct DataMember; provided method Prototype MethodMember; provided uint48 InlinedMethod(float Param); consumed event void EventMember; consumed field list<int32> FieldMember { get; notify; } internal reference<DOInterface> ReferenceMember; embedded DOInterface EmbeddedMember; embedded InlinedEmbeddedMember { internal data int32 EmbeddedDataMember; }}object ExplicitDO : DOInterface;object ExplicitReversed : reverse<DOInterface>{ [virtualNetwork=Network1]; refine Member;

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [vCDL/Language] vCDLE2EProtection


# E2E Protection

CANoe » vCDL » Language » PDU Timings

The E2E protection settings augment event and PDU based communication with counter measures against communication failure by software errors, hardware failure or environmental influences. They are designed along the AUTOSAR E2E library specifications. Since the E2E protection requires quite some parameters, not all of these are directly realized as attributes on the corresponding language elements but an E2E Profile definition allows to reuse the basic settings. The profile is declared by the keyword e2e followed by an identifier and the profile name to use. The actual profile settings are realized as attributes on that statement.

|  | Example: E2E profile definition. [ counterOffset=4, crcOffset=8, dataIdMode="E2E_P11_DATAID_NIBBLE", dataIdNibbleOffset=0, maxDeltaCounter=3]e2e E2EProfile11 "E2E_P11"; |

The parameters of the profile are the usually more stable and thus reused settings. More specific parameters are attached to individual Signal Groups, Service PDUs, PDUs or SOME/IP events and fields besides a reference to the profile. All these attributes can already be attached to the corresponding type definitions (e.g. a data group definition within a PDU type definition) and can be refined at the instance level.

|  | Example: Event refinement with E2E protection settings. refine EngineControl.OilPressure[ e2eDataLength=127*8, e2eDataIdList=(180,134,53,233,243,121,67,176,9,94,168,132,198,142,201,138), e2eProfile=E

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [vCDL/Language] vCDLEncodingSemanticAttributes


# Encodings and Semantic Attributes

CANoe » vCDL » Language » Encodings and Semantic Attributes

Encodings can be defined to support the use of distinct raw and implementation data types and formulas for calculating physical and textual representations from one or the other. In general, the model distinguishes an implementation data type, a raw (wire) data type and a physical data type. The actual declaration of a signal, event, field etc. always contains its implementation data type, e.g. declaring an int32 struct member implies that this is its implementation data type. Thus, an implementation data type is always given and cannot be omitted.

A specific raw data type can be associated with any declaration using a numeric data type by adding the rawType attribute. Without further specification the raw value will then be equivalent to the implementation value simply converted to the raw data type. In a network transmission this may for instance mean that you are losing precision or resolution.

A physical data type is again always defined and can be any of uint64, int64 or double, whichever can hold all values from the implementation data type. You never need to explicitly specify the physical data type. Physical values are by default the same as implementation values, i.e. there is no scaling or offset and because of the automatic choosing of their data type you cannot lose any precision or resolution by conversion.

If there should actually be a conversion between impleme

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [vCDL/Language] vCDLEndPoints


# Endpoints

CANoe » vCDL » Language » Endpoints

Endpoints identify the communication endpoints of a Service, Signal or PDU. Their type (and keyword) depends on the communication pattern used by the communication object: it will be consumer and provider for service oriented communication or sender and receiver for classical communication pattern. The keyword is only allowed in the body of communication object declarations and is followed by a comma separated list of endpoint names. Multiple such statements are allowed and the attributes associated with one endpoint statement apply to all endpoints defined in that statement.

Each endpoint name may as well be a reference to a participant, which implicitly adds the endpoint to that participant. The endpoint in that case gets the same name as the participant. This makes it easy to simply declare the list of (empty) participants and add endpoints from different communication objects by referencing these participants from the endpoint statements.

|  | Example: PDU declaration with referenced type. service AudioStream{ consumer Console; provider CDChanger, USBAdapter;} |

Endpoints must be declared at communication object level only.

## Supported Attributes

Endpoint declarations support the following attributes:

| Name | Type | Default | Semantics |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| simulated | Bool | true/falseIf the measured flag is set to true, the simulated flag’s default is assumed false.* | Enables the use of the endpoint in 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [vCDL/Language] vCDLEnumerations


# Enumerations

CANoe » vCDL » Language » Enumerations

Enumerations are declared using the enum keyword followed by an identifier and a body definition in curly braces. The body contains a list of comma-separated identifiers which represent the values of the enumeration where the comma after the last item is optional. Each enumeration value may be assigned an expression that evaluates to an integer value. If no value is assigned to an enumerator, it gets the value of its predecessor incremented by 1. The default value for the first enumerator is 0. Additionally, the type of an enumeration can be explicitly set to any other integer type by writing it after the enumeration name separated by a colon. By default, the smallest possible integer type with bitwidth of 8, 16, 32 or 64, either signed or unsigned, is chosen that covers all existing enumeration values.

|  | Example: Enumeration data type to encode a state. enum State : uint8{ SWITCHED_OFF, INITIALIZING, RUNNING, SHUTTING_DOWN = 7,} |

Enumerations must be declared at namespace level, i.e. they cannot be nested into complex types, interfaces or services. They define a named data type and can be used equivalently to integer data types.

## Supported Attributes

There are no specific attributes for enumerations or enumeration values.

Attributes for the used data type can be placed before or after the data type declaration or before the enum keyword.

Language Conventions

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [vCDL/Language] vCDLEvents


# Events

CANoe » vCDL » Language » Events

Events are declared within a service interface by the keyword event followed by the event’s data type, which identifies the data contained in the notification, and the event name. The event declaration is finished by a semicolon. Note that an event’s data type may be void.

|  | Example: Interface with event declaration. interface IMediaSource{ event uint16 NewTracks;} |

Events must be declared at service interface level or at service level when the interface is inlined.

## Supported Attributes

Event declarations support the following attributes:

| Name | Type | Default | Semantics |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| txValueRepr | String | "Default" | Whether transmitted event values should be stored in Default, Impl or Raw representation. Can be overridden per endpoint. |

Event attributes and attributes for the event’s data type may be placed before or after (before the delimiter) an event declaration.

Language Conventions

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [vCDL/Language] vCDLExpressions


# Expressions

CANoe » vCDL » Language » Expressions

The language allows to specify values in several places (e.g. constant declarations, attribute values, explicit enumerated values, encodings, bit fields, etc.). In all those places the constant may be an expression over literal values and references to constants. In general, the syntax of expressions as well as associativity and precedence of operators is compatible to the C programming language.

Supported operators are:

| Operators | Constraints |
| --- | --- |
| ?: | Ternary operator | The condition must have boolean type and the branches must have compatible types. |
| || | Logic Or operator | Both operands must have boolean type. |
| && | Logic And operator | Both operands must have boolean type. |
| | | Bitwise Or operator | The operands must have unsigned integer type. |
| ^ | Bitwise Xor operator | The operands must have unsigned integer type. |
| & | Bitwise And operator | The operands must have unsigned integer type. |
| == | Equal Comparison operator | The operands must have compatible types. |
| != | Unequal Comparison operator | The operands must have compatible types. |
| < | Less Than Comparison operator | The operands must have compatible numeric types. |
| > | Greater Than Comparison operator | The operands must have compatible numeric types. |
| <= | Less or Equal Comparison operator | The operands must have compatible numeric types. |
| >= | Greater or Equal Comparison operator | The operands must have

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [vCDL/Language] vCDLFields


# Fields

CANoe » vCDL » Language » Fields

Fields are declared within a service interface by the keyword field followed by the field’s data type and name. The statement can be finished by a semicolon, implying that a getter, setter and notification is available for the field, or by a body declaration in curly braces, explicitly listing the support for getter, setter and notification (at least one must be given). The body specification may contain the statements get, set and notify, each finished by a semicolon.

|  | Example: Interface with field declaration. interface IMediaSource{ field uint16 NumberOfTracks { get; } field uint16 CurrentTrack; // get, set and notify implied} |

Fields must be declared at service interface level or at service level when the interface is inlined.

## Supported Attributes

Field declarations support the following attributes:

| Name | Type | Default | Semantics |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| setterHasReturn | Bool | true/falseIf the attribute setterIsOneWay is specified, the default value is false, otherwise true.* | Specifies whether the setter function of the field returns the current field value. |
| setterIsOneWay | Bool | false | Specifies whether the setter function of the field is a one-way function. |
| defaultParamsRepr | String | "Default" | Whether simulation defaults should be stored in Default, Impl or Raw representation. Can be overridden per endpoint. |
| inParamsRepr | String | "Default" | Whether function call input parameter

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [vCDL/Language] vCDLFunctionPrototypes


# Function Prototypes

CANoe » vCDL » Language » Function Prototypes

The signature of service interface methods is defined by a function prototype declaration. The actual function or method declaration then only references the prototype. The signature definition can be inlined in an interface declaration even if the interface definition is also inlined in a service declaration.

The prototype declaration uses the typedef keyword, followed by the prototype name and its signature. The latter consisting of the functions return type (may be void) and in parentheses the function parameter specifications. Each parameter in turn consists of an optional direction keyword (in, out, inout), the parameter’s data type and finally its name. If no direction is specified, the parameter is considered an input parameter.

|  | Example: Function prototype definition. typedef GetTrackInfo = uint16(int16 disk, out array<TrackInfo, 50> trackInfo); |

Function prototypes must be declared at namespace level, i.e. they cannot be nested in other complex types, interfaces or services (except when inlined).

## Supported Attributes (Prototype)

The prototype declaration supports the following attributes:

| Name | Type | Default | Semantics |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| oneWay | Bool | false | Identifies a fire and forget function prototype. If true, the return type must be void and the function must not have any out or inout parameters. |

Attributes for the function prototype may be placed before t

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [vCDL/Language] vCDLImports


# Imports

CANoe » vCDL » Language » Imports

A vCDL model does not need to be declared in a single file but provides a mechanism to import the content of multiple source files from another source file. The keyword import followed by a quoted string containing a file system path to another vCDL file will implicitly make the contents of the imported file known in the importing file. In fact, in a first step the set of files to import is determined recursively, starting from a set of root files, and only when all declarations from these files are known, references between declarations are resolved. This allows to use even cyclic import statements independent from the direction of cross references between declarations.

|  | Example: Importing common type definitions. import "../common/types.vcdl"; |

Imports must be declared at global scope, i.e. they cannot be nested into namespaces, complex types, interfaces or services. They must immediately follow the version statement before any namespace is opened.

Language Conventions

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [vCDL/Language] vCDLIPAddressConfiguration


# IP Address Configuration

CANoe » vCDL » Language » IP Address Configuration

For IP-based communication, as for example SOME/IP, an IP configuration must be provided. It consists of various types of elements that are described in detail in the upcoming sections.

## VLANs

VLANs are declared on namespace level using the vlan keyword followed by its name, its unique ID (in laces) and a delimiter.

|  | Example: VLAN definition vlan VLAN1<1>;vlan VLAN2<2>; |

## Supported Attributes (VLAN)

There are no specific attributes for measurement point declarations.

## Stacks

TCP/IP stacks are used to easily handle the IP-based communication. An Ethernet adapter – also called network interface – connects the TCP/IP stack to the actual Ethernet network. Each Ethernet adapter is expected to have a unique MAC address. An Ethernet adapter may be assigned to one or multiple VLANs. In vCDL all three entity types (IP stacks, Ethernet adapters and VLAN configurations) are declared in a nested way. Note that the VLAN configurations within an Ethernet adapter reference VLAN instances that have to be explicitly defined somewhere else within the imported vCDL files.

|  | Example: IP-Stack definition [gateway ="192.168.2.1", defaultTTL=128, ipForwarding=true, tcpDelayedAcknowledge=false, tcpTimestampOption=false]ipstack IPStack{ [mac="B1:8D:F7:75:99:96", mtu=1000] ethernetadapter Adapter1 { [priority=2] VLAN1, [priority=4] VLAN2 }} |

## Supported Attributes (IP Stack)

| Name | Type | Defau

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [vCDL/Language] vCDLLanguage


# Technical Reference: vCDL

CANoe » vCDL » Language

You can use vCDL to define communication objects and their parameters for the CANoe communication concept.

| vCDL | Data Source Data sources for the CANoe configuration. |
| --- | --- |
| Language Conventions Attributes Character Set Comments Constant Declarations Literals E2E Protection Expressions Namespaces Preprocessor References Secure Onboard Communication Type Alias Versioning | Data Source – Import (vCDL) Data Source (System and Communication Setup) |
| Data Model Define and edit communication/distributed objects. |
| Communication Objects (vCDL) Encodings and Semantic Attributes Events Fields Function Prototypes Methods PDUs PDU Timings PDU Types Services Interfaces Service PDUs Signals Signal Groups Communication Objects (System and Communication Setup) Communication Objects (Concept) Distributed Objects (vCDL) Distributed Objects Interfaces Virtual Networks Distributed Objects (Concept) Participants (vCDL) Participants (System and Communication Setup) | Data Types Primitive Data Types Boolean Bytes Data Type Float Integer String Data Type Complex Data Types Array Data Types Bit Fields Enumerations List Data Types Struct Data Types Union Data Types |
| Communication (Endpoints) Endpoints of the bus and network communication of CANoe. | Binding Bind the communication objects to a transmission medium or a transmission type. |
| Endpoints (vCDL)Consumer, provider, receiver, sender Refinements (vCDL) Select Endpoin

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [vCDL/Language] vCDLListDataTypes


# List Data Types

CANoe » vCDL » Language » List Data Types

List data types are a more flexible linear collection than arrays. They are declared using the list keyword, specifying the list’s base data type and a maximum size in angle brackets. A minimum size can be defined for the list data type by the minSize attribute.

|  | Example: List type declaration with and without limits. typedef LimitedList = list<int8, 20> [minSize=5]; |

Same as array declarations, a list declaration is an unnamed data type which must be inlined in any declaration expecting a data type. A named type can be declared as an alias.

## Supported Attributes

Lists support the following attributes:

| Name | Type | Default | Semantics |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| minSize | UInt | 0 | Specifies the minimum number of elements the data type may ever contain. |
| byteOrder | String | "Intel" | Specifies whether values and length fields are serialized in Motorola or Intel byte order. Complex type list elements may override this and serialize in a different byte order. |
| fixLayout | Bool | false | Specifies whether the data type has a fix binary layout (requires all members to have a fix layout data type). |

List attribute placement is determined by the statement containing the array declaration.

Attributes for the list element data type may be placed before or after the data type.

Language Conventions

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [vCDL/Language] vCDLLiterals


# Literals

CANoe » vCDL » Language » Literals

vCDL often requires you to initialize elements such as attributes or constants with certain values. These are either single literals or expressions of multiple literals. Each data type in vCDL has its own type of literal. Following table outlines the different literals:

| Data Type | Example Literals | Additional Information |
| --- | --- | --- |
| bool | true, false | Boolean literal |
| int | 65, 0x4f, 0b1001 | Integer literals ca be written either binary, decimal or hexadecimal. |
| float, double | 4.5 | Decimal float literal |
| .067e-10 | Decimal float literal with exponent (base 10) in scientific notation |
| 0xE.3FA2p+7 | Hexadecimal float literal with exponent (base 2) in scientific notation |
| time | 56s | Available units are s, ms, us, ns, ps, fs and as |
| enum | BLUE, off, LONGER_VALUE | Enumeration literals are always written without the enumeration type. The vCDL parser derives the referred enumeration from the literal’s context. |
| string | "Description\n" | Standard string: Special characters need to be escaped with a backslash, in particular the backslash itself. |
| @"\ Description \" | Verbatim string: There is no character escaping. A single backslash remains a single backslash. |
| array, list | { "first", "second", "third" } | Full array/list literal: All elements must match the array’s/list’s data type. For arrays the number of elements must match the array’s length, for lists it must lie within the mi

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [vCDL/Language] vCDLMeasurementPoints


# Measurement Points

CANoe » vCDL » Language » Measurement Points

Measurement points allow read-only access to otherwise non-simulated ports of point-to-point communication objects. They are defined within the body of a communication object by the keyword measure, followed by the source and target endpoint combination identifying the communication path to be observed. The measurement point can optionally be named, in which case the name comes first, followed by an assignment of the endpoint combination.

|  | Example: Declaration of a measurement point for a source port. service AudioStream{ consumer Console [simulated=false]; provider CDChanger, USBAdapter; measure MP1=Console.CDChanger, Console.USBAdapter;} |

## Supported Attributes

There are no specific attributes for measurement point declarations.

Language Conventions

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [vCDL/Language] vCDLMethods


# Methods

CANoe » vCDL » Language » Methods

Methods are named function declarations within service interface declarations. Each method declaration requires a function prototype definition, which can be inlined. An inlined prototype definition does not require an explicit typedef keyword but looks similar to a normal C/C++ function declaration. It may be preceded by the optional keyword method and consists of the method’s return type followed by the method name and parameter declarations in parentheses. The parameter declarations are identical to those of an explicit prototype declaration.

|  | Example: Service with inlined method declaration. service CDPlayer{ uint16 GetTrackInfo(in string filter, out array<TrackInfo, 50> trackInfo);} |

If an explicit prototype declaration is used for methods, then the method keyword is mandatory because the declaration would otherwise look like an event or field declaration. The keyword is followed by a prototype reference and the name of the method.

|  | Example: Service with method declaration referencing an explicit prototype. typedef Prototype = uint16(in string filter, out array<TrackInfo, 50> trackInfo);service CDPlayer{ method Prototype GetTrackInfo;} |

Methods must be declared at service interface level or at service level when the interface is inlined.

## Supported Attributes

Method declarations support the following attributes:

| Name | Type | Default | Semantics |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| defaultParamsRepr | String | 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [vCDL/Language] vCDLNamespaces


# Namespaces

CANoe » vCDL » Language » Namespaces

Namespaces allow grouping top-level declarations like interfaces, services, functions, signals, data types, constants and alias names. Similar to C++, names need to be unique within the local namespace only and references to other declarations need absolute or relative qualification (including none for local references). A namespace is declared with the keyword namespace followed by its name and a body in curly braces, containing declarations belonging to the namespace. Namespace declarations can be nested, thus spanning a tree of named elements which can be qualified starting from the outermost namespace through nested namespaces up to the element name itself.

|  | Example: Nested namespace declaration with service interface. namespace Multimedia{ namespace Audio { interface IAudioSource { field uint16 CurrentTrack { get; set; notify; } } }} |

Namespace declarations may be reopened with different content, i.e. it is not necessary to declare all elements of a specific namespace within one and the same block. The content of repeated namespaces is simply considered as a single collection of declarations, all located within that namespace.

Similar to programming languages references into a namespace can be facilitated by a using statement. These statements can appear globally or within a namespace and have priority over parent namespaces when resolving references. It does not make a difference whether a using statement is l

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [vCDL/Language] vCDLParticipants


# Participants

CANoe » vCDL » Language » Participants

Participants are used to group communication endpoints which typically belong to one ECU or SWC. A participant declaration consists of the keyword participant followed by an identifier and a body in curly braces. The body consists of a list of endpoint references separated by comma.

A participant may as well be declared empty (by leaving the content of the body empty or by closing the statement with a delimiter). In that case the contained endpoints are automatically derived when the participant is referenced from endpoint declarations.

|  | Example: Declaration of a participant, grouping two endpoints. participant HeadUnit{ Multimedia::AudioStream.Console, Navigation::RoutePlanning.Display} |

Participants must be declared at namespace level, i.e. they cannot be nested in other complex types, interfaces or services.

## Supported Attributes

There are no specific attributes for participant declarations.

Language Conventions

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [vCDL/Language] vCDLPDUs


# PDUs

CANoe » vCDL » Language » PDUs

A (non-service) PDU is declared by the keyword pdu followed by its name and a reference to its type definition separated by colon. The body of the declaration contains sender and receiver endpoints.

|  | Example: PDU declaration with referenced type. pdutype DoorInfoType{ bool IsClosed; bool IsLocked; uint16 WindowPosition;}pdu DoorInfo : DoorInfoType{ sender DoorFL, DoorFR, DoorBL, DoorBR; receiver BodyController;} |

Similar as for services and service interfaces, PDU types can be declared inline, which results in mixing the endpoint declarations with the data type member declarations in the PDU’s body.

|  | Example: Inline PDU declaration. pdu DoorInfo{ bool IsClosed; bool IsLocked; uint16 WindowPosition; sender DoorFL, DoorFR, DoorBL, DoorBR; receiver BodyController;} |

PDUs must be declared at namespace level, i.e. they cannot be nested in other complex types, interfaces or services.

## Supported Attributes

PDU declarations support the following attributes:

| Name | Type | Default | Semantics |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| txValueRepr | String | "Default" | Whether transmitted PDU member values should be stored in Default, Impl or Raw representation. Can be overridden per endpoint. |
| broadcast | Bool | false | Whether the PDU is broadcast to all receiver endpoints when being sent. |

PDU attributes may be placed before keyword pdu only.

Language Conventions

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [vCDL/Language] vCDLPDUTimings


# PDU Timings

CANoe » vCDL » Language » PDU Timings

An interaction layer definition allows to specify how and when PDUs or Service PDUs are transmitted on the network. The keyword pdutiming allows to define named timings, which can be referenced from PDUs and Service PDUs through attribute timings. In case multiple timings are referenced from a communication object, the first is considered the active timing.

The timing definition allows to specify event controlled and cyclic transmission mode timings in its body. These are defined by the keywords onupdate and cyclic and both have a body definition themselves. They support the attributes onTrue and onFalse, which identifies them as a timing to be used when the PDUs timing conditions evaluate to true or false respectively. Within a timing definition there must be at most one cyclic timing for the true condition and at most one for the false condition. The same restriction applies to event-controlled timings.

The body of a cyclic timing requires the specification of an offset and a period, both time values which together define the cyclic transmission. Each of these values can be attributed with a tolerance specification, either an absolute time value or a relative value, i.e. a fractional number between 0 and 1. The offset specification can be omitted, in which case it defaults to 0s with 0 tolerance.

The body of an event-controlled timing requires the specification of a repeat count and a period, which define how to tran

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [vCDL/Language] vCDLPDUTypes


# PDU Types

CANoe » vCDL » Language » PDU Types

The layout of a PDU can be defined as a named PDU type, which consist of the list of signals being transported by the PDU and their layout information. The syntax is identical to a struct declaration but the supported set of attributes is different. A PDU type declaration starts with keyword pdutype followed by the type name and the body definition, listing the signals in the PDU.

|  | Example: Explicit PDU Type declaration. pdutype HealthInfo{ bool IsAlive; uint16 Age; uint16 LastCalibration;} |

The PDU name cannot be used like a generic data type definition but only for the declaration of a PDU or a service PDU. If there is no need to reuse the definition, then the type definition can be inlined in a PDU or service PDU declaration.

|  | Example: Inlined PDU Type declaration with layout attributes. [bitCount=64]pdu HealthInfo{ bool IsAlive; [relOffset=7] uint16 Age; uint16 LastCalibration;} |

PDU types must be declared at namespace level, i.e. they cannot be nested in other complex types, interfaces or services.

## Supported Attributes (PDU Type)

The PDU type declaration supports the following attributes:

| Name | Type | Default | Semantics |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| byteOrder | String | "Intel” | Specifies whether values are serialized in Motorola or Intel byte order. |
| fixLayout | Bool | false | Specifies whether the PDU type has a fix binary layout. Requires that all member types and signal groups have a fix l

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [vCDL/Language] vCDLPreprocessor


# Preprocessor

CANoe » vCDL » Language » Preprocessor

vCDL comes with an integrated preprocessor like those used in C/C++ and C#, but with a reduced feature set consisting of conditional parsing, regions, and pragmas. It does not support macros and include directives. However, the latter are supported through the import statements in vCDL. Every preprocessor directive starts with the #-symbol followed by the name of the directive. Any whitespace may precede the directive , e.g. for indentation purposes. As a result, a single line can contain only a single directive. As its name suggests the preprocessor is executed prior to the vCDL parser. Thus, preprocessor directives are not restricted to any syntactic vCDL structures like scopes and statements. It is even possible to break block comments with preprocessor directives. However, preprocessor constructs themselves must be properly nested, e.g. a region starting inside a conditional scope may not expand outside this scope. The introductory example shows some practical use cases for preprocessor directives.

#pragma encoding UTF-7#define CANOE_VERSION 15#define DEBUG#if CANOE_VERSION == 15 version 1.4;#elif CANOE_VERSION == 14version 1.3;#elseversion 1.2;#endifnamespace Content{ // Regions can be collapsed in the editor #region Data Types typedef IntType = int16; typedef FloatType = float; #endregion interface DOInterface { provided data IntType Member; }}

## Conditional Parsing

Conditional parsing can be used to activate 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [vCDL/Language] vCDLPrimitiveDataTypes


# Primitive Data Types

CANoe » vCDL » Language » Primitive Data Types

Primitive data types can be used for the declaration of complex data types, method parameters and return values, signals, fields and events. Supported primitive data types are signed and unsigned integer types of a specific bit width int<n>, uint<n> with n in the range 1 through 64, bool, float and double.

## Supported Attributes

When used in declarations of different type, more fine-grained control over the data type can be achieved by adding attributes to the declaration which apply to its data type. Which attributes are allowed also depends on the nature of the primitive data type, e.g. units can only be associated with numeric types.

| Name | Type | Default | Type Restrictions | Semantics |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| byteOrder | String | "Intel” |  | Specifies whether values are serialized in Motorola or Intel byte order. (only valid for multibyte data types) |
| rawType | Typename | Unspecified | Numeric | Specifies a primitive data type for serializing values. (numerical types only) |
| min | Numeric | Unspecified | Numeric | Specifies a minimum value in physical representation. (numerical types only) |
| max | Numeric | Unspecified | Numeric | Specifies a maximum value in physical representation. (numeric types only) |
| unit | String | "" | Numeric | Specifies a unit string to associate with the numerical values in physical representation. (numeric types only) |
| raw2phys | {Reference}

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [vCDL/Language] vCDLReferences


# References

CANoe » vCDL » Language » References

References allow to specify (directed) associations between declarations. The reference consists of an optional list of namespaces separated by :: (same as C++) and the name of the referenced declaration. If a sub-element of a declaration must be referenced (e.g. an event in a service interface declaration), its name is appended separated by a dot (again same as C++ member access).

Any reference is resolved as local as possible, i.e. the first attempt is to find a match by appending the reference to the current namespace. If that attempt fails, further attempts are made by traversing the hierarchy of namespaces upwards and each time appending the reference until at last the reference may be resolved through the (invisible) root namespace.

Reference resolution can be controlled by using namespace statements, which themselves reference a namespace. Declarations from that namespace are considered for resolution after local resolution failed and before resolution in the parent namespace is tried.

A reference can be globally qualified (by prefixing it with ::), which has the effect that resolution is only possible with the exact specified declaration path.

|  | Example: Reference to service interface from sub-namespace. In this example, the first attempt to resolve Audio::IAudioSource in the local namespace will succeed. Had it not succeeded, the next attempt would have been to find Audio::IAudioSource in the root namespace.

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [vCDL/Language] vCDLSecureOnboardCommunication


# Secure Onboard Communication

CANoe » vCDL » Language » PDU Timings

The Secure Onboard Communication shall provide authentication and integrity protection for sensitive data transmitted over the network. It is similar in nature to E2E protection and accordingly the language integration is comparable as well. There is an explicitly named profile definition, introduced by the keyword secoc followed by the profile name. Profile parameters are specified as attributes on the profile definition.

|  | Example: Secure Onboard Communication profile definition. [ authAlgorithm="CMAC/AES-128", authInfoTxLength=28, authenticationRetries=3, freshnessTimestampPeriodFactor=1, freshnessValueLength=64, freshnessValueTxLength=4, useFreshnessTimestamp=true]secoc SecOCProfile3; |

A defined profile can then be referenced from PDUs, Service PDUs and SOME/IP events and fields by attribute secOCProfile. Additional attributes allow individual settings per communication object instance. All these attributes can already be attached to the corresponding type definitions (e.g. a PDU type definition) and can be refined at the instance level.

|  | Example: PDU refinement with Secure Onboard Communication parameters. [ secOCDataId=17, secOCFreshnessValueId=72 secOCProfile=SecOCProfile3];refine EngineControl.EngineInfo |

The parameters are designed along the AUTOSAR Secure Onboard Communication specification and valid values are depending on the selected profile.

## Supported Attributes (Profile)

|

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [vCDL/Language] vCDLServiceInterfaces


# Interfaces

CANoe » vCDL » Language » Interfaces

A detailed explanation for each member along with the concept of member directions can be found on the Distributed Objects page.

If features are mixed, e.g., if a distributed object interface contains PDUs or a service interface inherits from other interfaces, there will be errors during import. See the common errors section below for what may be wrong. As rule of thumb respect following guidelines:

- Have a direction for each member (except for embedded members) when using the interface for distributed objects.

- Have no directions at all in the interface when using it for services.

- Only inherit from other interfaces when using the interface for distributed objects.

|  | Example: Two interfaces, one for distributed objects, the other for services interface DOInterface : DOBaseInterface{ internal data Struct DataMember; provided method Prototype MethodMember; provided uint48 InlinedMethod(float Param); consumed event void EventMember; consumed field list<int32> FieldMember { get; notify; } internal reference<OtherDOInterface> ReferenceMember; embedded OtherDOInterface EmbeddedMember; embedded InlinedEmbeddedMember { internal data int32 EmbeddedDataMember; }}interface ServiceInterface{ method Prototype MethodMember; event void EventMember; field list<int32> FieldMember { set; } pdu PDUType ServicePDU;} |

The syntax is similar to other object types in vCDL. The keyword interface followed by its name and a bracket encl

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [vCDL/Language] vCDLServicePDUs


# Service PDUs

CANoe » vCDL » Language » Service PDUs

Service PDUs are simply PDUs declared within a service interface. They reference a PDU type and thus specify a layout for data elements to be transmitted with the PDU. A service PDU is declared by the keyword pdu, followed by a PDU type reference and the PDU name. The statement is finished by a semicolon.

|  | Example: Explicit PDU type declaration and service PDU. pdutype TrackData{ string [maxBytes=32] Title; uint16 TrackNumber; float [unit="s", relOffset=16] Length;}interface IMediaSource{ pdu TrackData CurrentTrackData;} |

The PDU type declaration can be inlined with the PDU declaration. In that case the PDU type reference is omitted and the statement is finished by a body declaration identical to the PDU type declaration body.

|  | Example: Service PDU with inlined PDU type declaration. interface IMediaSource{ pdu CurrentTrackData { string [maxBytes=32] Title; uint16 TrackNumber; float [unit="s", relOffset=16] Length; }} |

Service PDUs must be declared at service interface level or at service level when the interface is inlined.

## Supported Attributes

Service PDU declarations support the following attributes:

| Name | Type | Default | Semantics |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| txValueRepr | String | "Default" | Whether transmitted signal values should be stored in Default, Impl or Raw representation. Can be overridden per endpoint. |

Service PDU attributes may be placed before a PDU declaration. If the PDU ty

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [vCDL/Language] vCDLServices


# Services

CANoe » vCDL » Language » Services

Services declare specific implementations of service interfaces, providing information about communication endpoints (consumers and providers) and probably inlined binding attributes and declarations. A service declaration starts with the keyword service followed by an identifier and an optional interface implementation spec consisting of a colon followed by an interface reference. The service body is enclosed in curly braces and contains endpoint and optionally inlined binding specific declarations.

|  | Example: Basic service declaration. service CDPlayer : IMediaSource{ // end point declarations} |

If the implementation spec is omitted, then the service implicitly defines an interface inline, i.e. method, field, event and PDU declarations can be provided directly within the service declaration body. Otherwise the service will implement the exact set of methods, fields, events and PDUs declared in the interface. Still the service declaration may refine the declarations of the implemented interface and add further attributes (e.g. SOME/IP specific method and event IDs).

|  | Example: Basic service declaration. service CDPlayer{ consumer TouchScreen, Dashboard; provider Console; field uint16 NumberOfTracks { get; } field uint16 CurrentTrack { get; set; notify; } void NextTrack(); void PrevTrack();} |

Services must be declared at namespace level, i.e. they cannot be nested in other complex types, interfaces or services.

## 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [vCDL/Language] vCDLSignalGroups


# Signal Groups

CANoe » vCDL » Language » PDU Timings

A Signal Group is a named group of signals defined within a PDU Type which allows to attach e.g. E2E configuration settings to that specific group of signals. A signal group definition is nested in a PDU Type definition by the keyword group and provides a list of signals as its body definition.

|  | Example: PDU type declaration with signal group. pdutype DoorInfoType{ group LockInfo { bool IsClosed; bool IsLocked; } uint16 WindowPosition;} |

The same Signal Group declaration is possible when a PDU Type is inlined in a PDU declaration.

## Supported Attributes (Signal Group)

| Name | Type | Default | Semantics |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| transferProperty | String | "Undefined" | Enumerated value which identifies how the signal group is transferred. |
| suppressRepetition | Bool | false | Whether configured transfer repetitions should be applied. |
| relOffset | UInt | 0 | Specifies an offset of a Signal Group relative to the end of its predecessor (or the beginning of the PDU in case of the first member) in bits. |
| fixLayout | Bool | false | Specifies whether the data type has a fix binary layout (requires all member data types to have a fix layout). |

## Supported transferProperty attribute values

The transferProperty attribute is enumerated, allowing the following values.

| Name | Semantics |
| --- | --- |
| "Undefined" | No transmission property is defined. Same as not specifying the attribute. |
| "Pending

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [vCDL/Language] vCDLSignals


# Signals

CANoe » vCDL » Language » Signals

A signal is declared by the keyword signal followed by the signal’s data type and name. The body of a signal declaration contains sender and receiver endpoints.

|  | Example: Primitive type and complex type signal. struct EngineInfoType{ float Temperature; float OilPressure; uint16 RPM;}signal uint16 Speed{ sender WheelFL, WheelFR, WheelBL, WheelBR; receiver Dashboard;}signal EngineInfoType EngineInfo{ sender Engine; receiver Dashboard, Navigation;} |

Signals can be attributed with PDU mappings, which implies that the signal is only sent via the mapped PDU and according endpoints. The signal attribute mappings contains a list of references to the PDUs to which the signal shall be mapped and the same attribute at signal endpoints lists the PDU endpoints which send or receive the signal. A signal must not be mapped to multiple members of the same PDU and if none of its endpoints is mapped to an endpoint of the same PDU, the mapping has no effect. Further it is necessary to map signal sender endpoints to PDU sender/provider endpoints only and signal receiver endpoints to PDU receiver/consumer endpoints only.

|  | Example: Inline PDU definition with mapped signal. pdu DoorInfo{ bool IsClosed; bool IsLocked; uint16 WindowPosition; sender DoorFL, DoorFR, DoorBL, DoorBR; receiver BodyController;}[mappings=DoorInfo.WindowPosition]signal uint16 DoorFL_WindowPosition{ sender DoorFL [mappings=DoorInfo.DoorFL]; receiver BodyController [ma

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [vCDL/Language] vCDLSOMEIPBinding


# SOME/IP Binding

CANoe » vCDL » Language » SOME/IP Binding

Binding communication objects to the SOME/IP protocol stack requires to configure numerical identifiers, addresses, event groups, service discovery and serialization parameters. Most of these are specified as attributes on language elements, though the address and event group configuration add some binding specific language elements. All attributes are defined in the binding namespace SOMEIP.

## Table of Contents

| Service Configuration |
| --- |
| Address Configuration |
| Serialization Configuration |
| Service Discovery |

## Service Configuration [▲ back]

The service configuration is concerned with the specification of SOME/IP identifiers to services, endpoints, events, methods, and PDUs as well as the definition of and subscription to event groups. All identifiers are simply attached to the corresponding language elements by binding specific attributes. The qualification with the binding namespace can be omitted when the service has SOME/IP binding.

|  | Example: Service definition with inlined identifier attributes. [SOMEIP::serviceId=12]service EngineControl : IEngineControl{ binding SOMEIP; provider Engine [instanceId=17]; consumer Dashboard [clientId=23], Navigation [clientId=24]; void RequestFuelSaveMode() [methodId=137]; event bool OilPressureLow [eventId=287]; fielduint16 EngineSpeed [getterId=28, eventId=91]; pdu EngInfoType EngineInfo [pduId=67];} |

The definition of event groups requires a bind

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [vCDL/Language] vCDLStringDataTypes


# String Data Type

CANoe » vCDL » Language » String Data Type

String data types support multiple character encodings, where UTF-8 is used as default encoding for simple string type declarations. Further strings are unlimited in length by default. It is possible to limit the string length either by number of characters or by number of bytes (in both cases the length specification includes the terminating 0).

|  | Example: Simple UTF-8 encoded string with unlimited length. string nextExit; |

The used encoding can be specified in attribute encoding. Attributes maxBytes and maxChars limit the string’s length. Only one of these latter attributes may be specified at the same time.

|  | Example: Latin-1 encoded string with limited length. string nextExit [encoding="ISO 8859-1", maxChars=100]; |

Further the string data type can be declared to have a fix memory layout (attribute fixLayout), which requires that the length is limited either in characters or in bytes.

String data types can be declared inline wherever a data type is expected. They can be named by defining an alias type.

## Supported Attributes

The string data type supports the following attributes:

| Name | Type | Default | Semantics |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| byteOrder | String | "Intel" | Specifies whether values and length fields are serialized in Motorola or Intel byte order. |
| encoding | String | "UTF-8" | Selects a character encoding for the string from a predefined set of encodings. Supported are UT

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [vCDL/Language] vCDLStuctDataTypes


# Struct Data Types

CANoe » vCDL » Language » Struct Data Types

Struct data types are declared using the struct keyword followed by the data type name and a body declaration in curly braces. In the body, the struct members are declared in order, specifying a data type and a name for the member.

|  | Example: Struct definition with primitive data type members. struct Position{ float x; float y; float z;} |

Struct members can be declared optional and may specify a bit offset relative to the end of the predecessor or beginning of the struct (attribute keywords optional and relOffset). In addition, primitive type members can use attributes from semantic type definitions (e.g. unit, min, max, …).

|  | Example: Struct definition with complex data type member and additional attributes. struct Sample{ Position Pos; float Timestamp [optional, unit="ns", relOffset=32];} |

Each struct definition implies a new data type, i.e. the data type name can be used like built-in primitive data types. Struct definitions always must be named and cannot be inlined with a member, parameter or communication object declaration.

Struct data types must be declared at namespace level, i.e. their declaration cannot be nested in other complex types, interfaces or services.

## Supported Attributes (Struct)

Struct declarations support the following attributes:

| Name | Type | Default | Semantics |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| byteOrder | String | "Intel" | Specifies whether values and length fields 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [vCDL/Language] vCDLTypeAlias


# Type Alias

CANoe » vCDL » Language » Type Alias

Type alias definitions allow to give a specific name to another type. The syntax is similar to type alias declarations in C++11, using the keyword typedef followed by an identifier for the new type, the assignment operator = and a reference to the original type (which can be a primitive type, a user defined type or an alias by itself). The new type can be used in just the same way as the original type.

|  | Example: Defining a temperature type. typedef Temperature = int32; |

Type aliases must be declared at namespace level, i.e. they cannot be nested in other complex types, interfaces or services.

## Supported Attributes

There are no specific attributes for type alias definitions.

Attributes for the referenced data type may be placed before the typedef keyword or before the statement delimiter.

Language Conventions

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [vCDL/Language] vCDLUnionDataTypes


# Union Data Types

CANoe » vCDL » Language » Union Data Types

Union data types are declared using the union keyword followed by the data type name and a body declaration in curly braces. In the body, the union members are declared in any order, specifying a data type and a name for the member.

|  | Example: Union definition with mixed data types. union Location{ Position RelativePos; Position AbsolutePos; float Distance;} |

Additional attributes allow to explicitly identify a union discriminator in a surrounding struct data type and to specify discriminator value ranges for each union member. Analogous to struct members, union members can be augmented by all attributes defined for their data type.

|  | Example: Union definition with explicit discriminator settings. union Location{ Position RelativePos [discriminatorValues=(0,3)]; Position AbsolutePos [discriminatorValues=(4,6)]; float Distance [discriminatorValues=7];}struct LocContainer{ int16 ValidMember; [discriminator=ValidMember] Location TheLocation;} |

If no explicit discriminator values are specified, then each member shall have a default discriminator value associated, which matches its index in the union declaration. If any member has an explicit discriminator value, then all members shall have an explicit value. The discriminator values can be expressed either as a single value or as a series of ranges, where every two consecutive values are the lower and upper limit of an inclusive range.

Each union defini

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [vCDL/Language] vCDLVersioning


# Versioning

CANoe » vCDL » Language » Versioning

Versioning vCDL allows the associated tooling to be forward and backward compatible. Each release of vCDL is associated with a current version in the form <major>.<minor>, which is used to restrict the set of allowed keywords, statements and attributes. The major version is typically incremented for incompatible changes, i.e. older versions cannot be parsed with newer tooling. The minor version is usually incremented when the set of supported attributes is modified or when additional optional statements are supported.

The version statement is started by the version keyword, followed by the version number. The statement must appear as the first statement of a vCDL file. In case of importing other files, each file must have the same major vCDL version.

|  | Example: Simple first version statement. version 1.0; |

Specifying a minor version below the current maximum supported will cause import errors in case any features not supported by that version are used. This supports writing vCDL source which can be imported by earlier CANoe versions than the one currently being used.

|  | Example You are writing vCDL source for a project, which must be compatible to CANoe 12.0 although currently you have CANoe 12.0 SP3 installed. In that case you specify version 1.0 in your sources such that you are warned if you try to use any feature not supported by CANoe 12.0 (e.g. PDU timings or E2E protection). |

Note that you cannot mix impo

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [vCDL/Language] vCDLVirtualNetworks


# Virtual Networks

CANoe » vCDL » Language » Virtual Networks

Virtual networks (VNs) are declared by the keyword virtualnetwork followed by the virtual network’s name. Any attribute that is allowed for distributed object interfaces, instances and their members may be attached to the virtual network serving as default for entities attached to the virtual network. To attach a distributed object interface, instance, reference or container to a virtual network use the attribute virtualNetworks. As the attribute name suggests multiple virtual networks can be assigned as list, but the entity is only attached to the first virtual network in the list. The other virtual networks simply provide default values for attributes. Members can also be attached to a virtual network via the attribute virtualNetwork (singular). If not specified, they are attached to the next VN found in the hierarchy up to the distributed objects interface. If there is no virtual network specified at all, they are attached to the default network.

virtualnetwork VN1;[CommunicationPattern=PublishSubscribe]virtualnetwork VN2;[AutoAnnounce]virtualnetwork VN3;[virtualNetworks=(VN1,VN2)]interface DOInterface{ [virtualNetwork=VN3] provided data int32 MemberA; provided data int32 MemberB;}

Note that although DOInterface is attached to VN1 it gets the communication pattern Publish-Subscribe from VN2. MemberA overrides the network set of the interface and is attached to VN3 instead. The same semantics apply to distri

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [VisualSequencer] VisualSequencer


# Visual Sequencer

CANoe » Visual Sequencer

|  | The Visual Sequencer allows you to create automated command sequences that can stimulate the network and control applications. You can use loops and conditional command blocks (If, Else If, Else, End If) to structure the individual steps, e.g. to set signal and system values or to send messages and wait. Each sequence is shown in a separate window and can be edited at any time, even while a measurement is running. You can start the sequence at measurement start or manually. In Debug mode you can set breakpoints to pause processing during the sequence. You can log the reaction of the network by using text output commands to automatically log output in the Write Window or in a file. |

| Quick Access Visual Sequencer Commands Configuration You can insert new sequences using the Automation Sequences dialog Creating a sequence Advanced settings | Quick Access | Visual Sequencer Commands | Configuration | You can insert new sequences using the Automation Sequences dialog Creating a sequence Advanced settings | Functions and Control Start, Pause, Stop Debug mode Overview of commands Description of the columns Toolbar Context Menu Keyboard operation | Functions and Control | Start, Pause, Stop Debug mode Overview of commands Description of the columns Toolbar Context Menu Keyboard operation |

| Visual Sequencer Commands |

### [VisualSequencer] VisualSequencerColumns


# Visual Sequencer Columns

Visual Sequencer » Columns

Meaning of the columns in the Visual Sequencer Window (from left to right):

| Column | Meaning |
| --- | --- |
| Icon column | This shows the icon of the group to which the command belongs.You can insert breakpoints in this column. |
| Command | Contains the relevant command for the row. |
| Object | Contains the relevant object of the command.Objects can be: Signals System variables Environment variables Frames/messages File paths Character strings |
| Operator | The operator determines how the object is processed. |
| Operand | Some commands use an operator to link the object with an operand.For instance the command Check | SysVarA | == | SysVarB checks whether the value of the object SysVarA is equal to the value of the operand SysVarB. |
| Wait | Wait time in milliseconds following execution of a command. |
| Comments | Comments about the command row.You can hide this column (see toolbar). |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [VisualSequencer] VisualSequencerCommands


# Visual Sequencer Commands

Visual Sequencer » Commands

| Set Signals/Variables |
| --- |
| Set | Sets the value of a signal, a system variable or an environment variable. |
| Map | Maps system variables or environment variables to other system variables, environment variables or signals. |
| Wait Commands |
| Wait | Pauses the sequence for a specified time period. |
| Wait For | Pauses the execution of the sequence until a variable meets a certain condition. |
| Wait For CAN Frame | Stops the sequence execution until a certain CAN frame arrives. |
| Wait For CAN Raw Frame | Stops the sequence execution until a CAN frame arrives. |
| Wait For GMLAN Frame | Stops the sequence execution until a certain GMLAN frame arrives. |
| Wait For LIN Frame | Stops the sequence execution until a certain LIN frame arrives. |
| Wait For Key | Stops the sequence execution until a certain key is pressed. |
| Wait For A429 Word | Stops the execution of the sequence until reception of an ARINC429 word that is defined in the database. |
| Wait For Raw A429 Word | Stops the execution of the sequence until reception of a specified ARINC429 word. |
| Messages: Parameterizing and Sending |
| Set CAN Raw Frame | Parametrizes a CAN frame without accessing a database. |
| Set CAN Cyclic Frame | Starts cyclical transmission of a CAN frame. |
| Set CAN Cyclic Raw Frame | Starts/stops cyclical transmission of a CAN frame that has been parameterized in raw format (without database support). |
| Send CAN

### [VisualSequencer] VisualSequencerConfiguration


# Configuration

Visual Sequencer » Configuration

Clicking the Configuration button () opens the Advanced Settings dialog.

## Settings

### Execution

Here you can define, where to execute the Visual Sequence.

- Standard:The sequence will be executed in the simulation environment.

- OnBoard:The sequence will be executed on a Vector Interface (VN1630/VN7600). In this mode the defined wait and cycle times are met extremely precise.

Please consider the following restrictions for OnBoard execution:

- Some commands cannot be used or only with restrictions (see notes on the command's page).

- You cannot use the signal layer.

- Within a sequence commands may only access buses that are accessed by one shared interface.

The following sequence for example can only be executed OnBoard if the channels CAN1 and CAN2 are both assigned to the same interface.

Send CAN Raw Frame | CAN1::0x100 | = | AA BB

Send CAN Raw Frame | CAN2::0x200 | = | CC DD

### Signal Layer

In CAN simulations, the CANoe Interaction Layer provides signal-oriented access to the bus. The Use Signal Layer setting lets you specify whether this intermediate layer is used when a sequence is executed.

In order to use the signal layer, the corresponding send node needs to be simulated for each of the signals used in the sequence (command: Set).

If the signal layer is not used (setting: Send messages manually), cyclical messages that transport signals need to be sent separately (commands: Set CAN Cyclic Frame an

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [VisualSequencer] VisualSequencerDebugMode


# Debug Mode

Visual Sequencer » Debug Mode

The Visual Sequencer's Debug mode is useful for error search and step-by-step execution.Press to activate the Debug mode.

## Progress Marker

In Debug mode the next command to be executed is marked by coloring the relevant row.

## Breakpoints

At so-called breakpoints the execution of a sequence is halted.

To insert and delete breakpoints, use the context menu or double-click the command icon. When the sequence pauses at a breakpoint, you can resume it using the [Start] button () or continue in [Step-by-Step] () mode.

|  | Note Inactive rows and comment rows do not affect the execution of a sequence. You cannot set breakpoints in these rows. |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [VisualSequencer] VisualSequencerKeyboardOperation


# Keyboard Operation

Visual Sequencer » Keyboard Operation

| Functional Description | Key Combination |
| --- | --- |
| Move the current cell selection | Arrow keys: <Up>, <Down>, <Left> and <Right> |
| Exit cell and apply changes | <Tab> |
| Exit cell and discard changes | <Esc> |
| Open selection list | <Alt> + <Down arrow> |
| Open symbol selection list | <Alt> + <Down arrow> |
| Delete row | <Del> |
| Select all rows | <Ctrl> + <A> |

Visual Sequencer Toolbar | Visual Sequencer Context Menu

### [VisualSequencer] VisualSequencerSequence


# Creating a Sequence

Visual Sequencer » Creating a Sequence

You can insert new sequences using the Automation Sequences dialog.

To create a sequence in the Visual Sequencer, you define a series of commands. To do this, you insert new rows into the Visual Sequencer table and then populate them.

Depending on the command you've selected, the row will have up to five cells (columns).

The following support functions make it easier to populate the rows:

- Selection lists for commands and operators

- Symbol selection with text completion in the Object and Operator columns

- Dragging & dropping symbols from the Symbol Explorer

- Exporting () and importing () sequences to/from a file

- After creating the command list you can validate the sequence with the button in the toolbar.

- If the cell contents are invalid an error symbol appears ().If you hover the mouse pointer over the error symbol, a note about the cause of the error and how to eliminate it is displayed.

- The status of the sequence is shown in the status line at the bottom of the window:

- in progress (),

- sequence executable (),

- sequence faulty ().

Start, Stop, Step-by-Step Execution | Advanced Settings | Toolbar | Context Menu

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [VisualSequencer] VisualSequencerShortcutMenu


# Context Menu

Visual Sequencer » Context Menu

The following context menu is available when you're working in the table:

Insert new row Inserts a new command row above the selected row.

Delete Deletes the selected row from the table.

Cut Removes the selected rows from the table and places them in the clipboard.

Copy Copies the selected rows to the clipboard.

Paste Inserts the command rows in the clipboard above the selected row.

Activate Activates all selected rows.

Deactivate Deactivates all selected rows. Deactivated rows are not included in the sequence execution.

Activate all Activates all rows in the sequence.

Deactivate all Deactivates all rows in the sequence.

Set/remove breakpoint Inserts or removes a breakpoint.

|  | Note A sequence cannot be edited while it is running; all the menu commands are therefore deactivated. |

Visual Sequencer: Toolbar | Visual Sequencer: Keyboard Operation

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [VisualSequencer] VisualSequencerStartPauseStop


# Start, Stop, Step-by-Step Execution

Visual Sequencer » Start, Stop, Step-by-Step Execution

You can start the visual sequence using the run button () when the following conditions are met:

- A measurement is started

- The sequence does not contain any errors, i.e., there is no error symbol () in any of the rows.You can temporarily deactivate rows that contain errors using the context menu.

You can pause () or stop () the sequence at any time.

## Repeated Execution

If the () button is activated in the toolbar, the sequence is executed repeatedly.

## Start on Measurement Start

To start a sequence as soon as the measurement starts, activate the () button. In standalone mode this is the only way to start a visual sequence.

## Execute until Measurement Stop

Here you can set whether the sequence shall run until measurement stop () after the sequence executed its last command. This setting equals a Wait command at the end of the sequence with the wait time set to infinite. This is especially useful with commands that are active as long as the sequence hasn't been stopped, like Set CAN Cyclic Frame and Map.

Toolbar | Debug mode

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [VisualSequencer] VisualSequencerToolbar


# Toolbar

Visual Sequencer » Toolbar

You can control the most important Visual Sequencer functions via the toolbar:

| Symbol | Description |
| --- | --- |
|  | Starts the sequence.This button is available only when the sequence is stopped or paused a measurement is running |
|  | Executes the next command in Debug mode |
|  | Stops execution of the sequence |
|  | Pauses execution of the sequence |
|  | Switches cyclical repetition mode on/off. |
|  | Switches Start Sequence on Measurement Start mode on/off |
|  | Switches the Execute until Measurement Stop mode on/off |
|  | Checks whether the sequence is error-free.Errors in the sequence are marked with the error symbol ().Place the mouse pointer on the error symbol to get information on the error cause and a correction. |
|  | Starts the Debug mode |
|  | Opens the Advanced Settings dialog |
|  | Imports the sequence from a file. The commands will be added to the end of the existing command list.Supported file formats: .vsq: exported Visual Sequence .gen: Send list (configured CAN messages) of the Generator. |
|  | Exports the sequence to a file (.vsq). |
|  | Undoes the most recent change |
|  | Reverts the most recent undo operation |
|  | Hides/shows the comments column |

Keyboard Operation | Context Menu

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [VisualSequencer] VSCANRawMessageConfiguration


# Configuration of a CAN Raw Message

Visual Sequencer » Configuration of a CAN Raw Message

## Settings

- Channel The CAN channel the message will be sent on.

- Identifier The identifier of the CAN message.The value of this field is interpreted as decimal number.To type in a hexadecimal value the prefix 0x must be applied (e.g. 0x100).Check the Extended flag below the field to specify an extended CAN identifier.

- Selector This field is only available with the Set CAN Raw Frame command.Here you can specify which part of the message you want to set in the Operand column of the command.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |

### [VisualSequencer/VSCommands] VSCommandCheck


# Check

Visual Sequencer » Commands » Check

## Description

The Check command performs a simple check on a system or environment variable, or checks the current value of an ARINC429 signal.

The result of the check is either displayed in the Write Window or saved to the logging file.

You can set the target path of the text output in the Advanced Settings dialog.

| Availability | Since Version | Restrictions |
| --- | --- | --- |
| CANoe | 7.5 8.5 SP4 (A429) | not available for OnBoard |
| CANalyzer | 7.5 8.5 SP4 (A429) | not available for OnBoard |

## Objects

| Object | Description |
| --- | --- |
| CAN signal | — |
| LIN signal | — |
| ARINC429 signal (an ARINC429 signal that is defined in the database) | — |
| System variable | — |
| Environment variable | — |

## Operators

| Operator | Description |
| --- | --- |
| =, !=, >, <, , <= | Checks the value of the object and the operand according to the specified operation. |
| in range | Checks whether the value of the object falls within or is at the limits of the specified range(low limit <= value <= high limit). |
| outside range | Checks whether the value of the object falls outside of the specified range. |
| Raw value operators (only with signals and members of system variables) | Operators with the addition [raw] compare the signal value without using the conversion formula from the database: = [raw] != [raw] > [raw] < [raw] >= [raw] <= [raw] in range [raw] outside range [raw] | = [raw] |  | != [raw] |  | > [raw]

### [VisualSequencer/VSCommands] VSCommandControlReplayblock


# Control Replay Block

Visual Sequencer » Commands » Control Replay Block

|  | Changes This command replaces the obsolete command Replay! |

## Description

Starts/stops a configured Replay Block or changes the source file of the Replay Block. The Replay Block must be configured in the Simulation Setup.

On stop of the sequence all replay blocks started by the sequence will be stopped. Please ensure the desired replay time by using additional waiting times or by setting option Execute until Measurement Stop.

| Availability | Since Version | Restrictions |
| --- | --- | --- |
| CANoe | 8.0 SP4 | not available for OnBoard |
| CANalyzer | — |  |

## Objects

| Object | Description |
| --- | --- |
| <name of the replay block> | Name of the Replay Blocks as it is in the Simulation Setup; content (value) of the Replay Name field in the configuration dialog of the Replay Block. The name of the Replay Block has to be unique. |

## Operators

| Operator | Description |
| --- | --- |
| start | Starts replaying the file. |
| stop | Stops replaying the file. |
| setfile | Changes the source file of the Replay Block to the path set in the operand. This is allowed even during running the previous file. In this case the replay is stopped, the previous file is closed, the new file is opened and the replay is started using the new file. If the start operator shall be used immediately after this operator you have to set a wait time after setfile (in the Wait field of the setfile operator;

### [VisualSequencer/VSCommands] VSCommandIf


# If

Visual Sequencer » Commands » If

## Description

If adds a conditional branch to the sequence.

- Else If adds an alternative conditional branch to the sequence in case the condition for the previous If or Else If command is not met.

- Else adds an alternate branch in case the condition for the previous If or Else If command is not met.

- End If ends the If block.

| Availability | Since Version | Restrictions |
| --- | --- | --- |
| CANoe | 7.5 | only system variables can be used OnBoard |
| CANalyzer | 7.5 | only system variables can be used OnBoard |

| Object | Description |
| --- | --- |
| CAN signal | — |
| CAN signal | — |
| System variable | — |
| Environment variable | — |

## Operators

| Operator | Description |
| --- | --- |
| ==, !=, >, <, >=, <= | Checks the value of the object and the operand according to the specified operation. |
| in range | Checks whether the value of the object falls within or is at the limits of the specified range (lowlimit <= value <= highlimit). |
| outside range | Checks whether the value of the object falls outside of the specified range. |
| Raw value operators (only with signals and members of system variables) | Operators with the addition [raw] compare the signal value without using the conversion formula from the database: = [raw] != [raw] > [raw] < [raw] >= [raw] <= [raw] in range [raw] outside range [raw] | = [raw] |  | != [raw] |  | > [raw] |  | < [raw] |  | >= [raw] |  | <= [raw] |  | in range [raw] |

### [VisualSequencer/VSCommands] VSCommandLINControlScheduler


# LIN Control Scheduler

Visual Sequencer » Commands » LIN Control Scheduler

## Description

Starts or stop the LIN Scheduler or switches the current LIN Scheduler table.

| Availability | Since Version | Restrictions |
| --- | --- | --- |
| CANoe | 8.1 | not available for OnBoard |
| CANalyzer | 8.1 | not available for OnBoard |

## Objects

| Object | Description |
| --- | --- |
| <channel> | The channel on which the command is effective. |

## Operators

| Operator | Description |
| --- | --- |
| start | starts the LIN Scheduler |
| stop | stops the LIN Scheduler |
| settable | switches the current LIN Scheduler table; the changes are immediately effective |

## Operands

| Description |
| --- |
| For the operators start and stop this filed remains empty. |
| Only for operator settable: The name of the table – as defined in the LDF – that has been assigned to the selected channel. The change is immediately effective and the LIN Scheduler starts executing the new table from slot 0. |

## Example

|  | In the following example Unconditional Frames from the Scheduler tables that are assigned to the two channels will be sent for 1 second on these two LIN channels. Finally the Scheduler will be stopped. If the Scheduler already was in state "stopped" at the beginning, only messages from tables 2 and 3 of the particular LDF files will be sent. Command Object Operator Operand Wait [ms] Wait 1000 ms Text 0 LIN Control Scheduler LIN1 settable LIN1Table2 0 LIN Control Scheduler LI

### [VisualSequencer/VSCommands] VSCommandLINSendSleep


# LIN Send Sleep

Visual Sequencer » Commands » LIN Send Sleep

NAVIGATION PATH

## Description

Sends a LIN Sleep Frame with an optional Wakeup Frame ID.

| Availability | Since Version | Restrictions |
| --- | --- | --- |
| CANoe | 8.1 | not available for OnBoard |
| CANalyzer | 8.1 | not available for OnBoard |

## Objects

| Object | Description |
| --- | --- |
| <channel> | The channel on which the command is effective. |

## Operators

| Operator | Description |
| --- | --- |
| id | After the successional wake-up an additional LIN frame will be sent with the frame ID that is set as operand. |
| <no operator> | No additional frame will be sent after the wake-up. |

## Operands

| Operand/with Operator | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Only for operator id | The ID of the LIN frame that shall be sent additionally after the wake-up. |

## Example

|  | In the following example network LIN1 will be sent to sleep for 1 second and waked up afterwards. After the wake-up nothing will be sent on the bus (as long as the is no active scheduler). After another second the bus will be sent to sleep anew for a second. Finally, after the wake-up a LIN Frame with ID 0x0B will be sent. ### [VisualSequencer/VSCommands] VSCommandMap


# Map

Visual Sequencer » Commands » Map

## Description

The Map command maps system variables or environment variables to other system variables, environment variables or signals.

Value changes of the source variable are written to the target variable immediately.

The value mapping to the target variable is valid as long as

- the measurement is running and

- the sequence is executed and

- no other variable is mapped to that source variable and

- the mapping has not been disconnected with the reset operator.

User the Execute until Measurement Stop option to execute a sequence until the end of the measurement.

| Availability | Since Version | Restrictions |
| --- | --- | --- |
| CANoe | 7.5 SP1 | not available for OnBoard |
| CANalyzer | 7.5 SP1 | not available for OnBoard |

## Objects

| Object | Description |
| --- | --- |
| CAN signal | — |
| LIN signal | — |
| System variable | — |
| Environment variable | — |

## Operators

| Operator | Description |
| --- | --- |
| = | Maps a source variable (operand) to a target variable (object). |
| = [raw] | Maps a source variable without using the conversion formula from the database. (Only with signals and members of system variables.) |
| reset | undoes a mapping |

## Operands

| Operand/with Operator | Description |
| --- | --- |
| <source variable> | System variable Environment variable |

## Example

|  | The following example maps changes of the system variable SysvarGear to the signal Gear. Command Object Operator
