### [VTSystem/VT1004] VT1004InternalElectronicLoad


# Internal Electronic Load

VT System » Modules » VT1004/VT1104 » Internal Electronic Load

The module has an internal electronic load for each channel. The load is connected via two relays. These relays can only be closed/opened together.

|  | Note To be able to change that with coming firmware versions there are still two system variables for the relays of the internal load of each channel. For compatibility reasons you should always activate both system variables. See description of the system variables (line "Internal load relay a") for details on the behavior. |

The electronic load can operate in two modes:

- Constant resistor The electronic load functions as a constant resistor against the input.The resistor value can be between 1.5 and 1000 Ohm.However, it is always regulated so that the current does not exceed 10 A.

- Constant current The electronic load is regulated so that a constant current flows between the two ECU lines.The current can be set to anywhere between 0.1 and 10 A.

In both modes, the electronic load requires a minimum voltage in order to regulate correctly. This depends on the specified current (this also applies to the resistor regulation; in this case the current is a parameter derived from the applied voltage). The minimum voltage rises in linearly from 3 V at 0.1 A to 7.5 V at 10 A.

A timeout can be specified so that when signals with short line breaks (especially PWM signals) are created, the electronic load does not need to re-regulate at 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [VTSystem/VT1004] VT1004PWMMeasurement


# VT1004 - PWM Measurement

VT System » Modules » VT1004/VT1104 » PWM Measurement

## General

The module can measure PWM signals. High and low levels are differentiated by means of comparators whose switching thresholds can be adjusted. So that a 0 % or 100 % duty cycle can be correctly detected, a suitable timeout must also be set. These values are displayed if no further edge has been detected after the set time. It is advisable to choose the set time to be greater than the period of the lowest frequency to be measured. However, so that a 0 % or 100 % duty cycle can be detected as quickly as possible, the value of the time set should also not be too large, as in the meantime the old measured values are also displayed.

The frequency and duty cycle (pulse-width modulation, pulse-duty factor) are measured. In addition, the number of measured values on the A/D converter (250 k samples/s) with a high or low level that have been detected is also output. The module also calculates the PWM signal's high and low levels as an average of the voltage during the high and low phases.

|  | Example: Start PWM measurement The following example demonstrates the measurement of a PWM signal with a threshold of 12 V and a measurement duration of 100 ms. CAPL double frequency;double dutyCycle; // Set PWM threshold to 12V sysvar::VTS::LowBeamLeft.SetPWMThreshold(12);// Set PWM measurement duration to 100mssysvar::VTS::LowBeamLeft.SetPWMMeasurementDuration(0.1); // Wait a bit until the PWM mea

[...truncated for brevity...]

| Original load re

### [VTSystem/VT1004] VT1004VoltageMeasurement


# Voltage Measurement

VT System » Modules » VT1004/VT1104 » Voltage Measurement

## General

The VT1004/VT1104 measures and pre-processes the ECU's output signal and makes it available to CANoe via the corresponding system variables.

The voltage is measured by an A/D converter with a resolution of 1 mV and a sampling rate of 250 kSamples/s. The measured values yield the momentary values from which averages and effective values are derived by means of various integration intervals (see system variables).

Voltage measurement can take place in different modes:

- Voltage differential (unfiltered) between the ECU's two output lines (default mode).

- Voltage differential between the ECU's two output lines, with analog filtering using a 10 KHz Low Pass Filter.This mode is necessary especially when the ECU's output signal contains very frequent disturbance elements.

- Line a voltage against ECU ground.

- Line b voltage against ECU ground.

The VT1004/VT1104 also derives maximum and minimum values from the momentary values. The min./max. calculation is triggered manually via the corresponding function.

PWM Measurement

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |

### [VTSystem/VT2004] VT2004ArbitraryWaveForms


# Arbitrary Wave Forms

VT System » Modules » VT2004 » Arbitrary Wave Forms

## Stimulation of Wave Forms

Not only can the VT2004 stimulation module be used to stimulate constant values and PWM signals, it also allows the use of arbitrary wave forms for stimulating. A maximum of 512 interpolation points are defined in a file for this. In addition to the stimulated resistance or voltage value, the number of intermediate points following an interpolation point must also be specified.

An example of such a wave form is shown in the following figure.

A curve can also be loaded from a file using CAPL command LoadWFVoltage or using the vtsystem_configure command in a channel of a VT2004 module. In addition, the SetWFParams command or the primitive can be used to set the following parameters: Time increment, Pause, Number of repeats, Starting point, and Start delay . Time increment and Pause can also be changed using system variables. The change has a direct effect on an active output for this module.

- The Time increment specifies the time increment between interpolation nodes or intermediate points. This parameter can also be changed during the output of the curve using a system variable. In addition, this value can be negative in order to change the direction of the output. The output can also be frozen at a point by setting the time increment to 0.0. If a different value is set afterwards, the output is resumed at the point at which it was frozen.

- The Number of repeats sp

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [VTSystem/VT2004] VT2004BusBars


# VT2004 - Bus Bars

VT System » Modules » VT2004 » VT2004 - Bus Bars

## VT2004

The VT2004 has two bus bars on which the lines a and b of the four channels can be switched. The two lines and can be switched independently of one another at bus bar 1, i.e. channel line a independent of channel line b to line a or b respectively of bus bar 1. At bus bar 2 the two lines cannot be connected independently. Either both channel lines are connected each with a line of the bus bar 2 or none.

The module also has swap relays between the bus bar connection and the internal bus bar lines on both bus bars. There relays can can only be switched together for the both lines of a bus bar, so that either both line a is connected to line a and line b is connected to line b or each line a with each line b.

## VT2004A

The VT2004A has two bus bars on which the lines a and b of the four channels can be switched. The two lines and can be switched independently of one another, i.e., channel line a can be switched independently of channel line b to line a in respect to line b of one of the two bus bars.

The module also has swap relays between the bus bar connection and the internal bus bar lines on both bus bars. These relays can be switched individually, so that for instance bus bar input a can be switched onto both internal bus bar lines a and b.

Via the bus bar connections a signal switched to the bus bar can be transmitted to an external device, additionally several channels can be bundled v

### [VTSystem/VT2004] VT2004PotentiometerStimulation


# Potentiometer Stimulation

VT System » Modules » VT2004 » Potentiometer Stimulation

|  | Note Potentiometer stimulation is only available for channel 1. While the potentiometer stimulation is active, the output voltage cannot be read back via the system variable CurVoltage. |

For potentiometer stimulation, a voltage is generated that is proportional to a specified factor and a set reference voltage. The reference voltage can vary; the factor (which corresponds to the potentiometer setting) is given in percent.

Permissible values for the output voltage are the same as given for the voltage stimulus. The internal resistance generated when the ECU applies a load to the switching is not simulated. Line b at the reference voltage input is connected to ECU line b.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [VTSystem/VT2004] VT2004ResistorStimulation


# VT2004 - Resistor Stimulation

VT System » Modules » VT2004 » VT2004 - Resistor Stimulation

To simulate a sensor, the VT2004 can generate a resistance via an internal decade resistor. The resistance value is specified by the test program via the corresponding system variable. A new resistance value can be applied at most every 1 ms because of relay switching times.

There are two variants of the module VT2004. With the newer variant (label VT2004A) the decade resistor supports a larger voltage range than the older variant (label VT2004). The following section lists the values for the new variant VT2004A; the values for the old variant are in brackets.

Channel 4 has an extended decade resistor. The following value ranges and tolerances apply:

|  | Channels 1, 2, 3 | Channel 4 |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Resistance | 10 Ohm…10 kOhm(extended: 10 kOhm…150 kOhm) | 1 Ohm...250 kOhm |
| Tolerance | ± 2% or ± 2 Ohm,the larger value is the decisive one(extended: ± 10%) | ± 2% or ± 2 Ohm,the larger value is the decisive one |
| Switching speed | ≤ 500 µs | ≤ 500 µs |
| Voltage range | -40 V…+40 V (-32 V…+32 V) | -40 V…+40 V (-32 V…+32 V) |
| Maximum capacity | 3 Watt (limited internally) | 3 Watt (limited internally) |

There are two resistor stimulation modes that differ in their switching behavior:

- R> While switching between a resistance value R1 and a value R2, interim values must fall within or above the value range of R1/R2, i.e., values must be greater R1 and R2.

- R< While

[...truncated for brevity...]

VT System » Modules » VT2004 » VT2004 - System Variables

There are two variants of the module VT2004. Among other features, the newer variant (label VT2004A) supports a wider range for voltage stimulation than the older variant (label VT2004). The following section lists the values for the new variant VT2004A; the values for the old variant are in brackets.

The following settings and output values can be read or written for each VT2004 channel via the system variables.The namespace is the name of the channel specified in the VT System configuration:

### [VTSystem/VT2004] VT2004VoltageStimulation


# VT2004 - Voltage Stimulation

VT System » Modules » VT2004 » VT2004 - Voltage Stimulation

To simulate an equivalent sensor, the module can generate a voltage that is applied at the ECU input. The voltage to be issued is specified via a system variable and can therefore also be varied during the course of a test. A new voltage value can be specified every 1 ms.

There are two variants of the module VT2004. The older variant (label VT2004) supports only an output voltage of …27 V, while the newer variant (label VT2004A) supports a voltage range of 0…40 V. The following section lists the values for the new variant VT2004A; the values for the old variant are in brackets.

An output voltage of 0 to 40 V (27 V) can be specified. The resolution is 2.5 mV. The voltage stimulus output can drive or receive a maximum of 150 mA.

The specified voltage is delivered as voltage to line a in respect to line b. Line b does not need to have ground potential in this case. The voltage output at line b must always be balanced against the ECU ground within a range of 0…40 V (27) Volt.

|  | Caution! Since the output voltage on line a is always referenced to line b, line b must always be set to a fixed potential, e.g. the ECU ground, when voltage is output. Outputting voltage, even if it is only for testing, only works if e.g. the ECU ground is connected to line b. |

The stimulation can occur with fixed values or as a result of a PWM signal directly from CANoe via the corresponding system vari

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [VTSystem/VT2516] VT2516


# VT2516 - Digital Module

VT System » Modules » VT2516 - Digital Module

| General |
| --- |
|  | The VT2516 digital module has 16 identical channels, each of which can be connected to an ECU input or output. Each channel is connected using one line (single-ended). The signals pertain to the ECU ground, which is connected separately to the VT2516 along with the ECU power supply (Vbatt). The voltage between the signal line and ECU ground can range from -40 V to +40 V. |
| Notes on Module Series VT2516 |
| The term VT2516 and the belonging description also cover variants of the module, e.g. VT2516A. If there are differing details for a variant it will be described explicitly in particular cases. There are no visible differences in CANoe between the VT2516 and the VT2516A. Please refer to the VTSystem Manual (PDF) for data about differences in the accuracy of analog voltage measurement. The VT2516 provides circuitry for ECU inputs and outputs, which essentially have two states, i.e. they operate digitally. Switch inputs are an example – the ECU detects whether or not the switch at this input is connected to a reference potential (ground or Vbatt). The input has no other states. External circuitry is also simple; the switch itself can generate a short circuit to ground or Vbatt. An example in the opposite direction is connecting simple LEDs. To turn on the LED, the ECU switches the output to Vbatt or ground. The ECU recognizes only two states in this case as well: the output is

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [VTSystem/VT2516] VT2516BitStreamOutput


# VT2516 - Bit Stream Output

VT System » Modules » VT2516 » VT2516 - Bit Stream Output

## Bit Stream Output

In addition to outputting digital values and generating PWM signals directly, the VT2516 can also output bit streams. The bit stream is defined in a file, and can have a maximum of 4096 bits.

You can load the bit stream from the file to the VT module via the LoadWFBitStream CAPL function or the vtsystem_configure command. In addition, you can set the time increment, pause and number of repetitions parameters via the SetWFParams CAPL function or the test primitive.

- The time increment parameter specifies the interval between two bits.

- The number of repetitions parameter specifies how many times the bit stream is repeated.

- If the bit stream is output repeatedly, the pause parameter specifies the length of the pause before each repetition.

## File Format

The file format is very simple. It contains the bit stream in the form of a string composed of the numbers 0 and 1. You can insert spaces and line breaks between the numbers to structure the bit stream. Comments can be inserted after //. All characters on this line are interpreted as comments and are ignored, up to the end of the line.

|  | Example 1: The following file defines the bit stream 001100110010101100: // Sample bit stream 0011001100 // bits may be defined in one line1 0 1 011 00 // definition in groups |

|  | Example 2: Outputting a bit sequence (bit stream) In this example, a bit sequence that 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [VTSystem/VT2516] VT2516BusBars


# VT2516 - Using the Bus Bars

VT System » Modules » VT2516 » VT2516 - Using the Bus Bars

In addition to making it possible to connect each channel to Vbatt or Gnd (battery voltage or ECU ground), the VT2516 Digital Module also provides a bus bar.

A relay can be used to lay each channel's signal line onto the internal bus bar. These can then be used to transmit the signal to an external device or another VT module via the bus bar connections. Several channels can also be bundled internally via one bus bar.

The image illustrates the wiring of a bus bar on the VT2516.

The bus bars always have two external lines. In the case of the VT2516, the relevant reference potential is connected to the second line (bus bar line b). This is either ECU ground or battery voltage, depending on the use case.

Measurement devices generally expect the lower potential at bus bar pin b and the higher potential at pin a. The bus bar lines can thus be switched internally before they are laid on the bus bar connection.

|  | These two examples serve to further clarify the use of bus bar relays: Example 1 The door switch ECU pin is connected to the VT2516. A switch referenced to ground, which is connected to this ECU pin in the vehicle, indicates whether the door is closed or not. The bus bar is now used to measure the voltage across this switch. The VT2516 can of course also execute this measurement, but we are assuming here that an especially precise level of measurement is needed and that an ex

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [VTSystem/VT2516] VT2516DigitalInputSignalMeasurement


# VT2516 - Measuring the Digital Input Signal

VT System » Modules » VT2516 » VT2516 - Measuring the Digital Input Signal

The digital data stream of each channel's signal line is captured. This happens regardless of whether the channel is used as in input or an output. An adjustable switching threshold (range 0…25 V) is used to differentiate between the High and Low states. This switching threshold is set for groups of channels, i.e. for channels 1…8 and 9…16 collectively.

The signal is sampled every 50 µs. The bit stream is made available to CANoe via a system variable. This system variable contains the 20 most recently sampled values, each of which is either 0 or 1, representing Low or High.

The module can also measure PWM signals. For PWM measurement to take place, you need to specify the maximum duration of the measurement window. If less than three signal edges are detected during this time period, the PWM parameters cannot be determined. These three signal edges are needed to determine the High and Low phases of the PWM signal and to calculate the PWM frequency and pulse/pause ratio. If no three edges are detected within this time, a duty cycle of 0% or 100% is output. The set time for the measurement window should be longer than the period of the lowest frequency to be measured. In order to detect 0% or 100% duty cycle as quickly as possible, the time should not be set to too high a value, as the old measured values are still displayed in the meantime.

|  | Exampl

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [VTSystem/VT2516] VT2516DigitalOutputSignal


# VT2516 - Outputting a Digital Signal

VT System » Modules » VT2516 » VT2516 - Outputting a Digital Signal

The VT2516 can output a digital voltage signal on each channel. The ECU interprets this as a sensor signal. High and Low levels can be set separately for each channel. They can range from 0 to 25 V. The current can be up to 30 mA (sink/source).

The following output modes are available:

- Inactive A signal is not generated. In this case, the Signal Generator is disconnected completely from the signal line. The channel can also be connected to the ECU's output pin in this mode.

- Constant Value The output value (High/Low) is specified via system variables in CANoe. It can be changed at any time via CANoe; however, the rate of change is limited to about 500–1000 changes per second due to the delay in transmission to the VT System.

- PWM Output The VT2516 generates a PWM signal.The pulse/pause ratio (duty cycle) and frequency are specified via CANoesystem variables.

- Bit Stream Output A specified bit stream is loaded in the module and is output independently (see Bit Stream Output).

|  | Cross Reference Please be sure to also refer to the notes, plug allocations and technical data in the VT System manual. |

|  | Example: Outputting a digital signal This example illustrates exertion of direct control over the state of a channel, switch, that is connected to the ECU input. Because the VT2516 outputs digital signals, only two states are possible. These states are set

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [VTSystem/VT2516] VT2516Functions


# VT2516 - Functions

VT System » Modules » VT2516 » VT2516 - Functions

The following settings can be adjusted via the member functions of the VT2516 system variables or the corresponding system variable namespaces in CAPL.

| Setting | System Variable | CAPL Function |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Start and stop stimulus output | Namespace of a VT2516 channel | StartStimulation / StopStimulation |
| Stimulation mode | SetStimulationMode |
| Stimulation output curve | SetCurveType |

| Set parameters for curve stimulation | SetWFParams |

| PWM measurement duration | SetPWMMeasurementDuration |

| Set integration time | System variable Avg | SetIntegrationTime |
| Set cycle time | All VT2516 read system variables | SetTransferCycle |

You can modify these settings in XML test modules using the vtsystem_configure command.

|  | Example 1: XML Test Module <vtsystem_configure title="change settings"> <pwm_voltage_high channel="DoorSwitch">10.0</pwm_voltage_high> <pwm_voltage_low channel=" DoorSwitch ">2.0</pwm_voltage_low> <start_stimulation channel=" DoorSwitch " /> <group_threshold channel=" DoorIOs" group=”1-8”>5.0</group_threshold></

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [VTSystem/VT2516] VT2516InternalLoad


# VT2516 - Internal Load

VT System » Modules » VT2516 » VT2516 - Internal Load

A resistor referenced to Vbatt or ground can be connected to each channel. The externally mounted resistor should be fixed to one of the reference potentials (Vbatt or ground).

The resistor can be used for a variety of purposes:

- To simulate a load, e.g. a control LED that would normally be connected to the ECU on this channel.

- As an external pull-up resistor, if one is expected by the ECU. This could be the case, for example, when you want to connect a switch referenced to ground to the ECU.

- As an external pull-down resistor, if one is expected by the ECU. This could be the case, for example, when you want to connect a switch referenced to Vbatt to the ECU.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |

### [VTSystem/VT2516] VT2516VoltageMeasurement


# VT2516 - Voltage Measurement

VT System » Modules » VT2516 » VT2516 - Voltage Measurement

The VT2516 measures and preprocesses the voltage level of each channel's signal line, and makes it available to CANoe via the corresponding system variables. It does this regardless of whether the channel is used as in input or an output.

The voltage is measured using an A/D converter that has a resolution of about 80 mV and a sampling rate of 1 kSamples/s. These measured values yield momentary values, from which averages for various integration intervals are derived (see VT2516 system variables).

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [VTSystem/VT2710] VT2710


# VT2710 - Serial Interface Module

VT System » Modules » VT2710 - Serial Interface Module

| General |
| --- |
|  | The Serial Interface Module VT2710 enables the test of ECUs and sensors who communicate via serial protocol interfaces. The VT2710 can be operated as sender or receiver which allows for sensor or ECU simulation. Furthermore, the communication between real sensor and real ECU can be analyzed and logged. The configuration and analysis of the buses and bus components is established with the CANoe Option Sensor. The VT2710 can provide up to four PSI5- or SENT channels with one PSI5/SENT piggy per channel. The PSI5/SENT piggy extends the basis module VT2710 to respectively one PSI5 or SENT channel. Sensor frames and protocol relevant electrical parameters like synchronization pulse forms, bit rates, current levels as well as the sensor supply can be configured and analyzed with the CANoe Option Sensor. Dedicated relays allow for shortcuts between the bus lines. The PSI5 or SENT channels can be interconnected or provided externally with two independent bus bars. The module VT2710 also supports further serial protocols like SPI, UART (incl. RS232, RS422, RS485) and I2C. |
| Switching Different Signal Paths | Usage, Functions, Technical Data |
| The connecting cables of the serial interfaces on the bus bars and the short-circuits between cables can be switched via relay. | Usage Resistor and Capacitor Cascade for PSI5 Bus Configuration with Option .Sensor Functions Sy

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [VTSystem/VT2710] VT2710BusBars


# VT2710 - Bus Bars

VT System » Modules » VT2710 » VT2710 - Bus Bars

The VT2710 is equipped with two bus bars with the lines a and b. The two lines can be switched, independently of one other, to the signal and supply lines of the serial automotive interfaces PSI5 or SENT.

The module also offers swap relays between the bus bar connection and the internal bus bar lines on both bus bars. These relays can be switched individually, so that for instance bus bar input a can be switched onto both internal bus bar lines a and b.

Via the bus bar connections a signal switched to the bus bar can be transmitted to an external device, additionally several channels can be bundled via one bus bar.

By default, the Vbatt and GND (battery voltage and device ground) are connected to the bus bar 1 so that it is possible to generate short-circuits from the individual signal lines to Vbatt and GND. The bus bar 2 is freely available.

The signals of the serial automotive interfaces PSI5 and SENT can be freely switched to this bus bar. Therefore, any connection configuration is possible.

|  | Note The maximum VDD / Sensor voltage for VT2710 SENT channels are +6 V. By using the short circuit option via the bus bar 1 to Vbatt, the Vbatt voltage shall be < +6 V. |

## Connecting the Bus Bars to other VT Modules

If there are no special system requirements, we recommend connecting the bus bars of all VT modules in the following way:

- Connect the battery voltage and ground (Vbatt and Gnd) to all

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [VTSystem/VT2710] VT2710ResistorAndCondenser


# VT2710 - Resistor and Capacitor Cascade for PSI5

VT System » Modules » VT2710 » VT2710 - Resistor and Capacitor Cascade for PSI5

The resistor value ranges and the interconnect capacitance for the PSI5 interface are set in the protocol specification. The VT2710 is equipped with a resistor and capacitor cascade through which it is possible to configure the default values via CANoe according to the specification. The resistor cascade allows to set the resistance between 0.0 Ω and 15.5 Ω and the capacitor cascade allows to set the capacitance between 0 nF and 127 nF on the signal lines.

RC Array

### [VTSystem/VT2808] VT2808MeasuringCurrentAndVoltage


# Measuring Current and Voltage

VT System » Modules » VT2808 » Measuring Current and Voltage

## Measurement Values

The VT2808 has eight identical measurement channels. Each of the channels provides four current measurement values and four voltage measurement values:

- Momentary current (via system variable CurCurrent)

- Average current (via system variable AvgCurrent)

- Minimum current (via system variable MinCurrent)

- Maximum current (via system variable MaxCurrent)

- Momentary voltage (via system variable CurVoltage)

- Average voltage (via system variable AvgVoltage)

- Minimum voltage (via system variable MinVoltage)

- Maximum voltage (via system variable MaxVoltage)

These measurement variables can be enabled and configured in the VT System Configuration dialog; see page System Variable for details.

## Current Measurement

Each VT2808 channel can measure a current up to 20 A between its input pins Cin a and Cin b.

## Measurement Ranges

The internal shunts on the VT2808 support measurement of currents between 1 mA and 20 A.Three different measurement ranges are available and automatically selected by the VT2808 channel:

- Range 1 (50 mA) can measure currents up to 50 mA

- Range 2 (1 A) can measure currents up to 1 A

- Range 3 (20 A) can measure currents up to 20 A

You can change the minimum measurement range to be used by the automatic range selector in the VT System Control Window or by calling the corresponding CAPL function; see page Functions for det

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [VTSystem/VT2816] VT2816


# VT2816 - General Purpose Analog I/O Module

VT System » Modules » VT2816 - General Purpose Analog I/O Module

| General |
| --- |
|  | The VT2816 General-Purpose Analog I/O Module has 12 channels for measuring analog voltages, of which 8 can also be used to measure currents. You can output analog voltages on 4 further independent channels. The measurement channels have a differential layout, but can also operate as single-ended channels if needed. In this case the reference potential is AGND. The output channels can also operate as differential or single-ended channels. The reference potential here is either DGND or the ECU ground, which is connected externally. You can switch the voltage measurement range between ±60 V and ±10 V for more precise measurement.The current measurement range is ±5 A.You can switch the output voltage range between ±10 V and 0…28 V. The outputs can carry up to 200 mA. |
| Switching Different Signal Paths | Usage, Functions, Technical Data |
| The VT2816 has two different measurement channels. You can measure both voltage and current on channels 1-8. This is done using different inputs, but further processing is handled by the same electronics with relays that are switched at the input. Channels 9-12 are wired exclusively for voltage measurement, and don't offer any switching at the input. You can output analog voltages on channels 13-16. The VT2816 does not have a bus bar for external voltages. However, you can switch line b of an output channel

### [VTSystem/VT2816] VT2816ArbitraryWaveForms


# VT2816 - Arbitrary Wave Forms

VT System » Modules » VT2816 » VT2816 - Arbitrary Wave Forms

## Stimulation of Wave Forms

With the VT2816, you can output constant values on channels 13 and 14, and you can also stimulate with arbitrary wave forms. To do this, you define a maximum of 512 interpolation points in a file. For each interpolation point, the file defines the value of the stimulated voltage and the number of intermediate points that follow the interpolation point.

Here is an example of such a wave form:

You use the LoadWFVoltage CAPL function or the vtsystem_configure command to load a wave form you have defined in a file to a channel on the VT2816 module. In addition, you can use the SetWFParams command or the primitive to set the time increment, pause, number of repetitions, start point and start delay parameters. Time increment and pause can also be modified via system variables; changes immediately influence the output of this module.

- The time increment parameter specifies the interval between the interpolation points and intermediate points. You can use a system variable to change this parameter during wave form output. In addition, you can make the value negative in order to reverse the direction of the output. The output can be frozen at a certain point by setting the time increment to 0.0. If a different value is set afterwards, the output is continued from the point where it has been frozen.

- The number of repetitions parameter specifies how many t

### [VTSystem/VT2816] VT2816SystemVariables


# VT2816 - System Variables

VT System » Modules » VT2816 » VT2816 - System Variables

On a certain channel of the VT2816 module you can only measure one at a time, either current or voltage. Therefore the system variables with measurement values of current or voltage are only updated if the according measurement mode is active. This means that e.g. the AvgVoltage system variable is only updated if voltage measurement is active and AvgCurrent is only updated if the current measurement is active. In CAPL you can use the SetMeasurementMode command to set the measurement mode.

You can use system variables to access the following measurement values on channels 1 to 12 of the VT2816.The namespace is the name of the channel in the VT System configuration:

| Value/Setting | System Variable | R/W | Value Semantic |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Momentary value of the input voltage (Supports lower transfer cycle times with ERT) | CurVoltage | R | Double, in volt |
| Average value of the input voltage (Supports lower transfer cycle times with ERT for integration time 1ms) | AvgVoltage | R | Double, in volt |
| RMS value of the input voltage | RMSVoltage | R | Double, in volt |
| Minimum value of the input voltage (The system variable is only updated if the value changes; the cycle time only influences the latency) | MinVoltage | R | Double, in volt |
| Maximum value of the input voltage (The system variable is only updated if the value changes; the cycle time only influences the laten

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [VTSystem/VT2816] VT2816VoltageCurrentMeasurement


# VT2816 - Voltage and Current Measurement

VT System » Modules » VT2816 » VT2816 - Voltage and Current Measurement

The VT2816 measures voltages and currents, pre-processes the results and makes them available in CANoe via a set of corresponding system variables.

The measured momentary values are used to derive average and RMS values across various integration intervals (see system variables). The maximum and minimum values are also derived from the momentary values.

To achieve a better resolution, you can switch the voltage measurement range from the default ±60 V to ±10 V. You can measure the voltage differential between the input and output of a given channel, as well as relative to ground. To measure relative to ground, you connect line b of that channel to AGND. When you do this, make sure to connect the measurement ground potential (AGND) to the ground potential of the VT System (DGND).For a more detailed explanation see VT System Ground Connection.

Currents are measured indirectly, by measuring the voltage drop across a special, very low-ohm measurement resistor (shunt). Channels 1-8 each have an additional current measurement input, which you can switch to when you want to measure currents with a given channel.

You can use the following measurement modes:

- Voltage difference between lines a and b; measurement range ±60 V (default mode).

- Voltage difference between lines a and b; measurement range ±10 V.

- Voltage on line a to AGND; measurement range ±60 V.


[...truncated for brevity...]

### [VTSystem/VT2816] VT2816VoltageStimulation


# VT2816 - Voltage Stimulation

VT System » Modules » VT2816 » VT2816 - Voltage Stimulation

You can switch the voltage output range between ±10 V and 0…28 V using the corresponding function. Each channel can carry up to 200 mA. You use a system variable to specify the voltage that is output, which means you can also vary it during the test. However, the next voltage value cannot be set until at least 1 ms has passed.

You can also output the specified voltage from line a to line b. Line b doesn't necessarily need to be connected to ground in this case. You can also apply the specified voltage to a ground potential, e.g. the ECU ground. In addition, you can use relays to connect line b either to DGND or to another suitable external potential attached to the VT2816.

|  | Note When voltage is output to line b, it should always be connected to a fixed potential, e.g. the ECU ground or DGND.Furthermore, you can only test the voltage output when line b is connected to a fixed potential. |

You can stimulate with fixed values directly from CANoe using the corresponding system variables. The module can also output a saved curve independently; see stimulating with arbitrary curves.

|  | Example: Generating a fixed value voltage signal The example below generates a voltage signal fixed at 12 V. CAPL // Set output range to 0-28V sysvar::VTS::TempSensor.SetOutputRange(eVTSOutputRangeZeroTo28V); // Switch line b to Ground @sysvar::VTS::TempSensor::RelayGnd = 1; // Activate voltage sti

### [VTSystem/VT2820] VT2820BusBars


# VT2820 - Using the Bus Bars

VT System » Modules » VT2820 » VT2820 - Using the Bus Bars

The module has two bus bars. You can switch line a of each of the 12 normally open contact channels to them.

You cannot switch line a to bus bars a and b at the same time, i.e. you can only switch it to one of the two bus bars at a given time.This prevents short circuits from occurring when e.g. one bus bar is connected to Vbatt and the other to ground.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |

VT System » Modules » VT2820 » VT2820 - System Variables

You can use system variables to modify the following settings for channels 1 to 12 of the VT2820. Each of these has a normally open relay that is used to connect the two lines, as well as two normally open relays with which you can connect line a to one of the two bus bar lines.

|  | Note The two bus bar lines are often connected to ground and Vbatt. To prevent a short circuit between the two lines, it is not possible to close both RelayBusBarA and RelayBusBarB for a given channel at the same time. |

| Normally open relay bus bar | RelayBusBarA | W | Integer(0 = open, 1 = closed) |

### [VTSystem/VT2832] VT2832


# VT2832 – Switch Matrix Module

VT System » Modules » VT2832 – Switch Matrix Module

| General |
| --- |
|  | The Switch Matrix Module VT2832 allows arbitrary switching of 8 columns (channels) with 4 rows (8×4 switch matrix). Additional 4 paths can be switched directly. By using Solid State Relays it is possible to switch currents up to 16 A under full load at voltages up to 60 V on each channel. The overall current per channel and the voltage of the path against DGND are measured on each channel. The frequency and the duty cycle for periodic switching operations can be chosen individually for each channel. Contact bouncing processes can be simulated by loading an arbitrarily defined bitstream on the module and executing it during the CANoe measurement. |
| Switching Different Signal Paths | Usage, Functions, Technical Data |
| Switching of signal paths is a core feature of the VT2832. The matrix setup allows arbitrary connections between Column 1 to Column 8 and Row 1 to Row 4 to be set. Multiple connections are also possible since the applied solid state relays are balancing the current more or less equally to all connected rails. Additionally, each of the switch lines Switch 1 to Switch 4 can be connected to one dedicated channel. An overload protection is realized by monitoring of the total current on each channel. If the maximum continuous current of 16 A is exceeded the measurement will be terminated and all channels will be disconnected automatically. By type of cons

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [VTSystem/VT2832] VT2833BitstreamSwitching


# Bitstream Switching

VT2832 » Bitstream Switching

For arbitrary switching of matrix paths, each channel can be operated separately in bitstream mode. The desired switching pattern is stored in a text file and loaded onto the module before the stimulation is started. The bit stream is defined in a file, and can have a maximum of 4096 bits.

You can load the bit stream from the file to the VT module via the LoadWFBitStream CAPL function or the vtsLoadWFBitStream CAPL function onto the VT System module. In addition, you can set the time increment, number of repetitions, pause, start delay and start point parameters via the SetWFParams CAPL function or the vtsSetWFParams CAPL function.

- The time increment parameter specifies the interval between two bits.

- The number of repetitions parameter specifies how many times the bit stream is repeated.

- If the bit stream is output repeatedly, the pause parameter specifies the length of the pause before each repetition.

- The start delay defers the start of the bit stream switching by the given time.

- The start point specifies at which point of the stream bit switching starts.

## File Format

The file format is very simple. It contains the bit stream in the form of a string composed of the numbers 0 and 1. You can insert spaces and line breaks between the numbers to structure the bit stream. Comments can be inserted after //. All characters on this line are interpreted as comments and are ignored, up to the end of the line..


[...truncated for brevity...]

VT2832 » Functions

You can modify the following settings in CAPL:

- using member functions of VT2832 system variables, or

- using system variable namespaces that represent a specific channel of the module.

| Value/Setting | System Variable | CAPL Function |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Start or stop stimulus output. | Namespace of a VT2832 channel. | StartStimulation / StopStimulation |
| Curve type | SetCurveType |
| Load digital output curve (bit stream) | LoadWFBitStream |
| Set curve stimulation parameters | SetWFParams |
| Number of output cycles for PWM stimulation and digital curves | SetPWMRepeats |
| Delay for the start of the PWM stimulation and curve stimulation | SetPWMStartDelay |
| Connection mask for row 1 to row 4 with the selected matrix channel | SetOutputMask |

### [VTSystem/VT2832] VT2833PWMSwitching


# PWM Switching

VT2832 » PWM Switching

For periodically switching of matrix paths, each channel can be operated in PWM mode.To run PWM switching, the following steps are required, either manually or programmatically:

- set Curve Type to PWM,

- select all rows which shall be connected to the desired channel,

- set the desired parameters for PWM switching,

- start the stimulation.

After start of the stimulation, all connected rows are switched synchronously with the configured PWM parameters.

|  | Note While a PWM stimulation is running, the static setting of each matrix node is overruled if and only if the corresponding row is selected. After stop of the stimulation, the last state of the mode is maintained. The last static configuration of the node is set when Curve Type is changed to Constant. |

|  | Example In this example, the matrix rows 2 and 4 are switched periodically with 500 Hz, a duty cycle of 40% and with 10 repetitions on matrix channel 3. The selection of rows 2 and 4 corresponds to the bit pattern I0I0, therefore the argument for the SetOutputMask method has an integer value of 10. CAPL Control: // This calls the method SetCurveType on VTS::MyMatrixChannel sysvar::VTS::MyMatrixChannel.SetCurveType(eVTSCurveTypePWM); // This calls the method SetOutputMask on VTS::MyMatrixChannel sysvar::VTS::MyMatrixChannel.SetOutputMask(10); // Set the system variable VTS::MyMatrixChannel::PWMOutputFreq to 500 @sysvar::VTS::MyMatrixChannel::PWMOutputFreq = 500; // Set 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [VTSystem/VT2832] VT2833SwitchingMatrixChannels


# Switching Matrix Channels

VT2832 » Switching Matrix Channels

Switching of matrix channels with the VT2832 can be realized either programmatically by CAPL, C# or VB or manually in the VT System Configuration dialog.

On module level, each matrix node can be opened/closed by a click on the gray pad. On channel level, only the selected channel can be operated.

|  | Example: Simple Path In this example, column C2 is connected to row R3. CAPL Control: // Set the system variable VTS::MyMatrixChannel_2::RowSwitch3 to True @sysvar::VTS::MyMatrixChannel_2::RowSwitch3 = 1; Manual Control: |

|  | Example: Complex Switching State This example describes a more complex switching scenario. The programming is done in consecutive steps. Please note that paths which are connected are displayed in principle in the same color (in this example, the paths C2-R1 and C2-R3 are connected via the column C2). CAPL Control: // Set the system variable VTS::MyMatrix_Channel_1::RowSwitch2 to True @sysvar::VTS::MyMatrixChannel_1::RowSwitch2 = 1; // Set the system variable VTS:: MyMatrixChannel_2::RowSwitch3 to True @sysvar::VTS::MyMatrixChannel_2::RowSwitch3 = 1; // Set the system variable VTS:: MyMatrixChannel_2::RowSwitch1 to True @sysvar::VTS::MyMatrixChannel_2::RowSwitch1 = 1; // Set the system variable VTS:: MyMatrixChannel_5::DirectSwitch to True @sysvar::VTS::MyMatrixChannel_5::DirectSwitch = 1; // Set the system variable VTS:: MyMatrixChannel_4::RowSwitch4 to True @sysvar::VTS::MyMatrixChanne

### [VTSystem/VT2832] VT2833VoltageCurrentMeasurement


# Voltage and Current Measurement

VT2832 » Voltage and Current Measurement

On each channel, the instantaneous values of the current and the voltage against DGND are measured, preprocessed and conducted to CANoe via the respective system variables by the VT2832. The number of connected rows does not affect the measurement.

## Measurement Ranges

| Measured Parameter | Measurement Range | Resolution |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Channel Current | -16 A…+16 A | ~ 1 mA |
| Voltage | 0 V…60 V | ~ 1 mV |
| Temporal resolution of measurement values | ~ 5 ms |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [VTSystem/VT2848] VT2848


# VT2848 - General-Purpose Digital I/O Module

VT System » Modules » VT2848 - General-Purpose Digital I/O Module

| General |
| --- |
|  | The VT2848 General-Purpose Digital I/O Module has 48 channels, each of which can be used as input or output. Each channel is connected using one line (single-ended). The signals are referenced to the ECU ground, which is connected to the VT2848 separately. A maximum voltage of 60 V is permissible between the signal line and the ECU ground. Voltages are applied via two connections, Vbatt (ECU power supply) and Vext (external power supply), which can be switched to the module's outputs as needed. |
| Switching Different Signal Paths | Usage, Functions, Technical Data |
| Transistors are used to switch the module outputs either to one of the two external voltages, Vbatt and Vext in High Side Switch mode, or to ECU GND in Low Side Switch mode. In Push-Pull mode, the output is switched either to one of the voltages or to ECU GND, depending on the state of the corresponding system variable. The ground potential (e.g. ECU ground) is also connected externally. The operating mode of the output stage is specified via the corresponding channel functions. All signal paths can carry a constant current of up to 200 mA. You can switch the module's outputs to the connections Vbatt, Vextand ECU GND in accordance with the selected operating mode. You have to switch 4 channels to either Vbatt or Vext collectively. Caution! Correct grounding is absolutely es

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [VTSystem/VT2848] VT2848BitStreamOutput


# VT2848 - Bit Stream Output

VT System » Modules » VT2848 » VT2848 - Bit Stream Output

## Bit Stream Output

## In addition to statically outputting digital values and generating PWM signals, the VT2848 can also output bit streams. The bit stream is defined in a file, and can have a maximum of 4096 bits.

You can load the bit stream from the file to the VT module using the LoadWFBitStream CAPL function or the vtsystem_configure command. In addition, you can set the time increment, pause, number of repetitions, start point and start delay parameters via the SetWFParams CAPL function or the test primitive. Time increment and pause can also be modified via system variables; changes immediately influence the output of this module.

- The time increment parameter specifies the interval between two bits. You can use a system variable to change this parameter during bit stream output. In addition, you can make the value negative in order to reverse the direction of the output. The output can be frozen at a certain point by setting the time increment to 0.0. If a different value is set afterwards, the output is continued from the point where it has been frozen.

- The number of repetitions parameter specifies how many times the bit stream is repeated.

- If the bit stream is output repeatedly, the pause parameter specifies the length of the pause before each repetition. You can use a system variable to change this parameter during bit stream output.

- The start point parameter sp

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [VTSystem/VT2848] VT2848DigitalInputSignalMeasurement


# VT2848 - Measuring the Digital Input Signal

VT System » Modules » VT2848 » VT2848 - Measuring the Digital Input Signal

The digital data stream of each channel's signal line is captured. This happens regardless of whether the channel is used as in input or an output. An adjustable switching threshold (range 0…40 V) is used to differentiate between the High and Low states. You can set the switching thresholds for groups of 8 channels collectively.

The signal is sampled every 50 µs. The bit stream is made available to CANoe via a system variable. This system variable contains the 20 most recently sampled values, each of which is either 0 or 1, representing Low or High.

The module can also measure PWM signals on channels 1-16. For PWM measurement, you need to specify the maximum duration of the measurement window. If less than three signal edges are detected during this time period, the PWM parameters cannot be determined. These three signal edges are needed to determine the High and Low phases of the PWM signal and to calculate the PWM frequency and pulse/pause ratio. If no three edges are detected within this time, a duty cycle of 0% or 100% is output. The set time for the measurement window should be longer than the period of the lowest frequency to be measured. In order to detect 0% or 100% duty cycle as quickly as possible, the time should not be set to too high a value, as the old measured values are still displayed in the meantime.

|  | Example: PWM Measurement In 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [VTSystem/VT2848] VT2848DigitalOutputSignal


# VT2848 - Outputting a Digital Signal

VT System » Modules » VT2848 » VT2848 - Outputting a Digital Signal

The VT2848 can output a digital voltage signal on each channel.

- The High level of the signal is the potential connected to Vbatt or Vext.It can be up to 60 V.

- The Low level is the ground potential connected to ECU GND.The current can be up to 200 mA (sink/source).

The following output modes are available:

- Inactive A signal is not generated. The channel's input remains active. The channel can also be connected to the ECU's output pin in this mode.

- Constant Value The output value (High/Low) is determined in CANoe by the value of the corresponding system variables and the specified output stage mode (High Side Switch, Low Side Switch or Push-Pull). This can be changed at any time via CANoe; however, the rate of change is limited to about 500–1000 changes per second due to the delay in transmission to the VT System.

- PWM Output The VT2848 generates a PWM signal in accordance with the selected output stage mode.The pulse/pause ratio (duty cycle) and frequency are specified via CANoe system variables. Only channels 33-48 are capable of PWM output.

- Bit Stream Output A specified bit stream is loaded in the module and is output independently in accordance with the selected output stage mode; see Bit Stream Output. Only channels 33-48 are capable of bit stream output.

|  | Example: Outputting a Digital Signal This example demonstrates the possibility of direc

### [VTSystem/VT6000] VT6000


# VT6000 - Realtime Module

VT System » Modules » VT6000 - Realtime Module

| General |
| --- |
|  | The real time module has an embedded computer that runs the real time specific parts of CANoe tests and simulations in the VT System rack. The user sees the VT6000 as a pre-configured RT server. The user computer (distributed mode) is connected to the VT6000 via Ethernet. The real time module comes with a range of processor boards and components, which affects the number of network interfaces that can be connected to it directly. These different versions are distinguished from one another by means of the last two places in the VT number (e.g. VT6011, VT6051). They do not differ in their usage, however. The term VT6000 encompasses all versions of the real time module. The variant VT6051A supports Extended Real Time (ERT). Please also see page VT System and Extended Real Time. |
| Switching Different Signal Paths | Technical Data |
| The real time module serves as a computational module that is integrated into the VT System rack. Hardware signals are not routed via the VT6000. | Please be sure to refer to the notes and technical data in the VT System Manual (PDF)(https://www.vector.com/vtsystem-manual). If you currently don't have a connection to the internet, please refer to the manual revision which was valid at the time of this release under VTSystem Manual (PDF). |
| Standard Usage | Expert Usage |
| The VT6000 can be used in much the same way as a RT system. For further in

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [VTSystem/VT6000] VT6000Configuration


# VT6000 - Prerequisites and Configuration

VT System » Modules » VT6000 » VT6000 - Prerequisites and Configuration

To prepare a computer for use with a VT6000 real time module, make sure the following prerequisites are met:

- The computer needs to be running CANoe version 7.2 SP5 or later, and the Vector driver needs to be installed. Installation works in the normal way, just like the standard version included on the installation medium. The computer then runs as a user computer while the VT6000 acts as the RT server. The CANoe installation on the user computer can also be used as a simple CANoe system without RT and thus without the VT System.

- For license activation (especially in standalone mode) you can directly attach a Keyman license dongle to a VT6000. For this purpose the appropriate driver has to be installed on the module.

- Network interfaces like the CANcaseXL must be connected directly to the VT6000, even if they are only used to activate licenses.

- If the license-carrying device, e.g. a CANcaseXL or a VT6104, is connected directly to a VT6000 at first a connection to the VT6000 has to be established to enable CANoe to recognize the license.

- Connect the two computers via TCP/IP using the Ethernet interface. If you are using a local Ethernet connection (using a cross-over cable or a switch) that is not connected to a further network, the network has to be used with the IP addresses 192.168.100.x and the subnet mask 255.255.255.0. The VT6000 then uses I

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [VTSystem/VT6000] VT6000Operation


# Operation

VT System » Modules » VT6000 » Operation

Operating the distributed mode on the VT6000 real time module is very similar to standard operation.

|  | To run a simulation, take the following steps: Start the VT system and connect it to the user computer. Start CANoe on the user computer. In the Vector Tool Platform dialog (Home ribbon tab|Group Measurement|Vector Tool Platform) under Connection|Device Selection the available devices are displayed.Select the corresponding device.To use the selected device as RT server, activate Configure currently selected device as RT server under CANoe/CANalyzer|Distributed Mode|RT Server.Configure the connection under CANoe/CANalyzer|Distributed Mode|Connection Control.Your settings are applied directly. Now activate VT System in the VT System configuration dialog (Home ribbon tab|Hardware|VT System|Configuration).Choose the network interface (Intel® 82574L Gigabit Network Connection) on the VT6000 that is connected to the VT System backplane. Load the desired CANoe configuration onto the user computer (distributed mode). You may be alerted about a non-supported modeling library (DLL file) at this point.If the simulation can run without this modeling library and no CAPL program makes direct calls on it, you can ignore this message. Setting up the configuration is essentially the same as usual. The channels and hardware are assigned and programmed via the usual dialogs. Only the [Driver] button in the hardware dialog cannot be us

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [VTSystem/VT6000] VT6000OSUpdate


# VT6000 - Updating the Operating System

VT6000 » VT6000 - Updating the Operating System

In certain rare cases, you may need to update the Windows XP Embedded operating system running on the VT6000. This is done by loading a memory image (provided by Vector) to the internal flash memory of the device.

|  | Step by Step Procedure: Preparation To prepare for this operation, unplug the VT System from the power supply. Remove the VT6000 from the rack. Connect the USB Type B port on the VT6000 front panel with the USB port on the user computer using an appropriate USB cable. |

|  | Step by Step Procedure: Updating the Operating System Start by locating the executable VN89Flash.EXE in the Vector CANoe installation directory. Double click the executable. In the upper part of the window, enter the path to the image file provided by Vector. Under Device storage medium, select the system drive of the connected VT6000; it is labeled VT6000Sys. Now start the update by clicking the [Update] button.You can observe the update progress in the lower part of the dialog.Please note that the update will take a few minutes. Caution! If you select another USB drive connected to the computer, instead of selecting the VT6000 drive as the storage medium, all files on the selected drive will be overwritten. Do not disconnect the device during the update. When the device is connected to the computer via its internal USB port, the VT6000's internal flash memory drives are also visible in Windows Ex

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [VTSystem/VT6000] VT6000RemoteDebugging


# VT6000 - Remote Debugging

VT System » VT6000 » VT6000 - Remote Debugging

## Overview

When working with CANoe it may be advisable, for performance reasons, to execute the real time portions on a separate, dedicated computer. You can do this using a standard computer or also a special real time computer (e.g. VN8900 or VT6000). If a CANoe configuration that is loaded in this way contains test nodes implemented in a .NET language, you can analyze their execution using the Microsoft Remote Debugging Monitor.

This page describes how to establish and use such a remote debugging connection between a computer and a Vector realtime module (VT6000 family).

When your system is set up as described below you can use CANoe's integrated remote debug support.

For Remote Debugging you need Visual Studio 2003 or higher as a full version (not Express version!).

## Concept

As described above, remote debugging of .NET code involves monitoring two computers, the Host PC and the Remote PC.

- The host PC The host computer runs the GUI portions of CANoe. It serves to load the configuration and control the measurement sequence.This requires installing Microsoft Visual Studio (Version 2003 or higher) on the host computer to enable remote debugging.

- The remote PC (VT real time module)The remote computer runs the real time portions of CANoe. Thus the .NET assemblies are also executed on this computer. In order to debug the .NET assemblies, you need Microsoft Visual Studio Remote Debugging 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [VTSystem/VT6000] VT6000Settings


# VT6000 - Extended Setting

VT System » Modules » VT6000 » Configuration » VT6000 - Extended Settings

You cannot adjust all of the settings from the user computer (distributed mode). For instance, you can only assign the channels for the different network interfaces in the Vector hardware configuration dialog on the VT6000. To adjust these settings, you need to create a remote desktop connection to the VT6000.

|  | Remote Desktop Connection You can easily create a remote desktop connection via the Vector Tool Platform dialog in CANoe. To do this, open the menu path Home ribbon tab|Group Measurement|Vector Tool Platform|Connection|General|Remote Desktop|Connect. To log in to the VT6000 using the remote desktop connection, use the following login: User Account: Administrator Password: (no password) |

Two console windows are usually opened on the VT6000 (Control Service Console of the Vector Tool Platform and RuntimeKernel.EXE). For further configuration the Control Service Console offers corresponding buttons, e.g. to open the Windows Explorer, the command line console, the control panel or the Vector hardware configuration as well as to start and stop the RT kernel.

## Changing System Settings

The VT6000 uses a write filter that protects the system from getting damaged when the module is switched off. Because of this write protection specific measures are necessary to change system settings. Without these steps the changes will get lost at reboot.

|  | Step by Step Pro

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [VTSystem/VT6000] VT6000USBRS232Converter


# VT6000 - USB RS232 Converter

VT6000 » VT6000 - USB RS232 Converter

## General

You can use serial interfaces with the VT6000 by means of a RS232 USB converter (serial port). Built-in drivers are utilized to support chipsets from the following manufacturers:

- Prolific (PL2303)

- FTDI (FT232)

|  | Note Vector has successfully tested a range of RS232 USB converters that use the abovementioned chipsets. However, there may be isolated instances when faults occur with the serial interface, caused e.g. by the specific hardware features of a given USB RS232 converter. For example, there may be problems with certain Prolific PL2303 adapters. In such instances, the Device Manager correctly recognizes the adapter, but there are problems sending and receiving data. With some adapters only the send functionality, with others only the receive functionality works. No problems have been identified with FTDI FT232-based RS232 USB adapters to date. However there is still a slight possibility that some of these adapters may also cause problems. |

## Setup

To set up the adapter, create a remote desktop connection to the VT6000. Once you have connected the RS232 USB adapter to the VT6000, it should be displayed in the Device Manager of the VT6000.

|  | Open the Device Manager on the VT6000 In RuntimeServer.EXE, enter Explorer and press <Enter>. In the Explorer, select Control Panel. Select Performance and Maintenance. Now select System. Switch to the Hardware page and click on [Device

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [VTSystem/VT6104] VT6104


# VT6104/VT6204 - Network Interface Module

VT System » Modules » VT6104/VT6204 - Network Interface Module

| General |
| --- |
|  | The VT6104 network module has four network interfaces. You can configure the four channels in the driver as CAN or LIN channels, as required. You can also use relays to activate various signal paths and to insert errors such as short circuits and line breaks. This is also valid for the VT6204 module. There you additionally operate FlexRay on the first channel instead of CAN or LIN, if an appropriate piggyback is used. The VT6104/VT6204 has two logical components: The actual network interface, which has four channels and offers the switching options for various signal paths and errors typical of the VT System. You configure the interface part using the Vector driver and use it almost exactly as you would a CANcardXLe. The relay part is controlled via the VT System and its configuration. This part thus behaves like a VT module with regard to its usage and configuration. |
| Notes on Module Series VT6104 |
| The term VT6104 and the belonging description also cover variants of the module. There are two variants of the VT6104 module. Compared to the older variant (labeled VT6104), the newer variant (labeled VT6104A)supports the following additional features: CAN FD K-Line However, there are no differences between the available relays and the system variables that control them and further features described here. |
| Switching Different Signal Paths

### [VTSystem/VT6104] VT6104BusBars


# VT6104/VT6204 Bus Bars

VT System » Modules » VT6104/VT6204 » VT6104/VT6204 Bus Bars

The VT6104/6204 network module has an internal bus bar. You can use a relay to allocate the High signal line of each channel to line A of the internal bus bar, and the Low signal line to line B of the internal bus bar. This makes it possible to connect the bus lines internally and to switch High and Low to other potentials or connect them to an external device (e.g. an oscilloscope). Additionally the module's bus bars have a swap relay between the bus bar connector and the internal bus bars lines. Via this relay the two input lines a and b of the bus bar can be switched directly or swapped to the lines a and b of the internal bus bar. Since a common (shared) relay is used, always both system variables have to be activated for swapping the two lines.

The image illustrates the wiring of the bus bar on the VT6104/6204.

## Connecting the Bus Bars to other VT Modules

If there are no special system requirements, we recommend connecting the bus bars of all VT modules in the following way:

- Connect the battery voltage and ground (Vbatt and Gnd) to all modules. This lets you short all lines to Vbatt and ground, and makes it possible to simulate switches referenced to Vbatt or ground. Some modules like the VT6104/6204 have dedicated connections for Vbatt/ground. In other modules, such as the VT1004 and VT2004, these connections are included in the first bus bar. In the case of modules with two

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [VTSystem/VT6104] VT6104TimeSynchronization


# VT6104/ VT6204 - Time Synchronization

VT System » Modules » VT6104/ VT6204 » VT6104/ VT6204 - Time Synchronization

All modules in a VT System are synchronized internally. The VT System is synchronized, via CANoe, with the Vector network interface and with all connected VT6104/VT6204 modules. You can use hardware synchronization to further improve synchronization between the VT System and the network interfaces. There are various ways of doing this, depending on which modules and network interfaces you are using:

- No VT6104/VT6204, no VT7001:Hardware synchronization with the VT System is not possible. However, you can synchronize other network interfaces with each other independently.

- No VT6104/VT6204, at least one VT7001:You can synchronize the network interfaces with each other and with the VT System via the VT7001; see VT7001 Synchronization.

- At least one VT6104/VT6204, no external network interfaces:No further connections are needed for hardware synchronization. You only need to activate hardware synchronization in the network driver.

- At least one VT6104/VT6204, other external network interfaces (e.g. VN2600, VN3600, etc.):Connect the external network interfaces with each other using a sync cable, and to the sync connection of the first VT6104/VT6204 module. The first VT6104/VT6204 must be the Sync Master in this case. This is usually set automatically.

Once you have connected the modules as described above using sync cables, you can activate the hardware 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [VTSystem/VT6306] VT6306


# VT6306 – Ethernet Network Interface Module

VT System » Module » VT6306 – Ethernet Network Interface Module

| General |
| --- |
|  | The VT6306 Ethernet Network Module supports six automotive Ethernet channels on a specific plug-in board (separate article). Various signal paths and faults such as short circuits and line interruptions can be connected via relays. Furthermore, the module has two additional 100/1000 Mbit/s Ethernet channels, which do not permit error activation. These channels can be used for media conversion, for example. The VT6306 logically consists of two parts: ...the actual network interface and ...the VT-typical switching options for various signal paths and errors. The interface part is configured via the CANoe Network Hardware Configuration and is almost identical in handling to a VN5640. The relays are controlled via the VT System Configuration. This part behaves like a VT module in terms of application and configuration. |
| Switching Different Signal Paths | Usage, Functions, Technical Data |
| Relays can be used to switch various connections between the plus and minus line, VBatt, Gnd and the bus bar. It is also possible to switch a variable resistor between plus and minus and to interrupt the plus and minus line. The relays are switched via the corresponding system variables of the respective channel. | Functions System Variables Other Bus Bars Technical Data Please be sure to refer to the notes and technical data in the VT System Manual (PDF)(

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [VTSystem/VT6306] VT6306BusBars


# Bus Bars

VT6306 » Bus Bars

The Ethernet Network Module VT6306 has an internal bus bar. The signal line Plus of each channel can be connected via a relay to the line a of the internal bus bar, the signal line Minus to the line b of the internal bus bar. This allows the bus lines to be connected internally, Plus and Minus to be switched against further potentials or routed to an external device (e.g. oscilloscope). The module also has an interchanging relay on the bus bars between the bus bar connection and the internal bus bar lines. Via this relay, the two input lines a and b of the bus bar can be switched directly or interchanged to the lines a and b of the internal bus bar. Since a common relay is used, both system variables must always be activated for the exchange of the two lines.

## Connecting the bus bar to other VT modules

If no special requirements have to be met, the following wiring is recommended for the bus bars of all VT modules of a system:

- Connect the battery voltage and ground (Vbatt and Gnd) to all modules. This lets you short all lines to Vbatt and ground, and makes it possible to simulate switches referenced to Vbatt or ground. Some modules like the VT6306 have dedicated connections for Vbatt/ground. In other modules, such as the VT1004 and VT2004, these connections are included in the first bus bar. In the case of modules with two bus bars, Vbatt/ground is connected to bus bar 1.

- A bus bar can connect all modules, regardless of module type. T

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [VTSystem/VT7001] VT7001


# VT7001/VT7101 – Power Module

VT System » Modules » VT7001/VT7101 - Power Module

| General |
| --- |
|  | The VT7001/VT7101 power module is used to control the power supply lines of the ECU being tested. The actual power for the ECU is delivered by an internal or external power supply. The VT7001/VT7101 controls this power supply, measures voltage and current, and allows for a variety of connections between the power supply unit and the ECU. The module can also be used to generate line breaks and short circuits on the lines connected to the ECU. |
| Notes on Module Series VT7001/VT7101 |
| The term VT7001/VT7101 and the belonging description also cover variants of the module, e.g. VT7001A. If there are differing details for a variant it will be described explicitly in particular cases. The VT7001/VT7101has three RS232 interfaces for communication with external devices, especially PSUs and displays. The interfaces are assigned to the two channels for external power supplies and the channel for the entire module. A more precise description can be found here. |
| Switching Different Signal Paths | Usage, Functions, Technical Data |
| The following image illustrates the VT7001/VT7101's different inputs and outputs. You can reverse the polarity of the input of power supply unit 2. This lets you run reverse polarity tests even with a power supply unit that can only supply positive voltages. When power supply unit 2 has reversed polarity, the corresponding voltage for controllin

### [VTSystem/VT7001] VT7001FunctionsRS232


# RS232 API Functions

VT System » Modules » VT7001/VT7101 » RS232 API Functions

The VT7001/VT7101 module has three serial interfaces with which external devices can be addressed. They are primarily intended to operate power supplies and displays. Values such as those from a voltage measurement of a power supply can also be received, however.

The following commands are used solely to access the serial interfaces of the VT7001/VT7101. They are heavily oriented toward the API for accessing the RS232 interfaces of a computer.

With the exception of callbacks, all commands are called up on a system variable namespace which specifies the channel used. A serial interface is available on each of the two channels for operating external power supplies (standard designation: PS1 and PS2) and on the module channel (standard designation: M1_Sup1 resp. M1_Sup2).

| Function | Short Description |
| --- | --- |
| SerialOpen | Opens a serial port. |
| SerialClose | Closes a serial port. |
| SerialConfigure | Configures a serial port. |
| SerialSend | Sends block of data asynchronously on serial port. |
| SerialReceive | Sets receiver buffer for serial port. |
| SerialSetOnSendHandler | Sets the callback handler for Send events. |
| SerialSetOnReceiveHandler | Sets the callback handler for Receive events. |
| SerialSetOnErrorHandler | Sets the callback handler for Error events. |
| RS232 Callback Handler | Short Description |
| <OnSerialSend> | Handler will be called after completion of re

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [VTSystem/VT7001] VT7001InterconnectionModes


# Interconnection Modes

VT System » Modules » VT7001/VT7101 » Interconnection Modes

The three available power supply units (two external, one internal) and the two output line pairs (OUT1 with GND1 and OUT2 with GND2) can be connected to each other in a variety of ways. The VT7001/VT7101 power module thus allows for a variety of power supply configurations and use cases. You can even switch between these while the system is running.

The following interconnection modes are supported:

- One Power Supply Only One of the three power supply units is used (modes: supint, sup1, sup2); it is connected to both outputs. This way you can power an ECU with two different power supply inputs (typically terminal 15 and terminal 30).The two power supply inputs can be switched and measured separately (current, voltage). Alternately you can also switch and control two ECUs, each with one power supply input, independently of each other.

- Two Independent Power Supplies One of the three available power supply units is used for each output. Six combinations are possible here (modes: supint_sup1, supint_sup2, sup1_supint, sup1_sup2, sup2_supint, sup2_sup1). As with One Power Supply Only, you can power one ECU that has two separate power supply inputs (e.g. terminals 15 and 30) or two separate ECUs that each have one power supply input. In this configuration, the lines can supply different voltages, since you are using separate power supply units. You can also use this mode for the Battery vo

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [VTSystem/VT7001] VT7001InternalPowerSupplyUnit


# Internal Power Supply Unit

VT System » Modules » VT7001/VT7101 » Internal Power Supply Unit

The VT7001/VT7101 provides a third power supply unit, which is an internal power supply. This is fed by the VT System's power supply unit. It provides up to 2 A, but does not have an adjustable current limiter. The output voltage can be set between the minimum and maximum voltage as shown in the table below. You can do this directly via a system variable or an arbitrary curve, as with the external power supply units. The rise time of the output voltage is quite moderate.

| VT Module | Minimum Voltage | Maximum Voltage |
| --- | --- | --- |
| VT7001 | 3 V | 18 V |
| VT7001A | 3 V | 30 V |
| VT7101 | 0 V | 60 V |

The internal power supply unit is especially useful for static powering less power-intensive ECUs when there are no special power supply requirements (e.g. regarding rise time).

|  | Example: Using the Internal Power Supply The internal power supply unit delivers power and the ECU is connected to OUT1 (clamp 30), OUT2 (clamp 15) and GND1 (clamp 31). In this example, the channel for the internal power supply unit is named IntSupply, the two output channels are named Clamp30 and Clamp15, and the VT7001 module is named PowerSupply. CAPL // set mode to one power supply only -> internal power supply sysvar::VTS::PowerSupply.SetInterconnectionMode (eVTSInterconnectionModeSupInt); // set voltage to 12.0 V sysvar::VTS::IntSupply.SetRefVoltageMode (eVTSRefVoltageModeConstant);@sy

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [VTSystem/VT7001] VT7001MeasuringCurrentAndVoltage


# Measuring Current and Voltage

VT System » Modules » VT7001/VT7101 » Measuring Current and Voltage

The output current is measured at the two outputs, OUT1 and OUT2, and made available to CANoe as an average value via the corresponding system variables. The current is measured in seven current ranges (maximum current 100 A to 100 µA). Switching between ranges happens automatically. This wide current range makes it possible to differentiate precisely between the ECU's different operating and load states, especially when detecting energy saving states (sleep mode).

Automatic measuring range changeover can be configured in a way that measurement ranges for low current ratings up to a given level will not be used. Thereby you can prevent an unwanted switching in certain situations.Nevertheless it is possible to measure at currents for which the according range is blocked, however the accuracy of the measurement isn't as high as ususal.

The input voltages are measured at the power supply unit inputs and at the outputs, and are made available to test cases in CANoe via the corresponding system variables.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### [VTSystem/VT7001] VT7001Outputs


# Outputs

VT System » Modules » VT7001/VT7101 » Outputs

The VT7001/VT7101 has two separate outputs (OUT1 and OUT2) that each have their own ground line (GND1 and GND2). A variety of configurations can be achieved by combining these with the three power supply units (two external, one internal). In particular, you can control the ECU's two power supply inputs (clamp 15 and clamp 30) separately. You can also connect two different ECUs, execute measurements with ground offset or Vbat offset, etc. For a description of these different modes, see Interconnection Modes.

There are also two auxiliary outputs, OUT1 aux and OUT2 aux. These correspond to OUT1 and OUT2, but are not included in the measurements. You can make separate connections to them, as their purpose is to supply power to any additional components that may be needed. While these additional connections are fed from the same operating voltage, they should not add to the ECU's power consumption.

An additional connection (OUT1 bus bar) lets you connect the battery voltage Vbat to the other VT modules (typically via bus bar 1 or dedicated connections). This makes it easier to wire the VT System, as does the fact that the module has its own independent fuse (polyfuse).

|  | Example 1: Outputting a Predefined Curve Form An external power supply unit (namespace ExtSupply) is used in this example. The ECU is connected to OUT1 (namespace ECUpower) and GND1; the VT7001/VT7101 module is named PowerSupply. The output voltage 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### [VTSystem/VT7001] VT7001PowerSupplyInputs


# Power Supply Inputs

VT System » Modules » VT7001/VT7101 » Power Supply Inputs

You can connect two external power supply units to the VT7001/VT7101. Control voltages that let you define the power supply unit's output voltage and current limit (i.e. the maximum output current of the power supply unit) are available for each of the two power supply units. You can use system variables to define the control voltages directly. The control voltage for the power supply unit voltage can also be controlled via a curve that the VT7001/VT7101 outputs, which CANoe loads onto the module (see Functions). These arbitrary curves correspond to the arbitrary wave forms used with the VT2004.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |
