### [Windows/Write/WriteWindowNotifications] 52VTSystem


# VT System

Write Window » Notifications » VT System

| [52] VT System |
| --- |
| ID | Notification / Description |
| 52-0036 | Disturbance of VT System communication detected!Trying to recover. The cyclical transmission of measurement values from the VT System to CANoe is interrupted for a short time because a large number of errors has occurred. During the interruption no commands can be sent to the VT System (e.g. relay switching). If this is attempted, error 52-0044 is reported. An attempt will be made to resume data transmission after this interruption. The cause of this problem is usually a longer blockage of the computer, which can occur e.g. when a USB device is connected or some other time-consuming I/O operation is carried out.Other programs with long processing times can also cause such problems. It is advisable to avoid such processes while running a CANoe measurement. If this message reappears constantly, it might be the case that the physical connection has been lost or the VT System switched off. |
| 52-0037 | Module left operational status!Check the following messages for further information.Stopping measurement… A VT system module has exited its normal operational status, causing an immediate termination of the measurement. Possible causes for this are e.g. overheating, excessive voltage, excessive current or the internal load of the VT1004 module being operated with negative voltages. Once the measurement ends, an additional message usually appears provid

# XCP/CCP/AMD

| [59] XCP/CCP/AMD |
| --- |
| ID | Notification / Description |
| 59-0001 | A database with file name <file name> and Cluster ID <cluster id> is not configured on the FlexRay channel! To ensure that the correct database is used for the XCP measurement, the following requirements must be met: The name of the database file from the CANoe configuration (for example "Cluster_1.XML") and the file name, which is defined in the XCP_ON_FLX section from the A2L file must match The cluster ID from the respective database (eg FIBEX file) shall be consistent with the cluster ID, which is defined in the XCP_ON_FLX section. If only one database is attached on the FlexRay channel/network, then the XCP measurement starts using this database. If multiple databases are configured on the same channel/network, the XCP measurement will fail. In this case the CTO cannot be sent. |

Option .AMD/XCP

| --- |
| ID | Notification / Description |
| 69-0015 | The connection to ERT was interrupted. An attempt is made to reestablish the connection. It was discovered that the Extended Real Time (ERT) process is no longer accessible. Possible causes: The ERT process crashed due to an error. The used configuration causes an overload in ERT, thus ERT can no longer respond to connection requests which leads to a disconnection. Lowering the configured task priority of ERT network nodes that run computationally intensive models or scripts might help to solve the issue. In both cases an attempt is made to restart the ERT process and reestablish the connection. |

Extended Real Time (ERT)

### CDAUTOSARCRCObservation


# AUTOSAR CRC Observation

Check Descriptions » Frame/PDU Evaluation » AUTOSAR CRC Observation

## Description

This check monitors the CRC of a PDU or a single signal group of a PDU.

The check condition is violated if the calculated CRC does not match the value of the CRC signal contained in the PDU or signal group. The check can be set up for a single PDU or a single signal group within a PDU. The functions/constructors without signalGroup parameter monitor all specified CRC's within the given PDU.

The numeric functions/constructors with aHeaderId parameter cannot be used for FlexRay.

|  | Since Version | CAPL Function | C# Function |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Availability | ✔ | ChkCreate_CRCObservationAsrPDU, ChkStart_CRCObservationAsrPDU | — |

## Check-specific Statistic Data in Report

This check can report the following statistic data:

| Parameter | Availability (since version) | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Runtime of the statistic | ✔ | Runtime of the check. |
| Number of failures | ✔ | Number of PDU's with invalid CRC. (This data record is only available if at least one check failure occurs.) |
| Number of samples | ✔ | Number of monitored PDU's. |
| Failure ratio (in %) | ✔ | Ratio of check failure due to invalid CRC. (This data record is only available if at least one check failure occurs.) |
| Checked CRC signals (signal groups) | ✔ | The names of the checked signals, signal groups and number of failures. |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |

[...truncated for brevity...]

This check monitors the UB of a PDU, a single signal group or a single signal of a PDU.

### CDCycleTime


# Cycle Time Absolute, Cycle Time Relative

Check Descriptions » Frame/PDU Evaluation » Cycle Time Absolute, Cycle Time Relative

## Description

These checks are useful to supervise a particular range in message cycle time changes.

The checks can be setup with relative- or with absolute time definitions. The checks for supervision of multiple messages (e.g. all messages of a node) are easily setup with relative deviations.

Cycle-time-checks that are setup for multiple messages, will measure all concerned messages independent to each other. For keeping the following figure simple, a check for one single message is illustrated.

These checks can be not started in the pre-start-section of CAPL. It is recommended to create them in the pre-start section and start them in the start section.

For FlexRay only valid data frames and PDUs are recognized as communication, Null Frames and Erroneous Frames are ignored.

|  | Since Version | CAPL Function | C# Function |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Availability | ✔ | ChkCreate_MsgAbsCycleTimeViolation, ChkStart_MsgAbsCycleTimeViolation ChkCreate_MsgRelCycleTimeViolation, ChkStart_MsgRelCycleTimeViolation ChkCreate_NodeMsgsRelCycleTimeViolation, ChkStart_NodeMsgsRelCycleTimeViolation | AbsoluteCycleTimeCheck, RelativeCycleTimeCheck |

## Check-specific Statistic Data in Report

This check can report the following statistic data:

| Parameter | Availability (since version) | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Runtime of the statistic |

### CDDLC


# DLC

Check Descriptions » Frame/PDU Evaluation » DLC

## Description

This check monitors the DLC, respectively payload length of a message.

The comparison value is read from the database. The check can be set up for one message, all Tx messages or all Rx messages of a node. If the DLC supervision is set up by a Tx node, then the check observes the DLCs of application messages. Thus, diagnostic requests, diagnostic responses, network management messages and TP messages are not supervised.

For FlexRay only valid data frames and PDUs are recognized as communication, Null Frames and Erroneous Frames are ignored.

|  | Since Version | CAPL Function | C# Function |

| Availability | ✔ | ChkCreate_InconsistentDLC, ChkStart_InconsistentDLC ChkCreate_InconsistentTxDLC, ChkStart_InconsistentTxDLC ChkCreate_InconsistentRxDLC, ChkStart_InconsistentRxDLC | DlcCheck |

| Number of samples | ✔ | Number of all transmitted messages. |
| Failure ratio (in %) | ✔ | Ratio of check failure due to invalid DLC. Ratio is calculated from the number of failures and the number of samples. (This data

### CDFallback


# Fallback

Check Descriptions » Signal Value Dependency Checks (Timing, Values) » Fallback

## Description

This check monitors the reaction (occurrence of a message with a dedicated signal condition) in case the specified messages are no longer measured.

The check reports an error if after the recognition of a dropout situation, no fallback reaction occurs within the timeout time period.

Another task of the check is to monitor the validity of the fallback reactions. On reception of a fallback reaction it is checked if a dropout situation has occurred before and a fallback reaction is expected.

The check can also be used in the context of MOST and CAN system gateways.

|  | Since Version | CAPL Function | C# Function |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Availability | ✔ | — | — |

This check can report the following statistic data:

| Parameter | Availability (since version) | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Runtime of the statistic | ✔ | Runtime of the check. |
| Number of successful reactions | ✔ | Number of fallback messages which close an occurred dropout situation. |
| Number of failed reactions | ✔ | Number of delayed or missed fallback messages. |
| Number of unforced reactions | ✔ | Number of fallback messages which are sent although no dropout situation occurred. |
| Probability of successful reaction (in %) | ✔ | Probability that fallback message is sent during a dropout situation. Probability is calculated from number o

[...truncated for brevity...]

### CDJ1939AddressClaiming


# J1939 Address Claiming

Check Descriptions » J1939-Specific Checks » J1939 Address Claiming

## Description

This check observes the address claiming.

The following validations are done during this check:

- For a global Request for Address Claim all configured nodes must respond with an ACL Parameter Group.

- For a specific request for ACL, only the addressed node must respond with an ACL message.

- The Address Claim Parameter Group must always be sent globally.

- The DLC of the Address Claim Parameter Group must be 8, the DLC of the request 3.

- The default time-out is 250 ms.

- Reserved bit must be 0.

This check is available for network nodes with the Address Claiming (ACL) Parameter Group (PGN EE00h) in the Tx list.

|  | Since Version | CAPL Function | C# Function |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Availability | ✔ | ChkCreate_J1939AddressClaimViolation | — |

## Check-specific Statistic Data in Report

This check can report the following statistic data:

| Parameter | Availability (since version) | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Runtime of the statistic | ✔ | Runtime of the check. |
| Last claimed address | ✔ | Last claimed address of all nodes (each node is indicated by <node name>[<configured address>]) |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### CDJ1939AnswerCorruptRequest


# J1939 Answer Corrupt Request

Check Descriptions » J1939-Specific Checks » J1939 Answer Corrupt Request

| Name | J1939 Answer Corrupt Request |
| --- | --- |
| Description | This check observes whether a node answers on a corrupt request. There are three kinds of corrupt requests observed: EDP — Request messages with wrong edp bit are answered. DP — Request messages with wrong dp bit are answered. SA — Requests not sent to observed node is answered. |
| Remark | This check is primarily for J1939-82 compliance test – characteristics for J1939-82-Compliance are available for this check. |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |

# J1939 Answer Own Global Request

Check Descriptions » J1939-Specific Checks » J1939 Answer Own Global Request

| Name | J1939 Answer Own Global Request |
| --- | --- |
| Description | This check detects if a node answers correctly on its own global request messages. |
| Remark | The sub elements j1939_node and j1939_pg can limit the check to defined nodes and messages (with optional specification of the sender). |

J1939 Request Check (Check Description)

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### CDJ1939BAM


# J1939 BAM

Check Descriptions » J1939-Specific Checks » J1939 BAM

## Description

This check observes the BAM transport protocol (Broadcast Announce Message).

The following validation are done during a BAM transmission:

- the transmission must start with a BAM.

- Number of bytes and number of packets must be consistent.

- Min. distance between packets (min, default = 50 ms).

- Max. distance between packets (max, default = 750 ms).

- sequence number must start with 1 and must be increasing.

- the last data packet (TPDT) must be filled with FFh.

- the destination address of the message must be 255 (a specific transmission is not allowed).

This check is available for network nodes with the Transport Protocol — Connection Management (TPCM) Parameter Group (PGN EC00h) in the Tx list.

|  | Since Version | CAPL Function | C# Function |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Availability | ✔ | ChkCreate_J1939BAM J1939TestChkCreate_BAMViolation (deprecated) | — |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |

Check Descriptions » J1939-Specific Checks » J1939 Byte Order

| Name | J1939 Byte Order |
| --- | --- |
| Description | The check detects incorrect byte ordering in the data field of J1939 Parameter Groups on the bus. |

### CDJ1939CycleTime


# J1939 Cycle Time

Check Descriptions » J1939-Specific Checks » J1939 Cycle Time

| Name | J1939 Cycle Time |
| --- | --- |
| Description | This check observes the cycle times of a message. The minimum or maximum deviance from the cycle time can be specified as an absolute or relative value. Therefore CAPL and XML functions are available. If the relative setting is used the cycle time value is retrieved from the database (GenMsgCycleTime). The following verifications are made by the cycle time check: The cyclic message must be received in between the min and max time boundaries. The check starts with the first received message. |
| CAPL Function | ChkCreate_MsgAbsCycleTimeViolation, ChkCreate_MsgRelCycleTimeViolation |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### CDJ1939DPBit


# J1939 DP-Bit

Check Descriptions » J1939-Specific Checks » J1939 DP-Bit

| Name | J1939 DP-Bit |
| --- | --- |
| Description | The DP bit of Parameter Groups on the bus is compared with the DP bit of defined Parameter Groups in the database. The check fails, if the Parameter Group on the bus is defined in the database (PGN part without DP bit) but the DP bit is inverted. |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### CDJ1939DataFieldByteLength


# J1939 Data Field Byte Length

Check Descriptions » J1939-Specific Checks » J1939 Data Field Byte Length

| Name | J1939 Data Field Byte Length |
| --- | --- |
| Description | It is checked if the data length of a transmitted PG matches with the data length as defined in the database for this PG. The check only applies to PGs which are defined in the database and that are assigned to the specified node, except the PGs with a variable length (e.g. DM1 (0xFECA), ECUID (0xFDC5) or proprietary messages like 0xFF00). |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### CDJ1939EDPBit


# J1939 EDP-Bit

Check Descriptions » J1939-Specific Checks » J1939 EDP-Bit

| Name | J1939 EDP-Bit |
| --- | --- |
| Description | The EDP bit of the Parameter Groups on the bus is compared with the EDP bit of defined Parameter Groups in the database. The check fails, if the Parameter Group on the bus is defined in the database (PGN part without EDP bit) but the EDP bit is inverted. |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### CDJ1939MessagesKnown


# J1939 Messages Known

Check Descriptions » J1939-Specific Checks » J1939 Messages Known

| Name | J1939 Messages Known |
| --- | --- |
| Description | With this check Parameter Groups on the bus are compared with Parameter Groups of the database. The following checks are done: The PGN of the message must be identical to the PGN of the database. |
| Remark | The check is available for all send nodes, Tx messages and database messages. Characteristics for J1939-82-Compliance are available for this check. |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### CDJ1939PGN


# J1939 PGN

Check Descriptions » J1939-Specific Checks » J1939 PGN

| Name | J1939 PGN |
| --- | --- |
| Description | The Parameter Group number (PGN) of messages on the bus is compared with PGNs defined in the database that have the same priority and source addresses as the messages on the bus. To pass the check the PGN of the message on the bus (inclusive the destination address at specific PGs) must be identical with one of the PGNs defined in the database that have the same originator and priority. |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### CDJ1939RTSCTS


# J1939 RTS/CTS

Check Descriptions » J1939-Specific Checks » J1939 RTS/CTS

## Description

This check observes the RTS/CTS transport protocol.

It is available for network nodes with the Transport Protocol — Connection Management (TPCM) Parameter Group (PGN EC00h) in the Tx list.

Following validations are done by the RTS/CTS check:

- the transmission must begin with a RTS.

- Number of data bytes and number of packets must be consistent.

- Max. distance between Parameter Groups must not be violated.

- sequence number must be start and increase.

- last data packet must be filled with FFh.

- No abort is allowed. Exception: an abort directly after RTS can be allowed optionally.

|  | Since Version | CAPL Function | C# Function |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Availability | ✔ | ChkCreate_J1939RTSCTS J1939TestChkCreate_RTSCTSViolation (deprecated) | — |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### CDJ1939Request2Check


# J1939 Request2 Check

Check Descriptions » J1939-Specific Checks » J1939 Request2 Check

## Description

e when it receives a J1939 request2 (C900h). The check observes if the node sends the request2 Parameter Group. The check can be used for a specific requested PGN, a specific node or all requests in the network.

If a request2 Parameter Group is received, a response must be send before timeout. The response can be the requested message (if Use Transfer PGN parameter of the request is 0) or the acknowledgment Parameter Group (E800h). The request must be sent from a node in the network. If the request should be sent from the test module, the J1939 Request2 command can be used.

The XML check is able to validate the signal values of the response.

Following validation are done:

- The response must send before timeout after a request2 (PGN EA00h).

- The DLC of the request Parameter Group must be 3.

- A request should not be sent, if the last response message was sent 50 ms before the request.

The request check differs between global request, which are addressed to all nodes and specific request which are addressed to a single node. Depending on this the response must be sent global or specific.

### Global Requests

| Requested PGN | DLC | TP | Response |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Global | DLC ≤ 8 | — | Global |
| Specific | DLC ≤ 8 | — | Global |
| Global | DLC > 8 | BAM | Global |
| Specific | DLC > 8 | BAM | Global |

### Specific Requests

| Requested PGN | DLC | 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### CDJ1939RequestCheck


# J1939 Request Check

Check Descriptions » J1939-Specific Checks » J1939 Request Check

## Description

This check observes the behavior of a node when it receives a J1939 request (EA00h). The check observes if the node sends the request Parameter Group. The check can be used for a specific requested PGN, a specific node or all requests in the network.

If a request Parameter Group is received, a response must be send before timeout. The response can be the requested message or the acknowledgment Parameter Group (E800h). The request must be sent from a node in the network. If the request should be sent from the test module, the J1939 Request Check command can be used.

The XML check is able to validate the signal values of the response.

Following validation are done:

- The response must send before timeout after a request (PGN EA00h).

- The DLC of the request Parameter Group must be 3.

- A request should not be sent, if the last response message was sent 50 ms before the request.

The request check differs between global request, which are addressed to all nodes and specific request which are addressed to a single node. Depending on this the response must be sent global or specific.

### Global Requests

| Requested PGN | DLC | TP | Response |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Global | DLC ≤ 8 | — | Global |
| Specific | DLC ≤ 8 | — | Global |
| Global | DLC > 8 | BAM | Global |
| Specific | DLC > 8 | BAM | Global |

### Specific Requests

| Requested PGN | DLC | TP | Respons

| Name | J1939 Request Invalid Acknowledgment |
| --- | --- |
| Description | This check detects if a peer-to-peer request is answered with an acknowledge message by a node different than the receiver node. Properties: expect limits the check to specified acknowledge messagesNACK = a negative ACK is expectedPACK = a positive ACK is expectedBUSY = a "cannot respond" ACK is expectedDENIED = an "access denied" ACK is expectedThe values can be separated by a comma, the each specified response is accepted. sendNode limits the checked (N)ACK messagesIf a node is specified, only (N)ACK messages of that PG are checked that are sent peer-to-peer to the specified node Sub elements <j1939_node> (N)ACK messages of the specified node only are checked <j1939_pg> (N)ACK messages of all nodes but only for requests of the specified Parameter Group, can be additionally limited by using the optional sendNode property. |

StableLock (ChkStart_MostStableLock(), <mostlightlock eventtype = "stablelock">) Variant 2 The diagram shows the sequence of Light & Lock states when turning off light modulation for 80 ms (tLightOff > tUnlock, see MOST specification): The Light & Lock checks show the following behavior, depending on their type and parameterization: 1. ### CDMOSTMethodProtocolObservation


# MOST Method Protocol Observation

Check Descriptions » MOST-Specific Checks » MOST Method Protocol Observation

| Name | MOST Method Protocol Observation |
| --- | --- |
| Description | This check monitors adherence to the MOST protocol in relation to message sequences and response times for methods with StartResult and StartResultAck command Optypes. You can specify three parameters when setting up the check. tWaitForProcessing1 and tWaitForProcessing2 correspond in their functionality to the MOST specification timeouts of the same name. tMaxDuration specifies the maximum length of time that the check will wait for the final Result/ResultAck or Error/ErrorAck message. The behavior of the check at run-time may be described as follows: The check uses spy messages to observe the methods. For this reason, it is important to make sure that the node or channel in which the check is implemented is receiving spy messages relevant to the monitored method correctly, before the check is used. When messages using the StartResult and StartResultAck command Optypes (i.e. requests) are received, the check saves them along with their source and target addresses, InstanceID, time stamp and SenderHandle parameter, if present. If a StartResultAck message does not contain a SenderHandle parameter (i.e. when the payload data is less than 2 bytes in length), a check violation is triggered immediately and the request is ignored. If a Processing or ProcessingAck Optype report message that matche

Check Descriptions » MOST-Specific Checks » MOST NetState Observation

| Name | MOST NetState Observation |
| --- | --- |
| Description | This check is used to monitor the NetState state of the MOST hardware interface. Concrete checks can be implemented for any NetState state changes, and are triggered when a NetState event having the corresponding target state occurs with a specified initial state. The diagram shows the state progress of NetInterfaceStatus and ConfigStatus when a light failure (no light) occurs (tLightOff < tUnlock). Both signals represented by the NetState event. In this scenario NetState checks show the following behavior depending on their parameter values for old and new state: 1. Initial State= "ConfigOk" (ChkStart_MostNetState(5, -1), <mostnetstate oldstate="configok">) After activating the check, the first NetState event occurring that leads to leaving the old state ConfigOk causes a check violation. 2. Target State= "ConfigOk" (ChkStart_MostNetState(-1, 5), <mostnetstate newstate="configok">) 3. Initial State= "PowerOff"Target State= "NetInterfaceInit" (ChkStart_MostNetState(2, 3), <mostnetstate oldstate = "poweroff" newstate="netinterfaceinit") |
| Remark | The check works on the NetState events that are generated by CANoe, as soon as a change of the NetState occurs. It will not automatically recognize the current NetState at the time of its activation. Therefore it’s possible, that a check waits for a NetState, which i

### CDMOSTPropertyProtocolObservation


# MOST Property Protocol Observation

Check Descriptions » MOST-Specific Checks » MOST Property Protocol Observation

| Name | MOST Property Protocol Observation |
| --- | --- |
| Description | This check observes the compliance to the MOST protocol with regard to message sequences and response times for a given property function. The behavior of the check at run-time may be described as follows: The check uses spy messages to observe the properties. For this reason, it is important to make sure that the node or channel in which the check is implemented is receiving the relevant spy messages before the check is used. Command OpType messages (Get, SetGet, GetInterface, Set, Increment, Decrement) — so-called requests — are saved upon receipt along with their time stamp. Incoming report OpType messages are compared with the saved messages to establish whether the response is valid. If this is the case and the difference between the receipt time stamps is smaller than the maximum response time, the request message is deleted again. The saved request messages are compared with the current time stamp at regular intervals. If the maximum response time is exceeded, a check violation is triggered for those request messages that require a response (Get, SetGet and GetInterface). If a sequence consists of multiple identical command messages that follow one another sequentially, only the last one received is saved and used for resolution with an appropriate report message. Conversely, t

[...truncated for brevity...]

### CDMessageCountObservation


# Message Count Observation

Check Descriptions » FlexRay-Specific Checks » Message Count Observation

## Description

Monitors the bus and reports if a minimum and/or a maximum number for each of the defined messages occurred within a specified cyclic time interval.

It is also allowed to specify one node as reference. In this case, all Tx messages of the node are observed. Then, an occurrence is counted per defined message. That means, for example, if maxcount was set to two, and four different Tx messages were specified as reference, the check fails if any of the messages was sent three times.

In contrast to the Occurrence Count (Check Description) check there is not only one counter per check, but one counter per defined message. Additionally a minimum and maximum number of allowed/required messages is observed. When the time interval expires, the minimum counter values are checked, each counter is reset to zero and the interval starts over again.

This check is only defined for FlexRay frames and PDUs. Null Frames and Erroneous Frames are ignored.

|  | Since Version | CAPL Function | C# Function |

| Availability | ✔ | TestCheck::CreateMsgSendCountViolation, TestCheck::StartMsgSendCountViolation TestCheck::CreateNodeMsgSendCountViolation, TestCheck::StartNodeMsgSendCountViolation | — |

Occurrence Count (Check Description)

### CDMessagesKnown


# Messages Known

Check Descriptions » Frame/PDU Evaluation » Messages Known

## Description

This check observes the current bus and reports messages that are not defined in any of the databases that are associated to the bus.

This check additionally checks some consistence of the databases. Multiply defined messages (of the same id) are reported by a warning notification during creation of the check.

For FlexRay only valid data frames are recognized as communication, Null frames and Erroneous frames are ignored.

Since CANoe 8.1 SP2 for a FlexRay PDU database (FIBEX ≥ 3.0 or AUTOSAR System Description) the mapped frames of all PDUs will be observed to be known. The check will fail on receiving any other frame. Before that CANoe version the check will always succeed for a FlexRay PDU database, because only the PDUs will be observed to be valid. (Unknown PDUs does not exist.)

### CDNodeActive


# Node Active

Check Descriptions » Frame/PDU Evaluation » Node Active

## Description

This check allows the supervision of the activity of nodes.

It reports a problem, if the node has not sent any of the configured Tx messages within a given time-interval. Use case: checks if the node still sends any message, e.g. an "alive" message.

For FlexRay only valid data frames and PDUs are recognized as communication, Null Frames and Erroneous Frames are ignored.

### CDNodeInactive


# Node Inactive

Check Descriptions » Frame/PDU Evaluation » Node Inactive

## Description

This check allows the observation of the end of the activity of nodes.

After the time interval, in which sending is allowed, no further message of the node or the CAN message list should be sent. From this time point forward each transmission of a message leads to a check failure and is reported. This is useful for shut-down phases or for the start of sleep active phases.

For FlexRay only valid data frames and PDUs are recognized as communication, Null Frames and Erroneous Frames are ignored.

|  | Since Version | CAPL Function | C# Function |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Availability | ✔ | ChkCreate_AllNodesBabbling, ChkStart_AllNodesBabbling ChkCreate_NodeBabbling, ChkStart_NodeBabbling | — |

| --- | --- | --- |
| Runtime of the statistic | ✔ | Runtime of the check. |

| Transmitted messages (without send node) | ✔ | Below this row all messages that have no transmitter specified are listed with the number of their occurrences. |
| Last transmission | ✔ | Time of last transmission after start of check. |

### CDOccurrenceCount


# Occurrence Count

Check Descriptions » Frame/PDU Evaluation » Occurrence Count

## Description

The check is used to monitor that certain messages are not sent on the bus. It can also be specified how often a defined message may occur until the check fails.

The definition of the monitored messages is carried out exclusively via message ID under CAPL. It is also possible to specify several messages at the same time in XML test modules. In this case, an occurrence of one of the defined messages counts as one occurrence. That means, for example, if maxcount was set to two, and three different messages were specified as reference, the check fails if each of the messages was sent once.

For FlexRay only valid data frames and PDUs are recognized as communication, Null Frames and Erroneous Frames are ignored.

| Number of distance undercuts | ✔ | Number of all occurred minimum distance undercuts. |
| Minimal measured distance | ✔ | Minimum measured send distance. |
| Maximal measured distance | ✔ | Maximum measured send distance. |
| Average measured distance | ✔ | Average measured send distance. |
| Rate in distance undercuts / min | ✔ | Average number of distance undercuts per minute. This data record is only available if at least one distance 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### CDOccurrenceOfMessages


# Occurrence Of Messages

Check Descriptions » Frame/PDU Evaluation » Occurrence Of Messages

## Description

This check is used to monitor the minimum and maximum distance of messages. This check is well-suited even for mixed, cyclic and spontaneous transmission modes. When setting up the check the times to be monitored are calculated from the message's cycle time (minimum of GenMsgCycleTime and GenSigCycleTime), a minimum send delay (GenMsgDelayTime) and relative time entries.

- The GenSigCycleTime attribute is not absolutely necessary.

- The GenMsgDelayTime attribute must be defined if a relative minimum time is entered.

The check can be set up for one message or for all Tx messages of a node.

When setting up the check for a node, only those Tx messages for which periodic transmission is defined are considered. This involves looking at the attributes GenMsgSendType (Enum values: 0, 4, 5) and GenSigSendType (Enum value 0). If the message attribute or the signal attribute of a signal of this message is set to one of the named Enum values, this message is monitored by the check; otherwise it is ignored.

The cycle period of the message is calculated according to the absolutely scheduled timing of the FlexRay frame.

For FlexRay only valid data frames and PDUs are recognized as communication, Null Frames and Erroneous Frames are ignored.

|  | Since Version | CAPL Function | C# Function |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Availability | ✔ | ChkCreate_MsgRelOccurrenceViolation, 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### CDPayloadGapsObservation


# Payload Gaps Observation

Check Descriptions » Frame/PDU Evaluation » Payload Gaps Observation

## Description

This check monitors the payload gaps(payload gaps are the unused bits between signals in a message) and the DLC of a message.

The check condition is violated if the payload gaps do not match the specified default bit value or the DLC does not match the specified DLC of the database. The check can be set up for one message, all Tx messages or all Rx messages of a node. If the check is set up by a Tx node, then the check observes the payload gaps and DLC of application messages. Thus, diagnostic requests, diagnostic responses, network management messages and TP messages are not supervised.

For FlexRay only valid data frames and PDUs are recognized as communication, Null Frames and Erroneous Frames are ignored. The numeric functions/constructors with the parameter aMessageId cannot be used for FlexRay. Instead use the numeric constructors with the parameter slotID (that can only be applied to a FlexRay bus).

|  | Since Version | CAPL Function | C# Function |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Availability | ✔ | ChkCreate_PayloadGapsObservation, ChkStart_ PayloadGapsObservation ChkCreate_ PayloadGapsObservationRx, PayloadGapsObservationRx ChkCreate_ PayloadGapsObservationTx, ChkStart_ PayloadGapsObservationTx | — |

| Parameter | Availability (since version) | Description |
|

### CDRequestResponse


# Request Response

Check Descriptions » Signal Value Dependency Checks (Timing, Values) » Request Response

## Description

In this check, observation is message-oriented but stimuli are not used. The check is used to validate processing times (e.g. by gateways), how long it takes until a signal is translated from one message into another message. The check will fail if no valid translation can be measured within a given timeout period.

This check is particularly suitable for gateway tests. Relations between CAN and MOST messages can also be checked, in both directions.

Only 1 request message and 1 response message can be specified. If several response messages need to be monitored, this check can be used several times in a row.

| Number of samples | ✔ | Number of received request and response messages. |
| Minimum routing time | ✔ | Minimum measured routing time. (This data record is only available if at

### CDSignalCycleTimeAbsolute


# Signal Cycle Time Absolute

Check Descriptions » Signal Evaluation » Signal Cycle Time Absolute

## Description

The check is useful to observe the send distance of a signal.

A check failure will be generated, if the time between two consecutive signal occurrences is out of the specified range.

Check also works for multiplexed signals.

### CDTimeout


# Timeout

Check Descriptions » Time Evaluation » Timeout

## Description

This check guards a sequence with a accumulated timeout for a certain sequence of test steps.

Useful particularly if the sum of timeouts for the individual test steps are much bigger than the allowed timeout for the whole sequence.

The check is only useful in test modules. In simulation nodes this task is executed by CAPL timers.

### CDValueDependency


# Value Dependency

Check Descriptions » Signal Value Dependency Checks (Timing, Values) » Value Dependency

## Description

This check observes the dependency of two signal values sets:

- Once the first value set satisfies the value conditions, after a specific period of time the second value set must satisfy the other value conditions.If the first value set has values that do not (or no longer) satisfy the conditions, the second value set is not validated.

- If the second value set violates the value conditions after the time mintime or the value conditions are not satisfied by the time timeout, then the check condition has been violated.

The check is performed on update of any related value.

The following figures demonstrate the behavior of the check if exactly one signal is configured in the while and in the match element.

| Number of samples | ✔ | Number of value updates of the seco

### CDValueValid


# Value Valid

Check Descriptions » Signal Evaluation » Value Valid

## Description

This check is useful to supervise the value of signals and environment/system variables. The check is performed only if a value changes. Value ranges (intervals) can be specified that should never be left or that should never be reached.

These checks can be used for the following application use cases:

- Supervise alarm states of signals and handle them in CAPL

- Supervise the allowed value range of a signal and report violations

### CRReport


# Report

Report

## Description

The Report command can be used to enter comments in text form as part of test cases or preparation and finalization steps.

- In addition to the title attributes of the test/control commands, comments are used to provide information concerning the test sequence as part of the test report (test unit/test module). They are inserted in the report at the relevant location.

- In addition to the text, object values can be inserted into the report. Use the Object Value property to add the current value of a signal, system variable or environment variable to the report.

- Additionally, the comment can be supplemented with an image in extended syntax. Supported image types are: BMP, GIF, JPG, PNG. The existence of the file is not checked.

- A file path or URL can be provided for the image resource. Relative file paths are based on the folder that will contain the test report.

- Optionally, the size to which the image should be scaled in the report can be specified in the width and height properties.If width or height is specified, the HTML report will automatically show the image with a link which opens the image in full size in a new window. The width and height parameters are transferred to the HTML report as is, their values can be given as pixels or percentage. See an HTML reference for details.

## Properties

| Property | Availability (since version) | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Height (optional) | 1.0 | Height to which the image s

### CheckReport


# Check Report

Check Descriptions » Check Report

## General

For some checks statistic information is available in the automatically generated test report (test unit/test module).

On the configuration level of the check the "Statistic" of the check consists of the check parameters and the measured statistic data. The statistic data is check-specific. Information about the check-specific statistic data can be found in each check description.

Additional on each execution level of the check, in which the check fails, a "Mini-Statistic" of the check is available. It consists of the check parameters and of run-time-limited, not check-specific statistic data, which is composed of the number of failures and the number of samples.

## Checks with statistic information within the automatically generated report

| Cycle Time Absolute, Cycle Time Relative DLC Error Frame Count Fallback Message Distance Messages Known Node Active Node Inactive | No Value Change Occurrence Count Occurrence of Messages Request Response Timeout Value Dependency Value Valid |

## Check report elements

The following XML elements can be used in XML test modules to report information in the test report:

| Test Report Element | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Elements for Test Sequence |
| Comment (Report) | Output general information in the test report |
| Value Comment | Output run-time specific values in the test report |
| Elements for detailed information to specific structuring elements of the test mo

### CAPLBrowserBreakPointWindow


# Breakpoint Window

CAPL Browser » Windows » Breakpoint Window

The Breakpoint Window displays the breakpoints that were set in the Debugger. The breakpoints can be enabled, disabled or deleted there via the Debugging ribbon tab or the context menu of the Text Editor.

|  | Note To make sure that the correct breakpoints are visible, always open files via the toolbar or the context menu of the Debugger. To display modified breakpoints also in the Debugger, you have to save the modified file in the CAPL Browser. |

You can enable, disable, delete, and navigate to set breakpoints using the table view.

| Enabled | You can enable/disable an individual breakpoint in this column. |
| --- | --- |
| Name | In this column file name and row number are displayed. |
| File | The file path of the file in which a breakpoint was set is displayed in this column.Double-click in this column to navigate to the corresponding breakpoint regardless of whether it is currently enabled or disabled. |
| Line | The line number in which you have set the breakpoint is displayed in this column. |

## Context Menu

Delete Deletes a selected breakpoint. To do so, select the table line of the desired breakpoint. The Enabled column has no effect here.

Navigate to Location The cursor will be positioned at the start of the relevant line in the open file, regardless of whether the breakpoint is enabled or disabled.

Enable Breakpoint Enables a breakpoint.

Disable Breakpoint Disables a breakpoint.

## Context

[...truncated for brevity...]

### CAPLBrowserConcept


# CAPL Browser: Concept

CAPL Browser » CAPL Browser: Concept

| Overview |
| --- |
| Using the CAPL Browser, you can write CAPL programs (Communication Access Programming Language) for stimulating, simulating, testing and diagnostics.Thus the problem of remaining bus subscribers not yet being available for tests arises when developing control units, for example. To map the system environment, the data traffic of all the other network subscribers can be simulated, for example, using CAPL.You can also use CAPL to write programs for problem-oriented analysis of the data traffic or program a gateway to exchange data between different buses. The well-laid-out program interface with various windows offers you the option of composing and compiling complex program sequences and easily locating possible programming errors in the program code. CAPL programs are added in CANoe as CAPL program nodes. Event handlers serve as an input in CAPL, with which you can respond to external events (e.g. the arrival of certain messages). These language elements and the symbolic access to the various symbols defined in the database enable easy creation of CAPL program nodes. |
| Environment Information and Data |
| The CAPL Browser monitors the symbols, i.e. database symbols, diagnostic parameters, system and environment variables are updated automatically if changes are made. Configuration data of the current CANoe configuration, such as networks, channels, nodes etc., is not updated automatically

[...truncated for brevity...]

### CAPLBrowserWindows


# Window Overview

CAPL Browser » Window Overview

|  | Document View All opened CAPL programs are displayed in the Document View. Every time a new program is opened, a page appears (this means that multiple programs can be edited in parallel). |
| --- | --- |
|  | Symbol Explorer All available symbols are shown in the Symbol Explorer. They can be inserted into an opened CAPL program using drag and drop. |
|  | MOST Symbol Explorer All available MOST symbols are shown in the MOST Symbol Explorer. They can be inserted into an opened CAPL program using drag and drop. |
|  | CAPL Function Explorer All available predefined functions and event handlers are displayed in the CAPL Function Explorer. They can be inserted into an opened CAPL program using drag and drop. |
|  | Output Window Compiler messages on the currently active program are displayed in the Output Window. |
|  | Find Results Window The results of the functions Search in Files and Replace in Files are displayed in the Find Results Window. |
|  | Bookmark Window The bookmarks set in the Text Editor are displayed in the Bookmark Window. |
|  | Breakpoint Window The breakpoints set in the Text Editor are displayed in the Breakpoint Window. |

Window Management

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### CAPLBrowserCompileCAPLPrograms


# Compile CAPL Programs

CAPL Introduction » Compile CAPL Programs

In order to create an executable program file in CBF format (CAPL Binary Format, file extension *.cbf), you must compile the program with the CAPL Compiler.

A successfully compiled file will then be saved automatically insofar as this is set accordingly in the editor options.

Compiling of a program is started by the Compile command by clicking in the Home ribbon tab or by pressing the <F9> key. If the CAPL program does not have a file name yet, you are asked to enter a name for the CBF file. If the program already has a name, the name of the CBF file is composed of the program name and the extension .cbf.

Any errors or warnings that occur during the compiling process are listed in the Output view.

|  | Note In the CAPL code outside of quotation marks (""), no special characters are allowed. This concerns, among others, especially the German "Umlaut" characters space characters of some local code pages with an encoding of more than one byte Starting with CANoe 8.5 SP3, special characters are not tolerated any more by the CAPL Compiler, but result in a compile error. |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### CAPLBrowserDatabases


# Working with Databases

CANoe » Working with DatabasesCAPL Introduction » Working with Databases

CANoe supports work with symbolic databases. Among other things these contain assignments between message identifiers and symbolic message names as well as signals where data segments within the messages are described symbolically.

Databases are created and edited with the help of the CANdb++ Editor. The assignment of databases must be made in the Simulation Setup. Afterwards, you can use these symbolic parameters in the CAPL models and in function blocks and measurement windows of the measurement configuration instead of identifiers and data bytes.

You can enter symbolic message names or signal names as well as environment variables via drag and drop from the Symbol Explorer/MOST Symbol Explorer.

You can find an overview of the assigned databases on the File ribbon tab|Overview.

|  | Example If you assign a database to CANoe in which the message EngineData with identifier 123 is defined, in the Trace Window, for example, you would obtain the clear text EngineData in symbolic mode instead of the identifier 123. In the database you can also define signals, i.e. symbolic descriptions of data segments within a message. Besides the entry of the data segment, the signal definition also includes characteristics such as the machine format (Motorola/Intel), sign handling, a conversion formula and physical units of measurement. This permits direct display of physical parameters in 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### CAPLBrowserDatabasesMultiple


# Use of Multiple Databases

CAPL Introduction » Use of Multiple Databases

With large systems it may be sensible to distribute the descriptions of messages and signals as well as environment variables to several partial databases. Also, when operation with several buses it makes sense to describe each system by its own database.

CANoe supports the simultaneous use of multiple databases. The assignment of databases must be made in the Simulation Setup.

You can find an overview of the assigned databases on the File ribbon tab|Overview.

Afterwards, you can use symbolic names in all function blocks and in CAPL for the messages, signals and environment variables of all databases. To do this, enter the symbolic name in the appropriate input box. You will find a list of all symbolic names in the signal selection dialogs. The list is opened by activating the small buttons located next to the appropriate input boxes. Then you can select the desired symbolic name from this list.

If you are using more than one database, the messages in the databases following the first database are qualified with the database name. However, you only need these qualified names to resolve ambiguities. As long as the symbolic names are unique in all databases, you can skip qualification of symbolic names in all function blocks and when editing CAPL programs.

Simulation Setup | Simulation Setup - Renaming a Database | Database Filter | Use of Multiple Databases | Working with Symbolic Identifiers | U

[...truncated for brevity...]

### CAPLBrowserDialogChangedProgram


# Dialog: Changed File

CAPL Introduction » Dialog: Changed File

This dialog is opened automatically when files opened in the CAPL Browser are changed in another application (e.g., if you have copied a new version or received a version from a version management system).

The name of the modified file is displayed.

With [Yes] the current version of the file is loaded. If you have previously made changes to this file in the CAPL Browser and these changes have not yet been saved, you can save the file under a new name.

With [No] the current version of the file is not loaded. When the file is saved via the CAPL Browser, the changes made in another application are overwritten.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### CAPLBrowserMacrosReuseCAPLCode


# Reuse CAPL Code

CAPL Introduction » Macros » Reuse CAPL Code

With the help of special macros, you can reuse CAPL code more easily in multiple Simulation Setup nodes. The macros are replaced with text by the CAPL Compiler prior to subsequent processing in the program.

The following macros are available:

| Macro | Short Description |
| --- | --- |
| %BASE_FILE_NAME% | Name of the .can file that is just compiled (e.g. SomeFile.can); especially useful in include files. |
| %BASE_FILE_NAME_NO_EXT% | Name of the .can file that is just compiled but without file extension (e.g. SomeFile); especially useful in include files. |
| %BUS_TYPE% | Bus type of the channel to which the node is assigned. |
| %CHANNEL% | Number of the channel to which the node is assigned. |
| %FILE_NAME% | Name of the file that contains the source code (e.g. SomeIncludeFile.cin). |
| %FILE_NAME_NO_EXT% | Name of the file that contains the source code but without file extension (e.g. SomeIncludeFile). |
| %LINE_NUMBER% | Number of the line in the file containing the macro (available with CANoe 13.0). |
| %NETWORK_NAME% | Name of the network to which the node is assigned. |
| %NODE_NAME% | Name of the node. |

|  | Example write("The node name is %NODE_NAME%");sysSetVariableString(sysvar::MySpace::VarString, "%CHANNEL%");write("Activated functionality in network %NETWORK_NAME% (Channel %BUS_TYPE%%CHANNEL%)"); |

|  | Note The macros %NODE_NAME%, %BUS_TYPE%, %CHANNEL% and %NETWORK_NAME% are only useful in 

[...truncated for brevity...]

### CAPLBrowserRXSemantics


# Rx Semantics for CAPL Nodes in the CANoe Simulation Setup

CAPL Introduction » Rx Semantics for CAPL Nodes in the CANoe Simulation Setup

With the help of the DIR selector you can determine in the Measurement Setup whether a message has been transmitted from an external network node (DIR == Rx) or from a CAPL program in the Simulation Setup (DIR == Tx).

However, since network nodes in CANoe are simulated as independent units, the semantics was changed in the Simulation Setup for CANoe versions 1.2 and 2.0: CAPL nodes in the Simulation Setup now receive (with the exception of the sender) all messages with DIR == Rx, regardless of whether the sender is real or simulated by CANoe. The selector SIMULATED is provided so that you can differentiate whether the received message was transmitted by a real or simulated component.

If you wish to use models from older versions, in which the DIR selector was used to differentiate between a real and simulated sender, you can make CANoe compatible with the semantics of earlier versions (DIR == Rx for a real sender and DIR == Tx for a sender in the CANoe Simulation Setup) by making the following entry in the section [System] of the project file CAN.INI:

TXRXSWAP = 1

However, for new projects you should use selectors with the new and clearer semantics.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### CAPLBrowserSourceCodeHints


# Source Code Notes

CAPL Introduction » Source Code Notes

There are the following hints for the CAPL programming:

- The source code size (in MB) is not limited.

- Every source code nesting (the section between { and }) can contain about 9990 instructions.

- The line length is unlimited.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### CBRCommandLineApplication


# Command Line Application

CAPL Browser » Command Line Application

With the console application CAPLBrowserCLI.exe you can encrypt CAPL files.

The application is especially designed for integration in a CI pipeline (e.g. Jenkins).

| Parameter | Short Description |
| --- | --- |
| -h | Shows the help |
| -v | Shows the version of the application |
| -e {file} | Encrypts the specified CAPL file |

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |


### CBRibbon


# Ribbon

CAPL Browser » Ribbon

Ribbon

In the CAPL Browser ribbon the following tabs are available:

| File | Home | Filter | Debug | Layout |  |

## Quick Access Toolbar

As default in the Quick Access Toolbar the following functions are available:

- Open With a click on the symbol you open a dialog to select a previously saved file. The file is opened in a new tab. By entering the name of a non-existent file in the file selection dialog, you can create a new document with the entered name. If the CAPL Browser cannot display the program, you are asked whether you want to view and edit the program in a text editor.

- Save With a click on the symbol you save the active file. If you save a file for the first time, you will be prompted to enter a file name in a dialog.

- Undo With a click on the symbol you undo the last action. This is possible until another procedure is selected.

- Redo With a click on the symbol you repeat the previously undone action. This is possible until another procedure is selected.

- Navigate backwards With a click on the symbol you navigate in the Navigator of the Document View backwards.

- Navigate forwards With a click on the symbol you navigate in the Navigator of the Document View forward.

- Options

|  | Note You can use the context menu of the symbols and group titles in the ribbon to add each symbol or group to the toolbar. You can remove symbols again using the context menu of the Quick Access Toolbar. |

### CBRibbonDebug


# Debug

CAPL Browser » Ribbon » Debug

Ribbon: Tab Debug

| File | Home | Debug | Filter | Layout |  |

| Symbol | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Group Breakpoints |
|  | Toggle Breakpoint With a click on the symbol you add a new breakpoint or delete an existing one. |
|  | Go to Previous With a click on the symbol you navigate to the previous breakpoint. |
|  | Go to Next With a click on the symbol you navigate to the next breakpoint. |
|  | Enable All With a click on the symbol you enable all breakpoints. |
|  | Disable All With a click on the symbol you disable all breakpoints. |
|  | Delete All With a click on the symbol you delete all breakpoints. |
| Group CAPL Runtime Error |
|  | Find Runtime Error With a click on the symbol you open the Find Runtime Error dialog. Localizes a runtime error that occurred while running a CAPL file. Runtime errors are identified clearly by a number that is displayed when the error occurs. CAPL Browser asks for this number in an input dialog and displays a message in the Output Window. With a double click on this message the location in the CAPL file that caused the runtime error is displayed. |
|  | Show All Possible Runtime Errors Localizes all possible runtime errors that occurred while running a CAPL file. All possible runtime errors are displayed in the Output Window of the CAPL Browser. With a double click on one of these messages the location in the CAPL file that caused the runtime error is displayed. |

### CBRibbonFilter


# Filter

CAPL Browser » Ribbon » Filter

Ribbon: Tab Filter

| File | Home | Filter | Debug | Layout |  |

| Symbol | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Group Filter |
|  | Configuration With a click on the symbol you reset the filter settings to the settings of the CANoe environment. With a click on the symbol you activate all possible filters. All available functions, event handlers and symbols are displayed, regardless of the CANoe environment. With a click on the symbol you deactivate all filters. |
|  | Activate/deactivate filter The filters in the CAPL Browser depend on the CANoe/CANalyzer environment and control the visibility of the predefined functions, event handlers and symbols and affect the display in the Function Explorer, the autocomplete input assistance and Symbol Explorer/MOST Symbol Explorer.If a MOST bus is not used in the CANoe configuration, no MOST functions are displayed. The protocol and bus system filters (CAN, FlexRay etc.) control the visibility of the predefined functions and event handlers. They affect the Function Explorer and the autocomplete input assistance function. |

### CBRibbonHome


# Home

CAPL Browser » Ribbon » Home

Ribbon: Tab Home

| File | Home | Filter | Debug | Layout |  |

| Symbol | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Group Clipboard |
|  | Paste With a click on the symbol you insert the contents of the clipboard at the active cursor position in the text |
|  | Cut With a click on the symbol you cut the selected text and paste it to the clipboard. Click on Paste to add the text to the file again. |
|  | Copy With a click on the symbol you copy the selected text to the clipboard. This does not change the file. Click on Paste to add the text to the file again. |
|  | Delete With a click on the symbol you delete the selected text. The text is not copied to the clipboard. |
| Group Compile |
|  | Compile With a click on the symbol you create an executable CBF file (CAPL Binary Format, file extension CBF) from the active CAPL program. The name of the CBF file is composed of the name of the CAPL program file and the CBF extension. The CBF file is always saved in the same directory as the CAPL program file. If the CAPL program was not read from a file and has not been saved in a file yet, CAPL Browser prompts for the name of the CBF file in a File Save dialog. |
|  | Compile All With a click on the symbol you create executable CBF files (CAPL Binary Format, file extension CBF) from all loaded CAPL programs. The name of the CBF files is composed of the name of the CAPL program file and the CBF extension. The CBF files are always saved in the same directory

### SaveFileEncrypted


# Save As Encrypted

CAPL Browser » Save As Encrypted

Ribbon: File Tab|Save As Encrypted…

The CAPL Browser can save files encrypted. Afterwards they are no longer editable and cannot be reviewed. CANoe can still use these files like normal CAPL files.

- Encrypted CAPL files have the extension .canencr (in place of .can).

- Encrypted CAPL Include files have the extension .cinencr (in place of .cin).

To use an encrypted CAPL file in CANoe, the plain file (extension .can) has to be configured in CANoe. Thereafter the original CAN file can be deleted and replaced by the encrypted CANENCR file. The CANoe configuration doesn't need to be customized.

If both encrypted and original CAPL files are available, CANoe will use the original file.

Encrypted CAPL Include files are used in the same manner. The #include statement always refers to the original CIN file. The encrypted file is automatically used if the original file doesn't exist but an encrypted CINENCR file is available at the same place.

| Version 15© Vector Informatik GmbH |  |
